[Topic: strip-mining. Claim: should\nbe tightly controlled for three reasons.]\nNotice that statements 3 and 6 clearly predict the organization of the essay that will follow. Notice,\ntoo, that every statement conveys the purpose of its writer. Statements 1 to 4 announce that the\nwriters mainly want to explain something to readers: music choices for consumers, a company’s\nsuccess in avoiding a scandal, and so on. Statements 5 and 6 announce that the authors mainly want\nto convince readers of something: the federal government should aid qualified college students;\nstrip-mining should be controlled.\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 9\nIn some cultures it is considered rude or unnecessary for a writer to state his or her main idea\noutright or to state it near the beginning. When writing in American schools or workplaces, you can\nassume that your readers expect a clear and early idea of what you think.\n2 Drafting and revising a thesis statement\nTo draft a thesis statement, begin with your question about your subject. If you have updated\nthe question as you generated ideas and information, answering it can get you started.\nQuestion Why did Lincoln delay in emancipating the slaves?\nAnswer Lincoln’s goal was primarily to restore and preserve the Union.\nQuestion How might the Internet help to break down barriers between people?\nAnswer Anonymous Internet communication can bypass physical differences.\nThe next step is to spell out the answer in a sentence that names the topic and makes a claim about\nit. Creating this sentence may require several drafts.\nSara Ling went through a common process in writing and revising her thesis statement on\nInternet communication. She first answered her starting question, as shown in the second pair of\nexamples above. Then she tried a statement derived from her answer:\nInternet communication that is anonymous can bypass physical differences.\nLing saw that this statement focused on her starting topic (Internet communication) but somewhat\nburied the crucial quality of ano-nymity. And the claim lacked significance: So what? Ling first\ntried to emphasize her intended subject:\nThe anonymity of Internet communication . . .\nThen she worked on her claim:\n. . . can bypass physical differences, and it could build diversity into\ncommunity.\nThis statement said why the subject was significant (it could build diversity into community) but the\nidea was tacked on with and. Ling tried again, emphasizing cause and effect:\nThrough bypassing physical differences, the unique anonymity of Internet communication could build\ndiversity into community.\nFor her final revision, Ling responded to a friend’s comment that bypassing physical differences\nwas too vague. She spelled out her meaning:\nBy lowering the barriers of physical appearance, the unique anonymity of Internet communication could\nbuild diversity into community.\nWhen you are writing and revising your thesis statement, check it against the following\nquestions:\nHere are other examples of thesis statements revised to meet these requirements:\nOriginal Revised\nSeat belts can save lives, but now If drivers had used lifesaving\ncarmakers are installing air bags. seat belts more often, carmakers\n[Not unified: how do the two might not have needed to\nparts of the sentence relate?] install air bags.\nThis new product brought in This new product succeeded\nover $300,000 last year. [A state- because of its innovative mar-\nment of fact, not a claim about keting campaign, including\nthe product: what is significant widespread press coverage,\nabout the product’s success?] in-store entertainment, and a\nconsumer newsletter.\nPeople should not go on fad Fad diets can be dangerous\ndiets. [A vague statement that when they deprive the body of\nneeds limiting with one or essential nutrients or rely ex-\nmore reasons: what’s wrong cessively on potentially harm-\nwith fad diets?] ful foods.\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 10\nTelevised sports are different Although television cannot\nfrom live sports. [A general transmit all the excitement of\nstatement: how are they differ- a live game, its close-ups and\nent, and why is the difference slow-motion replays reveal\nsignificant?] much about the players and the\nstrategy of the game.\nNote You may sometimes need more than one sentence for your thesis statement, particularly\nif it requires some buildup:\nModern English, especially written English, is full of bad habits that interfere with clear thinking. Getting\nrid of these habits is a first step to political regeneration.—Adapted from George Orwell,\n―Politics and the English Language‖\nHowever, don’t use this leeway to produce a wordy, general, or disunified statement. The two (or\nmore) sentences must build on each other, and the final sentence must present the key assertion of\nyour paper.\nEXERCISE 2.6 Evaluating thesis statements\nEvaluate the following thesis statements, considering whether each one is sufficiently significant,\nspecific, and unified. Rewrite the statements as necessary to meet these goals.\n1. Aggression usually leads to violence, injury, and even death, and we should use it constructively.\n2. The religion of Islam is widely misunderstood in the United States.\n3. Manners are a kind of social glue.\n4. One episode of a radio talk show amply illustrates both the ap-peal of such shows and their silliness.\n5. The poem is about motherhood.\nEXERCISE 2.7 Considering your past work:\nDeveloping a thesis\nHave you been aware in the past of focusing your essays on a central idea, or thesis? Have you found it\nmore efficient to try to pin down your idea early or to let it evolve during drafting? To what extent has a\nthesis helped or hindered you in shaping your draft?\nEXERCISE 2.8 Drafting and revising your own thesis statement\nContinuing from Exercise 2.5 (p. 27), write a significant, specific, and unified thesis statement for your\nessay-in-progress.\n2c Organizing ideas\nAn effective essay has a recognizable shape—an arrangement of parts that guides readers,\nhelping them see how ideas and details relate to each other and contribute to the whole. You may\nsometimes let an effective organization emerge over one or more drafts. But many writers find that\norganizing ideas to some extent before drafting can provide a helpful sense of direction, as a map\ncan help a driver negotiate a half-familiar system of roads. If you feel uncertain about the course\nyour essay should follow or have a complicated topic with many parts, devising a shape for your\nmaterial can clarify your options.\nBefore you begin organizing your material, look over all the writing you’ve done so far—\nfreewriting, notes from reading, whatever. Either on paper or on a computer, pull together a master\nlist of all the ideas and details you think you might want to include. You can add to or subtract\nfrom the list as you think about shape.\n1 Distinguishing the general and the specific\nTo organize material for an essay, you need to distinguish general and specific ideas and see\nthe relations between ideas. General and specific refer to the number of instances or objects\nincluded in a group signified by a word. The ―ladder‖ below illustrates a general-to-specific\nhierarchy.\nMost general\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 11\nlife form\nplant\nflowering plant\nrose\nAmerican Beauty rose\nUncle Dan’s prize-winning American Beauty rose\nMost specific\nHere are some tips for arranging the ideas in your preliminary writing:\nUnderline, boldface, or circle the most general ideas. These are the ideas that offer the main\nsupport for your thesis statement. They will be more general than the evidence that in turn\nsupports them.\nMake connections between each general idea and the more specific details that support it.\nOn paper, start with a fresh sheet, write each general idea down with space beneath it, and add\nspecific information in the appropriate spaces. On a computer, rearrange supporting\ninformation under more general points. Your word processor may include a Comment\nfunction that allows you to add notes about connections.\nRespect the meanings of ideas. Think through the implications of ideas as you sort them.\nOtherwise, your hierarchies could become jumbled, with rose, for instance, illogically\nsubordinated to animal, or life form somehow subordinated to rose.\nRemove information that doesn’t fit. If you worry about losing deleted information, transfer\nthe notes to a separate sheet of paper or word-processing file.\nFill holes where support seems skimpy. If you recognize a hole but don’t know what to fill it\nwith, try using a discovery technique such as freewriting or clustering, or go back to your\nresearch sources.\nExperiment with various arrangements of general ideas and supporting information.\nSeek an order that presents your material clearly and logically. On paper, you can cut the\nmaster list apart and paste or tape each general idea and its support on a separate piece of\npaper. Then try different orders for the pages. On a computer, first save the master list and\nduplicate it. To move material around, select a block of text and either copy and then paste it\nwhere you want it or (a little quicker) drag the selected text to where you want it.\n2 Choosing an organizing tool\nSome writers view outlines as chores and straitjackets, but they need not be dull or confining.\nThere are different kinds of outlines, some more flexible than others. All of them can enlarge and\nclarify your thinking, showing you patterns of general and specific, suggesting proportions, and\nhighlighting gaps or overlaps in coverage.\nMany writers use outlines not only before but also after drafting—to check the underlying\nstructure of the draft when revising it (see p. 49). No matter when it’s made, though, an outline can\nchange to reflect changes in your thinking. View any outline you make as a tentative sketch, not as\na fixed paint-by-numbers diagram.\nA scratch or informal outline\nFor many essays, especially those with a fairly straightforward structure, a simple listing of\nideas and perhaps their support may provide adequate direction for your writing.\nA scratch outline lists the key points of the paper in the order they will be covered. Here is\nSara Ling’s scratch outline for her essay on Internet communication:\nThesis statement\nBy lowering the barriers of physical appearance, the unique anonymity of Internet communication could\nbuild diversity into community.\nScratch outline\nNo fear of prejudgment\nPhysical attributes unknown—age, race, gender, etc.\nWe won’t be shut out because of appearance\nInability to prejudge others\nAssumptions based on appearance\nMeeting of minds only\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 12\nFinding shared interests and concerns\nLing put more into this outline than its simplicity might imply, not only working out an order for\nher ideas but also sketching their implications.\nAn informal outline is usually more detailed than a scratch outline, including key general\npoints and the specific evidence for them. A student’s informal outline appears below.\nThesis statement\nAfter Home Inc.’s hiring practices were exposed in the media, the company avoided a scandal with policy\nchanges and a well-publicized outreach to employees and consumers.\nInformal outline\nBackground on scandal\nPrevious hiring practices\nMedia exposure and public response (brief)\nPolicy changes\nApplication forms\nInterviewing procedures\nTraining of personnel\nOutreach to employees\nSigns and letters\nMeetings and workshops\nOutreach to consumers\nPress conference\nStore signs\nAdvertising—print and radio\nA tree diagram\nIn a tree diagram, ideas and details branch out in increasing specificity. Like any outline, the\ndiagram can warn of gaps, overlaps, and digressions. But unlike more linear outlines, it can be\nsupplemented and extended indefinitely, so it is easy to alter for new ideas and arrangements\ndiscovered during drafting and revision.\nBelow is a tree diagram by Johanna Abrams, based on her earlier brainstorming about a\nsummer job (p. 22) and the following thesis statement:\nThesis statement\nTwo months working in a large government agency taught me that an office’s pecking order should be\nrespected.\nEach main part of the four-part diagram represents a different general idea about the summer-job\nexperience. Within each part, information grows more specific as it branches downward.\nA tree diagram or other visual map can be especially useful for planning a\nproject for the World Wide Web. The diagram can help you lay out the\norganization of your project and its links and then later can serve as a site\nmap for your readers. (For more on composing for the Web, see pp. 832–\n38.)\nA formal outline\nFor complex topics requiring complex arrangements of ideas and support, you may want or be\nrequired to construct a formal outline. More rigidly arranged and more detailed than other\noutlines, a formal outline not only lays out main ideas and their support but also shows the relative\nimportance of all the essay’s elements and how they connect with one another.\nNote Because of its structure, a formal outline can be an excellent tool for checking the\narrangement of a draft before revising it (see p. 49).\nOn the basis of her scratch outline (p. 34), Sara Ling prepared this formal outline for her essay\non the Internet:\nThesis statement\nBy lowering the barriers of physical appearance, the unique anonymity of Internet communication could\nbuild diversity into community.\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 13\nFormal outline\nI. No fear of being prejudged\nA. Unknown physical attributes\n1. Gender\n2. Age\n3. Race\n4. Style\nB. Freer communication\nC. No automatic rejection\nII. Inability to prejudge others\nA. No assumptions based on appearance\n1. Body type\n2. Physical disability\n3. Race\nB. Discovery of shared interests and concerns\n1. Sports and other activities\n2. Family values\n3. Political views\nC. Reduction of physical bias\nLing’s outline illustrates several principles of outlining that can help ensure completeness,\nbalance, and clear relationships. (These principles largely depend on distinguishing between the\ngeneral and the specific. See pp. 32–33.)\nAll the outline’s parts are systematically indented and labeled. Roman numerals (I, II)\nlabel primary divisions of the essay, indented capital letters (A, B) label secondary divisions,\nand farther indented Arabic numerals (1, 2) label principal supporting points and examples. A\nfourth level would be indented farther still and labeled with small letters (a, b). Each\nsucceeding level contains more specific information than the one before it.\nThe outline divides the material into several groups. An uninterrupted listing of ideas like\nthe one following would indicate a need for tighter, more logical relationships among ideas.\n(Compare this example with part II of Ling’s actual outline.)\nII. Inability to prejudge others\nA. Body type\nB. Physical disability\nC. Race\nD. Sports and other activities\nE. Family values\nF. Political views\nG. Reduction of physical bias\nWithin each part of the outline, distinct topics of equal generality appear in parallel\nheadings, with the same indention and numbering or lettering. In the following example,\npoints B, C, and D are more specific than point A, not equally general, so they should be\nsubheadings 1, 2, and 3 under it. (See section IIA of Ling’s outline.)\nA. No assumptions based on appearance\nB. Body type\nC. Physical disability\nD. Race\nAll subdivided headings in the outline break into at least two parts because a topic cannot\nlogically be divided into only one part. The following example violates this principle:\nB. Discovery of shared views\n1. Interests and concerns\nAny single subdivision should be combined with the heading\nabove it (as in section IIB of Ling’s actual outline), matched with another\nsubdivision, or rechecked for its relevance to the heading above it.\nAll headings are expressed in parallel grammatical form (see pp. 405–09 on parallelism).\nLing’s is a topic outline, in which the headings consist of a noun (fear, attributes, gender, and\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 14\nthe like) with modifiers (no, unknown physical, no automatic, and the like). In a sentence\noutline all headings are expressed as full sen-tences, as in the following rewrite of part II of\nLing’s outline.\nII. On the Internet, we are unable to prejudge others.\nA. We cannot make common assumptions based on physical appearance.\n1. People with athletic builds must be unintelligent.\n2. People in wheelchairs must be unapproachable or pathetic.\n3. People of other races must hold views different from our own.\nB. We discover shared interests and concerns.\n1. We find common ground in sports and other activities.\n2. We see that we all feel much the same about our families.\n3. We learn the similarities in each other’s political views.\nC. The Internet could reduce physical bias in the world.\nSee pages 692–93 for a complete sentence outline.\nThe outline covers only the body of the essay, omitting the introduction and the\nconclusion. The beginning and the ending are important in the essay itself, but you need not\ninclude them in the outline unless you are required to do so or anticipate special problems\nwith their organization.\n3 Choosing a structure\nIntroduction, body, and conclusion\nMost essays share a basic shape:\nThe introduction, usually a paragraph or two, draws readers into the world of the essay.\nAt a minimum, it announces and clarifies the topic. Often, it ends with the thesis statement,\nmaking a commitment that the rest of the essay delivers on. (See pp. 102–06 for more on\nintroductions.)\nThe body of the essay develops the thesis and thus fulfills the commitment of the\nintroduction. The paragraphs in the body develop the general points that support the thesis—\nthe items that would be labeled with Roman numerals and capital letters in a formal outline\nlike the one on page 36. These general points are like the legs of a table supporting the top, the\nthesis. Each general point may take a paragraph or more, with the bulk of the content\nproviding the details, examples, and reasons (the wood of the table) to support the general\npoint and thus the thesis.\nThe conclusion gives readers something to take away from the essay—a summary of ideas,\nfor instance, or a suggested course of action. (See pp. 106–09 for more on conclusions.)\nThis basic shape applies mainly to traditional essays. A composition for the World Wide Web\nprobably will have a more flexible structure and will lack a formal conclusion. See pages 832–38\nfor more on composing for the Web.\nIf you are not used to reading and writing American academic prose, its pattern of\nintroduction-body-conclusion and the particular schemes discussed below may seem unfamiliar.\nFor instance, instead of introductions that focus quickly on the topic and thesis, you may be used to\nopenings that establish personal connections with readers or that approach the thesis indirectly.\nAnd instead of body paragraphs that first emphasize general points and then support those points\nwith specific evidence, you may be used to general statements without support (because writers can\nassume that readers will supply the evidence themselves) or to evidence without explanation\n(because writers can assume that readers will infer the general points themselves). When writing\nAmerican academic prose, you need to take into account readers’ expectations for directness and\nfor the statement and support of general points.\nOrganizing the body by space or time\nTwo organizational schemes—spatial and chronological—grow naturally out of the topic. A\nspatial organization is especially appropriate for essays that describe a place, an object, or a\nperson. Following the way people normally survey something, you move through space from a\nchosen starting point to other features of the subject. Describing a building, for instance, you might\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 15\nbegin with an impression of the whole, then scan exterior details from top to bottom, and then\ndescribe interior spaces.\nA chronological organization reports events as they occurred in time, usually from first to\nlast. This pattern, like spatial organization, corresponds to readers’ own experiences and\nexpectations. It suits an essay in which you do one of the following:\nRecount a sequence of events, such as a championship baseball game or the Battle of\nGettysburg.\nExplain a process from beginning to end—for instance, how to run a marathon or how a tree\nconverts carbon dioxide to oxygen.\nExplain the causes that led to an effect, such as the lobbying that helped to push a bill\nthrough the legislature. Alternatively, explain how a cause, such as a flood or a book, had\nmultiple\neffects.\nTell a story about yourself or someone else.\nProvide background—for instance, the making of a film you are analyzing or the procedure\nused in an experiment you are reporting.\nOrganizing the body for emphasis\nSome organizational schemes must be imposed on ideas and information to aid readers’\nunderstanding and achieve a desired emphasis.\nGeneral to specific\nTwo ways of organizing essays depend on the distinction between the general and the specific,\ndiscussed on pages 32–33. The general-to-specific scheme is common in expository and\nargumentative essays that start with a general discussion of the main points and then proceed to\nspecific examples, facts, or other evidence. The following thesis statement forecasts a general-to-\nspecific organization:\nAs an investment in its own economy, the United States should provide a tuition grant to any college\nstudent who qualifies academically.\nThe body of the essay might first elaborate on the basic argument and then provide the supporting\ndata.\nSpecific to general\nSometimes you may anticipate that readers will not appreciate or agree with your general ideas\nbefore they see the support for them—for instance, in an expository essay that presents a unique\nway of looking at common experience, or in an argumentative\nessay that takes an unpopular view. In these cases a specific-to-general scheme can arouse readers’\ninterest in specific examples or other evidence, letting the evidence build to statements of more\ngeneral ideas. The following thesis statement could be developed in this way:\nAlthough most of us are unaware of the public relations campaigns directed at us, they can significantly\naffect the way we think and live.\nThe writer might devote most of the essay to a single specific example of a public relations\ncampaign and then explain more generally how the example typifies public relations campaigns.\nProblem-solution\nMany arguments use a problem-solution scheme: first outline a problem that needs solving;\nthen propose a solution. (If the so-\nlution involves steps toward a goal, it may be arranged chrono-\nlogically.) The following thesis statement announces a problem-\nsolution paper:\nTo improve workflow and quality, the data-processing department should add one part-time staffer and\nretrain three others in the new systems.\nClimax\nA common scheme in both explanations and arguments is the climactic organization, in\nwhich ideas unfold in order of increasing drama or importance to a climax. For example, the\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 16\nfollowing thesis statement lists three effects of strip-mining in order of their increasing severity,\nand the essay would cover them in the same order:\nStrip-mining should be tightly controlled in this region to reduce its pollution of water resources, its\ndestruction of the land, and its devastating effects on people’s lives.\nAs this example suggests, the climactic organization works well in arguments because it leaves\nreaders with the most important point freshest in their minds. In exposition such an arrangement\ncan create suspense and thus hold readers’ attention.\nFamiliarity or complexity\nExpository essays can also be arranged to take account of reader’s knowledge of the subject.\nAn essay on the effects of air pollution might proceed from most familiar to least familiar—from\neffects readers are likely to know to ones they may not know. Similarly, an explanation of animals’\nnervous systems might proceed from simplest to most complex, so that the explanation of each\nnervous system provides a basis for readers to understand the more difficult one following.\n4 Checking for unity and coherence\nIn conceiving your organization and writing your essay, you should be aware of two qualities\nof effective writing that relate to organization: unity and coherence. When you perceive that\nsomeone’s writing ―flows well,‖ you are probably appreciating these two qualities. An essay has\nunity if all its parts relate to and support the thesis statement. Check for unity with these questions:\nIs each main section relevant to the main idea (thesis) of the essay?\nWithin main sections, does each example or detail support the principal idea of that\nsection?\nAn essay has coherence if readers can see the relations among parts and move easily from one\nthought to the next. Check for coherence with these questions:\nDo the ideas follow in a clear sequence?\nAre the parts of the essay logically connected?\nAre the connections clear and smooth?\nA unified and coherent outline will not necessarily guide you to a unified and coherent essay,\nbecause so much can change during drafting. Thus you shouldn’t be too hard on your outline, in\ncase a seemingly wayward idea proves useful. But do cut obvious digressions and rearrange\nmaterial that clearly needs moving.\nSample essay\nThe following essay illustrates some ways of achieving unity and coherence (highlighted in\nthe annotations).\nA Picture of Hyperactivity\nA hyperactive committee member can contribute to efficiency.\nA hyperactive salesperson can contribute to profits. When children are hyperactive, though, people—even\nparents—may wish they had never been born. A collage of those who must cope with hyperactivity in\nchildren is a picture of frustration, anger, and loss.\nThe first part of the collage is the doctors. In their terminology, the word hyperactivity has been replaced\nby ADHD, attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder. They apply the term to children who are abnormally or\nexcessively busy. But doctors do not fully understand the problem and thus differ over how to treat it. Some\nrecommend a special diet, others recommend behavior-modifying drugs, and still others, who do not consider\nADHD a medical problem, recommend psychotherapy. The result\nis a merry-go-round of tests, confusion, and frustration for the children and their parents.\nAs the mother of an ADHD child, I can say what the disorder means to the parents who form the second\npart of the collage. It means worry that is deep and enduring. It means despair that is a constant companion. It\nmeans a mixture of frustration, guilt, and anger. And finally, since there are times when parents’ anger goes out\nof control and threatens the children, it means self-loathing.\nThe weight of ADHD, however, does not rest on the doctors and parents. The darkest part of the collage\nbelongs to the children. From early childhood they may be dragged from doctor to doctor, attached to\nmachines, medicated until they feel numb, and tested or discussed by physicians, teachers, neighbors, and\nstrangers on the street. They may be highly intelligent, but they’ll still do poorly in school because of their short\nattention spans. Their playmates dislike them because of their temper and their unwillingness to follow rules.\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 17\nEven their pets mistrust them because of their erratic behavior. As time goes on, the children see their parents\nmore and more in tears and anger, and they know they are the cause.\nThe collage is complete, and it is dark and somber. ADHD, as applied to children, is a term with\nuncertain, unattractive, and bitter associations. The picture does have one bright spot, however, for inside every\nADHD child is a lovely, trusting, calm person waiting to be recognized.\n----Linda Devereaux\n(student)\nUnity and coherence within paragraphs\nThe unity and coherence of writing begin in its paragraphs, so the two concepts are treated in\ngreater detail in Chapter 4. You may want to consult several sections in particular before you begin\ndrafting:\nThe topic sentence and unity (pp. 72–75)\nTransitions and coherence (pp. 77–88, 108)\nLinking paragraphs in the essay (pp. 109–10)\nUnity and coherence on the Web\nUnity and coherence may seem unimportant in compositions for the World Wide Web, in\nwhich entire documents are linked to each other so that it’s easy to move among them. However,\nprecisely because the Web is such a fluid medium, you risk losing or confusing your readers if you\ndon’t consider unity and coherence. Your project should have a clear purpose and clear ideas\nrelating to that purpose, and the connections between ideas should be spelled out to orient readers.\n(For more on composing for the Web, see pp. 832–38.)\nEXERCISE 2.9 ORGANIZING IDEAS\nThe following list of ideas was extracted by a student from freewriting he did for a brief paper on soccer\nin the United States. Using his thesis statement as a guide, pick out the general ideas and arrange the\nrelevant specific points under them. In some cases you may have to infer general ideas to cover specific\npoints in the list. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nThesis statement\nDespite increasing interest within the United States, soccer may never be the sport here that it is\nelsewhere because both the potential fans and the potential backers resist it.\nList of ideas\nSports seasons are already too crowded for fans.\nSoccer rules are confusing to Americans.\nA lot of kids play soccer in school, but the game is still ―foreign.‖\nSports money goes where the money is.\nBackers are wary of losing money on new ventures.\nFans have limited time to watch.\nFans have limited money to pay for sports.\nBackers are concerned with TV contracts.\nPrevious attempts to start a pro soccer league failed.\nTV contracts almost matter more than live audiences.\nFailure of the US Football League was costly.\nBaseball, football, hockey, and basketball seasons already overlap.\nSoccer fans couldn’t fill huge stadiums.\nAmerican soccer fans are too few for TV interest.\nEXERCISE 2.10 Creating a formal outline\nUse your arrangement of general ideas and specific points from Exercise 2.9 as the basis for a formal\ntopic or sentence outline. Follow the principles given on pages 36–38. (If you completed Exercise 2.9\nonline, you can use that file to create this outline.)\nEXERCISE 2.11 Considering your past work:\nOrganizing ideas\nWhat has been your experience with organizing your writing? Many writers find it difficult. If you do,\ntoo, can you say why? What kinds of outlines or other organizing tools have you used? Which have been\nhelpful and which have not?\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 18\nEXERCISE 2.12 Organizing your own essay\nContinuing from Exercise 2.8 (p. 32), choose an appropriate organization for your essay-in-progress.\nThen experiment with organizing tools by preparing a tree diagram or other visual map or a scratch,\ninformal, or formal outline.\n3\nCHAPTER\nDrafting and Revising\nThe separation of drafting and revising from the planning and development discussed in\nChapters 1 and 2 is somewhat artificial because the stages almost always overlap during the writing\nproc-ess. Indeed, if you compose on a computer, you may not experience any boundaries between\nstages at all. Still, your primary goal\nduring the writing process will usually shift from gathering and shaping information to forming\nconnected sentences and paragraphs in a draft and then restructuring and rewriting the draft.\n3a Writing the first draft\nThe only correct drafting style is the one that works for you. Generally, though, the freer and\nmore fluid you are, the better. Some writers draft and revise at the same time, but most let\nthemselves go during drafting and especially do not worry about errors. Drafting is the occasion to\nfind and convey meaning through the act of writing. If you fear making mistakes while drafting,\nthat fear will choke your ideas. You draft only for yourself, so errors do not matter. Write freely\nuntil you have worked out what you want to say; then focus on any mistakes you may have made.\nStarting to draft sometimes takes courage, even for seasoned professionals. Students and pros\nalike find elaborate ways to procrastinate—rearranging shelves, napping, lunching with friends.\nSuch procrastination may actually help you if you let ideas for writing simmer at the same time. At\nsome point, though, enough is enough: the deadline looms; you’ve got to get started. If the\nblankness still stares back at you, then try one of the following techniques for unblocking.\nYou should find some momentum once you’ve started writing. If not, however, or if your\nenergy flags, try one or more of the following techniques to keep moving ahead.\nIf you write on a computer, frequently save the text you’re drafting—at least every five or ten\nminutes and every time you leave the computer. See pages 52–53 for tips on saving documents.\nWhether you compose on paper or on a computer, you may find it difficult to tell whether a\nfirst draft is finished. The distinction between drafts can be significant because creating text is\ndifferent from rethinking it and because your instructor may ask you and your classmates to submit\nyour drafts, either on paper or over a computer network, so that others can give you feedback on\nthem. For your own revision or others’ feedback, you might consider a draft finished for any\nnumber of reasons: perhaps you’ve reached the assigned length and have run out of ideas; perhaps\nyou find yourself writing the conclusion; perhaps you’ve stopped adding content and are just\ntinkering with words.\nSample first draft\nSara Ling’s first draft on Internet communication appears below. As you read the draft, mark\nthe thesis statement and each key idea developing the thesis. Note places where you think the ideas\ncould be clearer or better supported.\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 19\nTitle?\nIn ―Welcome to Cyberbia,‖ written in 1995, M. Kadi predicts that the Internet will lead to more\nfragmentation in society because people just seek out others like themselves. But Kadi fails to foresee how the\nunique anonymity of Internet communication could actually build diversity into community by lowering the\nbarriers of physical appearance.\nAnonymity on the Internet. It’s one of the best things about technology. Most people who communicate\nonline use an invented screen name to avoid revealing personal details such as age, gender, and ethnic\nbackground. No one knows whether you’re fat or thin or neat or sloppy. What kind of clothes you wear.\n(Maybe you’re not wearing clothes at all). People who know you personally don’t even know who you are with\nan invented screen name.\nWe can make ourselves known without first being prejudged because of our physical attributes. For\nexample, I participate in a snowboarding forum that has mostly men. I didn’t realize what I was getting into\nwhen I used my full name as my screen name. Before long, I had received unfriendly responses such as ―What\ndoes a girl know?‖ and ―Why don’t you go back to knitting?‖ I guess I had run into a male prejudice against\nfemale snowboarders. However, another woman on the forum had no such problems. At first she signed on\nwith a screen name that did not reveal her gender, and no one responded negatively to her messages. When she\nhad contributed for a while, she earned respect from the other snowboarders. When she revealed that she was a\nwoman at that point, no one responded negatively in the way I had experienced. She posed at first as someone\ndifferent from who she really was and could make herself heard.\nWe also cannot prejudge others because of their appearance. Often in face-to-face interaction we assume\nwe know things about people just because of the way they look. Assumptions prevent people from discovering\ntheir shared interests and concerns, and this is particularly true where race is concerned. The anonymity of the\nInternet makes physical barriers irrelevant, and only people’s minds meet. Because of this, the Internet could\ncreate a world free of physical bias.\nLogged on to the Internet we can become more tolerant of others. We can become a community.\nEXERCISE 3.1 Analyzing a first draft\nCompare Ling’s draft with the previous step in her planning (her formal outline) on page 36. List the\nplaces in the draft where the act of drafting led Ling to rearrange her information, add or delete material,\nor explore new ideas. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/ littlebrown.)\nEXERCISE 3.2 CONSIDERING YOUR PAST WORK:\nDRAFTING\nThink back over a recent writing experience. At what point in the writing process did you begin drafting?\nHow did drafting go—smoothly, haltingly, painfully, painlessly? If you had difficulties, what were they?\nIf you didn’t, why do you think not?\nEXERCISE 3.3 Drafting your essay\nPrepare a draft of the essay you began in Chapters 1 and 2. Use your thesis statement and your outline as\nguides, but don’t be unduly constrained by them. Concentrate on opening up options, not on closing them\ndown. Do not, above all, worry about mistakes.\n3b Revising the first draft\nRevision literally means ―re-seeing‖—looking anew at ideas and details, their relationships\nand arrangement, the degree to which they work or don’t work for the thesis. While drafting, you\nfocus inwardly, concentrating on pulling your topic out of yourself. In revising, you look out to\nyour readers, trying to anticipate how they will see your work. You adopt a critical perspective\ntoward your work (see Chapter 8), examining your draft as a pole-vaulter or dancer would examine\na videotape of his or her performance. (Writing teachers often ask students to read each other’s\ndrafts partly to train the students in using and benefiting from this critical perspective. See p. 66 for\nmore on such collaboration.)\n1 Gaining distance from your work\nReading your own work critically requires that you create some distance between it and\nyourself—not always an easy task. The following techniques may help.\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 20\n2 Revising, then editing\nStrictly speaking, revision includes editing—refining the manner of expression to improve\nclarity or style or to correct errors. In this chapter, though, revision and editing are treated\nseparately to stress their differences: in revision you deal with the underlying meaning and structure\nof your essay; in editing you deal with its surface. By making separate drafts beyond the first—a\nrevised one and then an edited one—you’ll be less likely to waste time tinkering with sentences\nthat you end up cutting, and you’ll avoid the temptation to substitute editing for more substantial\nrevision.\nThe temptation to edit while revising can be especially attractive on a word processor because\nit’s easy to alter copy. Indeed, writers sometimes find themselves editing compulsively, spinning\ntheir wheels with changes that cease to have any marked effect on meaning or clarity and that may\nin fact sap the writing of energy. Planning to revise and then to edit encourages you to look beyond\nthe confines of the screen so that deeper issues of meaning and structure aren’t lost to surface\nmatters such as word choice and sentence arrangement.\n3 Titling your essay\nThe revision stage is a good time to consider a title. After drafting, you have a clearer sense of\nyour direction, and the attempt to sum up your essay in a title phrase can help you focus sharply on\nyour topic, purpose, and audience.\nHere are suggestions for titling an essay:\nA descriptive title is almost always appropriate and is often expected for academic\nwriting. It announces the topic clearly, accurately, and as briefly as possible. The final title of\nSara Ling’s essay—―The Internet: Fragmentation or Community?‖—is an example, as are\n―Images of Lost Identity in North by Northwest,‖ ―An Experiment in Small-Group Dynamics,‖\n―Why Lincoln Delayed Emancipating the Slaves,‖ and ―Food Poisoning Involving E. coli\nBacteria: A Review of the Literature.‖\nA suggestive title—the kind often found in popular magazines—may be appropriate for\nmore informal writing. Examples include ―Making Peace‖ (for an essay on the Peace Corps)\nand ―Anyone for Soup?‖ (for an essay on working in a soup kitchen). For a more suggestive\ntitle, Ling might have chosen ―What We Don’t Know Can Help Us‖ or ―Secrets of the\nInternet.‖ Such a title conveys the writer’s attitude and main concerns but not the precise\ntopic, thereby pulling readers into the essay to learn more. A source for such a title may be a\nfamiliar phrase, a fresh image, or a significant expression from the essay itself.\nA title tells readers how big the topic is. For Ling’s essay, the title ―The Internet‖ or\n―Anonymity‖ would have been too broad, whereas ―Lose Your Body‖ or ―Discovering\nCommon Ground‖ would have been too narrow because each deals with only part of the\npaper’s content.\nA title should not restate the assignment or the thesis statement, as in ―The Trouble with\nM. Kadi’s Picture of the Internet‖ or ―What I Think About Diversity on the Internet.‖\nFor more information on essay titles, see page 354 (avoiding reference to the title in the\nopening of the paper), 491 (capitalizing words in a title), and 687–88 (the format of a title in the\nfinal paper).\n4 Using a revision checklist\nSet aside at least as much time to revise your essay as you took to draft it. Plan on going\nthrough the draft several times to answer the questions in the checklist opposite and to resolve any\nproblems. (If you need additional information on any of the topics in the checklist, refer to the page\nnumbers given in parentheses.) Note that the checklist can also help you if you have been asked to\ncomment on another writer’s draft (see p. 67).\n5 Revising on a word processor\nWord processors have removed the mechanical drudgery of revising by hand or on a\ntypewriter, but they have also complicated the process: you must conscientiously save your changes\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 21\nand manage the files you create, both discussed below. At the same time, like the more\ncumbersome revision methods, word processors allow you to display changes as you make them.\nSaving changes\nComputers malfunction frequently. You can avoid losing your work by taking two\nprecautions:\nSave your work every five to ten minutes. Most word processors have an Auto Save\nfunction that will save your work automatically as you type, at the interval you specify. Still,\nget in the habit of saving manually whenever you make major changes.\nAfter doing any major work on a project, create a backup version of the file. Use a second\nhard drive, a removable disk, or a removable ―flash‖ drive. If you need to share files but have\ndifficulty e-mailing them, or if you need to save large multimedia files, you can also store\nbackups on a drive accessed through the Internet. XDrive, for example, offers storage space\nfor a reasonable price (xdrive.com).\nThe following screen shot illustrates the essentials of a word processor’s File menu, the key to\nsaving and organizing drafts.\nManaging files\nWith the Save As option in the File menu, you can organize your work for a course or the\ndrafts of a paper by giving each file a name that indicates the content, the date, and (if you’re\nsharing work with others) the author. The following screen shot shows Sara Ling saving a\ndocument she named Sara.NetcomD1.10-28-05.doc in a new folder titled English 120. In addition\nto her name and the date, the file name includes an identifier for the paper (Netcom) and the draft\nnumber (D1).\nDisplaying changes\nYou can keep track of the revisions you make in a document with an\noption often called Track Changes and usually found under the Tools or\nFile menu. The function highlights additions and deletions so they’re easy\nto spot (see the screen shot on the next page). Tracking changes may\nencourage you to revise more freely because you can always revert to your\noriginal text. You can weigh the original and revised versions as you view\nthem side by side. You can also evaluate the kinds of changes you are\nmaking. For instance, if during revision you see only minor surface\nalterations (word substitutions, added punctuation, and the like), then you\nmight consider whether and where to read more deeply for more\nfundamental changes.\n3c Examining a sample revision\nIn revising her first draft, Sara Ling had the help of her instructor and several of her\nclassmates, to whom she showed the draft as part of her assignment. Based on the revision\nchecklist, she thought that she wanted to stick with her initial purpose and thesis statement and that\nthey had held up well in the draft. But she also knew without being told that her introduction and\nconclusion were too hurried, that the movement between paragraphs was too abrupt, that the\nexample of the snowboarding forum went on too long, and that the fourth paragraph was thin: she\nhadn’t supplied enough details to support her ideas and convince her readers.\nLing’s readers confirmed her self-evaluation. Several, however, raised points that she had not\nconsidered, reflected in these comments by classmates:\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 22\nComment 1\nWhy do you say (par. 2) that most people use invented screen names? I don’t, and I know other people\nwho don’t either. Do you have evidence of how many people use invented names or why they do?\nComment 2\nI would have an easier time agreeing with you about the Internet if you weren’t quite so gung-ho. For\ninstance, what about the dangers of the Internet, as when adults prey on children or men prey on women?\nIn par. 3, you don’t acknowledge that such things can and do happen. People will still meet in person, after all.\nAt first Ling was tempted to resist these comments because the writers seemed to object to her\nideas. A carefully developed essay will fall flat with readers if you overlook\nawkwardness and errors.\n1 Discovering what needs editing\nTry these approaches to spot possible flaws in your work:\nIn your editing, work first for clarity and a smooth movement among sentences and then for\ncorrectness. Use the questions in the following checklist to guide your editing, referring to the page\nnumbers in parentheses as needed.\nThe second paragraph of Sara Ling’s edited draft appears below. One change Ling made\nthroughout the essay shows up here: she resolved an inconsistency in references to you, people, and\nwe, settling on a consistent we. In addition, Ling corrected several sentence fragments in the middle\nof the paragraph.\nInternet communication can be anonymous on at least two levels. The people weyou communicate with\ndo not know ouryour age., Wwhether we’reyou’re fat or thin or neat or sloppy., Wwhat kind of clothes weyou\nwear. (Maybe you’re not if we’re wearing clothes at all)., Oor anything else about physical appearance. If\nweyou use an invented screen names instead of ouryour real names, readers don’t even know whatever ouryour\nnames may reveal or suggest says about usyou, such as gender or ethnic background.\n2 Editing on a word processor\nWhen you work on a word processor, consider these additional approaches to editing:\nDon’t rely on your word processor’s spelling or grammar and style checker to find what\nneeds editing. See the discussion of these checkers below.\nIf possible, work on a double-spaced paper copy. Most people find it much harder to spot errors\non a computer screen than on paper.\nUse the Find command to locate and correct your common problems—certain\nmisspellings, overuse of there is, wordy phrases such as the fact that, and so on.\nResist overediting. The ease of editing on a computer can lead to rewriting sentences over\nand over, stealing the life from your prose. If your grammar and style checker contributes to\nthe temptation, consider turning it off.\nTake special care with additions and omissions. Make sure you haven’t omitted needed\nwords or left in unneeded words.\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 25\n3 Working with spelling and grammar/style checkers\nThe spelling checker and grammar and style checker that may come with your word processor\ncan be helpful if you work within their limitations. The programs miss many problems and may\neven flag items that are actually correct. Further, they know nothing of your purpose and your\naudience, so they cannot make important decisions about your writing. Always use these tools\ncritically:\nRead your work yourself to ensure that it’s clear and error-free.\nConsider a checker’s suggestions carefully, weighing each one against your intentions. If\nyou aren’t sure whether to accept a checker’s suggestion, consult a dictionary, writing\nhandbook, or other source. Your version may be fine.\nUsing a spelling checker\nYour word processor’s spelling checker can be a great ally: it will flag words that are spelled\nincorrectly and usually suggest alternative spellings that resemble what you’ve typed. However,\nthis ally also has the potential to undermine you because of its limitations:\nThe checker may flag a word that you’ve spelled correctly, just because the word does not\nappear in its dictionary.\nThe checker may suggest incorrect alternatives. In providing a list of alternative spellings\nfor your word, the checker may highlight the one it considers most likely to be correct. You\nneed to verify that this alternative is actually what you intend before selecting it. Consult an\nonline or printed dictionary when you aren’t sure of the checker’s recommendations.\nMost important, a spelling checker will not flag words that appear in its dictionary but\nyou have misused. The jingle in the following screen shot has circulated widely as a warning\nabout spelling checkers (we found it in the Bulletin of the Mis-souri Council of Teachers of\nMathematics).\nUsing a grammar and style checker\nWord processors’ grammar and style checkers can flag incorrect grammar or punctuation and\nwordy or awkward sentences. However, these programs can call your attention only to passages\nthat may be faulty. They miss many errors because they are not yet capable of analyzing language\nin all its complexity (for instance, they can’t accurately distinguish a word’s part of speech when\nthere are different possibilities, as light can be a noun, a verb, or an adjective). And they often\nquestion passages that don’t need editing, such as an appropriate passive verb or a deliberate and\nemphatic use of repetition. The screen shot on the next page illustrates the limitations.\nYou can customize a grammar and style checker to suit your needs and habits as a writer.\n(Select Options under the Tools menu.) Most checkers allow you to specify whether to check\ngrammar only or grammar and style. Some style checkers can be set to the level\nof writing you intend, such as formal, standard, and informal. (For academic writing choose\nformal.) You can also instruct the checker to flag specific grammar and style problems that tend to\noccur in your writing, such as mismatched subjects and verbs, apostrophes in plural nouns,\noverused passive voice, or a confusion between its and it’s.\nEXERCISE 3.7 Considering your past work: Editing\nHow do you find what needs editing in your drafts?
