If your readers often comment on editing concerns in your work, what can you do to reduce such\ncomments?\nEXERCISE 3.8 Editing your own draft\nUse the checklist for editing and your own sense of your essay’s needs to edit the revised draft of your\nessay-in-progress.\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 26\n3e Preparing and proofreading the final draft\nAfter editing your essay, retype or print it once more for submission to your instructor. You\nmay be required to use one of the following formats: MLA (pp. 687–89), Chicago (pp. 775–77),\nAPA (pp. 800–03), or CSE (p. 820). If no format is specified, consult the document-design\nguidelines in Chapter 5. If you’ve composed on a word processor, use the Print Preview function\nunder the File menu to check for formatting problems that may not otherwise show up on your\nscreen.\nBe sure to proofread the final essay several times to spot and correct errors. To increase the\naccuracy of your proofreading, you may need to experiment with ways to keep yourself from\nrelaxing into the rhythm and the content of your prose. The box below gives a few tricks, including\nsome used by professional proofreaders.\n3f Examining a final draft\nSara Ling’s final essay begins below, typed in MLA format except for page breaks. Comments\nin the margins point out key features of the essay’s content.\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 27\nSara Ling\nProfessor Nelson\nEnglish 120A\n4 November 2005\nThe Internet:\nFragmentation or Community?\nWe hear all sorts of predictions about how the Internet will enrich our individual lives and promote\ncommunication, tolerance, and thus community in our society. But are these promises realistic? In her 1995\nessay ―Welcome to Cyberbia,‖ M. Kadi argues that they are not. Instead, she predicts that the Internet will lead\nto more fragmentation, not community, because users merely seek out others with the same biases, concerns,\nand needs as their own. The point is an interesting one, but Kadi fails to foresee that the unique anonymity of\nInternet communication could actually build diversity into community by lowering the barriers of physical\nappearance.\nInternet communication can be anonymous on at least two levels. The people we communicate with do\nnot know our age, whether we’re fat or thin or neat or sloppy, what kind of clothes we wear (if we’re wearing\nclothes at all), or anything else about physical appearance. If we use invented screen names instead of our real\nnames, readers don’t even know whatever our names may reveal or suggest about us, such as gender or ethnic\nbackground.\nInternet anonymity seems a popular option, judging by the numbers of invented user names seen in\nonline forums. To determine the extent of invented user names as well as the reasons for them, I surveyed\nseventy-eight students. I asked two questions: (1) Do you ever write with an invented user name when\ncontributing to chat rooms, newsgroups, Web logs, and so on? (2) If yes, why do you use an invented name: to\nprotect your privacy, to avoid revealing personal information, or for some other reason? The results are shown\nin fig. 1. A large majority of the students (seventy-eight percent) do use invented names online. And most of\nthem do so to protect their privacy (thirty-seven percent) or to avoid revealing personal details (thirty percent).\nUsers of the Internet clearly value the anonymity it can give them. This anonymity allows users to communicate\nfreely without being prejudged because of physical attributes. In follow-up interviews, twenty students said that\nthey use invented names to mask personal details because they think the details might work against them in\nonline communication. One said she is able to participate in a physics discussion list without fear of being\nignored by the group’s professional physicists. Another said he thinks he can contribute more freely to a\npolitical forum because no one knows he’s African American. I learned the benefits of anonymity myself when\nI joined a snowboarding forum using my full name and received hostile responses such as ―What does a girl\nknow?‖ and ―Why don’t you go back to knitting?‖ I assumed I had run into a male prejudice against female\nsnow-boarders. However, another woman on the forum had no such problems when she contributed for a while\nbefore revealing her gender.\nGranted, concealing or altering identities on the Internet can be a problem, as when adults pose as\nchildren to seduce or harm them. These well-publicized occurrences say much about the need to monitor\nchildren’s use of the Internet and to be cautious about meeting Internet corre-spondents. However, they do not\nundermine the value of being able to make ourselves heard in situations where normally (in the real world) we\nwould\nbe shut out.\nThe Internet’s anonymity has a flip side, too: just as we cannot be prejudged, so we cannot prejudge\nothers because of their appearance. Often in face-to-face interaction, we assume we know things about people\njust because of the way they look. Someone with an athletic build must be unintelligent. Someone who is heavy\nmust be uninteresting. Perhaps most significant, someone of another race must have fixed and contrary views\nabout all kinds of issues, from family values to crime to affirmative action. Assumptions like these prevent us\nfrom discovering the interests and concerns we share with people who merely look different. But with the\nanonymity of the Internet, such physical barriers to understanding are irrelevant.\nA world without physical bias may be an unreachable ideal. However, the more we communicate with\njust our minds, the more likely it is that our minds will find common ground and put less emphasis on physical\ncharacteristics. Logged on, we can begin to become more accepted and more accepting, more tolerated and\nmore tolerant. We can begin to become a community.\nWork Cited\nKadi, M. ―Welcome to Cyberbia.‖ Utne Reader Mar.-Apr. 1995: 57-59.\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 28\nEXERCISE 3.9 Proofreading\nProofread the following passage, using any of the techniques listed on page 63 to bring errors into the\nforeground. There are thirteen errors in the passage: missing and misspelled words, typographical errors,\nand the like. If you are in doubt about any spellings, consult a diction-ary. (You can do this exercise\nonline at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nAn envirnmental group, Natural Resources Defense Council, has estimated that 5500 to 6200\nchildren who are preschool today may contract cancer durng there lives becuase of the pesticides they\nconsume in there food In addition, these children will be at greater risk for kidney damage, problems with\nimmunity, and other serious imparments. The government bases it’s pesticide-safety standards on adults,\nbut childen consume many more the fruits and fruit products likely too contain pestcides.\nEXERCISE 3.10 Preparing your final draft\nPrepare the final draft of the essay you have been working on throughout Chapters 1–3. Proofread\ncarefully and correct all errors before submitting your essay for review.\n3g Giving and receiving comments\n1 Working collaboratively\nAlmost all the writing you do in college will generate responses from an instructor. In courses\nthat stress writing, you may submit early drafts as well as your final paper, and your readers may\ninclude your classmates as well as your instructor. Like Sara Ling’s, such courses may feature\ncollaborative learning, in which students work together on writing, from completing exercises to\ncommenting on each other’s work to producing whole papers. (At more and more schools this\ngroup work occurs over a computer network. See pp. 829–32.)\nWhether you participate as a writer or as a writing ―coach,‖ collaboration can give you\nexperience in reading written work and in reaching readers through writing. You may at first be\nanxious about criticizing others’ work or sharing your own rough drafts, but you’ll soon grow to\nappreciate the interaction and the confidence it gives you in your own reading and writing.\nIn some cultures writers do not expect criticism from readers, or readers do not expect to\nthink critically about what they read. If critical responses are uncommon in your native culture,\ncollaboration may at first be uncomfortable for you. As a writer, consider that readers are\nresponding to your draft or even your final paper more as an exploration of ideas than as the last\nword on your subject; then you may be more receptive to readers’ suggestions. As a reader, allow\nyourself to approach a text skeptically, and know that your tactful questions and suggestions will\nusually be considered appropriate.\n2 Responding to the writing of others\nIf you are the reader of someone else’s writing, keep the following principles in mind:\nIf you are reviewing others’ drafts on a word processor, its Comment function will allow you\nto add comments without inserting words into the document’s text. Usually found on the Insert\nmenu, the function creates something like a sticky Post-it note that pops up when readers move\ntheir cursors across words you have highlighted. The following screen shot shows one such\ncomment.\n3 Responding to comments on your own writing\nWhen you receive comments from others, whether your classmates or your instructor, you will\nget more out of the process if you follow the guidelines below.\nAs the last item in the preceding box indicates, you’ll gain the most from collaboration if you\ncarry your learning from one assignment into the next. To keep track of things to work on, try a\nchart like the one below, with a vertical column for each assignment (or draft) and a horizontal row\nfor each weakness. Check marks indicate how often the problem occurs in each essay. The chart\nalso provides a convenient place to keep track of words you misspell so that you can master their\nspellings.\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 29\n3h Preparing a writing portfolio\nYour writing teacher may ask you to assemble samples of your writing into a portfolio, or\nfolder, once or more during the course. Such a portfolio gives you a chance to consider all your\nwriting over a period and to showcase your best work.\nTeachers’ requirements for portfolios vary. For instance, some teachers ask students to choose\ntheir five or so best papers and to submit final drafts only. Others ask for final papers illustrating\ncertain kinds of writing—say, one narrative, one critique, one argument, one research paper, and so\non. Still others ask for notes and drafts along with selected papers. If your class is using online\nwriting tools, your work may be archived as part of the course site and you may be asked to submit\nyour portfolio electronically.\nJust as teachers’ requirements differ, so do their purposes. But most are looking for a range of\nwriting that demonstrates your progress and strengths as a writer. You, in turn, see how you have\nadvanced from one assignment to the next, as you’ve had time for new knowledge to sink in and\ntime for practice. Teachers often allow students to revise papers before placing them in the\nportfolio, even if the papers were submitted earlier. In that case, every paper in the portfolio can\nbenefit from all your learning.\nAn assignment to assemble a writing portfolio will probably also provide guidelines for what\nto include, how the portfolio will be evaluated, and how (or whether) it will be weighted for a\ngrade. Be sure you understand the purpose of the portfolio and who will read it. For instance, if\nyour composition teacher will be the only reader and her guidelines urge you to show evidence of\nprogress, you might include a paper that took big risks but never entirely succeeded. In contrast, if\na committee of teachers will read your work and the guidelines urge you to demonstrate your\ncompetence as a writer, you might include only papers that did succeed.\nUnless the guidelines specify otherwise, provide error-free copies of your final drafts, label all\nyour samples with your name, and assemble them all in a folder. Add a cover letter or memo that\nlists the samples, explains why you’ve included each one, and evaluates your progress as a writer.\nThe self-evaluation involved should be a learning experience for you and will help your teacher\nassess your development as a writer.\n4\nCHAPTER\nWriting and Revising Paragraphs\nA paragraph is a group of related sentences set off by a beginning indention or, sometimes,\nby extra space. For you and your readers, paragraphs provide breathers from long stretches of text\nand indicate key changes in the development of your thesis. They help to organize and clarify ideas.\nIn the body of an essay, you may use paragraphs for any of these purposes:\nTo introduce and give evidence for a main point supporting your essay’s central idea (its\nthesis). See pages 27–31 for a discussion of an essay’s thesis.\nWithin a group of paragraphs centering on one main point, to\ndevelop a key example or other important evidence.\nTo shift approach—for instance, from pros to cons, from problem to solution, from questions\nto answers.\nTo mark movement in a sequence, such as from one reason or step to another.\nIn addition, you will use paragraphs for special purposes:\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 30\nTo introduce or to conclude an essay. See pages 102 and 106.\nTo emphasize an important point or to mark a significant tran-sition between points. See\npage 108.\nIn dialog, to indicate that a new person has begun speaking. See pages 108–09.\nThe following paragraph illustrates simply how an effective body\nparagraph works to help both writer and reader. The thesis of the essay in\nwhich this paragraph appears is that a Texas chili championship gives\nundue attention to an unpleasant food.\nSome people really like chili, apparently, but nobody can agree how the\nstuff should be made. C. V. Wood, twice winner at Terlingua, uses flank steak,\npork chops, chicken, and green chilis. My friend Hughes Rudd of CBS News,\nwho imported five hundred pounds of chili powder into Russia as a condition\nof accepting\nemployment as Moscow correspondent, favors coarse-ground beef. Isadore\nBleckman, the cameraman I must live with on the road, insists upon one-inch\ncubes of stew beef and puts garlic in his chili, an Illinois affectation. An Indian\nof my acquaintance, Mr. Fulton Batisse, who eats chili for breakfast when he\ncan, uses buffalo meat and plays an Indian drum while it’s cooking. I ask you.\n—Charles Kuralt, Dateline America\nWhile you are drafting, conscious attention to the requirements of the paragraph may\nsometimes help pull ideas out of you or help you forge relationships. But don’t expect effective\nparagraphs like Kuralt’s to flow from your fingertips while you are grappling with what you want\nto say. Instead, use the checklist on the next page to guide your revision of paragraphs so that they\nwork to your and your readers’ advantage.\nNote On the Web the paragraphing conventions described here do not always apply. Web\nreaders sometimes skim text instead of reading word for word, and they are accustomed to\nembedded links that may take them from the paragraph to another page. Writing for the Web, you\nmay want to write shorter paragraphs than you would in printed documents, and save embedded\nlinks for the ends of paragraphs lest readers miss important information. (For more on composing\nfor the Web, see pp. 832–38.)\nNot all cultures share the paragraphing conventions of American academic writing. The\nconventions are not universal even among users of standard American English: for instance, US\nnewspaper writers compose very short paragraphs that will break up text in narrow columns. In\nsome other languages, writing moves differently from English—not from left to right, but from\nright to left or down rows from top to bottom. Even in languages that move as English does, writers\nmay not use paragraphs at all. Or they may use paragraphs but not state the central ideas or provide\ntransitional expressions to show readers how sentences relate. If your native language is not\nEnglish and you have difficulty with paragraphs, don’t worry about paragraphing during drafting.\nInstead, during a separate step of revision, divide your text into parts that develop your main\npoints. Mark those parts with indentions.\n4a Maintaining paragraph unity\nReaders generally expect a paragraph to explore a single idea. They will be alert for that idea\nand will patiently follow its development. In other words, they will seek and appreciate paragraph\nunity: clear identification and clear elaboration of one idea and of that idea only.\nIn an essay the thesis statement often asserts the main idea as a commitment to readers (see p.\n27). In a paragraph a topic sentence often alerts readers to the essence of the paragraph by\nasserting the central idea and expressing the writer’s attitude toward it. In a brief essay each body\nparagraph will likely treat one main point supporting the essay’s thesis statement; the topic\nsentences simply elaborate on parts of the thesis. In longer essays paragraphs tend to work in\ngroups, each group treating one main point. Then the topic sentences will tie into that main point,\nand all the points together will support the thesis.\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 31\n1 Focusing on the central idea\nLike the thesis sentence, the topic sentence is a commitment to readers, and the rest of the\nparagraph delivers on that commitment. Look again at Kuralt’s paragraph on chili on page 71: the\nopening statement conveys the author’s promise that he will describe various ways to make chili,\nand the following sentences keep the promise. But what if Kuralt had written this paragraph\ninstead?\nSome people really like chili, apparently, but nobody can agree how the\nstuff should be made. C. V. Wood, twice winner at Terlingua, uses flank steak,\npork chops, chicken, and green chilis. My friend Hughes Rudd, who imported\nfive hundred pounds of chili powder into Russia as a condition of accepting\nemployment as Moscow correspondent, favors coarse-ground beef. He had\nsome trouble finding the beef in Moscow, though. He sometimes had to scour\nall the markets and wait in long lines. For any American used to overstocked\nsupermarkets and department stores, Russia can be quite a shock.\nBy wandering off from chili ingredients to consumer deprivation\nin Russia, the paragraph fails to deliver on the commitment of its topic sentence.\nYou should expect digressions while you are drafting: if you allow yourself to explore ideas,\nas you should, then of course every paragraph will not be tightly woven, perfectly unified. But\nspare your readers the challenge and frustration of repeatedly shifting focus to follow your rough\nexplorations: revise each paragraph so that it develops a single idea.\nWhile revising your paragraphs for unity, you may want to highlight the central idea of each\nparagraph to be sure it’s stated and then focus on it. On paper you can bracket or circle the idea. On\na computer you can format the idea in color or highlight it with a color background. Just be sure to\nremove the color or highlighting before printing the final draft.\n2 Placing the topic sentence\nThe topic sentence of a paragraph and its supporting details may be arranged variously,\ndepending on how you want to direct readers’ attention and how complex your central idea is. In\nthe most common arrangements, the topic sentence comes at the beginning of the paragraph, comes\nat the end, or is not stated at all but is nonetheless apparent. The advantages of each approach are\ndescribed on these two pages. If you write on a computer, you can easily experiment with the\nposition of the topic sentence by moving the sentence around (or deleting it) to see the effect. (The\nsentence will probably take some editing to work smoothly into various positions.)\nTopic sentence at the beginning\nWhen the topic sentence appears first in a paragraph, it can help you select the details that\nfollow. For readers, the topic-first model establishes an initial context in which all the supporting\ndetails can be understood. Reading Kuralt’s paragraph on page 71, we easily relate each detail or\nexample back to the point made in the first sentence.\nThe topic-first model is common not only in expository paragraphs, such as Kuralt’s, but also\nin argument paragraphs, such as the one following:\nIt is a misunderstanding of the American retail store to think we go there\nnecessarily to buy. Some of us shop. There’s a difference. Shopping has many\npurposes, the least interesting of which is to acquire new articles. We shop to\ncheer ourselves up. We shop to practice decision-making. We shop to be useful\nand productive members of our class and society. We shop to remind ourselves\nhow much is available to us. We shop to remind ourselves how much is to be\nstriven for. We shop to assert our superiority to the material objects that spread\nthemselves before us.\n—Phyllis Rose, ―Shopping and Other\nSpiritual Adventures‖\nTopic sentence at the end\nIn some paragraphs the central idea may be stated at the end, after supporting sentences have\nmade a case for the general statement. Since this model leads the reader to a conclusion by\npresenting all the evidence first, it can prove effective in argument. And because the point of the\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 32\nparagraph is withheld until the end, this model can be dramatic in exposition, too, as illustrated by\nthe following example from an essay about William Tecumseh Sherman, a Union general during\nthe US Civil War:\nSherman is considered by some to be the inventor of ―total war‖: the first\ngeneral in human history to carry the logic of war to its ultimate extreme, the\nfirst to scorch the earth, the first to consciously demoralize the hostile civilian\npopulation in order to subdue its army, the first to wreck an economy in order\nto starve its soldiers. He has been called our first ―merchant of terror‖ and seen\nas the spiritual father of our Vietnam War concepts of ―search and destroy,‖\n―pacification,‖ ―strategic hamlets,‖ and ―free-fire zones.‖ As such, he remains a\ncardboard figure of our history: a monstrous arch-villain to unreconstructed\nSoutherners, and an embarrassment to Northerners.\n—Adapted from James Reston, Jr.,\n―You Cannot Refine It‖\nExpressing the central idea at the end of the paragraph does not eliminate the need to unify the\nparagraph. The idea in the topic sentence must still govern the selection of all the preceding details.\nCentral idea not stated\nOccasionally, a paragraph’s central idea will be stated in the previous paragraph or will be so\nobvious that it need not be stated at all. The following is from an essay on the actor Humphrey\nBogart:\nUsually he wore the trench coat unbuttoned, just tied with the belt, and a\nslouch hat, rarely tilted. Sometimes it was a captain’s cap and a yachting jacket.\nAlmost always his trousers were held up by a cowboy belt. You know the kind:\none an Easterner waiting for a plane out of Phoenix buys just as a joke and then\ntakes a liking to. Occasionally, he’d hitch up his slacks with it, and he often\njabbed his thumbs behind it, his hands ready for a fight or a dame.\n—Peter Bogdanovich, ―Bogie in Excelsis‖\nParagraphs in descriptive writing (like the one above) and in narrative writing (relating a\nsequence of events) often lack stated topic sentences. But a paragraph without a topic sentence still\nshould have a central idea, and its details should develop that idea.\nEXERCISE 4.1 Finding the central idea\nWhat is the central idea of each of the following paragraphs? In what sentence or sentences is it\nexpressed? (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\n1. Today many black Americans enjoy a measure of economic se-curity beyond any we have known in\nthe history of black America. But if they remain in a nasty blue funk, it’s because their very existence\nseems an affront to the swelling ranks of the poor. Nor have black intellectuals ever quite made peace\nwith the concept of the black bourgeoisie, a group that is typically seen as devoid of cultural authenticity,\ndoomed to mimicry and pallid assimilation. I once gave a talk before an audience of black academics and\neducators, in the course of which I referred to black middle-class culture. Afterward, one of the academics\nin the audience, deeply affronted, had a question for me. ―Professor Gates,‖ he asked rhetorically, his\nvoice dripping with sarcasm, ―what is black middle-class culture?‖ I suggested that if he really wanted to\nknow, he need only look around the room. But perhaps I should just have handed him a mirror: for just as\nnothing is more American than anti-Americanism, nothing is more characteristic of the black bourgeoi-\nsie than the sense of shame and denial that the identity inspires.\n—Henry Louis Gates, Jr., ―Two Nations . . . Both Black‖\n2. Though they do not know why the humpback whale sings, scientists do know something about the\nsong itself. They have measured the length of a whale’s song: from a few minutes to over half an hour.\nThey have recorded and studied the variety and complex arrangements of low moans, high squeaks, and\nsliding squeals that make up the song. And they have learned that each whale sings in its own unique\npattern. —Janet Lieber (student), ―Whales’ Songs‖\nEXERCISE 4.2 Revising a paragraph for unity\nThe following paragraph contains ideas or details that do not support its central idea. Identify the topic\nsentence in the paragraph and\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 33\ndelete the unrelated material. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nIn the southern part of the state, some people still live much as they did a century ago. They use\ncoal- or wood-burning stoves for heating and cooking. Their homes do not have electricity or indoor\nbathrooms or running water. The towns they live in don’t receive adequate funding from the state and\nfederal governments, so the schools are poor and in bad shape. Beside most homes there is a garden\nwhere fresh vegetables are gathered for canning. Small pastures nearby support livestock, including cattle,\npigs, horses, and chickens. Most of the people have cars or trucks, but the vehicles are old and beat-up\nfrom traveling on unpaved roads.\nEXERCISE 4.3 Considering your past work: Paragraph unity\nFor a continuing exercise in this chapter, choose a paper you’ve written in the past year. Examine the\nbody paragraphs for unity. Do they have clear topic sentences? If not, are the paragraphs’ central ideas\nstill clear? Are the paragraphs unified around their central ideas? Should any details be deleted for unity?\nShould other, more relevant details be added in their stead?\nEXERCISE 4.4 Writing a unified paragraph\nDevelop the following topic sentence into a unified paragraph by using the relevant information in the\nsupporting statements. Delete each statement that does not relate directly to the topic, and then rewrite\nand combine sentences as appropriate. Place the topic sentence in the position that seems most effective\nto you. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nTopic sentence\nMozart’s accomplishments in music seem remarkable even today.\nSupporting information\nWolfgang Amadeus Mozart was born in 1756 in Salzburg, Austria.\nHe began composing music at the age of five.\nHe lived most of his life in Salzburg and Vienna.\nHis first concert tour of Europe was at the age of six.\nOn his first tour he played harpsichord, organ, and violin.\nHe published numerous compositions before reaching adolescence.\nHe married in 1782.\nMozart and his wife were both poor managers of money.\nThey were plagued by debts.\nMozart composed over six hundred musical compositions.\nHis most notable works are his operas, symphonies, quartets, and\npiano concertos.\nHe died at the age of thirty-five.\nEXERCISE 4.5 Turning topic sentences\ninto unified paragraphs\nDevelop three of the following topic sentences into detailed and\nunified paragraphs. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/\nlittlebrown.)\n1. Men and women are different in at least one important respect.\n2. The best Web search engine is [name].\n3. Fans of music [country, classical, rock, rap, jazz, or an-other kind] come in [number] varieties.\n4. Professional sports have [or have not] been helped by extending the regular season with\nchampionship play-offs.\n5. Working for good grades can interfere with learning.\n4b Achieving paragraph coherence\nA paragraph is unified if it holds together—if all its details and examples support the central\nidea. A paragraph is coherent if readers can see how the paragraph holds together—how the\nsentences relate to each other—without having to stop and reread.\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 34\nIncoherence gives readers the feeling of being yanked around, as the following example\nshows.\nThe ancient Egyptians were masters of preserving dead people’s bodies by\nmaking mummies of them. Mummies several thousand years old have been\ndiscovered nearly intact. The skin, hair, teeth, finger- and toenails, and facial\nfeatures of the mummies were evident. One can diagnose the diseases they\nsuffered in life, such as smallpox, arthritis, and nutritional deficiencies. The\nprocess was remarkably effective. Sometimes apparent were the fatal afflictions\nof the dead people: a middle-aged king died from a blow on the head, and polio\nkilled a child king. Mummification consisted of removing the internal organs,\napplying natural preservatives inside and out, and then wrapping the body in\nlayers of bandages.\nThe paragraph as it was actually written appears below. It is much clearer\nbecause the writer arranged information differently and also built links\ninto his sentences so that they would flow smoothly:\nAfter stating the central idea in a topic sentence, the writer moves to two more specific\nexplanations and illustrates the second with four sentences of examples.\nCircled words repeat or restate key terms or concepts.\nBoxed words link sentences and clarify relationships.\nUnderlined phrases are in parallel grammatical form to reflect their parallel content.\nThe ancient Egyptians were masters of\npreserving dead people’s bodies by making mummies of them. Basically,\nmummification consisted of removing the internal organs, applying natural\npreservatives inside and out, and then wrapping the body in layers of bandages.\nAnd the process was remarkably effective.\nIndeed, mummies several thousand years old have been discovered nearly\nintact. Their skin, hair, teeth, finger- and toenails, and facial features are still\nevident. Their diseases in life, such as smallpox, arthritis, and nutritional\ndeficiencies, are still diagnosable. Even their fatal afflictions are still apparent:\na middle-aged king died from a blow on the head; a child king died from polio.\n—Mitchell Rosenbaum (student),\n―Lost Arts of the Egyptians‖\nThough some of the connections in this paragraph were added in revision, the writer attended\nto them while drafting as well. Not only superficial coherence but also an underlying clarity of\nrelationships can be achieved by tying each sentence to the one before—generalizing from it,\nclarifying it, qualifying it, adding to it, illustrating it. Each sentence in a paragraph creates an\nexpectation of some sort in the mind of the reader, a question such as ―How was a mummy made?‖\nor ―How intact are the mummies?‖ or ―What’s another example?‖ When you recognize these\nexpectations and try to fulfill them, readers are likely to understand relationships without struggle.\n1 Organizing the paragraph\nThe paragraphs on mummies illustrate an essential element of coherence: information must be\narranged in an order that readers can follow easily and that corresponds to their expectations. The\ncommon organizations for paragraphs correspond to those for entire essays: by space, by time, and\nfor emphasis. (In addition, the patterns of development also suggest certain arrangements. See\npp. 91–100.)\nNote On a computer you can experiment with different paragraph organizations and emphases.\nCopy a paragraph, paste the copy into your document, and then try moving sentences around. To\nevaluate the versions, you’ll need to edit each one so that sentences flow smoothly, attending to\nrepetition, parallelism, transitions, and the other techniques discussed in this section.\nOrganizing by space or time\nA paragraph organized spatially focuses readers’ attention on one point and scans a person,\nobject, or scene from that point. The movement usually parallels the way people actually look at\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 35\nthings, from top to bottom, from side to side, from near to far. Virginia Woolf follows the last\npattern in the following paragraph:\nSpatial organization\nThe sun struck straight upon the house, making the white walls glare\nbetween the dark windows. Their panes, woven thickly with green branches,\nheld circles of impenetrable darkness. Sharp-edged wedges of light lay upon the\nwindow-sill and showed inside the room plates with blue rings, cups with\ncurved handles, the bulge of a great bowl, the criss-cross pattern in the rug, and\nthe formidable corners and lines of cabinets and bookcases. Behind their\nconglomeration hung a zone of shadow in which might be a further shape to be\ndisencumbered of shadow or still denser depths of darkness.\n—Virginia Woolf, The Waves\nAnother familiar way of organizing the elements of a paragraph is chronologically—that is, in\norder of their occurrence in time. In a chronological paragraph, as in experience, the earliest events\ncome first, followed by more recent ones.\nChronological organization\nNor can a tree live without soil. A hurricane-born mangrove island may\nbring its own soil to the sea. But other mangrove trees make their own soil—\nand their own islands—from scratch. These are the ones which interest me. The\nseeds germinate in the fruit on the tree. The germinated embryo can drop\nanywhere—say, onto a dab of floating muck. The heavy root end sinks; a leafy\nplumule unfurls. The tiny seedling, afloat, is on its way. Soon aerial roots\nshooting out in all directions trap debris. The sapling’s networks twine, the\ninterstices narrow, and water calms in the lee. Bacteria thrive on organic broth;\namphipods swarm. These creatures grow and die at the tree’s wet feet. The soil\nthickens, accumulating rainwater, leaf rot, seashells, and guano; the island\nspreads.\n—Annie Dillard, ―Sojourner‖\nOrganizing for emphasis\nSome organizational schemes are imposed on paragraphs to achieve a certain emphasis. The\nmost common is the general-to-specific scheme, in which the topic sentence often comes first and\nthen the following sentences become increasingly specific. The para-graph on mummies (pp. 78–\n79) illustrates this organization: each sentence is either more specific than the one before it or at the\nsame level of generality. Here is another illustration:\nGeneral-to-specific organization\nPerhaps the simplest fact about sleep is that individual needs for it vary\nwidely. Most adults sleep between seven and nine hours, but occasionally\npeople turn up who need twelve hours or so, while some rare types can get by\non three or four. Rarest of all are those legendary types who require almost no\nsleep at all; respected researchers have recently studied three such people. One\nof them—a healthy, happy woman in her seventies—sleeps about an hour every\ntwo or three days. The other two are men in early middle age, who get by on a\nfew minutes a night. One of them complains about the daily fifteen minutes or\nso he’s forced to ―waste‖ in sleeping.\n—Lawrence A. Mayer,\n―The Confounding Enemy of Sleep‖\nIn the less common specific-to-general organization, the elements of the paragraph build to a\ngeneral conclusion:\nSpecific-to-general organization\nIt’s disconcerting that so many college women, when asked how their\nchildren will be cared for if they themselves work, refer with vague confidence\nto ―the day care center‖ as though there were some great amorphous kiddie\nwatcher out there that the state provides. But such places, adequately funded,\nwell run, and available to all, are still scarce in this country, particularly for\nmiddle-class women. And figures show that when she takes time off for family-\nconnected reasons (births, child care), a woman’s chances for career\nadvancement plum-met. In a job market that’s steadily tighten-\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 36\ning and getting more competitive, these obstacles bode the kind of danger\nahead that can shatter not only professions, but egos. A hard reality is that\nthere’s not much more support\nfor our daughters who have family-plus-career goals than there was for us;\nthere’s simply a great deal more self and societal pressure.\n—Judith Wax,\nStarting in the Middle\nAs its name implies, the problem-solution arrangement introduces a problem and then\nproposes or explains a solution. The next paragraph explains how to gain from Internet newsgroups\ndespite their limitations:\nProblem-solution organization\nEven when you do find a newsgroup with apparently useful material, you have no assurance of a\ncorrespondent’s authority because of e-mail’s inherent anonymity. Many people don’t cite their credentials.\nBesides, anyone can pose as an expert. The best information you can get initially is apt to be a reference to\nsomething of which you were not aware but can then investigate for yourself. Internet newsgroups can be\nvaluable for that alone. I have been directed to software-problem solutions, owners of out-of-print books, and\nimportant people who know nothing about communicating through elec-\ntronic communities. It is best to start with the assumption that you are conversing with peers, people who know\nthings that you don’t, while you probably know things that they don’t. Gradually, by trading information, you\ndevelop some virtual relationships and can assess the relative validity of your sources. Meanwhile, you will\nprobably have learned a few things along the way.\n—Adapted from John A. Butler,\nCybersearch\nWhen your details vary in significance, you can arrange them in a climactic order, from least\nto most important or dramatic:\nClimactic organization\nNature has put many strange tongues into the heads of her creatures.\nThere is the frog’s tongue, rooted at the front of the mouth so it can be\nprotruded an extra distance for nabbing prey. There is the gecko lizard’s\ntongue, so long and agile that the lizard uses it to wash its eyes. But the ultimate\nlingual whopper has been achieved in the anteater. The anteater’s head, long as\nit is, is not long enough to contain the tremendous tongue which licks deep into\nant-hills. Its tongue is not rooted in the mouth or throat: it is fastened to the\nbreastbone.\n—Alan Devoe, ―Nature’s Utmost‖\nIn other organizations, you can arrange details according to how you think readers are likely to\nunderstand them. In discussing the virtues of public television, for instance, you might proceed\nfrom most familiar to least familiar, from a well-known program your readers have probably seen\nto less well-known programs they may not have seen. Or in defending the right of government\nemployees to strike, you might arrange your reasons from simplest to most complex, from the\nemployees’ need to be able to redress grievances to more subtle consequences for relations between\nemployers and employees.\n2 Repeating or restating key words\nRepeating or restating the important words in a paragraph binds the sentences together and\nkeeps the paragraph’s topic uppermost in readers’ minds. In the next example, notice how the\ncircled words relate the sentences and stress the important ideas of the paragraph:\nHaving listened to both Chinese and English, I also tend to be suspicious of any comparisons\nbetween the two languages. Typically, one language—that of the person doing the comparing—is often\nused as the standard, the benchmark for a logical form of expression. And so the language being\ncompared is always in danger of being judged deficient or superfluous, simplistic or unnecessarily\ncomplex, melodious or cacophonous. English speakers point out that Chinese is extremely difficult\nbecause it relies on variations in tone barely discernible to the human ear. By the same token, Chinese\nspeakers tell me English is extremely difficult because it is inconsistent, a language of too many broken\nrules, of Mickey Mice and Donald Ducks.\n—Amy Tan, ―The Language of Discretion‖\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 37\nThis paragraph links sentences through their structure, too, because the subject of each one\npicks up on key words used earlier:\nSentence 1: Having listened to both Chinese and English, I tend to be suspicious of any comparisons\nbetween the two languages.\nSentence 2: Typically, one language . . .\nSentence 3: And so the language . . .\nSentence 4: English speakers . . .\nSentence 5: Chinese speakers . . .\nIn many incoherent paragraphs, such as the one on mummification on page 78, each sentence\nsubject introduces a topic new to the paragraph so that readers have trouble following the thread.\n(See pp. 386–87 for more on linking sentences through their subjects.)\n3 Using parallel structures\nAnother way to achieve coherence is through parallelism—the use of similar grammatical\nstructures for similar elements of meaning within a sentence or among sentences. (See Chapter 25\nfor a\ndetailed discussion of parallelism.) Parallel structures help tie together the last three sentences in\nthe paragraph on mummies\n(p. 79). In the following paragraph, underlining highlights the parallel structures linking sentences.\nAphra Behn (lived 1640–89) was the first Englishwoman to write professionally.\nIn addition to her busy career as a writer, Aphra Behn also found time to briefly marry and spend a\nlittle while in debtor’s prison. She found time to take up a career as a spy for the English in their war\nagainst the Dutch. She made the long and difficult voyage to Suriname [in South America] and became\ninvolved in a slave rebellion there. She plunged into political debate at Will’s Coffee House and defended\nher position from the stage of the Drury Lane Theater. She actively argued for women’s rights to be\neducated and to marry whom they pleased, or not at all. She defied the seventeenth-century dictum that\nladies must be ―modest‖ and wrote freely about sex.\n—Angeline Goreau, ―Aphra Behn‖\nNote Though planned repetition can be effective, careless or excessive repetition weakens\nprose (see pp. 532–33).\n4 Using pronouns\nPronouns such as she, he, it, they, and who refer to and function as nouns (see p. 237). Thus\npronouns naturally help relate sentences to one another. In the following paragraph the pronouns\nand the nouns they refer to are circled:\nAfter dark, on the warrenlike streets of Brooklyn where I live, I often see women who fear the worst\nfrom me. They seem to have set their faces on neutral, and with their purse straps strung across their\nchests bandolier-style, they forge ahead as though bracing themselves against being tackled. I understand,\nof course, that the danger they perceive is not a hallucination. Women are particularly vulnerable to street\nviolence, and young black males are drastically overrepresented among the perpetrators of that violence.\nYet these truths are no solace against the kind of alienation that comes of being ever the suspect, a\nfearsome entity with whom pedestrians avoid making eye contact.\n—Brent Staples, ―Black Men and Public Space‖\n5 Being consistent\nBeing consistent is the most subtle way to achieve paragraph coherence because readers are\naware of consistency only when it is absent. Consistency (or the lack of it) occurs primarily in the\ntense of verbs and in the number and person of nouns and pronouns (see Chapter 20). Although\nsome shifts will be necessary to reflect\nyour meaning, inappropriate shifts, as in the following passages, will interfere with a reader’s\nability to follow the development of ideas:\nShifts in tense\nIn the Hopi religion, water is the driving force. Since the Hopi lived in the Arizona desert, they needed\nwater urgently for drinking, cooking, and irrigating crops. Their complex beliefs are focused in part on\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 38\ngaining the assistance of supernatural forces in obtaining water. Many of the Hopi kachinas, or spirit\nessences, were directly concerned with clouds, rain, and snow.\nShifts in number\nKachinas represent the things and events of the real world, such as clouds, mischief, cornmeal, and even\ndeath. A kachina is not worshiped as a god but regarded as an interested friend. They visit the Hopi from\nDecember through July in the form of men who dress in kachina costumes and perform dances and other\nrituals.\nShifts in person\nUnlike the man, the Hopi woman does not keep contact with kachinas through costumes and dancing.\nInstead, one receives a small likeness of a kachina, called a tihu, from the man impersonating the kachina.\nYou are more likely to receive a tihu as a girl approaching marriage, though a child or older woman\nsometimes receives one, too.\nNote The grammar checker on a word processor cannot help you locate shifts in tense, number,\nor person among sentences. Shifts are sometimes necessary (as when tenses change to reflect actual\ndifferences in time). Furthermore, a passage with needless shifts may still consist of sentences that\nare grammatically correct, as all the sentences are in the preceding examples. The only way to\nachieve consistency in your writing is to review it yourself.\n6 Using transitional expressions\nSpecific words and word groups, called transitional expressions, can connect sentences\nwhose relationships may not be instantly clear to readers. Notice the difference in the following two\nversions of the same paragraph:\nMedical science has succeeded in identifying the hundreds of viruses that\ncan cause the common cold. It has discovered the most effective means of\nprevention. One person transmits the cold viruses to another most often by\nhand. An infected person covers his mouth to cough. He picks up the telephone.\nHis daughter picks up the telephone. She rubs her eyes. She has a cold. It\nspreads. To avoid colds, people should wash their hands often and keep their\nhands away from their faces.\nMedical science has thus succeeded in\nidentifying the hundreds of viruses that can cause the common cold. It has also\ndiscovered the most effective means of prevention. One person transmits the\ncold viruses to another most often by hand. For instance, an infected person\ncovers his mouth to cough. Then he picks up the telephone. Half an hour later,\nhis daughter picks up the same telephone. Immediately afterward, she rubs her\neyes. Within a few days, she, too, has a cold. And thus it spreads. To avoid\ncolds, therefore, people should wash their hands often and keep their hands\naway from their faces.\n—Kathleen LaFrank (student),\n―Colds: Myth and Science‖\nThere are scores of transitional expressions on which to draw. The box below shows many\ncommon ones, arranged according to the relationships they convey.\nTo see where transitional expressions might be needed in your paragraphs, examine the\nmovement from each sentence to the next. (On a computer or on paper, you can highlight the\ntransitional expressions already present and then review the sentences that lack them.) Abrupt\nchanges are most likely to need a transition: a shift from cause to effect, a contradiction, a contrast.\nYou can smooth and clarify transitions between paragraphs, too. See pages 108 and 109–10.\nIf transitional expressions are not common in your native language, you may be tempted to\ncompensate when writing in English by adding them to the beginnings of most sentences. But such\nexplicit transitions aren’t needed everywhere, and in fact too many can be intrusive and awkward.\nWhen inserting transitional expressions, consider the reader’s need for a signal: often the\nconnection from sentence to sentence is already clear from the context, or it can be made clear by\nrelating the content of sentences more closely (see pp. 83–84). When you do need transitional\nexpressions, try varying their positions in your sentences, as shown in the sample paragraph on the\nfacing page.\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 39\nPunctuating transitional expressions\nA transitional expression is usually set off by a comma or commas from the rest of the\nsentence:\nImmediately afterward , she rubs her eyes. Within a few days , she ,\ntoo , has a cold.\nSee page 438 for more on this convention and its exceptions.\n7 Combining devices to achieve coherence\nThe devices for achieving coherence rarely appear in isolation in effective paragraphs. As any\nexample in this chapter shows, writ-ers usually combine sensible organization, parallelism,\nrepetition, pronouns, consistency, and transitional expressions to help readers follow the\ndevelopment of ideas.\nEXERCISE 4.6 Analyzing paragraphs for coherence\nStudy the paragraphs by Janet Lieber (p. 79), Hillary Begas (p. 91),\nand Freeman Dyson (p. 93) for the authors’ use of various devices to achieve coherence. Look especially\nfor organization, parallel structures and ideas, repetition and restatement, pronouns, and transitional\nexpressions.\nEXERCISE 4.7 Arranging sentences coherently\nAfter the topic sentence (sentence 1), the sentences in the student paragraph below have been deliberately\nscrambled to make the paragraph incoherent. Using the topic sentence and other clues as guides,\nrearrange the sentences in the paragraph to form a well-organized, coherent unit. (You can do this\nexercise online at ablongman.com/\nlittlebrown.)\nWe hear complaints about the Postal Service all the time, but we should not forget what it does\nright. The total volume of mail de-\nlivered by the Postal Service each year makes up almost half\nthe total delivered in all the world. Its 70,000 employees handle 140,000,000,000 pieces of mail each\nyear. And when was the last time they failed to deliver yours? In fact, on any given day the Postal Service\ndelivers almost as much mail as the rest of the world combined. That huge number means over 2,000,000\npieces per employee and over 560 pieces per man, woman, and child in the country.\nEXERCISE 4.8 Eliminating inconsistencies\nThe following paragraph is incoherent because of inconsistencies in person, number, or tense. Identify the\ninconsistencies and revise the paragraph to give it coherence. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.) For further exercises in eliminating inconsistencies, see pages 359, 360–61,\nand 363.\nThe Hopi tihu, or kachina likeness, is often called a ―doll,‖ but its owner, usually a girl or woman,\ndoes not regard them as a plaything. Instead, you treated them as a valued possession and hung them out\nof the way on a wall. For its owner the tihu represents a connection with the kachina’s spirit. They are\nconsidered part of the kachina, carrying a portion of the kachina’s power.\nEXERCISE 4.9 Using transitional expressions\nTransitional expressions have been removed from the following paragraph at the numbered blanks. Fill in\neach blank with an appropriate transitional expression (1) to contrast, (2) to intensify, and (3) to show\neffect. Consult the list on pages 86–87 if necessary. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nAll over the country, people are swimming, jogging, weightlifting, dancing, walking, playing\ntennis—doing anything to keep fit. (1) this school has consistently refused to construct and equip a\nfitness center. The school has (2) refused to open existing athletic facilities to all students, not just those\nplaying organized sports. (3) students have no place to exercise except in their rooms and on dangerous\npublic roads.\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 40\nEXERCISE 4.10 Considering your past work:\nParagraph coherence\nContinuing from Exercise 4.3 (p. 76), examine the body paragraphs of your essay to see how coherent\nthey are and how their coherence could be improved. Do the paragraphs have a clear organization? Do\nyou use repetition and restatement, parallelism, pronouns, and transitional expressions to signal\nrelationships? Are the paragraphs consistent in person, number, and tense? Revise two or three\nparagraphs in ways you think will improve their coherence.\nEXERCISE 4.11 Writing a coherent paragraph\nWrite a coherent paragraph from the following information, combining and rewriting sentences as\nnecessary. First, begin the paragraph with the topic sentence given and arrange the supporting sentences\nin a climactic order. Then combine and rewrite the supporting sentences, helping the reader see\nconnections by introducing repetition and restatement, parallelism, pronouns, consistency, and\ntransitional expressions. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nTopic sentence\nHypnosis is far superior to drugs for relieving tension.\nSupporting information\nHypnosis has none of the dangerous side effects of the drugs that\nrelieve tension.\nTension-relieving drugs can cause weight loss or gain, illness, or even\ndeath.\nHypnosis is nonaddicting.\nMost of the drugs that relieve tension do foster addiction.\nTension-relieving drugs are expensive.\nHypnosis is inexpensive even for people who have not mastered self-\nhypnosis.\nEXERCISE 4.12 Turning topic sentences\ninto coherent paragraphs\nDevelop three of the following topic sentences into coherent paragraphs. Organize your information by\nspace, by time, or for emphasis, as seems most appropriate. Use repetition and restatement, parallelism,\npronouns, consistency, and transitional expressions to link sentences. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\n1. The most interesting character in the book [or movie] was .\n2. Of all my courses, is the one that I think will serve me best throughout life.\n3. Although we in the United States face many problems, the one we should concentrate on solving first\nis .\n4. The most dramatic building in town is the .\n5. Children should not have to worry about the future.\n4c Developing the paragraph\nIn an essay that’s understandable and interesting to readers, you will provide plenty of solid\ninformation to support your general statements. You work that information into the essay through\nthe paragraph, as you build up each point relating to the thesis.\nA paragraph may be unified and coherent but still be inadequate if you skimp on details. Take\nthis example:\nUntruths can serve as a kind of social oil when they smooth connections\nbetween people. In preventing confrontation and injured feelings, they allow\neveryone to go on as before.\nThis paragraph lacks development, completeness. It does not provide enough information for us to\nevaluate or even care about the writer’s assertions.\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 41\n1 Using specific information\nIf they are sound, the general statements you make in any writing will be based on what you\nhave experienced, observed, read, and thought. Readers will assume as much and will expect you to\nprovide the evidence for your statements—sensory details, facts, statistics, examples, quotations,\nreasons. Whatever helps you form your views you need, in turn, to share with readers.\nHere is the actual version of the preceding sample paragraph. With examples, the paragraph is\nmore interesting and convincing.\nUntruths can serve as a kind of social oil when they smooth connections\nbetween people. Assuring a worried friend that his haircut is flattering, claiming\nan appointment to avoid an aunt’s dinner invitation, pretending interest in an\nacquaintance’s children—these lies may protect the liar, but they also protect\nthe person lied to. In preventing confrontation and injured feelings, the lies\nallow everyone to go on as before.\n—Joan Lar (student), ―The Truth of Lies‖\nIf your readers often comment that your writing needs more specifics, you should focus on\nthat improvement in your revisions. Try listing the general statements of each paragraph on lines by\nthemselves with space underneath. Then use one of the discovery techniques discussed on pages\n16–26 (freewriting, brainstorming, and so on) to find the details to support each sentence. Write\nthese into your draft. If you write on a computer, you can do this revision directly on your draft.\nFirst create a duplicate of your draft, and then, working on the copy, separate the sentences and\nexplore their support. Rewrite the supporting details into sentences, reassemble the paragraph, and\nedit it for coherence.\n2 Using a pattern of development\nIf you have difficulty developing an idea or shaping your information, then try asking yourself\nquestions derived from the patterns of development. (The same patterns can help with essay\ndevelopment, too. See pp. 24–25.)\nYou can download the following questions from ablongman .com/littlebrown. When you’re\nhaving difficulty with a paragraph, you can duplicate the list and explore answers. You may be able\nto import what you write directly into your draft.\nHow did it happen? (Narration)\nNarration retells a significant sequence of events, usually in the order of their occurrence\n(that is, chronologically):\nJill’s story is typical for ―recruits‖ to religious cults. She was very lonely\nin college and appreciated the attention of the nice young men and women who\nlived in a house near campus. They persuaded her to share their meals and then\nto move in with them. Between intense bombardments of ―love,‖ they deprived\nher of sleep and sometimes threatened to throw her out. Jill became increasingly\nconfused and dependent, losing touch with any reality besides the one in the\ngroup. She dropped out of school and refused to see or communicate with her\nfamily. Before long she, too, was preying on lonely college students.\n—Hillary Begas (student),\n―The Love Bombers‖\nAs this paragraph illustrates, a narrator is concerned not just with the sequence of events but\nalso with their consequence, their importance to the whole. Thus a narrative rarely corresponds to\nreal time; instead, it collapses transitional or background events and focuses on events of particular\ninterest. In addition, writers some-times rearrange events, as when they simulate the workings of\nmem-ory by flashing back to an earlier time.\nHow does it look, sound, feel, smell, taste? (Description)\nDescription details the sensory qualities of a person, place, thing, or feeling. You use concrete\nand specific words to convey a dominant mood, to illustrate an idea, or to achieve some other\npurpose. Some description is subjective: the writer filters the subject through his or her biases and\nemotions. In the subjective description by Virginia Woolf on page 80, the glare of the walls, the\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 42\nimpenetrable darkness, the bulge of a great bowl, and the formidable corners and lines all indicate\nthe author’s feelings about what she describes.\nIn contrast to subjective description, journalists and scientists often favor description that is\nobjective, conveying the subject without bias or emotion:\nThe two toddlers, both boys, sat together for half an hour in a ten-foot-\nsquare room with yellow walls (one with a two-way mirror for observation) and\na brown carpet. The room was unfurnished except for two small chairs and\nabout two dozen toys. The boys’ interaction was generally tense. They often\nstruggled physically and verbally over several toys, especially a large red beach\nball and a small wooden fire engine. The larger of the two boys often pushed\nthe smaller away or pried his hands from the desired object. This larger boy\nnever spoke, but he did make grunting sounds when he was engaging the other.\nIn turn, the smaller boy twice uttered piercing screams of ―No!‖ and once\nshouted ―Stop that!‖ When he was left alone, he hummed and muttered to\nhimself.\n—Ray Mattison (student),\n―Case Study: Play Patterns of Toddlers‖\nWhat are examples of it or reasons for it? (Illustration\nor support)\nSome ideas can be developed simply by illustration or support—supplying detailed\nexamples or reasons. The writer of the paragraph on lying (pp. 90–91) developed her idea with\nseveral specific examples of her general statements. You can also supply a single extended\nexample:\nThe language problem that I was attacking loomed larger and larger as I\nbegan to learn more. When I would describe in English certain concepts and\nobjects enmeshed in Korean emotion and imagination, I became slowly aware\nof nuances, of differences between two languages even in simple expression.\nThe remark ―Kim entered the house‖ seems to be simple enough, yet, unless a\nreader has a clear visual image of a Korean house, his understanding of the\nsentence is not complete. When a Korean says he is ―in the house,‖ he may be\nin his courtyard, or on his porch, or in his small room! If I wanted to give a\nspecific picture of entering the house in the Western sense, I had to say ―room‖\ninstead of house—sometimes. I say ―sometimes‖ because many Koreans\nentertain their guests on their porches and still are considered to be hospitable,\nand in the Korean sense, going into the ―room‖ may be a more intimate act than\nit would be in the English sense. Such problems!\n—Kim Yong Ik, ―A Book-Writing Venture‖\nSometimes you can develop a paragraph by providing your reasons for stating a general idea:\nThere are three reasons, quite apart from scientific considerations, that\nmankind needs to travel in space. The first reason is the need for garbage\ndisposal: we need to transfer industrial processes into space, so that the earth\nmay remain a green and pleasant place for our grandchildren to live in. The\nsecond reason is the need to escape material impoverishment: the resources of\nthis planet are finite, and we shall not forgo forever the abundant solar energy\nand minerals and living space that are spread out all around us. The third reason\nis our spiritual need for an open frontier: the ultimate purpose of space travel is\nto bring to humanity not only scientific discoveries and an occasional\nspectacular show on television but a real expansion of our spirit.\n—Freeman Dyson, ―Disturbing the Universe‖\nWhat is it? What does it encompass, and what does it exclude? (Definition)\nA definition says what something is and is not, specifying the characteristics that distinguish\nthe subject from the other members of its class. You can easily define concrete, noncontroversial\nterms in a single sentence: A knife is a cutting instrument (its class) with a sharp blade set in a\nhandle (the characteristics that set it off from, say, scissors or a razor blade). But defining a\ncomplicated or controversial topic often requires extended explanation, and you may need to\ndevote a whole paragraph or even an essay to it. Such a definition may provide examples to identify\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 43\nthe subject’s characteristics. It may also involve other methods of development discussed here,\nsuch as classification or comparison and contrast.\nThe following definition of the word quality comes from an essay asserting that ―quality in\nproduct and effort has become a vanishing element of current civilization‖:\nIn the hope of possibly reducing the hail of censure which is certain to\ngreet this essay (I am thinking of going to Alaska or possibly Patagonia in the\nweek it is published), let me say that quality, as I understand it, means\ninvestment of the best skill and effort possible to produce the finest and most\nadmirable result possible. Its presence or absence in some degree characterizes\nevery man-made object, service, skilled or unskilled labor—laying bricks,\npainting a picture, ironing shirts, practicing medicine, shoemaking, scholarship,\nwriting a book. You do it well or you do it half-well. Materials are sound and\ndurable or they are sleazy; method is painstaking or whatever is easiest. Quality\nis achieving or reaching for the highest standard as against being satisfied with\nthe sloppy or fraudulent. It is honesty of purpose as against catering to cheap or\nsensational sentiment. It does not allow compromise with the second-rate.\n—Barbara Tuchman,\n―The Decline of Quality‖\nWhat are its parts or characteristics? (Division or analysis)\nDivision and analysis both involve separating something into its elements, the better to\nunderstand it. Here is a simple example:\nA typical daily newspaper compresses considerable information into the\ntop of the first page, above the headlines. The most prominent feature of this\nspace, the newspaper’s name, is called the logo or nameplate. Under the logo\nand set off by rules is a line of small type called the folio line, which contains\nthe date of the issue, the volume and issue numbers, copyright information, and\nthe price. To the right of the logo is a block of small type called a weather ear,\na summary of the day’s forecast. And above the logo is a skyline, a kind of\nadvertisement in which the paper’s editors highlight a special feature of the\nissue.\n—Kansha Stone (student),\n―Anatomy of a Paper‖\nGenerally, analysis goes beyond simply identifying elements. Often used as a synonym for\ncritical thinking, analysis also involves interpreting the elements’ meaning, significance, and\nrelationships. You identify and interpret elements according to your particular interest in the\nsubject. (See pp. 157–63 for more on critical thinking and analysis.)\nThe following paragraph comes from an essay about soap operas. The analytical focus of the\nwhole essay is the way soap operas provide viewers with a sense of community missing from their\nown lives. The paragraph itself has a narrower focus related to the broader one.\nThe surface realism of the soap opera conjures up an illusion of\n―liveness.‖ The domestic settings and easygoing rhythms encourage the viewer\nto believe that the drama, however ridiculous, is simply an extension of daily\nlife. The conversation is so slow that some have called it ―radio with pictures.‖\n(Advertisers have always assumed that busy housewives would listen, rather\nthan watch.) Conversation is casual and colloquial, as though one were\neavesdropping on neighbors. There is plenty of time to ―read‖ the character’s\nface; close-ups establish intimacy. The sets are comfortably familiar: well-lit\ninteriors of living rooms, restaurants, offices, and hospitals. Daytime soaps\nhave little of the glamour of their prime-time relations. The viewer easily\nimagines that the conversation is taking place in real time.\n—Ruth Rosen,\n―Search for Yesterday‖\nWhat groups or categories can it be sorted into?\n(Classification)\nClassification involves sorting many things into groups based on their similarities. Using the\npattern, we scan a large group composed of many members that share at least one characteristic—\noffice workers, say—and we assign the members to smaller groups on the basis of some principle—\nsalary, perhaps, or dependence on computers. Here is an example:\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 44\nIn my experience, the parents who hire daytime sitters for their school-age\nchildren tend to fall into one of three groups. The first group includes parents\nwho work and want someone to be at home when the children return from\nschool. These parents are looking for an extension of themselves, someone who\nwill give the care they would give if they were at home. The second group\nincludes parents who may be home all day themselves but are too disorgan-ized\nor too frazzled by their children’s demands to handle child care alone. They are\nlooking for an organizer and helpmate. The third and final group includes\nparents who do not want to be bothered by their children, whether they are\nhome all day or not. Unlike the parents in the first two groups, who care for\ntheir children whenever and however they can, these parents are looking for a\npermanent substitute for themselves.\n—Nancy Whittle (student),\n―Modern Parenting‖\nHow is it like, or different from, other things?\n(Comparison and contrast)\nAsking about similarities and differences leads to comparison and contrast: comparison\nfocuses on similarities, whereas contrast focuses on differences. The two may be used separately or\ntogether to develop an idea or to relate two or more things. Commonly, comparisons are organized\nin one of two ways. In the first, subject by subject, the two subjects are discussed separately, one\nat a time:\nConsider the differences also in the behavior of rock and classical music\naudiences. At a rock concert, the audience members yell, whistle, sing along,\nand stamp their feet. They may even stand during the entire performance. The\nbetter the music, the more active they’ll be. At a classical concert, in contrast,\nthe better the performance, the more still the audience is. Members of the\nclassical audience are so highly disciplined that they refrain from even clearing\ntheir throats or coughing. No matter what effect the powerful music has on their\nintellects and feelings, they sit on their hands.\n—Tony Nahm (student),\n―Rock and Roll Is Here to Stay‖\nIn the second comparative organization, point by point, the two subjects are discussed side by side\nand matched feature for feature:\nThe first electronic computer, ENIAC, went into operation just over fifty\nyears ago, yet the differences between it and today’s personal computer are\nenormous. ENIAC was enormous itself, consisting of forty panels, each two feet\nwide and four feet deep. Today’s notebook PC or Macintosh, by contrast, can\nfit easily on one’s lap. ENIAC had to be configured by hand, with its\nprogrammers taking up to two days to reset switches and cables. Today, the\naverage user can change programs in an instant. And for all its size and\ninconvenience, ENIAC was also slow. In its time, its operating speed of\n100,000 pulses per second seemed amazingly fast. However, today’s notebook\ncan operate at more than 1 bil-lion pulses per second.\n—Shirley Kajiwara (student),\n―The Computers We Deserve‖\nThe following examples show the two organizing schemes in outline form. The one on the left\ncorresponds to the point-by-point paragraph about computers. The one on the right uses the same\ninformation but reorganizes it to cover the two subjects separately: first one, then the other.\nPoint by point Subject by subject\nIII. Size II. ENIAC\nA. ENIAC A. Size\nB. Personal computer B. Ease of programming\nIII. Ease of programming C. Speed\nA. ENIAC II. Personal computer\nB. Personal computer A. Size\nIII. Speed B. Ease of programming\nA. ENIAC C. Speed\nB. Personal computer\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 45\nIs it comparable to something that is in a different class\nbut more familiar to readers? (Analogy)\nWhereas we draw comparisons and contrasts between elements in the same general class\n(audiences, computers), we link elements in different classes with a special kind of comparison\ncalled analogy. Most often in analogy we illuminate or explain an unfamiliar, abstract class of\nthings with a familiar and concrete class of things:\nWe might eventually obtain some sort of bedrock understanding of\ncosmic structure, but we will never understand the universe in detail; it is just\ntoo big and varied for that. If we possessed an atlas of our galaxy that devoted\nbut a single page to each star system in the Milky Way (so that the sun and all\nits planets were crammed on one page), that atlas would run to more than ten\nmillion volumes of ten thousand pages each. It would take a library the size of\nHarvard’s to house the atlas, and merely to flip through it, at the rate of a page\nper second, would require over ten thousand years.\n—Timothy Ferris,\nComing of Age in the Milky Way\nWhy did it happen, or what results did it have?\n(Cause-and-effect analysis)\nWhen you use analysis to explain why something happened or what is likely to happen, then\nyou are determining causes and effects. Cause-and-effect analysis is especially useful in writing\nabout social, economic, or political events or problems. In the next paragraph the author looks at\nthe causes of Japanese collectivism, which he elsewhere contrasts with American individualism:\nThe shinkansen or ―bullet train‖ speeds across the rural areas of Japan\ngiving a quick view of cluster after cluster of farmhouses surrounded by rice\npaddies. This particular pattern did not develop purely by chance, but as a\nconsequence of the technology peculiar to the growing of rice, the staple of the\nJapanese diet. The growing of rice requires the construction and maintenance of\nan irrigation system, something that takes many hands to build. More\nimportantly, the planting and the harvesting of rice can only be done efficiently\nwith the co-\noperation of twenty or more people. The ―bottom line‖ is that a single family\nworking alone cannot produce enough rice to survive, but a dozen families\nworking together can produce a surplus. Thus the Japanese have had to develop\nthe capacity to work together in harmony, no matter what the forces of\ndisagreement or social disintegration, in order to survive.\n—William Ouchi, Theory Z\nCause-and-effect paragraphs tend to focus either on causes, as Ouchi’s does, or on effects, as\nthis paragraph does:\nAt each step, with every graduation from one level of education to the\nnext, the refrain from bystanders was strangely the same: ―Your parents must be\nso proud of you.‖ I suppose that my parents were proud, although I suspect, too,\nthat they felt more than pride alone as they watched me advance through my\neducation. They seemed to know that my education was separating us from one\nanother, making it difficult to resume familiar intimacies. Mixed with the\ninstincts of parental pride, a certain hurt also communicated itself—too private\never to be adequately expressed in words, but real nonetheless.\n—Richard Rodriguez, ―Going Home Again‖\nHow does one do it, or how does it work?\n(Process analysis)\nWhen you analyze how to do something or how something works, you explain the steps in a\nprocess. Paragraphs developed by process analysis are usually organized chronologically, as the\nsteps in the process occur. Some process analyses tell the reader how to do a task:\nAs a car owner, you waste money when you pay a mechanic to change the\nengine oil. The job is not difficult, even if you know little about cars. All you\nneed is a wrench to remove the drain plug, a large, flat pan to collect the\ndraining oil, plastic bottles to dispose of the used oil, and fresh oil. First, warm\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 46\nup the car’s engine so that the oil will flow more easily. When the engine is\nwarm, shut it off and remove its oil-filler cap (the owner’s manual shows where\nthis cap is). Then locate the drain plug under the engine (again consulting the\nowner’s manual for its location) and place the flat pan under the plug. Remove\nthe plug with the wrench, letting the oil flow into the pan. When the oil stops\nflowing, replace the plug and, at the engine’s filler hole, add the amount and\nkind of fresh oil specified by the owner’s manual. Pour the used oil into the\nplastic bottles and take it to a waste-oil collector, which any garage mechanic\ncan recommend.\n—Anthony Andreas (student),\n―Do-It-Yourself Car Care‖\nOther process analyses explain how processes are done or how they work in nature. Annie\nDillard’s paragraph on mangrove islands (p. 80) is one example. Here is another:\nWhat used to be called ―laying on of hands‖ is now practiced seriously by\nnurses and doctors. Studies have shown that therapeutic touch, as it is now\nknown, can aid relaxation and ease pain, two effects that may in turn cause\nhealing. A ―healer‖ must first concentrate on helping the patient. Then, hands\nheld a few inches from the patient’s body, the healer moves from head to foot.\nHealers claim that they can detect energy disturbances in the patient that\nindicate tension, pain, or sickness. With further hand movements, the healer\ntries to redirect the energy. Patients report feeling heat from the healer’s hands,\nperhaps indicating an energy transfer between healer and patient.\n—Lisa Kuklinski (student),\n―Old Ways to Noninvasive Medicine‖\nDiagrams, photographs, and other figures can do much to clarify process analyses. See pages\n120–25 for guidelines on creating and clearly labeling figures.\nCombining patterns of development\nWhatever pattern you choose as the basis for developing a paragraph, other patterns may also\nprove helpful. Combined patterns have appeared often in this section: Dyson analyzes causes and\neffects in presenting reasons (p. 93); Tuchman uses contrast to define quality (p. 94); Nahm uses\ndescription to compare (p. 96); Ouchi uses process analysis to explain causes (p. 98).\n3 Checking length\nThe average paragraph contains between 100 and 150 words, or between four and eight\nsentences. The actual length of a paragraph depends on the complexity of its topic, the role it plays\nin developing the thesis of the essay, and its position in the essay. Nevertheless, very short\nparagraphs are often inadequately developed; they may leave readers with a sense of\nincompleteness. And very long paragraphs often contain irrelevant details or develop two or more\ntopics; readers may have difficulty following, sorting out, or remembering ideas.\nWhen you are revising your essay, reread the paragraphs that seem very long or very short,\nchecking them especially for unity and adequate development. If the paragraph wanders, cut\neverything from it that does not support your main idea (such as sentences that you might begin\nwith By the way). If it is underdeveloped, supply the specific details, examples, or reasons needed,\nor try one of the methods of development we have discussed here.\nEXERCISE 4.13 Analyzing paragraph development\nExamine the paragraphs by Henry Louis Gates, Jr. (p. 76), and Judith Wax (p. 81) to discover how the\nauthors achieve paragraph development. What pattern or patterns of development does each author use?\nWhere does each author support general statements with specific evidence?\nEXERCISE 4.14 Analyzing and revising skimpy paragraphs\nThe following paragraphs are not well developed. Then rewrite one into a well-developed paragraph,\nsupplying your own concrete details or examples. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 47\n1. One big difference between successful and unsuccessful teachers is the quality of communication. A\nsuccessful teacher is sensitive to students’ needs and excited by the course subject. In contrast, an un-\nsuccessful teacher seems uninterested in students and bored by the subject.\n2. Gestures are one of our most important means of communication. We use them instead of speech.\nWe use them to supplement the words we speak. And we use them to communicate some feelings or\nmeanings that words cannot adequately express.\n3. I’ve discovered that a word processor can do much—but not everything—to help me improve my\nwriting. I can easily make changes and try out different versions of a paper. But I still must do the hard\nwork of revising.\nEXERCISE 4.15 Considering your past work:\nParagraph development\nContinuing from Exercises 4.3 (p. 76) and 4.10 (p. 89), examine the development of the body paragraphs\nin your writing.
