Process analysis\nPreparing for a job\ninterview\nSetting up a Web log\nProtecting your home from burglars\nMaking a jump shot\n4d Writing special kinds of paragraphs\nSeveral kinds of paragraphs do not always follow the guidelines for unity, coherence,\ndevelopment, and length because they serve special functions. These are the essay introduction, the\nessay conclusion, the transitional or emphatic paragraph, and the paragraph of spoken dialog.\n1 Opening an essay\nMost of your essays will open with a paragraph that draws readers from their world into your\nworld. A good opening paragraph usually satisfies several requirements:\nIt focuses readers’ attention on your subject and arouses their curiosity about what you\nhave to say.\nIt specifies what your topic is and implies your attitude.\nOften it provides your thesis statement.\nIt is concise and sincere.\nThe box on the facing page provides a range of options for achieving these goals.\nIf you are composing on the World Wide Web, you’ll want to consider\nNote\nthe expectations of Web readers. Your opening page may take the place of\na conventional introduction, providing concise text indicating your site’s\nsubject and purpose, a menu of its contents, and links to other pages. (See\npp. 832–38 for more on composing for the Web.)\nThe requirements and options for essay introductions may not be what you are used to if your\nnative language is not English. In other cultures, readers may seek familiarity or reassurance from\nan author’s introduction, or they may prefer an indirect approach to the subject. In academic and\nbusiness English, however, writers and readers prefer concise, direct expression.\nThe funnel introduction\nOne reliably effective introduction forms a kind of funnel:\nHere are two examples of the funnel introduction:\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 49\nCan your home or office computer make you sterile? Can it strike you\nblind or dumb? The answer is: probably not. Nevertheless, reports of side\neffects relating to computer use should be examined, especially in the area of\nbirth de-fects, eye complaints, and postural difficulties. Although little\nconclusive evidence exists to establish a causal link between computer use and\nproblems of this sort, the circumstantial evidence can be disturbing.\n—Thomas Hartmann, ―How Dangerous\nIs Your Computer?‖\nThe Declaration of Independence is so widely regarded as a statement of\nAmerican ideals that its origins in practical politics tend to be forgotten.\nThomas Jefferson’s draft was intensely debated and then revised in the\nContinental Congress. Jefferson was disappointed with the result. However, a\nclose reading of both the historical context and the revisions themselves\nindicates that the Congress improved the document for its intended purpose.\n—Ann Weiss (student), ―The Editing\nof the Declaration of Independence‖\nOther effective introductions\nSeveral other types of introduction can be equally effective, though they are sometimes harder\nto invent and control.\nQuotation leading into the thesis statement\n―It is difficult to speak adequately or justly of London,‖ wrote Henry\nJames in 1881. ―It is not a pleasant place; it is not agreeable, or cheerful, or\neasy, or exempt from reproach. It is only magnificent.‖ Were he alive today,\nJames, a connoisseur of cities, might easily say the same thing about New York\nor Paris or Tokyo, for the great city is one of the paradoxes of history. In\ncountless different ways, it has almost always been an unpleasant, disagreeable,\ncheerless, uneasy and reproachful place; in the end, it can only be described as\nmagnificent.\n—Time\nIncident or image setting up the thesis statement\nCanada is pink. I knew that from the map I owned when I was six. On it,\nNew York was green and brown, which was true as far as I could see, so there\nwas no reason to distrust the map maker’s portrayal of Canada. When my\nparents took me across the border and we entered the immigration booth, I\nlooked excitedly for the pink earth. Slowly it dawned on me: this foreign,\n―different‖ place was not so different.\nI discovered that the world in my head and the world at my feet were not the\nsame.\n—Robert Ornstein, Human Nature\nStartling opinion or question\nCaesar was right. Thin people need watching. I’ve been watching them for\nmost of my adult life, and I don’t like what I see. When these narrow fellows\nspring at me, I quiver to my toes. Thin people come in all personalities, most of\nthem menacing. You’ve got your ―together‖ thin person, your mechanical thin\nperson, your condescending thin person, your tsk-tsk thin per-son. All of them\nare dangerous.\n—Suzanne Britt,\n―That Lean and Hungry Look‖\nBackground, such as a historical comparison\nThroughout the first half of this century, the American Medical\nAssociation, the largest and most powerful medical organization in the world,\nbattled relentlessly to rid the country of quack potions and cure-alls; and it is\nthe AMA that is generally credited with being the single most powerful force\nbehind the enactment of the early pure food and drug laws. Today, however,\nmedicine’s guardian seems to have done a complete about-face and become one\nof the pharmaceutical industry’s staunchest allies—\noften at the public’s peril and expense.\n—Mac Jeffery, ―Does Rx Spell Rip-off?‖\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 50\nAn effective introductory paragraph need not be long, as the following opener shows:\nI’ve often wondered what goes into a hot dog. Now I know and I wish I didn’t.—William Zinsser,\nThe Lunacy Boom\nIneffective introductions\nWhen writing and revising an introductory paragraph, avoid the following approaches that are\nlikely to bore readers or make them question your sincerity or control:\n2 Closing an essay\nMost of your compositions will end with a closing statement or conclusion, a signal to readers\nthat you have not simply stopped writing but have actually finished. The conclusion completes an\nessay, bringing it to a climax while assuring readers that they have understood your intention.\nNote Compositions for the Web usually do not provide the kind of closure featured in essays.\nIn fact, you’ll need to ensure that your Web pages don’t dead-end, leaving the reader stranded\nwithout options for moving backward or forward through your material. (For more on Web\ncomposition, see pp. 832–38.)\nEffective conclusions\nAn essay conclusion may consist of a single sentence or a group of sentences, usually set off\nin a separate paragraph. The conclusion may take one or more of the following approaches:\nThe following paragraph concludes the essay on the Declaration of\nIndependence (the introduction appears on p. 104):\nThe Declaration of Independence has come to be a statement of this\nnation’s political philosophy, but that was not its purpose in 1776.\nJefferson’s passionate expression had to bow to the goals of the Congress as a\nwhole to forge unity among the colonies and to win the support of foreign\nnations.\n—Ann Weiss (student), ―The Editing\nof the Declaration of Independence‖\nMaxine Hong Kingston uses a different technique—a vivid im-age—to conclude an essay\nabout an aunt who committed suicide by drowning:\nMy aunt haunts me—her ghost drawn to me because now, after fifty years\nof neglect, I alone devote pages of paper to her, though not origamied into\nhouses and clothes. I do not think she always means me well. I am telling on\nher, and she was a spite suicide, drowning herself in the drinking water. The\nChinese are always very frightened of the drowned one, whose weeping ghost,\nwet hair hanging and skin bloated, waits silently by the water to pull down a\nsubstitute.\n—Maxine Hong Kingston,\n―No Name Woman‖\nIn the next paragraph the author concludes an essay on environmental protection with a call\nfor action:\nUntil we get the answers, I think we had better keep on building power\nplants and growing food with the help of fertilizers and such insect-controlling\nchemicals as we now have. The risks are well known, thanks to the\nenvironmentalists. If they had not created a widespread public awareness of the\necological crisis, we wouldn’t stand a chance. But such awareness by itself is\nnot enough. Flaming manifestos and prophecies of doom are no longer much\nhelp, and a search for scapegoats can only make matters worse. The time for\nsensations and manifestos is about over. Now we need rigorous analysis, united\neffort and very hard work.\n—Peter F. Drucker, ―How Best to\nProtect the Environment‖\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 51\nIneffective conclusions\nThe preceding examples illustrate ways of avoiding several pitfalls of conclusions:\n3 Using short emphatic or transitional paragraphs\nA short emphatic paragraph can give unusual stress to an important idea, in effect asking the\nreader to pause and consider before moving on.\nIn short, all those who might have taken responsibility ducked it, and catastrophe was inevitable.\nA transitional paragraph, because it is longer than a word or phrase and is set off by itself,\nmoves a discussion from one point to another more slowly or more completely than does a single\ntransitional expression or even a transitional sentence attached to a larger paragraph.\nThese, then, are the causes of the current contraction in hospital facilities. But how does this\ncontraction affect the medical costs of the government, private insurers, and individuals?\nSo the debates were noisy and emotion-packed. But what did they accomplish? Historians have\nidentified at least three direct results.\nUse transitional paragraphs only to shift readers’ attention when your essay makes a significant\nturn. A paragraph like the following one betrays a writer who is stalling:\nNow that we have examined these facts, we can look at some others that are equally central to an\nexamination of this important issue.\n4 Writing dialog\nWhen recording a conversation between two or more people, start a new paragraph for each\nperson’s speech. The paragraphing establishes for the reader the point at which one speaker stops\ntalking and another begins.\nThe dark shape was indistinguishable. But once I’d flooded him with light, there he stood, blinking.\n―Well,‖ he said eventually, ―you’re a sight for sore eyes. Should I stand here or are you going to let\nme in?‖\n―Come in,‖ I said. And in he came.\n—Louise Erdrich, The Beet Queen\nThough dialog appears most often in fictional writing (the source of the preceding example), it\nmay occasionally freshen or enliven narrative or expository essays. (For guidance in using quo-\ntation marks and other punctuation in passages of dialog, see pp. 444–46 and 471–72.)\nEXERCISE 4.17 Analyzing an introduction and conclusion\nAnalyze the introductory and concluding paragraphs in the first and final drafts of the student essay in\nChapter 3, pages 47–48 and 63–65. What is wrong with the first-draft paragraphs? Why are the final-draft\nparagraphs better? Could they be improved still further?\nEXERCISE 4.18 CONSIDERING YOUR PAST WORK:\nINTRODUCTIONS AND CONCLUSIONS\nExamine the opening and closing paragraphs of the essay you’ve been analyzing in Exercises 4.3, 4.10,\nand 4.15. Do the paragraphs fulfill the requirements and avoid the pitfalls outlined on pages 102–08?\nRevise them as needed for clarity, conciseness, focus, and interest.\n4e Linking paragraphs in the essay\nYour paragraphs do not stand alone: each one is a key unit of a larger piece of writing.\nThough you may draft paragraphs or groups of paragraphs almost as mini-essays, you will\neventually need to stitch them together into a unified, coherent, well-developed whole. The\ntechniques parallel those for linking sentences in paragraphs:\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 52\nMake sure each paragraph contributes to your thesis.\nArrange the paragraphs in a clear, logical order. See pages 32–43 for advice on essay\norganization.\nCreate links between paragraphs. Use repetition and restatement to stress and connect key\nterms, and use transitional expressions and transitional sentences to indicate sequence,\ndirection, contrast, and other relationships.\nThe essay ―A Picture of Hyperactivity‖ on pages 42–43 illustrates the first two of these\ntechniques. The following passages from the essay illustrate the third technique, with circled\nrepetitions and restatements, boxed transitional expressions, and transitional sentences noted in\nannotations.\nA hyperactive committee member can contribute to\nefficiency. A hyperactive salesperson can contribute to\nprofits. When children are hyperactive, though, people—even parents—may wish they had never been born. A\ncollage of those who must cope with hyperactivity in\nchildren is a picture of frustration, anger, and loss.\nThe first part of the collage is the doctors. In their terminology, the word hyperactivity has been replaced\nby ADHD, attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder. They\napply the term to children who are abnormally or excessively busy. . . .\nAs the mother of an ADHD child, I can say what the disorder means to the parents who form the second\npart of the collage. . . .\nThe weight of ADHD, however, does not rest on the doctors and parents. The darkest part of the collage\nbelongs to the children. . . .\nThe collage is complete, and it is dark and somber. ADHD, as applied to children, is a term with\nuncertain,\nunattractive, and bitter associations. The picture does\nhave one bright spot, however, for inside every ADHD\nchild is a lovely, trusting, calm person waiting to be\nrecognized.\nEXERCISE 4.19 Analyzing paragraphs in an essay\nAnalyze the ways in which paragraphs combine in the student essay in Chapter 3, pages 63–65. Where, if at\nall, does the writer seem to stray from the thesis or fail to show how paragraphs relate to it? How would\nyou revise the essay to solve any problems it exhibits?\nEXERCISE 4.20 Considering your past work:\nParagraphs in the essay\nExamine the overall effect of the essay you’ve been analyzing in Exercises 4.3, 4.10, 4.15, and 4.18. Do\nall the paragraphs relate to your thesis? Are they arranged clearly and logically? How do repetition and\nrestatement, transitional expressions, or transitional sentences connect the paragraphs? Can you see ways\nto improve the essay’s unity, coherence, and development?\n5\nCHAPTER\nDesigning\nDocuments\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 53\nImaginehowharditwouldbetoreadandwriteiftextlookedlikethis. To make reading and writing\neasier, we place a space between words. This convention and many others—such as page margins,\npage numbers, and paragraph breaks—have evolved over time to help writers communicate clearly\nwith readers.\n5a Designing academic papers and other documents\nThe design guidelines offered in this chapter apply to all types of documents, including\nacademic papers, Web sites, business reports, flyers, and newsletters. Each type has specific\nrequirements as well, covered elsewhere in this book.\n1 Designing academic papers\nMany academic disciplines prefer specific formats for students’ papers. This book details four\nsuch formats:\nMLA, used in English, foreign languages, and some other humanities (pp. 687–89).\nChicago, used in history, art history, religion, and some other humanities (pp. 775–77).\nAPA, used in the social sciences (pp. 800–03).\nCSE, used in some natural and applied sciences (p. 820).\nOther academic formats can be found in the style guides listed on pages 764, 784, and 812.\nThe design guidelines in this chapter extend the range of elements and options covered by\nmost academic styles. Your instructors may want you to adhere strictly to a particular style or may\nallow some latitude in design. Ask them for their preferences.\n2 Writing online\nIn and out of school, you are likely to do a lot of online writing—certainly e-mail and possibly\nWeb logs and other Web sites. The purposes and audiences for online writing vary widely, and so\ndo readers’ expectations for its design. Chapter 54 details the approaches you can take in different\nonline writing situations.\n3 Designing business documents and other public writing\nWhen you write outside your college courses, your audience will have certain expectations for\nhow your documents should look and read. Guidelines for such writing appear in the following\nchapters:\nChapter 55 on public writing: letters, job applications, reports, proposals, flyers,\nnewsletters, brochures.\nChapter 56 on oral presentations: PowerPoint slides and other visual aids.\n5b Considering principles of design\nMost of the principles of design respond to the ways we read. White space, for instance,\nrelieves our eyes and helps to lead us through a document. Groupings or lists help to show\nrelationships. Type sizes, images, and color add variety and help to emphasize important elements.\nThe sample documents on pages 114–15 illustrate quite different ways of presenting a report\nfor a marketing course. Even at a glance, the revised document is easier to scan and read. It makes\nbetter use of white space, groups similar elements, uses bullets and fonts for emphasis, and more\nsuccessfully integrates and explains the chart.\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 54\nAs you design your own documents, think about your purpose, the expectations of your\nreaders, and how readers will move through your document. Also consider the following general\nprinciples of design, noting how they overlap and support each other.\n1 Creating flow\nMany of the other design principles work in concert with the larger goal of conducting the\nreader through a document by establishing flow, a pattern for the eye to follow. In text-heavy\ndocuments like that on page 115, flow may be achieved mainly with headings, lists, and\nillustrations. In more visual documents, flow will come from the arrangement and spacing of\ninformation as well as from headings.\n2 Spacing\nThe white space on a page eases crowding and focuses readers’ attention. On an otherwise full\npage, just the space indicating paragraphs (an indention or a line of extra space) gives readers a\nbreak and reassures them that ideas are divided into manageable chunks.\nIn papers, reports, and other formal documents, spacing appears mainly in paragraph breaks,\nin margins, and around headings and lists. In publicity documents, such as flyers and brochures,\nspac-ing is usually more generous between elements and helps boxes, headings, and the like pop\noff the page.\n3 Grouping\nGrouping information shows relationships visually, reinforcing the sense of the text itself.\nHere in this discussion, we group the various principles of design under visually identical headings\nto emphasize them and their similar importance. In the revised design on page 115, the bulleted list\ndetails statistics about students’ computer use. The list uses similar wording for each item to\nreinforce the similarities in the data. Thinking of likely groups as you write can help you organize\nyour material so that it makes sense to you and your readers.\n4 Standardizing\nAs we read a document, the design of its elements quickly creates expectations in us. We\nassume, for instance, that headings in the same size and color signal information of the same\nimportance or that a list contains items of parallel content. Just as the design creates expectations,\nso it should fulfill them, treating similar elements similarly. Anticipating design standards as you\nwrite a document can help you treat its elements consistently and emphasize the elements you want\nto draw attention to.\nStandardizing also creates clear, uncluttered documents. Even if they are used consistently,\ntoo many variations in type fonts and sizes, colors, indentions, and the like overwhelm readers as\nthey try to determine the significance of the parts. Most formal documents, such as papers and\nreports, need no more than a single type font for text and headings, with type size and highlighting\n(such as CAPITAL LETTERS, boldface, or italics) distinguishing the levels of headings. Publicity\ndocuments, such as flyers and brochures, generally employ more variation to arrest readers’\nattention.\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 55\n5 Emphasizing\nPart of a critical reader’s task is to analyze and interpret the meaning of a document, and\ndesign helps the reader by stressing what’s important. Type fonts and sizes, headings, indentions,\ncolor, boxes, white space—all of these establish hierarchies of information, so that the reader\nalmost instinctively grasps what is crucial, what is less so, and what is merely supplementary. In\nthis book, for example, the importance of headings is clear from their size and from the use of\ndecorative elements, such as the boxes around 5c and the heading below; and boxes like the one on\npage 113 clearly mark summaries and other key information. As you design a document,\nconsidering where and how to emphasize elements can actually help you determine your\ndocument’s priorities.\n5c Using the elements of design\nApplying the preceding principles involves margins, text, lists, headings, color, and\nillustrations. You won’t use all these elements for every project, and in many writing situations you\nwill be required to follow a prescribed format (see pp. 111–12). If you are addressing readers who\nhave vision disabilities, consider as well the points discussed on pages 125–26.\nNote Your word processor may provide wizards or templates for many kinds of documents,\nsuch as letters, memos, reports, agen-\ndas, résumés, and brochures. Wizards guide you through setting up and writing complicated\ndocuments. Templates are preset forms to which you add your own text, headings, and other\nelements. Wizards and templates can be helpful, but not if they lead you to create cookie-cutter\ndocuments no matter what the writing situation. Al-\nways keep in mind that a document should be appropriate for your subject, audience, and purpose.\n1 Setting margins\nMargins at the top, bottom, and sides of a page help to prevent the pages from overwhelming\nreaders with unpleasant crowding. Most academic and business documents use a minimum one-\ninch margin on all sides. Publicity documents, such as flyers and bro-chures, often use narrower\nmargins, compensating with white space between elements.\n2 Creating readable text\nA document must be readable. You can make text readable by attending to line spacing, type\nfonts and sizes, highlighting, word spacing, and line breaks.\nLine spacing\nMost academic documents are double-spaced, with an initial in-\ndention for paragraphs, while most business documents are single-spaced, with an extra line of\nspace between paragraphs. Double or triple spacing sets off headings in both. Web sites and\npublicity documents, such as flyers and brochures, tend to use more line spacing to separate and\ngroup distinct parts of the content.\nType fonts and sizes\nThe readability of text also derives from the type fonts (or faces) and their sizes. For academic\nand business documents, generally choose a type size of 10 or 12 points, as in these samples:\n10-point Courier 10-point Times New Roman\n12-point Courier 12-point Times New Roman\nFonts like these and the one you’re reading have serifs—the small lines finishing the letters, such\nas the downward strokes on the top of this T. Serif fonts are appropriate for formal writing and are\neasier for most people to read on paper. Sans serif fonts (sans means ―without‖ in French) include\nthis one found on many word processors:\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 56\n10-point Arial 12-point Arial\nSans serif fonts are usually easier to read on a computer screen and are clearer on paper for readers\nwith some vision disabilities (see p. 126).\nYour word processor probably offers many decorative fonts as well:\n10-point Bodega Sans 10-point Tekton\n10-POINT STENCIL 10-point Ruzicka Freehand\n10-point Lucinda Sans 10-point Park Avenue\nDecorative fonts are generally inappropriate in academic and business writing, where letter forms\nshould be conventional and regular. But on some Web sites and in publicity documents, decorative\nfonts can attract attention, create motion, and reinforce a theme.\nNote The point size of a type font is often an unreliable guide to its actual size, as the\ndecorative fonts above illustrate: all the samples are 10 points, but they vary considerably. Before\nyou use a font, print out a sample to be sure it is the size you want.\nHighlighting\nWithin a document’s text, underlined, italic, boldface, or even color type can emphasize key\nwords or sentences. Underlining is rarest these days, having been replaced by italics in all but a few\ndisciplines. (It remains called for in MLA style. See p. 660.) Both aca-\ndemic and business writing sometimes use boldface to give strong emphasis—for instance, to a\nterm being defined—and publicity documents often rely extensively on boldface to draw the\nreader’s eye. Neither academic nor business writing generally uses color within passages of text. In\nWeb and publicity documents, however, color may be effective if the color is dark enough to be\nreadable.\n(See p. 120 for more on color in document design.)\nNo matter what your writing situation, use highlighting selectively to complement your\nmeaning, not merely to decorate your work. Many readers consider type embellishments to be\ndistracting.\nWord spacing\nIn most writing situations, follow these guidelines for spacing within and between words:\nLeave one space between words.\nLeave one space after all punctuation, with these exceptions:\nDash (two hyphens or the so-called\nem dash on a computer) book--its book----its\nHyphen one-half\nApostrophe within a word book’s\nTwo or more adjacent marks book.’’)\nOpening quotation mark, paren-\nthesis, or bracket (‘‘book [book\nLeave one space before and after an ellipsis mark. In the examples below, ellipsis marks\nindicate omissions within a sentence and at the end of a sentence. See pages 484–86for\nadditional examples.\nbook . . . in\nbook. . . . The\nLine breaks\nYour word processor will generally insert appropriate breaks between lines of continuous text:\nit will not, for instance, automatically begin a line with a comma or period, and it will not end a\nline with an opening parenthesis or bracket. However, you will have to prevent it from breaking a\ntwo-hyphen dash or a three-dot ellipsis mark by spacing to push the beginning of each mark to the\nnext line.\nWhen you instruct it to do so (usually under the Tools menu), your word processor will also\nautomatically hyphenate words to prevent very short lines. If you must decide yourself where to\nbreak words, follow the guidelines on page 556.\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 57\n3 Using lists\nLists give visual reinforcement to the relations between like items—for example, the steps in a\nprocess or the elements of a proposal. A list is easier to read than a paragraph and adds white space\nto the page.\nWhen wording a list, work for parallelism among items—for instance, all\ncomplete sentences or all phrases (see also p. 408). Set the list with space\nabove and below. Number the items, or mark them with bullets: centered\ndots or other devices, such as the squares used in the list below about\nheadings. On most word processors you can format a numbered or bulleted\nlist automatically using the Format menu.\n4 Using headings\nHeadings are signposts: they direct the reader’s attention by focusing the eye on a document’s\nmost significant content. In Web and publicity documents, headings may be decorative as well as\nfunctional, capturing the reader’s attention with large sizes, lots of white space, and unconventional\nfonts. In academic and much business writing, however, headings are more purely functional. They\nbreak the text into discrete parts, create emphasis, and orient the reader.\nWhen you use headings in academic and business writing, follow these guidelines:\nUse one, two, or three levels of headings depending on the needs of your material and the\nlength of your document. Some level of heading every two or so pages will help keep readers\non track. (A three-page paper or a one-page letter probably will not need headings.)\nCreate an outline of your document to plan where headings should go. Reserve the first\nlevel of heading for the main points (and sections). Use a second and perhaps a third level of\nheading to mark subsections of supporting information.\nKeep headings as short as possible while making them specific about the material that\nfollows.\nWord headings consistently—for instance, all questions (What Is the Scientific Method?), all\nphrases with -ing words (Under-\nstanding the Scientific Method), or all phrases with nouns (The Scientific Method).\nIndicate the relative importance of headings with type size, positioning, and highlighting,\nsuch as capital letters or boldface.\nFirst-Level Heading\nSecond-Level Heading\nThird-Level Heading\nGenerally, you can use the same type font and size for headings as for the text.\nDon’t break a page immediately after a heading. Push the heading to the next page.\nNote Document format in psychology and some other social sciences requires a particular\ntreatment of headings. See pages 800–03.\n5 Using color\nWith a color printer, most word processors and desktop publishers can produce documents\nthat use color for bullets, headings, borders, boxes, illustrations, and other elements. Web and\npublicity documents almost always use color, whereas academic and business documents consisting\nonly of text and headings may not need color. (Ask your instructor or supervisor for his or her\npreferences.) If you do use color, follow these guidelines:\nEmploy color to clarify and highlight your content. Too much color or too many colors on a\npage will distract rather than focus readers’ attention.\nMake sure that color type is readable. For text, where type is likely to be relatively small,\nuse only dark colors. For headings, lighter colors may be readable if the type is large and\nboldfaced.\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 58\nStick to the same color for all headings at the same level—for instance, red for primary\nheadings, black for secondary headings.\nUse color for bullets, lines, and other nontext elements. But use no more than a few colors\nto keep pages clean.\nUse color to distinguish the parts of illustrations—the segments of charts, the lines of\ngraphs, and the parts of diagrams. Use only as many colors as you need to make your\nillustration clear. (See below.)\nSee also page 126 on the use of color for readers who have vision disabilities.\nEXERCISE 5.1 Redesigning a paper\nSave a duplicate copy of a recent paper or one you are currently working on. Then format the duplicate\nusing appropriate elements of design, such as type fonts, lists, and headings. (For a new paper, be sure\nyour instructor will accept your new design.) When you have finished the redesign, share the work with\nyour instructor.\n5d Using illustrations\nIllustrations can often make a point for you more efficiently than words can. Tables present\ndata. Figures (such as graphs and charts) usually recast data in visual form. Diagrams, drawings,\nphotographs, and clip art can explain processes, represent what something looks like, add\nemphasis, or convey a theme.\nNote The Web is an excellent resource for photographs (see pp. 594–96), and you can edit the\nimages with a program such as Adobe Photoshop or Corel Paint Shop. Your word processor may\ninclude a program for creating tables, graphs, diagrams, and other illustrations, or you can work\nwith specialized software such as Excel or Quattro Pro (for graphs and charts) or Adobe Illustrator\nor CorelDRAW (for diagrams, maps, and the like). Use PowerPoint or a similar program for visuals\nin oral presentations (see pp. 860–62).\n1 Using illustrations appropriately for the writing situation\nAcademic and many business documents tend to use illustrations differently from publicity\ndocuments. In the latter, illustrations generally attract attention, enliven, or emphasize, and they\nmay not be linked directly to the document’s text. In academic and business writing, however,\nillustrations directly reinforce and amplify the text. Follow these guidelines for academic and most\nbusiness writing:\nFocus on a purpose for your illustration—a reason for including it and a point you want it to\nmake. Otherwise, readers may find it irrelevant or confusing.\nProvide a source note whenever the data or the entire illustration is someone else’s\nindependent material (see p. 633). Each discipline has a slightly different style for such\nsource notes: those in the illustrations on the next four pages reflect MLA style for English\nand some other humanities. See also Chapters 47 and 51–53.\nNumber figures, photographs, and other images together, and label them as figures: Fig. 1,\nFig. 2, and so on.\nNumber and label tables separately: Table 1, Table 2, and so on.\nRefer to each illustration in your text—for instance, ―See\nfig. 2.‖ Place the text reference at the point(s) in the text where readers will benefit by\nconsulting the illustration.\nDetermine the placement of illustrations. The social sciences and some other disciplines\nrequire each illustration to fall on a page by itself immediately after the text reference to it (see\np. 803). You may want to follow this rule in other situations as well if you have a large\nnumber of illustrations. Otherwise, you can place them on your text pages just after their\nreferences.\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 59\n2 Using tables\nTables usually present raw data, making complex information accessible to readers. The data\nmay show how variables relate to one another, how two or more groups contrast, or how variables\nchange over time. The table on the following page emphasizes the last two functions.\nSome tables use rows and columns to present related textual information rather than data. An\nexample appears on page 145.\n3 Using figures\nFigures represent data or show concepts visually. They include charts, graphs, and diagrams.\nPie charts\nPie charts show the relations among the parts of a whole. The whole totals 100 percent, and\neach pie slice is proportional in size to its share of the whole. Use a pie chart when shares, not the\nunderlying data, are your focus.\nBar charts\nBar charts compare groups or time periods on a measure such as quantity or frequency. Use a\nbar chart when relative size is your focus.\nLine graphs\nLine graphs show change over time in one or more subjects. They are an economical and\nhighly visual way to compare many points of data.\nDiagrams\nDiagrams show concepts visually, such as the structure of an organization, the way something\nworks or looks, or the relations among subjects. Often, diagrams show what can’t be described\neconomically in words. For other examples of diagrams, see pages 3, 103, and 833.\nPhotographs and other images\nSometimes you may focus an entire paper on an image such\nas a photograph, painting, or advertisement (see pp. 175–77). But most commonly you’ll use\nimages to add substance to ideas or to enliven them. You might clarify an astronomy paper with a\nphotograph of Saturn (see opposite), add information to an analysis of a novel with a drawing of\nthe author, or capture the theme of a brochure with a cartoon. Images grab readers’ attention, so use\nthem carefully to explain, reinforce, or enhance your writing.\nOne kind of image rarely appears in academic writing: clip art, consisting of icons and\ndrawings such as those below from the free site Barry’s Clipart Server\n(http://www.barrysclipart.com/index.php):\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 60\nMany word processors provide files of clip art, and they are also available from CD-ROMs\nand Web sites (links appear on this book’s Web site at ablongman.com/littlebrown). Be selective in\nusing these resources: clip art is mostly decorative (which is why it seldom appears in academic\nand business documents), and an overdecorated document is not only cluttered but unemphatic. In\na Web or publicity document, use only clip art that is relevant to your theme and content, directing\nreaders’ attention to elements you want to stress.\nNote When using an image prepared by someone else—for in-stance, a photograph or an item\nof clip art downloaded from the Web —you must verify that the source permits reproduction of the\nim-age before you use it. In most documents but especially academic papers, you must also fully\ncite the source of any borrowed image. See pages 635–37 on copyright issues with Internet sources.\n5e Considering readers with disabilities\nYour audience may include readers who have low vision, problems with color perception, or\ndifficulties processing visual information. If so, consider adapting your design to meet these\nreaders’ needs. Here are a few pointers:\nUse large type fonts. Most guidelines call for 14 points or larger.\nUse standard type fonts. Many people with low vision find it easier to read sans serif fonts\nsuch as Arial than serif fonts (see p. 117). Avoid decorative fonts with unusual flourishes,\neven in headings.\nAvoid words in ALL-CAPITAL LETTERS.\nAvoid relying on color alone to distinguish elements. Label elements, and distinguish them\nby position or size.\nUse red and green selectively. To readers who are red-green colorblind, these colors will\nappear in shades of gray, yellow, or blue.\nUse contrasting colors. To make colors distinct, choose them from opposite sides of the color\nspectrum—violet and yellow, for instance, or orange and blue.\nUse only light colors for tints behind type. Make the type itself black or a very dark color.\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 61\nVisit the companion Web site for more help with discovering and shaping\nideas.\nTechniques for developing a subject\nKeep a journal (below).\nObserve your surroundings (p. 19).\nFreewrite (p. 20).\nMake a list or brainstorm (p. 21).\nCluster (p. 23).\nUse the journalist’s questions (p. 23).\nUse the patterns of development (p. 24).\nRead (p. 25).\nThink critically (p. 26).\nClustering\nFunctions of the thesis statement\nThe thesis statement narrows your subject to a single, central idea that you want readers to gain\nfrom your essay.\nIt claims something specific and significant about your subject, a claim that requires support.\nIt conveys your purpose, your reason for writing.\nIt often concisely previews the arrangement of ideas.\nChecklist for revising the thesis statement\nHow well does the subject of your statement capture the subject of your paper?\nWhat claim does your statement make about your subject?\nWhat is the significance of the claim? How does it answer ―So what?‖ and convey your purpose?\nHow can the claim be limited or made more specific? Does it state a single idea and clarify the\nboundaries of the idea?\nHow unified is the statement? How does each word and phrase contribute to a single idea?\nTree diagram\nPrinciples of the formal outline\nLabels and indentions indicate order and relative importance.\nSections and subsections reflect logical relationships.\nTopics of equal generality appear in parallel headings.\nEach subdivision has at least two parts.\nHeadings are expressed in parallel grammatical form.\nThe introduction and conclusion may be omitted (though not, of course, from the essay).\nSchemes for organizing ideas in an essay\nSpace\nTime\nEmphasis\nGeneral to specific Increasing importance (climax)\nSpecific to general Decreasing familiarity\nProblem-solution Increasing complexity\nIntroduction\nestablishing subject of essay\nThesis\nstatement\nParagraph idea, linked\nto thesis\nstatement\nParagraph developed with evidence supporting its idea\nParagraph idea, linked\nto thesis\nstatement\nParagraph developed with evidence supporting its idea\nTransition\nParagraph idea, linked\nto thesis\nstatement\nParagraph developed with evidence supporting its idea\nConclusion echoing thesis statement, summarizing, and looking ahead\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 62\nVisit the companion Web site for more help with drafting, revising, collaborating, and preparing a writing\nportfolio.\nWays to start drafting\nRead over what you’ve already written—notes, outlines, and so on. Immediately start your draft\nwith whatever comes to mind.\nFreewrite (see p. 20).\nWrite scribbles or type nonsense until words you can use start\ncoming.\nPretend you’re writing to a friend about your subject.\nDescribe an image that represents your subject—a physical object, a facial expression, two people\narguing over something, a giant machine gouging the earth for a mine, whatever.\nWrite a paragraph. Explain what you think your essay will be about when you finish it.\nSkip the opening and start in the middle. Or write the conclusion.\nStart writing the part that you understand best or feel most strongly about. Using your outline,\ndivide your essay into chunks—say, one for the introduction, another for the first point, and so on.\nOne of these chunks may call out to be written.\nWays to keep drafting\nSet aside enough time for yourself. For a brief essay, a first draft is likely to take at least an hour or\ntwo.\nWork in a quiet place.\nMake yourself comfortable.\nIf you must stop working, write down what you expect to do next. Then you can pick up where\nyou stopped with minimal disruption.\nBe as fluid as possible, and don’t worry about mistakes. Spontaneity will allow your attitudes\ntoward your subject to surface naturally in your sentences, and it will also make you receptive to ideas\nand relations you haven’t seen before. Mistakes will be easier to find and correct later, when you’re\nnot also trying to create.\nKeep going. Skip over sticky spots; leave a blank if you can’t find the right word; put alternative\nideas or phrasings in brackets so that you can consider them later without bogging down. If an idea\npops out of nowhere but doesn’t seem to fit in, quickly jot it down on a separate sheet, or write it into\nthe draft and bracket or boldface it for later attention. You can use an asterisk (*) or some other\nsymbol to mark places where you feel blocked or uncertain. (With a word processor, you can later\nreturn to these places by using the Find command to\nlocate the symbol.)\nResist self-criticism. Don’t worry about your style, grammar, spelling, punctuation, and the like.\nDon’t worry about what your readers will think. These are very important matters, but save them for\nrevision. On a word processor, help yourself resist self-criticism by turning off automatic spelling- or\ngrammar-checking functions or by trying invisible writing (p. 20).\nUse your thesis statement and outline to remind you of your planned purpose, organization, and\ncontent.\nBut don’t feel constrained by your thesis and outline. If your writing leads you in a more\ninteresting direction, follow.\nWays to gain distance from your work\nTake a break after finishing the draft. A few hours may be enough; a whole night or day is\npreferable. The break will clear your mind, relax you, and give you some objectivity.\nAsk someone to read and react to your draft. Many writing instructors ask their students to submit\ntheir first drafts so that the instructor and, often, the other members of the class can serve as an actual\naudience to help guide revision. (See also pp. 68–69 on receiving and benefiting from comments.)\nType a handwritten draft. The act of transcription can reveal gaps in content or problems in\nstructure.\nPrint out a word-processed draft. You’ll be able to view all pages of the draft at once, and the\ndifferent medium can reveal weaknesses you didn’t see on screen.\nOutline your draft. Highlight the main points supporting the thesis, and write these sentences down\nseparately in outline form. (If you’re working on a word processor, you can copy and paste these\nsentences.) Then examine the outline you’ve made for logical order, gaps, and digressions. A formal\noutline can be especially illuminating because of its careful structure. (See pp. 33–38 for a discussion\nof outlining.)\nListen to your draft. Read the draft out loud to yourself or a friend or classmate, read it into a tape\nrecorder and play the tape, or have someone read the draft to you. Experiencing your words with ears\ninstead of eyes can alter your perceptions.\nEase the pressure. Don’t try to re-see everything in your draft at once. Use a checklist like the one on\np. 51, making a separate pass through the draft for each item.\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 63\nChecklist for revision\nSee also specific revision checklists for arguments (pp. 213–14), research papers (p. 645), and literary\nanalyses (p. 749).\nPurpose\nWhat is the essay’s purpose? Does that purpose conform to the assignment? Is it consistent throughout the\npaper? (See pp. 13–15.)\nThesis\nWhat is the thesis of the essay? Where does it become clear? How well do thesis and paper match: Does\nthe paper stray from the thesis? Does it fulfill the commitment of the thesis? (See pp. 27–31.)\nStructure\nWhat are the main points of the paper? (List them.) How well does each support the thesis? How effective\nis their arrangement for the paper’s purpose? (See pp. 32–33, 38–42.)\nDevelopment\nHow well do details, examples, and other evidence support each main point? Where, if at all, might\nreaders find support skimpy or have trouble understanding the content? (See pp. 16–26, 90–91.)\nTone\nWhat is the tone of the paper? How appropriate is it for the purpose, topic, and intended readers? Where\nis it most and least successful? (See p. 12.)\nUnity\nWhat does each sentence and paragraph contribute to the thesis? Where, if at all, do digressions occur?\nShould these be cut, or can they be rewritten to support the thesis? (See pp. 41–43, 72–75.)\nCoherence\nHow clearly and smoothly does the paper flow? Where does it seem rough or awkward? Can any\ntransitions be improved? (See pp. 41–43, 77–78.)\nTitle, introduction, conclusion\nHow accurately and interestingly does the title reflect the essay’s content? (See opposite.) How well does\nthe introduction engage and focus readers’ attention? (See pp. 102–06.) How effective is the conclusion\nin providing a sense of completion? (See pp. 106–09.)\nYou can download this checklist from ablongman.com/littlebrown. Save the list in a file of its own,\nduplicate the file for each writing project, and insert appropriate answers between the questions along\nwith ideas for changes. Print the expanded list so it’s handy as you revise.\nSave menu\nSelect File to create new documents, to save documents and choose where to store them, and to open\nexisting documents.\nSelect Save to preserve changes in an existing document or to save, name, and file a new document.\nSelect Save As to copy a document and rename the copy or to copy a document in\na new location. The original remains intact.\nSelect Versions to save and time-stamp stages of a document under one file name.\nSave As screen\nSave As copies an existing document.\nSave the file in an existing folder if it relates to others already in the folder.\nSelect the New Folder icon if the document doesn’t fit in existing folders—for instance, the first draft of a paper or the first\npaper in a course.\nGive the copied document a distinctive name.\nTrack Changes function\nUse Save As from the File menu to copy and rename the original document. Otherwise, you’re likely to read what you\nintended to write but didn’t.\nRead as if you are encountering the draft for the first time. Put yourself in the reader’s place.\nHave a classmate, friend, or relative read your work. Make sure you understand and consider the\nreader’s suggestions, even if eventually you decide not to take them.\nRead the draft aloud or, even better, record it. Listen for awkward rhythms, repetitive sentence\npatterns, and missing or clumsy transitions.\nLearn from your own experience. Keep a record of the problems that others have pointed out in\nyour writing. (See p. 69 for a suggested format.) When editing, check your work against this record.\nChecklist for editing\nClarity\nHow well do words and sentences convey their intended meanings? Which words and sentences are\nconfusing? Check especially for these:\nExact language (pp. 518–28)\nParallelism (pp. 405–11)\nClear modifiers (pp. 364–72)\nClear reference of pronouns (pp. 350–56)\nComplete sentences (pp. 334–40)\nSentences separated correctly (pp. 342–48)\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 65\nEffectiveness\nHow well do words and sentences engage and focus readers? Where does the writing seem wordy,\nchoppy, or dull? Check especially for these:\nEmphasis of main ideas (pp. 384–93)\nSmooth and informative transitions (pp. 85–88, 108)\nVariety in sentence length and structure (pp. 412–19)\nAppropriate language (pp. 510–17)\nConcise sentences (pp. 529–35)\nCorrectness\nHow little or how much do surface errors interfere with clarity and effectiveness? Check especially\nfor these:\nSpelling (pp. 542–54)\nPronoun forms, especially subjective (he, she, they, who) vs. objective (him, her, them, whom) (pp. 267–\n74)\nVerb forms, especially -s and -ed endings, correct forms of irregular verbs, and appropriate helping verbs\n(pp. 275–92)\nVerb tenses, especially consistency (pp. 292–98, 359–60)\nAgreement between subjects and verbs, especially when words come between them or the subject is each,\neveryone, or a similar word (pp. 305–12)\nAgreement between pronouns and antecedents, especially when the antecedent contains or or the\nantecedent is each, everyone, person, or a similar word (pp. 131–17)\nSentence fragments (pp. 334–40)\nCommas, especially with comma splices (pp. 342–47), with and or but (432), with introductory elements\n(433–34), with nonessential elements (435–38), and with series (441–42)\nApostrophes in possessives but not plural nouns (Dave’s/witches) and\nin contractions but not possessive personal pronouns (it’s/its) (pp. 461–66)\nYou can download this checklist from ablongman.com/littlebrown. Save the list in a file of its own,\nduplicate the file for each writing project, and insert appropriate answers between the questions along\nwith notes on specific changes to make.\nSpelling checker\nA spelling checker failed to catch any of the thirteen errors in this jingle. Can you spot them?\nGrammar/style checker\nThe checker flagged only one repetition of light, not the other three.\nThe checker flagged this sentence because it is long, but it is\nactually clear and\ncorrect.\nTechniques for proofreading\nRead printed copy, even if you will eventually submit the paper electronically. Most people\nproofread more accurately when reading type on paper than when reading it on a computer screen. (At\nthe same time, don’t view the printed copy as necessarily error-free just because it’s clean. Clean-\nlooking copy may still harbor errors.)\nRead the paper aloud. Slowly and distinctly pronounce exactly what you see.\nPlace a ruler under each line as you read it.\nRead “against copy.” Compare your final draft one sentence at a time against the edited draft you\ncopied it from.\nIgnore content. To keep the content of your writing from distracting you while you proofread, read\nthe essay backward, end to beginning, examining each sentence as a separate unit. Or, taking\nadvantage of a computer, isolate each paragraph from its context by printing it on a separate page. (Of\ncourse, reassemble the paragraphs before submitting the paper.)\nDescriptive title\nIntroduction\nQuestion to be addressed\nSummary of Kadi’s essay\nThesis\nstatement\nExplanation of Internet’s anonymity\nPresentation of survey conducted to gauge use of\ninvented screen names\nExplanation of survey method\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 66\nSummary of\nsurvey results\nDo not use invented names (22%)\nUse invented names to avoid revealing personal details (30%)\nGraph displaying survey\nresults, with self-explanatory labels and caption\nUse invented names for other reasons (11%)\nUse invented names to protect privacy (37%)\nFig. 1. Use of invented screen names among seventy-eight Internet\nusers.\nFirst main point: We are not prejudged by others.\nExamples of first point\nQualification of first point\nConclusion of first point\nSecond main point: We cannot prejudge others.\nClarification of second point\nExamples of\nsecond point\nEffects of\nassumptions\nConclusion of second point\nConclusion, summarizing\nessay\nWork cited in MLA style (see\np. 656)\nCommenting on others’ writing\nBe sure you know what the writer is saying. If necessary, summarize the paper to understand its\ncontent. (See pp. 140–42.)\nAddress only your most significant concerns with the work. Use the revision checklist on p. 51 as\na guide to what is significant. Unless you have other instructions, ignore mistakes in grammar,\npunctuation, spelling, and the like. (The temptation to focus on such errors may be especially strong if\nthe writer is less experienced than you are with standard American English.) Emphasizing mistakes\nwill contribute little to the writer’s revision.\nRemember that you are the reader, not the writer. Don’t edit sentences, add details, or otherwise\nassume responsibility for the paper.\nPhrase your comments carefully. Avoid misunderstandings by making sure comments are both\nclear and respectful. If you are responding on paper or online, not face to face with the writer,\nremember that the writer has nothing but your written words to go on. He or she can’t ask you for\nimmediate clarification and can’t infer your attitudes from gestures, facial expressions, and tone of\nvoice.\nBe specific. If something confuses you, say why. If you disagree with a conclusion, say why.\nBe supportive as well as honest. Tell the writer what you like about the paper. Word comments\npositively: instead of This paragraph doesn’t interest me, say You have an interesting detail here that\nI almost missed. Comment in a way that emphasizes the effect of the work on you, the reader: This\nparagraph confuses me because. . . . And avoid measuring the work against a set of external\nstandards: This essay is poorly organized. Your thesis statement is inadequate.\nWhile reading, make your comments in writing. Even if you will be delivering your comments in\nperson later on, the written record will help you recall what you thought.\nLink comments to specific parts of a paper. Especially if you are reading the paper on a computer,\nbe clear about what part of the paper each comment relates to. You can embed your comments\ndirectly into the paper, distinguishing them with highlighting or color. Or you can use the Comment\nfunction of a word processor (see below).\nComment function\nUse Save As from the File menu to copy and rename the document. The reviewer added RevPotter to the original\ndocument name.\nClicking on highlighted text shows the comment in a pop-up box.\nMake comments spe- cific and supportive.\nHighlight text to be commented on by clicking and dragging the cursor across it.\nBenefiting from comments on your writing\nThink of your readers as counselors or coaches. They can help you see the virtues and flaws in\nyour work and sharpen your awareness of readers’ needs.\nRead or listen to comments closely.\nKnow what the critic is saying. If you need more information, ask for it, or consult the appropriate\nsection of this handbook.\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 67\nDon’t become defensive. Letting comments offend you will only erect a barrier to improvement in\nyour writing. As one writing teacher advises, ―Leave your ego at the door.‖\nRevise your work in response to appropriate comments. Whether or not you are required to act on\ncomments, you will learn more from actually revising than from just thinking about it.\nRemember that you are the final authority on your work. You should be open to suggestions, but\nyou are free to decline advice when you think it is inappropriate.\nKeep track of both the strengths and the weaknesses others identify. Then in later assignments\nyou can build on your successes and give special attention to problem areas.\nRecord of errors\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 68\nVisit the companion Web site for more help and additional exercises on paragraphs .\nGeneral statement relating to thesis: announces topic of paragraph\nFour specific examples, all providing evidence for general statement\nChecklist for revising paragraphs\nIs the paragraph unified? place\nabove, adjacent to, below, elsewhere, farther on, here, near, nearby, on the other side, opposite to, there, to\nthe east, to the left\nTo indicate time\nafter a while, afterward, as long as, as soon as, at last, at length, at that time, before, earlier, formerly,\nimmediately, in the meantime, in the past, lately, later, meanwhile, now, presently, shortly,\nsimultaneously, since, so far, soon, subsequently, then, thereafter, until, when\nTo repeat, summarize, or conclude\nall in all, altogether, as has been said, in brief, in conclusion, in other words, in particular, in short, in\nsimpler terms, in summary, on the whole, that is, therefore, to put it differently, to summarize\nTo show cause or effect\naccordingly, as a result, because, consequently, for this purpose, hence, otherwise, since, then, therefore,\nthereupon, thus, to this end, with this object\nNote Draw carefully on the preceding list of transitional expressions\nbecause the ones in each group are not Don’t extend your reach too wide with a line such as Throughout\nhuman history . . . or In today’s world. . . . Readers can do without the warm-up.\nA flat announcement. Don’t start with The purpose of this essay is . . . , In this essay I will . . . , or\nany similar presentation of your intention or topic.\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 71\nA reference to the essay’s title. Don’t refer to the title of the essay in the first sentence—for\nexample, This is a big problem or This book is about the history of the guitar.\nAccording to Webster. . . . Don’t start by citing a dictionary definition. A definition can be an\neffective springboard to an essay, but this kind of lead-in has become dull with overuse.\nAn apology. Don’t fault your opinion or your knowledge with I’m not sure if I’m right, but . . . ; I\ndon’t know much about this, but . . . ; or a similar line.\nSome strategies for closing paragraphs\nRecommend a course of action.\nSummarize the paper.\nEcho the approach of the intro-\nduction.\nRestate your thesis and reflect\non its implications.\nStrike a note of hope or despair.\nGive a symbolic or powerful fact or other detail.\nGive an especially compelling\nexample.\nCreate an image that represents\nyour subject.\nUse a quotation.\nEcho of introduction: contrast between past and present\nRestatement and\nelaboration of thesis\nSummary\nImage\nSummary and opinion\nCall for action\nClosings to avoid\nA repeat of the introduction. Don’t simply replay your introduction. The conclusion should capture\nwhat the paragraphs of the body have added to the introduction.\nA new direction. Don’t introduce a subject different from the one your essay has been about. If you\narrive at a new idea, this may be a signal to start fresh with that idea as your thesis.\nA sweeping generalization. Don’t conclude more than you reasonably can from the evidence you\nhave presented. If your essay is about your frustrating experience trying to clear a parking ticket, you\ncannot reasonably conclude that all local police forces are tied up in red tape.\nAn apology. Don’t cast doubt on your essay. Don’t say, Even though I’m no expert or This may not\nbe convincing, but I believe it’s true or anything similar. Rather, to win your readers’ confidence,\ndisplay confidence.\nIntroduction establishing subject and stating thesis\nThesis statement\nTransitional topic sentence relating to thesis statement\nTransitional topic sentence relating to thesis statement\nTransitional sentence\nTopic sentence relating to thesis statement\nTransitional sentence into conclusion, restating\nthesis statement\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 72\nVisit the companion Web site for more help with document design.\nPrinciples of document design\nCreate flow to conduct the reader through the document.\nSpace elements to give the reader’s eye a rest and to focus the reader’s attention.\nGroup related elements in lists or under similar headings.\nStandardize elements to match appearance with content and to minimize variations.\nEmphasize important elements.\nOriginal design\nRuns title and subtitle\ntogether. Does not\ndistinguish title from text.\nCrowds the page with minimal margins.\nDownplays paragraph breaks with small\nindentions.\nBuries statistics in a paragraph. Obscures\nrelationships with nonparallel wording.\nDoes not introduce the figure, leaving readers to infer its meaning and purpose.\nOveremphasizes the figure with large size and excessive white space.\nPresents the figure undynamically, flat on.\nDoes not caption the\nfigure to explain what\nit shows, offering only\na figure number and a\npartial text explanation.\nRevised design\nDistinguishes title from subtitle and both from text.\nProvides adequate\nmargins.\nEmphasizes paragraph breaks with white space.\nGroups statistics in a bulleted list set off with white space. Uses parallel wording for parallel information.\nIntroduces the figure to indicate its meaning and purpose.\nReduces white space around the figure.\nPresents the figure to emphasize the most\nsignificant segment.\nCaptions the figure so that it can be read\nindependently from the text.\nTable\nA self-explanatory title falls above the table.\nTable 1\nPercentage of Young Adults Living at Home, 1960-\n2000\n1960 1970 1980 1990 2000\nMales\nAge 18-24 52 54 54 58 57\nAge 25-34 9 9 10 15 13\nFemales\nAge 18-24 35 41 43 48 47\nAge 25-34 7 7 7 8 8\nSource: Data from United States, Dept. of\nCommerce, Census Bureau,\nCensus 2000 Summary Tables, 1 July 2002\n<http:www.census.gov/\nservlet/QTTTable?_ts=30543101060>.\nSelf-explanatory headings label horizontal rows and vertical columns.\nThe layout of rows and columns is clear: headings align with their data, and numbers\nalign vertically down columns.\nPie chart\n\nFOWLcp02-05.doc - 73\nColor distinguishes segments of the chart.
