The basic aim of this community—whether in English, psychology,\nbiology, or some other discipline—is to contribute to and build knowledge through questioning,\nresearch, and communication. The differences among disciplines lie mainly in the kinds of\nquestions asked, the kinds of research done to find the answers, and the ways of communicating the\nanswers.\nAcademic writers communicate using conventional forms, such as case studies, research\nreports, and reviews of others’ writings on a particular subject. Both a discipline’s concerns and the\nkind of writing create the writing situation, which in turn shapes a writer’s choice of subject,\nconception of audience, definition of purpose, choice of structure and content, and even choice of\nlanguage. This chapter introduces academic writing situations in general. Chapters 50–53 detail the\nparticular goals and expectations of the humanities, the social sciences, and the natural and applied\nsciences.\n6a Becoming an academic writer\nAs an academic writer, you participate in a discipline community first by studying a subject,\nacquiring its vocabulary, and learning to express yourself in its ways. As you gain experience and\nknowledge, you begin to contribute to the community by asking questions and communicating your\nanswers. Such a contribution may seem beyond you at first, as you try to grasp the content of\nassigned reading and identify important ideas. In any discipline, making the transition to academic\nwriting will be easier if you practice the strategies outlined in the box on the facing page.\n6b Analyzing audience\nSome of your writing assignments may specify an identifiable group of readers—for instance,\nfellow students, the city council, or the editors of a newspaper. Such readers’ needs and\nexpectations vary widely; the discussion on pages 9–13 can help you discover what they might be.\nMany assignments will specify or assume an educated audience or an academic audience. This\nmore general group of readers looks for writing that is clear, balanced, well organized, and well\nreasoned, among other qualities discussed on page 131. Still other assignments will specify or\nassume an audience of experts on your subject, readers who look in addition for writing that meets\nthe subject’s requirements for claims and evidence, organization, language, format, and other\nqualities discussed in Chapters 49–53.\nOf course, much of your academic writing will have only one reader besides you: the\ninstructor of the course for which you are writing. Instructors fill two main roles as readers:\nThey represent the audience you are addressing. They may actually be members of the\naudience, as when you address aca-demic readers or subject experts. Or they may imagine\nthemselves as members of your audience—reading, for instance, as if they sat on the city\ncouncil. In either case, they’re interested in how effectively you write for the audience.\nThey serve as coaches, guiding you toward achieving the goals of the course and, more\nbroadly, toward the academic aims of building and communicating knowledge.\nLike everyone else, instructors have preferences and peeves, but you’ll waste time and energy\ntrying to anticipate them. Do attend to written and spoken directions for assignments, of course.\nBut otherwise view your instructors as representatives of the community you are writing for. Their\nresponses will be guided by the community’s aims and expectations and by a desire to teach you\nabout them.\n6c Determining purpose\n\nFOWLcp06.doc - 5\nFor most academic writing, your general purpose will be mainly explanatory or mainly\nargumentative. That is, you will aim to clarify your subject so that readers understand it as you do,\nor you will aim to gain readers’ agreement with a debatable idea about the subject. (See p. 14 for\nmore on general purposes and Chapters 9–11 for more on argument.) Although the general purpose\nfor writing may not be stated outright in an assignment, it will probably be implied, as you can see\nin these two abbreviated assignments:\nExplanation\nCompare the depiction of war in two films viewed this semester, considering plots, characters, dialog,\nbattle scenes, production designs, and other elements of the films.\nArgument\nWhat ideology do you see informing the movies of director Steven Spielberg? What beliefs about the\nworld do his choices convey, whether explicitly or implicitly, intentionally or unintentionally? Support\nyour claim with evidence from at least four of Spielberg’s movies, considering plots, characters, dialog,\nproduction designs, and other elements.\nYour specific purpose—including your subject and how you hope readers will respond—\ndepends on the kind of writing you’re doing. In a biology lab report, for instance, you want your\nreaders to understand why you conducted your study, how you conducted it, what the results were,\nand what their significance is. Not coincidentally, these topics correspond to the major sections of a\nbiology lab report. In following the standard format, you both help to define your purpose and\nbegin to meet the discipline’s (and thus your instructor’s) expectations.\nYour specific purpose will be more complex as well. You take a course to learn about a\nsubject and the ways experts think about it. Your writing, in return, contributes to the discipline\nthrough the knowledge you uncover and the lens of your perspective. At the same time, as a student\nyou want to demonstrate your competence with research, evidence, format, and other requirements\nof the discipline.\n6d Choosing structure and content\nMany academic writing assignments will at least imply how you should organize your paper\nand even how you should develop your ideas. Like the biology lab report mentioned earlier, the\ntype of paper required will break into discrete parts, each with its own requirements for content. A\ncommon academic writing assignment, the analysis, does not predict definite content but still\nimplies certain requirements: you are asked to make a claim about your subject’s meaning or\nsignificance, to support the claim with specific evidence, and, often, to show that you understand\nwhat others think about the subject. (See Chapter 8 for more on analysis.)\nNo matter what type of paper an assignment specifies, the broad academic aims of building\nand exchanging knowledge deter-\nmine features that are common across disciplines. Follow these gen-eral guidelines for your\nacademic writing, supplementing them as indicated with others elsewhere in this book:\nDevelop a central idea or claim, called a thesis. Everything in the paper should relate clearly\nto this claim. For more on theses, see pages 27–31.\nState your thesis, usually near the beginning of the paper.\nSupport the thesis with evidence, drawn usually from research and sometimes from your\nown experience. The kinds of evidence will depend on the discipline you’re writing in and the\ntype of paper you’re doing. For more on evidence in the disciplines, see pages 743 (literature),\n759–60 (other humanities), 778–79 (social sciences), and 807–08 (natural and applied\nsciences).\nInteract with sources. Do not merely summarize sources but evaluate and synthesize them\nfrom your own perspective. For more on using sources, see pages 599–611.\nAcknowledge sources fully, using the documentation style appropriate to the discipline. See\npages 764, 784, and 812 for lists of disciplines’ style guides and pages 648–86 (MLA style for\nEnglish and some other humanities), 764–75 (Chicago style for history, philosophy, and some\nother humanities), 784–800 (APA style for the social sciences), and 812–19 (CSE style for the\nnatural and applied sciences).\nBalance your presentation. Discuss evidence and opposing views fairly, and take a serious\nand impartial approach.\n\nFOWLcp06.doc - 6\nOrganize clearly within the framework of the type of writing you’re doing. Develop your\nideas as simply and directly as your purpose and content allow. Clearly relate sentences,\nparagraphs, and sections so that readers always know where they are in the paper’s\ndevelopment.\nThese features are far from universal. In other cultures, academic writers may be indirect, may\nexpect readers to discover the thesis, or may assume that readers do not require acknowledgment of\nwell-known sources. Recognizing such differences between practices in your native culture and in\nthe United States can help you adapt to US academic writing.\n6e Using academic language\nAmerican academic writing relies on a dialect called standard American English. The dialect is\nalso used in business, the professions, government, the media, and other sites of social and\neconomic power where people of diverse backgrounds must communicate with one another. It is\n―standard‖ not because it is better than other forms of English, but because it is accepted as the\ncommon language, much as the dollar bill is accepted as the common currency.\nYou’ll recognize standard American English as the dialect used in this handbook, in\nmagazines and newspapers, and on television news. But you might also notice that the dialect\nvaries a lot, from the formal English of a President’s State of the Union address through the middle\nformality of this handbook to the informal chitchat between anchors on morning TV. Even in\nacademic writing, standard American English allows much room for the writer’s own tone and\nvoice, as these passages on the same topic show:\nMore formal\nUsing the technique of ―color engineering,‖ manufacturers and advertisers\ncan heighten the interest of consumers in a product by adding color that does\nnot contribute to the utility of the product but appeals more to emotions. In one\nexample from the 1920s, manufacturers of fountain pens, which had previously\nbeen made of hard black rubber, dramatically increased sales simply by\nproducing the pens in bright colors.\nLess formal\nA touch of ―color engineering‖ can sharpen the emotional appeal of a\nproduct or its ad. New color can boost sales even when the color serves no other\nuse. In the 1920s, for example, fountain pen makers introduced brightly colored\npens along with the familiar ones of hard black rubber. Sales shot up.\nAs different as they are, both examples illustrate several common features of academic\nlanguage:\nIt follows the conventions of standard American English for grammar and usage. These\nconventions are detailed in guides to the dialect, such as this handbook.\nIt uses a standard vocabulary, not one that only some groups understand, such as slang, an\nethnic or regional dialect, or another language. (See pp. 510–13 for more on specialized\nvocabularies.)\nIt creates some distance between writer and reader with the third person (he, she, it,\nthey). The first person (I, we) is sometimes appropriate to express personal opinions or invite\nreaders to think along, but not with a strongly explanatory purpose (I discovered that “color\nengineering” can heighten . . .). The second person (you) is appropriate only in addressing\nreaders\ndirectly (as in this handbook), and even then it may seem condescending or too chummy (You\nshould know that “color engineering” can heighten . . .).\nIt is authoritative and neutral. In the examples on the facing page, the writers express\nthemselves confidently, not timidly (as in One possible example of color engineering that\nmight be considered in this case is . . .). They also refrain from hostility (Advertisers will stop\nat nothing to achieve their goals) and enthusiasm (Color engineering is genius at work).\nAt first, the diverse demands of academic writing may leave you groping for an appropriate\nvoice. In an effort to sound fresh and confident, you may write too casually:\n\nFOWLcp06.doc - 7\nToo casual\n―Color engineering‖ is a great way to get at consumers’ feelings. . . . When the guys jazzed up the color,\nsales shot through the roof.\nIn an effort to sound ―academic,‖ you may produce wordy and awkward sentences:\nWordy and awkward\nThe emotions of consumers can be made more engaged by the technique known as ―color engineering.‖ . .\n. A very large increase in the sales of fountain pens was achieved by the manufacturers of the pens as a\nresult of this color enhancement technique. [The passive voice in this example, such as increase . . . was\nachieved instead of the manufacturers achieved, adds to its wordiness and indirection. See pp. 302–03\nfor more on voice.]\nA cure for writing too informally or too stiffly is to read academic writing so that the language and\nstyle become familiar and to edit your writing (see pp. 58–59).\nIf your first language is not English or is an English dialect besides standard American, you\nknow well the power of communicating with others who share your language. Learning to write\nstandard American English in no way requires you to abandon your first language. Like most\nmultilingual people, you are probably already adept at switching between languages as the situation\ndemands—speaking one way with your relatives, say, and another way with an employer. As you\npractice academic writing, you’ll develop the same flexibility with it.\nEXERCISE 6.1 Using academic language\nRevise the following paragraph to make the language more academic while keeping the factual\ninformation the same. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nIf you buy into the stereotype of girls chatting away on their cell phones, you should think again.\nOne of the major wireless companies surveyed 1021 cell phone owners for a period of five years and—\nsurprise!—reported that guys talk on cell phones more than girls do. In fact, guys were way ahead of girls,\nusing an average of 571 minutes a month compared to 424 for girls. That’s 35 percent more time on the\nphone! The survey also asked about conversations on home phones, and while girls still beat the field, the\nguys are catching up.\nEXERCISE 6.2 CONSIDERING YOUR PAST WORK:\nWRITING IN ACADEMIC SITUATIONS\nLook back at a paper you wrote for a course in high school or college. To what extent does it share the\nfeatures of academic writing discussed in this chapter? How does it differ? Write a revision plan for\nmaking the paper more academic.\nEXERCISE 6.3 Considering your native language or dialect\nWhat main similarities and differences do you notice between writing in your native language or dialect\nand writing for US college courses? Consider especially audience, purpose, content, structure, and the\nexpression of ideas. Which differences do you think are easiest to bridge? Which are most difficult?\nWhy?\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\nVisit the companion Web site for more help with academic writing.\nTips for becoming an academic writer\nStudy the syllabus for each course. This outline lays out the instructor’s expectations as well as the\ncourse topics, assignments, and deadlines.\nDo the assigned reading. You’ll gain experience with the discipline’s terms and ideas, and you’ll\nbecome familiar with the kinds of writing expected of you.\nAttend and participate in class. Make class attendance a priority, whether or not the instructor\nchecks the roll. Listen carefully, take notes (see p. 137 for tips), ask questions, and join in discussions.\nAsk questions. Instructors, advisers, tutors, other students—all can help you.\nUnderstand the writing situation posed by each assignment. Knowing your audience, purpose,\noptions for subjects, and other el-ements of the situation will help you meet the assignment’s\nexpectations. (See pp. 6–7 on analyzing assignments.)\nTwo complicated sentences, one explaining the technique and one giving the example\nDrawn-out phrasing, such as interest of consumers instead of consumers’ interest\nFormal vocabulary, such as heighten, contribute, and utility\nFour sentences, two each explaining the technique and giving the example\n\nFOWLcp06.doc - 8\nMore informal phrasing, such as Sales shot up\nMore informal vocabulary, such as boost and ad\n\nFOWLcp07.doc - 1\n7\nCHAPTER\nStudying Effectively\nand Taking Exams\nAcademic success depends on active, involved learning. If you haven’t already, read the\nprevious chapter on academic writing. Use the principles there along with this chapter’s practical\ntips for managing your time, getting the most from your classes and your reading, and taking\nexams.\nAs a first step, become familiar with your school’s resources and procedures:\n7a Managing your time\nPlanning and pacing your schoolwork and other activities will help you study more efficiently\nwith less stress.\n1 Surveying your activities\nHow do you spend your days? For a week, keep track of all your activities and the time they\nabsorb. How many of the week’s 168 hours do you spend eating, sleeping, watching television,\ntalking on the phone, attending classes, studying, working at a job, attending religious services,\nexercising, commuting, caring for children, doing laundry, socializing, and so on? How much time\ncan you realistically devote to studying?\n2 Scheduling your time\nOne way to organize your time is to use a calendar that divides each day into waking hours.\nBlock out your activities that occur regularly and at specific times, such as commuting, attending\nclasses, and working. Then fill in the other activities (such as exercise, eating, and studying) that do\nnot necessarily occur at fixed times. Be sure to leave time for relaxing: an unrealistic schedule that\nassigns all available time to studying will quickly prove too difficult to live by. If you use a\ncomputer regularly, consider keeping your schedule online using a calendar program. Set it to\nremind you of important due dates.\n3 Organizing your workload\nUse the syllabuses for your courses to estimate the amount of weekly study time required for\neach course. Generally, plan on two hours of studying for each hour in class—that is, about six\nhours for a typical course. Block out study periods using these guidelines:\nSchedule study time close to class time. You’ll study more productively if you review notes,\nread assigned material, or work on projects shortly after each class period.\nPace assignments. Plan to start early and work regularly on projects requiring extensive time,\nsuch as research papers, so that you will not be overwhelmed near the deadline. (See pp. 559–\n60 for advice on scheduling research projects.)\nAdjust the weekly plan as needed to accommodate changes in your workload. Before each\nweek begins, examine its schedule to be sure you’ve built in enough time to study for an\nexam, finish a paper, or meet other deadlines and commitments.\n4 Making the most of study time\nWhen you sit down to study, use your time efficiently:\nSet realistic study goals. Divide your study sessions into small chunks, each with a short-term\ngoal, such as previewing a textbook chapter or drafting three paragraphs of a paper. Plan\nbreaks, too, so that you can clear your mind, stretch, and re-\nfocus on your goals.\nTackle difficult homework first. Resist any urge to put off demanding jobs, such as working\non papers, reading textbooks, or doing math problems. Save easy tasks for when you’re less\nalert.\n\nFOWLcp07.doc - 2\nEvaluate how you use your study time. At the end of each week, ask yourself whether you\nwere as productive as you needed to be. If not, what changes can you make to accomplish\nyour goals for the coming week?\n7b Listening and taking notes in class\nWhen you begin each class, push aside other concerns so that you can focus and listen. Either\non paper or on a computer, record what you hear as completely as possible while sorting out the\nmain ideas from the secondary and supporting ones. (See the box below.) Such active note taking\nwill help you understand the instructor’s approach to the course and provide you with complete\nmaterial for later study.\n7c Reading for comprehension\nThe assigned reading you do for college courses—such as textbooks, journal articles, and\nworks of literature—requires a greater focus on understanding and retention than does the reading\nyou do for entertainment or for practical information. The process discussed here may seem time\nconsuming, but with practice you’ll become efficient at it.\nNote The following process stresses ways of understanding what you read. In critical reading,\ncovered in the next chapter, you extend this process to analyze and evaluate what you read and see.\n1 Writing while reading\nReading for comprehension is an active process. Students often believe they are reading\nactively when they roll a highlighter over the important ideas in a text, but truly engaged reading\nrequires more than that. If you take notes while reading, you ―translate‖ the work into your own\nwords and reconstruct it for yourself.\nThe substance of your reading notes will change as you preview, read, and summarize. At\nfirst, you may jot quick, short notes in the margins, on separate pages, or on a computer. (Use the\nlast two for material you don’t own or are reading online.) As you delve into the work, the notes\nshould become more detailed, restating important points, asking questions, connecting ideas. (See\np. 154 for an example of a text annotated in this way by a student.) For in-depth critical reading,\nyou may want to keep a reading journal that records both what the work says and what you think\nabout it. (See p. 154.)\n2 Previewing\nFor most course reading, you should skim before reading word for word. Skimming gives you\nan overview of the material that will help you understand any part of it. Your goal is not to\ncomprehend all the details or even the structure of the author’s argument. Rather, working as\noutlined below, aim for a general sense of the length and difficulty of the material, its organization,\nand its principal ideas.\nGauge length and level. Is the material brief and straightforward enough to read in one\nsitting, or do you need more time?\nExamine the title and introduction.The title and first couple of\nparagraphs will give you a sense of the topic, the author’s approach, and\nthe main ideas. As you read them, ask yourself what you already know\nabout the subject so that you can integrate new information with old.\nMove from heading to heading. Viewing the headings as headlines or as the levels of an\noutline will give you a feeling for which ideas the author sees as primary and which\nsubordinate.\nNote highlighted words. You will likely need to learn the meanings of terms in bold, italic,\nor color.\nSlow down for pictures, diagrams, tables, graphs, and other illustrations. They often\ncontain concentrated information.\nRead the summary or conclusion. These paragraphs often recap the main ideas.\nThink over what you’ve skimmed. Try to recall the central idea, or thesis, and the sequence\nof ideas.\n3 Reading\nAfter previewing a text, you can settle into it to learn what it has to say.\n\nFOWLcp07.doc - 3\nFirst reading\nThe first time through new material, read as steadily and smoothly as possible, trying to get\nthe gist of what the author is saying.\nRead in a place where you can concentrate. Choose a quiet environment away from\ndistractions such as music or talking.\nGive yourself time. Rushing yourself or worrying about something else you have to do will\nprevent you from grasping what you read.\nTry to enjoy the work. Seek connections between it and what you already know. Appreciate\nnew information, interesting relationships, forceful writing, humor, good examples.\nMake notes sparingly during this first reading. Mark major stumbling blocks—such as a\nparagraph you don’t understand—so that you can try to resolve them before rereading.\nIf English is not your first language and you come across unfamiliar words, don’t stop and\nlook up every one. You will lose more in concentration than you will gain in understanding.\nInstead, try to guess the meanings of unfamiliar words from their contexts, circle them, and look\nthem up later.\nRereading\nAfter the first reading, plan on at least one other. This time read slowly. Your main concern\nshould be to grasp the content and how it is constructed. That means rereading a paragraph if you\ndidn’t get the point or using a dictionary to look up words you don’t know.\nUse your pen, pencil, or keyboard freely to highlight and distill the text:\nDistinguish main ideas from supporting ideas. Look for the central idea, or thesis, for the\nmain idea of each paragraph or section, and for the evidence supporting ideas.\nLearn key terms. Understand both their meanings and their applications.\nDiscern the connections among ideas. Be sure you see why the author moves from point A to\npoint B to point C and how those points relate to support the central idea. It often helps to\noutline the text or summarize it (see below).\nAdd your own comments. In the margins or separately, note links to other readings or to class\ndiscussions, questions to explore further, possible topics for your writing, points you find\nespecially strong or weak. (This last category will occupy much of your time when you are\nexpected to read critically. See pp. 157–63.)\n4 Summarizing\nA good way to master the content of a text is to summarize it: reduce it to its main points, in\nyour own words. Some assignments call for brief summaries, as when you summarize the plot in a\ncritical essay about a novel (p. 747). Summary is also an essential tool in research papers and other\nwriting that draws on sources (p. 617). Here, though, we’re concerned with summarizing for\nyourself—for your own enlightenment.\nA summary should state in as few words as possible the main ideas of a passage. When you\nneed to summarize a few paragraphs or a brief article, your summary should not exceed one-fifth\nthe length of the original. For longer works, such as chapters of books or whole books, your\nsummary should be quite a bit shorter in proportion to the original. A procedure for drafting a\nsummary appears in the following box.\nSummarizing even a passage of text can be tricky. Below is one attempt to summarize the\nfollowing material from an introductory biology textbook.\nOriginal text\nAs astronomers study newly discovered planets orbiting distant stars, they hope to find evidence of\nwater on these far-off celestial bodies, for water is the substance that makes possible life as we know it\nhere on Earth. All organisms familiar to us are made mostly of water and live in an environment\ndominated by water. They require water more than any other substance. Human beings, for example, can\nsurvive for quite a few weeks without food, but only a week or so without water. Molecules of water\nparticipate in many chemical reactions necessary to sustain life. Most cells are surrounded by water, and\ncells themselves are about 70–95% water. Three-quarters of Earth’s surface is submerged in water.\nAlthough most of this water is in liquid form, water is also present on Earth as ice and vapor. Water is the\nonly common substance to exist in the natural environment in all three physical states of matter: solid,\nliquid, and gas.\n\nFOWLcp07.doc - 4\n—Neil A. Campbell and Jane B. Reece, Biology\nDraft summary\nAstronomers look for water in outer space because life depends on it. It is the most common substance on\nEarth and in living cells, and it can be a liquid, a solid (ice), or a gas (vapor).\nThis summary accurately restates ideas in the original, but it does not pare the passage to its\nessence. The work of astronomers and the three physical states of water add color and texture to the\noriginal, but they are asides to the key concept that water sustains life because of its role in life. The\nfollowing revision narrows the summary to this concept:\nRevised summary\nWater is the most essential support for life, the dominant substance on Earth and in living cells and a\ncomponent of life-sustaining chemical processes.\nNote Do not count on the AutoSummarize function on your word processor for summarizing\ntexts that you may have copied onto your computer. The summaries are rarely accurate, and you\nwill not gain the experience of interacting with the texts on your own.\n7d Preparing for and taking exams\nExaminations give you a chance to demonstrate what you have learned from listening, reading,\nand writing. Studying for an exam involves three main steps, each requiring about a third of the\npreparation time: reviewing the material, organizing summaries of the material, and testing\nyourself. Your main goals are to strengthen your understanding of the subject, making both its\nideas and its details more memorable, and to increase the flexibility of your new knowledge so that\nyou can apply it in new contexts.\nThe procedure outlined here works for any exam, no matter how much time you have, what\nmaterial you’re studying, or what kind of test you’ll be taking. Because an essay exam requires a\ndistinctive approach during the exam itself, it receives special attention on pages 144–49.\nNote Cramming for an exam is about the least effective way of preparing for one. It takes\nlonger to learn under stress, and the learning is shallower, more difficult to apply, and more quickly\nforgotten. Information learned under stress is even harder to apply in stressful situations, such as\ntaking an exam. And the lack of sleep that usually accompanies cramming makes a good\nperformance even more unlikely. If you must cram for a test, face the fact that you can’t learn\neverything. Spend your time reviewing main concepts and facts.\n1 Reviewing and memorizing the material\nDivide your class notes and reading assignments into manageable units. Reread the material,\nrecite or write out the main ideas and selected supporting ideas and examples, and then skim for an\noverview. Proceed in this way through all the units of the course, returning to earlier ones as\nneeded to refresh your memory or to relate ideas.\nDuring this stage you should be memorizing what you don’t already know by heart. Try these\nstrategies for strengthening your memory:\nLink new and known information. For instance, to remember a sequence of four dates in\ntwentieth-century African history, link the dates to simultaneous and more familiar events in\nthe United States.\nCreate groups of ideas or facts that make sense to you. For instance, memorize French\nvocabulary words in related groups, such as words for parts of the body or parts of a house.\nKeep the groups small: research has shown that we can easily memorize about seven items at a\ntime but have trouble with more.\nCreate narratives and visual images. You may recall a story or a picture more easily than\nwords. For instance, to remember how the economic laws of supply and demand affect the\nmarket for rental housing, you could tie the principles to a narrative about the aftermath of the\n1906 San Francisco earthquake, when half the population was suddenly homeless. Or you\ncould visualize a person who has dollar signs for eyes and is converting a spare room into a\nhigh-priced rental unit, as many did after the earthquake to meet the new demand for housing.\nUse mnemonic devices, or tricks for remembering. Say the his-tory dates you want to\nremember are separated by five years, then four, then nine. By memorizing the first date and\nrecalling 5 + 4 = 9, you’ll have command of all four dates.\n2 Organizing summaries of the material\n\nFOWLcp07.doc - 5\nAllow time to reorganize the material in your own way, creating categories that will help you\napply the information in various contexts. For instance, in studying for a biology exam, work to\nunderstand a process, such as how a plant develops or how photosynthesis occurs. Or in studying\nfor an American government test, explain the structures of the local, state, and federal levels of\ngovernment. Other useful categories include causes/effects and advantages/disadvantages. Such\nanalytical thinking will improve your mastery of the course material and may even prepare you\ndirectly for specific essay questions.\n3 Testing yourself\nConvert each heading in your lecture notes and course reading into a question. Answer in\nwriting, going back to the course mate-rial to fill in what you don’t yet know. Be sure you can\ndefine and explain all key terms. For subjects that require solving problems (such as mathematics,\nstatistics, or physics), work out a difficult problem for every type on which you will be tested. For\nall subjects, focus on the main themes and questions of the course. In a psychology course, for\nexample, be certain you understand principal theories and their implications. In a literature course,\ntest your knowledge of literary movements and genres or the relations among specific works.\nWhen you are satisfied with your preparation, stop studying and get a good night’s sleep.\n4 Taking essay exams\nIn writing an essay for an examination, you summarize or analyze a topic, usually in several\nparagraphs or more and usually within a time limit. An essay question not only tests your\nknowledge of a subject (as short-answer and objective questions do) but also tests your ability to\nthink critically about what you have learned.\nPlanning your time and your answer\nWhen you first receive an exam, take a few minutes to get your bearings and plan an approach.\nThe time spent will not be wasted.\nRead the exam all the way through at least once. Don’t start answering any questions until\nyou’ve seen them all.\nWeigh the questions. Determine which questions seem most important, which ones are going\nto be most difficult for you, and approximately how much time you’ll need for each question.\n(Your instructor may help by assigning a point value to each question as a guide to its\nimportance or by suggesting an amount of time for you to spend on each question.)\nPlanning continues when you turn to an individual essay question. Resist the temptation to\nrush right into an answer without some planning, for a few minutes can save you time later and\nhelp you produce a stronger essay.\nRead the question at least twice. You will be more likely to stick to the question and answer\nit fully.\nExamine the words in the question and consider their implications. Look especially for\nwords such as describe, define, explain, summarize, analyze, evaluate, and interpret, each of\nwhich requires a different kind of response. Here, for example, is an essay question whose key\nterm is explain:\nQuestion\nGiven humans’ natural and historical curiosity about themselves, why did a scientific discipline of\nanthropology not arise until the 20th century? Explain, citing specific details.\nSee the box opposite, and consult other discussions of such terms on pages 24–25 and 91–\n100.\nMake a brief outline of the main ideas you want to cover. Use the back of the exam sheet or\nbooklet for scratch paper. In the brief outline on page 146, a student planned her answer to the\nanthropology question above.\nOutline\n1. Unscientific motivations behind 19th-c anthro.\nImperialist/colonialist govts.\nPractical goals\nNonobjective and unscientific (Herodotus, Cushing)\n2. 19th-c ethnocentricity (vs. cultural relativism)\n\nFOWLcp07.doc - 6\n3. 19th-c anthro. = object collecting\n20th-c shift from museum to univ.\nAnthro. becomes acad. disc. and professional (Boas, Malinowski)\nWrite a thesis statement for your essay that responds directly to the question and\nrepresents your view of the topic. (If you are unsure of how to write a thesis statement, see\npp. 27–31.) Include key phrases that you can expand with supporting evidence for your view.\nThe thesis statement of the student whose outline appears above concisely previews a three-\npart answer to the sample question:\nThesis statement\nAnthropology did not emerge as a scientific discipline until the 20th century because of the practical and\npolitical motivations behind 19th-century ethnographic studies, the ethnocentric bias of Western\nresearchers, and a conception of culture that was strictly material.\nStarting the essay\nAn essay exam does not require a smooth and inviting opening. Instead, begin by stating your\nthesis immediately and giving an overview of the rest of your essay. Such a capsule version of your\nanswer tells your reader (and grader) generally how much command you have and also how you\nplan to develop your answer. It also gets you off to a good start.\nThe opening statement should address the question directly and exactly, as it does in the\nsuccessful essay answer beginning on the facing page. In contrast, the opening of the unsuccessful\nessay (pp. 148–49) restates the question but does not answer it, nor does the opening provide any\nsense of the writer’s thesis.\nDeveloping the essay\nDevelop your essay as you would develop any piece of sound academic writing:\nObserve the methods, terms, or other special requirements of the discipline in which you\nare writing.\nSupport your thesis statement with solid generalizations, each one perhaps the topic\nsentence of a paragraph.\nSupport each generalization with specific, relevant evidence.\nIf you observe a few don’ts as well, your essay will have more substance:\nAvoid filling out the essay by repeating yourself.\nAvoid other kinds of wordiness that pad and confuse, whether intentionally or not. (See pp.\n529–35.)\nAvoid resorting to purely subjective feelings. Keep focused on analysis or whatever is asked\nof you. (It may help to abolish the word I from the essay.)\nThe following essays illustrate a successful and an unsuccessful answer to the sample essay\nquestion on page 144 about anthropology. Both answers were written in the allotted time of forty\nminutes. Marginal comments on each essay highlight their effective and ineffective elements.\nSuccessful essay answer\nAnthropology did not emerge as a scientific discipline until the 20th\ncentury because of the practical and political motivations behind 19th-\ncentury ethnographic studies, the ethnocentric bias of Western researchers,\nand a conception of culture that was strictly material.\nBefore the 20th century, ethnographic studies were almost always used\nfor practical goals. The study of human culture can be traced back at least\nas far as Herodotus’s investigations of the Mediterranean peoples.\nHerodotus was like many pre-20th-century ‘‘anthropologists’’ in that he was\nemployed by a government that needed information about its neighbors, just\nas the colonial nations in the 19th century needed information about their\nnewly conquered subjects. The early politically motivated ethnographic\nstudies that the colonial nations sponsored tended to be isolated projects,\nand they aimed less to advance general knowledge than to solve a specific\nproblem. Frank Hamilton Cushing, who was employed by the American\ngovernment to study the Zuni tribe of New Mexico, and who is considered one\nof the pioneers of anthropology, didn’t even publish his findings. The\npolitical and practical aims of anthropologists and the nature of their\n\nFOWLcp07.doc - 7\nresearch prevented their work from being a scholarly discipline in its own\nright.\nAnthropologists of the 19th century also fell short of the standards\nof objectivity needed for truly scientific study. This partly had to do\nwith anthropologists’ close connection to imperialist governments. But even\nindependent researchers were hampered by the prevailing assumption that\nWestern cultures were inherently superior. While the modern anthropologist\nbelieves that a culture must be studied in terms of its own values, early\nethnographers were ethnocentric: they judged ‘‘primitive’’ cultures by\ntheir own ‘‘civilized’’ values. ‘‘Primitive’’ peoples were seen as\nuninteresting in their own right. The reasons to study them, ultimately,\nwere to satisfy curiosity, to exploit them, or to prove their inferiority.\nThere was even some debate as to whether so-called savage peoples were\nhuman.\nFinally, the 19th century tended to conceive of culture in narrow,\nmaterial terms, often reducing it to a collection of artifacts. When not\nworking for a government, early ethnographers usually worked for a museum.\nThe enormous collections of exotica still found in many museums today are\nthe legacy of this 19th-century object-oriented conception of anthropology,\nwhich ignored the myths, symbols, and rituals the objects related to. It\nwas only when the museum tradition was broadened to include all aspects of\na culture that anthropology could come into existence as a scientific\ndiscipline. When anthropologists like Franz Boas and Bronislaw Malinowski\nbegan to publish their findings for others to read and criticize and began\nto move from the museum to the university, the discipline gained stature\nand momentum.\nIn brief, anthropology required a whole series of ideological shifts\nto become modern. Once it broke free of its purely practical bent, the\ncultural prejudices of its practitioners, and the narrow conception that\nlimited it to a collection of objects, anthropology could grow into a\nscience.\nUnsuccessful essay answer\nThe discipline of anthropology, the study of humans and their\ncultures, actually began in the early 20th century and was strengthened by\nthe Darwinian revolution, but the discipline did not begin to take shape\nuntil people like Franz Boas and Alfred Kroeber began doing scientific\nresearch among nonindustrialized cultures. (Boas, who was born in Germany\nbut emigrated to the US, is the father of the idea of historical\nparticularism.)\nSince the dawn of time, humans have always had a natural curiosity\nabout themselves. Art and literature have always reflected this need to\nunderstand human emotions, thought, and behavior. Anthropology is yet\nanother reflection of this need. Anthropologists have a different way of\nlooking at human societies than artists or writers. Whereas the latter\npaint an individualistic, impressionistic portrait of the world they see,\nanthropologists study cultures systematically, scientifically. They are\nthus closer to biologists. They are social scientists, with the emphasis on both\nwords.\nAnother reason why anthropology did not develop until the 20th century\nis that people in the past did not travel very much. The expansion of the\nautomobile and the airplane has played a major role in the expansion of the\ndiscipline.\nCushing’s important work among the Zuni Indians in New Mexico is a\ngood example of the transition between 19th-century and 20th-century\napproaches to anthropology. Cushing was one of the first to develop the\nmethod of participant observation. Instead of merely coming in as an outsider,\ntaking notes, and leaving, Cushing actually lived among the Zuni, dressing\nlike them and following their customs. In this way, he was able to build a\nrelationship of trust with his informants, learning much more than someone\nwho would have been seen as an outsider.\nFranz Boas, as mentioned earlier, was another anthropology pioneer. A\nGerman immigrant, Boas proposed the idea of historical particularism as a response\nto the prevailing theory of cultural evolution. Cultural evolution is the idea\nthat cultures gradually evolve toward higher levels of efficiency and\ncomplexity. Historical particularism is the idea that every culture is\nunique and develops differently. Boas developed his theory to counter those\nwho believed in cultural evolution. Working with the Kwakiutl Indians, he\nwas also one of the first anthropologists to use a native assistant to help\nhim gain access to the culture under study.\nA third pioneer in anthropology was Malinowski, who developed a theory\nof functionalism----that culture responds to biological, psychological, and\n\nFOWLcp07.doc - 8\nother needs. Malinowski’s work is extremely important and still influential\ntoday.\nAnthropologists have made great contributions to society over the\ncourse of the past century. One can only hope that they will continue the\ngreat strides they have made, building on the past to contribute to a\nbright new future.\nRereading the essay\nThe time limit on an essay examination does not allow for the careful rethinking and revision\nyou would give an essay or research paper. You need to write clearly and concisely the first time.\nBut try to leave yourself a few minutes after finishing the entire exam for rereading the essay (or\nessays) and doing touch-ups.\nCorrect mistakes: illegible passages, misspellings, grammatical errors, and accidental\nomissions.\nVerify that your thesis is accurate—that it is, in fact, what you ended up writing about.\nEnsure that you have supported all your generalizations. Cross out irrelevant ideas and\ndetails, and add any information that now seems important. (Write on another page, if\nnecessary, keying each addition to the page on which it belongs.)\nCampus know-how\nLearn the answers to the following questions from your school’s Web site, its catalog, or, best yet, an\norientation session.\nWhere are the important buildings and offices? Locate the library, classrooms, registrar, financial-\naid office, health center, gym, and other facilities.\nWhat support services are available for students? Investigate your options for getting help when\nyou need it. Most schools provide advisers and counselors, writing centers, tutoring centers, computer\nassistance, library tours, and other services.\nWhat are the key procedures for students? Learn how to register for classes, drop or add a class,\ndeclare or change your major, request a transcript, or pay tuition bills.\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\nVisit the companion Web site for more help with studying effectively and taking exams.\nTips for taking class notes\nUse your own words. You will understand and retain the material better if you rephrase it. But use\nthe speaker’s words if necessary to catch everything.\nLeave space in your notes if you miss something. Instructors usually welcome questions about\ncontent but not requests for simple repetition, and it’s not fair to distract fellow students during class.\nAsk someone for the missing information as soon as possible after class.\nInclude any reading content mentioned by your instructor. Use the notes to integrate all the\ncomponents of the course—your instructor’s views, your own thoughts, and the assigned reading,\neven if you’ve already read it.\nReview your notes shortly after class. Reinforce your new knowledge when it is fresh by\nunderlining key words and ideas, adding headings and comments in the margins, converting your\nnotes to questions, or outlining the lecture based on your notes. If you don’t understand something in\nthe notes, consult a classmate for his or her version.\nWriting a summary\nUnderstand the meaning. Look up words or concepts you don’t know so that you understand the\nauthor’s sentences and how they relate to one another.\nUnderstand the organization. Work through the text to identify its sections—single paragraphs or\ngroups of paragraphs focused on a single topic. To understand how parts of a work relate to one\nanother, try drawing a tree diagram or creating an outline (pp. 34–38).\n\nFOWLcp07.doc - 9\nDistill each section. Write a one- or two-sentence summary of each section you identify. Focus on the\nmain point of the section, omitting examples, facts, and other supporting evidence.\nState the main idea. Write a sentence or two capturing the author’s central idea.\nSupport the main idea. Write a full paragraph (or more, if needed) that begins with the central idea\nand supports it with the sentences that summarize sections of the work. The paragraph should\nconcisely and accurately state the thrust of the entire work.\nUse your own words. By writing, you re-create the meaning of the work in a way that makes sense for\nyou.\nSample instructions for essay examinations\nSample Key Strategies Examples of\ninstructions terms for answers wrong answers\nDefine dyslexia Define Specify the mean- Feelings of\nand compare ing of dyslexia— children with\nand contrast it distinctive char- dyslexia.\nwith two other acteristics, ways Causes of\nlearning disabil- the impairment dyslexia.\nities. works, etc.\nCompare Analyze similarities Similarities with-\nand contrast and differences out differences,\n(severity, causes, or vice versa.\ntreatments, etc.).\nAnalyze the role Analyze Break Horatio’s Plot summary\nof Horatio in role into its ele- of Hamlet.\nHamlet. ments (speeches, Description\nrelations with of Horatio’s\nother characters, personality.\netc.).\nExplain the Explain Set forth the Argument for\neffects of the facts and theories or against\ndrug Thorazine. objectively. The word critical does not mean\n―negative‖ in this context: you can think critically about something you like, don’t like, or just view\nneutrally.\nYou already operate critically every day of your life, as when you probe a friendship (―What\ndid she mean by that?‖) or when you discuss a movie you just saw (―Don’t you think the bad guy\nwas too obvious?‖). Such questioning helps you figure out why things happen to you or what your\nexperiences mean.\nThis chapter introduces more formal methods for thinking and reading critically (opposite),\nviewing images critically (p. 164), and writing critically (p. 172). Learning and applying these\nmethods will both engage you in and prepare you for school courses, career, and life in a\ndemocratic society:\nTeachers and employers will expect you to think critically. In every field, you will need to\nassess what you read, see, and hear and to make a good case for your own ideas.\nCritical thinking helps you understand and express yourself. With it, you gain insight into\nyour actions and ideas, can weigh them against opposing views, and can persuasively\narticulate your reasoning and motivations.\nYour very independence and freedom depend on your ability to think, read, and write\ncritically. An open democracy allows as much play for stupid and false claims as for sound\nones, and the claims that seem sound often conflict with each other. Crit-ical thinking\nempowers you to decide for yourself what’s useful, fair, and wise—and what’s not.\nThere’s no denying that critical thinking, reading, and writing require discipline and hard\nwork. Besides channeling your curiosity, paying attention, and probing, you will often need to\nconsult experts, interpreting and evaluating their ideas. Such an approach also requires a healthy\ntolerance for doubt or uncertainty—that feeling you may have when the old rules don’t seem to\napply or when a change is frightening but still attractive. Out of uncertainty, though, comes\ncreativity—the capacity to organize and generate knowledge, to explain, resolve, illuminate, play.\nCompared to passive, rote learning, creative work is more involving, more productive, and more\nenjoyable.\n8a Thinking and reading critically\nIn college much of your critical thinking will focus on written texts (a short story, a journal\narticle, a Web log) or on visual objects (a photograph, a chart, a film). Like all subjects worthy of\ncritical consideration, such works operate on at least three levels: (1) what the creator actually says\nor shows, (2) what the creator does not say or show but builds into the work (intentionally or not),\nand (3) what you think. Discovering the first of these levels—reading for comprehension—is\ndiscussed in the preceding chapter as part of effective study skills (see pp. 138–40). This chapter\nbuilds on the earlier material to help you discover the other two levels. The box below summarizes\nthe reading techniques involved.\nThe techniques of critical reading are not steps in a firm sequence. You will not use all of\nthem for all the reading you do. On some occasions, even when a close, critical reading is required,\nyou may simply lack the time to preview, read, and reread. (But if your reading time is continually\nsqueezed by your schedule, you may need to rethink your schedule.) On other occasions your\nreason for reading (your purpose) will determine which techniques you use.\n\nFOWLcp08.doc - 2\nEven a publication like People magazine is open to different methods of reading for different\npurposes:\nPurpose Learn some gossip while filling time in the dentist’s office.\nKind of reading Quick, uncritical\nPurpose Examine People as an artifact of our popular culture that reflects and perhaps even\nmolds contemporary values.\nKind of reading Close, critical\nCourse assignments, too, differ in their requirements. A book report may require writing,\npreviewing, reading, and summarizing but not intense critical reading. An evaluation of a journal\narticle, in contrast, requires all the techniques discussed here.\nThe idea of reading critically may require you to make some adjustments if readers in your\nnative culture tend to seek understanding or agreement more than engagement from what they read.\nReaders of English use texts for all kinds of reasons, including pleasure, reinforcement,\ninformation, and many others. But they also read skeptically, critically, to see the author’s motives,\ntest their own ideas, and arrive at new knowledge.\n1 Writing while reading\nReading a work for comprehension and then for a critical approach is an active process.\nMaking notes on what you read involves you by helping you understand how the text works, why,\nand what you think about it. The notes help you bring to the work your own experiences,\nknowledge, and questions.\nIf you own the material you’re reading (a book, a photocopy, or a printout), you can make\nnotes in the margins (see p. 154 for an example). If you don’t own the material or if your notes\nwon’t fit in the margins, make notes separately using pen and paper or your computer. Many\nreaders keep a reading journal in which they regularly work out questions and thoughts about\nwhat they read. One technique for keeping such a journal is to divide a page or computer screen\ninto two vertical columns, the left side for the work itself, such as summary and questions, and the\nright side for what the work makes you think, such as agreements or doubts based on your own\nexperiences, comparisons with other works, and ideas for writing. A two-column journal can\nencourage you to go beyond summarizing what you read to interacting critically with it because the\nblank right column will beckon you to respond. See the next page for an example of this\ntechnique.\nNote Whenever you photocopy or download a document or take notes separately from the text\nyou’re reading, be sure to record all necessary information about the text’s location so that you can\nfind it again and cite it fully if you use it. See page 569 for a list of information to record.\n2 Previewing the material\nWhen you’re reading a work of literature, such as a story or a poem, it’s often best to plunge\nright in (see pp. 736–37). But for critical reading of other works, it’s worthwhile to skim before\nreading word for word, forming expectations and even preliminary questions. The preview will\nmake your reading more informed and fruitful.\nUse the questions in the box below as a guide to previewing a text.\n3 Reading\nReading is itself more than a one-step process. You want to understand the first level on which\nthe text operates—what the author actually says—and begin to form your impressions.\nA procedure for this stage appears in the preceding chapter (pp. 139–40). To recap: Read once\nthrough fairly smoothly, trying to appreciate the work and keeping notes to a minimum. Then read\nagain more carefully, this time making detailed notes, to grasp the ideas and their connections and\nto pose questions.\nFollowing are examples of active reading from a student, Charlene Robinson. She was\nresponding to Thomas Sowell’s ―Student Loans,‖ reprinted on the next two pages. First Robinson\nannotated a photocopy of the essay (the first four paragraphs appear below):\n\nFOWLcp08.doc - 3\nThe first lesson of economics is scarcity: There is never enough of anything to fully\nsatisfy all those who want it.\nThe first lesson of politics is to disregard the first lesson of economics. When\npoliticians discover some group that is being vocal about not having as much as they want,\nthe ―solution‖ is to give them more. Where do politicians get this ―more‖? They rob Peter\nto pay Paul.\nAfter a while, of course, they discover that Peter doesn’t have enough. Bursting with\ncompassion, politicians rush to the rescue. Needless to say, they do not ad-mit that robbing\nPeter to pay Paul was a dumb idea in the first place. On the contrary, they now rob Tom,\nDick, and Harry to help Peter.\nThe latest chapter in this long-running saga is that politicians have now suddenly\ndiscovered that many college students graduate heavily in debt. To politicians it follows, as\nthe night follows the day, that the government should come to their rescue with the\ntaxpayers’ money.\nAfter reading the text, Robinson wrote about it in the journal she kept on her computer. She\ndivided the journal into two col-umns, one each for the text and her responses. Here is the portion\npertaining to the paragraphs above:\nText Responses\nEconomics teaches lessons (1), and Is economics truer or more\nreliable\npolitics (politicians) and economics than politics? More\nscientific?\nare at odds\nPoliticians don’t accept econ. Politicians do spend a lot of our\nlimits----always trying to satisfy money. Is that what they’re\n‘‘vocal’’ voters by giving them what elected to do, or do they go\ntoo\nthey want (2) far?\n‘‘Robbing Peter to pay Paul’’ (2)----\nfrom the Bible (the Apostles)?\nPoliticians support student loan I support the loan program, too.\nprogram with taxpayer funds bec. Are politicians being irresponsible\nof ‘‘vocal’’ voters (2-4): another ex. when they do? (Dismissive lan-\nof not accepting econ. limits guage underlined on copy.)\nYou should try to answer the questions about meaning that you raise in your annotations and\nyour journal, and that may take another reading or some digging in other sources, such as\ndictionaries and encyclopedias. Recording in your journal what you think the author means will\nhelp you build an understanding of the text, and a focused attempt to summarize will help even\nmore (see pp. 140–42 and 157). Such efforts will resolve any confusion you feel, or they will give\nyou the confidence to say that your confusion is the fault of the author, not the reader.\nEXERCISE 8.1 Reading\nReprinted below is an essay by Thomas Sowell on the federal government’s student-loan program. An\neconomist, Sowell is also a newspaper columnist and the author of many books on economics, politics,\nand education. This essay appeared in Sowell’s collection Is Re- ality Optional?\nRead this essay at least twice, until you think you understand what the author is saying. Either on\nthese pages or separately, note your questions and reactions in writing.\nStudent Loans\nThe first lesson of economics is scarcity: There is never enough of anything to fully satisfy all those\nwho want it.\nThe first lesson of politics is to disregard the first lesson of economics. When politicians discover\nsome group that is being vocal about not having as much as they want, the ―solution‖ is to give them\nmore. Where do politicians get this ―more‖? They rob Peter to pay Paul.\nAfter a while, of course, they discover that Peter doesn’t have enough. Bursting with compassion,\npoliticians rush to the rescue. Needless to say, they do not admit that robbing Peter to pay Paul was a\ndumb idea in the first place. On the contrary, they now rob Tom, Dick, and Harry to help Peter.\nThe latest chapter in this long-running saga is that politicians have now suddenly discovered that\nmany college students graduate heavily in debt. To politicians it follows, as the night follows the day, that\nthe government should come to their rescue with the taxpayers’ money.\nHow big is this crushing burden of college students’ debt that we hear so much about from\npoliticians and media deep thinkers? For those students who graduate from public colleges owing money,\n\nFOWLcp08.doc - 4\nthe debt averages a little under $7,000. For those who graduate from private colleges owing money, the\naverage debt is a little under $9,000.\nBuying a very modestly priced automobile involves more debt than that. And a car loan has to be\npaid off faster than the ten years that college graduates get to repay their student loans. Moreover, you\nhave to keep buying cars every several years, while one college education lasts a lifetime.\nCollege graduates of course earn higher incomes than other people. Why, then, should we panic at\nthe thought that they have to repay loans for the education which gave them their opportunities? Even\ngraduates with relatively modest incomes pay less than 10 percent of their annual salary on the loan the\nfirst year—with declining percentages in future years, as their pay increases.\nPolitical hysteria and media hype may focus on the low-income student with a huge debt. That is\nwhere you get your heart-rending stories—even if they are not at all typical. In reality, the soaring student\nloans of the past decade have resulted from allowing high-income people to borrow under government\nprograms.\nBefore 1978, college loans were available through government programs only to students whose\nfamily income was below some cut-off level. That cut-off level was about double the national average\nincome, but at least it kept out the Rockefellers and the Vanderbilts. But, in an era of ―compassion,‖\nCongress took off even those limits.\nThat opened the floodgates. No matter how rich you were, it still paid to borrow money through the\ngovernment at low interest rates. The money you had set aside for your children’s education could be\ninvested somewhere else, at higher interest rates. Then, when the student loan became due, parents could\npay it off with the money they had set aside—pocketing the difference in interest rates.\nTo politicians and the media, however, the rapidly growing loans showed what a great ―need‖ there\nwas. The fact that many students welshed when time came to repay their loans showed how ―crushing‖\ntheir burden of debt must be. In reality, those who welsh typically have smaller loans, but have dropped\nout of college before finishing. People who are irresponsible in one way are often irresponsible in other\nways.\nNo small amount of the deterioration of college standards has been due to the increasingly easy\navailability of college to people who are not very serious about getting an education. College is not a bad\nplace to hang out for a few years, if you have nothing better to do, and if someone else is paying for it. Its\ncosts are staggering, but the taxpayers carry much of that burden, not only for state universities and city\ncolleges, but also to an increasing extent even for ―private‖ institutions.\nNumerous government subsidies and loan programs make it possible for many people to use vast\namounts of society’s resources at low cost to themselves. Whether in money terms or in real terms, federal\naid to higher education has increased several hundred percent since 1970. That has enabled colleges to\nraise their tuition by leaps and bounds and enabled professors to be paid more and more for doing less\nand less teaching.\nNaturally all these beneficiaries are going to create hype and hysteria to keep more of the taxpayers’\nmoney coming in. But we would be fools to keep on writing blank checks for them.\nWhen you weigh the cost of things, in economics that’s called ―trade-offs.‖ In politics, it’s called\n―mean-spirited.‖ Apparently, if we just took a different attitude, scarcity would go away.\n—Thomas Sowell\n4 Summarizing\nSummarizing a text—distilling it to its essential ideas, in your own words—is an important\nstep for comprehending it and is discussed in detail in the previous chapter (pp. 140–42). Here,\nwe’ll look at how Charlene Robinson summarized paragraphs 1–4 of Thomas Sowell’s ―Student\nLoans.‖ She first drafted this sentence:\nDraft summary\nAs much as politicians would like to satisfy voters by giving them everything they ask for, the\ngovernment cannot afford a student loan program.\nRereading the sentence and Sowell’s paragraph, Robinson saw that this draft misread the text by\nasserting that the government cannot afford student loans. She realized that Sowell’s point is more\ncomplicated than that and rewrote her summary:\nRevised summary\nAs their support of the government’s student loan program illustrates, politicians ignore the economic\nreality that using resources to benefit one group (students in debt) involves taking the resources from\nanother group (taxpayers).\n\nFOWLcp08.doc - 5\nNote Using your own words when writing a summary not only helps you understand the\nmeaning but also constitutes the first step in avoiding plagiarism. The second step is to cite the\nsource when you use it in something written for others. See Chapter 45.\nEXERCISE 8.2 Summarizing\nStart where Robinson’s summary of Thomas Sowell’s essay ends (at paragraph 5) to summarize the entire\nessay. Your summary, in your own words, should not exceed one paragraph. For additional exercises in\nsummarizing, see pp. 622–23.\n5 Developing a critical response\nOnce you’ve grasped the content of what you’re reading—what the author\nsays—then you can turn to understanding what the author does not say\noutright but suggests or implies or even lets slip. At this stage you are\nconcerned with the purpose or intention of the author and with how he or\nshe carries it out. Depending on what you are reading and why, you may\nexamine evidence, organization, attitude, use of language, and other\nelements of the text.\nCritical thinking and reading consist of four operations: analyzing, interpreting, synthesizing,\nand (often) evaluating. Although we’ll look at them one by one, these operations interrelate and\noverlap. Indeed, the first three are often combined under the general label analysis, and evaluation\nis sometimes taken for granted as a result of the process.\nIn the following pages, we use two quite different examples\nto show how critical reading can work: People magazine and Sowell’s ―Student Loans.‖\nAnalyzing\nAnalysis is the separation of something into its parts or elements, the better to understand it.\nTo see these elements in what you are reading, begin with a question that reflects your purpose in\nanalyzing the text: why you’re curious about it or what you’re trying to make out of it. This\nquestion will serve as a kind of lens that highlights some features and not others.\nHere are some questions you might ask about People magazine, listed along with the elements\nof the magazine that each question highlights:\nQuestions for analysis Elements\nDoes People challenge or Stereotypes: explicit and implicit\nperpetuate stereotypes? stereotypes or challenges in the\nmagazine\nDoes the magazine offer posi- Role models: text and photo-\ntive role models for its readers? graphs presenting positive or\nnegative role models\nDoes the magazine’s editorial Encouragement of consumption:\nmaterial (articles and accom- references to goods and enter-\npanying photographs) en- tainment, focus on consumers,\ncourage readers to consume equation of consumption with\ngoods and entertainment? happiness or success\nAs these examples show, a question for analysis concentrates your attention on relevant\nfeatures and eliminates irrelevant features. To answer the question about People’s encouragement\nof consumption, you would focus on items that feature consumption and the products consumed:\nphotographs of designer clothes and celebrities’ well-appointed homes, articles on the authors of\nbest-selling books and the stars of new movies. At the same time, you would skip over items that\nhave little or no relevance to consumption, such as uplifting stories about families or the physically\nchallenged.\nAnalyzing Thomas Sowell’s ―Student Loans‖ (pp. 155–56), you might ask these questions:\nQuestions for analysis Elements\n\nFOWLcp08.doc - 6\nWhat is Sowell’s attitude References to politicians: content,\ntoward politicians? words, tone\nHow does Sowell support his Support: evidence, such as statis-\nassertions about the loan pro- tics and examples\ngram’s costs?\nA difference in the kinds of questions asked is a key distinction among academic disciplines.\nA sociologist neatly outlined three disciplines’ approaches to poverty:\nPolitical science does a wonderful job looking at poverty as a policy issue. Economics does an equally\nwonderful job looking at it from an income-distribution perspective. But sociology asks how people in\npoverty live and what they aspire to.\nEven within disciplines, approaches may differ. The sociologist quoted above may focus on how\npeople in poverty live, but another may be more interested in the effects of poverty on cities or the\nchanges in the poor population over the last fifty years. (See Chapters 49–53 for more on the\ndisciplines’ analytical questions.)\nInterpreting\nIdentifying the elements of something is of course only the beginning: you also need to\ninterpret the meaning or significance of the elements and of the whole. Interpretation usually\nrequires you to infer the author’s assumptions, opinions or beliefs about what\nis or what could or should be. (Infer means to draw a conclusion based on evidence.)\nThe word assumption here has a more specific meaning than it does in everyday usage, where\nit may stand for expectation (―I assume you’ll pay‖), speculation (―It was a mere assumption‖), or\nerror (―The report was riddled with assumptions‖). Defined more strictly as what a person supposes\nto be true, assumptions are unavoidable. We all adhere to certain values and beliefs; we all form\nopinions. We live our lives by such assumptions.\nThough pervasive, assumptions are not always stated outright. Speakers and writers may judge\nthat their audience already understands and accepts their assumptions; they may not even be aware\nof their assumptions; or they may deliberately refrain from stating their assumptions for fear that\nthe audience will disagree. That is why your job as a critical thinker is to interpret what the\nassumptions are.\nReasonable inferences\nLike an author deciding what to say in an article, the publishers of People magazine make\nassumptions that guide their selection of content for the magazine. One set of assumptions, perhaps\nthe most important, concerns what readers want to see: as a for-profit enterprise, the magazine\nnaturally aims to maintain and even expand its readership (currently about 3.4 million each week).\nIf your analysis of the magazine’s editorial material reveals that much of it features consumer\nproducts, you might infer the following:\nReasonable The publishers of People assume that the magazine’s readers are consumers who want to\nsee and hear about goods and entertainment.\nNowhere in People will you find a statement of this assumption, but the evidence implies it.\nSimilarly, Thomas Sowell’s ―Student Loans‖ (pp. 155–56) is based on certain assumptions,\nsome obvious, some not so obvious. If you were analyzing Sowell’s attitude toward politicians, as\nsuggested earlier, you would focus on his statements about them. Sowell says that they ―disregard\nthe first lesson of economics‖ (paragraph 2), which implies that they ignore important principles\n(knowing that Sowell is an economist himself makes this a reasonable assumption on your part).\nSowell also says that politicians ―rob Peter to pay Paul,‖ are ―[b]ursting with compassion,‖ ―do not\nadmit . . . a dumb idea,‖ are characters in a ―long-running saga,‖ and\narrive at the solution of spending taxes ―as the night follows the day‖—that is, inevitably\n(paragraphs 2–4). From these statements and others, you can infer the following:\nReasonable Sowell assumes that politicians become compassionate when a cause is loud and popular,\nnot necessarily just, and they act irresponsibly by trying to solve the problem with other\npeople’s (taxpayers’) money.\nUnreasonable inferences\n\nFOWLcp08.doc - 7\nInterpreting assumptions gives you greater insight into an author’s intentions. But it’s crucial\nthat inferences fit the evidence of the text, as those above about People and Sowell’s essay do.\nSometimes it’s tempting to read too much into the text, as in the next examples:\nFaulty People’s publishers deliberately skew the magazine’s editorial material to promote\nproducts on which they receive kickbacks. [The inference is far-fetched, even absurd. It\nwould be reasonable only if there were hard evidence of kickbacks.]\nFaulty Sowell thinks that politicians should not be entrusted with running the country. [The\ninference misreads Sowell. Although he does not outline a solution for politicians’\nirresponsibility, there’s no evidence that he would overhaul our democratic political\nsystem.]\nFaulty inferences like these are often based on the reader’s own assumptions about the text or its\nsubject. When thinking and reading critically, you need to look hard at your ideas, too.\nSynthesizing\nIf you stopped at analysis and interpretation, critical thinking and reading might leave you\nwith a pile of elements and possible meanings but no vision of the whole. With synthesis you make\nconnections among parts or among wholes. You create a new whole by drawing conclusions about\nrelationships and implications.\nThe following conclusion pulls together the earlier analysis of People magazine’s editorial\ncontent and the interpretation of the publisher’s assumptions about readers:\nConclusion People magazine appeals to its readers’ urge to con-sume by displaying, discussing, and\nglamorizing consumer goods.\nThe statement below about Thomas Sowell’s essay ―Student Loans‖ connects his assumptions\nabout politicians to a larger idea also implied by the essay:\nConclusion Sowell’s view that politicians are irresponsible with tax-\npayers’ money reflects his overall opinion that the laws of economics, not politics, should\ndrive government.\nSynthesis may involve working within the text, as in the preceding examples, or it may take\nyou outside the text to the surroundings. (This emphasis is important in research writing, as\ndiscussed on pp. 610–11.) The following questions can help you investigate the context of a work:\nHow does the work compare with works by others? For instance, how does People’s\njuxtaposition of articles and advertisements compare with that in similar magazines, such as\nUs Weekly, Entertainment Weekly, and Interview? Or how have other writers responded to\nSowell’s views on student loans?\nHow does the work fit into the context of other works by the same author or group? What\ndistinguishes People from the many other magazines published by Time Inc., such as Time\nmagazine, Sports Illustrated, Family Circle, and Fortune? How do Sowell’s views on student\nloans typify, or not, the author’s other writings on political and economic issues?\nWhat cultural, economic, or political forces influence the work? Why, for instance, are\nPeople and other celebrity magazines increasingly popular with readers? What other examples\nmight Sowell have given to illustrate his view that economics, not politics, should determine\ngovernment spending?\nWhat historical forces influence the work? What changes does People reflect in the ways\nreaders choose magazines? How has the indebtedness of college students changed over the\npast four decades?\nTo create links among the elements of a work or between a work and its context, it helps\n(again) to write while reading and thinking. The active reading recommended earlier is the place to\nstart, as you note your questions and opinions about the text. You can also create connections with\na combination of writing and drawing: start with your notes, expand them as needed to take\naccount of context, and draw connections between related thoughts with lines and arrows. (On a\nword processor you can use the Highlight function or different colors to link related ideas, or use\nthe Comment function to annotate connections.) You want to open up your thinking, so experiment\nfreely.\nWith synthesis, you create something different from what you started with. To the supermarket\nshopper reading People while standing in line, the magazine may be entertaining and\n\nFOWLcp08.doc - 8\ninconsequential. To you—after a critical reading in which you analyze,\ninterpret, and synthesize—the magazine is (at least in part) a sig-nificant vehicle of our consumer\nculture. The difference depends entirely on the critical reading.\nEvaluating\nMany critical reading and writing assignments end at analysis, interpretation, and synthesis:\nyou explain your understanding of what the author says and doesn’t say. Only if you are expected\nto evaluate the work will you state and defend the judgments you’ve made about its quality and its\nsignificance.\nYou’ll inevitably form judgments while reading the work: What a striking series of images or\nThat just isn’t enough evidence. In evaluating, you collect your judgments, determine that they are\ngenerally applicable and are themselves not trivial, and turn them into assertions: The poet creates\nfresh, intensely vivid images. The author does not summon the evidence to support his case. And\nyou support these statements with citations from the text.\nEvaluation takes a certain amount of confidence. You may think that you lack the expertise to\ncast judgment on another’s writing, especially if the text is difficult or the author well known. True,\nthe more informed you are, the better a critical reader you are. But conscientious reading and\nanalysis will give you the internal authority to judge a work as it stands and as it seems to you,\nagainst your own unique bundle of experiences, observations, and attitudes.\nThe box below gives questions that can help you evaluate many kinds of works. There’s more\non evaluation (including evaluation of online sources) on pages 599–609. For arguments and in\nacademic\ndisciplines, you’ll require additional, more specific criteria. See Chap-ters 9, 11, and 49–53.\nEXERCISE 8.3 Thinking critically\nFollowing are some statements about the communications media. Use systematic critical thinking to\nunderstand not only what the statement says but also why its author might have said it. As in the example,\ndo your thinking in writing: the act of writing will help you think, and your notes will help you discuss\nyour ideas with your classmates. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nStatement: Every year sees the disappearance of more book publishers because the larger companies\ngobble up the smaller ones.\nAnalysis: Why did the author make this statement? Certain words reveal the author’s purpose:\ndisappearance of more book publishers; because; larger companies gobble up smaller ones.\nInterpretation: More book publishers means others have disappeared. Because specifies cause.\nGobble up implies consumption, predator to prey. Author’s assumptions: Large publishers behave\nlike predators. The predatory behavior of large companies causes the disappearance of small\ncompanies. The more publishing companies there are, the better.\nSynthesis: The author objects to the predatory behavior of large publishing companies, which he or\nshe holds responsible for eliminating small companies and reducing the total number of companies.\nEvaluation: This biased statement against large publishers holds them responsible for the shrinking\nnumber of book publishers.
