But are the large companies solely responsible? And why is the shrinking\nnecessarily bad?\n1. Newspapers and newsmagazines are better news sources than television because they demand\nreading, not just viewing.\n2. Radio call-in shows are the true democratic forum, giving voice to people of all\npersuasions.\n3. Online communication threatens to undermine our ability to inter-act face to face.\nEXERCISE 8.4 Reading an essay critically\nReread Thomas Sowell’s ―Student Loans‖ (pp. 155–56) in order to form your own critical response to it.\nFollow the guidelines for analysis, interpretation, synthesis, and evaluation in the boxes on pages 158and\n163. Focus on any elements suggested by your question about the text: possibilities are assumptions,\nevidence, organization, use of language, tone, authority, vision of education or students. Be sure to write\n\nFOWLcp08.doc - 9\nwhile reading and thinking; your notes will help your analysis and enhance your creativity, and they will\nbe essential for writing about the selection (Exercise 8.10, p. 178).\nEXERCISE 8.5 Reading a magazine critically\nDo your own critical reading of People or another magazine. What do you see beyond the obvious? What\nquestions does your reading raise? Let the guidelines on pages 158 and 163 direct your response, and do\nyour work in writing.\n8b Viewing images critically\nEvery day we are bombarded with images—pictures on billboards, commercials on television,\ngraphs and charts in newspapers and textbooks, to name just a few examples. Most images slide by\nwithout our noticing them, or so we think. But images, sometimes even more than text, can\ninfluence us covertly. Their creators have purposes, some worthy, some not, and understanding\nthose purposes requires critical reading. The method parallels that in the previous section for\nreading text critically: write while reading, preview, read for comprehension, analyze, interpret,\nsynthesize, and (often) evaluate.\n1 Writing while reading an image\nWriting as you read an image helps you view it deliberately and allows you to record your\nimpressions precisely. If possible, print a copy of the image or scan it into your reading journal, and\nwrite your comments in the image margins or separately. The example on the next page shows how\none student annotated an image he was reading.\n2 Previewing an image\nYour first step in exploring an image is to form initial impressions of the work’s origin and\npurpose and to note distinctive features. This previewing process is like the one for previewing a\ntext (p. 153):\n3 Reading an image\nReading an image requires the same level of concentration as reading a text. Plan to spend\nmore than one session working with the image to absorb its meaning and purpose and then to\nanalyze and maybe challenge its message.\nTry to answer the following questions about the image. If some answers aren’t clear at this\npoint, skip the question until later.\nWhat is the purpose of the image? Is it mainly explanatory, conveying information, or is it\nargumentative, trying to convince readers of something or persuade them to act? What\ninformation or point of view does it seem intended to get across?\nWho is the intended audience for the image? What does the source of the image, including\nits publication facts, tell about the image creator’s expectations for readers’ knowledge,\ninterests, and attitudes? What do the features of the image itself add to your impression?\nWhat do any words or symbols add to the image? Whether located on the image or outside\nit (such as in a caption), do words or symbols add information, focus your attention, or alter\nyour impression of the image?\nWhat people, places, things, or action does the image show? Does the image tell a story?\nDo its characters or other features tap into your knowledge, or are they unfamiliar?\nWhat is the form of the image? Is it a photograph, advertisement, painting, graph, diagram,\ncartoon, or something else? How do its content and apparent purpose and audience relate to its\nform?\nThe following notes by a student, John Latner, illustrate the results of asking questions like\nthose above. Reading an advertisement for Time magazine, Latner first annotated a copy of the\nimage and then filled out his ideas in his reading journal.\n\nFOWLcp08.doc - 10\nImage details Responses\nRed Time border positioned The framing of the wand makes this\nclearly\nover boy’s chest and a story about airport (and homeland) secu-\naround scanning wand rity, not about the boy or the guard.\nBoy with blond hair and The boy is a stereotyped all-American kid,\nblue eyes, USA colors in almost doll-like. Most American kids don’t\nclothes and Time border look like this, though. How would the ad be\ndifferent if the kid looked different?\nCaption asking ‘‘At what The position of the wand seems to imply\npoint do national security that the point of collision is in the\nmiddle\nand common sense collide?’’ of the boy or maybe the moment when\nan\ninnocent-looking kid is treated as a security\nthreat. (But aren’t there lots of kids\nthroughout history who have become sol-\ndiers or were used by adults as weapons? Is\nit completely illogical to consider kids as\npotentially dangerous?) ‘‘Common sense’’\nseems to mean taking things at face value.\nIs that the view of people who read Time?\nWouldn’t it be just the opposite----people\nbuy the mag. because they know there’s\nmore to the story than meets the eye?\nConfusing.\n4 Analyzing an image\nElements for analysis\nAs when analyzing a written work, you analyze an image by identifying its elements. The\nimage elements you might consider appear in the box below. Keep in mind that an image is a visual\ncomposition whose every element likely reflects a deliberate effort to communicate. Still, few\nimages include all the elements, and you can narrow the list further by posing a question about the\nimage you are reading, as illustrated on the next page.\nQuestion for analysis\nAs discussed on pages 158–59, you can focus your analysis of elements by framing your main\ninterest in the image as a question. John Latner concentrated his analysis of the Time ad with the\nquestion Does the ad challenge readers to view airport security differently, or does it just reinforce\ncommon perceptions? The question led Latner to focus on certain elements of the ad and to ignore\nothers, as seen in the following entry from his reading journal:\nImage elements Responses\nEmphasis The ad foregrounds the boy (especially his eyes\nlooking upward), the security agent, and the familiar\nTime cover over a scanner like a target around a\nbull’s-eye.\nPoint of view We identify with the boy----so innocent and uncom-\nfortable. We identify even more because we’re posi-\ntioned at his eye level. The security agent almost\nhovers over us, too.\nNarration The collision point of the caption (‘‘At what point do\nnational security and common sense collide?’’) seems\nto be the bull’s-eye of the scanning wand----treating a\nboy as a security threat. The ad’s commonsense opin-\nion seems to be that airport security procedures are\nflawed, unfair. But the caption’s question mark and\n‘‘Join the conversation’’ imply that there may be\nother views, too.\nColor The boy is much the brightest figure in the image,\nand his brightness emphasizes his fairness. His\nclothes are in patriotic colors (white shirt, blue\nand\nwhite pants, red tag on the pants). The Time cover\nadds more red to the flag colors.\n\nFOWLcp08.doc - 11\nAllusions The familiar Time cover and the security checkpoint\nstand out. Also, is there Christian symbolism in the\nboy’s outstretched arms, open hands, and upward\ngaze----like Christ on the cross?\nCharacterization The boy plays the role of unlikely terrorist, maybe\neven a victim. He is the stereotyped all-American\nkid,\nblond, blue-eyed, wholesome. The security agent is\nthe boy’s ‘‘interrogator’’----serious, dark, even\nmenacing.\nTension The security agent hovering over the boy is\ndisturbing.\nSo are what they’re doing and the whole busy scene\nbe-\nhind them----bound to evoke a negative response from\nanyone who’s experienced air travel in recent years.\nSample images for analysis\nThe following images give you a chance to analyze selective elements in two kinds of images,\na painting and a Web page. Questions in the annotations can help to open up your thinking.\n5 Interpreting an image\nThe strategies for interpreting an image parallel those for interpreting a written text (pp. 159–\n61). In this process you look more deeply at the elements, considering them in relation to the image\ncreator’s likely assumptions and intentions. You aim to draw reasonable inferences about why the\nimage looks as it does. Here’s a reasonable inference about the Time advertisement on page 166:\nReasonable The creators of the Time ad assume that the magazine’s readers are concerned about both\nnational security and the treatment of air travelers.\nThis inference is supported by the ad’s text and photograph: the caption specifically mentions\nnational security, and the photograph clearly emphasizes the experience of air travelers. In contrast,\nthe next inference is not reasonable because it leaps to a conclusion that is not supported by the ad:\nFaulty The creators of the Time ad assume that the magazine’s readers believe airport security\ncheckpoints are unnecessary.\nThe ad implies that readers may object to some checkpoint procedures, not that they believe the\ncheckpoints themselves are unnecessary.\n6 Synthesizing ideas about an image\nAs discussed on pages 161–62, with synthesis you take analysis and interpretation a step\nfurther to consider how a work’s elements and underlying assumptions mesh: How do the elements\nand assumptions relate to one another? What is the overall message of the image? You may also\nexpand your synthesis to view the whole image in a larger context: How does the work compare\nwith works by others? How does the work fit into the context of other works by the same author or\ngroup? What cultural, economic, political, or historical forces influence the work?\nPlacing an image in its context often requires research. For instance, to learn more about the\nassumptions underlying the Time advertisement, John Latner investigated data on the backgrounds\nand perceptions of the magazine’s readers. And to understand the marketing strategies at work in\nthe image, he consulted a book on adver-tising campaigns that, like the Time ad, promote products\nto people who already use them. The following entry from his reading journal shows the ideas\nresulting from his synthesis:\nSocial and political context\nThe emphasis on the boy plays to two views often held by travelers and Time readers: airport searches\nneedlessly inconvenience people who are highly unlikely to be terrorists, and the better alternative may be\nprofiling, treating people differently on the basis of physical characteristics such as skin and hair color.\n“Common sense”\nThe ad implies certain understandings of readers’ ―common sense‖ about national security and airport\nsecurity: security is a serious issue, many airport procedures are unreasonably broad based and time\n\nFOWLcp08.doc - 12\nconsuming, and profiling might be used to focus on people who look like terrorists. The ad doesn’t\nchallenge these perceptions, but with ―Join the conversation‖ it does suggest that the problem is open to\ninterpretation.\nMarketing context\nThe provocative photograph seems to promise an unconventional perspective on the subject, but the ad\nmostly reinforces the views assumed to be held by readers. Time’s strategy reflects marketing studies:\npeople are more likely to purchase a product that reflects their own opinions and values, even when\nthey’re acquiring it to broaden their understanding.\n7 Evaluating an image\nIf your critical reading moves on to evaluation, you’ll form judgments about the quality and\nsignificance of the image. Questions to ask for evaluation appear in the box on page 163. Briefly: Is\nthe message of the image accurate and fair, or is it distorted and biased? Can you support, refute, or\nextend the message? Does the image achieve its apparent purpose, and is the purpose worthwhile?\nHow does the image affect you?\nSee John Latner’s paper on pages 175–77 for an evaluation of the Time advertisement.\nEXERCISE 8.6 Viewing an image critically\nReview the list of visual elements on pages 167–68 and then take another close look at the Time\nadvertisement on page 166. Using the guidelines on the preceding pages, draw your own conclusions\nabout the ad. Write while reading and thinking to help yourself concentrate and develop ideas. A writing\nsuggestion based on this activity appears in Exercise 8.11, page 178.\nEXERCISE 8.7 Viewing an image critically\nSelect either the painting Liberty’s Children (p. 169) or the CNN Web page (p. 170) to examine in more\ndetail. Using the guidelines on the preceding pages, read the image methodically and critically. Write\ndown your ideas. A writing suggestion based on this activity appears in Exercise 8.12, page 178.\nEXERCISE 8.8 Comparing images critically\nEach image in this section—the advertisement, the painting, and the Web page—communicates a\nperspective on the causes or effects of terrorism in the United States. Using the guidelines on the\npreceding pages, read these three images as a group, focusing on the story they tell together and on their\nrelative effectiveness in contributing to that story. Write down your responses. A writing suggestion based\non this activity appears in Exercise 8.13, page 178.\n8c Writing critically\nCritical writing, often called critique, is largely influenced by the discipline or profession in\nwhich it occurs. Thus the topic is covered more extensively in Chapters 10–11 (argument), 50\n(literature), 51 (other humanities), 52 (social sciences), and 53 (natural and applied sciences). In\nthis introduction, we’ll look at two illustrations, one on a written work and the other on an image.\nNote Critical writing is not summarizing. You may write a summary to clarify for yourself\nwhat an author says or what an image shows, and you may briefly summarize a work in your own\nlarger piece of writing. But your job in critical writing is not just to report; it is to transmit your\nanalysis, interpretation, synthesis, and perhaps evaluation of the text.\n1 Writing critically about a text\nThe following essay by the student Charlene Robinson responds to Thomas Sowell’s ―Student\nLoans.‖ Robinson arrived at her response through the process of critical reading outlined in this\nchapter and then by gathering and organizing her ideas, developing her own central idea (or thesis)\nabout Sowell’s text, and drafting and revising until she believed she had supported her central idea.\nRobinson does not assume that her readers see the same things in Sowell’s essay or share her\nviews, so she offers evidence of Sowell’s ideas in the form of direct quotations, summaries, and\n\nFOWLcp08.doc - 13\nparaphrases (restatements in her own words). (See pp. 617–22 for more on these techniques.)\nRobinson then documents these borrowings from Sowell using the style of the Modern Language\nAssociation (MLA): the numbers in parentheses are page numbers in the book containing Sowell’s\nessay, listed at the end as a ―work cited.‖ (See Chapter 47 for more on MLA style.)\nWeighing the Costs\nIn his essay ―Student Loans,‖ the economist Thomas Sowell challenges the US government’s student-\nloan program for several reasons: a scarce resource (taxpayers’ money) goes to many undeserving students,\na high number of recipients fail to repay their loans, and the easy availability of money has led to both lower\nacademic standards and higher\ncollege tuitions. Sowell wants his readers to ―weigh the costs of things‖ (133) in order to see, as he does, that\nthe loan program should not receive so much government funding. But does he provide the evidence of cost\nand other problems to lead the reader to agree with him? The answer is no, because hard evidence is less\ncommon than debatable and unsupported assumptions about students, scarcity, and the value of education.\nSowell’s portrait of student-loan recipients is questionable. It is based on averages, some statistical and\nsome not, but averages are often deceptive. For example, Sowell cites college graduates’ low average debt of\n$7,000 to $9,000 (131) without acknowledging the fact that many students’ debt is much higher or giving the\nfull range of statistics. Similarly, Sowell dismisses ―heart-rending stories‖ of ―the low-income student with a\nhuge debt‖ as ―not at all typical‖ (132), yet he invents his own exaggerated version of the typical loan recipient:\nan affluent slacker (―Rockefellers‖ and ―Vanderbilts‖) for whom college is a ―place to hang out for a few years‖\nsponging off the government, while his or her parents clear a profit from making use of the loan program (132).\nAlthough such students (and parents) may well exist, are they really typical? Sowell does not offer any data one\nway or the other—for instance, how many loan recipients come from each income group, what percentage of\nloan funds go to each group, how many loan recipients receive significant help from their parents, and how\nmany receive none.\nAnother set of assumptions in the essay has to do with ―scarcity‖: ―There is never enough of anything to\nfully satisfy all those who want it,‖ Sowell says (131). This statement appeals to readers’ common sense, but\ndoes the ―lesson‖ of scarcity necessarily apply to the student-loan program? Sowell omits many important\nfigures needed to prove that the nation’s resources are too scarce to support the program, such as the total cost\nof the program, its percentage of the total education budget and the total federal budget, and its cost compared\nto the cost of defense, Medi-care, and other expensive programs. Moreover, Sowell does not mention the\ninterest paid by loan recipients, even though the interest must offset some of the costs of running the program\nand covering unpaid loans.\nThe most fundamental and most debatable assumption underlying Sowell’s essay is that higher education\nis a kind of commodity that not everyone is entitled to. In order to diminish the importance of graduates’\naverage debt from education loans, Sowell claims that a car loan will probably be higher (131). This\ncomparison between education and an automobile implies that the two are somehow equal as products and that\nan affordable higher education is no more a right than a new car is. Sowell also condemns the ―irresponsible‖\nstudents who drop out of school and ―the increasingly easy availability of college to people who are not very\nserious about getting an education‖ (132). But he overlooks the value of encouraging education, including\neducation of those who don’t finish college or who aren’t scholars. For many in the United States, education\nhas a greater value than that of a mere commodity like a car. And even from an economic perspective such as\nSowell’s, the cost to society of an uneducated public needs to be taken into account.\nSowell writes with conviction, and his concerns are valid: high taxes, waste, unfairness, declining\neducational standards, obtrusive government. However, the essay’s flaws make it unlikely that Sowell could\nconvince readers who do not already agree with him. He does not support his\nportrait of the typical loan recipient, he fails to demonstrate a lack of\nresources for the loan program, and he neglects the special nature of\neducation compared to other services and products. Sowell may have\nthe evidence to back up his assumptions, but by omitting it he himself does not truly weigh the costs of the loan\nprogram.\nWork Cited\nSowell, Thomas. ―Student Loans.‖ Is Reality Optional? and Other Essays.\nStanford: Hoover, 1993. 131-33.\n----Charlene Robinson (student)\n2 Writing critically about an image\nThe essay below, by the student John Latner, responds to the Time magazine advertisement.\nAs you’ve seen earlier in this chapter, Latner examined the image over several stages, each time\ndiscovering more in it and gradually developing his own ideas. In his paper Latner takes pains to be\nsure that readers will see the image as he does: he reproduces the ad, captions it, and clearly\ndescribes its features. He cites his sources using the style of the Modern Language Association\n\nFOWLcp08.doc - 14\n(Chapter 47). (All but one of Latner’s text citations lack page numbers because the sources\nthemselves are not numbered.)\nNote An image is a source just as a written work is, and like a written source it must be\nacknowledged. Latner cites the Time ad both in the image caption and in the list of works cited. If\nhe published his paper online, he would also need to seek the copyright owner’s permission to use\nthe image. See pages 633–34 for more about acknowledging sources and pages 635–37 for more\nabout permissions for online publication.\nPlaying It Safe\nAt first glance, the ―Boy at Security‖ advertisement for Time magazine (fig. 1) is both a humorous and a\nprovocative depiction of a troubling subject: airport security since September 11, 2001. A boy with an angelic\nface and a nervous expression stands obediently as a security agent passes a large scanner over his chest. The\nfamiliar red border and banner of a Time cover surround the scanner, making it the bull’s-eye of a target. The\ncaption reads, ―At what point do national security and common sense collide?‖ and then ―Join the\nconversation.‖ This eye-catching ad implies that by reading Time magazine consumers will gain alternative\nperspectives on important issues. However, a close examination of the ad reveals that it actually reinforces\npublic opinion instead of challenging it.\nThe awkward stance of the boy evokes the common complaint that airport security is intrusive and time\nconsuming (Sharkey). This attitude\nis encouraged by the cropping of the photograph, which puts the boy at our eye level (increasing our empathy\nwith him) and makes the looming security agent seem intimidating. As the agent does his work, the boy waits\nuncomfortably and travelers mill around in the background. The ad represents a travel experience that is\nfamiliar to the target audience: according to a recent survey, the typical Time reader is a white, middle-\nincome adult who takes commercial flights at least yearly (Schulman, Ronca, and Bucuvalas).\nThe advertisement also taps into a dilemma and debate about airport security. Everyone wants terrorists\nstopped from boarding airplanes, but many people believe that security procedures go too far by treating all\ntravelers as potential threats, even those who aren’t the least bit suspicious (Sharkey). The boy in the ad is a\nmost unlikely terrorist. Besides\nbeing quite young, he looks like the stereotype of the all-American child: blond, blue-eyed, neatly dressed in\npatriotic colors (white shirt and blue and white pants with a red tag). With such a figure at its center, the ad\nimplies and appeals to a preference for profiling travelers—singling people out for suspicion solely because of\nnationality or physical traits such as skin and hair color. Judging from the public comments in the news media,\nmany Americans, reluctantly or not, favor profiling over the discomfort and inconvenience caused by searching\neveryone.\nWhy would a news magazine print a seemingly provocative ad that actually does little more than appeal\nto beliefs that its readers already hold? An answer can be found in the research of marketing analyst Marc\nGobe, who has found that customers prefer to build long-term relationships with companies that represent their\nviews (32). The intended audience for the Time ad is people who are already reading the magazine. The ad\nattempts to reinforce the loyalty of these customers by flattering their eagerness for ―conversation.‖ The\nattention-getting image communicates a cutting-edge approach—something to make this magazine seem more\ninteresting than its competitors—but the ad doesn’t risk challenging and thus alienating readers.\nIf Time truly wanted to demonstrate that it offers rich and diverse opinions on airport security and other\nissues, it might have juxtaposed two different passengers receiving the same treatment: the seemingly innocent\nchild and someone fitting the profile of a terrorist. Or, to challenge readers’ assumptions, the existing\nphotograph might have shown the boy looking defiantly into the camera, raising doubts about his innocence.\nEither image would have been more thought provoking, but at the risk of disturbing consumers instead of\nattracting them to the magazine. Ultimately, the Time advertisement achieves its apparent purpose because it\ncreates the illusion of daring without actually taking readers outside their comfort zone.\nWorks Cited\nGobe, Marc. Citizen Brand. New York: Allworth, 2002.\nSchulman, Ronca, and Bucuvalas, Inc. ―Time Magazine/SRBI—March\n15-17, 2005, Survey.‖ 18 Mar. 2005. 27 May 2005 <http:// www.srbi.com/TimePoll-3-18-05b.pdf>.\nSharkey, Joe. ―Pants and Profiling: Life on the Front Lines of Checkpoint Security at the Nation’s Airports.‖\nNew York Times on the Web 9 Jan. 2003. 20 May 2005 <http://travel2.nytimes.com/mem/travel/\narticle-page.html?res=9D07E2DE1239>.\nTime magazine. Advertisement. Time 7 Oct. 2002: 42.\n----John Latner (student)\nEXERCISE 8.9 Responding to critical writing\nRead Charlene Robinson’s and John Latner’s essays carefully. Do you think the authors’ critiques are\naccurate and fair? Are they perceptive? Do the authors provide enough evidence to convince you of their\npoints? Do they miss anything you would have mentioned? Write your responses to one of the essays in a\nbrief critique of your own. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\n\nFOWLcp08.doc - 15\nEXERCISE 8.10 Writing critically about a text\nWrite an essay based on your own critical reading of Thomas Sowell’s ―Student Loans‖ (Exercise 8.4, p.\n164). Your critique may be entirely different from Charlene Robinson’s, or you may have developed some\nof the same points. If there are similarities, they should be expressed and supported in your own way, in\nthe context of your own critical perspective.\nEXERCISE 8.11 Writing critically about an image\nWrite an essay based on your own critical reading of the Time advertisement (Exercise 8.6, p. 172). Your\ncritique may be entirely different from John Latner’s, or you may have developed some of the same\npoints. If there are similarities, they should be expressed and supported in your own way, in the context of\nyour own critical perspective.\nEXERCISE 8.12 Writing critically about an image\nWrite an essay based on your critical reading of the painting Liberty’s Children or the CNN Web page\n(Exercise 8.7, p. 172).\nEXERCISE 8.13 Writing critically about several images\nWrite an essay based on your critical reading and comparison of the three images in this chapter (Exercise\n8.8, p. 172).\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp08.doc - 16\nVisit the companion Web site for more help and additional exercises on\ncritical thinking, reading, and writing.\nTechniques of critical reading\nFor reading a work of literature, which requires a somewhat different approach, see pp. 735–43.\nWriting: making notes on your reading throughout the process (next page)\nPreviewing: getting background; skimming (p. 153)\nReading: interacting with and absorbing the text (pp. 153–54)\nSummarizing: distilling and understanding content (p. 157)\nForming your critical response (pp. 157–63)\nAnalyzing: separating into parts\nInterpreting: inferring meaning and assumptions\nSynthesizing: reassembling parts; making connections\nEvaluating: judging quality and value\nQuestions for previewing a text\nWhat is the work’s subject and structure? Following the steps outlined on pages 138–39, gauge\nlength and level, read the title and introduction for clues to the topic and main ideas, read the headings,\nnote highlighted words (defined terms), examine illustrations, and read the summary or conclusion.\nWhat are the facts of publication? Does the date of publication suggest currency or datedness? Does\nthe publisher or publication specialize in a particular kind of material—scholarly articles, say, or\npopular books? For a Web document, who or what sponsors the site: an individual? a nonprofit\norganization? an academic institution? a corporation? a government body? (See pp. 672 and 675 on\nlocating the authors of online sources.)\nWhat do you know about the author? Does a biography tell you about the author’s publications,\ninterests, biases, and reputation in the field? For an online source, which may be posted by an\nunfamiliar or anonymous author, what can you gather about the author from his or her words? If\npossible, trace unfamiliar authors to learn more about them.\nWhat is your preliminary response? What do you already know about the author’s topic? What\nquestions do you have about either the topic or the author’s approach to it? What biases of your own\nmight influence your reception of the work—for instance, curiosity, boredom, or an outlook similar or\nopposed to the author’s?\nBasic contradiction between economics and politics\nbiblical reference?\nironic and dismissive language\npoliticians = fools? or irresponsible\nGuidelines for analysis, interpretation,\nand synthesis\nWhat is the purpose of your reading?\nWhat questions do you have about the work, given your purpose?\nWhat elements does the most interesting question highlight? What elements might you ignore as a\nresult?\nHow do you interpret the meaning and significance of the elements? What are your assumptions\nabout the work? What do you infer about the author’s assumptions?\nWhat patterns can you see in (or synthesize from) the elements? How do the elements relate? How\ndoes this whole work relate to other works?\nWhat do you conclude about the work? What does this conclusion add to the work?\nYou can download these guidelines from ablongman.com/littlebrown. Create a copy each time you’re\nreading a work critically, and use the questions to prompt your written responses.\nGuidelines for evaluation\nWhat are your reactions to the work? What in the work are you responding to?\nHow sound are the work’s central idea and evidence?\nHow well does the author achieve his or her purpose? How worthwhile is the purpose?\nHow authoritative, trustworthy, and sincere is the author?\nHow unified and coherent is the work? Do its parts all support a central idea and clearly relate to\none another?\nWhat do color, graphics, or (online) sound or video contribute to the work? Do such elements\nadd meaning or merely decoration?\n\nFOWLcp08.doc - 17\nWhat is the overall quality and significance of the work?\nDo you agree or disagree with the work? Can you support, refute, or extend it?\nYou can download these guidelines from ablongman.com/littlebrown. Create a copy each time you’re\nevaluating a work, and use the questions to prompt your written responses.\nQuestions for previewing an image\nWhat do you see? What is most striking about the image? What is its subject? What is the gist of any\ntext or symbols? What is the overall effect of the image?\nWhat are the facts of publication? Where did you first see the image? Do you think the image was\ncreated especially for that location or for others as well? What can you tell about when the image was\ncreated?\nWhat do you know about the person or group that created the image? For instance, was the\ncreator an artist, scholar, news organization, or corporation? What seems to have been the creator’s\npurpose?\nWhat is your preliminary response? What about the image interests, confuses, or disturbs you? Are\nthe form, style, and subject familiar or unfamiliar? How might your knowledge, experiences, and\nvalues influence your reception of the image?\nAnnotation of an image\nAgent checking boy’s body (for weapons?)\nSign = airport security area\nBlond kid in preppy clothes = typical American child?\nWhat’s she doing?\nRed Time cover = target\nRed with boy’s clothes = USA\nInteresting question—crux\nof controversy\nAuthor and source both = Time mag. Why does Time\npromote itself to people\nwho are already reading it?\nAdvertisement appearing in Time\nmagazine, October 7, 2002\nElements of images\nEmphasis: Most images pull your eyes to certain features: a graph line moving sharply upward, a\nprovocative figure, bright color, thick lines, light against shadow, and so on. The cropping of a\nphotograph or, say, the date range in a chart will also reflect what the image creator considers most\nimportant.\nNarration: Most images tell stories, whether in a sequence (a TV commercial or a graph showing\nchanges over time) or at a single moment (a photograph, a painting, or a pie chart). Sometimes dialog\nor a title or caption contributes to the story.\nPoint of view: The image creator influences responses by taking account of both the viewer’s\nphysical relation to the image subject—for instance, whether it is seen head-on or from above—and\nthe viewer’s assumed attitude toward the subject.\nArrangement: Patterns among colors or forms, figures in the foreground and background, and\nelements that are juxtaposed or set apart contribute to the image’s meaning and effect.\nColor: An image’s colors can direct the viewer’s attention and convey the creator’s attitude toward\nthe subject. Color may also suggest a mood, an era, a cultural connection, or another frame for\nviewing the image.\nCharacterization: The figures and objects in an image have certain qualities—sympathetic or not,\ndesirable or not, and so on. Their characteristics reflect the roles they play in the image’s story.\nContext: The source of an image or the background in an image affects its meaning, whether it is a\ngraph from a scholarly journal or a photo of a car on a sunny beach.\nTension: Images often communicate a problem or seize attention with features that seem wrong, such\nas misspelled or misaligned words, distorted figures, or controversial relations between characters.\nAllusions: An allusion is a reference to something the audience is likely to recognize and respond to.\nExamples include a cultural symbol such as a dollar sign, a mythological figure such as a unicorn, or a\nfamiliar movie character such as Darth Vader from Star Wars.\nElements in a painting\nEmphasis: What does light emphasize? Where does the painting guide your eyes next, and how?\nNarration: What story does the painting tell? How does it tell the story?\nColor: What mood do the colors create? How do they relate to the subject?\n\nFOWLcp08.doc - 18\nCharacterization: What role does the central figure play in the story? What other characters can you identify?\nTension: What elements are unexpected, even disturbing?\nLiberty’s Children, 2002, painting by Ron Oden\nElements of a Web page\nNarration: What story is being told by the Web page as a whole and by the chart? Who is telling the story, and why?\nArrangement: How are the bars in the chart organized? What does their arrangement contribute to the story?\nPoint of view: What can you tell about the intended audience?What is the audience’s interest in the story?\nContext: How do the CNN source and the page’s banner and titles affect the story being told by the chart?\nWeb page from CNN.com, 2001\nIntroduction\nSummary of Sowell’s essay\nRobinson’s critical question\nThesis statement\nFirst main point\nEvidence for\nfirst point: paraphrases and quotations from Sowell’s text\nEvidence for first point: Sowell’s\nomissions\nTransition to second main point\nSecond main point\nEvidence for second point: Sowell’s\nomissions\nThird main point\nEvidence for third point: paraphrases and quotations of Sowell’s text\nConclusion\nAcknowledgment of Sowell’s concerns\nSummary of three main points\nReturn to theme of\nintroduction: weighing costs\nReference to complete source (in MLA style)\nIntroduction\nDescription of the image\nThesis statement\nCaption giving the ad’s main idea and source\nFig. 1. ‘‘Boy at Security,’’ an advertisement appearing in Time\nmagazine, promotes the magazine as a source of challenging views on\ncurrent events. From Time 7 Oct. 2002: 42.\nFirst main point\nEvidence for first point: news article (Sharkey) and Latner’s interpretation of image details\nEvidence for first point: survey of magazine’s readers\nSecond main point\nEvidence for second point: news article\nEvidence for second point: Latner’s interpretation of image details\nLatner’s critical question\nEvidence for answer: findings of an\nanalyst\nLatner’s interpretation of source and\napplication to the image\nConclusion\nPossible alternatives to ad’s safe approach\nSummary and restatement of thesis\nList of works cited (in MLA style)\n\nFOWLcp09.doc - 1\n9\nCHAPTER\nReading Arguments Critically\nArgument is writing that attempts to open readers’ minds to an opinion, change readers’ own\nopinions, or move readers to action. A good argument is neither a cold exercise in logic nor an\nattempt to beat others into submission. It is a work of negotiation and problem solving in which\nboth writer and reader search for the knowledge that will create common ground between them.\nOf course, not all arguments are ―good.‖ Whether deliberately or not, some are unclear,\nincomplete, misleading, or downright false. The negotiation fails; the problem remains unsolved.\nThis chapter will help you read written arguments critically, and the next chapter will help you\nwrite effective arguments. After that, in Chapter 11, you’ll see how to read and use visual\narguments.\nThe ways of reading and writing arguments described in this chapter and the next may be\nuncomfortable to you if your native culture approaches such writing differently. In some cul-tures,\nfor example, a writer is expected to begin indirectly, to avoid asserting his or her opinion outright,\nor to establish a compromise rather than argue a position. In American academic and business\nsettings, readers and writers look or aim for a well-articulated opinion, evidence gathered from\nmany sources, and a direct and concise argument for the opinion.\nRecognizing the elements of argument\nFew arguments are an easy read. Most demand the attentive critical reading discussed in the\nprevious chapter. (If you haven’t read pp. 151–63, you should do so before continuing.) As a\nreader of argument, your purpose will almost always be the same: you’ll want to know whether you\nshould be convinced by the argument. This purpose focuses your attention on the elements that\nmake an argument convincing, or not.\nIn a scheme adapted from the work of the British philosopher Stephen Toulmin, an argument\nhas three main elements:\nClaims: positive statements that require support. In a written argument the central claim is\nstated outright in a thesis statement (see p. 27). This central claim is what the argument is\nabout. For instance:\nIn both its space and its equipment, the college’s chemistry laboratory is outdated.\nSeveral minor claims, such as that the present equipment is\ninadequate, will contribute to the central assertion.\nEvidence: the facts, examples, expert opinions, and other information that support the claims.\n(Toulmin calls evidence data or grounds, terms that indicate both its specificity and its work\nas an argument’s foundation.) Evidence to support the preceding claim might include the\nfollowing:\nThe present lab’s age and square feet\nAn inventory of equipment\nThe testimony of chemistry professors\nLike the claims, the evidence is always stated outright.\nAssumptions: the writer’s underlying (and often unstated) beliefs, opinions, principles, or\ninferences that tie the evidence to the claims. (Toulmin calls these assumptions warrants: they\njustify making the claims on the basis of the evidence provided.) For instance, the following\n\nFOWLcp09.doc - 2\nassumption might connect the evidence of professors’ testimony with the claim that a new lab\nis needed:\nChemistry professors are the most capable of evaluating the present lab’s quality.\nIn the following pages, we’ll examine each of these elements along with several others: tone\nand language, reasonableness, and common errors in reasoning. Charlene Robinson’s ―Weighing\nthe Costs‖ in the previous chapter (pp. 173–75) provides a good example of critically reading an\nargument for its claims, evidence, and assumptions.\nTesting claims\nThe claims or assertions in an argument will likely be statements of opinion, fact, belief, or\nprejudice. It’s important to distinguish between the kinds of statements and to analyze the\ndefinitions of terms.\nRecognizing opinions\nAn opinion is a judgment based on facts and arguable on the basis of facts. Reasonable people\ncould and probably do disagree over opinions, and they are potentially changeable: with more facts,\na writer might change opinions partly or wholly.\nThe thesis statement of an effective argument is an opinion, often one of the following:\nA claim about past or present reality:\nIn both its space and its equipment, the college’s chemistry laboratory is outdated.\nAcademic cheating increases with students’ economic insecurity.\nA claim of value:\nThe new room fees are unjustified given the condition of the dormitories.\nComputer music pirates undermine the system that encourages the very creation of music.\nA recommendation for a course of action, often a solution to a\nperceived problem:\nThe college’s outdated chemistry laboratory should be replaced incrementally over the next five years.\nSchools and businesses can help to resolve the region’s traffic congestion by implementing car pools and\nrewarding participants.\nThe backbone of an argument consists of specific claims that support the thesis statement. These\nmay be statements of opinion, too, or they may state facts or beliefs.\nOpinions do not make arguments by themselves. As a critical reader, you\nmust satisfy yourself that the writer has specified the evidence for the\nopinions and that the assumptions linking claims and evidence are clear\nand believable.\nRecognizing facts\nA fact may be a verifiable statement—that is, one that can be proved as true:\nLast year tuition increased 16 percent.\nOr it may be an inference from verifiable facts:\nOver their lifetimes, four-year college graduates earn almost twice as much as high school graduates.\nA claim of fact does not work as the thesis of an argument. Although people often dispute\nfacts, they are not fundamentally arguable because ultimately they can be verified. Facts have\nanother important role in argument, providing crucial evidence for other claims (see p. 184).\n\nFOWLcp09.doc - 3\nRecognizing beliefs\nA belief is a conviction based on cultural or personal faith, morality, or values:\nAbortion is legalized murder.\nCapital punishment is legalized murder.\nThe primary goal of government should be to provide equality of opportunity for all.\nSuch statements are often called opinions because they express viewpoints, but they are not\nbased on facts and other evidence. Since they cannot be disproved by facts or even contested on the\nbasis of facts, they cannot serve as the central or supporting claims of an argument. Statements of\nbelief do figure in argument, however: they can serve as a kind of evidence, and they often form\nthe assumptions that link claims and evidence (see pp. 185 and 187–88).\nRecognizing prejudices\nOne kind of assertion that has no place in argument is a prejudice, an opinion based on\ninsufficient or unexamined evidence:\nWomen are bad drivers.\nFat people are jolly.\nTeenagers are irresponsible.\nUnlike a belief, a prejudice is testable: it can be contested and disproved on the basis of facts.\nVery often, however, we form prejudices or accept them from others—parents, friends, the\ncommunications media—without questioning their meaning or testing their truth. Writers who\ndisplay prejudice do not deserve the confidence and agreement of readers. Readers who accept\nprejudice are not thinking critically.\nWhen reading arguments that appear online, you should be especially vigilant for claims of\nbelief or prejudice that pose as considered opinions. Anyone with a computer and an Internet\nconnection can post anything on the Internet, without passing it through an editorial screening like\nthat undergone by books and articles in journals and magazines. The filtering of such material is\nentirely up to the reader.\nLooking for defined terms\nIn any argument, but especially in arguments about abstract ideas, clear and consistent\ndefinition of terms is essential. In the following claim, the writer is not clear about what she means\nby the crucial term justice:\nOver the past few decades, justice has deteriorated so badly that it almost does not exist anymore.\nThe word justice is abstract: it does not refer to anything specific or concrete and in fact has varied\nmeanings. (The five definitions in The American Heritage Dictionary include ―the principle of\nmoral rightness‖ and ―the administration and procedure of law.‖) When the writer specifies her\nmeaning, her assertion is much clearer:\nIf by justice we mean treating people fairly, punishing those who commit crimes, and protecting the\nvictims of those crimes, then justice has deteriorated badly over the past few decades.\nWriters who use highly abstract words such as justice, equality, success, and maturity have a\nresponsibility to define them. If the word is important to the argument, such a definition may take\nan entire paragraph. As a reader you have the obligation to evaluate the writer’s definitions before\nyou accept his or her assertions. (See pp. 93–94 for more on definition and a paragraph defining the\nabstract word quality.)\nWeighing evidence\nIn argument, evidence demonstrates the validity of the writer’s claims. If the evidence is\ninadequate or questionable, the claims are at best doubtful.\n\nFOWLcp09.doc - 4\nRecognizing kinds of evidence\nWriters draw on several kinds of evidence to support their claims.\nFacts\nFacts are statements whose truth can be verified or inferred (see p. 182).\nFacts employing numbers are statistics:\nOf those polled, 62 percent stated a preference for a flat tax.\nIn 2005 there were 1,370,237 men and women on active duty in the US armed forces.\nThe average American household consists of 2.58 persons.\nNumbers may be implied:\nEarth is closer to the sun than Saturn is.\nThe cost of medical care is rising.\nOr a fact may involve no numbers at all:\nThe city council adjourned without taking a vote.\nThe President vetoed the bill.\nExamples\nExamples are specific instances of the point being made, including historical precedents and\npersonal experiences. The passage below uses a personal narrative as partial support for the claim\nin the first sentence:\nBesides broadening students’ knowledge, required courses can also introduce students to possible careers\nthat they otherwise would have known nothing about. Somewhat reluctantly, I enrolled in a psychology\ncourse to satisfy the requirement for work in social science. But what I learned in the course about human\nbehavior has led me to consider becoming a clinical psychologist instead of an engineer.\nExpert opinions\nExpert opinions are the judgments formed by authorities on the basis of their own\nexamination of the facts. In the following passage the writer cites the opinion of an expert to\nsupport the claim in the first sentence:\nDespite the fact that affirmative action places some individuals at a disadvantage, it remains necessary to\nright the wrongs inflicted historically on whole groups of people. Howard Glickstein, a past director of the\nUS Commission on Civil Rights, maintains that ―it simply is not possible to achieve equality and\nfairness‖ unless the previous grounds for discrimination (such as sex, race, and national origin) are now\nconsidered as grounds for admission to schools and jobs (26).\nAs this passage illustrates, a citation of expert opinion should always refer the reader to the source,\nhere indicated by author’s name and the page number in parentheses, ―(26).‖ Such a citation is also\ngenerally accompanied by a reference to the expert’s credentials. See pages 626–27 and 637–38.\nIn some cultures a person with high standing in government, society, or organized religion\nmay be considered an authority on many different subjects. In American academic and business\nsettings, authority tends to derive from study, learning, and experience: the more knowledge a\nperson can demonstrate about a subject, the more authority he or she has. See the next page on\nrelevant evidence.\nAppeals to beliefs or needs\nAn appeal to beliefs or needs asks readers to accept an assertion in part because they already\naccept it as true without evidence or because it coincides with their needs. Each of the following\nexamples combines such an appeal (second sentence) with a summary of factual evidence (first\nsentence).\nThus the chemistry laboratory is outdated in its equipment. In addi-tion, its shabby, antiquated\nappearance shames the school, making it seem a second-rate institution. [Appeals to readers’ belief that\ntheir school is or should be first-rate.]\n\nFOWLcp09.doc - 5\nThat police foot patrollers reduce crime has already been demonstrated. Such officers might also restore\nour sense that our neighborhoods are orderly, stable places. [Appeals to readers’ need for order and\nstability.]\n(For more on beliefs, see p. 182. For more on appeals to emotion, see pp. 208–09).\nJudging the reliability of evidence\nTo support claims and convince readers, evidence must be reliable. The tests of reliability for\nappeals to readers’ beliefs and needs are specific to the situation: whether they are appropriate for\nthe argument and correctly gauge how readers actually feel (see p. 209). With the other kinds of\nevidence, the standards are more general, applying to any argument.\nAccuracy\nAccurate evidence is true:\nIt is drawn from trustworthy sources.\nIt is quoted exactly.\nIt is presented with the original meaning undistorted.\nIn an essay favoring gun control, a writer should not rely exclusively on\nprocontrol sources, which are undoubtedly biased. Instead, the writer\nshould also cite anticontrol sources (representing the opposite bias) and\nneutral sources (attempting to be unbiased). If the writer quotes an expert,\nthe quotation should present the expert’s true meaning, not just a few\nwords that happen to support the writer’s argument. (As a reader you may\nhave difficulty judging the accuracy of quotations if you are not familiar\nwith the expert’s opinions.)\nNot just opinions but also facts and examples may be misinterpreted or distorted. Suppose you\nwere reading an argument for ex-tending a three-year-old law allowing the police to stop vehicles\nrandomly as a means of apprehending drunk drivers. If the author cited statistics showing that the\nnumber of drunk-driving accidents dropped in the first two years of the law but failed to note that\nthe number rose back to the previous level in the third year, then the\nevidence would be distorted and thus inaccurate. You or any reader would be justified in\nquestioning the entire argument, no matter how accurate the rest seemed.\nRelevance\nRelevant evidence pertains to the argument:\nIt comes from sources with authority on the subject.\nIt relates directly to the point the writer is making.\nIt is current.\nIn an argument against a method of hazardous-waste disposal, a writer should not offer his\nchurch minister’s opinion as evidence unless she is an authority on the subject and her expertise is\nup to date. If she is an authority on Method A and not Method B, the writer should not use her\nopinion as evidence against Method B. Similarly, the writer’s own experience of living near a\nhazardous-waste site may be relevant evidence if it pertains to his thesis. His authority in this case\nis that of a close observer and a citizen. (See also p. 194 on the fallacy of false authority.)\nRepresentativeness\nRepresentative evidence is true to its context:\nIt reflects the full range of the sample from which it is said to be drawn.\nIt does not overrepresent any element of the sample.\nIn an essay arguing that dormitories should stay open during school holidays, a writer might\nsay that ―the majority of the school’s students favor leaving the dormitories open.‖ But that writer\n\nFOWLcp09.doc - 6\nwould mislead you and other readers if the claim were based only on a poll of her roommates and\ndormitory neighbors. A few dormitory res-idents could not be said to represent the entire student\nbody, par-ticularly the nonresident students. To be representative, the poll would have to take in\nmany more students in proportions that reflect the numbers of resident and nonresident students on\ncampus.\nAdequacy\nAdequate evidence is sufficient:\nIt is plentiful enough to support the writer’s assertions.\nIt is specific enough to support the writer’s assertions.\nA writer arguing against animal abuse cannot hope to win over readers solely with statements\nabout her personal experiences and claims of her opinions. Her experience may indeed be relevant\nevidence if, say, she has worked with animals or witnessed animal abuse. And her opinions are\nindeed important to the argument, so that readers know what she thinks. But even together these are\nnot adequate evidence: they cannot substitute entirely for facts, nonpersonal examples, and the\nopinions of experts to demonstrate abuse and describe the scope of the problem.\nDiscovering assumptions\nAssumptions connect evidence to claims: they are the opinions or beliefs that explain why a\nparticular piece of evidence is relevant to a particular claim. As noted in the preceding chapter on\ncritical thinking, assumptions are not flaws in arguments but necessities: we all acquire beliefs and\nopinions that shape our view of the world. Here are some examples that you, or people you know,\nmay hold:\nCriminals should be punished.\nHard work is virtuous.\nTeachers’ salaries are too low.\nAssumptions are inevitable in argument, but they aren’t neutral. For one thing, an assumption\ncan weaken an argument. Say that a writer claims that real estate development should be prevented\nin your town. As evidence for this claim, the writer offers facts about past developments that have\nreplaced older buildings. But the evidence is relevant to the claim only if you accept the writer’s\nextreme assumptions that old buildings are always worthy and new development is always bad.\nIn such a case, the writer’s bias may not even be stated. Hence a second problem: in\narguments both sound and unsound, assumptions are not always explicit. Following are a claim and\nevidence forming a reasonable argument. What is the unstated assumption?\nClaim\nThe town should create a plan to manage building preservation and new development.\nEvidence\nExamples of how such plans work; expert opinions on how and why both preservation and development\nare needed.\nIn this instance the assumption is that neither uncontrolled development nor zero development is\nhealthy for the town. If you can accept this assumption, you should be able to accept the writer’s\nclaim (though you might still disagree over particulars).\nHere are some tips for dealing with assumptions:\nWatching language, hearing tone\nTone is the expression of the writer’s attitudes toward himself or herself,\ntoward the subject, and toward the reader (see p. 12 for a discussion). Tone\n\nFOWLcp09.doc - 7\ncan tell you quite a bit about the writer’s intentions, biases, and\ntrustworthiness. For example:\nSome women cite personal growth as a reason for pursuing careers while raising children. Of course, they\nare equally concerned with the personal growth of the children they relegate to ―child-care specialists‖\nwhile they work.\nIn the second sentence this writer is being ironic, saying one thing while meaning another. The\nword relegate and the quotation marks with child-care specialists betray the writer’s belief that\nworking mothers may selfishly neglect their children for their own needs. Irony can sometimes be\neffective in argument, but here it marks the author as insincere in dealing with the complex issues\nof working parents and child care.\nWhen reading arguments, you should be alert for the author’s language. Look for words that\nconnote, or suggest, certain attitudes and evoke certain responses in readers. (Notice your own\nresponses to these word pairs with related meanings but different connotations: daring/foolhardy,\ndislike/detest, glad/joyous, angry/rabid, freedom/license.) Connotative language is no failure in\nargument; indeed, the strongest arguments use it skillfully to appeal to readers’ hearts as well as\ntheir minds (see pp. 208–09). But be suspicious if the language runs counter to the substance of the\nargument.\nLook also for evasive words. Euphemisms, such as attack of a sexual nature for ―rape‖ or\npeace-keeping force for a war-making army, are supposedly inoffensive substitutes for words that\nmay frighten or offend readers (see pp. 513–14). In argument, though, they are sometimes used to\nhide or twist the truth. An honest, forthright arguer will avoid them.\nFinally, watch carefully for sexist, racist, and other biased language that reveals deep\nignorance or, worse, entrenched prejudice on the part of the writer. Obvious examples are broad\nfor woman and fag for homosexual. (See pp. 514–17 for more on such language.)\nJudging reasonableness\nThe reasonableness of an argument is the sense you get as a reader that the author is fair and\nsincere. The reasonable writer does not conceal or distort facts, hide prejudices, mask belief as\nopinion, manipulate you with language, or resort to any of dozens of devices used unconsciously\nby those who don’t know better and deliberately by those who do.\nReasonableness involves all the elements of argument examined so far: claims, evidence,\nassumptions, and language. In addition, the fair, sincere argument always avoids so-called fallacies\n(covered in the next section), and it acknowledges the opposition.\nJudging whether a writer deals adequately with his or her opposition is a fairly simple matter\nfor the reader of argument. By def-inition, an arguable issue has more than one side. Even if you\nhave no preconceptions about a subject, you will know that another side exists. If the writer\npretends otherwise or dismisses the opposition too quickly, you are justified in questioning the\nhonesty and fairness of the argument. (For the more complicated business of writ-ing an\nacknowledgment of opposing views, see pp. 210–11.)\nEXERCISE 9.1 Reading arguments critically\nFollowing are two brief arguments. Though not directly opposed, the two arguments do represent different\nstances on environmental issues. Read each argument critically, following the process outlined in the\nprevious chapter (pp. 152–63) and answering the questions in the box on page 181 (questions about\nclaims, evidence, assumptions, and the other elements of argument). Develop your responses in writing so\nthat you can refer to them for later exercises and class discussion.\nThe Environmental Crisis Is Not Our Fault\nI am as responsible as most eco-citizens: I bike everywhere; I don’t own a car; I recycle newspapers,\nbottles, cans, and plastics; I have a vegetable garden in the summer; I buy organic products; and\nI put all vegetable waste into my backyard compost bin, probably\nthe only one in all of Greenwich Village. But I don’t at the same time believe that I am saving the planet,\nor in fact doing anything of much consequence about the various eco-crises around us. What’s more, I\ndon’t even believe that if ―all of us‖ as individuals started doing the same it would make any but the\nslightest difference.\n\nFOWLcp09.doc - 8\nLeave aside ozone depletion and rain forest destruction—those are patently corporate crimes that no\nindividual actions can remedy\nto any degree. Take, instead, energy consumption in this country. In the most recent figures, residential\nconsumption was 7.2 percent of the total, commercial 5.5 percent, and industrial 23.3 percent; of the\nremainder, 27.8 percent was transportation (about one-third of it by private car) and 36.3 percent was\nelectric generation (about one-third for residential use). Individual energy use, in sum, was something like\n28 percent of total consumption. Although you and I cutting down on energy consumption would have\nsome small effect (and should be done), it is surely the energy consumption of industry and other large\ninstitutions such as government and agribusiness that needs to be addressed first. And it is industry and\ngovernment that must be forced to explain what their consumption is for, what is produced by it, how\nnecessary it is, and how it can be drastically reduced.\nThe point is that the ecological crisis is essentially beyond ―our‖ control, as citizens or householders\nor consumers or even voters. It is not something that can be halted by recycling or double-pane insulation.\nIt is the inevitable by-product of our modern industrial civilization, dominated by capitalist production\nand consumption and ser- viced and protected by various institutions of government, federal to local. It\ncannot possibly be altered or reversed by simple individual ac-tions, even by the actions of the millions\nwho take part in Earth Day\n—even if they all go home and fix their refrigerators and from then\non walk to work. Nothing less than a drastic overhaul of this civilization and an abandonment of its\ningrained gods—progress, growth, exploitation, technology, materialism, anthropocentricity, and power\n—will do anything substantial to halt our path to environmental de-struction, and it’s hard to see how life-\nstyle solutions will have an ef-fect on that.\nWhat I find truly pernicious about such solutions is that they get people thinking they are actually\nmaking a difference and doing their part to halt the destruction of the earth: ―There, I’ve taken all the\nbottles to the recycling center and used my string bag at the grocery store; I guess that’ll take care of\nglobal warming.‖ It is the kind of thing that diverts people from the hard truths and hard choices and hard\nactions, from the recognition that they have to take on the larger forces of society—corporate and\ngovernmental—where true power, and true destructiveness, lie.\nAnd to the argument that, well, you have to start somewhere to raise people’s consciousness, I\nwould reply that this individualistic ap-proach does not in fact raise consciousness. It does not move\npeople beyond their old familiar liberal perceptions of the world, it does nothing to challenge the belief in\ntechnofix or write-your-Congressperson solutions, and it does not begin to provide them with the new\nvocabulary and modes of thought necessary for a true change of consciousness. We need, for example, to\nthink of recycling centers not as the answer to our waste problems, but as a confession that the system of\npackaging and production in this society is out of control. Recycling centers are like hospitals; they are\nthe institutions at the end of the cycle that take care of problems that would never exist if ecological\ncriteria had operated at the beginning of the cycle. Until we have those kinds of understandings, we will\nnot do anything with consciousness except reinforce it with the same misguided ideas that created the\ncrisis.\n—Kirkpatrick Sale\nMyths We Wouldn’t Miss\nThere are tall tales and legends. There are fables and apocryphal stories. And there are myths—a\nnumber of which we would like to see disappear. Here are some myths that would not be missed:\nMYTH: Offshore drilling would be an ecological disaster.\nTruth is, there hasn’t been a serious spill in US waters resulting from offshore drilling operations in\nmore than thirty years—and even that one, in Santa Barbara Channel in 1969, caused no permanent\ndamage to the environment.\nThis is why we always have such a problem with the reasoning of those who call for moratoriums or\noutright bans on such activity while the nation continues to import foreign oil. The fact is, oil industry\noffshore drilling operations cause less pollution than urban runoff, atmos-pheric phenomena, municipal\ndischarges or natural seeps.\nWhy this nation would choose not to drill for oil and not to\nprovide the jobs, profits and taxes such activity would mean for the American economy when there are no\nbetter alternatives is a mystery we hope puzzles others as much as it does us.\nMYTH: America is a profligate waster of energy.\nThe myth makers like to throw around numbers that read like this: with only 5 percent of the\nworld’s population, the US uses about 25 percent of the world’s energy. But ours is a big country—three\nthousand miles from one ocean to the next. Transportation accounts for more than 60 percent of US oil\nuse. We could probably cut down if we moved everybody into one corner of the country, but where is the\nwaste?\nIt certainly isn’t the automobiles that are inefficient. They are twice as efficient as the ones we used\nthirty years ago. If American\ndrivers use more gasoline than their counterparts in Europe and Japan, it may just have something to do\nwith the country’s size.\n\nFOWLcp09.doc - 9\nIn fact, proof of the country’s size may be in our economic output—and may also hold a clue as to\nwhy we use the energy we do. Despite having only 5 percent of the world’s population, America may\nindeed use 25 percent of the world’s energy. However, according to the latest statistics, we also produce\nabout 25 percent of the world’s goods and services. Again, where’s the waste?\nMYTH: Conservation is the answer to America’s energy problems.\nNo doubt about it, we all need to be careful of the amount of energy we use. But as long as this\nnation’s economy needs to grow, we are going to need energy to fuel that growth.\nFor the foreseeable future, there are no viable alternatives to petroleum as the major source of\nenergy, especially for transportation fuels. Let’s face it. Over the past thirty years we have learned to\nconserve—in our factories, our homes, our cars. We probably can—and should—do more. But\nconservation and new exploration should not be mutually exclusive, because even without an increase in\nenergy consumption, we are using up domestic reserves of oil and gas and must replace them. For the\ngood of the economy, those reserves should be replaced with new domestic production, to the extent\neconomically possible. Otherwise, the only solutions would be additional imports or no growth. And\nstifling growth would be a gross disservice to the people for whom such growth would provide the\nopportunity for a better life.\nSimply put, America is going to need more energy for all its people.\nAnd that is no myth.\n—Oil corporation advertisement\nRecognizing fallacies\nFallacies—errors in argument— fall into two groups. Some evade the issue of the argument.\nOthers treat the argument as if it were much simpler than it is.\nRecognizing evasions\nThe central claim of an argument defines an issue or question: Should real estate development\nbe controlled? Should drug testing be mandatory in the workplace? An effective argument faces the\ncentral issue squarely with relevant opinions, beliefs, and evidence. An ineffective argument\ndodges the issue.\nBegging the question\nA writer begs the question by treating an opinion that is open to question as if it were already\nproved or disproved. (In essence, the writer begs readers to accept his or her ideas from the start.)\nThe college library’s expenses should be reduced by cutting subscriptions to useless periodicals. [Begged\nquestions: Are some of the library’s periodicals useless? Useless to whom?]\nThe fact is that the welfare system is too corrupt to be reformed. [Begged questions: How corrupt is the\nwelfare system? Does corruption, even if extensive, put the system beyond reform?]\nNon sequitur\nA non sequitur occurs when no logical relation exists between two or more connected ideas.\nIn Latin non sequitur means ―it does not follow.‖ In the sentences below, the second thought does\nnot follow from the first:\nShe uses a wheelchair, so she must be unhappy. [The second clause does not follow from the first.]\nKathleen Newsome has my vote for mayor because she has the best-run campaign organization.\n[Shouldn’t one’s vote be based on the candidate’s qualities, not the campaign’s organization?]\nRed herring\nA red herring is literally a kind of fish that might be drawn across a path to distract a\nbloodhound from a scent it’s following. In argument, a red herring is an irrelevant issue intended to\ndistract readers from the relevant issues. The writer changes the subject rather than pursue the\nargument.\nA campus speech code is essential to protect students, who already have enough problems coping with\nrising tuition. [Tuition costs and speech codes are different subjects. What protections do students need\nthat a speech code will provide?]\n\nFOWLcp09.doc - 10\nInstead of developing a campus speech code that will infringe on students’ First Amendment rights,\nadministrators should be figuring out how to prevent another tuition increase. [Again, tuition costs and\nspeech codes are different subjects. How would the code infringe on rights?]\nFalse authority\nArguments often cite as evidence the opinions of people who are experts on the subject (see\npp. 184–85). But writers use false authority when they cite as an expert someone whose expertise\nis doubtful or nonexistent.\nJason Bing, a recognized expert in corporate finance, maintains that pharmaceutical companies do not\ntest their products thoroughly enough. [Bing’s expertise in corporate finance bears no apparent relation to\nthe testing of pharmaceuticals.]\nAccording to Helen Liebowitz, the Food and Drug Administration has approved sixty dangerous drugs in\nthe last two years alone. [Who is Helen Liebowitz? On what authority does she make this claim?]\nInappropriate appeals\nAppeals to readers’ emotions are common in effective arguments. But such appeals must be\nrelevant and must supplement rather than substitute for facts, examples, and other evidence.\nWriters sometimes ignore the question with appeals to readers’ fear or pity.\nBy electing Susan Clark to the city council, you will prevent the city’s economic collapse. [Trades on\npeople’s fears. Can Clark singlehandedly prevent economic collapse? Is collapse even likely?]\nShe should not have to pay taxes, because she is an aged widow with no friends or relatives. [Appeals to\npeople’s pity. Should age and loneliness, rather than income, determine a person’s tax obligation?]\nSometimes writers ignore the question by appealing to readers’ sense of what other people\nbelieve or do. One approach is snob appeal, inviting readers to accept an assertion in order to be\nidentified with others they admire.\nAs any literate person knows, James Joyce is the best twentieth-century novelist. [But what qualities of\nJoyce’s writing make him a superior novelist?]\nBarry Bonds has an account at Big City Bank, and so should you. [A celebrity’s endorsement of course\ndoes not automatically guarantee the worth of a product, a service, an idea, or anything else.]\nA similar tactic invites readers to accept an assertion because everybody else does. This is the\nbandwagon approach.\nAs everyone knows, marijuana use leads to heroin addiction. [What is the evidence?]\nYet another diversion involves flattery of readers, in a way inviting them to join in a conspiracy.\nWe all understand campus problems well enough to see the disadvantages of such a policy. [What are the\ndisadvantages of the policy?]\nThe argument ad populum (―argument to the people‖) asks readers to accept a conclusion\nbased on shared values or even prejudices and nothing else.\nAny truly patriotic American will support the President’s action. [But why is the action worth taking?]\nOne final and very common kind of inappropriate emotional appeal addresses not the pros and\ncons of the issue itself but the real or imagined negative qualities of the people who hold the\nopposing view. This kind of argument is called ad hominem, Latin for ―to the man.‖\nOne of the scientists has been treated for emotional problems, so his pessimism about nuclear waste\nmerits no attention. [Do the scientist’s previous emotional problems invalidate his current views?]\nRecognizing oversimplifications\nTo oversimplify is to conceal or ignore complexities in a vain attempt to create a neater, more\nconvincing argument than reality allows.\nHasty generalization\nA hasty generalization, also called jumping to a conclusion, is a claim based on too little\nevidence or on evidence that is unrepresentative. (See also p. 187.)\n\nFOWLcp09.doc - 11\nIt is disturbing that several of the youths who shot up schools were users of violent video games.\nObviously, these games can breed vio-lence, and they should be banned. [A few cases do not establish the\nrelation between the games and violent behavior. Most youths who play violent video games do not\nbehave violently.]\nFrom the way it handled this complaint, we can assume that the consumer protection office has little\nintention of protecting consumers. [One experience with the office does not demonstrate its intention or\noverall performance.]\nSweeping generalization\nWhereas a hasty generalization comes from inadequate evidence, a sweeping generalization\nprobably is not supportable at all. One kind of sweeping generalization is the absolute statement\ninvolving words such as all, always, never, and no one that allow no exceptions. Rarely can\nevidence support such terms. Moderate words such as some, sometimes, rarely, and few are more\nreasonable.\nAnother common sweeping generalization is the stereotype,\na conventional and oversimplified characterization of a group of people.\nPeople who live in cities are unfriendly.\nCalifornians are fad-crazy.\nWomen are emotional.\nMen can’t express their feelings.\n(See also pp. 514–17 on sexist and other biased language.)\nReductive fallacy\nThe reductive fallacy oversimplifies (or reduces) the relation be-tween causes and their\neffects. The fallacy (sometimes called over-simplification) often involves linking two events as if\none caused the other directly, whereas the causes may be more complex or the relation may not\nexist at all. For example:\nPoverty causes crime. [If so, then why do people who are not poor commit crimes? And why aren’t all\npoor people criminals?]\nThe better a school’s athletic facilities are, the worse its academic programs are. [The sentence assumes a\ndirect cause-and-effect link be-tween athletics and scholarship.]\nPost hoc fallacy\nRelated to the reductive fallacy is the assumption that because A preceded B, then A must have\ncaused B. This fallacy is called in Latin post hoc, ergo propter hoc, meaning ―after this, therefore\nbecause of this,‖ or the post hoc fallacy for short.\nIn the two months since he took office, Mayor Holcomb has allowed crime in the city to increase 12\npercent. [The increase in crime is probably attributable to conditions existing before Holcomb took\noffice.]\nThe town council erred in permitting the adult bookstore to open, for shortly afterward two women were\nassaulted. [It cannot be assumed without evidence that the women’s assailants visited or were influenced\nby the bookstore.]\nEither/or fallacy\nIn the either/or fallacy (also called false dilemma), the writer assumes that a complicated\nquestion has only two answers, one good and one bad, both bad, or both good.\nCity police officers are either brutal or corrupt. [Most city police of-ficers are neither.]\nEither we permit mandatory drug testing in the workplace or productivity will continue to decline.\n[Productivity is not necessarily dependent on drug testing.]\nFalse analogy\nAn analogy is a comparison between two essentially unlike things for the purpose of\ndefinition or illustration. In arguing by analogy, a writer draws a likeness between things on the\nbasis of a single shared feature and then extends the likeness to other features. For instance, the\n―war on drugs‖ equates a battle against a foe with a program to eradicate (or at least reduce) sales\n\nFOWLcp09.doc - 12\nand use of illegal drugs. Both involve an enemy, a strategy of overpowering the enemy, a desired\ngoal, officials in uniform, and other similarities.\nAnalogy can only illustrate a point, never prove it: just because things are similar in one\nrespect, they are not necessarily alike in other respects. In the fallacy called false analogy, the\nwriter assumes such a complete likeness. Here is the analogy of the war on drugs taken to its false\nextreme:\nTo win the war on drugs, we must wage more of a military-style operation. Prisoners of war are locked up\nwithout the benefit of a trial by jury, and drug dealers should be, too. Soldiers shoot their enemy on sight,\nand officials who encounter big drug operations should, too. Military traitors may be executed, and\ncorrupt law enforcers could be, too.\nEXERCISE 9.2 ANALYZING ADVERTISEMENTS\nLeaf through a magazine or watch commercial television for half an hour, looking for advertisements that\nattempt to sell a product not on the basis of its worth but by snob appeal, flattery, or other inappro-\npriate appeals to emotions. Be prepared to discuss the advertisers’ techniques. (See Chapter 11, p. 229, if\nyou need help analyzing the appeals in images.)\nEXERCISE 9.3 Identifying and revising fallacies\nFallacies tend to appear together, as each of the following sentences illustrates. Identify at least one\nfallacy in each sentence. Then revise the sentences to make them more reasonable. (You can do this\nexercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\n1. The American government can sell nuclear technology to non-nuclear nations, so why can’t\nindividuals, who after all have a God-given right to earn a living as they see fit?\n2. A successful marriage demands a maturity that no one under twenty-five possesses.\n3. Students’ persistent complaints about the grading system prove that it is unfair.\n4. People watch television because they are too lazy to talk or read or because they want mindless\nescape from their lives.\n5. Racial tension is bound to occur when people with different backgrounds are forced to live side by\nside.\nEXERCISE 9.4 Identifying fallacies in arguments\nAnalyze the two arguments on pages 190–92 for fallacies. To what extent do any fallacies weaken either\nargument? Explain.\nEXERCISE 9.5 Identifying fallacies online\nAt groups.yahoo.com, find a conversation about drug testing in the workplace, environmental pollution,\nviolence in the media, or any other subject that interests you and that is debatable. Read through the\narguments made in the conversation, noting the fallacies you see.
