List the fallacious statements as well as\nthe types of fallacies they illustrate, keeping in mind that a given statement may illustrate more than a\nsingle type.\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp09.doc - 13\nVisit the companion Web site for more help and additional exercises on\nreading arguments critically.\nQuestions for critically reading an argument\nWhat claims does the writer make?\nWhat kinds and quality of evidence does the writer provide to support the claims?\nWhat assumptions underlie the argument, connecting evidence to claims?\nWhat is the writer’s tone? How does the writer use language?\nIs the writer reasonable?\nIs the argument logical? Has the writer committed any fallacies?\nAre you convinced? Why or why not?\nYou can download these questions from ablongman.com/littlebrown. Create a copy for each argument\nyou are reading, and use the questions to prompt your written responses.\nEvidence for argument\nFacts: verifiable statements\nStatistics: facts expressed in numbers\nExamples: specific cases\nExpert opinions: the judgments of authorities\nAppeals to readers’ beliefs or needs\nCriteria for weighing evidence\nIs it accurate: trustworthy, exact, undistorted?\nIs it relevant: authoritative, pertinent, current?\nIs it representative: true to context?\nIs it adequate: plentiful, specific?\nGuidelines for analyzing assumptions\nWhat are the assumptions underlying the argument? How does the writer connect claims with\nevidence?\nAre the assumptions believable? Do they express your values? Do they seem true in your\nexperience?\nAre the assumptions consistent with one another? Is the argument’s foundation solid, not slippery?\nChecklist of fallacies\nEvasions\nBegging the question: treating an opinion that is open to question as if it were already proved or\ndisproved.\nNon sequitur (―it does not follow‖): drawing a conclusion from irrelevant evidence.\nRed herring: introducing an irrelevant issue to distract readers.\nFalse authority: citing as expert opinion the views of a person who is not an expert.\nInappropriate appeals:\nAppealing to readers’ fear or pity.\nSnob appeal: appealing to readers’ wish to be like those who are more intelligent, famous, rich, and\nso on.\nBandwagon: appealing to readers’ wish to be part of the group.\nFlattery: appealing to readers’ intelligence, taste, and so on.\nArgument ad populum (―to the people‖): appealing to readers’ gen-eral values, such as patriotism or\nlove of family.\nArgument ad hominem (―to the man‖): attacking the opponent rather than the opponent’s argument.\nOversimplifications\nHasty generalization (jumping to a conclusion): asserting an opinion based on too little evidence.\nSweeping generalization: asserting an opinion as applying to all instances when it may apply to\nsome, or to none. Absolute statements and stereotypes are variations.\nReductive fallacy: generally, oversimplifying causes and effects.\nPost hoc fallacy: assuming that A caused B because A preceded B.\nEither/or fallacy (false dilemma): reducing a complicated question to two alternatives.\nFalse analogy: exaggerating the similarities in an analogy or ignoring key differences.\n\nFOWLcp10-11.doc - 1\n10\nCHAPTER\nWriting an Argument\nIn composing an argument, you try to clarify an issue or solve\na problem by finding the common ground between you and your readers. Using critical thinking,\nyou develop and test your own ideas. Using a variety of techniques, you engage readers in an\nattempt to narrow the distance between your views and theirs.\nThis chapter introduces the process and techniques of composing a written argument. The next\nchapter discusses the use of images, such as photographs and charts, as an effective tool for\nargument.\n10a Finding a subject\nAn argument subject must be arguable—that is, reasonable people will disagree over it and be\nable to support their positions with evidence. This sentence implies the dos and don’ts listed below.\nAdditional help on subjects for writing appears earlier in this book:\nWorking with a specific assignment, pages 6–7.\nWorking with a general assignment, page 7.\nNarrowing a subject to a question, pages 7–9.\nChoosing a subject for argument may seem difficult if you’re not familiar with what people in\nthe United States find debatable. One way to find a subject is to scout online discussion groups,\nsuch as those listed at groups.yahoo.com, for subjects on which there is a range of opinion.\nAnother approach is to read a newspaper every day for at least a week, looking for issues that\ninvolve or interest you. Following the development of the issues in articles, editorials, and letters to\nthe editor will give you a sense of how controversial they are, what the positions are, and what your\nposition might be.\nEXERCISE 10.1 Finding a subject for argument\nExplain why each subject below is or is not appropriate for argument. Refer to the box on the previous\npage if you need help. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\n1. Granting of athletic scholarships\n2. Care of automobile tires\n3. Censoring the Web sites of hate groups\n4. History of the town park\n5. Housing for the homeless\n6. Billboards in urban residential areas or in rural areas\n7. Animal testing for cosmetics research\n8. Cats versus dogs as pets\n9. Ten steps in recycling wastepaper\n10. Benefits of being a parent\n10b Conceiving a thesis statement\nThe thesis is the main idea of your paper (see pp. 27–31). In an argument the thesis statement\nmakes the claim that you want your readers to accept or act on. Here are two thesis statements on\nthe same subject:\n\nFOWLcp10-11.doc - 2\nThe new room fees are unjustified given the condition of the dormitories.\nThe administration should postpone the new room fees at least until conditions in the dormitories are\nimproved.\nYour thesis statement must satisfy the same requirements as your subject (see the box on p.\n199). But it must also specify the\nbasis for your claim. In both of the preceding thesis statements, the basis for protesting the room\nfees is that the dormitories are in poor condition.\nNote that the writer of either of these arguments must clarify the definition of condition(s) if\nthe argument is to be clear and reasonable. Always take pains to define abstract and general terms\nthat are central to your argument, preferably in or just after the thesis statement. (See p. 183.)\nEXERCISE 10.2 Conceiving a thesis statement\nFor each subject in Exercise 10.1 that you deemed arguable, draft a tentative thesis statement that\nspecifies the basis for an argument. If you prefer, choose five arguable subjects of your own and draft a\nthesis statement for each one. One thesis statement should interest you enough to develop into a complete\nargument in later exercises.\n10c Analyzing your purpose and your audience\nYour purpose in argument is, broadly, to engage readers in order to convince them of your\nposition or persuade them to act. But arguments have more specific purposes as well, such as the\nfollowing:\nTo strengthen the commitment of existing supporters\nTo win new supporters from the undecided or uninformed\nTo get the opposition to reconsider\nTo inspire supporters to act\nTo deter the undecided from acting\nIt’s no accident that each of these purposes characterizes the audience (existing supporters, the\nundecided, and so on). In argument, even more than in other kinds of writing, achieving your\npurpose depends on the response of your readers, so you need a sense of who they are and where\nthey stand. The ―Questions About Audience‖ on page 11 can help you identify readers’ knowledge,\nbeliefs, and other pertinent information. In addition, you need to know how readers stand on your\nsubject—not only whether they agree or disagree generally, but also which specific assertions they\nwill find more or less convincing.\nYour purpose can help you fill in this information. If you decide to address supporters or\nopponents, you essentially select readers with certain inclinations and ignore other readers who\nmay tune in. If you decide to win new supporters from those who are undecided on your topic,\nyou’ll have to imagine skeptical readers who will be convinced only by an argument that is\ndetailed, logical, and fair. Like you when you read an argument critically, these skeptical readers\nseek to be reasoned with, not manipulated into a position or hammered over the head.\nEXERCISE 10.3 ANALYZING PURPOSE AND AUDIENCE\nSpecify a purpose and likely audience for the thesis statement you chose to develop in Exercise 10.2.\nWhat do purpose and audience suggest about the way you should develop the argument?\n10d Using reason\nAs a reader of argument, you seek evidence for the writer’s claims and clear reasoning about\nthe relationship of evidence to claims. As a writer of argument, you seek to provide what the reader\nneeds in a way that furthers your case.\nThe thesis of your argument is a conclusion you reach by reasoning about evidence. Two\ncommon processes of reasoning are induction and deduction—methods of thinking that you use all\n\nFOWLcp10-11.doc - 3\nthe time even if you don’t know their names. You can think of induction and deduction as two\ndifferent ways of moving among claims, evidence, and assumptions—the elements of argument\nderived from Stephen Toulmin’s work and discussed on pages 180–81.\n1 Reasoning inductively\nWhen you’re about to buy a used car, you consult friends, relatives, and consumer guides\nbefore deciding what kind of car to buy. Using inductive reasoning, you make specific\nobservations about cars (your evidence) and you induce, or infer, a generalization (or claim) that\nModel X is the most reliable. Writing a paper on the effectiveness of print advertising, you might\nalso use inductive reasoning:\nFirst analyze statistics on advertising in print and in other media (evidence).\nThen read comments by advertisers and publishers (more evidence).\nFinally, form a conclusion that print is the most cost-effective advertising medium (generalization).\nThis reasoning builds from the evidence to the claim, with assumptions connecting evidence\nto claim. By predicting something about the unknown based on what you know, you create new\nknowledge out of old.\nThe more evidence you accumulate, the more probable it is that your generalization is true.\nNote, however, that absolute certainty is not possible. At some point you must assume that your\nevidence justifies your generalization, for yourself and your readers. Most errors in inductive\nreasoning involve oversimplifying either the evidence or the generalization. See pages 192–97 on\nfallacies.\n2 Reasoning deductively\nYou use deductive reasoning when you proceed from your generalization that Model X is the\nmost reliable used car to your own specific circumstances (you want to buy a used car) to the\nconclusion (or claim) that you should buy a Model X car. Like induction, deduction uses the\nelements of argument—claims, evidence, and assumptions—but with it you apply old information\nto new.\nThe deductive syllogism\nThe conventional way of displaying a deductive argument is in a syllogism:\nPremise: All human beings are mortal. [A generalization, fact, principle, or belief that you assume to be\ntrue.]\nPremise: I am a human being. [New information: a specific case of the first premise.]\nConclusion: Therefore, I am mortal.\nAs long as the premises of a syllogism are true, the conclusion derives logically and certainly from\nthem. If you want the school\nadministration to postpone new room fees for one dormitory, your\ndeductive argument might be expressed in this syllogism:\nPremise: The administration should not raise fees on dorm rooms in poor condition.\nPremise: The rooms in Polk Hall are in poor condition.\nConclusion: Therefore, the administration should not raise fees on the rooms in Polk Hall.\nThe force of deductive reasoning depends on the reliability of the premises and the care taken\nto apply them in drawing conclusions. The reasoning process is valid if the premises lead logically\nto the conclusion. It is true if the premises are believable.\nProblems with syllogisms\nSometimes the reasoning in a deductive argument is true because the premises are believable,\nbut it is not valid because the conclusion doesn’t derive logically from the premises:\nPremise: The administration should not raise fees on dorm rooms in poor condition.\nPremise: Tyler Hall is a dormitory.\nConclusion: Therefore, the administration should not raise fees on the rooms in Tyler Hall.\nBoth premises may be true, but the first does not necessarily apply to the second, so the conclusion\nis invalid.\n\nFOWLcp10-11.doc - 4\nSometimes, too, deductive reasoning is valid but not true:\nPremise: All college administrations are indifferent to students’ needs.\nPremise: The administration of Central State is a college administration.\nConclusion: Therefore, the administration of Central State is indifferent to students’ needs.\nThis syllogism is valid but useless: the first premise is an untrue assumption, so the entire argument\nis untrue. Invalid and untrue syllogisms underlie many of the fallacies discussed on pages 192–97.\nA particular hazard of deductive reasoning is the unstated premise: the basic assumption\nlinking evidence and conclusion is not stated but implied. Here the unstated premise is believable\nand the argument is reasonable:\nMs. Stein has worked with drug addicts for fifteen years, so she knows a great deal about their problems.\n[Unstated premise: Anyone who has worked fifteen years with drug addicts knows about their problems.]\nBut when the unstated premise is wrong or unfounded, the argument is false. For example:\nSince Jane Lightbow is a senator, she must receive money illegally from lobbyists. [Unstated premise: All\nsenators receive money illegally from lobbyists.]\nTo avoid such false conclusions, you may be tempted to make your claims sound more\nreasonable. But even a conclusion that sounds reasonable must be supportable. For instance,\nchanging must to might modifies the unstated assumption about Senator Lightbow:\nSince Jane Lightbow is a senator, she might receive money illegally from lobbyists. [Unstated premise:\nSome senators receive money illegally from lobbyists.]\nBut it does not necessarily follow that Senator Lightbow is one of the ―some.‖ The sentence,\nthough logical, is not truly reasonable unless evidence demonstrates that Senator Lightbow should\nbe linked with illegal activities.\nEXERCISE 10.4 Reasoning inductively\nStudy the facts below and then evaluate each of the numbered conclusions following them. Which of the\ngeneralizations are reasonable given the evidence, and which are not? Why? (You can do this exercise\nonline at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nIn 2004–05 each American household viewed an average of 50 hours and 12 minutes of television,\nDVDs, or videos weekly.\nEach individual viewed an average of 30 hours and 23 minutes per week.\nThose viewing the most television per week (43 hours and 6 minutes) were women over age 55.\nThose viewing the least television per week (19 hours and 17 minutes) were children ages 6 to 11.\nHouseholds earning under $30,000 a year watched an average of 53 hours and 19 minutes a week.\nHouseholds earning more than $60,000 a year watched an average of 48 hours and 7 minutes a week.\n1. Households with incomes under $30,000 tend to watch more tele-vision than average.\n2. Women watch more television than men.\n3. Nonaffluent people watch less television than affluent people.\n4. Women over age 55 tend to watch more television than average.\n5. Children watch less television than critics generally assume.\nEXERCISE 10.5 Reasoning deductively\nConvert each of the following statements into a syllogism. (You may have to state unstated assumptions.)\nUse the syllogism to evaluate both the validity and the truth of the statement. (You can do this exercise\nonline at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nDiSantis is a banker, so he does not care about the poor.\nPremise: Bankers do not care about the poor.\nPremise: DiSantis is a banker.\nConclusion: Therefore, DiSantis does not care about the poor.\nThe statement is untrue because the first premise is untrue.\n\nFOWLcp10-11.doc - 5\n1. The mayor opposed pollution controls when he was president of a manufacturing company, so he\nmay not support new controls or vigorously enforce existing ones.\n2. Information on corporate Web sites is unreliable because the sites are sponsored by for-profit entities.\n3. Schroeder is a good artist because she trained at Parsons, like many other good artists.\n4. Wealthy athletes who use their resources to help others deserve our particular appreciation.\n5. Jimson is clearly a sexist because she has hired only one woman.\n10e Using evidence\nWhether your argument is reasonable or not depends heavily on the evidence you marshal to\nsupport it. The kinds of evidence and the criteria for evaluating evidence are discussed in detail on\npages 183–87. Finding evidence is discussed under research writing on pages 571–97. Evaluating\nsources of evidence, including online sources, is discussed under research writing on pages 599–\n609.\nThe kind and quantity of evidence you use should be determined by your purpose, your\nsubject, and the needs of your audience. Some arguments, such as an appeal for volunteer help in a\nsoup kitchen, will rely most heavily on examples (including perhaps a narrative of your own\nexperience) and on appeals to readers’ beliefs. Other arguments, such as a proposal for mandatory\nside air bags in cars, will rely much more on statistics and expert opinions. Most arguments,\nincluding these, will mingle facts, examples, expert opinions, and appeals to readers’ beliefs and\nneeds.\nIn using evidence for argument, you’ll need to be especially wary of certain traps that\ncarelessness or zeal can lure you into. These are listed in the following box.\nEXERCISE 10.6 Using reason and evidence in your argument\nDevelop the structure and evidence for the argument you began in Exercises 10.2 and 10.3 (pp. 201 and\n202). (You may want to begin drafting at this stage.) Is your argument mainly inductive or mainly\ndeductive? Use the box on page 205 to test the reasoning of the argument. Use the boxes on page 186 and\nabove to test your evidence.\n10f Reaching your readers\nTo reach your readers in argument, you appeal directly to their reason and emotions, you\npresent yourself as someone worth heeding, and you account for views opposing your own.\n1 Appealing to readers\nIn forming convictions about arguable issues, we generally interpret the factual evidence\nthrough the filter of our values, beliefs, tastes, desires, and feelings. You may object to placing the\nnew town dump in a particular wooded area because the facts suggest that the site is not large\nenough and that prevailing winds will blow odors back through the town. But you may also have\nfond memories of playing in the wooded area as a child, feelings that color your interpretation of\nthe facts and strengthen your conviction that the dump should be placed elsewhere. Your\nconviction is partly ra-tional, because it is based on evidence, and partly emotional, because it is\nalso based on feelings.\nRational and emotional appeals\nIn most arguments you will combine rational appeals to readers’ capacities for reasoning\nlogically between evidence and claims with emotional appeals to readers’ beliefs and feelings. The\nfollowing passages, all arguing the same view on the same subject, illustrate how either a primarily\nrational or a primarily emotional appeal may be weaker than an approach that uses both:\n\nFOWLcp10-11.doc - 6\nRational appeal\nAdvertising should show more physically challenged people. The millions of disabled Americans have\nconsiderable buying power, yet so far advertisers have made no attempt to tap that power. [Appeals to the\nlogic of financial gain.]\nEmotional appeal\nAdvertising should show more physically challenged people. By keeping the physically challenged out of\nthe mainstream depicted in ads, advertisers encourage widespread prejudice against disability, prejudice\nthat frightens and demeans those who hold it. [Appeals to the sense of fairness, open-mindedness.]\nRational and emotional appeals\nAdvertising should show more physically challenged people. The millions of disabled Americans have\nconsiderable buying power, yet so far advertisers have made no attempt to tap that power. Further, by\nkeeping the physically challenged out of the mainstream depicted in ads, advertisers encourage\nwidespread prejudice against disability, prejudice that frightens and demeans those who hold it.\nThe third passage, in combining both kinds of appeal, gives readers both rational and emotional\nbases for agreeing with the writer.\nFor an emotional appeal to be successful, it must be appropriate for the audience and the\nargument:\nIt must not misjudge readers’ actual feelings.\nIt must not raise emotional issues that are irrelevant to the claims and the evidence. See\npages 194–95 for a discussion of specific inappropriate appeals, such as the bandwagon\napproach.\nOne further caution: Photographs and other images can reinforce your claims with a strong\nemotional appeal, but they must be relevant to your claims, and you must explain their relevance in\nyour text and captions. See page 299 for more on the appeals in images.\nEthical appeal\nA third kind of approach to readers, the ethical appeal, is the sense you give of being a\ncompetent, fair, trustworthy person. A sound argument backed by ample evidence—a rational\nappeal—will convince readers of your knowledge and reasonableness. (So will your\nacknowledging the opposition. See the next page.) Appropriate emotional appeals will demonstrate\nthat you share readers’ beliefs and needs. An argument that is concisely written and correct in\ngrammar, spelling, and other matters will underscore your competence. In addition, a sincere and\neven tone will assure readers that you are a balanced person who wants to reason with them.\nA sincere and even tone need not exclude language with emotional appeal—words such as\nfrightens and demeans at the end of the third example on the previous page. But avoid certain\nforms of expression that will mark you as unfair:\nInsulting words, such as idiotic or fascist.\nBiased language, such as rednecks or fags. (See pp. 514–17.)\nSarcasm—for instance, using the phrase What a brilliant idea to indicate contempt for the\nidea and its originator.\nExclamation points! They’ll make you sound shrill!\nSee also pages 188–89 on tone.\n2 Answering opposing views\nA good test of your fairness in argument is how you handle possible objections. Assuming\nyour thesis is indeed arguable, then others can marshal their own evidence to support a different\nview or views. You need to find out what these other views are and what the support is for them.\nThen, in your argument, you need to take these views on, refute those you can, grant the validity of\nothers, and demonstrate why, despite their validity, the opposing views are less compelling than\nyour own.\nThe following paragraph illustrates this approach:\nThe athletic director argues against reducing university support for\nathletic programs on the grounds that they make money that goes toward\n\nFOWLcp10-11.doc - 7\nacademic programs. It is true that here at Springfield the surpluses from the\nfootball and basketball programs have gone into the general university fund,\nand some of that money may have made it into academic departments (the\nfund’s accounting methods make it impossible to say for sure). But the athletic\ndirector misses the point. The problem is not that the athletic programs may\ncost more than they take in but that they demand too much to begin with. For\nan institution that hopes to become first-rate academically, too many facilities,\ntoo much money, too much energy, and too many people are tied up in the\neffort to produce championship sports teams.\n—William Hoving (student),\n‖Scholarship Versus Gamesmanship‖\nBefore or while you draft your essay, list for yourself all the opposing views you can think of.\nYou’ll find them in your research, by talking to friends and classmates, and by critically thinking\nabout your own ideas. You can also look for a range of views in a dis-\ncussion group that deals with your subject. A place to start is the archive at groups.yahoo.com.\nTo deal with opposing views, figure out which ones you can refute (do more research if\nnecessary), and prepare to concede those views you can’t refute. It’s not a mark of weakness or\nfailure to\nadmit that the opposition has a point or two. Indeed, by showing yourself to be honest and fair, you\nstrengthen your ethical appeal and thus your entire argument.\nEXERCISE 10.7 Identifying appeals\nIdentify each passage below as primarily a rational appeal or primarily an emotional appeal. Which\npassages make a strong ethical appeal as well? (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\n1. Web surfing may contribute to the global tendency toward breadth rather than depth of knowledge.\nUsing those most essential of skills—pointing and clicking—our brightest minds may now never\nencounter, much less read, the works of Plato, Shakespeare, and Darwin.\n2. Thus the data collected by these researchers indicate that a man-datory sentence for illegal possession\nof handguns may lead to reduction in handgun purchases.\n3. Most broadcasters worry that further government regulation of television programming could breed\ncensorship—certainly, an undesirable outcome. Yet most broadcasters also accept that children’s\ntelevision is a fair target for regulation.\n4. Anyone who cherishes life in all its diversity could not help being appalled by the mistreatment of\nlaboratory animals. The so-called scientists who run the labs are misguided.\n5. Many experts in constitutional law have warned that the rule violates the right to free speech. Yet\nother experts have viewed the rule, however regretfully, as necessary for the good of the community\nas a whole.\nEXERCISE 10.8 Reaching your readers\nContinuing your argument-in-progress from Exercise 10.6 (p. 208), analyze whether your claims are\nrational or emotional and whether the mix is appropriate for your audience and argument. Analyze your\nethical appeal, too, considering whether it can be strengthened. Then make a list of possible opposing\nviews. Think freely at first, not stopping to censor views that seem far-fetched or irrational. When your\nlist is complete, decide which views must be taken seriously and why, and develop a response to each\none.\n10g Organizing your argument\nAll arguments include the same parts:\nThe introduction establishes the significance of the subject and provides background. The\nintroduction generally includes the thesis statement. However, if you think your readers may\nhave difficulty accepting your thesis statement before they see at least some support for it,\nthen it may come later in the paper. (See pp. 102–06 for more on introductions.)\nThe body states and develops the claims supporting the thesis. In one or more paragraphs,\nsupport each claim with clearly relevant evidence. See below for more on organizing the body.\n\nFOWLcp10-11.doc - 8\nThe response to opposing views details and addresses those views, either demonstrating\nyour argument’s greater strengths or conceding the opponents’ points. See below for more on\norganizing this response.\nThe conclusion completes the argument, restating the thesis, summarizing the supporting\nclaims, and making a final appeal to readers. (See pp. 106–08 for more on conclusions.)\nThe arrangement of the body and the response to opposing views depends on your subject,\npurpose, audience, and form of reasoning. The box below shows several possibilities.\nYou may want to experiment with various organizations—for instance, trying out your\nstrongest claims first or last in the body, stating claims outright or letting the evidence build to\nthem, answering the opposition near the beginning or near the end or claim by claim. You can do\nthis experimentation on paper, of course, but it’s easier on a computer. Try rearranging your outline\nas described on page 33. Or try rearranging your draft (work with a copy) by cutting and pasting\nparts of it for different emphases.\nEXERCISE 10.9 Organizing your argument\nContinuing from Exercise 10.8 (p. 211), develop a structure for your argument. Consider especially how\nyou will introduce it, how you will arrange your claims, where you will place your responses to opposing\nviews, and how you will conclude.\n10h Revising your argument\nWhen you revise your argument, do it in at least two stages—revising underlying meaning and\nstructure, and editing more superficial elements. The checklists on pages 51 and 58–59 can be a\nguide. Supplement them with the checklist below, which encourages you to think critically about\nyour own argument.\nEXERCISE 10.10 Writing and revising your argument\nDraft and revise the argument you have developed in the exercises in this chapter. Use the revision\nchecklists on page 51 and above to review your work.\n10i Examining a sample argument\nThe following essay by the student Craig Holbrook illustrates the principles discussed in this\nchapter. As you read the essay, notice especially the organization, the relation of claims and\nsupporting evidence (including illustrations), the kinds of appeals Holbrook makes, and the ways\nhe responds to opposing views.\nTV Can Be Good for You\nTelevision wastes time, pollutes minds, destroys brain cells, and turns some viewers into murderers. Thus\nruns the prevailing talk about the medium, supported by serious research as well as simple belief. But television\nhas at least one strong virtue, too, which helps to explain its endurance as a cultural force. It provides\nreplacement voices that ease loneliness, spark healthful laughter, and even educate young children.\nMost people who have lived alone understand the curse of silence, when the only sound is the buzz of\nunhappiness or anxiety inside one’s own head. Although people of all ages who live alone can experience\nintense loneliness, the elderly are especially vulnerable to solitude. For example, they may suffer increased\nconfusion or depression when left alone for long periods but then rebound when they have steady\ncompanionship (Bondevik and Skogstad 329-30).\nA study of elderly men and women in New Zealand found that television can actually serve as a\ncompanion by assuming ―the role of social contact with the wider world,‖ reducing ―feelings of isolation and\nloneliness because it directs viewers’ attention away from themselves‖ (―Television Programming‖). (See fig.\n1.) Thus television’s replacement voices can provide comfort because they distract from a focus on being alone.\nThe absence of real voices can be most damaging when it means a lack of laughter. Here, too, research\nshows that television can have a positive effect on health. Laughter is one of the most powerful calming forces\navailable to human beings, proven in many studies to reduce heart rate, lower blood pressure, and ease other\nstress-related ailments (Burroughs, Mahoney, and Lippman 172; Griffiths 18). (See fig. 2.) Television offers\n\nFOWLcp10-11.doc - 9\nplenty of laughter: the recent listings for a single Friday night included more than twenty comedy programs\nrunning on the networks and on basic cable.\nA study reported in a health magazine found that laughter inspired by television and video is as healthful as the\nlaughter generated by live comedy. Volunteers laughing at a video comedy routine ―showed significant\nimprovements in several immune functions, such as natural killer-cell activity‖ (Laliberte 78). Further, the\neffects of the comedy were so profound that ―merely anticipating watching a funny video improved mood,\ndepression, and anger as much as two days beforehand‖ (Laliberte 79). Even for people with plenty of\ncompanionship, television’s replacement voices can have healthful effects by causing laughter.\nTelevision also provides information about the world. This service can be helpful to everyone but\nespecially to children, whose natural curiosity can exhaust the knowledge and patience of their parents and\ncaretakers. While the TV may be baby-sitting children, it can also enrich them. For example, educational\nprograms such as those on the Discovery Channel, the Disney Channel, and PBS offer a steady stream of\ninformation at various cognitive levels. (See fig. 3.) Even many cartoons, which are generally dismissed as\nmindless or worse, familiarize children with the material of literature, including strong characters enacting\nclassic narratives.\nTwo researchers studying children and television found that TV is a source of creative and psychological\ninstruction, inspiring children ―to play imaginatively and develop confidence and skills‖ (Colman and Colman\n9). Instead of passively watching, children ―interact with the programs and videos‖ and ―sometimes include the\nfictional characters in reality’s play time‖ (Colman and Colman 8). Thus television’s voices both inform young\nviewers and encourage exchange.\nThe value of these replacement voices should not be oversold. For one thing, almost everyone agrees that\ntoo much TV does no one any good and may cause much harm. Many studies show that excessive TV watching\nincreases violent behavior, especially in children, and can cause, rather than ease, other antisocial behaviors\n(Reeks 114; Walsh 34). In addition, human beings require the give and take of actual interaction. Steven\nPinker, an expert in children’s language acquisition, warns that children cannot develop language properly by\nwatching television. They need to interact with actual speakers who respond directly to their needs (282).\nReplacement voices are not real voices and in the end can do only limited good.\nBut even limited good is something, especially for those who are lonely or neglected. Television is not an\nentirely positive force, but neither is it an entirely negative one. Its voices stand by to provide company,\nlaughter, and information whenever they’re needed.\nWorks Cited\nBondevik, Margareth, and Anders Skogstad. ―The Oldest Old, ADL, Social Network, and Loneliness.‖ Western\nJournal of Nursing Research 20.3 (1998): 325-43.\nBurroughs, W. Jeffrey, Diana L. Mahoney, and Louis G. Lippman.\n―Attributes of Health-Promoting Laughter: Cross-Generational Comparison.‖ Journal of Psychology\n136.2 (2004): 171-81.\nColman, Robyn, and Adrian Colman. ―Inspirational Television.‖ Youth Studies in Australia 21.3 (2003): 8-10.\nFoujouls, Jean Michel. Photograph. Corbis image 42-15243193.\n13 June 2005 <http://pro.corbis.com>.\nGriffiths, Joan. ―The Mirthful Brain.‖ Omni Aug. 1996: 18-19.\nKibiuk, Lydia. Illustration. Society for Neuroscience. Brain Briefings. Dec. 2001. 12 June 2005\n<http://apu.sfn.org/BrainBriefings/\nbb_humor.htm>.\nLaliberte, Richard W. ―The Benefits of Laughter.‖ Shape Sept. 2003: 78-79.\nPinker, Steven. The Language Instinct: How the Mind Creates Language. New York: Harper, 1994.\nReeks, Anne. ―Kids and TV: A Guide.‖ Parenting Apr. 2005: 110-15.\n―Television Programming for Older People: Summary Research Report.‖ NZ on Air. 25 July 2004. 15 Oct.\n2005 <http:// www.nzonair.gov.nz/media/oldpeoplesreport.pdf>.\nUnited Nations Children’s Fund. Photograph. The State of the World’s Children. 2002. 12 June 2005\n<http://unicef.org/\nsowc02/features10.htm>.\nWalsh, Teri. ―Too Much TV Linked to Depression.‖ Prevention Feb. 2001: 34-36.\n—Craig Holbrook (student)\nEXERCISE 10.11 Critically reading an argument\nAnalyze the construction and effectiveness of the preceding essay by answering the following questions.\n(You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\n1. Where does Holbrook make claims related to his thesis statement, and where does he provide\nevidence to support the claims?\n2. Where does Holbrook appeal primarily to reason, and where does he appeal primarily to emotion?\nWhat specific beliefs and values of readers does he appeal to?\n3. How would you characterize Holbrook’s ethical appeal?\n\nFOWLcp10-11.doc - 10\n4. How effective do you find the illustrations as support for Holbrook’s claims? What appeals do they\nmake? (For an analysis of Holbrook’s first illustration, see p. 229.)\n5. What objections to his argument does Holbrook anticipate? How does he respond to them?\n6. How effective do you find this argument? To what extent does Holbrook convince you that television\nhas virtues? Do some claims seem stronger or weaker than others? Does Holbrook respond ade-\nquately to objections?\n7. Write a critical evaluation of ―TV Can Be Good for You.‖ First summarize Holbrook’s views. Then\nrespond to those views by answering the questions posed in number 6 above.\n11\nCHAPTER\nReading and Using\nVisual Arguments\nA visual argument uses images to engage and convince readers. Advertisements often\nprovide the most vivid and memorable examples, but writers in almost every field—from\nastronomy to music to physiology—support their claims with images. In this chapter you’ll learn\nhow to read visual arguments critically (below) and how to use images to strengthen your own\narguments (p. 225).\nNote This chapter builds on the previous three, which discuss forming a critical perspective\n(including viewing images critically), reading an argument critically, and writing an argument. If\nyou haven’t already done so, read those chapters before this one.\n11a Reading visual arguments critically\nChapter 9 explains the three main elements of any argument: claims, evidence, and\nassumptions. To read visual arguments critically, you’ll analyze all three elements.\n1 Testing claims\nClaims are positive statements that require support (see pp. 180, 181–83). In a visual\nargument, claims may be made by composition as well as by content, with or without\naccompanying words. Here are a few examples of visual claims:\nImage A magnetic sticker shaped like a ribbon and decorated with the colors and symbols of the\nAmerican flag, positioned prominently on a car.\nClaim I support American troops overseas, and you should, too.\nImage A photograph framing hundreds of chickens crammed into small cages, resembling familiar\nimages of World War II concentration camps.\nClaim Commercial poultry-raising practices are cruel and unethical.\nImage A chart with dramatically contrasting bars that represent the optimism, stress, and weight\nreported by people before and after they participated in a program of daily walking.\nClaim Daily exercise leads to a healthier and happier life.\nImage A cartoon featuring affluent-looking young adults on an affluent-looking college campus,\nconversing and frowning sadly as they gaze downhill at rough-looking teens in a dilapidated schoolyard. The\ncaption reads, ―Yes, it’s sad what’s happening to schools today. But everyone knows that throwing money at\nthe problem isn’t the solution.‖\nClaim Better funding makes for better schools.\n\nFOWLcp10-11.doc - 11\nThe following image is one of a series of advertisements featuring unnamed but well-known\npeople as milk drinkers. The celebrity here is Oscar de la Hoya, a boxing champion. The\nadvertisement makes several claims both in the photograph and in the text.\n2 Weighing evidence\nThe kinds of evidence provided in images parallel those found in written arguments (see pp.\n183–85):\nFacts can be verified by observation or research. In visual arguments they may be data, as in a\ngraph showing a five-year rise in oil prices. Or they may be inferences drawn from data, as\nin the statement in the preceding ad that milk provides ―high-quality protein for your muscles\nwithout the fat.‖ Sometimes images serve as facts themselves, objects that are analyzed in\naccompanying writing, as the milk ad is examined by this text or as the ad from Time\nmagazine is examined by John Latner in the essay on pages 175–77.\nExamples illustrate and reinforce a point. Visual arguments often focus on an instance of the\nargument’s claims, as Oscar de la Hoya does in the milk ad. Another ad might feature multiple\nimages as examples: a Sizzler TV commercial, for instance, shows a sequence of luscious-\nlooking foods to be had at the restaurant. An image might also illustrate a claim made in\naccompanying writing, as, again, the milk ad does in this text.\nExpert opinions are the findings of subject-matter authorities based on their research and\nexperience. A visual argument might present a chart from an expert showing a trend in, say,\nunemployment among high school graduates. The familiar TV ad that features a doctor\nrecommending a particular medicine to a patient offers the doctor as an expert.\nAppeals to beliefs or needs reinforce readers’ values or truths. Many visual arguments make\nsuch appeals by depicting how things clearly ought to be (an antidrug ad featuring a teenager\nwho is confidently refusing peer pressure) or, in contrast, by showing how things clearly\nshould not be (a Web site for an\nantihunger campaign featuring images of emaciated children).\nThe evidence in a visual argument should be judged by the same criteria as that in a written\nargument (pp. 185–87):\nIs the evidence accurate? Images can be manipulated just as words can, and like words they\nshould be analyzed for their fairness, precision, and trustworthiness. For example, a graph\nclaiming to show changes in college living expenses between 1995 and 2005 should identify\nthe source and purpose of the research, supply data for all the years, and clarify the definition\nof living expenses (the cost of room and board only, or transportation, recreation, and other\nexpenses as well?).\nIs the evidence relevant and adequate? An image should pertain to the claims made in the\nlarger argument and should\nsufficiently demonstrate its own claims. In an article on eating disorders, for instance, relevant\nand adequate images might include a medical diagram showing the liver damage from\nmalnutrition and a photograph of a frail-bodied person suffering from anorexia. However, a\nphotograph of a skinny model or actor would be neither relevant nor adequate, merely\nsensationalistic, unless the subject had publicly confirmed that his or her low weight resulted\nfrom an eating disorder.\nDoes the evidence represent the context? Representative visual evidence reflects the full\nrange of the sample it’s drawn from and does not overrepresent or hide important elements of\nthe subject. For example, a photographic essay claiming to document the poor working\nconditions of migrant farm workers might reasonably include images of one worker’s scarred\nhands and another worker suffering from heat prostration. But to be representative, the essay\nwould also need to illustrate the full range of migrant workers’ experiences.\nThe annotations on the following pie chart demonstrate a way to analyze the evidence in a\nvisual argument—even when, as in this case, the image comes from a highly reputable source. (The\nSocial Science Data Analysis Network is a scholarly organization.)\n3 Discovering assumptions\nLike a written argument, a visual argument is based on assumptions—the creator’s ideas,\noften unstated, about why the evidence relates to the claims (see pp. 187–88). In visual arguments\n\nFOWLcp10-11.doc - 12\nmany assumptions involve the creator’s beliefs about the audience, as detailed on the facing page.\nThe examples analyze the milk ad on page 220, featuring the boxer Oscar de la Hoya.\nWho readers are and where they will see the argument. The de la Hoya ad first appeared in\nsports magazines, so the advertiser could assume readers who are interested in sports and\nathletes.\nWhat readers already know about the subject. To sports fans, de la Hoya would be a\nfamiliar subject. The advertiser presumably considers readers less familiar with the benefits of\nmilk or with its appeal to celebrities like de la Hoya.\nHow familiar readers are with the purpose, format, and style of the argument. With\nnearly two hundred print and TV ads since 1994, the milk-mustache campaign has become a\nfixture of US popular culture. Each new ad fits into the framework established by its\npredecessors.\nWhether readers are likely to lean toward the argument’s claims. The advertiser clearly\nassumes that the endorsement of a sports star like de la Hoya will carry weight with readers.\nAt the same time, it seems to assume that the benefits of milk still need selling to readers.\nWhat kinds of information, ideas, and images readers will find persuasive. The advertiser\nseems to assume that a strictly factual claim about the health benefits of milk would not be\npersuasive enough to readers, so it shows that admirable people like de la Hoya consume milk.\nThe photograph of de la Hoya emphasizes qualities that the advertiser presumably thinks will\nappeal to readers: fitness, toughness, directness, and even (in the robe draped over the boxer’s\nshoulders) patriotism.\n4 Recognizing fallacies\nFallacies, or errors in argument, are sometimes accidental, but they are often used deliberately\nto manipulate readers’ responses. All the fallacies of written arguments discussed on pages 192–97\nappear in visual arguments as well. Here we’ll focus on examples of the two main categories.\nEvasions attempt to deflect the reader from the central claim of the argument. One\nevasion is snob appeal, inviting readers to think or be like someone they admire. Look again\nat the de la Hoya milk ad on page 220. Like all celebrity ads in the milk-mustache campaign,\nthis one appeals to the reader’s wish to emulate a famous person. If you drink milk, the ad says\nsubtly, you too may become fit, skillful, and direct (notice that de la Hoya looks unguardedly\ninto the camera). The ad does have some substance in its specific and verifiable claim that\nmilk contains ―high-quality protein for your muscles without the fat,‖ but de la Hoya himself,\nwith his milk mustache, makes a stronger claim.\nOversimplifications imply that subjects are less complex than they are. Two examples are\nthe either/or fallacy, which asserts that a complicated situation has only two sides worth\nconsid-ering, and the sweeping generalization, which asserts that a single view applies to all\ninstances when it may apply only to some, or to none. Both fallacies appear in the map below,\nwhich represents the Electoral College vote in the 2004 US Presidential election: red for states\nwon by Republican George W. Bush, blue for states won by Democrat John F. Kerry. The\ncolors represent majority votes and Electoral College, not popular, votes. Still, the colors have\nbeen used to characterize the political and social preferences of each state’s entire population\nand to reinforce stereotypes about rural vs. urban, heartland vs. coastal, and conservative vs.\nliberal citizens.\nEXERCISE 11.1 Reading a visual argument critically\nThe image on the facing page is an e-card found on the Web site of Big Brothers Big Sisters of America,\na community organization that brings children and mentors together. The organization invites site visitors\nto send its cards ―to encourage friends and family members to support Big Brothers Big Sisters.‖ Examine\nthe card’s visual argument closely, and jot down your answers to the following questions. (You can do\nthis exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\n1. Who appears to be the intended audience? What aspects of the text and photograph seem best suited\nto that audience?\n2. What can you tell about Big Brothers Big Sisters from this e-card?\n3. What claims does the image make?\n4. What evidence supports the claims? How effective is it?\n5. What assumptions underlie the argument, connecting evidence to claims?\n\nFOWLcp10-11.doc - 13\n6. How does the visual organization (cropping of the photograph, placement of the text) make the\nargument more or less effective?\n7. Is the argument persuasive to you? Why or why not?\nEXERCISE 11.2 Identifying fallacies in visual arguments\nLocate a current or historical source with extreme views on a subject, such as the Web site of an\noutspoken political commentator, a sensationalist tabloid newspaper, or a collection of wartime\npropaganda (from any nation or era). Photocopy or print an image that seems especially ripe with fallacies\nand, referring to the complete list of fallacies on p. 193, find as many as possible in the image. The\nfollowing sites can help you begin your search:\nPolitical commentators\nAl Franken: shows.airamericaradio.com/alfrankenshow\nRush Limbaugh: rushlimbaugh.com/home/today.guest.html\nTabloids\nNational Enquirer (US): nationalenquirer.com\nDaily Mail (Great Britain): http://www.dailymail.co.uk/pages/live/dailymail/\nhome.html?in_page_id=1766\nWorld War II propaganda\nNorthwestern University library (American images): http://www.library\n.northwestern.edu/govpub/collections/wwii-posters\nCalvin College (German images): http://www.calvin.edu/academic/cas/ gpa\n11b Using visual arguments effectively\nChapter 10 explains how you can use written argument to convince readers of your claims and\nperhaps move them to take action. Weaving images into an argument can strengthen it if the images\nare well chosen to provide evidence and make appropriate appeals.\nNote Any visual you include in a paper requires the same detailed citation as a written source.\nIf you plan to publish your argument online, you will also need to seek permission from the author.\nSee pages 633–37 for more on acknowledging sources and obtaining permissions.\n1 Choosing images\nYou can wait until you’ve drafted an argument before concentrating on what images to\ninclude. This approach keeps your focus on the research and writing needed to craft the best\nargument from sources. But you can also begin thinking visually at the beginning of a project, as\nyou might if your initial interest in the subject was sparked by a compelling image. Either way, ask\nyourself some basic questions as you consider visual options:\nWhich parts of your argument can use visual reinforcement? What can be explained better\nvisually than verbally? Can a graph or chart present data compactly and interestingly? Can\na photograph appeal effectively to readers’ beliefs and values?\nWhat are the limitations or requirements of your writing situation? What do the type of\nwriting you’re doing and its format allow? Look through examples of similar writing to gauge\nthe kinds of illustrations readers will expect. And consider the me-dium you’re writing in: a\nshort animation sequence might be ter-rific in a PowerPoint presentation or on the Web, but a\nprinted document requires photographs, drawings, and other static means of explanation.\nWhat kinds of visuals are readily available on your subject? As you researched your\nsubject, what images seemed especially effective? What sources have you not yet explored?\nTips for\nlocating images, whether printed or online, appear on pages 594–96.\nShould you create original images tailored to your argument? Instead of searching for\nexisting images, would your time be better spent taking your own photographs or using\ncomputer software to compose visual explanations, such as diagrams, charts, and graphs? Tips\nfor creating images appear on pages 120–25.\n2 Using images as evidence\nAn image can attract readers’ attention, but if it stops there it will amount to mere decoration\nor, worse, it will distract readers from the substance of your argument. When you use images as\nevidence for your argument, you engage readers both intellectually and visually.\n\nFOWLcp10-11.doc - 14\nThe images used as evidence in visual arguments fall into four general categories:\nArtifacts serving as the subject of the argument, such as a painting or advertisement you are\nanalyzing. (See pp. 175–77 for an example.)\nVisual records of a subject or incident, such as a historical photograph or a seismic record of\nan earthquake tremor.\nVisual explanations of a concept or trend, such as a diagram of the human respiratory\nsystem or a graph of financial data.\nVisual examples of claims made in the argument, such as a photograph of a school building\nabandoned after funding short-falls or a screen shot of a Web pop-up advertisement.\nTo make an image work hard as evidence, be sure it relates directly to a point in your\nargument, adds to that point, and gives readers something to think about. Always include a caption\nthat explicitly ties the image to your text, so that readers don’t have to puzzle out your intentions,\nand that provides source information. Number images in sequence (Fig. 1, Fig. 2, and so on), and\nrefer to them by number at the appropriate points in your text. (See Chapter 5, pp. 120–25, for\nmore on captioning and numbering illustrations.)\nThe images below and on the next page illustrate approaches to using visual evidence in an\nargument with the following thesis: Television shows focusing on cosmetic procedures are\nencouraging women to opt for such procedures in order to conform to a particular standard of\nbeauty.\n3 Considering images’ appeals to readers\nImages can help to strengthen your argument’s appeals to readers. The appeals are discussed\nin detail on pages 208–10. The summary below suggests how they apply to images:\nRational appeals target readers’ capacity for reasoning logically. Images can strengthen\nthe evidence for an argument if they come from reliable sources, present information fairly\nand accurately, and relate clearly to the paper’s claims.\nEmotional appeals tap into readers’ beliefs and feelings. Images can appeal to a host of\nideas and emotions, including patriotism, curiosity, moral values, sympathy, and anger. Any\nsuch appeal should correctly gauge readers’ beliefs and feelings and should be clearly relevant\nto the argument.\nEthical appeals show readers that you are a competent, fair, and trustworthy source of\ninformation. Images make ethical appeals largely by making appropriate rational and\nemotional appeals. In addition, they can show awareness of readers’ knowledge, prove your\nseriousness, and demonstrate your neutrality.\nTo see how all three appeals can work in images, look again at a photograph used in the\nsample argument paper on pages 214–18. This image illustrates the writer’s claim that television\ncan ease loneliness.\nEXERCISE 11.3 Brainstorming images for a visual argument\nWorking on your own or with others in a small group, apply the four questions for choosing images (p.\n226) to the argument subjects below. Which subject would most likely benefit from images? Which\nwould be most difficult to illustrate? Why? (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\n1. A program to help senior citizens adopt and care for a pet would improve seniors’ lives and benefit\nthe community.\n2. Smoking cigarettes is a good way to meet interesting people.\n3. Today’s military-recruitment advertising targets certain kinds of people more than others.\n4. Our campus needs a better recycling program.\n5. Listening to music while studying helps one retain information.\nEXERCISE 11.4 Filling gaps in a visual argument\nTake another look at the graph and the paired photographs on pages 227–28, taken from a paper claiming\nthat TV makeover shows encourage women to have cosmetic procedures in order to conform to a\nparticular beauty standard. What additional images might bolster the\nargument? Consider especially how you might supplement the graph to connect the increase in cosmetic\nprocedures with the growing popularity of TV makeover shows that feature such procedures.\n\nFOWLcp10-11.doc - 15\nEXERCISE 11.5 Revising an oversimplified visual argument\nThe red and blue map on page 224 comes from Maps and Cartograms of the 2004 US Presidential\nElection Results at www-personal.umich.edu/ ~mejn/election. Visit the site to see illustrations that\ncapture more of the complexity of the 2004 election. Then write a brief visual argument based on one of\nthe other images. Alternatively, write a brief argument about the original red and blue map, explaining its\nflaws. (Remember to include and cite in your paper any image you discuss.)\nEXERCISE 11.6 Creating a deliberately bad visual argument\nPurposely breaking the rules of argument can be fun and illuminating, building your knowledge about\nwhat works best and why. Using one of the topics listed in Exercise 11.3 or a new one, create a visual\nargument and an accompanying paragraph of text that deliberately antagonize readers instead of\nappealing to them. Do your best to do your worst: instead of demonstrating logic, use flawed reasoning or\nconfusing examples; instead of appealing to readers’ values and emotions, let your argument be boring or\nhostile; instead of communicating your credibility and expertise, display ignorance or ineptness.\nEXERCISE 11.7 Revising an ineffective visual argument\nLocate an ineffective visual argument, and use the guidelines on page 229 to improve its likely appeal to\nreaders. If your classmates completed Exercise 11.6, you could revise another student’s deliberately bad\nargument.\nTests for an argument subject\nA good subject:\nConcerns a matter of opinion—a conclusion drawn from evidence.\nCan be disputed: others might take a different position.\nWill be disputed: it is controversial.\nIs something you care about and know about or want to research.\nIs narrow enough to argue in the space and time available.\nA bad subject:\nCannot be disputed because it concerns a fact, such as the distance to Saturn or the functions of the\nhuman liver.\nCannot be disputed because it concerns a personal preference or belief, such as a liking for a certain\nvacation spot or a moral commitment to vegetarianism.\nWill not be disputed because few if any disagree over it—the virtues of a secure home, for instance.\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp10-11.doc - 16\nVisit the companion Web site for more help and additional exercises on writing arguments.\nInductive reasoning\nEVIDENCE\nFacts and expert opinions\nStatistics and opinions about the cost-\neffectiveness of advertising in different media.\nASSUMPTION\nWhat is true in one set of circumstances is true in a similar set of circumstances\nSince many print ads are cost-effective,\nothers must be, too.\nCLAIM\nA generalization from the evidence\nPrint is the most cost-effective advertising medium.\nDeductive reasoning\nASSUMPTION\nA fact, a principle, a belief, or (as here)\na generalization from induction\nPrint is the most cost-effective advertising medium.\nEVIDENCE\nNew information\nCompanies on lean budgets should advertise in the most cost-effective\nmedium.\nCLAIM\nA conclusion linking assumption and\nevidence\nCompanies on lean budgets should advertise in print.\nTests for inductive and deductive reasoning\nInduction\nHave you stated your evidence clearly?\nIs your evidence complete enough and good enough to justify your claim? What is the assumption that\nconnects evidence and claim? Is it believable?\nHave you avoided fallacies?\nDeduction\nWhat are the premises leading to your conclusion? Look especially for unstated premises.\nWhat does the first premise assume? Is the assumption believable?\nDoes the first premise necessarily apply to the second premise?\nIs the second premise believable?\nHave you avoided fallacies?\nResponsible use of evidence\nDon’t distort. You mislead readers when you twist evidence to suit your argument—for instance,\nwhen you claim that crime in your city occurs five times more often than it did in 1955, without\nmentioning that the population is also seven times larger.\nDon’t stack the deck. Ignoring damning evidence is like cheating\nat cards. You must deal forthrightly with the opposition. (See pp. 210–11.)\nDon’t exaggerate. Watch your language. Don’t attempt to manipulate readers by characterizing your\nown evidence as pure and rock-solid and the opposition’s as ridiculous and half-baked. Make the\nevidence speak for itself.\nDon’t oversimplify. Avoid forcing the evidence to support more than it can. (See also p. 196.)\nDon’t misquote. When you cite experts, quote them accurately and fairly.\nStatement of opposing view\nConcession that opposing view is partly valid\nDemonstration that opposing view is irrelevant\nOrganizing an argument’s body\nand response to opposing views\nThe traditional scheme The problem-solution scheme\nClaim 1 and evidence The problem: claims and evidence\nClaim 2 and evidence The solution: claims and evidence\nClaim X and evidence Response to opposing views\nResponse to opposing views\n\nFOWLcp10-11.doc - 17\nVariations on the traditional scheme\nUse a variation if you believe your readers will reject your argument without an early or intermittent\nresponse to opposing views.\nResponse to opposing views Claim 1 and evidence\nClaim 1 and evidence Response to opposing views\nClaim 2 and evidence Claim 2 and evidence\nClaim X and evidence Response to opposing views\nClaim X and evidence\nResponse to opposing views\nChecklist for revising an argument\nThesis\nWhat is your thesis? Where is it stated?\nIn what ways is your thesis statement an arguable claim?\nReasoning\nIf your thesis derives from induction, where have you related the evidence to your generalization?\nIf your thesis derives from deduction, is your syllogism both true and valid?\nHave you avoided fallacies in reasoning?\nEvidence\nWhere have you provided the evidence readers need?\nWhere might your evidence not be accurate, relevant, representative, or adequate? (Answer this\nquestion from the point of view of a neutral or even skeptical reader.)\nAppeals\nWhere have you considered readers’ probable beliefs and values?\nHow are your rational appeals and emotional appeals appropriate for your readers?\nWhat is your ethical appeal? How can you improve it?\nOpposing views\nWhat opposing views have you answered?\nHow successfully have you refuted opposing views? (Again, consider the neutral or skeptical reader.)\nOrganization\nHow clearly does your argument move from one point to the next?\nHow appropriate is your organization given your readers’ likely views?\nYou can download this checklist from ablongman.com/littlebrown. Create a copy and answer the\nquestions for each argument you write.\nIntroduction\nIdentification of prevailing view\nDisagreement with prevailing view\nThesis statement\nmaking three claims for television\nBackground for\nclaim 1: effects of\nloneliness\nEvidence for effects of\nloneliness\nEvidence for effects of\ntelevision on loneliness\nStatement of claim 1\nIllustration supporting claim 1\nFig. 1. Household incomes in the United States, based on data from the\n2000 US Census, from\nSocial Science Data Analysis Network, CensusScope, 15 Oct. 2005\n<http://www.censusscope.org/us/\nchart_income.html>.\nFallacies in a visual argument\nEither/or fallacy: Solid colors implying that all of the voters in each state chose\neither the Republican or the Democratic candidate, when every state had voters for both candidates and for candidates from\nother political parties\nSweeping generalization: Strong contrast implying\nthat voters’ concerns were unconflicted and were represented by a single vote\nThe Electoral College vote in the 2004 US Presidential election: red states for Bush, blue states for Kerry\nE-card from the Web site of\nBig Brothers Big Sisters of America\nImage as evidence\nGraph from a reputable source providing a visual explanation of the overall increase in cosmetic\nprocedures\nCaption explaining the graph and highlighting the 2004 increase, the most\nrelevant to the paper’s claims\nFig. 1.
