Television can be a source of companionship for people whose\nliving situations and limited mobility leave them lonely. Photograph by\nJean Michel Foujols, Corbis image 42-15243193, 13 June 2005\n<http://pro.corbis.com>.\n\nFOWLcp12.doc - 1\n3\nPART\n\nFOWLcp12.doc - 2\nGrammatical\nSentences\n12\nCHAPTER\nUnderstanding\nSentence Grammar\n13\nCHAPTER\nCase of Nouns and\nPronouns\n14\nCHAPTER\nVerbs\n15\nCHAPTER\nAgreement\n16\nCHAPTER\nAdjectives and Adverbs\n\nFOWLcp12.doc - 3\n12\nCHAPTER\nUnderstanding Sentence Grammar\nGrammar describes how language works. Following the rules of standard English grammar is\nwhat allows you to communicate with others across barriers of personality, region, class, or ethnic\norigin. If you are a native English speaker, you follow these rules mostly unconsciously. But when\nyou’re trying to improve your ability to communicate, it can help to make the rules conscious and\nlearn the language used to describe them.\nGrammar reveals a lot about a sentence, even if you don’t know the meanings of all the words:\nThe rumfrums prattly biggled the pooba.\nYou don’t know what this sentence means, but you can infer that some things called rumfrums did\nsomething to a pooba. They big-gled it, whatever that means, in a prattly way. Two grammatical\ncues, especially, make this sentence like The students easily passed the\ntest:\nWord forms. The ending -s means more than one rumfrum. The ending -ed means that\nbiggled is an action that happened in the past. The ending -ly means that prattly probably\ndescribes how the rumfrums biggled.\nWord order. Rumfrums biggled pooba resembles a common se-quence in English: something\n(rumfrums) performed some action (biggled) to or on something else (pooba). Since prattly\ncomes right before the action, it probably describes the action.\nThis chapter explains how such structures work and shows how practicing with them can help\nyou communicate more effectively.\nNote Grammar and style checkers can both offer assistance and cause problems as you\ncompose sentences. Look for the cautions and tips for using such checkers in this and the next five\nparts of this book. For more information about grammar and style checkers, see pages 60–62.\n12a Understanding the basic sentence\nThe sentence is the basic unit of thought. Its grammar consists of words with specific forms\nand functions arranged in specific ways.\n1 Identifying subjects and predicates\nMost sentences make statements. First the subject names something; then the predicate\nmakes an assertion about the subject or describes an action by the subject.\nSubject Predicate\nArt thrives.\nThe simple subject consists of one or more nouns or pronouns, whereas the complete subject also\nincludes any modifiers. The simple predicate consists of one or more verbs, whereas the com-\nplete predicate adds any words needed to complete the meaning of the verb plus any modifiers.\nSometimes, as in the short example Art thrives, the simple and complete subject and predicate\nare the same. More often, they are different:\n\nFOWLcp12.doc - 4\nSubject Predicate\nSome contemporary art stirs controversy.\nCritics and the media discuss and dispute its value.\nIn the second example, the simple subject and simple predicate are both compound: in each, two\nwords joined by a coordinating conjunction (and) serve the same function.\nNote If a sentence contains a word group such as that makes it into established museums or\nbecause viewers finally agree about its quality, you may be tempted to mark the subject and verb in\nthe word group as the subject and verb of the sentence. But these word groups are subordinate\nclauses, made into modifiers by the words they begin with: that and because. See pages 252–53 for\nmore on subordinate clauses.\nThe subject of an English sentence may be a noun (art) or a pronoun that refers to the noun\n(it), but not both. (See p. 235.)\nFaulty Some art it stirs controversy.\nRevised Some art stirs controversy.\n2 Identifying the basic words: Nouns and verbs\nThe following five simple sentences consist almost entirely of two quite different kinds of\nwords:\nSubject Predicate\nThe earth trembled.\nThe earthquake destroyed the city.\nThe result was chaos.\nThe government sent the city aid.\nThe citizens considered the earthquake a disaster.\nThe words in the subject position name things, such as earth, earthquake, and government. In\ncontrast, the words in the predicate position express states or actions, such as trembled, destroyed,\nand sent.\nThese two groups of words work in different ways. Citizen can become citizens, but not\ncitizened. Destroyed can become destroys, but not destroyeds. Grammar reflects such differences\nby identifying the parts of speech or word classes shown in the box above. Except for the and a,\nwhich simply point to and help identify the words after them, the five sentences about the\nearthquake consist entirely of nouns and verbs.\nNouns\nMeaning\nNouns name. They may name a person (Hilary Duff, Jesse Jackson, astronaut), a thing (chair,\nbook, Mt. Rainier), a quality (pain, mystery, simplicity), a place (city, Washington, ocean, Red\nSea), or an idea (reality, peace, success).\nForm\nMost nouns form the possessive to indicate ownership or source. Singular nouns usually add\nan apostrophe plus -s (Auden’s poems); plural nouns usually add just an apostrophe (citizens’\nrights).\nNouns also change form to distinguish between singular (one) and plural (more than one).\nMost nouns add -s or -es for the plural: earthquake, earthquakes; city, cities. Some nouns have\nirregular plurals: woman, women; child, children.\nSome useful rules for forming noun plurals appear on pages 548–49. The irregular plurals\nmust be memorized. Note that some nouns (noncount nouns) do not form plurals in English—for\ninstance, equality, anger, oxygen, equipment. (See p. 237.)\nNouns with the, a, and an\nNouns are often preceded by the or a (an before a vowel sound: an apple). These words are\nusually called articles or determiners and always indicate that a noun follows.\n\nFOWLcp12.doc - 5\nSee pages 326–30 for the rules governing the use of the, a/an, or no article at all before a\nnoun.\nVerbs\nMeaning\nVerbs express an action (bring, change, grow), an occurrence (become, happen), or a state of\nbeing (be, seem).\nForm\nMost verbs can be recognized by two changes in form:\nMost verbs add -d or -ed to indicate a difference between pres-ent and past time: They\nplay today. They played yesterday. Some verbs indicate past time irregularly: eat, ate; begin,\nbegan (see pp. 278–80).\nMost present-time verbs add -s or -es with subjects that are singular nouns: The bear\nescapes. It runs. The woman begins. She sings. The exceptions are be and have, which change\nto is and has.\n(See Chapter 14, pp. 275–92, for more on verb forms.)\nHelping verbs\nCertain forms of all verbs can combine with other words such as do, have, can, might, will,\nand must. These other words are called helping verbs or auxiliary verbs. In verb phrases such as\ncould run, will be running, and has escaped, they help to convey time and other attributes. (See\nChapter 14, pp. 276–77, 283–87.)\nA note on form and function\nIn different sentences an English word may serve different functions, take correspondingly\ndifferent forms, and belong to different word classes. For example:\nThe government sent the city aid. [Aid functions as a noun.]\nGovernments aid citizens. [Aid functions as a verb.]\nBecause words can function in different ways, we must always determine how a particular word\nworks in a sentence before we can identify what part of speech it is. The function of a word in a\nsentence always determines its part of speech in that sentence.\nPronouns\nMost pronouns substitute for nouns and function in sentences as nouns do. In the following\nsentence all three pronouns—who, they, their—refer to nurses:\nSome nurses who have families prefer the night shift because they have more time with their children.\nThe most common pronouns are the personal pronouns (I, you, he, she, it, we, they) and the\nrelative pronouns (who, whoever, which, that). Most of these change form to indicate their\nfunction in the sentence—for instance, He called me. I called him back. (See Chapter 13 for a\ndiscussion of these form changes.)\nEXERCISE 12.1 Identifying subjects and predicates\nIn the following sentences, insert a slash between the complete subject and the complete predicate.\nUnderline each simple subject once and each simple predicate twice. Then use each sentence as a model\nto create a sentence of your own. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nThe pony, the light horse, and the draft horse are three types of domestic horses.\nSample imitation: Toyota, Honda, and Nissan are three brands of Japanese cars.\n1. The leaves fell.\n2. October ends soon.\n3. The orchard owners made apple cider.\n4. They examined each apple carefully before using it.\n\nFOWLcp12.doc - 6\n5. Over a hundred people will buy cider at the roadside stand.\nEXERCISE 12.2 Identifying nouns, verbs, and pronouns\nIn the following sentences identify all words functioning as nouns with N, all words functioning as verbs\nwith V, and all pronouns with P. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nP V N N\nThey took the tour through the museum.\n1. The trees died.\n2. They caught a disease.\n3. The disease was a fungus.\n4. It ruined a grove that was treasured.\n5. Our great-grandfather planted the grove in the last century.\nEXERCISE 12.3 Using nouns and verbs\nIdentify each of the following words as a noun, as a verb, or as both. Then create sentences of your own,\nusing each word in each possible function. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nfly\nNoun and verb.\nThe fly sat on the meat loaf. [Noun.] The planes fly low. [Verb.]\n1. wish 6. label\n2. tie 7. door\n3. swing 8. company\n4. mail 9. whistle\n5. spend 10. glue\n3 Forming sentence patterns with nouns and verbs\nEnglish builds all sentences on the five basic patterns shown in the box on the facing page. As\nthe diagrams indicate, the patterns differ in their predicates because the relation between the verb\nand the remaining words is different.\nThe word order in English sentences may not correspond to word order in the sentences of\nyour native language. English, for instance, strongly prefers subject first, then verb, then any other\nwords, whereas some other languages prefer the verb first. The main exceptions to the word\npatterns discussed below appear on pages 263–64. See also pages 364–70 on positioning modifiers\nin sentences.\nPattern 1: The earth trembled.\nIn the simplest pattern the predicate consists only of the verb. Verbs in this pattern do not\nrequire following words to complete their meaning and thus are called intransitive (from Latin\nwords meaning ―not passing over‖).\nSubject Predicate\nIntransitive verb\nThe earth trembled.\nThe hospital may close.\nPattern 2: The earthquake destroyed the city.\nIn pattern 2 the predicate consists of a verb followed by a noun that identifies who or what\nreceives the action of the verb. This noun is a direct object. Verbs that require direct objects to\ncomplete their meaning are called transitive (―passing over‖): the verb transfers the action from\nsubject to object.\n\nFOWLcp12.doc - 7\nSubject Predicate\nTransitive Direct\nverb object\nThe earthquake destroyed the city.\nEducation opens doors.\nThe distinction between transitive verbs and intransitive verbs like those in pattern 1 is\nimportant because only transitive verbs may be used in the passive voice (The city was destroyed).\n(See p. 302.) Your dictionary says whether a verb is transitive or intransitive. Some verbs (begin,\nlearn, read, write, and others) are both.\nPattern 3: The result was chaos.\nIn pattern 3 the predicate also consists of a verb followed by a noun, but here the noun\nrenames or describes the subject. We could write the sentence The result = chaos. The verb serving\nas an equal sign is a linking verb because it links the subject and the following description. The\nlinking verbs include be, seem, appear, become, grow, remain, stay, prove, feel, look, smell, sound,\nand taste. The word that describes the subject is called a subject complement (it complements, or\ncompletes, the subject).\nSubject Predicate\nLinking Subject\nverb complement\nThe result was chaos.\nThe man became an accountant.\nSubject complements in this sentence pattern may also be adjectives, words such as tall and\nhopeful (see p. 242):\nSubject Predicate\nLinking Subject\nverb complement\nThe result was chaotic.\nThe apartments seem expensive.\nPattern 4: The government sent the city aid.\nIn pattern 4 the predicate consists of a verb followed by two nouns. The second noun, aid, is a\ndirect object (see pattern 2). But the first noun, city, is an indirect object, identifying to or for\nwhom the action of the verb is performed. The direct object and indirect object refer to different\nthings, people, or places.\nSubject Predicate\nTransitive Indirect Direct\nverb object object\nThe government sent the city aid.\nOne company offered its employees bonuses.\nA number of verbs can take indirect objects, including those above and allow, bring, buy,\ndeny, find, get, leave, make, pay, read, sell, show, teach, and write.\nWith some verbs expressing action done to or for someone, the indirect object must be turned\ninto a phrase beginning with to or for. These verbs include admit, announce, demonstrate, explain,\nintroduce, mention, prove, recommend, say, and sug-gest. The to or for phrase then falls after the\ndirect object.\nFaulty The manual explains workers the new procedure.\n\nFOWLcp12.doc - 8\nRevised The manual explains the new procedure to workers.\nPattern 5: The citizens considered the earthquake a disaster.\nIn pattern 5, as in pattern 4, the predicate consists of a verb\nfollowed by two nouns. But in pattern 5 the first noun is a direct\nobject and the second noun renames or describes it. Here the second noun is an object complement\n(it complements, or completes, the object):\nSubject Predicate\nTransitive Direct Object\nverb object complement\nThe citizens considered the earthquake a disaster.\nThe class elected Joan O’Day president.\nLike a subject complement (pattern 3), an object complement may be a noun or an adjective,\nas below:\nSubject Predicate\nTransitive Direct Object\nverb object complement\nThe citizens considered the earthquake disastrous.\nSuccess makes some people nervous.\nEXERCISE 12.4 Identifying sentence patterns\nIn the following sentences, identify each verb as intransitive, transitive, or linking. Then identify each\ndirect object (DO), indirect object (IO), subject complement (SC), and object complement (OC). (You\ncan do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\ntransitive\nverb IO DO DO\nChildren give their parents both headaches and pleasures.\n1. Many people find New York City exciting.\n2. Tourists flock there each year.\n3. Often they visit Times Square first.\n4. The square’s lights are astounding.\n5. The flashing signs sell visitors everything from TVs to underwear.\nEXERCISE 12.5 Creating sentences\nCreate sentences by using each of the following verbs in the pattern indicated. (For the meanings of the\nabbreviations, see the directions for Exercise 12.4.) You may want to change the form of the verb. (You\ncan do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\ngive (S-V-IO-DO)\nSam gave his brother a birthday card.\n1. laugh (S-V) 6. seem (S-V-SC)\n2. elect (S-V-DO-OC) 7. call (S-V-DO-OC)\n3. steal (S-V-DO) 8. become (S-V-SC)\n4. catch (S-V-DO) 9. buy (S-V-IO-DO)\n5. bring (S-V-IO-DO) 10. study (S-V)\n12b Expanding the basic sentence with single words\nMost of the sentences we read, write, or speak are more complex and also more informative\nand interesting than those examined so far. Most sentences contain one or more of the following:\n\nFOWLcp12.doc - 9\n(1) modifying words (discussed here); (2) word groups, called phrases and clauses (p. 244); and (3)\ncombinations of two or more words or word groups of the same kind (p. 258).\n1 Using adjectives and adverbs\nThe simplest expansion of sentences occurs when we add modifying words to describe or limit\nthe nouns and verbs. Modifying words add details:\nRecently, the earth trembled.\nThe earthquake nearly destroyed the old city.\nThe federal government soon sent the city aid.\nThe grant was a very generous one but disappeared too quickly.\nThe underlined words represent two different parts of speech:\nAdjectives describe or modify nouns and pronouns. They specify which one, what quality, or\nhow many.\nold city generous one two pears\nadjective noun adjective pronoun adjective noun\nAdverbs describe or modify verbs, adjectives, other adverbs, and whole groups of words.\nThey specify when, where, how, and to what extent.\nnearly destroyed too quickly\nadverb verb adverb adverb\nvery generous Unfortunately, taxes will rise.\nadverb adjective adverb word group\nAn -ly ending often signals an adverb, but not always: friendly is an adjective; never, not, and\nalways are adverbs. The only way to tell whether a word is an adjective or an adverb is to\ndetermine what it modifies.\nAdjectives and adverbs appear in three forms:\nThe positive form is the basic form, the one listed in the diction-ary: good, green, angry;\nbadly, quickly, angrily.\nThe comparative form indicates a greater degree of the quality named by the word: better,\ngreener, angrier; worse, more quickly, more angrily.\nThe superlative form indicates the greatest degree of the quality named: best, greenest,\nangriest; worst, most quickly, most\nangrily.\n(For further discussion of these forms, see p. 322.)\n2 Using other words as modifiers\nNouns and special forms of verbs may sometimes serve as modifiers of other nouns. In\ncombinations such as office buildings, Thanksgiving prayer, and shock hazard, the first noun\nmodifies the second. In combinations such as singing birds, corrected papers, and broken finger,\nthe first word is a verb form modifying the following noun. (These modifying verb forms are\ndiscussed in more detail on pp. 247–50.) Again, the part of speech to which we assign a word\nalways depends on its function in a sentence.\nEXERCISE 12.6 Identifying and using adjectives and adverbs\nIdentify the adjectives and adverbs in the following sentences. Then use each sentence as a model for\ncreating a sentence of your own. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nadjective adverb adjective\nThe red barn sat uncomfortably among modern buildings.\n\nFOWLcp12.doc - 10\nSample imitation: The little girl complained loudly to her busy mother.\n1. The blue water glistened in the hot afternoon sunlight.\n2. Happily, children dipped their toes in the cool lake.\n3. Excitedly, some of the children hopped into the water.\n4. Cautious parents watched from their shady porches.\n5. The children played contentedly until the day finally ended.\nEXERCISE 12.7 Using verb forms as modifiers\nUse each of the following verb forms to modify a noun in a sentence\nof your own. You will have to delete and rearrange words. (You can do this exercise\nonline at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nParis offers tourists food. Paris offers food proudly. The food is delicious.\nParis proudly offers tourists delicious food.\n1. The turn of the century ushered in technology and materials. The century was the twentieth. The\ntechnology was improved. The materials were new.\n2. A skeleton made the construction of skyscrapers possible. The skeleton was sturdy. It was made of\nsteel.\n3. By 1913 the Woolworth Building, with its ornaments, stood 760 feet (55 stories). The building was\ntowering. The ornaments were Gothic.\n4. At 1450 feet the Sears Tower in Chicago doubles the height of the Woolworth Building. The\ndoubling is now. The Woolworth height is puny. The puniness is relative.\n5. Skyscrapers would not have been practical if Elisha Graves Otis had not built the elevator in 1857. It\nwas the first elevator. The elevator was safe. It served passengers.\n12c Expanding the basic sentence with word groups\nMost sentences we read or write contain whole word groups that serve as nouns and modifiers.\nSuch word groups enable us to combine several bits of information into one sentence and to make\nthe relations among them clear, as in the following sentence:\nsubject\nWhen the experiment succeeded, the researchers, excited by the re-\nverb object\nsults, expanded the study to enroll more patients.\nAttached to researchers expanded the study, the skeleton of this sentence, are three groups of\nwords that add related information: When the experiment succeeded, excited by the results, to\nenroll more patients. These constructions are phrases and clauses:\nA phrase is a group of related words that lacks either a subject or a predicate or both:\nexcited by the results, to enroll more patients.\nA clause contains both a subject and a predicate: When the\nexperiment succeeded and the researchers expanded the study are both clauses, though only\n\nFOWLcp12.doc - 11\nthe second can stand alone as a\nsentence.\n1 Using prepositional phrases\nPrepositions are connecting words. Unlike nouns, verbs, and modifiers, which may change\nform, prepositions never change form. As the box below shows, many prepositions signal\nrelationships of time or space; others signal relationships such as addition, comparison or contrast,\ncause or effect, concession, condition, opposition, possession, and source. Notice that some\nprepositions con-sist of more than one word.\nA preposition connects a noun or pronoun to another word in the sentence:\nRobins nest in trees. The noun or pronoun so connected (trees) is the\nobject of the preposition. The preposition plus its object and any\nmodifiers is a prepositional phrase:\nPreposition Object\non the surface\nupon entering the room\nfrom where you are standing\nexcept for ten employees\nPrepositional phrases function as adjectives (modifying nouns) or as adverbs (modifying\nverbs, adjectives, or other adverbs). As modifiers, they add details that make sentences clearer and\nmore interesting for readers.\nLife on a raft was an opportunity for adventure.\nadjective phrase adjective phrase\nHuck Finn rode the raft by choice.\nadverb phrase\nThe meanings and idiomatic uses of English prepositions can be difficult to master; most\nmust be memorized or looked up in a dictionary. (A list of dictionaries for English as a second\nlanguage appears on p. 537.) See pages 291–92 for the uses of prepositions in two-word verbs such\nas look after and pages 523–25 for the uses of prepositions in idioms.\nPunctuating prepositional phrases\nSince a prepositional phrase lacks a subject and a predicate, it should not be punctuated as a\ncomplete sentence. If it is, the result is a sentence fragment (Chapter 17):\nFragment Toward the sun.\nThe phrase must be attached to another group of words containing both a subject and a predicate:\nRevised The plane turned toward the sun.\nA prepositional phrase that introduces a sentence is set off with punctuation, usually a comma,\nunless it is short (see pp. 433–34):\nAccording to the newspaper and other sources the governor has re-luctantly decided to veto the bill.\nIn 1865 the Civil War finally ended.\nA prepositional phrase that interrupts or concludes a sentence is not set off with punctuation when\nit is essential to the meaning of the word or words it modifies (see p. 435):\nThe announcement of a tuition increase surprised no one.\nStudents expected new fees for the coming year.\nWhen an interrupting or concluding prepositional phrase is not essential to meaning, but merely\nadds information to the sentence, then it is set off with punctuation, usually a comma or commas\n(see p. 435):\n\nFOWLcp12.doc - 12\nThe governor according to the newspaper and other sources has reluctantly decided to veto the bill.\nAs all the preceding examples illustrate, a preposition and its object are not separated by a\ncomma.\nEXERCISE 12.9 IDENTIFYING PREPOSITIONAL PHRASES\nIdentify the prepositional phrases in the following passage. Indicate whether each phrase functions as an\nadjective or as an adverb, and name the word that the phrase modifies. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nadverb adverb adjective\nAfter an hour I finally arrived at the home of my professor.\nOn July 3, 1863, at Gettysburg, Pennsylvania, General Robert E. Lee gambled unsuccessfully for a\nConfederate victory in the American Civil War. Called Pickett’s Charge, the battle was one of the most\ndisastrous conflicts of the war. Confederate and Union forces faced each other on parallel ridges separated\nby almost a mile of open fields. After an artillery bombardment of the Union position, about 12,000\nConfederate infantry marched toward the Union ridge. The Union guns had been silent but suddenly\nroared, mowing the approaching Confederates. Within an hour, perhaps half of the Confederate soldiers\nlay wounded or dead.\nEXERCISE 12.10 Sentence combining: Prepositional phrases\nTo practice writing sentences with prepositional phrases, combine each group of sentences below into one\nsentence that includes one or two prepositional phrases. You will have to add, delete, and rearrange\nwords. Some items have more than one possible answer. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nI will start working. The new job will pay the minimum wage.\nI will start working at a new job for the minimum wage.\n1. The slow loris protects itself well. Its habitat is Southeast Asia. It pos-sesses a poisonous chemical.\n2. To frighten predators, the loris exudes the chemical. The chem-ical comes from a gland. The gland is\non the loris’s upper arm.\n3. The loris’s chemical is highly toxic. The chemical is not like a skunk’s spray. Even small quantities\nof the chemical are toxic.\n4. A tiny dose can affect a human. The dose would get in the mouth. The human would be sent into\nshock.\n5. Predators probably can sense the toxin. They detect it at a distance. They use their nasal organs.\n2 Using verbals and verbal phrases\nVerbals are special verb forms such as smoking or hidden or to win that can function as nouns\n(smoking is dangerous) or as modifiers (the hidden money, the urge to win).\nNote A verbal cannot stand alone as the complete verb in the predicate of a sentence. For\nexample, The man smoking and The money hidden are not sentences but sentence fragments (see p.\n334). Any verbal must combine with a helping verb to serve as the predicate of a sentence: The\nman was smoking. The money is hidden.\nBecause verbals cannot serve alone as sentence predicates, they are sometimes called\nnonfinite verbs (in essence, they are ―unfinished‖). Finite verbs, in contrast, can make an assertion\nor express a state of being without a helping verb (they are ―finished‖). Either of the two tests\nbelow can distinguish finite and nonfinite verbs.\nThere are three kinds of verbals: participles, gerunds, and infinitives.\nParticiples\nAll verbs have two participle forms, a present and a past. The present participle consists of\nthe dictionary form of the verb plus the ending -ing: beginning, completing, hiding. The past\nparticiple of most verbs consists of the dictionary form plus -d or -ed: believed, completed. Some\ncommon verbs have an irregular past participle, such as begun or hidden. (See pp. 278–80.)\nBoth present and past participles function as adjectives to modify nouns and pronouns:\nShopping malls sometimes frustrate shoppers.\n\nFOWLcp12.doc - 13\nShoppers may feel trapped.\nFor English verbs expressing feeling, the present and past participles have different meanings:\nIt was a boring lecture. The bored students slept. See pages 325–26.\nGerunds\nGerund is the name given to the -ing form of the verb when it serves as a noun:\nsubject\nStrolling through stores can exhaust the hardiest shopper.\nobject\nMany children learn to hate shopping.\nPresent participles and gerunds can be distinguished only by their function in a sentence. If the\n-ing form functions as an adjective (a teaching degree), it is a present participle. If the -ing form\nfunctions as a noun (Teaching is difficult), it is a gerund.\nIn English, always use a gerund, not any other verb form, as the object of a preposition:\nDiners are prohibited from smoking. See also the culture-language note below.\nInfinitives\nThe infinitive is the to form of the verb, the dictionary form preceded by the infinitive marker\nto: to begin, to hide, to run. Infinitives may function as adjectives, nouns, or adverbs:\nadjective\nThe question to answer is why shoppers endure mall fatigue.\nnoun\nThe solution for mall fatigue is to leave.\nadverb\nStill, shoppers find it difficult to quit.\nInfinitives and gerunds may follow some English verbs and not others and may differ in\nmeaning after a verb: The singer stopped to sing. The singer stopped singing. (See pp. 288–90.)\nVerbal phrases\nParticiples, gerunds, and infinitives—like other verb forms—may take subjects, objects, or\ncomplements, and they may be modified by adverbs. The verbal and all the words immediately\nrelated to it make up a verbal phrase. With verbal phrases, we can create concise sentences packed\nwith information.\nLike participles, participial phrases always serve as adjectives, modifying nouns or pronouns:\nBuying things, most shoppers feel themselves in control.\nThey make selections determined by personal taste.\nGerund phrases, like gerunds, always serve as nouns:\nsubject\nShopping for clothing and other items satisfies personal needs.\nobject of preposition\nMalls are good at creating such needs.\nInfinitive phrases may serve as nouns, adverbs, or adjectives:\nsentence subject subject complement\nTo design a mall is to create an artificial environment.\nnoun phrase noun phrase\nMalls are designed to make shoppers feel safe.\nadverb phrase\nThe environment supports the impulse to shop for oneself.\nadjective phrase\n\nFOWLcp12.doc - 14\nNote When an infinitive or infinitive phrase serves as a noun after verbs such as hear, help,\nlet, make, see, and watch, the infinitive marker to is omitted: We all heard her tell [not to tell] the\nstory.\nPunctuating verbals and verbal phrases\nVerbal phrases punctuated as complete sentences are sentence fragments (Chapter 17). A\ncomplete sentence must contain a subject and a finite verb (p. 248):\nFragment Treating the patients kindly.\nRevised She treats the patients kindly.\nA verbal or verbal phrase serving as a modifier is almost always set off with a comma when it\nintroduces a sentence (see p. 433):\nTo pay tuition some students work at two jobs.\nA modifying verbal or verbal phrase that interrupts or concludes a sentence is not set off with\npunctuation when it is essential to the meaning of the word or words it modifies (see p. 435):\nJobs paying well are hard to find.\nWhen an interrupting or concluding verbal modifier is not essential to meaning but merely adds\ninformation to the sentence, it is set off with punctuation, usually a comma or commas (see p. 435):\nOne good job paying twelve dollars an hour was filled in fifteen\nminutes.\nEXERCISE 12.11 Identifying verbals and verbal phrases\nThe following sentences contain participles, gerunds, and infinitives as well as participial, gerund, and\ninfinitive phrases. First identify each verbal or verbal phrase. Then indicate whether it is used as an\nadjective, an adverb, or a noun. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/ littlebrown.)\nExample:\nadjective adverb\nLaughing, the talk-show host prodded her guest to speak.\n1. Written in 1850 by Nathaniel Hawthorne, The Scarlet Letter tells the story of Hester Prynne.\n2. Shunned by the community, Hester endures her loneliness.\n3. Hester is humble enough to withstand her Puritan neighbors’ cutting remarks.\n4. Despite the cruel treatment, the determined young woman re-fuses to leave her home.\n5. By living a life of patience and unselfishness, Hester eventually be-comes the community’s angel.\nEXERCISE 12.12 SENTENCE COMBINING:\nVERBALS AND VERBAL PHRASES\nTo practice writing sentences with verbals and verbal phrases, combine each of the following pairs of\nsentences into one sentence. You will have to add, delete, change, and rearrange words. Each item has\nmore than one possible answer. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nMy father took pleasure in mean pranks. For instance, he hid the neighbor’s cat.\nMy father took pleasure in mean pranks such as hiding the neighbor’s cat.\n1. Air pollution is a health problem. It affects millions of Americans.\n2. The air has been polluted mainly by industries and automobiles. It contains toxic chemicals.\n3. Environmentalists pressure politicians. They think politicians should pass stricter laws.\n4. Many politicians waver. They are not necessarily against environmentalism.\n5. The problems are too complex. They cannot be solved easily.\n3 Using absolute phrases\nAbsolute phrases consist of a noun or pronoun and a participle, plus any modifiers:\n\nFOWLcp12.doc - 15\nabsolute phrase\nMany ethnic groups, their own place established, are making way for new arrivals.\nabsolute phrase absolute phrase\nTheir native lands left behind, an uncertain future looming, immigrants face many obstacles.\nThese phrases are called absolute (from a Latin word meaning ―free‖) because they have no\nspecific grammatical connection to a noun, verb, or any other word in the rest of the sentence.\nInstead, they modify the entire rest of the sentence, adding information.\nNotice that absolute phrases, unlike participial phrases, always contain a subject. Compare the\nfollowing sentences:\nparticipial phrase\nFor many immigrants learning English, the language introduces American culture.\nabsolute phrase\nThe immigrants having learned English, their opportunities widen.\nWe often omit the participle from an absolute phrase when it is some form of be, such as being\nor having been:\nabsolute phrase\nTwo languages [being] at hand, bilingual citizens in fact have many cultural and occupational\nadvantages.\nPunctuating absolute phrases\nAbsolute phrases are always set off from the rest of the sentence with punctuation, usually a\ncomma or commas (see also pp. 440–41):\nTheir future more secure these citizens will make room for new ar-rivals.\nThese citizens their future more secure will make room for new ar-rivals.\nEXERCISE 12.13 Sentence combining: Absolute phrases\nTo practice writing sentences with absolute phrases, combine each pair of sentences below into one\nsentence that contains an absolute phrase. You will have to add, delete, change, and rearrange words.\n(You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nThe flower’s petals wilted. It looked pathetic.\nIts petals wilted, the flower looked pathetic.\n1. Geraldine Ferraro’s face beamed. She enjoyed the crowd’s cheers after her nomination for Vice\nPresident.\n2. A vacancy had occurred. Sandra Day O’Connor was appointed the first female Supreme Court\njustice.\n3. Her appointment was confirmed. Condoleezza Rice became the first female national security adviser.\n4. The midterm elections were over. Nancy Pelosi was elected the first female minority leader of the\nHouse of Representatives.\n5. The election was won. Elizabeth Dole was a US senator from North Carolina.\n4 Using subordinate clauses\nA clause is any group of words that contains both a subject and a predicate. There are two\nkinds of clauses, and the distinction between them is important:\nA main or independent clause makes a complete statement and can stand alone as a\nsentence: The sky darkened.\nA subordinate or dependent clause is just like a main clause except that it begins with a\nsubordinating word: when the sky darkened; because he wants it; whoever calls. The\nsubordinating word reduces the clause to a single part of speech—an adjective, an adverb, or a\nnoun—that supports the idea in a main clause. Because it only modifies or names something, a\nsubordinate clause cannot stand alone as a sentence (see the discussion of punctuation on p.\n255). (The word subordinate means ―secon-dary‖ or ―controlled by another.‖ It comes from\nthe Latin sub, ―under,‖ and ordo, ―order.‖)\nThe following examples show the differences between main and subordinate clauses:\nmain clause main clause\n\nFOWLcp12.doc - 16\nThe school teaches parents. It is unusual.\nmain clause\nBecause the school teaches parents, it is unusual.\nmain clause main clause\nSome parents avoid their children’s schools. They are often illiterate.\nmain clause\nParents who are illiterate often avoid their children’s schools.\nsubordinate clause\nTwo kinds of subordinating words introduce subordinate clauses:\nsubordinating conjunctions and relative pronouns.\nSubordinating conjunctions\nSubordinating conjunctions, like prepositions, never change form in any way. In the\nfollowing box they are arranged by the relationships they signal. (Some fit in more than one group.)\nSubordinating conjunctions convey their meaning without help from other function words,\nsuch as the coordinating conjunctions and, but, for, and so (p. 259).\nFaulty Even though the parents are illiterate, but their children may read well. [Even though and but\nhave the same meaning, so both are not needed.]\nRevised Even though the parents are illiterate, their children may read well.\nRelative pronouns\nUnlike subordinating conjunctions, relative pronouns usually act as subjects or objects in\ntheir own clauses, and two of them (who and whoever) change form accordingly (see pp. 271–73).\nSubordinate clauses\nSubordinate clauses function as adjectives, adverbs, or nouns.\nAdjective clauses\nAdjective clauses modify nouns and pronouns. They usually begin with the relative pronoun\nwho, whom, whose, which, or that, although a few adjective clauses begin with when or where\n(standing for in which, on which, or at which). The pronoun is the subject or object of the clause it\nbegins. The clause ordinarily falls immediately after the noun or pronoun it modifies:\nParents who are illiterate often have bad memories of school.\nSchools that involve parents are more successful with children.\nOne school, which is open year-round, helps parents learn to read.\nThe school is in a city where the illiteracy rate is high.\nAdverb clauses\nLike adverbs, adverb clauses modify verbs, adjectives, other adverbs, and whole groups of\nwords. They usually tell how, why, when, where, under what conditions, or with what result. They\nalways begin with subordinating conjunctions.\nThe school began teaching parents when adult illiteracy gained national attention.\nAt first the program was not as successful as its founders had hoped.\nBecause it was directed at people who could not read, advertising had to be inventive.\nNoun clauses\nNoun clauses function as subjects, objects, and complements in sentences. They begin with\nthat, what, whatever, who, whom, who-ever, whomever, when, where, whether, why, or how.\nUnlike adjective and adverb clauses, noun clauses replace a word (a noun) within a clause;\ntherefore, they can be difficult to identify.\nsentence subject\n\nFOWLcp12.doc - 17\nWhether the program would succeed depended on door-to-door ad-vertising.\nobject of verb\nTeachers explained in person how the program would work.\nobject of preposition\nA few parents were anxious about what their children would think.\nElliptical clauses\nA subordinate clause that is grammatically incomplete but clear in meaning is an elliptical\nclause (ellipsis means ―omission‖). The meaning of the clause is clear because the missing element\ncan be supplied from the context. Most often the elements omitted are the pronouns that, which,\nand whom or the predicate from the second part of a comparison.\nSkepticism and fear were among the feelings [that] the parents voiced.\nThe parents knew their children could read better than they [could read].\nPunctuating subordinate clauses\nSubordinate clauses punctuated as complete sentences are sentence fragments (Chapter 17).\nThough a subordinate clause contains a subject and a predicate and thus resembles a complete\nsentence, it also begins with a subordinating word that makes it into an adjective, adverb, or noun.\nA single part of speech cannot stand alone as a complete sentence.\nFragment Because a door was ajar.\nRevised A door was ajar.\nRevised The secret leaked because a door was ajar.\nA subordinate clause serving as an adverb is almost always set off with a comma when it\nintroduces a sentence (see p. 433):\nAlthough the project was almost completed it lost its funding.\nA modifying subordinate clause that interrupts or concludes a main clause is not set off with\npunctuation when it is essential to the meaning of the word or words it modifies (see p. 435):\nThe woman who directed the project lost her job.\nThe project lost its funding because it was not completed on tim.\nWhen an interrupting or concluding subordinate clause is not essential to meaning, but merely adds\ninformation to the sentence, it is set off with punctuation, usually a comma or commas (see p. 435):\nThe project lost its funding although it was almost completed.\nThe director who holds a PhD sought new funding.\nEXERCISE 12.14 Identifying subordinate clauses\nIdentify the subordinate clauses in the following sentences. Then indicate whether each is used as an\nadjective, an adverb, or a noun. [Object of explained.]\n1. Scientists who want to catch the slightest signals from space use extremely sensitive receivers.\n2. Even though they have had to fight for funding, these scientists have persisted in their research.\n3. The research is called SETI, which stands for Search for Extra-terrestrial Intelligence.\n4. Each item has\nmore than one possible answer. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\n\nFOWLcp12.doc - 18\nExample:\nShe did not have her tire irons with her. She could not change her bicycle tire.\nBecause she did not have her tire irons with her, she could not change her bicycle tire.\n1. Moviegoers expect something. Movie sequels should be as ex-\nciting as the original films.\n2. A few sequels are good films. Most are poor imitations of the originals.\n3. A sequel to a blockbuster film arrives in the theater. Crowds quickly line up to see it.\n4. Viewers pay to see the same villains and heroes. They remember these characters fondly.\n5. Afterward, viewers often grumble about filmmakers. The filmmakers rehash tired plots and\ncharacters.\n5 Using appositives\nAn appositive is usually a noun that renames another noun nearby, most often the noun just\nbefore the appositive. (The word appositive derives from a Latin word that means ―placed near to‖\nor ―applied to.‖) An appositive phrase includes modifiers as well.\nBizen ware, a dark stoneware, has been produced in Japan since the fourteenth century.\nThe name Bizen comes from the location of the kilns used to fire the pottery.\nAll appositives can replace the words they refer to: A dark stoneware has been produced in Japan.\nAppositives are often introduced by words and phrases such as or, that is, such as, for\nexample, and in other words:\nBizen ware is used in the Japanese tea ceremony, that is, the Zen Buddhist observance that links\nmeditation and art.\nAppositives are economical alternatives to adjective clauses con-taining a form of be, as\nshown in the next example.\nBizen ware, [which is] a dark stoneware, has been produced in Japan since the fourteenth century.\nAlthough most appositives are nouns that rename other nouns, they may also be and rename\nother parts of speech, such as the verb thrown in the sentence below:\nThe pottery is thrown, or formed on a potter’s wheel.\nPunctuating appositives\nAppositives punctuated as complete sentences are sentence fragments (see Chapter 17). To\ncorrect such fragments, you can usually connect the appositive to the main clause containing the\nword referred to:\nFragment An exceedingly tall man with narrow shoulders.\nRevised He stood next to a basketball player, an exceedingly tall\nman with narrow shoulders.\nAn appositive is not set off with punctuation when it is essential to the meaning of the word it\nrefers to (see pp. 437–38):\nThe verb howl comes from the Old English verb houlen.\nWhen an appositive is not essential to the meaning of the word it refers to, it is set off with\npunctuation, usually a comma or commas (see pp. 437–38):\nAn aged elm the tree was struck by lightning.\nThe tree an aged elm was struck by lightning.\nLightning struck the tree an aged elm.\nA nonessential appositive is sometimes set off with a dash or dashes, especially when it contains\ncommas (see p. 480):\nThree people—Will, Erica, and Alex—object to the new procedure.\nA concluding appositive is sometimes set off with a colon (see p. 477):\n\nFOWLcp12.doc - 19\nTwo principles guide the judge’s decisions: justice and mercy.\nEXERCISE 12.16 Sentence combining: Appositives\nTo practice writing sentences with appositives, combine each pair of sentences into one sentence that\ncontains an appositive. You will have to delete and rearrange words. Some items have more than one\npossible answer. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nThe largest land animal is the elephant. The elephant is also one of the most intelligent animals.\nThe largest land animal, the elephant, is also one of the most intelligent animals.\n1. Some people perform amazing feats when they are very young. These people are geniuses from birth.\n2. John Stuart Mill was a British philosopher. He had written a history of Rome by age seven.\n3. Two great artists began their work at age four. They were Paul Klee and Gustav Mahler.\n4. Mahler was a Bohemian composer of intensely emotional works. He was also the child of a brutal\nfather.\n5. Paul Klee was a Swiss painter. As a child he was frightened by his own drawings of devils.\n12d Compounding words, phrases, and clauses\nA compound construction combines words that are closely related and equally important. It\nmakes writing clearer and more economical because it pulls together linked information.\nHeadaches can be controlled by biofeedback. Heart rate can be controlled by biofeedback.\ncompound subject\nHeadaches and heart rate can be controlled by biofeedback.\nWithout medication, biofeedback cures headaches. It steadies heart rate. It lowers blood pressure. It\nrelaxes muscles.\ncompound predicate\nWithout medication, biofeedback cures headaches, steadies heart\nrate, lowers blood pressure, and relaxes muscles.\n1 Using coordinating conjunctions and correlative conjunctions\nTwo kinds of words create compound constructions: coordinating and correlative\nconjunctions. Coordinating conjunctions are few and do not change form. In the following box\nthe relationship that each conjunction signals appears in parentheses.\nThe coordinating conjunctions and, but, nor, and or always con- nect words or word groups of\nthe same kind—that is, two or more nouns, verbs, adjectives, adverbs, phrases, subordinate clauses,\nor main clauses:\nBiofeedback or simple relaxation can relieve headaches.\nBiofeedback is effective but costly.\nRelaxation also works well, and it is inexpensive.\nThe conjunctions for and so connect only main clauses. For indicates\ncause; so indicates effect.\nBiofeedback can be costly, for the training involves technical equipment and specialists.\nRelaxation can be difficult to learn alone, so some people do seek help from specialists.\nSome coordinating conjunctions pair up with other words to form correlative conjunctions.\nIn the following box the relationship each conjunction signals appears in parentheses.\nBoth biofeedback and relaxation can relieve headaches.\nThe techniques require neither psychotherapy nor medication.\nThe headache sufferer learns not only to recognize the causes of head-aches but also to control those\ncauses.\n\nFOWLcp12.doc - 20\nPunctuating compounded words, phrases, and clauses\nTwo words, phrases, or subordinate clauses that are connected by a coordinating conjunction\nare not separated by a comma (see\np. 449):\nThe library needs renovation and rebuilding.\nThe work will begin after the spring term ends but before the fall term begins.\nWhen two main clauses are joined into one sentence with a coordinating conjunction, a\ncomma precedes the conjunction (see\np. 432):\nThe project will be lengthy and everyone will suffer some incon-venience.\nWhen two main clauses are joined without a coordinating conjunction, they must be separated with\na semicolon to avoid the error called a comma splice (see p. 342):\nThe work cannot be delayed; it’s already overdue.\nIn a series of three or more items, commas separate the items, with and usually preceding the\nlast item (see p. 441):\nThe renovated library will feature new study carrels new shelving and a larger reference section.\nSemicolons sometimes separate the items in a series if they are long or contain commas (see p.\n457).\nA comma also separates two or more adjectives when they mod-ify a noun equally and are not\njoined by a coordinating conjunction (see p. 442):\nCracked crumbling walls will be repaired.\nThe comma does not separate adjectives when the one nearer the noun is more closely related to it\nin meaning (see p. 442):\nNew reading lounges will replace the old ones.\n2 Using conjunctive adverbs\nOne other kind of connecting word, called a conjunctive adverb, relates only main clauses,\nnot words, phrases, or subordinate clauses. In the following box the conjunctive adverbs are\narranged by the relationships they signal.\nIt’s important to distinguish between conjunctive adverbs and conjunctions (coordinating and\nsubordinating) because they demand different punctuation (see the next page). Conjunctive adverbs\nare adverbs: they describe the relation of ideas in two clauses, and, like most adverbs, they can\nmove around in their clause:\nRelaxation techniques have improved; however, few people know them.\nRelaxation techniques have improved; few people know them, how-ever.\nIn contrast, conjunctions bind two clauses into a single grammatical unit, and they cannot be\nmoved:\nAlthough few people know them, relaxation techniques have im-proved. [The subordinating conjunction\ncan’t be moved: Few people know them although, relaxation techniques have improved.]\nRelaxation techniques have improved, but few people know them. [The coordinating conjunction can’t be\nmoved: Relaxation techniques have improved, few people know them but.]\nNote Some connecting words have more than one use. After, until, and some other words may\nbe either prepositions or subordinating conjunctions. Some prepositions, such as behind and in, can\nserve also as adverbs, as in He trailed behind. And some conjunctive adverbs, particularly however,\nmay also serve simply as adverbs in sentences such as However much the books cost, we must have\nthem. Again, the part of speech of a word depends on its function in a sen-tence.\n\nFOWLcp12.doc - 21\nPunctuating sentences containing conjunctive adverbs\nBecause the two main clauses related by a conjunctive adverb remain independent units, they\nmust be separated by a semicolon (see p. 253). If they are separated by a comma, the result is a\ncomma splice (Chapter 18):\nComma splice Interest rates rose, therefore, real estate prices de-clined.\nRevised Interest rates rose; therefore, real estate prices de-clined.\nA conjunctive adverb is almost always set off from its clause with a comma or commas (see p.\n438):\nThe decline was small; however, some investors were badly hurt.\nThe decline was small; some investors, however, were badly hurt.\nEXERCISE 12.17 Sentence combining: Compound constructions\nTo practice compounding words, phrases, and clauses, combine each of the following pairs of sentences\ninto one sentence that is as short\nas possible without altering meaning. Use an appropriate connecting word of the type specified in\nparentheses, referring to the lists on pages 260–61 as necessary. You will have to add, delete, and\nrearrange words, and you may have to change or add punctuation. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nThe encyclopedia had some information. It was not detailed enough. (Conjunctive adverb.)\nThe encyclopedia had some information; however, it was not detailed enough.\n1. All too often people assume that old age is not a productive time. Many people in their nineties have\nhad great achievements. (Con-junctive adverb.)\n2. In his nineties the philosopher Bertrand Russell spoke vigorously for international peace. He spoke\nfor nuclear disarmament. (Cor-\nrelative conjunction.)\n3. Grandma Moses did not retire to an easy chair. She began painting at age seventy-six and was still\ngoing at one hundred. (Con-junctive adverb.)\n4. The British general George Higginson published his memoirs after\nhe was ninety. The British archaeologist Margaret Murray published her memoirs after she was\nninety. (Coordinating conjunction.)\n5. The architect Frank Lloyd Wright designed his first building at age twenty. He designed his last\nbuilding at age ninety. (Coordinating conjunction.)\n12e Changing the usual word order\nSo far, all the examples of basic sentence grammar have been similar: the subject of the\nsentence comes first, naming the performer of the predicate’s action, and the predicate comes\nsecond. This arrangement describes most English sentences, but four kinds of sentences change the\norder.\nQuestions\nIn most questions the verb or part of it precedes the subject:\nverb subject verb\nHave interest rates been rising?\nverb subject verb\nDid rates rise?\nverb subject verb\nWhy did rates rise today?\nsubject verb\nWhat is the answer? [Normal subject-verb order.]\nCommands\nIn commands the subject you is omitted:\nverb\n\nFOWLcp12.doc - 22\nThink of the options.\nverb\nWatch the news.\nPassive sentences\nGenerally, the subject performs the action of a verb in the active voice. But sometimes the\nsubject receives the action of a verb in the passive voice:\nsubjectverb\nActive Kyong wrote the paper.\nsubject verb\nPassive The paper was written by Kyong.\nSee pages 302–03 for more on forming the passive voice, and see page 303 on overuse of the\npassive voice.\nSentences with postponed subjects\nThe subject follows the predicate in two sentence patterns. The normal order may be reversed\nfor emphasis:\nHenry comes here. [Normal order.]\nHere comes Henry. [Reversed order.]\nOr the word there or it may postpone the subject:\nverb subject\nThere will be eighteen people attending the meeting.\nverb subject\nIt was surprising that Marinetti was nominated.\nThere and it in such sentences are called expletives. Expletive sentences have their uses, but they\ncan also be wordy and unemphatic (see p. 534).\nWhen you use an expletive construction, be careful to include there or it. Only commands and\nsome questions can begin with verbs.\nFaulty No one predicted the nomination. Were no polls showing\nMarinetti ahead.\nRevised No one predicted the nomination. There were no polls show-\ning Marinetti ahead.\nEXERCISE 12.18 Forming questions and commands\nForm a question and a command from the following noun and verb pairs. Berry was congratulated by the press for being the first African American to win the award.\n12f Classifying sentences\nWe describe and classify sentences in two different ways: by function (statement, question,\ncommand, exclamation, and so on) or by structure. Four basic sentence structures are possible:\nsimple, compound, complex, and compound-complex. Each structure gives different emphasis to\nthe sentence’s main idea or ideas and to any supporting information.\n1 Writing simple sentences\nA simple sentence consists of a single main clause and no subordinate clause:\nmain clause\nLast summer was unusually hot.\nmain clause\nThe summer made many farmers leave the area for good or reduced\nthem to bare existence.\n2 Writing compound sentences\nA compound sentence consists of two or more main clauses and no subordinate clause. The\nclauses may be joined by a coordinating conjunction and a comma, by a semicolon alone, or by a\nconjunctive adverb and a semicolon.\nmain clause main clause\nLast July was hot, but August was even hotter.\nmain clause main clause\nThe hot sun scorched the earth; the lack of rain killed many crops.\n3 Writing complex sentences\nA complex sentence contains one main clause and one or more subordinate clauses:\nmain clause subordinate clause\nRain finally came, although many had left the area by then.\nmain clause subordinate clause\nThose who remained were able to start anew because the government\ncame to their aid.\nNotice that length does not determine whether a sentence is complex or simple; both kinds can be\nshort or long.\n4 Writing compound-complex sentences\nA compound-complex sentence has the characteristics of both the compound sentence (two\nor more main clauses) and the complex sentence (at least one subordinate clause):\nsubordinate clause main clause\nEven though government aid finally came, many people had already\nmain clause\nbeen reduced to poverty, and others had been forced to move.\nEXERCISE 12.20 Identifying sentence structures\nMark the main clauses and subordinate clauses in the following sentences. In the example, the word group that makes it into\nmuseums does not name the actor but modifies it.\nSimple subject art\nIdentify the predicate.\nAsk what the sentence asserts about the subject: what is its action, or what state is it in? In the\nexample, the assertion about art is that it has often survived controversy.\nComplete predicate has often survived controversy\nIsolate the verb, the simple predicate, by changing the time of the subject’s action. The simple\npredicate is the word or words that change as a result.\nExample Art . . . has often survived controversy.\nPresent Art . . . often survives controversy.\nFuture Art . . . often will survive controversy.\nSimple predicate has survived\nThe parts of speech\nNouns name persons, places, things, ideas, or qualities: Roosevelt, girl, Kip River, coastline, Koran,\ntable, strife, happiness. (See below.)\nPronouns usually substitute for nouns and function as nouns: I, you, he, she, it, we, they, myself, this,\nthat, who, which, everyone. (See p. 237.)\nVerbs express actions, occurrences, or states of being: run, bunt, inflate, become, be. (See the next page.)\nAdjectives describe or modify nouns or pronouns: gentle, small, helpful. (See p. 242.)\nAdverbs describe or modify verbs, adjectives, other adverbs, or whole groups of words: gently, helpfully,\nalmost, really, someday. (See p. 242.)\nPrepositions relate nouns or pronouns to other words in a sentence: about, at, down, for, of, with. (See p.\n245.)\nConjunctions link words, phrases, and clauses. Coordinating conjunctions and correlative\nconjunctions link words, phrases, or clauses of equal importance: and, but, or, nor; both . . . and, not\nonly . . . but also, either . . . or. (See pp. 259–60.) Subordinating conjunctions introduce\nsubordinate clauses and link them to main clauses: al-though, because, if, whenever. (See p. 253.)\nInterjections express feeling or command attention, either alone or in a sentence: hey, oh, darn, wow.\nThe five basic sentence patterns\nSubject Predicate\nCommon prepositions\nTime or space Other relationships\n(position or direction) (addition, comparison, etc.)\nabout by out of according to in spite of\n\nFOWLcp12.doc - 26\nabove down outside as instead of\nacross during over as for like\nafter for past aside from of\nagainst from since because of on account of\nalong in through concerning regarding\nalong with inside throughout despite regardless of\namong inside of till except unlike\naround into to except for with\nat near toward excepting without\nbefore next to under in addition to\nbehind off underneath\nbelow on until\nbeneath onto up\nbeside on top of upon\nbetween out within\nbeyond\nTests for finite and nonfinite verbs (verbals)\nTest 1 Does the word require a change in form when a third-person subject\nchanges from singular to plural?\nYes Finite verb: It sings. As shown in the box on\nthe next page, only I, we, he, she, they, and who change form for each case. Thus these pronouns\nare the focus of this chapter.\nThe subjective case generally indicates that the word is a subject or a subject complement.\n(See pp. 233 and 239–40.)\nsubject\nShe and Novick discussed the proposal.\nsubject\nThe proposal ignores many who need help.\nsubject complement\nThe disgruntled planners were she and Novick.\nThe objective case generally indicates that the word is the object of a verb or preposition. (See pp.\n238–41 and 245.)\nobject of verb\nThe proposal disappointed her and Novick.\nobject object\nof verb of verb\nA colleague whom they respected let them down.\nobject of\npreposition\nTheir opinion of him suffered.\nThe possessive case generally indicates ownership or source:\nHer counterproposal is in preparation.\nTheirs is the more defensible position.\nThe problem is not his.\nDo not use an apostrophe to form the possessive of personal pronouns: yours (not your’s);\ntheirs (not their’s). (See p. 465. See also p. 461 for the possessive forms of nouns, which do use\napostrophes.)\nNote Grammar and style checkers may flag some problems with pronoun case, but they will\nalso miss a lot. For instance, one checker spotted the error in We asked whom would come (should\nbe who would come), but it overlooked We dreaded them coming (should be their coming).\nIn standard American English, -self pronouns do not change form to show function. Their\nonly forms are myself, your-self, himself, herself, itself, ourselves, yourselves, themselves. Avoid\nnon-standard forms such as hisself, ourself, and theirselves.\nUse the subjective case for compound subjects\nand for subject complements.\nIn compound subjects use the same pronoun form you would use if the\npronoun stood alone as a subject:\nsubject\n\nFOWLcp13-14.doc - 2\nShe and Novick will persist.\nsubject\nThe others may lend their support when she and Novick get a hearing.\nIf you are in doubt about the correct form, try the test in the box\nbelow.\nAfter a linking verb, such as a form of be, a pronoun renaming the subject (a subject\ncomplement) should be in the subjective case:\nsubject complement\nThe ones who care most are she and Novick.\nsubject\ncomplement\nIt was they whom the mayor appointed.\nIf this construction sounds stilted to you, use the more natural order: She and Novick are the ones\nwho care most. The mayor appointed them.\nUse the objective case for compound objects.\nIn compound objects use the same pronoun form you would use if the pronoun stood alone as\nan object:\ndirect object\nThe mayor nominated Zhu and him.\nindirect object\nThe mayor gave Zhu and him awards.\nobject of preposition\nCredit goes equally to them and the mayor.\nIf you are in doubt about the correct form, try the test in the box above.\nEXERCISE 13.1 Choosing between subjective and objective pronouns\nFrom the pairs in brackets, select the appropriate subjective or objective pronoun(s) for each of the\nfollowing sentences. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\n―Between you and [I, me],‖ the seller said, ―this deal is a steal.‖\n―Between you and me,‖ the seller said, ―this deal is a steal.‖\n1. When the pronoun is subjective, it must serve as the subject of an\nomitted verb:\nsubject\nSome critics like Glass more than he [does].\nWhen the pronoun is objective, it must serve as the object of an omitted verb:\nobject\nSome critics like Glass more than [they like] him.\nUse the objective case for pronouns that are\nsubjects or objects of infinitives.\nsubject of\ninfinitive\nThe school asked him to speak.\nobject of\ninfinitive\nStudents chose to invite him.\nThe case of the pronoun who depends on its\nfunction in its clause.\nTo choose between who and whom, whoever and whomever, you need to figure out whether\nthe word serves as a subject or as an object. Use who where you would use he or she—all ending in\nvowels. Use whom where you would use him or her—all ending in consonants.\nAt the beginning of questions use who for a subject and whom for an object.\nsubject object\nWho wrote the policy? Whom does it affect?\n\nFOWLcp13-14.doc - 4\nNote In speech the subjective case who is commonly used whenever it is the first word of a\nquestion, regardless of whether it is a subject or an object. But formal writing requires a distinction\nbetween the forms:\nSpoken Who should we credit?\nobject\nWritten Whom should we credit?\nIn subordinate clauses use who and whoever for all subjects, whom and whomever for\nall objects.\nThe case of a pronoun in a subordinate clause depends on its function in the clause, regardless\nof whether the clause itself functions as a subject, an object, or a modifier:\nsubject\nCredit whoever wrote the policy.\nobject\nResearch should reveal whom to credit.\nNote Don’t let expressions such as I think and she says confuse you when they come between\nthe subject who and its verb:\nsubject\nHe is the one who the polls say will win.\nTo choose between who and whom in such constructions, delete the interrupting phrase: He is the\none who will win.\nEXERCISE 13.3 Choosing between who and whom\nFrom the pairs in brackets, select the appropriate form of the pronoun in each of the following sentences.\n(You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nMy mother asked me [who, whom] I was going out with.\nMy mother asked me whom I was going out with.\n1. Like nouns,\ngerunds are commonly preceded by possessive nouns and pronouns: our vote (noun), our voting\n(gerund).\nThe coach disapproved of their lifting weights.\nThe coach’s disapproving was a surprise.\nA noun or pronoun before an -ing verb form is not always possessive. Sometimes the -ing\nform will be a present participle modifying the preceding word:\nEveryone had noticed him weightlifting. [Emphasis on him.]\nobjective participle\npronoun\nEveryone had noticed his weightlifting. [Emphasis on the activity.]\npossessive gerund\npronoun\nNote that a gerund usually is not preceded by the possessive when the possessive would create\nan awkward construction:\nAwkward A rumor spread about everybody’s on the team want- ing to quit.\nLess awkward A rumor spread about everybody on the team wanting to quit.\nBetter A rumor spread that everybody on the team wanted to quit.\nEXERCISE 13.5 Revising: Case\nRevise all inappropriate case forms in the following paragraph, and explain the function of each case\nform. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nWritten four thousand years ago, The Epic of Gilgamesh tells of the friendship of Gilgamesh and Enkidu.\nGilgamesh was a bored king who his people thought was too harsh. Then he met Enkidu, a wild man\nwhom had lived with the animals in the mountains. Immediately, him and Gilgamesh wrestled to see\nwhom was more powerful. After hours of struggle, Enkidu admitted that Gilgamesh was stronger than\nhim. Now the friends needed adventures worthy of the two strongest men on earth. Gilgamesh said,\n―Between you and I, mighty deeds will be accomplished, and our fame will be everlasting.‖ Among their\nacts, Enkidu and him defeated a giant bull, Humbaba, and cut down the bull’s cedar forests. Them\nbringing back cedar logs to Gilgamesh’s treeless land won great praise from the people. When Enkidu\ndied, Gilgamesh mourned his death, realizing that no one had been a better friend than him. When\nGilgamesh himself died many years later, his people raised a monument praising Enkidu and he for their\nfriendship and their mighty deeds of courage.\nNote See page 331 for an exercise involving case along with other aspects of grammar.\n14\nCHAPTER\nVerbs\n\nFOWLcp13-14.doc - 6\nThe verb is the most complicated part of speech in English, changing form to express a wide\nrange of information.\nVerb Forms\nAll verbs except be have five basic forms. The first three are the verb’s principal parts.\nThe plain form is the dictionary form of the verb. When the subject is a plural noun or the\npronoun I, we, you, or they, the plain form indicates action that occurs in the present, occurs\nhabitually, or is generally true.\nA few artists live in town today.\nThey hold classes downtown.\nThe past-tense form indicates that the action of the verb occurred before now. It usually\nadds -d or -ed to the plain form, although some irregular verbs form it in other ways (see p.\n278).\nMany artists lived in town before this year.\nThey held classes downtown. [Irregular verb.]\nThe past participle is the same as the past-tense form, except in most irregular verbs. It\ncombines with forms of have or be (has climbed, was created), or by itself it modifies nouns\nand pronouns (the sliced apples).\nArtists have lived in town for decades.\nThey have held classes downtown. [Irregular verb.]\nThe present participle adds -ing to the verb’s plain form. It combines with forms of be (is\nbuying), modifies nouns and pronouns (the boiling water), or functions as a noun (Running\nexhausts me).\nA few artists are living in town today.\nThey are holding classes downtown.\nThe -s form ends in -s or -es. When the subject is a singular\nnoun, a pronoun such as everyone, or the personal pronoun he, she, or it, the -s form indicates\naction that occurs in the present, occurs habitually, or is generally true.\nThe artist lives in town today.\nShe holds classes downtown.\nThe verb be has eight forms rather than the five forms of most other verbs:\nPlain form be\nPresent participle being\nPast participle been\nI he, she, it we, you, they\nPresent tense am is are\nPast tense was was were\nIf standard American English is not your native language or dialect, you may have difficulty\nwith verbs’ -s forms (including those for be: is, was) or with the forms that indicate time (such as\nthe past-tense form). See pages 282–83 and 295–96, respectively, for more on these forms.\nHelping verbs\nHelping verbs, also called auxiliary verbs, combine with some verb forms to indicate time\nand other kinds of meaning, as in can run, was sleeping, had been eaten. These combinations are\nverb phrases. Since the plain form, present participle, or past participle in any verb phrase always\ncarries the principal meaning, it is sometimes called the main verb.\n\nFOWLcp13-14.doc - 7\nVerb phrase\nHelping Main\nArtists can train others to draw.\nThe techniques have changed little.\nThese are the most common helping verbs:\nbe able to had better must used to\nbe supposed to have to ought to will\ncan may shall would\ncould might should\nForms of be: be, am, is, are, was, were, been, being\nForms of have: have, has, had, having\nForms of do: do, does, did\nThe helping verbs of standard American English may be problematic if you are used to\nspeaking another language or dialect. See pages 283–87 for more on helping verbs.\nUse the correct forms of regular and irregular\nverbs.\nMost verbs are regular; that is, they form their past tense and past participle by adding -d or -\ned to the plain form.\nPlain form Past tense Past participle\nlive lived lived\nact acted acted\nSince the past tense and past participle are created in the same way, the forms of regular verbs do\nnot often cause problems in speech and writing (but see p. 282).\nAbout two hundred English verbs are irregular; that is, they form their past tense and past\nparticiple in some irregular way.\nPlain form Past tense Past participle\nbegin began begun\nbreak broke broken\nsleep slept slept\nYou can see the difference between a regular and an irregular verb in these examples:\nPlain form Today the birds twitter.\nToday the birds sing.\nPast tense Yesterday the birds twittered.\nYesterday the birds sang.\nPast participle In the past the birds have twittered.\nIn the past the birds have sung.\nCheck a dictionary under the plain form if you have any doubt about a verb’s principal parts.\nIf no other forms are listed, the verb is regular: both the past tense and the past participle add -d or\n-ed to the plain form. If the verb is irregular, the dictionary will list the plain form, the past tense,\nand the past participle in that order (go, went, gone). If the dictionary gives only two forms (as in\nthink, thought), then the past tense and the past participle are the same.\nSome English dialects use distinctive verb forms that differ from those of standard American\nEnglish: for instance, drug for dragged, growed for grew, come for came, or went for gone. In\nsituations requiring standard American English, use the forms in the list opposite or in a dictionary.\nNote A grammar and style checker may flag incorrect forms of irregular verbs, but it may also\nfail to do so. For example, a checker flagged The runner stealed second base (stole is correct) but\nnot The runner had steal second base (stolen is correct). When in doubt about the forms of\nirregular verbs, refer to the preceding list, consult a dictionary, or consult the links at this book’s\ncompanion Web site (ablongman.com/littlebrown).\n\nFOWLcp13-14.doc - 8\nEXERCISE 14.1 Using irregular verbs\nFor each irregular verb in brackets, give either the past tense or the past\nparticiple, as appropriate, and identify the form you used. (You can do this\nexercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nThough we had [hide] the cash box, it was [steal].\nThough we had hidden the cash box, it was stolen. [Two past\nparticiples.]\n1. The world population has [grow] by two-thirds of a billion people in less than a decade.\n2. In 2000 it [break] the 6 billion mark.\n3. Experts have [draw] pictures of a crowded future.\n4. They predict that the world population may have [slide] up to as much as 10 billion by the year 2050.\n5. Though the food supply [rise] in the last decade, the share to each person [fall].\nDistinguish between sit and set, lie and lay, and\nrise and raise.\nThe forms of sit and set, lie and lay, and rise and raise are easy to confuse:\nPlain form Past tense Past participle\nsit sat sat\nset set set\nlie lay lain\nlay laid laid\nrise rose risen\nraise raised raised\nIn each of these confusing pairs, one verb is intransitive (it does not take an object) and one is\ntransitive (it does take an object). (See pp. 238–41 for more on this distinction.)\nIntransitive\nThe patients lie in their beds. [Lie means ―recline‖ and takes no ob-ject.]\nVisitors sit with them. [Sit means ―be seated‖ or ―be located‖ and takes no object.]\nPatients’ temperatures rise. [Rise means ―increase‖ or ―get up‖ and takes no object.]\nTransitive\nOrderlies lay the dinner trays on tables. [Lay means ―place‖ and takes an object, here trays.]\nOrderlies set the trays down. [Set means ―place‖ and takes an object, here trays.]\nNursing aides raise the shades. [Raise means ―lift‖ or ―bring up‖ and takes an object, here shades.]\nEXERCISE 14.2 DISTINGUISHING SIT/SET, LIE/LAY,\nRISE/RAISE\nChoose the correct verb from the pair given in brackets. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nAfter I washed all the windows, I [lie, lay] down the squeegee and then I [sit, set] the table.\nAfter I washed all the windows, I laid down the squeegee and then I set the table.\n1. She has [rise, raise] at dawn most mornings.\n5. But it will miss many omissions, too.\nRequired -s ending\nUse the -s form of a verb when both of these situations hold:\nThe subject is a singular noun (boy), an indefinite pronoun (everyone), or he, she, or it.\nThese subjects are third person, used when someone or something is being spoken about.\nThe verb’s action occurs in the present.\nThe letter asks [not ask] for a quick response.\nDelay costs [not cost] money.\nBe especially careful with the -s forms of be (is), have (has), and do (does, doesn’t). These forms\nshould always be used to indicate pres-ent time with third-person singular subjects.\nThe company is [not be] late in responding.\nIt has [not have] problems.\nIt doesn’t [not don’t] have the needed data.\nThe contract does [not do] depend on the response.\nIn addition, be has an -s form in the past tense with I and with third-person singular subjects:\nThe company was [not were] in trouble before.\nI, you, and plural subjects do not take the -s form of verbs:\nI am [not is] a student.\nYou are [not is] also a student.\nThey are [not is] students, too.\nRequired -ed or -d ending\nThe -ed or -d verb form is required in any of these situations:\nThe verb’s action occurred in the past:\nYesterday the company asked [not ask] for more time.\nThe verb form functions as a modifier:\nThe data concerned [not concern] should be retrievable.\nThe verb form combines with a form of be or have:\nThe company is supposed [not suppose] to be the best.\nIt has developed [not develop] an excellent reputation.\nWatch especially for a needed -ed or -d ending when it isn’t pronounced clearly in speech, as in\nasked, discussed, mixed, supposed, walked, and used.\nEXERCISE 14.3 Using -s and -ed verb endings\nSupply the correct form of each verb in brackets.
