A checker flagged Many been fortunate and She\nworking but overlooked other examples on the following pages, such as The conference will be\noccurred.\nUse helping verbs when they are required.\nStandard American English requires helping verbs in certain situations:\nThe main verb ends in -ing:\nResearchers are conducting fieldwork all over the world. [Not Re-searchers conducting. . . .]\nThe main verb is been or be:\nMany have been fortunate in their discoveries. [Not Many been. . . .]\nSome could be real-life Indiana Joneses. [Not Some be. . . .]\nThe main verb is a past participle, such as talked, begun, or thrown.\nTheir discoveries were covered in newspapers and magazines. [Not Their discoveries covered. . . .]\nOften the researchers have done TV interviews. [Not the researchers done. . . .]\nIn every example above, omitting the helping verb would create an incomplete sentence, or\nsentence fragment (see Chapter 17). In a complete sentence, some part of the verb (helping or\nmain) must be capable of changing form to show changes in time: I run, I ran; you are running,\nyou were running (see p. 335). But a present participle (conducting), an irregular past participle\n(been), and the infinitive be cannot change form in this way. They need helping verbs to work as\nsentence verbs.\nCombine helping verbs and main verbs appropriately\nfor your meaning.\nHelping verbs and main verbs combine into verb phrases in specific ways.\nNote The main verb in a verb phrase (the one carrying the main meaning) does not change to\nshow a change in subject or time: she has sung, you had sung. Only the helping verb may change.\nForm of be + present participle\nThe progressive tenses indicate action in progress (see p. 295). Create them with be, am, is,\nare, was, were, or been followed by the main verb’s present participle:\nShe is working on a new book.\nBe and been always require additional helping verbs to form the progressive tenses:\ncan might should have\ncould must will be working has been working\nmay shall would had\nWhen forming the progressive tenses, be sure to use the -ing form of the main verb:\nFaulty Her ideas are grow more complex. She is developed a new approach to ethics.\nRevised Her ideas are growing more complex. She is developing a new approach to ethics.\nForm of be + past participle\nThe passive voice of the verb indicates that the subject receives the action of the verb (see p.\n302). Create the passive voice with\nbe, am, is, are, was, were, being, or been followed by the main verb’s past participle:\n\nFOWLcp13-14.doc - 11\nHer latest book was completed in four months.\nBe, being, and been always require additional helping verbs to form the passive voice:\nhave am was\nhas been completed is were being completed\nhad are\nwill be completed\nBe sure to use the main verb’s past participle for the passive voice:\nFaulty Her next book will be publish soon.\nRevised Her next book will be published soon.\nNote Only transitive verbs may form the passive voice:\nFaulty A philosophy conference will be occurred in the same week.\n[Occur is not a transitive verb.]\nRevised A philosophy conference will occur in the same week.\nSee pages 302–03 for advice on when to use and when to avoid the passive voice.\nForms of have\nFour forms of have serve as helping verbs: have, has, had, having. One of these forms plus the\nmain verb’s past participle creates one of the perfect tenses, those expressing action completed\nbefore another specific time or action (see p. 294):\nSome students have complained about the laboratory.\nOthers had complained before.\nWill and other helping verbs sometimes accompany forms of have in the perfect tenses:\nSeveral more students will have complained by the end of the week.\nForms of do\nDo, does, and did have three uses as helping verbs, always with the plain form of the main\nverb:\nTo pose a question: How did the trial end?\nTo emphasize the main verb: It did end eventually.\nTo negate the main verb, along with not or never: The judge did not withdraw.\nBe sure to use the main verb’s plain form with any form of do:\nFaulty The judge did remained in court.\nRevised The judge did remain in court.\nModals\nThe modal helping verbs include can, could, may, and might, along with several two- and\nthree-word combinations, such as have to and be able to. (See p. 277 for a list of helping verbs.)\nUse the plain form of the main verb with a modal unless the modal combines with another helping\nverb (usually have):\nFaulty The equipment can detects small vibrations. It should have\ndetect the change.\nRevised The equipment can detect small vibrations. It should have\ndetected the change.\nModals convey various meanings, with these being most common:\nAbility: can, could, be able to\nThe equipment can detect small vibrations. [Present.]\n\nFOWLcp13-14.doc - 12\nThe equipment could detect small vibrations. [Past.]\nThe equipment is able to detect small vibrations. [Present. Past: was able to. Future: will be able to.]\nPossibility: could, may, might, could/may/might have + past participle\nThe equipment could fail. [Present.]\nThe equipment may fail. [Present or future.]\nThe equipment might fail. [Present or future.]\nThe equipment may have failed. [Past.]\nNecessity or obligation: must, have to, be supposed to\nThe lab must purchase a backup. [Present or future.]\nThe lab has to purchase a backup. [Present or future. Past: had to.]\nThe lab will have to purchase a backup. [Future.]\nThe lab is supposed to purchase a backup. [Present. Past: was supposed to.]\nPermission: may, can, could\nThe lab may spend the money. [Present or future.]\nThe lab can spend the money. [Present or future.]\nThe lab could spend the money. [Present or future, more tentative.]\nWith budget approval, the lab could have spent the money. [Past.]\nIntention: will, shall, would\nThe lab will spend the money. [Future.]\nShall we offer advice? [Future. Use shall for questions requesting opinion or consent.]\nWe would have offered advice. [Past.]\nRequest: could, can, would\nCould [or can or would] you please obtain a bid? [Present or future.]\nAdvisability: should, had better, ought to, should have + past par-ticiple\nYou should obtain three bids. [Present or future.]\nYou had better obtain three bids. [Present or future.]\nYou ought to obtain three bids. [Present or future.]\nYou should have obtained three bids. [Past.]\nPast habit: would, used to\nIn years past we would obtain five bids.\nWe used to obtain five bids.\nEXERCISE 14.4 Using helping verbs\nAdd helping verbs in the following sentences where they are needed for standard English. A gerund is the -ing form of a verb used as a noun (opening). An infinitive is the plain form\nof a verb preceded by to (to open). (See pp. 248–49 for more on these forms.)\nGerunds and infinitives may follow certain verbs but not others. And sometimes the use of a\ngerund or infinitive with the same verb changes the meaning.\nNote A grammar and style checker will spot some but not all errors in matching gerunds or\ninfinitives with verbs. For example, a checker failed to flag I practice to swim and I promise\nhelping out. Use the lists given here and a dictionary of English as a second language to determine\nwhether an infinitive or a gerund is appropriate. (See p. 537 for a list of ESL dictionaries.)\nEither gerund or infinitive\nA gerund or an infinitive may follow these verbs with no significant difference in meaning:\nbegin hate love\ncan’t bear hesitate prefer\ncan’t stand intend start\ncontinue like\nThe pump began working.\nThe pump began to work.\nMeaning change with gerund or infinitive\nWith four verbs, a gerund has quite a different meaning from an infinitive:\nforget stop\nremember try\nThe engineer stopped eating. [He no longer ate.]\nThe engineer stopped to eat. [He stopped in order to eat.]\nGerund, not infinitive\nDo not use an infinitive after these verbs:\nadmit discuss mind recollect\nadore dislike miss resent\nappreciate enjoy postpone resist\navoid escape practice risk\nconsider finish put off suggest\ndeny imagine quit tolerate\ndetest keep recall understand\nFaulty He finished to eat lunch.\n\nFOWLcp13-14.doc - 14\nRevised He finished eating lunch.\nInfinitive, not gerund\nDo not use a gerund after these verbs:\nagree decide mean refuse\nappear expect offer say\nask have plan wait\nassent hope pretend want\nbeg manage promise wish\nclaim\nFaulty He decided checking the pump.\nRevised He decided to check the pump.\nNoun or pronoun + infinitive\nSome verbs may be followed by an infinitive alone or by a noun or pronoun and an infinitive.\nThe presence of a noun or pronoun changes the meaning.\nask dare need wish\nbeg expect promise would like\nchoose help want\nHe expected to watch.\nHe expected his workers to watch.\nSome verbs must be followed by a noun or pronoun before an infinitive:\nadmonish encourage oblige require\nadvise forbid order teach\nallow force permit tell\ncause hire persuade train\nchallenge instruct remind urge\ncommand invite request warn\nconvince\nHe instructed his workers to watch.\nDo not use to before the infinitive when it follows one of these verbs and a noun or pronoun:\nfeel make (―force‖)\nhave see\nhear watch\nlet\nHe let his workers learn by observation.\nEXERCISE 14.6 REVISING: VERBS PLUS GERUNDS OR\nINFINITIVES\nRevise the following sentences so that gerunds or infinitives are used\ncorrectly with verbs. For example:\nLook up the answer. [Research the answer.]\nLook over the answer. [Examine the answer.]\nThe meanings of these two-word verbs are often quite different from the meanings of the individual\nwords that make them up. (There are some three-word verbs, too, such as put up with and run out\nof.)\nA dictionary of English as a second language will define two-word verbs and say whether the\nverb may be separated in a sentence, as explained below. (See p. 537 for a list of ESL dictionaries.)\nA grammar and style checker will recognize few if any misuses of two-word verbs.\nNote Many two-word verbs are more common in speech than in more formal academic or\nbusiness writing. For formal writing, consider using research instead of look up, examine or inspect\nin-stead of look over.\nInseparable two-word verbs\nVerbs and particles that may not be separated by any other words include the following:\ncall on go out with run across stay away\ncatch on go over run into stay up\ncome across grow up run out of take care of\nget along keep on speak up turn out\nget up look for speak with turn up at\ngive in look into stand up work for\ngo on play around\nFaulty Children grow quickly up.\nRevised Children grow up quickly.\nSeparable two-word verbs\nMost two-word verbs that take direct objects may be separated by the object:\nParents help out their children.\nParents help their children out.\nIf the direct object is a pronoun, the pronoun must separate the verb from the particle:\nFaulty Parents help out them.\nRevised Parents help them out.\nThe separable two-word verbs include the following:\nbring up give back make up throw out\ncall off hand in point out try on\ncall up hand out put away try out\ndrop off help out put back turn down\nfill out leave out put off turn on\nfill up look over take out wrap up\ngive away look up take over\nEXERCISE 14.7 Revising: Verbs plus particles\nThe two- and three-word verbs in the sentences below are underlined.\nSome are correct as given, and some are not because they should or\nshould not be separated by other words. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\n\nFOWLcp13-14.doc - 16\nExample:\nHollywood producers never seem to come up with entirely new plots, but they also never run new\nways out of to present old ones.\nHollywood producers never seem to come up with [correct] entirely new plots, but they also never\nrun out of new ways to present old ones.\n1. American movies treat everything from going out with someone to making up an ethnic identity, but\nfew people look their significance into.\n2. While some viewers stay away from topical films, others turn at the theater up simply because a\nmovie has sparked debate.\n3. Some movies attracted rowdy spectators, and the theaters had to throw out them.\n4. Filmmakers have always been eager to point their influence out to the public.\n5. Everyone agrees that filmmakers will keep creating controversy on, if only because it can fill up\ntheaters.\nTense\nTense shows the time of a verb’s action. The table on the facing page defines and illustrates\nthe tense forms for a regular verb in the active voice. (See pp. 278 and 302 on regular verbs and\nvoice.)\nNote Grammar and style checkers can provide little help with incorrect verb tenses and tense\nsequences because correctness usually depends on meaning. You’ll have to proofread carefully\nyourself to catch errors in tense or tense sequence.\nIn standard American English, a verb conveys time and sequence through its form. In some\nother languages and English dialects, various markers besides verb form may indicate the time of a\nverb. For instance, in African American dialect I be attending class on Friday means that the\nspeaker attends class every Friday. To a speaker of standard American English, however, the\nsentence may be unclear: last Friday? this Friday? every Friday? The intended meaning must be\nindicated by verb tense: I attended class on Friday. I will attend class on Friday. I attend class on\nFriday.\nUse the appropriate tense to express your meaning.\nMany errors in verb tense are actually errors in verb form like those discussed earlier. Still, the\npresent tense, the perfect tenses, and the progressive tenses can cause problems.\nObserve the special uses of the present tense.\nMost academic and business writing uses the past tense (the rebellion occurred), but the\npresent tense has several distinctive uses:\nAction occurring now\nShe understands the problem.\nWe define the problem differently.\nHabitual or recurring action\nBanks regularly undergo audits.\nThe audits monitor the banks’ activities.\nA general truth\nThe mills of the gods grind slowly.\nThe earth is round.\nDiscussion of literature, film, and so on (see also p. 744)\nHuckleberry Finn has adventures we all envy.\nIn that article, the author examines several causes of crime.\nFuture time\nNext week we draft a new budget.\n\nFOWLcp13-14.doc - 17\nFunding ends in less than a year.\n(In the last two examples, time is really indicated by Next week and in less than a year.)\nObserve the uses of the perfect tenses.\nThe perfect tenses generally indicate action completed before another specific time or action.\n(The term perfect derives from the Latin perfectus, ―completed.‖) The present perfect tense also\nindicates action begun in the past and continued into the present. The perfect tenses consist of a\nform of have plus the verb’s past participle.\npresent perfect\nThe dancer has performed here only once. [The action is completed at the time of the statement.]\npresent perfect\nCritics have written about the performance ever since. [The action began in the past and continues now.]\npast perfect\nThe dancer had trained in Asia before his performance. [The action was completed before another past\naction.]\nfuture perfect\nHe will have performed here again by next month. [The action begins now or in the future and will be\ncompleted by a specified time in the future.]\nWith the present perfect tense, the words since and for are followed by different information.\nAfter since, give a specific point in time: The United States has been a member of the United\nNations since 1945. After for, give a span of time: The United States has been a member of the\nUnited Nations for many decades.\nObserve the uses of the progressive tenses.\nThe progressive tenses indicate continuing (therefore progressive) action.\nIn standard American English the progressive tenses consist of a form of\nbe plus the verb’s -ing form (present participle). (The words be and been\nmust be combined with other helping verbs. See pp. 284–85.)\npresent progressive\nThe economy is improving.\npast progressive\nLast year the economy was stagnating.\nfuture progressive\nEconomists will be watching for signs of growth.\npresent perfect progressive\nThe government has been expecting an upturn.\npast perfect progressive\nVarious indicators had been suggesting improvement.\nfuture perfect progressive\nBy the end of this month, investors will have been pushing the mar-kets up for half a year.\nNote Verbs that express unchanging states (especially mental states) rather than physical\nactions do not usually appear in the progressive tenses. These verbs include adore, appear, believe,\nbelong, care, doubt, hate, have, hear, imagine, know, like, love, mean, need, own, prefer, realize,\nremember, see, sound, taste, think, understand, and want.\nFaulty She is wanting to study ethics.\nRevised She wants to study ethics.\nUse the appropriate sequence of verb tenses.\nThe term sequence of tenses refers to the relation between the verb tense in a main clause and\nthe verb tense in a subordinate clause or phrase. The tenses should change when necessary to\nreflect changes in actual or relative time. (For a discussion of tense shifts—changes not required by\nmeaning—see pp. 359–60.)\n\nFOWLcp13-14.doc - 18\nUse the appropriate tense sequence with infinitives.\nThe present infinitive is the verb’s plain form preceded by to. It indicates action at the same\ntime as or later than that of the verb:\nverb: infinitive:\npresent perfect present\nShe would have liked to see [not to have seen] change before now.\nThe verb’s perfect infinitive consists of to have followed by the past\nparticiple, as in to have talked, to have won. It indicates action earlier than\nthat of the verb:\nverb: infinitive:\npresent perfect\nOther researchers would like [not would have liked] to have seen change as well.\nUse the appropriate tense sequence with participles.\nThe present participle shows action occurring at the same time as that of the verb:\nparticiple: verb:\npresent past perfect\nTesting a large group, the researcher had posed multiple-choice questions.\nThe past participle and the present perfect participle show action occurring earlier than that of the\nverb:\nparticiple: verb:\npast past\nPrepared by earlier failures, she knew not to ask open questions.\nparticiple: verb:\npresent perfect past\nHaving tested many people, she understood the process.\nUse the appropriate tense sequence with the past or\npast perfect tense.\nWhen the verb in the main clause is in the past or past perfect tense, the verb in the\nsubordinate clause must also be past or past perfect:\nmain clause: subordinate clause:\npast past\nThe researchers discovered that people varied widely in their knowledge of public events.\nmain clause: subordinate clause:\npast past perfect\nThe variation occurred because respondents had been born in different decades.\nmain clause: subordinate clause:\npast perfect past\nNone of them had been born when Warren G. Harding was President.\nException Always use the present tense for a general truth, such as The earth is round:\nmain clause: subordinate clause:\npast present\nMost understood that popular Presidents are not necessarily good Presidents.\nUse the appropriate tense sequence in conditional sentences.\nA conditional sentence states a factual relation between cause and effect, makes a prediction,\nor speculates about what might happen. Such a sentence usually contains a subordinate clause\nbeginning with if, when, or unless along with a main clause stating the result. The three kinds of\nconditional sentences use distinctive verbs.\nFactual relation\nStatements linking factual causes and effects use matched tenses in the subordinate and main\nclauses:\n\nFOWLcp13-14.doc - 19\nsubordinate clause: main clause:\npresent present\nWhen a voter casts a ballot, he or she has complete privacy.\nsubordinate clause: main clause:\npast past\nWhen voters registered in some states, they had to pay a poll tax.\nPrediction\nPredictions generally use the present tense in the subordinate clause and the future tense in the\nmain clause:\nsubordinate clause: main clause:\npresent future\nUnless citizens regain faith in politics, they will not vote.\nSometimes the verb in the main clause consists of may, can, should, or might plus the verb’s plain\nform: If citizens regain faith, they may vote.\nSpeculation\nThe verbs in speculations depend on whether the linked events are possible or impossible. For\npossible events in the present, use the past tense in the subordinate clause and would, could, or\nmight plus the verb’s plain form in the main clause:\nsubordinate clause: main clause:\npast would + verb\nIf voters had more confidence, they would vote more often.\nAlways use were in the subordinate clause, even when the subject is I, he, she, it or a singular\nnoun. (See p. 300 for more on this distinctive verb form.)\nsubordinate clause: main clause:\npast would + verb\nIf the voter were more confident, he or she would vote more often.\nFor impossible events in the present—events that are contrary to fact—use\nthe same forms as above (including the distinctive were when applicable):\nsubordinate clause: main clause:\npast might + verb\nIf Lincoln were alive, he might inspire confidence.\nFor impossible events in the past, use the past perfect tense in the subordinate clause and would,\ncould, or might plus the present perfect tense in the main clause:\nsubordinate clause: main clause:\npast perfect might + present perfect\nIf Lincoln had lived past the Civil War, he might have helped stabilize the country.\nEXERCISE 14.8 Adjusting tense sequence: Past or past perfect tense\nThe tenses in each sentence below are in correct sequence. Change the tense of one verb as instructed.\nThen change the tense of infinitives, participles, and other verbs to restore correct sequence. Some items\nhave more than one possible answer. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nDelgado will call when he reaches his destination. (Change will call to called.)\nDelgado called when he reached [or had reached] his destination.\n1. Diaries that Adolf Hitler is supposed to have written have surfaced in Germany. (Change have\nsurfaced to had surfaced.)\n2. Many people believe that the diaries are authentic because a well-known historian has declared them\nso. (Change believe to be-lieved.)\n3. However, the historian’s evaluation has been questioned by other authorities, who call the diaries\nforgeries. (Change has been questioned to was questioned.)\n4. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nIf Babe Ruth or Jim Thorpe [be] athletes today, they [remind] us that even sports heroes must\ncontend with a harsh reality.\nIf Babe Ruth or Jim Thorpe were athletes today, they might [or could or would] remind us that even\nsports heroes must contend with a harsh reality.\n1. When an athlete [turn] professional, he or she [commit] to a gruel-ing regimen of mental and\nphysical training.\n2. If athletes [be] less committed, they [disappoint] teammates, fans, and themselves.\n3. If professional athletes [be] very lucky, they [play] until age forty.\n4. Unless an athlete [achieve] celebrity status, he or she [have] few employment choices after\nretirement.\n5. If professional sports [be] less risky, athletes [have] longer careers and more choices after retirement.\nMood\nMood in grammar is a verb form that indicates the writer’s or speaker’s attitude toward what\nhe or she is saying. The indicative mood states a fact or opinion or asks a question:\nThe theater needs help. [Opinion.]\nThe ceiling is falling in. [Fact.]\nWill you contribute to the theater? [Question.]\nThe imperative mood expresses a command or gives a direction. It omits the subject of the\nsentence, you:\nHelp the theater. [Command.]\nSend contributions to the theater. [Direction.]\nThe subjunctive mood expresses a suggestion, a requirement, or a desire, or it states a condition\nthat is contrary to fact (that is, imaginary or hypothetical). The subjunctive mood uses distinctive\nverb forms.\nSuggestion or requirement: plain form with all subjects.\nThe manager asked that he donate money. [Suggestion.]\nRules require that every donation be mailed. [Requirement.]\nDesire or present condition contrary to fact: past tense; for be, the past tense were for all\nsubjects, singular as well as plural.\nWe wish that the theater had more money. [Desire.]\nIt would be in better shape if it were better funded. [Present condition contrary to fact.]\nPast condition contrary to fact: past perfect.\nThe theater could have been better funded if it had been better managed.\nNote that with conditions contrary to fact, the verb in the main clause also expresses the imaginary\nor hypothetical with the helping verb could, would, or might. (See also p. 298.)\nFor a discussion of keeping mood consistent within and among sentences, see page 360.\n\nFOWLcp13-14.doc - 21\nUse the subjunctive verb forms appropriately.\nContemporary English uses distinctive subjunctive verb forms in only a few constructions and\nidioms. (For the sequence of tenses in many subjunctive sentences, see pp. 297–98.)\nNote A grammar and style checker may spot some simple errors in the subjunctive mood, but\nit may miss others. For example, a checker flagged I wish I was home (should be were home) but\nnot If I had a hammer, I will hammer in the morning (should be would hammer).\nUse the subjunctive in contrary-to-fact clauses beginning with if or expressing desire.\nIf the theater were saved, the town would benefit.\nWe all wish the theater were not so decrepit.\nI wish I were able to donate money.\nNote The indicative form was (We all wish the theater was not so decrepit) is common in\nspeech and in some informal writing, but the subjunctive were is usual in formal English.\nNot all clauses beginning with if express conditions contrary to fact. In the sentence If Joe is\nout of town, he hasn’t heard the news, the verb is is correct because the clause refers to a condition\npresumed to exist.\nUse would, could, or might only in the main clause of a conditional statement.\nThe helping verb would, could, or might appears in the main clause of a sentence expressing a\ncondition contrary to fact. The helping verb does not appear in the subordinate clause beginning\nwith if:\nNot Many people would have helped if they would have known.\nBut Many people would have helped if they had known.\nUse the subjunctive in that clauses following verbs that demand, request, or recommend.\nVerbs such as ask, demand, insist, mandate, recommend, request, require, suggest, and urge\nindicate demand or suggestion. They often precede subordinate clauses beginning with that and\ncontaining the substance of the demand or suggestion. The verb in such a that clause should be in\nthe subjunctive mood:\nThe board urged that everyone contribute.\nThe members insisted that they themselves be donors.\nThey suggested that each donate both time and money.\nNote These constructions have widely used alternative forms that do not require the\nsubjunctive, such as The board urged everyone to contribute or The members insisted on donating.\nEXERCISE 14.10 Revising: Subjunctive mood\nRevise the following sentences with appropriate subjunctive verb forms. (You can do this exercise online\nat ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nI would help the old man if I was able to reach him.\nI would help the old man if I were able to reach him.\n1. If John Hawkins would have known of the dangerous side effects of smoking tobacco, would he have\nintroduced the dried plant to England in 1565?\n2. Hawkins noted that if a Florida Indian was to travel for several days, he would have smoked tobacco\nto satisfy his hunger and thirst.\n3. Early tobacco growers feared that their product would not gain acceptance unless it was perceived as\nhealthful.\n4. To prevent fires, in 1646 the General Court of Massachusetts passed a law requiring that colonists\nsmoked tobacco only if they were five miles from any town.\n5. To prevent decadence, in 1647 Connecticut passed a law mandating that one’s smoking of tobacco\nwas limited to once a day in one’s own home.\n\nFOWLcp13-14.doc - 22\nVoice\nThe voice of a verb tells whether the subject of the sentence performs the action (active voice)\nor is acted upon (passive voice). In the passive voice, the actual actor may be named in a\nprepositional phrase (such as by the city) or may be omitted.\nA passive verb always consists of a form of be plus the past participle of the main verb: rents\nare controlled. Other helping verbs must also be used with be, being, and been: rents have been\ncontrolled. Only a transitive verb (one that takes an object) may be used in the passive voice. (See\np. 285.)\nConverting active to passive\nTo change a transitive verb from active to passive voice, convert either an indirect object or a\ndirect object into the subject of the sentence, and use the passive verb form:\ntransitive indirect direct\nsubject verb object object\nActive The city gives tenants leases.\nnew passive direct\nsubject verb object\nPassive Tenants are given leases.\nnew passive indirect old\nsubject verb object subject\nLeases are given tenants by the city.\nConverting passive to active\nTo change a passive verb to active, name the verb’s actor as the subject, use an active verb\nform, and convert the old subject into an object:\nsubject passive verb\nPassive Tenants are protected by leases.\nnew active old subject\nsubject verb = object\nActive Leases protect tenants.\nGenerally, prefer the active voice. Use the passive\nvoice when the actor is unknown or unimportant.\nBecause the passive omits or de-emphasizes the actor (the performer of the verb’s action), it\ncan deprive writing of vigor and is often vague or confusing. The active voice is usually stronger,\nclearer, and more forthright.\nWeak The Internet is used for research by many scholars, and its ex-\npassive pansion to the general public has been criticized by some.\nStrong Many scholars use the Internet for research, and some have\nactive criticized its expansion to the general public.\nThe passive voice is useful in two situations: when the actor is unknown and when the actor is\nunimportant or less important than the object of the action.\nThe Internet was established in 1969 by the US Department of Defense. The network has now been\nextended internationally to governments, foundations, corporations, educational institutions, and private\nindividuals. [In the first sentence the writer wishes to stress the Internet rather than the Department of\nDefense. In the second sentence the actor is unknown or too complicated to name.]\nAfter the solution had been cooled to 10°C, the acid was added. [The person who cooled and added,\nperhaps the writer, is less important than the facts that the solution was cooled and acid was added.\nPassive sentences are common in scientific writing. See p. 809.]\nExcept in such situations, however, you should prefer the active voice in your writing.\nNote Most grammar and style checkers can be set to spot the passive voice. (See p. 61.) But\nthe checkers will flag appropriate passives as well, such as when the actor is unknown.\n\nFOWLcp13-14.doc - 23\nEXERCISE 14.11 Converting between active and passive voices\nTo practice using the two voices of the verb, convert the following sentences from active to passive or\nfrom passive to active. (See p. 292.)\nMood\nThe attitude of the verb’s speaker or writer—the difference, for example,\nin I kick the ball, Kick the ball, and I suggest that you kick the ball. (See\np. 299.)\nVoice\nThe distinction between the active, in which the subject performs the verb’s action (I kick the ball), and\nthe passive, in which the subject is acted upon (The ball is kicked by me). (See p. 302.)\nPerson\nThe verb form that reflects whether the subject is speaking (I/we kick the\nball), spoken to (You kick the ball), or spoken about (She kicks the ball). Subjects\nand verbs agree in number and person:\nMore Japanese Americans live in Hawaii and California than elsewhere. subject verb\nDaniel Inouye was the first Japanese American in Congress.\nsubject verb\nPronouns and their antecedents—the words they refer to—agree in person, number, and gender:\nInouye makes his home in Hawaii.\nantecedent pronoun\nHawaiians value his work for them.\nantecedent pronoun\n15a Make subjects and verbs agree in number.\nMost subject-verb agreement problems arise when endings are omitted from subjects or verbs\nor when the relation between sentence parts is uncertain.\nNote A grammar and style checker will catch many simple errors in subject-verb agreement,\nsuch as Addie and John is late, and some more complicated errors, such as Is Margaret and Tom\ngoing with us? (should be are in both cases). But a checker failed to flag The old group has gone\ntheir separate ways (should be have) and offered a wrong correction for The old group have gone\ntheir separate ways, which is already correct.\n1 The -s and -es endings work differently for nouns and verbs.\nAn -s or -es ending does opposite things to nouns and verbs: it usually makes a noun plural,\nbut it always makes a present-tense verb singular. Thus a singular noun as subject will not end in -\ns, but its verb will. A plural noun as subject will end in -s, but its verb will not. Between them,\nsubject and verb use only one -s ending.\nSingular Plural\nThe boy plays. The boys play.\nThe bird soars. The birds soar.\nThe only exceptions to these rules involve the nouns that form irregular plurals, such as\nchild/children, woman/women. The irregular plural still requires a plural verb: The children play.\nIf your first language or dialect is not standard American English, subject-verb agreement\nmay be problematic, especially for these reasons:\nSome English dialects follow different rules for subject-verb agreement, such as omitting\nthe -s ending for singular verbs or using the -s ending for plural verbs.\nNonstandard The voter resist change.\nStandard The voter resists change.\nStandard The voters resist change.\nThe verb be changes spelling for singular and plural in both present\nand past tenses. (See also p. 276.)\n\nFOWLcp15-16.doc - 2\nNonstandard Taxes is high. They was raised just last year.\nStandard Taxes are high. They were raised just last year.\nHave also has a distinctive -s form, has:\nNonstandard The new tax have little chance of passing.\nStandard The new tax has little chance of passing.\nSome other languages change all verb phrases to match their subjects, but in English only\nthe helping verbs be, have, and do change for different subjects. The modal helping verbs—\ncan, may, should, will, and others—do not change:\nNonstandard The tax mays pass next year.\nStandard The tax may pass next year.\nThe main verb in a verb phrase also does not change for different subjects:\nNonstandard The tax may passes next year.\nStandard The tax may pass next year.\n2 Subject and verb should agree even when other words\ncome between them.\nWhen the subject and verb are interrupted by other words, make sure the verb agrees with the\nsubject:\nA catalog of courses and requirements often baffles [not baffle] stu-dents.\nThe requirements stated in the catalog are [not is] unclear.\nNote Phrases beginning with as well as, together with, along with, and in addition to do not\nchange the number of the subject:\nThe president, as well as the deans, has [not have] agreed to revise the catalog.\nIf you really mean and in such a sentence, use it. Then the subject is compound, and the verb\nshould be plural: The president and the deans have agreed to revise the catalog.\n3 Subjects joined by and usually take plural verbs.\nTwo or more subjects joined by and usually take a plural verb, whether one or all of the\nsubjects are singular:\nFrost and Roethke were contemporaries.\nFrost, Roethke, Stevens, and Pound are among the great American poets.\nExceptions When the parts of the subject form a single idea or refer to a single person or thing,\nthey take a singular verb:\nAvocado and bean sprouts is a California sandwich.\nWhen a compound subject is preceded by the adjective each or every, the verb is usually singular:\nEach man, woman, and child has a right to be heard.\nBut a compound subject followed by each takes a plural verb:\nThe man and the woman each have different problems.\n4 When parts of a subject are joined by or or nor, the verb agrees with the nearer\npart.\nWhen all parts of a subject joined by or or nor are singular, the verb is singular; when all parts\nare plural, the verb is plural.\nEither the painter or the carpenter knows the cost.\nThe cabinets or the bookcases are too costly.\n\nFOWLcp15-16.doc - 3\nWhen one part of the subject is singular and the other plural, avoid awkwardness by placing\nthe plural part closer to the verb so that the verb is plural:\nAwkward Neither the owners nor the contractor agrees.\nRevised Neither the contractor nor the owners agree.\nWhen the subject consists of nouns and pronouns of different person requiring different verb\nforms, the verb agrees with the nearer part of the subject. Reword if this construction is awkward:\nAwkward Either Juarez or I am responsible.\nRevised Either Juarez is responsible, or I am.\n5 With an indefinite pronoun, use a singular or plural verb\nas appropriate.\nAn indefinite pronoun is one that does not refer to a specific person or thing. Most indefinite\npronouns take a singular verb, but some take a plural verb and some take a singular or a plural\nverb.\nThe singular indefinite pronouns refer to a single unspecified person or thing, and they take a\nsingular verb:\nSomething smells. Neither is right.\nThe plural indefinite pronouns refer to more than one unspecified thing, and they take a plural\nverb:\nBoth are correct. Several were invited.\nThe other indefinite pronouns take a singular or a plural verb depending on whether the word\nthey refer to is singular or plural. The word may be stated in the sentence:\nAll of the money is reserved for emergencies.\nAll of the funds are reserved for emergencies.\nThe word referred to by the pronoun may also be implied:\nAll are planning to attend. [All implies ―all the people.‖]\nAll is lost. [All implies ―everything.‖]\nSee page 330 for the distinction between few (―not many‖) and a few (―some‖).\n6 Collective nouns take singular or plural verbs depending\non meaning.\nA collective noun has singular form but names a group of individuals or things—for example,\narmy, audience, committee, crowd, family, group, team. As a subject, a collective noun may take a\nsingular or plural verb, depending on the context. When the group acts as one unit, use a singular\nverb:\nThe group agrees that action is necessary.\nBut when considering the group’s members as individuals who act separately, use the plural form\nof the verb:\nThe old group have gone their separate ways.\nThe collective noun number may be singular or plural. Preceded by a, it is\nplural; preceded by the, it is singular.\n\nFOWLcp15-16.doc - 4\nA number of people are in debt.\nThe number of people in debt is very large.\nIn English some noncount nouns (nouns that don’t form plurals) are collective nouns because\nthey name groups: for\ninstance, furniture, clothing, mail. These noncount nouns usually take singular verbs: Mail arrives\ndaily. But some of these nouns take plural verbs, including clergy, military, people, police, and any\ncollective noun that comes from an adjective, such as the poor, the rich, the young, the elderly. If\nyou mean one representative of the group, use a singular noun such as police officer or poor\nperson.\n7 The verb agrees with the subject even when the normal word order is inverted.\nInverted subject-verb order occurs mainly in questions and in constructions beginning with\nthere or it and a form of be:\nIs voting a right or a privilege?\nAre a right and a privilege the same thing?\nThere are differences between them.\nIn constructions beginning with there, you may use is before a compound subject when the first\nelement in the subject is singular:\nThere is much work to do and little time to do it.\nWord order may sometimes be inverted for emphasis. The verb still agrees with its subject:\nFrom the mountains comes an eerie, shimmering light.\n8 A linking verb agrees with its subject, not the subject complement.\nA linking verb such as is or are should agree with its subject, usually the first element in the\nsentence, not with the noun or pronoun serving as a subject complement (see p. 240):\nThe child’s sole support is her court-appointed guardians.\nHer court-appointed guardians are the child’s sole support.\n9 Who, which, and that take verbs that agree with their antecedents.\nWhen used as subjects, who, which, and that refer to another word in the sentence, called the\nantecedent. The verb agrees with the antecedent:\nMayor Garber ought to listen to the people who work for her.\nBardini is the only aide who has her ear.\nAgreement problems often occur with relative pronouns when the sentence includes one of the\nor the only one of the:\nBardini is one of the aides who work unpaid. [Of the aides who work unpaid, Bardini is one.]\nBardini is the only one of the aides who knows the community. [Of the aides, only one, Bardini, knows\nthe community.]\nIn phrases like those above beginning with one of the, be sure the noun is plural: Bardini is\none of the aides [not aide] who work unpaid.\n\nFOWLcp15-16.doc - 5\n10 Nouns with plural form but singular meaning\ntake singular verbs.\nSome nouns with plural form (that is, ending in -s) are usually regarded as singular in\nmeaning. They include athletics, economics, mathematics, measles, mumps, news, physics, politics,\nand statistics, as well as place names such as Athens, Wales, and United States.\nAfter so long a wait, the news has to be good.\nStatistics is required of psychology majors.\nA few of these words take plural verbs only when they describe individual items rather than whole bodies of activity or\nknowledge: The statistics prove him wrong.\nMeasurements and figures ending in -s may also be singular when the quantity they refer to is\na unit:\nThree years is a long time to wait.\nThree-fourths of the library consists of reference books.\n11 Titles and words named as words take singular verbs.\nWhen your sentence subject is the name of a corporation, the title of a work (such as a book),\nor a word you are defining or describing, the verb should be singular even if the name, title, or\nword is plural:\nHakada Associates is a new firm.\nDream Days remains a favorite book.\nFolks is a down-home word for people.\nEXERCISE 15.1 Revising: Subject-verb agreement\nRevise the verbs in the following sentences as needed to make subjects and verbs agree in number. If the\nsentence is already correct as given, circle the number preceding it. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nEach of the job applicants type sixty words per minute.\nEach of the job applicants types sixty words per minute.\n1. Weinstein & Associates are a consulting firm that try to make businesspeople laugh.\n2. Statistics from recent research suggests that humor relieves stress.\n3. Reduced stress in businesses in turn reduce illness and absenteeism.\n4. Reduced stress can also reduce friction within an employee group, which then work more\nproductively.\n5. In special conferences held by one consultant, each of the participants practice making the others\nlaugh.\n6. One consultant to many companies suggest cultivating office humor with practical jokes such as a\nrubber fish in the water cooler.\n7. When employees or their manager regularly post cartoons on the bulletin board, office spirit usually\npicks up.\n8. When someone who has seemed too easily distracted is entrusted with updating the cartoons, his or\nher concentration often im-proves.\n9. In the face of levity, the former sourpuss becomes one of those who hides bad temper.\n10. Every one of the consultants caution, however, that humor has no place in life-affecting corporate\nsituations such as employee layoffs.\n15b Make pronouns and their antecedents agree\nin person, number, and gender.\nThe antecedent of a pronoun is the noun or other pronoun to which the pronoun refers.\n\nFOWLcp15-16.doc - 6\nHomeowners fret over their tax bills.\nantecedent pronoun\nIts constant increases make the tax bill a dreaded document.\npronoun antecedent\nSince a pronoun derives its meaning from its antecedent, the two must agree in person, number, and gender.\nNote Grammar and style checkers cannot help with agreement between pronoun and\nantecedent because they cannot recognize the intended relation between the two.\nThe gender of a pronoun should match its antecedent, not a noun that the pronoun may\nmodify: Sara Young invited her [not his] son to join the company’s staff. Also, nouns in\nEnglish have only neuter gender unless they specifically refer to males or females. Thus nouns such\nas book, table, sun, and earth take the pronoun it.\n1 Antecedents joined by and usually take plural pronouns.\nTwo or more antecedents joined by and usually take a plural pronoun, whether one or all of\nthe antecedents are singular:\nMr. Bartos and I cannot settle our dispute.\nThe dean and my adviser have offered their help.\nExceptions When the compound antecedent refers to a single idea, person, or thing, then the\npronoun is singular:\nMy friend and adviser offered her help.\nWhen the compound antecedent follows each or every, the pronoun is singular:\nEvery girl and woman took her seat.\n2 When parts of an antecedent are joined by or or nor, the pronoun agrees with the\nnearer part.\nWhen the parts of an antecedent are connected by or or nor, the pronoun should agree with\nthe part closer to it:\nTenants or owners must present their grievances.\nEither the tenant or the owner will have her way.\nWhen one subject is plural and the other singular, the sentence will be awkward unless you\nput the plural one second:\nAwkward Neither the tenants nor the owner has yet made her case.\nRevised Neither the owner nor the tenants have yet made their case.\n3 With an indefinite word as antecedent, use a singular or plural pronoun as\nappropriate.\nIndefinite words do not refer to any specific person or thing. They include indefinite\npronouns such as anyone, everybody, and no one (see p. 309 for a list). They also include generic\nnouns, or singular nouns that refer to typical members of a group, as in The individual has rights or\nThe job requires a person with computer skills.\nMost indefinite pronouns and all generic nouns are singular in meaning. When they serve as\nantecedents of pronouns, the pronouns should be singular.\nEveryone on the women’s team now has her own locker.\nindefinite\npronoun\n\nFOWLcp15-16.doc - 7\nEvery person on the women’s team now has her own locker.\ngeneric\nnoun\nFive indefinite pronouns—all, any, more, most, some—may be singular or plural in meaning\ndepending on what they refer to:\nFew women athletes had changing spaces, so most had to change in\ntheir rooms.\nMost of the changing space was dismal, its color a drab olive green.\nFour indefinite pronouns—both, few, many, several—are always plural in meaning:\nFew realize how their athletic facilities have changed.\nMost agreement problems arise with the singular indefinite words. We often use these words\nto mean something like ―many‖ or ―all‖ rather than ―one‖ and then refer to them with plural\npronouns, as in Everyone has their own locker or A person can padlock their locker. Often, too, we\nmean indefinite words to include both masculine and feminine genders and thus resort to they\ninstead of the generic he—the masculine pronoun referring to both genders, as in Everyone\ndeserves his privacy. (For more on the generic he, which many readers view as sexist, see p. 516.)\nAlthough some experts accept they, them, and their with singular indefinite words, most do\nnot, and many teachers and employers regard the plural as incorrect. To be safe, work for\nagreement between singular indefinite words and the pronouns that refer to them. You have several\noptions:\n4 Collective noun antecedents take singular or plural pronouns depending on meaning.\nCollective nouns such as army, committee, family, group, and team have singular form but\nmay be referred to by singular or plural pronouns, depending on the meaning intended. When the\ngroup acts as a unit, the pronoun is singular:\nThe committee voted to disband itself.\nWhen the members of the group act separately, the pronoun is plural:\nThe old group have gone their separate ways.\nIn the last example, note that the verb and pronoun are consistent in number (see also pp.\n358–59).\nInconsistent The old group has gone their separate ways.\nConsistent The old group have gone their separate ways.\nIn standard American English, collective nouns that are noncount nouns (they don’t form\nplurals) usually take singular pronouns: The mail sits in its own basket. A few noncount nouns take\nplural pronouns, including clergy, military, people, police, the rich, and the poor: The police\nsupport their unions. (See also p. 310.)\nEXERCISE 15.2 Revising: Pronoun-antecedent agreement\nRevise the following sentences so that pronouns and their ante-\ncedents agree in person and number. Try to avoid the generic he. (You can do this exercise\nonline at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nThe writers Richard Rodriguez and Maxine Hong Kingston, despite their differences, shares one\ncharacteristic: their parents was immigrants to California. A frequent theme of their writings are the\ndifficulties of growing up with two languages and two cultures.\nA child whose first language is not English is often ridiculed because they cannot communicate\n―properly.‖ Rodriguez learned Spanish at home, but at school everyone expected him to use their\nlanguage, English. He remembers his childish embarrassment because of his parents’ poor English.\nCollege and graduate school, which usually expands one’s knowledge, widened the gap between\nRodriguez and his Latino culture. His essays suggests that he lost a part of himself, a loss that continue to\nbother him.\nKingston spoke Chinese at home and also learned her first English at school. She sometimes write\nof these experiences, but more often she write to recover and preserve her Chinese culture. The Woman\nWarrior, which offer a blend of autobiography, family history, and mythic tales, describe the struggle of\nKingston’s female relatives. China Men focus on Kingston’s male ancestors; each one traveled to Hawaii\nor California to make money for their wife back in China. Kingston’s work, like Rodriguez’s essays,\nreflect the tension and confusion that the child of immigrants often feel when they try to blend two\ncultures.\nNote See page 331 for an exercise involving agreement along with other aspects of grammar.\n\nFOWLcp15-16.doc - 9\n16\nCHAPTER\nAdjectives and Adverbs\nAdjectives and adverbs are modifiers that describe, restrict, or otherwise qualify the words to\nwhich they relate.\nMany of the most common adjectives are familiar one-syllable words such as bad, strange,\nlarge, and wrong. Many others are formed by adding endings such as -al, -able, -ful, -less, -ish, -\nive, and -y to nouns or verbs: optional, fashionable, beautiful, fruitless, selfish, expressive, dreamy.\nMost adverbs are formed by adding -ly to adjectives: badly, strangely, largely, beautifully. But\nnote that we cannot depend on -ly to identify adverbs, since some adjectives also end in -ly\n(fatherly, lonely) and since some common adverbs do not end in -ly (always, here, not, now, often,\nthere). Thus the only sure way to distinguish between adjectives and adverbs is to determine what\nthey modify.\nNote Grammar and style checkers will spot some but not all problems with misused adjectives\nand adverbs. For instance, a checker flagged Some children suffer bad and Chang was the most\nwisest person in town and Jenny did not feel nothing. But it did not flag Educating children good is\neveryone’s focus.\nIn standard American English an adjective does not change along with the noun it modifies to\nshow plural number: white [not whites] shoes, square [not squares] spaces. Only nouns form\nplurals.\n16a Use adjectives only to modify nouns and pronouns.\nAdjectives modify only nouns and pronouns. Using adjectives instead of adverbs to modify\nverbs, adverbs, or other adjectives is non-standard:\nNonstandard The groups view family values different.\nStandard The groups view family values differently.\nThe adjectives good and bad often appear where standard English requires the adverbs well and\nbadly:\nNonstandard Educating children good is everyone’s focus.\nStandard Educating children well is everyone’s focus.\nNonstandard Some children suffer bad.\nStandard Some children suffer badly.\nTo negate a verb or an adjective, use the adverb not:\nThey are not learning. They are not stupid.\nTo negate a noun, use the adjective no:\nNo child should fail to read.\n\nFOWLcp15-16.doc - 10\n16b Use an adjective after a linking verb to modify the\nsubject. Use an adverb to modify a verb.\nA linking verb is one that links, or connects, a subject and its\ncomplement: They are golfers. He is lucky. (See also pp. 239–40.) Linking\nverbs are forms of be, the verbs associated with our five senses (look,\nsound, smell, feel, taste), and a few others (appear, seem, become, grow,\nturn, prove, remain, stay).\nSome of these verbs may or may not be linking, depending on their meaning in the sentence.\nWhen the word after the verb modi-\nfies the subject, the verb is linking and the word should be an adjective: He feels strong. When the\nword modifies the verb, however, it should be an adverb: He feels strongly about that.\nTwo word pairs are especially troublesome in this context. One is bad and badly:\nThe weather grew bad. She felt bad.\nlinking adjective linking adjective\nverb verb\nFlowers grow badly in such soil.\nverb adverb\nThe other pair is good and well. Good serves only as an adjective. Well may serve as an adverb\nwith a host of meanings or as an adjective meaning only ―fit‖ or ―healthy.‖\nDecker trained well. She felt well. Her health was good.\nverb adverb linking adjective linking adjective\nverb verb\n16c After a direct object, use an adjective to modify\nthe object and an adverb to modify the verb.\nAfter a direct object, an adjective modifies the object, whereas an adverb modifies the verb of\nthe sentence. (See p. 238 for more on direct objects.)\nCampus politics made Mungo angry.\nadjective\nMungo repeated the words angrily.\nadverb\nYou can test whether a modifier should be an adjective or an adverb by trying to separate it\nfrom the direct object. If you can separate it, it should be an adverb: Mungo angrily repeated the\nwords. If you cannot separate it, it is probably an adjective.\nThe instructor considered the student’s work thorough. [The adjective can be moved in front of work\n(student’s thorough work), but it cannot be separated from work.]\nThe instructor considered the student’s work thoroughly. [The adverb can be separated from work.\nCompare The instructor thoroughly considered the student’s work.]\nEXERCISE 16.1 Revising: Adjectives and adverbs\nRevise the following sentences so that adjectives and adverbs are used appropriately. If any sentence is\nalready correct as given, mark the number preceding it. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman\n.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nThe announcer warned that traffic was moving very slow.\nThe announcer warned that traffic was moving very slowly.\n1. King George III of England declared Samuel Johnson suitably for a pension.\n\nFOWLcp15-16.doc - 11\n2. Johnson was taken serious as a critic and dictionary maker.\n3. Thinking about his meeting with the king, Johnson felt proudly.\n4. Johnson was relieved that he had not behaved badly in the king’s presence.\n5. After living cheap for over twenty years, Johnson finally had enough money from the pension to eat\nand dress good.\n16d Use the comparative and superlative forms of\nadjectives and adverbs appropriately.\nAdjectives and adverbs can show degrees of quality or amount with the endings -er and -est or\nwith the words more and most or less and least. Most modifiers have three forms:\nPositive The basic Comparative A Superlative The\nform listed in greater or lesser greatest or least\nthe dictionary degree of the degree of the\nquality quality\nAdjectives\nred redder reddest\nawful more/less awful most/least awful\nAdverbs\nsoon sooner soonest\nquickly more/less quickly most/least quickly\nIf sound alone does not tell you whether to use -er/-est or more/most, consult a dictionary. If\nthe endings can be used, the dictionary will list them. Otherwise, use more or most.\n1 Use the correct forms of irregular adjectives and adverbs.\nCertian adjectives and adverbs are irregular: they change the spelling of their positive form to\nshow comparative and superlative degrees.\n2 Use either -er/-est or more/most, not both.\nA double comparative or double superlative combines the -er or -est\nending with the word more or most. It is redundant.\nChang was the wisest [not most wisest] person in town.\nHe was smarter [not more smarter] than anyone else.\n3 Use the comparative for comparing two things and the superlative for comparing\nthree or more things.\nIt is the shorter of her two books. [Comparative.]\nThe Yearling is the most popular of the six books. [Superlative.]\nIn conversation the superlative form is often used to compare only two things: When two\npeople argue, the angriest one is usually wrong. But the distinction between the forms should be\nobserved in writing.\n4 Use comparative or superlative forms only for modifiers that can logically be\ncompared.\nSome adjectives and adverbs cannot logically be compared—for instance, perfect, unique,\ndead, impossible, infinite. These abso-lute words can be preceded by adverbs like nearly and\nalmost that mean ―approaching,‖ but they cannot logically be modified by more or most (as in most\nperfect). This distinction is sometimes ignored in speech, but it should always be made in writing:\nNot He was the most unique teacher we had.\nBut He was a unique teacher.\n\nFOWLcp15-16.doc - 12\nEXERCISE 16.2 Revising: Comparatives and superlatives\nRevise the sentences below so that the comparative and superlative forms of adjectives and adverbs are\nappropriate for formal usage. Mark the number preceding any sentence that is already correct. (You can\ndo this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nAttending classes full time and working at two jobs was the most impossible thing I ever did.\nAttending classes full time and working at two jobs was imposs-ible [or the hardest thing I ever did\n].\n1. Charlotte was the older of the three Brontë sisters, all of whom were novelists.\n2. Some readers think Emily Brontë’s Wuthering Heights is the most saddest novel they have ever read.\n3. Of the other two sisters, Charlotte and Anne, Charlotte was probably the more talented.\n4. Critics still argue about whether Charlotte or Emily wrote more better.\n5. Certainly this family of women novelists was the most unique.\n16e Watch for double negatives.\nIn a double negative two negative words such as no, not, none, neither, barely, hardly, or\nscarcely cancel each other out. Some double negatives are intentional: for instance, She was not\nunhappy indicates with understatement that she was indeed happy. But most double negatives say\nthe opposite of what is intended: Jenny did not feel nothing asserts that Jenny felt other than\nnothing, or something. For the opposite meaning, one of the negatives must be eliminated or\nchanged to a positive: She felt nothing or She did not feel anything.\nFaulty The IRS cannot hardly audit all tax returns. None of its audits never touch many cheaters.\nRevised The IRS cannot audit all tax returns. Its audits never touch many cheaters.\nEXERCISE 16.3 Revising: Double negatives\nIdentify and revise the double negatives in the following paragraph. Each error may have more than one\ncorrect revision. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nInterest in books about the founding of the United States seems to vary with the national mood.\nAmericans show hardly no interest in books about the founders when things are going well in the United\nStates. However, when Americans can’t barely agree on major issues, sales of books about the\nRevolutionary War era increase. During such periods, one cannot go to no bookstore without seeing\nseveral new volumes about John Adams, Thomas Jefferson, and other founders. When Americans feel\nthey don’t have nothing in common, their increased interest in the early leaders may reflect a desire for\nunity.\n16f\nUse nouns sparingly as modifiers.\nWe often use one noun to modify another. For example:\nchild care flood control security guard\nSuch phrases can be both clear and concise, but overuse of noun modifiers can lead to flat, even\nsenseless, writing. To avoid awkwardness or confusion, observe two principles. First, prefer\npossessives or adjectives as modifiers:\nNot A student takes the state medical board exams to become a dentist technician.\nBut A student takes the state medical board’s exams to become a dental technician.\nSecond, use only short nouns as modifiers and use them only in two- or three-word sequences:\nConfusing Minimex maintains a plant employee relations improvement program.\nRevised Minimex maintains a program for improving relations among plant employees.\n\nFOWLcp15-16.doc - 13\n16g Distinguish between present and past participles\nas adjectives.\nBoth present participles and past participles may serve as adjectives: a burning building, a\nburned building. As in the examples, the two participles usually differ in the time they indicate.\nBut some present and past participles—those derived from verbs expressing feeling—can have\naltogether different meanings. The present participle modifies something that causes the feeling:\nThat was a frightening storm (the storm frightens). The past participle modifies something that\nexperiences the feeling: They quieted the frightened horses (the horses feel fright).\nThe following participles are among those likely to be confused:\namazing/amazed confusing/confused\namusing/amused depressing/depressed\nannoying/annoyed embarrassing/embarrassed\nastonishing/astonished exciting/excited\nboring/bored exhausting/exhausted\nfascinating/fascinated satisfying/satisfied\nfrightening/frightened shocking/shocked\nfrustrating/frustrated surprising/surprised\ninteresting/interested tiring/tired\npleasing/pleased worrying/worried\nEXERCISE 16.4 Revising: Present and past participles\nRevise the adjectives in the following sentences as needed to distinguish between present and past\nparticiples. If the sentence is already correct as given, mark the number preceding it. (You can do this\nexercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nThe subject was embarrassed to many people.\nThe subject was embarrassing to many people.\n1. Several critics found Alice Walker’s The Color Purple to be a fas-cinated book.\n2. One confused critic wished that Walker had deleted the scenes set in Africa.\n3. Another critic argued that although the book contained many depressed episodes, the overall impact\nwas excited.\n4. Since other readers found the book annoyed, this critic pointed out its many surprised qualities.\n5. In the end most critics agreed that the book was a satisfied novel.\n16h Use a, an, the, and other determiners\nappropriately.\nDeterminers are special kinds of adjectives that mark nouns because they always precede\nnouns. Some common determiners are a, an, and the (called articles) and my, their, whose, this,\nthese, those, one, some, and any. They convey information to readers—for instance, by specifying\nwho owns what, which one of two is meant, or whether a subject is familiar or unfamiliar.\nNative speakers of standard American English can rely on their intuition when using\ndeterminers, but speakers of other languages and dialects often have difficulty with them. In\nstandard English the use of determiners depends on the context they appear in and the kind of noun\nthey precede:\nA proper noun names a particular person, place, or thing and begins with a capital letter:\nFebruary, Joe Allen, Red River. Most proper nouns are not preceded by determiners.\nA count noun names something that is countable in English and can form a plural: girl/girls,\napple/apples, child/children. A singular count noun is always preceded by a determiner; a plu-\nral count noun sometimes is.\nA noncount noun names something not usually considered countable in English, and so it\ndoes not form a plural. A noncount noun is sometimes preceded by a determiner. Here is a\nsample of noncount nouns, sorted into groups by meaning:\n\nFOWLcp15-16.doc - 14\nAbstractions: confidence, democracy, education, equality, evidence, health, information, intelligence,\nknowledge, luxury, peace, pollution, research, success, supervision, truth, wealth, work\nFood and drink: bread, candy, cereal, flour, meat, milk, salt, water, wine\nEmotions: anger, courage, happiness, hate, joy, love, respect, satis-faction\nNatural events and substances: air, blood, dirt, gasoline, gold, hair, heat, ice, oil, oxygen, rain, silver,\nsmoke, weather, wood\nGroups: clergy, clothing, equipment, furniture, garbage, jewelry, junk, legislation, machinery, mail,\nmilitary, money, police, vocabulary\nFields of study: architecture, accounting, biology, business, chemistry, engineering, literature,\npsychology, science\nA dictionary of English as a second language will tell you whether a noun is a count noun, a\nnoncount noun, or both. (See\np. 537 for recommended dictionaries.)\nNote Many nouns can be both count and noncount nouns:\nThe library has a room for readers. [Room is a count noun meaning ―walled area.‖]\nThe library has room for reading. [Room is a noncount noun meaning ―space.‖]\nPartly because the same noun may fall into different groups, grammar and style checkers are\nunreliable guides to missing or misused articles and other determiners. For instance, a checker\nflagged the omitted a before Scientist in Scientist developed new processes; it did not flag the\nomitted a before new in A scientist developed new process; and it mistakenly flagged the correctly\nomitted article the before Vegetation in Vegetation suffers from drought.\n1 Use a, an, and the where they are required.\nWith singular count nouns\nA or an precedes a singular count noun when the reader does not already know its identity,\nusually because you have not mentioned it before:\nA scientist in our chemistry department developed a process to strengthen metals. [Scientist and process\nare being introduced for the first time.]\nThe precedes a singular count noun that has a specific identity for the reader, for one of the\nfollowing reasons:\nYou have mentioned the noun before:\nA scientist in our chemistry department developed a process to strengthen metals. The scientist patented\nthe process. [Scientist and process were identified in the preceding sentence.]\nYou identify the noun immediately before or after you state it:\nThe most productive laboratory is the research center in the chemistry department. [Most productive\nidentifies laboratory. In the chemistry department identifies research center. And chemistry department\nis a shared facility.]\nThe noun names something unique—the only one in existence:\nThe sun rises in the east. [Sun and east are unique.]\nThe noun names an institution or facility that is shared by the community of readers:\nMany men and women aspire to the presidency. [Presidency is a shared institution.]\nThe fax machine has changed business communication. [Fax machine is a shared facility.]\nThe is not used before a singular noun that names a general category:\nSherman said that war is hell. [War names a general category.]\nThe war in Croatia left many dead. [War names a specific war.]\n\nFOWLcp15-16.doc - 15\nWith plural count nouns\nA or an never precedes a plural noun. The does not precede a plural noun that names a general\ncategory. The does precede a plu-ral noun that names specific representatives of a category.\nMen and women are different. [Men and women name general categories.]\nThe women formed a team. [Women refers to specific people.]\nWith noncount nouns\nA or an never precedes a noncount noun. The does precede a noncount noun when it names\nspecific representatives of a general category:\nVegetation suffers from drought. [Vegetation names a general category.]\nThe vegetation in the park withered or died. [Vegetation refers to specific plants.]\nWith proper nouns\nA or an never precedes a proper noun. The generally does not precede a proper noun:\nGarcia lives in Boulder.\nThere are exceptions, however. For instance, we generally use the before plural proper nouns (the\nMurphys, the Boston Celtics) and the names of groups and organizations (the Department of\nJustice, the Sierra Club), ships (the Lusitania), oceans (the Pacific), mountain ranges (the Alps),\nregions (the Middle East), rivers (the Mississippi), and some countries (the United States, the\nSudan).\n2 Use other determiners appropriately.\nThe uses of English determiners besides articles also depend on context and kind of noun. The\nfollowing determiners may be used as indicated with singular count nouns, plural count nouns, or\nnoncount nouns.\nWith any kind of noun (singular count, plural count, noncount)\nmy, our, your, his, her, its, their\npossessive nouns (boy’s, boys’)\nwhose, which(ever), what(ever)\nsome, any, the other\nno\nTheir account is overdrawn. [Singular count.]\nTheir funds are low. [Plural count.]\nTheir money is running out. [Noncount.]\nOnly with singular nouns (count and noncount)\nthis, that\nThis account has some money. [Count.]\nThat information may help. [Noncount.]\nOnly with noncount nouns and plural count nouns\nmost, enough, other, such, all, all of the, a lot of\nMost money is needed elsewhere. [Noncount.]\nMost funds are committed. [Plural count.]\nOnly with singular count nouns\none, every, each, either, neither, another\nOne car must be sold. [Singular count.]\nOnly with plural count nouns\nthese, those\nboth, many, few, a few, fewer, fewest, several\n\nFOWLcp15-16.doc - 16\ntwo, three, and so forth\nTwo cars are unnecessary. [Plural count.]\nNote Few means ―not many‖ or ―not enough.‖ A few means ―some‖ or ―a small but sufficient\nquantity.‖\nFew committee members came to the meeting.\nA few members can keep the committee going.\nDo not use much with a plural count noun:\nMany [not Much] members want to help.\nOnly with noncount nouns\nmuch, more, little, a little, less, least, a large amount of\nLess luxury is in order. [Noncount.]\nNote Little means ―not many‖ or ―not enough.‖ A little means ―some‖ or ―a small but\nsufficient quantity.‖\nLittle time remains before the conference.\nThe members need a little help from their colleagues.\nDo not use many with a noncount noun:\nMuch [not Many] work remains.\nEXERCISE 16.5 Revising: Articles\nFor each blank below, indicate whether a, an, the, or no article should be inserted.
