We eat.\nSecond You eat. You eat.\nThird He/she/it eats. They eat.\nThe bird eats. Birds eat.\nSummary of subject-verb agreement\nBasic subject-verb agreement (p. 305):\nSingular Plural\nThe kite flies. The kites fly.\nWords between subject and verb (p. 307):\nThe kite with two tails flies badly. The tails of the kite compete.\nSubjects joined by and (p. 307):\nThe kite and the bird are almost indistinguishable.\nSubjects joined by or or nor (below):\nThe kite or the bird dives. Kites or birds fill the sky.\nIndefinite pronouns as subjects (opposite):\nNo one knows. All the spectators wonder.\nCollective nouns as subjects (p. 310):\nA flock appears. The flock disperse.\nInverted word order (p. 310):\nIs the kite or the bird blue? Are the kite and the bird both blue?\nLinking verbs (p. 311):\nThe kite is a flier and a dipper.\nWho, which, that as subjects (p. 311):\nThe kite that flies longest wins. Kites that fall lose.\nSubjects with plural form and singular meaning (p. 312):\nAeronautics plays a role in kite flying.\nTitles and words named as words (p. 312):\nKite Dynamics is one title. Vectors is a key word.\nCommon indefinite pronouns\nSingular\nSingular or plural Plural\nanybody everyone no one all both\nanyone everything nothing any few\nanything much one more many\neach neither somebody most several\neither nobody someone some\neverybody none something\nPerson, number, and gender in\npronoun-antecedent agreement\n\nFOWLcp15-16.doc - 19\nNumber\nPerson Singular Plural\nFirst I we\nSecond you you\nThird he, she, it, they,\nindefinite pronouns, plural nouns\nsingular nouns\nGender\nMasculine he, nouns naming males\nFeminine she, nouns naming females\nNeuter it, all other nouns\nSummary of pronoun-antecedent agreement\nBasic pronoun-antecedent agreement:\nOld Faithful spews its columns of water, each of them over 115 feet high.\nAntecedents joined by and (below):\nOld Faithful and Giant are geysers known for their height.\nAntecedents joined by or or nor (opposite):\nEither Giant or Giantess ejects its column the highest.\nIndefinite words as antecedents (opposite):\nEach of the geysers has its own personality. Each person who visits has his or her memories.\nCollective nouns as antecedents (p. 316):\nA crowd amuses itself watching Old Faithful. The crowd go their separate ways.\nWays to correct agreement with indefinite words\nChange the indefinite word to a plural, and use a plural pronoun to match:\nFaulty Every athlete deserves their privacy.\nRevised Athletes deserve their privacy.\nRewrite the sentence to omit the pronoun:\nFaulty Everyone is entitled to their own locker.\nRevised Everyone is entitled to a locker.\nUse he or she (him or her, his or her) to refer to the indefinite word:\nFaulty Now everyone has their private space.\nRevised Now everyone has his or her private space.\nHowever, used more than once in several sentences, he or she quickly becomes awkward. (Many\nreaders do not accept the alternative he/she.) In most cases, using the plural or omitting the pronoun\nwill not only correct agreement problems but also create more readable sentences.\nFunctions of adjectives and adverbs\nAdjectives modify nouns: serious student\npronouns: ordinary one\nAdverbs modify verbs: warmly greet\nadjectives: only three people\nadverbs: quite seriously\nphrases: nearly to the edge of the cliff\nclauses: just when we arrived\nsentences: Fortunately, she is employed.\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp15-16.doc - 20\nVisit the companion Web site for more help and additional exercises on adjectives and adverbs.\nDegrees of irregular adjectives and adverbs\nPositive Comparative Superlative\nAdjectives\ngood better best\nbad worse worst\nlittle littler, less littlest, least\nmany\nsome more most\nmuch\nAdverbs\nwell better best\nbadly worse worst\n\nFOWLcp17.doc - 1\n4\nPART\n\nFOWLcp17.doc - 2\nClear Sentences\n17\nCHAPTER\nSentence Fragments\n18\nCHAPTER\nComma Splices and Fused\nSentences\n19\nCHAPTER\nPronoun Reference\n20\nCHAPTER\nShifts\n21\nCHAPTER\nMisplaced and Dangling\nModifiers\n22\nCHAPTER\nMixed and Incomplete\nSentences\n\nFOWLcp17.doc - 3\n17\nCHAPTER\nSentence Fragments\nA sentence fragment is part of a sentence that is set off as if it were a whole sentence by an\ninitial capital letter and a final period or other end punctuation. Although writers occasionally use\nfragments deliberately and effectively (see p. 341), readers perceive most fragments as serious\nerrors because, expecting complete sentences, they find partial sentences distracting or confusing.\n(Before reading further, you may find it helpful to review pp. 233–41 and 252–56 on sentences and\nclauses.)\nNote A grammar and style checker can spot many but not all sentence fragments, and it may\nflag sentences that are actually correct commands, such as Continue reading.\n17a Test your sentences for completeness, and revise\nany fragments.\nThe following three tests will help you determine whether a word group punctuated as a\nsentence is actually a complete sentence. If the word group does not pass all three tests, it is a\nfragment and needs to be revised.\nTest 1: Find the verb.\nLook for a verb in the group of words. If you do not have one, the word group is a fragment:\nFragment Uncountable numbers of sites on the World Wide Web.\nRevised Uncountable numbers of sites make up the World Wide\nWeb.\nAny verb form you find must be a finite verb, one that changes form as indicated below. A\nverbal does not change; it cannot serve as a sentence verb without the aid of a helping verb.\nFinite verbs in Verbals in\ncomplete sentences sentence fragments\nSingular The network grows. The network growing.\nPlural Networks grow. Networks growing.\nPresent The network grows.\nPast The network grew. The network growing.\nFuture The network will grow.\nSome languages allow forms of be to be omitted as helping or linking verbs. But English\nrequires stating forms of be:\nFragments The network growing. It already larger than its developers anticipated.\nRevised The network is growing. It is already larger than its de-\nvelopers anticipated.\nTest 2: Find the subject.\nIf you find a finite verb, look for its subject by asking who or what performs the action or\nmakes the assertion of the verb. The subject of the sentence will usually come before the verb. If\nthere is no subject, the word group is probably a fragment:\nFragment And has great popular appeal.\nRevised And the Web has great popular appeal.\n\nFOWLcp17.doc - 4\nIn one kind of complete sentence, a command, the subject you is understood: [You]\nExperiment with the Web.\nSome languages allow the omission of the sentence subject, especially when it is a pronoun.\nBut in English, except in commands, the subject is always stated:\nFragments Web commerce is expanding dramatically. Is threaten-\ning traditional stores.\nRevised Web commerce is expanding dramatically. It is threaten-\ning traditional stores.\nTest 3: Make sure the clause is not subordinate.\nA subordinate clause usually begins with a subordinating word:\nSubordinating conjunctions Relative pronouns\nafter once until that who/whom\nalthough since when which whoever/whomever\nas than where whose\nbecause that whereas\nif unless while\n(See p. 253 for a longer list of subordinating conjunctions.)\nSubordinate clauses serve as parts of sentences (nouns or modifiers), not as whole sentences:\nFragment When the government devised the Internet.\nRevised The government devised the Internet.\nRevised When the government devised the Internet, no expansive computer network existed.\nFragment The reason that the government devised the Internet.\nRevised The reason that the government devised the Internet was to provide secure links among\ndepartments and defense contractors.\nNote Questions beginning with how, what, when, where, which, who, whom, whose, and why\nare not sentence fragments: Who was responsible? When did it happen?\nRevising sentence fragments\nAlmost all sentence fragments can be corrected in one of two ways, the choice depending on\nthe importance of the information in the fragment:\nRewrite the fragment as a complete sentence, giving the information in the fragment the\nsame importance as that in other complete sentences:\nFragment A major improvement of the Internet occurred with the World Wide Web. Which allows\nusers to move easily be-tween sites.\nRevised A major improvement of the Internet occurred with the World Wide Web. It allows users to\nmove easily between sites.\nTwo main clauses may be separated by a semicolon instead of a period (see p. 453).\nCombine the fragment with a main clause, subordinating the information in the fragment to\nthe information in the main clause:\nFragment The Web is easy to use. Loaded with links and graphics.\nRevised The Web, loaded with links and graphics, is easy to use.\nPunctuating corrected fragments\nIn the preceding example, commas separate the inserted phrase from the rest of the sentence\nbecause the phrase is not essential to the meaning of any word in the main clause but simply adds\ninformation (see p. 435). When a phrase or subordinate clause is essential to the meaning of a word\nin the main clause, a comma or commas do not separate the two elements:\nFragment With the links, users can move to other Web sites. That they want to consult.\nRevised With the links, users can move to other Web sites that\nthey want to consult.\n\nFOWLcp17.doc - 5\nSometimes a fragment may be combined with the main clause using a\ncolon or a dash (see pp. 477 and 480, respectively):\nFragment The Web connects sites from all over the Internet. Different databases, different software,\ndifferent machines.\nRevised The Web connects sites from all over the Internet: different databases, different software,\ndifferent machines.\nFragment The Web is a boon to researchers. A vast and accessible library.\nRevised The Web is a boon to researchers—a vast and accessi-\nble library.\nEXERCISE 17.1 Identifying and revising sentence fragments\nApply the tests for completeness to each of the following word groups. If a word group is a complete\nsentence, mark the number preceding it. If it is a sentence fragment, revise it in two ways: by making it a\ncomplete sentence, and by combining it with a main clause written from the information given in other\nitems. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nAnd could not find his money. [The word group has a verb (could\n. . . find) but no subject.]\nRevised into a complete sentence: And he could not find his money.\nCombined with a new main clause: He was lost and could not find his money.\n1. In an interesting article about vandalism against works of art.\n2. The motives of the vandals varying widely.\n3. Those who harm artwork are usually angry.\n4. But not necessarily at the artist or the owner.\n5. For instance, a man who hammered at Michelangelo’s Pietà.\n6. And knocked off the Virgin Mary’s nose.\n7. Because he was angry at the Roman Catholic Church.\n8. Which knew nothing of his grievance.\n9. Although many damaged works can be repaired.\n10. Usually even the most skillful repairs are forever visible.\n17b A subordinate clause is not a complete sentence.\nSubordinate clauses contain both subjects and verbs, but they always begin with a\nsubordinating conjunction (although, if, and so on) or a relative pronoun (who, which, that). (See\npp. 252–53.) Subordinate clauses serve as nouns or modifiers, but they cannot stand alone as\ncomplete sentences.\nTo correct a subordinate clause set off as a sentence, combine it with the main clause or\nremove or change the subordinating word to create a main clause.\nFragment Many pine trees bear large cones. Which appear in Au-gust.\nRevised Many pine trees bear large cones, which appear in Au-gust.\nRevised Many pine trees bear large cones. They appear in August.\n17c A verbal phrase or a prepositional phrase is not\na complete sentence.\nA verbal phrase consists of an infinitive (to choose), a past participle (chosen), or a present\nparticiple or gerund (choosing) together with any objects and modifiers it may have (see p. 249). A\nverbal phrase is a noun or modifier and cannot serve as the verb in a complete sentence:\nFragment For many of the elderly, their house is their only asset. Offering some security but no\nincome.\nRevised For many of the elderly, their house is their only asset, offering some security but no\nincome.\n\nFOWLcp17.doc - 6\nRevised For many of the elderly, their house is their only asset. It offers some security but no\nincome.\nA prepositional phrase is a modifier consisting of a preposition (such as in, on, to, and with)\ntogether with its object and any modifiers (see p. 245). A prepositional phrase cannot stand alone\nas a complete sentence:\nFragment In a squeeze between a valuable asset and little income. Eventually many elderly people\nsell their homes.\nRevised In a squeeze between a valuable asset and little income, eventually many elderly people\nsell their homes.\nRevised Many elderly people are in a squeeze between a valuable asset and little income.\nEventually they may sell their homes.\nSome English prepositions consist of two or three words: as well as, along with, in addition\nto, on top of, and others. Don’t let prepositions of more than one word mislead you into writing\nsentence fragments.\nFragment In today’s retirement communities, the elderly may have health care, housekeeping, and\nnew friends. As well as financial security.\nRevised In today’s retirement communities, the elderly may have health care, housekeeping, and\nnew friends, as well as financial security.\n17d Any word group lacking a subject or a verb or\nboth is not a complete sentence.\nWe often follow a noun with a modifier. No matter how long the noun and its modifier are,\nthey cannot stand alone as a sentence:\nFragments People waving flags and cheering. Lined the streets for the parade.\nRevised People waving flags and cheering lined the streets for the parade.\nFragment Veterans who fought in Vietnam. They are finally being honored.\nRevised Veterans who fought in Vietnam are finally being hon-ored.\nAppositives are nouns, or nouns and their modifiers, that rename or describe other nouns (see\np. 257). They cannot stand alone as sentences:\nFragment When I was a child, my favorite adult was an old uncle. A retired sea captain who always\ntold me long stories of wild adventures in faraway places.\nRevised When I was a child, my favorite adult was an old uncle, a retired sea captain who always\ntold me long stories of wild adventures in faraway places.\nCompound predicates are predicates made up of two or more verbs and their objects, if any\n(see p. 258). A verb or its object cannot stand alone as a sentence:\nFragment Uncle Marlon drew out his tales. And embellished them.\nRevised Uncle Marlon drew out his tales and embellished them.\nFragment He described characters he had met. And storms at sea.\nRevised He described characters he had met and storms at sea.\nNote Beginning a sentence with a coordinating conjunction such as and or but can lead to a\nsentence fragment. Check every sentence you begin with a coordinating conjunction to be sure it is\ncomplete.\nEXERCISE 17.2 Revising: Sentence fragments\nCorrect any sentence fragment below either by combining it with a main clause or by making it a main\nclause. If an item contains no sentence fragment, mark the number preceding it. (You can do this exercise\nonline at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\n\nFOWLcp17.doc - 7\nExample:\nJujitsu is good for self-protection. Because it enables one to overcome an opponent without the use of\nweapons.\nJujitsu is good for self-protection because it enables one to overcome an opponent without the use of\nweapons.\n1. Human beings who perfume themselves. They are not much different from other animals.\n2. Animals as varied as insects and dogs release pheromones. Chemicals that signal other animals.\n3. Human beings have a diminished sense of smell. And do not consciously detect most of their own\nspecies’ pheromones.\n4. The human substitute for pheromones may be perfumes. Especially musk and other fragrances\nderived from animal oils.\n5. Some sources say that humans began using perfume to cover up the smell of burning flesh. During\nsacrifices to the gods.\n6. Perfumes became religious offerings in their own right. Being ex-pensive to make, they were highly\nprized.\n7. The earliest historical documents from the Middle East record the use of fragrances. Not only in\nreligious ceremonies but on the body.\n8. In the nineteenth century chemists began synthesizing perfume oils. Which previously could be made\nonly from natural sources.\n9. The most popular animal oil for perfume today is musk. Although some people dislike its heavy,\nsweet odor.\n10. Synthetic musk oil would help conserve a certain species of deer. Whose gland is the source of musk.\n17e Be aware of the acceptable uses of incomplete\nsentences.\nA few word groups lacking the usual subject-predicate combination are not sentence\nfragments because they conform to the expectations of most readers. They include exclamations\n(Oh no!); questions and answers (Where next? To Kansas.); and commands (Move along. Shut the\nwindow.). Another kind of incomplete sentence, occurring in special situations, is the transitional\nphrase (So much for the causes, now for the results. One final point.).\nExperienced writers sometimes use sentence fragments when they want to achieve a special\neffect. Such fragments appear more in informal than in formal writing. Unless you are experienced\nand thoroughly secure in your own writing, you should avoid all fragments and concentrate on\nwriting clear, well-formed sentences.\nEXERCISE 17.3 Revising: Sentence fragments\nRevise the following paragraph to eliminate sentence fragments by combining them with main clauses or\nrewriting them as main clauses. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nBaby red-eared slider turtles are brightly colored. With bold patterns on their yellowish undershells.\nWhich serve as a warning to predators. The bright colors of skunks and other animals. They signal that\nthe animals will spray nasty chemicals. In contrast, the turtle’s colors warn largemouth bass. That the\nbaby turtle will actively defend itself. When a bass gulps down a turtle. The feisty baby claws and bites.\nForcing the bass to spit it out. To avoid a similar painful experience. The bass will avoid other baby red-\neared slider turtles. The turtle loses its bright colors as it grows too big. For a bass’s afternoon snack.\nNote See page 381 for an exercise involving sentence fragments along with comma splices,\nfused sentences, and other sentence errors.\nComplete sentence versus sentence fragment\nA complete sentence or main clause\ncontains a subject and a verb (The wind blows)\nand is not a subordinate clause (beginning with a word such as be-cause or who).\nA sentence fragment\nlacks a verb (The wind blowing)\nor lacks a subject (And blows)\nor is a subordinate clause not attached to a complete sentence (Be-cause the wind blows).\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp17.doc - 8\nVisit the companion Web site for more help and additional exercises on sentence fragments.\nTests for complete sentences\nPerform all three of the following tests to be sure your sentences are\ncomplete.\n1. Find the verb.\n2. Find the subject.\n3. Make sure the clause is not subordinate.\n\nFOWLcp18.doc - 1\n18\nCHAPTER\nComma Splices and\nFused Sentences\nA sentence or main clause contains at least a subject and a predicate, which together express a\ncomplete thought (see p. 233). We can separate two consecutive main clauses in one of four ways:\nWith a period:\nThe ship was huge. Its mast stood eighty feet high.\nWith a semicolon:\nThe ship was huge; its mast stood eighty feet high.\nWith a comma preceding a coordinating conjunction that joins the clauses and specifies the\nrelation between them:\nThe ship was huge, and its mast stood eighty feet high.\nWith a colon when the second clause explains the first (see p. 477):\nThe ship was huge: its mast stood eighty feet high.\nThe period, semicolon, or colon alone or the comma plus coordinating conjunction signals readers\nthat one main clause (complete thought) is ending and another is beginning.\nThe comma splice and the fused sentence deprive readers of this signal and often force them to\nreread for sense. In a comma splice the two main clauses are joined (or spliced) only with a\ncomma, not with a coordinating conjunction as well.\nComma splice\nThe ship was huge, its mast stood eighty feet high.\nIn a fused sentence no punctuation or coordinating conjunction appears between the main clauses.\nFused sentence\nThe ship was huge its mast stood eighty feet high.\nException Experienced writers sometimes use a comma without a coordinating conjunction\nbetween very brief main clauses that are grammatically parallel:\nHe’s not a person, he’s a monster.\nHowever, many readers view such punctuation as incorrect. Unless you are certain that your\nreaders will not object to the comma in a sentence like this one, separate the clauses with periods or\nsemicolons, as described in this chapter.\nNote Grammar and style checkers can detect many comma splices, but they will miss most\nfused sentences. For example, a checker flagged Money is tight, we need to spend carefully but not\nMoney is tight we need to spend carefully. A checker may also question sentences that are actually\ncorrect, such as Money being tighter now than before, we need to spend carefully. Verify that\nrevision is actually needed on any flagged sentence.\nAn English sentence may not include more than one main clause unless the clauses are\nseparated by a comma and a coordinating conjunction or by a semicolon or colon. If your native\nlanguage does not have such a rule or has accustomed you to writing long sentences, you may need\nto edit your English writing especially for comma splices and fused sentences.\n\nFOWLcp18.doc - 2\nComma Splices\nSeparate two main clauses with a comma only\nwhen they are joined by a coordinating\nconjunction.\nA comma cannot separate main clauses unless they are linked by a coordinating conjunction\n(and, but, or, nor, for, so, yet). Readers expect the same main clause to continue after a comma\nalone. When they find themselves reading a second main clause before they realize they have\nfinished the first, they may have to reread.\nYou have several options for revising comma splices.\nMaking separate sentences\nRevising a comma splice by making separate sentences from the main\nclauses will always be correct. The period is not only correct but preferable\nwhen the ideas expressed in the two main clauses are only loosely related:\nComma splice Chemistry has contributed much to our understanding of foods, many foods such as wheat\nand beans can be produced in the laboratory.\nRevised Chemistry has contributed much to our understanding of foods. Many foods such as\nwheat and beans can be produced in the laboratory.\nMaking separate sentences may be the best option if you are used to writing very long\nsentences in your native language and often write comma splices in English.\nInserting a coordinating conjunction\nWhen the ideas in the main clauses are closely related and equally important, you may correct\na comma splice by inserting the appropriate coordinating conjunction immediately after the comma\nto join the clauses:\nComma splice Some laboratory-grown foods taste good, they are nutritious.\nRevised Some laboratory-grown foods taste good, and they are nutritious.\nUsing a semicolon\nIf the relation between the ideas expressed in the main clauses is very close and obvious\nwithout a conjunction, you can separate the clauses with a semicolon.\nComma splice Good taste is rare in laboratory-grown vegetables, they are usually bland.\nRevised Good taste is rare in laboratory-grown vegetables; they are usually bland.\nSubordinating one clause\nWhen the idea in one clause is more important than that in the other, you can express the less\nimportant idea in a phrase or a subordinate clause. (See p. 253 for a list of subordinating\nconjunctions and pp. 398–402 for more on subordination.) Subordination is often more effective\nthan forming separate sentences because it defines the relation between ideas more precisely:\nComma splice The vitamins are adequate, the flavor is deficient.\nRevised The vitamins are adequate. The flavor is deficient. [Both ideas receive equal weight.]\nImproved Even though the vitamins are adequate, the flavor is deficient. [Emphasis on the\nsecond idea.]\nSeparate main clauses related by however, for\nexample, and so on.\nTwo kinds of words that are not conjunctions describe how one main clause relates to another:\n\nFOWLcp18.doc - 3\nConjunctive adverbs, such as consequently, finally, hence, however, indeed, therefore, or\nthus. (See p. 261 for a longer list.)\nOther transitional expressions, such as even so, for example, in fact, of course, to the right,\nand to this end. (See pp. 86–87 for a longer list.)\nWhen two main clauses are related by a conjunctive adverb or\na transitional expression, they must be separated by a period or by\na semicolon. The adverb or expression is also generally set off by a comma or commas (see p. 437):\nComma splice Most Americans refuse to give up unhealthful habits, consequently\nour medical costs are higher than those of many other countries.\nRevised Most Americans refuse to give up unhealthful habits. Consequently, our medical costs\nare higher than those of many other countries.\nRevised Most Americans refuse to give up unhealthful habits;\nconsequently, our medical costs are higher than\nthose of many other countries.\nConjunctive adverbs and transitional expressions are different from coordinating conjunctions\n(and, but, and so on) and subordinating conjunctions (although, because, and so on):\nUnlike conjunctions, conjunctive adverbs and transitional expressions do not join two\nclauses into a grammatical unit. They merely describe the way two clauses relate in meaning.\nUnlike conjunctions, conjunctive adverbs and transitional expressions can be moved\nwithin a clause (see also p. 261). No matter where in the clause an adverb or expression falls,\nthough, the clause must be separated from another main clause by a period or semicolon.\nComma splice\nThe increased time devoted to watching television is not the only cause of the decline in reading ability,\nhowever, it is one of the im-portant causes.\nPeriod\nThe increased time devoted to watching television is not the only cause of the decline in reading ability.\nHowever, it is one of the im-portant causes.\nSemicolon\nThe increased time devoted to watching television is not the only cause of the decline in reading ability;\nhowever, it is one of the im-portant causes.\nThe increased time devoted to watching television is not the only cause of the decline in reading ability; it\nis, however, one of the im-portant causes.\nEXERCISE 18.1 Identifying and revising comma splices\nCorrect each comma splice below in two of the ways described on pages 344–46. If an item contains no\ncomma splice, mark the number preceding it. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/\nlittlebrown.)\nExample:\nCarolyn still had a headache, she could not get the child-proof cap off the aspirin bottle.\nCarolyn still had a headache because she could not get the child-proof cap off the aspirin bottle.\n[Subordination.]\nCarolyn still had a headache, for she could not get the child-proof cap off the aspirin bottle.\n[Coordinating conjunction.]\n1. Money has a long history, it goes back at least as far as the earliest records.\n2. Many of the earliest records concern financial transactions, in-deed, early history must often be\ninferred from commercial activity.\n3. Every known society has had a system of money, though the objects serving as money have varied\nwidely.\n\nFOWLcp18.doc - 4\n4. Sometimes the objects have had real value, in modern times, how-ever, their value has been more\nabstract.\n5. Cattle, fermented beverages, and rare shells have served as money, each one had actual value for the\nsociety.\nFused Sentences\nCombine two main clauses only with an\nappropriate conjunction or punctuation mark\nbetween them.\nWhen two main clauses are joined without a word to connect them or a\npunctuation mark to separate them, the result is a fused sentence. Fused\nsentences can rarely be understood on first reading, and they are never\nacceptable in standard written English.\nFused Our foreign policy is not well defined it confuses many countries.\nFused sentences may be corrected in the same ways as comma splices. See pages 344–46.\nSeparate sentences\nOur foreign policy is not well defined. It confuses many coun-\ntries.\nComma and coordinating conjunction\nOur foreign policy is not well defined, and it confuses many countries.\nSemicolon\nOur foreign policy is not well defined; it confuses many countries.\nSubordinating conjunction\nBecause our foreign policy is not well defined, it confuses many countries.\nEXERCISE 18.2 Identifying and revising fused sentences\nRevise each of the fused sentences below in two of the four ways shown above. (You can do this exercise\nonline at ablongman.com/\nlittlebrown.)\nExample:\nTim was shy he usually refused invitations.\nTim was shy, so he usually refused invitations.\nTim was shy; he usually refused invitations.\n1. Throughout history money and religion were closely linked there was little distinction between\ngovernment and religion.\n2. The head of state and the religious leader were often the same person all power rested in one ruler.\n3. These powerful leaders decided what objects would serve as money their backing encouraged public\nfaith in the money.\n4. Coins were minted of precious metals the religious overtones of money were then strengthened.\n5. People already believed the precious metals to be divine their use in money intensified its allure.\nEXERCISE 18.3 Sentence combining: Comma splices and fused sentences\nCombine each pair of sentences below into one sentence without creating a comma splice or fused\nsentence. Combine sentences by (1) supplying a comma and coordinating conjunction, (2) supplying a\nsemicolon, or (3) subordinating one clause to the other. You will have to add, delete, or change words as\nwell as punctuation. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\n\nFOWLcp18.doc - 5\nExample:\nThe sun sank lower in the sky. The colors gradually faded.\nAs the sun sank lower in the sky, the colors gradually faded. [The first clause is subordinated to the\nsecond.]\n1. The exact origin of paper money is unknown. It has not survived as coins, shells, and other durable\nobjects have.\n2. Perhaps goldsmiths were also bankers. Thus they held the gold of their wealthy customers.\n3. The goldsmiths probably gave customers receipts for their gold. These receipts were then used in\ntrade.\n4. The goldsmiths were something like modern-day bankers. Their receipts were something like\nmodern-day money.\n5. The goldsmiths became even more like modern-day bankers. They began issuing receipts for more\ngold than they actually held in their vaults.\nEXERCISE 18.4 Revising: Comma splices and fused sentences\nIdentify and revise the comma splices and fused sentences in the following paragraph. (You can do this\nexercise online at ablongman.com/ littlebrown.)\nAll those parents who urged their children to eat broccoli were right, the vegetable really is\nhealthful. Broccoli contains sulforaphane, moreover, this mustard oil can be found in kale and Brussels\nsprouts. Sulforaphane causes the body to make an enzyme that attacks carcinogens, these substances\ncause cancer. The enzyme speeds up the work of the kidneys then they can flush harmful chemicals out of\nthe body. Other vegetables have similar benefits however, green, leafy vegetables like broccoli are the\nmost efficient. Thus wise people will eat their broccoli it could save their lives.\nNote See page 381 for an exercise involving comma splices and fused sentences along with\nother sentence errors.\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp18.doc - 6\nVisit the companion Web site for more help and additional exercises on\ncomma splices and fused sentences.\nSituations that may produce comma splices\nand fused sentences\nThe first clause is negative; the second, positive:\nSplice Petric is not a nurse, she is a doctor.\nRevised Petric is not a nurse; she is a doctor.\nThe second clause amplifies or illustrates the first:\nFused She did well in college her average was 3.9.\nRevised She did well in college: her average was 3.9.\nThe second clause contains a conjunctive adverb or other transitional expression, such as\nhowever or for example (see p. 346):\nSplice She had intended to become a biologist, however, medicine\nseemed more exciting.\nRevised She had intended to become a biologist; however, medicine\nseemed more exciting.\nThe subject of the second clause repeats or refers to the subject of the first clause:\nFused Petric is an internist she practices in Topeka.\nRevised Petric is an internist. She practices in Topeka.\nSplicing or fusing is an attempt to link related ideas or to smooth choppy sentences:\nSplice She is very committed to her work, she devotes almost all her\ntime to patient care.\nRevised Because she is very committed to her work, she devotes almost\nall her time to patient care.\nRevised She is so committed to her work that she devotes almost all\nher time to patient care.\nWords identifying the speaker divide a quotation between two\ncomplete sentences. (See p. 444 for the punctuation to use in this case.)\nSplice ‘‘Medicine is a human frontier,’’ Petric says, ‘‘The\nboundaries are unknown.’’\nRevised ‘‘Medicine is a human frontier,’’ Petric says. ‘‘The\nboundaries are unknown.’’\nRevision of comma splices and fused sentences\n1. Underline the main clauses in your draft.\n2. When two main clauses fall in the same sentence, check the\nconnection between them.\n3. If nothing falls between the clauses or only a comma does,\nrevise in one of the following ways. The revision depends on the relation\nyou want to establish between the clauses. (See the text discussion for\nexamples.)\nMake the clauses into separate sentences.\nInsert a comma followed by and, but, or another coordinating conjunction. Or, if the comma is\nalready present, insert just the coordinating conjunction.\nInsert a semicolon between clauses.\nSubordinate one clause to the other.\n\nFOWLcp19-20.doc - 1\n19\nCHAPTER\nPronoun Reference\nA pronoun such as it or they derives its meaning from its antecedent, the noun it substitutes\nfor. Therefore, a pronoun must refer clearly and unmistakably to its antecedent in order for the\nmeaning to be clear. A sentence such as Jim told Mark he was not\ninvited is not clear because the reader does not know whether he refers to Jim or to Mark.\nOne way to make pronoun reference clear is to ensure that the pronoun and antecedent agree\nin person, number, and gender (see p. 313). The other way is to ensure that the pronoun refers\nunambiguously to a single, close, specific antecedent.\nNote Grammar and style checkers cannot recognize unclear pronoun reference. For instance, a\nchecker did not spot any of the problems in Exercise 19.2 on page 356.\nAn English pronoun does need a clear antecedent nearby, but don’t use both a pronoun and\nits antecedent as the subject of the same sentence or clause: Jim [not Jim he] told Mark to go alone.\n(See also pp. 375–76.)\nMake a pronoun refer clearly to one antecedent.\nWhen either of two nouns can be a pronoun’s antecedent, the reference will not be clear:\nConfusing Emily Dickinson is sometimes compared with Jane Aus-\nten, but she was quite different.\nRevise such a sentence in one of two ways:\nReplace the pronoun with the appropriate noun:\nClear Emily Dickinson is sometimes compared with Jane Aus-ten, but Dickinson [or Austen]\nwas quite different.\nAvoid repetition by rewriting the sentence. If you use the pronoun, make sure it has only\none possible antecedent:\nClear Despite occasional comparison, Emily Dickinson and Jane Austen were quite different.\nClear Though sometimes compared with her, Emily Dickinson\nwas quite different from Jane Austen.\nSentences that report what someone said, using verbs such as said or told, often require direct\nrather than indirect quotation:\nConfusing Juliet Noble told Ann Torre that she was next in line for the job.\nClear Juliet Noble told Ann Torre, ―I am next in line for the job.‖\nClear Juliet Noble told Ann Torre, ―You are next in line for the job.‖\nNote Avoid the awkward device of using a pronoun followed by the appropriate noun in\nparentheses, as in the following example:\nWeak Noble and Torre had both hoped for the job, so she (Noble)\nwas disappointed.\nImproved Noble was disappointed because she and Torre had both hoped for the job.\n\nFOWLcp19-20.doc - 2\nPlace a pronoun close enough to its antecedent to\nensure clarity.\nA clause beginning who, which, or that generally should fall immediately after the word to\nwhich it refers:\nConfusing Jody found a lamp in the attic that her aunt had used.\nClear In the attic Jody found a lamp that her aunt had used.\nEven when only one word could possibly serve as the ante-\ncedent of a pronoun, the relationship between the two may still be unclear if they are widely\nseparated:\nConfusing Jane Austen had little formal education but was well educated at home. Far from living an\nisolated life in the English countryside, the Austens were a large family with a wide circle of friends who\nprovided entertainment and cultural enrichment. They also provided material for her stories.\nClear Jane Austen had little formal education but was well educated at home. Far from living an\nisolated life in the English countryside, the Austens were a large family with a wide circle of friends who\nprovided entertainment and cultural enrichment. They also provided material for Jane Austen’s stories.\nEXERCISE 19.1 Revising: Ambiguous and remote pronoun reference\nRewrite the following sentences to eliminate unclear pronoun reference. If you use a pronoun in your\nrevision, be sure that it refers to only one antecedent and that it falls close enough to its antecedent to\nensure clarity. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/\nlittlebrown.)\nExample:\nSaul found an old gun in the rotting shed that was just as his grandfather had left it.\nIn the rotting shed Saul found an old gun that was just as his grandfather had left it.\n1. There is a difference between the heroes of the twentieth century and the heroes of earlier times: they\nhave flaws in their characters.\n2. Sports fans still admire Pete Rose, Babe Ruth, and Joe Namath even though they could not be\nperfect.\n3. Fans liked Rose for having his young son serve as batboy when he was in Cincinnati.\n4. The reputation Rose earned as a gambler and tax evader may over-shadow his reputation as a\nballplayer, but it will survive.\n5. Rose amassed an unequaled record as a hitter, using his bat to do things no one else has ever done. It\nstands even though Rose was banned from baseball.\nMake a pronoun refer to a specific antecedent,\nnot an implied one.\nA pronoun should refer to a specific noun or other pronoun. The reader can only guess at the\nmeaning of a pronoun when its antecedent is implied by the context, not stated outright.\nUse this, that, which, and it cautiously.\nThe most common kind of implied reference occurs when the pronoun this, that, which, or it\nrefers to a whole idea or situation described in the preceding clause, sentence, or even paragraph.\nSuch reference, often called broad reference, is acceptable only when the pronoun refers clearly to\nthe entire preceding clause. In the following sentence, which could not possibly refer to anything\nbut the whole preceding clause:\nI can be kind and civil to people, which is more than you can.\n—George Bernard Shaw\n\nFOWLcp19-20.doc - 3\nBut if a pronoun might confuse a reader, you should avoid using it or provide an appropriate noun:\nConfusing The British knew little of the American countryside, and\nthey had no experience with the colonists’ guerilla tac-\ntics. This gave the colonists an advantage.\nClear The British knew little of the American countryside, and they had no experience with the\ncolonists’ guerrilla tactics. This ignorance and inexperience gave the colonists an advantage.\nImplied nouns are not clear antecedents.\nA noun may be implied in some other word or phrase, as happiness is implied in happy, driver\nis implied in drive, and mother is implied in mother’s. But a pronoun cannot refer clearly to an\nimplied noun, only to a specific, stated one:\nConfusing Cohen’s report brought her a lawsuit.\nClear Cohen was sued over her report.\nConfusing Her reports on psychological development generally go unnoticed outside it.\nClear Her reports on psychological development generally go unnoticed outside the field.\nTitles of papers are not clear antecedents.\nThe title of a paper is entirely separate from the paper itself, so a pronoun should not be used\nin the opening sentence of a paper to refer to the title:\nTitle How to Row a Boat\nNot This is not as easy as it looks.\nBut Rowing a boat is not as easy as it looks.\nUse it and they to refer only to definite\nantecedents.\nAlthough common in speech, using it and they to refer to indefinite antecedents is\ninappropriate in writing.\nConfusing In Chapter 4 of this book, it describes the early flights of the Wright brothers.\nClear Chapter 4 of this book describes the early flights of the Wright\nbrothers.\nConfusing Even in reality TV shows, they present a false picture of life.\nClear Even reality TV shows present a false picture of life.\nUse you only to mean “you, the reader.”\nYou should clearly mean ―you, the reader.‖ The context must be appropriate for such a\nmeaning:\nInappropriate In the fourteenth century you had to struggle simply to survive.\nRevised In the fourteenth century one [or a person] had to struggle simply to survive.\nWriters sometimes drift into you because one, a person, the individual, or a similar indefinite word\ncan be difficult to sustain. Sentence after sentence, the indefinite word may sound stuffy, and it\n\nFOWLcp19-20.doc - 4\nrequires the sexist he or the awkward he or she for pronoun-antecedent agreement (see pp. 315–\n16). To avoid these difficulties, try using plural nouns and pronouns:\nOriginal In the fourteenth century one had to struggle simply to survive.\nRevised In the fourteenth century people had to struggle simply to survive.\nUse the pronoun it only one way in a sentence.\nWe use it idiomatically in expressions such as It is raining. We use it to postpone the subject\nin sentences such as It is true that more jobs are available to women today. And we use it as a\npersonal pronoun in sentences such as Nicole wanted the book, but she couldn’t find it. All these\nuses are standard, but two of them in the same passage can confuse the reader:\nConfusing It is true that the Constitution sets limits, but it is also flexible.\nClear The Constitution does set limits, but it is also flexible.\nUse who, which, and that for appropriate\nantecedents.\nThe relative pronouns who, which, and that commonly refer to persons, animals, or things.\nWho refers most often to persons but may also refer to animals that have names:\nDorothy is the girl who visits Oz.\nHer dog, Toto, who accompanies her, gives her courage.\nWhich refers to animals and things:\nThe Orinoco River, which is 1600 miles long, flows through Venezuela into the Atlantic Ocean.\nThat refers to animals and things and occasionally to persons when they are collective or\nanonymous:\nThe rocket that failed cost millions.\nInfants that walk need constant tending.\n(See also p. 437 for the use of which and that in nonessential and essential clauses.)\nThe possessive whose generally refers to people but may refer to animals and things to avoid\nawkward and wordy of which constructions:\nThe book whose binding broke was rare. [Compare The book of which the binding broke was rare.]\nEXERCISE 19.2 Revising: Indefinite and inappropriate pronoun reference\nMany of the pronouns in the following sentences do not refer to specific, appropriate antecedents. Revise\nthe sentences as necessary to make them clear. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/\nlittlebrown.)\nExample:\nIn Glacier National Park, they have moose, elk, and wolves.\nMoose, elk, and wolves live in Glacier National Park.\n1. ―Life begins at forty‖ is a cliché many people live by, and this may well be true.\n2. When she was forty, Pearl Buck’s novel The Good Earth won the Pulitzer Prize.\n3. Buck was a novelist which wrote primarily about China.\n4. In The Good Earth you have to struggle, but fortitude is rewarded.\n5. Buck received much critical praise and earned over $7 million, but she was very modest about it.\n6. Kenneth Kaunda, past president of Zambia, was elected to it in 1964, at age forty.\n7. When Catherine I became empress of Russia at age forty, they feared more than loved her.\n8. At forty, Paul Revere made his famous ride to warn American revolutionary leaders that the British\nwere going to arrest them. This gave the colonists time to prepare for battle.\n\nFOWLcp19-20.doc - 5\n9. In the British House of Commons they did not welcome forty-year-old Nancy Astor as the first\nfemale member when she entered in 1919.\n10. In 610 CE, Muhammad, age forty, began to have a series of vi-sions that became the foundation of\nthe Muslim faith. Since then, millions of people have become one.\nEXERCISE 19.3 Revising: Pronoun reference\nRevise the following paragraph so that each pronoun refers clearly to a single specific and appropriate\nantecedent. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nIn Charlotte Brontë’s Jane Eyre, she is a shy young woman that takes a job as governess. Her\nemployer is a rude, brooding man named Rochester. He lives in a mysterious mansion on the English\nmoors, which contributes an eerie quality to Jane’s experience. Eerier still are the fires, strange noises,\nand other unexplained happenings in the house; but Rochester refuses to discuss this. Eventually, they fall\nin love. On the day they are to be married, however, she learns that he has a wife hidden in the house. She\nis hopelessly insane and violent and must be guarded at all times, which explains his strange behavior.\nHeartbroken, Jane leaves the moors, and many years pass before they are reunited.\nNote See page 381 for an exercise involving unclear pronoun reference along with sentence\nfragments, comma splices, and other sentence errors.\n20\nCHAPTER\nShifts\nInconsistencies in grammatical elements will confuse your read-ers and distort your meaning.\nIn the following passage from a first draft, the underlining indicates confusing inconsistencies in\nverbs, nouns, and pronouns:\nFirst draft\nA bank commonly owes more to its customers than is held in reserve. They kept enough assets to meet\nreasonable withdrawals, but panicked customers may demand all their deposits. Then demands will\nexceed supplies, and banks failed. These days, a person’s losses are not likely to be great because the\ngovernment insures your deposits.\nRevised\nA bank commonly owes more to its customers\nthan it holds in reserve. It keeps enough assets to meet reasonable withdrawals,\nbut panicked customers may demand all their deposits. Then demands will\nexceed supplies, and the bank will fail. These days, the losses of customers are\nnot likely to be great because the government insures their deposits.\nShifts like those in the first draft are likely to occur while you are trying to piece together\nmeaning during drafting. But during editing you should make your sentences consistent in\ngrammatical elements.\nNote Grammar and style checkers cannot recognize most shifts in sentences. Proofread your\nwork on your own, looking carefully for inconsistencies.\nKeep a sentence or related sentences consistent\n\nFOWLcp19-20.doc - 6\nin person and number.\nPerson in grammar refers to the distinction among the person talking (first person), the person\nspoken to (second person), and the person, object, or concept being talked about (third person).\nNum-ber refers to the distinction between one (singular) and more than one (plural).\nShifts in person\nMost shifts in person occur because we can refer to people in general, including our readers,\neither in the third person (a person, one; people, they) or in the second person (you):\nPeople should not drive when they have been drinking.\nOne should not drive when he or she has been drinking.\nYou should not drive when you have been drinking.\nAlthough any one of these possibilities is acceptable in an appropriate context, a mixture of\nthem is inconsistent:\nInconsistent If a person works hard, you can gain satisfaction.\nRevised If you work hard, you can gain satisfaction.\nRevised If a person works hard, he or she can gain satisfaction.\nBetter If people work hard, they can gain satisfaction.\nShifts in number\nInconsistency in number occurs most often between a pronoun and its antecedent (see p. 313):\nInconsistent If a student does not understand a problem, they should consult the instructor.\nRevised If a student does not understand a problem, he or she should consult the instructor.\nBetter If students do not understand a problem, they should consult the instructor.\nOr A student who does not understand a problem should consult the instructor.\nNote Generic nouns and most indefinite pronouns take singular pronouns with a definite\ngender: he, she, or it. When we use a generic noun like student or person or an indefinite pronoun\nlike everyone or each, we often mean to include both males and females. To indicate this meaning,\nuse he or she rather than he (as in the first of the preceding revisions) or, better still, rewrite in the\nplural or rewrite to avoid the pronoun (as in the second and third of the revisions). See page 316 for\nmore discussion and examples.\nInconsistency in number can also occur between other words (usually nouns) that relate to\neach other in meaning.\nInconsistent All the boys have a good reputation.\nRevised All the boys have good reputations.\nThe consistency in the revised sentence is called logical agreement because the nouns are\nconsistent (the boys have reputations, not a single reputation).\nEXERCISE 20.1 Revising: Shifts in person and number\nRevise the following sentences to make them consistent in person and\nnumber. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nA plumber will fix burst pipes, but they won’t repair waterlogged appliances.\nPlumbers will fix burst pipes, but they won’t repair waterlogged appliances.\n1. When a taxpayer is waiting to receive a tax refund from the Internal Revenue Service, you begin to\nnotice what time the mail carrier arrives.\n2. If the taxpayer does not receive a refund check within six weeks of filing a return, they may not have\nfollowed the rules of the IRS.\n3. If a taxpayer does not include a Social Security number on a re-turn, you will have to wait for a\nrefund.\n4. When taxpayers do not file their return early, they will not get a refund quickly.\n\nFOWLcp19-20.doc - 7\n5. If one makes errors on the tax form, they might even be audited, thereby delaying a refund even\nlonger.\nKeep a sentence or related sentences consistent\nin tense and mood.\nShifts in tense\nWithin a sentence or from one sentence to another, certain changes in tense may be required to\nindicate changes in actual or relative time (see p. 296). The following changes are necessary:\nRamon will graduate from college thirty-one years after his father arrived in the United States.\nBut changes that are not required by meaning distract readers. Unnecessary shifts between past and\npresent in passages narrating a series of events are particularly confusing:\nInconsistent Immediately after Booth shot Lincoln, Major Rathbone threw himself upon the assassin.\nBut Booth pulls a knife and plunges it into the major’s arm.\nRevised Immediately after Booth shot Lincoln, Major Rathbone threw himself upon the assassin.\nBut Booth pulled a knife and plunged it into the major’s arm.\nUse the present tense consistently to describe what an author has written, including the action\nin literature or a film:\nInconsistent The main character in the novel suffers psychologically because he has a clubfoot, but he\neventually triumphed over his disability.\nRevised The main character in the novel suffers psychologically because he has a clubfoot, but he\neventually triumphs over his disability.\nShifts in mood\nShifts in the mood of verbs occur most frequently in directions when the writer moves\nbetween the imperative mood (Unplug the appliance) and the indicative mood (You should unplug\nthe appliance). (See p. 299.) Directions are usually clearer and more concise in the imperative, as\nlong as its use is consistent:\nInconsistent Cook the mixture slowly, and you should stir it until the sugar is dissolved.\nRevised Cook the mixture slowly, and stir it until the sugar is dissolved.\nEXERCISE 20.2 REVISING: SHIFTS IN TENSE AND MOOD\nRevise the following sentences to make them consistent in tense and mood. (You can do this exercise\nonline at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nLynn ran to first, rounded the base, and keeps running until she slides into second.\nLynn ran to first, rounded the base, and kept running until she slid into second.\n1. When your cholesterol count is too high, adjusting your diet and exercise level reduced it.\n2. After you lowered your cholesterol rate, you decrease the chances of heart attack and stroke.\n3. First eliminate saturated fats from your diet; then you should consume more whole grains and raw\nvegetables.\n4. To avoid saturated fats, substitute turkey and chicken for beef, and you should use cholesterol-free\nsalad dressing and cooking oil.\n5. A regular program of aerobic exercise, such as walking or swimming, improves your cholesterol rate\nand made you feel much healthier.\n\nFOWLcp19-20.doc - 8\nKeep a sentence or related sentences consistent\nin subject and voice.\nWhen a verb is in the active voice, the subject names the actor: Linda passed the peas. When\na verb is in the passive voice, the subject names the receiver of the action: The peas were passed\n[by Linda]. (See pp. 302–03.)\nA shift in voice may sometimes help focus the reader’s attention on a single subject, as in The\ncandidate campaigned vigorously and was nominated on the first ballot. However, most shifts in\nvoice also involve shifts in subject. They are unnecessary and confusing.\nInconsistent Internet newsgroups cover an enormous range of topics for discussion. Forums for\nmeeting people with like interests are provided in these groups.\nRevised Internet newsgroups cover an enormous range of topics for discussion and provide\nforums for meeting people with like interests.\nEXERCISE 20.3 Revising: Shifts in subject and voice\nMake the following sentences consistent in subject and voice. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nAt the reunion they ate hot dogs and volleyball was played.\nAt the reunion they ate hot dogs and played volleyball.\n1. If students learn how to study efficiently, much better grades will be made on tests.\n2. Conscientious students begin to prepare for tests immediately after the first class is attended.\n3. Before each class all reading assignments are completed, and the students outline the material and\nanswer any study questions.\n4. In class they listen carefully and good notes are taken.\n5. Questions are asked by the students when they do not understand the professor.\nKeep a quotation or a question consistently\ndirect or indirect.\nDirect quotations or questions report the exact words of a quotation or question:\n―I am the greatest,‖ bragged Muhammad Ali.\nIn his day few people asked, ―Is he right?‖\nIndirect quotations or questions report that someone said or asked something, but not in the exact words:\nMuhammad Ali bragged that he was the greatest.\nIn his day few people asked whether he was right.\nShifts between direct and indirect quotations or questions are difficult to follow.\nShift in quotation Kapek reported that the rats avoided the maze and ―as of this writing, none\nresponds to conditioning.‖\nRevised (indirect) Kapek reported that the rats avoided the maze and that as of his writing none\nresponded to conditioning.\nRevised (direct) Kapek reported, ―The rats avoid the maze. As of this writing, none responds to\nconditioning.‖\nShift in question The reader wonders whether the experiment failed or did it perhaps succeed?\nRevised (indirect) The reader wonders whether the experiment failed or whether it perhaps\nsucceeded.\nRevised (direct) Did the experiment fail? Or did it perhaps suc-ceed?\nFor more on quotations, see pages 444–46 (commas with signal phrases such as she said),\n469–75 (quotation marks), and 623–28 (integrating quotations into your writing). For more on\nquestions, see pages 427–28.\n\nFOWLcp19-20.doc - 9\nEXERCISE 20.4 Revising: Shifts in direct and indirect\nquotations and questions\nRevise each of the following sentences twice, once to make it consistently direct, once to make it\nconsistently indirect. You will have to guess at the exact wording of direct quotations and questions that\nare now stated indirectly. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nWe all wonder what the next decade will bring and will we thrive or not?\nDirect: What will the next decade bring? Will we thrive or not?\nIndirect: We all wonder what the next decade will bring and whether we will thrive or not.\n1. One anthropologist says that the functions of marriage have changed and ―nowhere more\ndramatically than in industrialized cultures.‖\n2. The question even arises of whether siblings may marry and would the union be immoral?\n3. The author points out, ―Sibling marriage is still illegal everywhere in the United States‖ and that\npeople are still prosecuted under the law.\n4. She says that incest could be considered a universal taboo and ―the questions asked about the taboo\nvary widely.‖\n5. Some ask is the taboo a way of protecting the family or whether it may be instinctive.\nEXERCISE 20.5 Revising: Shifts\nRevise the following paragraph to eliminate unnecessary shifts in person, number, tense, mood, and\nvoice. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nDriving in snow need not be dangerous if you practice a few rules. First, one should avoid fast\nstarts, which prevent the wheels from gaining traction and may result in the car’s getting stuck. Second,\ndrive more slowly than usual, and you should pay attention to the feel of the car: if the steering seemed\nunusually loose or the wheels did not seem to be grabbing the road, slow down. Third, avoid fast stops,\nwhich lead to skids. One should be alert for other cars and intersections that may necessitate that the\nbrakes be applied suddenly. If you need to slow down, the car’s momentum can be reduced by\ndownshifting as well as by applying the brakes. When braking, press the pedal to the floor only if you\nhave antilock brakes; otherwise, the pedal should be pumped in short bursts. If you feel the car skidding,\nthe brakes should be released and the wheel should be turned into the direction of the skid, and then the\nbrakes should be pressed or pumped again. If one repeated these motions, the skid would be stopped and\nthe speed of the car would be reduced.\nNote See page 381 for an exercise involving shifts along with sentence fragments, comma\nsplices, and other sentence errors.\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp19-20.doc - 10\nVisit the companion Web site for more help and additional exercises on\npronoun reference.\nClear reference\nPrincipal causes of unclear pronoun reference\nMore than one possible antecedent (facing page and below):\nConfusing\nTo keep birds from eating seeds, soak\nthem in blue food coloring.\nClear To keep birds from eating seeds, soak the seeds in blue\nfood coloring.\nAntecedent too far away (p. 352):\nConfusing Employees should consult with their supervisor who require\npersonal time.\nClear Employees who require personal time should consult with\ntheir supervisor.\nAntecedent only implied (p. 353):\nConfusing Many children begin reading on their own by watching\ntelevision, but this should probably be discounted in government\npolicy.\nClear Many children begin reading on their own by watching\ntelevision, but such self-instruction should probably be discounted\nin government policy.\nSee also pages 354–56.\nMatch bank . . . owes\nMatches bank\nMatches will fail\nMatches customers\nMatches customers\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp19-20.doc - 11\nVisit the companion Web site for more help and additional exercises on shifts.\n\nFOWLcp21.doc - 1\n21\nCHAPTER\nMisplaced and Dangling Modifiers\nIn reading a sentence in English, we depend principally on the arrangement of the words to\ntell us how they are related. In writing, we may create confusion if we fail to connect modifiers to\nthe words they modify.\nNote Grammar and style checkers cannot recognize most problems with modifiers. For\ninstance, a checker failed to flag the misplaced modifiers in Gasoline high prices affect usually car\nsales or the dangling modifier in The vandalism was visible passing the building.\nMisplaced Modifiers\nA modifier is misplaced if readers can’t easily relate it to the word it modifies. Misplaced\nmodifiers may be awkward, confusing, or even unintentionally funny.\nPlace modifiers where they will clearly modify the\nwords intended.\nReaders tend to link a modifying word, phrase, or clause to the nearest word it could modify: I\nsaw a man in a green hat. Thus the writer must place the phrase so that it clearly modifies the\nintended word and not some other.\nConfusing He served steak to the men on paper plates.\nRevised He served the men steak on paper plates.\nConfusing Many dogs are killed by automobiles and trucks roaming\nunleashed.\nRevised Many dogs roaming unleashed are killed by automobiles and trucks.\nConfusing This is the only chocolate chip cookie in a bag that tastes\nlike Mom’s. [Actual advertisement.]\nRevised This is the only bagged [or packaged] chocolate chip\ncookie that tastes like Mom’s.\nEXERCISE 21.1 Revising: Misplaced phrases and clauses\nRevise the following sentences so that phrases and clauses clearly modify the appropriate words. (You\ncan do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nI came to enjoy flying over time.\nOver time I came to enjoy flying.\n1. Women have contributed much to knowledge and culture of great value.\n2. Emma Willard founded the Troy Female Seminary, the first institution to provide a college-level\neducation for women in 1821.\n3. Sixteen years later Mary Lyon founded Mount Holyoke Female Seminary, the first true women’s\ncollege with directors and a campus who would sustain the college even after Lyon’s death.\n\nFOWLcp21.doc - 2\n4. Una was the first US newspaper, which was founded by Pauline Wright Davis in 1853, that was\ndedicated to gaining women’s rights.\n5. Mitchell’s Comet was discovered in 1847, which was named for Maria Mitchell.\nPlace limiting modifiers carefully.\nLimiting modifiers include almost, even, exactly, hardly, just, merely, nearly, only, scarcely,\nand simply. In speech these modifiers often occur before the verb, regardless of the words they are\nintended to modify. In writing, however, these modifiers should fall immediately before the word\nor word group they modify to avoid any ambiguity:\nUnclear She only found that fossil on her last dig.\nRevised She found only that fossil on her last dig.\nRevised She found that fossil only on her last dig.\nEXERCISE 21.2 Using limiting modifiers\nUse each of the following limiting modifiers in two versions of the same sentence. (You can do this\nexercise online at ablongman.com/\nlittlebrown.)\nExample:\nonly\nHe is the only one I like. He is the one only I like.\n1. almost 3. hardly 5. nearly\n2. even 4. simply\nMake each modifier refer to only one grammatical\nelement.\nA modifier can modify only one element in a sentence—the subject, the verb, or some other\nelement. A squinting modifier seems confusingly to refer to either of two words:\nSquinting Snipers who fired on the soldiers often escaped capture.\nClear Snipers who often fired on the soldiers escaped capture.\nClear Snipers who fired on the soldiers escaped capture often.\nWhen an adverb modifies an entire main clause, as in the last example, it can usually be moved to\nthe beginning of the sentence: Often, snipers who fired on the soldiers escaped capture.\nEXERCISE 21.3 Revising: Squinting modifiers\nRevise each sentence twice so that the squinting modifier applies clearly first to one element and then to\nthe other. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nThe work that he hoped would satisfy him completely frustrated him.\nThe work that he hoped would completely satisfy him frustrated him.\nThe work that he hoped would satisfy him frustrated him completely.\n1. People who sunbathe often can damage their skin.\n2. Sunbathers who apply a sunscreen frequently block some of the sun’s harmful ultraviolet rays.\n3. Men and women who lie out in the sun often have leathery, dry skin.\n4. Doctors tell sunbathers when they are older they risk skin cancer.\n5. People who stay out of the sun usually will have better skin and fewer chances of skin cancer.\n\nFOWLcp21.doc - 3\nKeep subjects, verbs, and objects together.\nEnglish sentences tend to move from subject to verb to object. The movement is so familiar\nthat modifiers between these elements can be awkward.\nA subject and verb may be separated by an adjective that modifies the subject: Kuwait, which\nhas a population of 1.3 million, is a rich nation. But an adverb of more than a word usually stops\nthe flow of the sentence:\nsubject adverb verb\nAwkward Kuwait, after the first Gulf War ended in 1991, began re-turning to normal.\nadverb subject verb\nRevised After the first Gulf War ended in 1991, Kuwait began re-turning to normal.\nEven a one-word adverb will be awkward between a verb and its object:\nverb adverb object\nAwkward The war had damaged badly many of Kuwait’s oil fields.\nverbobject\nRevised The war had badly dam aged many of Kuwait’s oil fields.\nadverb\nKeep parts of infinitives or verb phrases together.\nAn infinitive consists of the marker to plus the plain form of a verb: to produce, to enjoy. The\ntwo parts of the infinitive are widely regarded as a grammatical unit that should not be split:\ninfinitive\nAwkward The weather service expected temperatures to not rise.\ninfinitive\nRevised The weather service expected temperatures not to rise.\nA split infinitive may sometimes be natural and preferable, though it may still bother some\nreaders:\ninfinitive\nSeveral US industries expect to more than triple their use of robots.\nHere the split infinitive is more economical than the alternatives, such as Several US industries\nexpect to increase their use of robots by more than three times.\nA verb phrase consists of a helping verb plus a main verb, as in will call, was going, had\nbeen writing (see p. 277). A single-word adverb may be inserted after the helping verb in a verb\nphrase (or the first helping verb if more than one): Scientists have lately been using spacecraft to\nstudy the sun. But when longer adverbs interrupt verb phrases, the result is almost always awkward:\nhelping\nverb adverb\nAwkward The spacecraft Ulysses will after traveling close to the sun\nmain verb\nreport on the sun’s energy fields.\nadverb\nRevised After traveling close to the sun, the spacecraft Ulysses\nverb phrase\nwill report on the sun’s energy fields.\nIn an English question, place a one-word adverb after the first helping verb and the subject:\nhelping rest of\nverb subject adverb verb phrase\nWill spacecraft ever be able to leave the solar system?\nEXERCISE 21.4 Revising: Separated sentence parts\nRevise the following sentences to connect separated parts (subject-predicate, verb-object, verb phrase,\ninfinitive). (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nMost children have by the time they are seven lost a tooth.\nBy the time they are seven, most children have lost a tooth.\n\nFOWLcp21.doc - 4\n1. Myra Bradwell founded in 1868 the Chicago Legal News.\n2. Bradwell was later denied, although she had qualified, admission to the Illinois Bar Association.\n3. In an attempt to finally gain admission to the bar, she carried the case to the Supreme Court, but the\njustices decided against her.\n4. Bradwell was determined that no other woman would, if she were qualified, be denied entrance to a\nprofession.\n5. The Illinois legislature finally passed, in response to Bradwell’s persuasion, a bill ensuring that no\none on the basis of gender would be restricted from a profession.\nPosition adverbs with care.\nA few adverbs are subject to conventions that can trouble non-native speakers of English.\nAdverbs of frequency\nAdverbs of frequency include always, never, often, rarely, seldom, sometimes, and usually.\nThey appear at the beginning of a sentence, before a one-word verb, or after the helping verb in a\nverb phrase:\nverb phrase adverb\nAwkward Robots have put sometimes humans out of work.\nhelping main\nverb adverb verb\nRevised Robots have sometimes put humans out of work.\nadverb verb phrase\nRevised Sometimes robots have put humans out of work.\nAdverbs of frequency always follow the verb be:\nadverb verb\nAwkward Robots often are helpful to workers.\nverb adverb\nRevised Robots are often helpful to workers.\nAdverbs of degree\nAdverbs of degree include absolutely, almost, certainly, completely, especially, extremely,\nhardly, and only. They fall just before the word modified (an adjective, another adverb, sometimes\na verb).\nadjective adverb\nAwkward Robots have been useful especially in making cars.\nadverb adjective\nRevised Robots have been especially useful in making cars.\nAdverbs of manner\nAdverbs of manner include badly, beautifully, openly, sweetly, tightly, well, and others that\ndescribe how something is done. They usually fall after the verb:\nadverb verb\nAwkward Robots smoothly work on assembly lines.\nverb adverb\nRevised Robots work smoothly on assembly lines.\nThe adverb not\nWhen the adverb not modifies a verb, place it after the helping verb (or the first helping verb\nif more than one):\nhelping main\nverb verb\nAwkward Robots do think not.\nhelping main\nverb verb\nRevised Robots do not think.\nPlace not after a form of be: Robots are not thinkers.\n\nFOWLcp21.doc - 5\nWhen not modifies another adverb or an adjective, place it before the other modifier: Robots\nare not sleek machines.\nArrange adjectives appropriately.\nEnglish follows distinctive rules for arranging two or three adjectives before a noun. (A string\nof more than three adjectives before a noun is rare.) The order depends on the meaning of the\nadjectives, as indicated in the following table:\nmany state laws\nstriking green Thai birds\na fine German camera\nthis square wooden table\nall business reports\nthe blue litmus paper\nSee page 442 for guidelines on punctuating two or more adjectives before a noun.\nEXERCISE 21.5 Revising: Placement of adverbs and adjectives\nRevise the sentences below to correct the positions of adverbs or adjectives. If a sentence is already\ncorrect as given, circle the number preceding it. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman\n.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nGasoline high prices affect usually car sales.\nHigh gasoline prices usually affect car sales.\n1. Some years ago Detroit cars often were praised.\n2. Luxury large cars especially were prized.\n3. Then a serious oil shortage led drivers to value small foreign cars that got good mileage.\n4. When gasoline ample supplies returned, consumers bought again American large cars.\n5. However, the large cars not were luxury sedans but vans and sport-utility vehicles.\nDangling Modifiers\nRelate dangling modifiers to their sentences.\nA dangling modifier does not sensibly modify anything in its sentence:\nDangling Passing the building, the vandalism became visible.\nDangling modifiers usually introduce sentences, contain a verb form, and imply but do not\nname a subject: in the preceding example, the implied subject is the someone or something passing\nthe building. Readers assume that this implied subject is the same as the subject of the sentence\n(vandalism in the example). When it is not, the modifier ―dangles‖ unconnected to the rest of the\nsentence.\nCertain modifiers are the most likely to dangle:\nParticipial phrases:\nDangling Passing the building, the vandalism became visible.\nRevised As we passed the building, the vandalism became visible.\nInfinitive phrases:\nDangling To understand the causes, vandalism has been extensively investigated.\nRevised To understand the causes, researchers have extensively investigated vandalism.\nPrepositional phrases in which the object of the preposition is a gerund:\n\nFOWLcp21.doc - 6\nDangling After studying the problem, vandals are now thought to share certain characteristics.\nRevised After studying the problem, researchers think that vandals share certain characteristics.\nElliptical clauses in which the subject and perhaps the verb are omitted:\nDangling When destructive, researchers have learned that vandals are more likely to be in groups.\nRevised When vandals are destructive, researchers have learned, they are more likely to be in groups.\nDangling modifiers are especially likely when the verb in the main clause is in the passive\nvoice instead of the active voice, as in vandalism has been investigated and vandals are thought.\n(See pp. 302–03 for more on the passive voice.)\nNote A modifier may be dangling even when the sentence elsewhere contains a word the\nmodifier might seem to describe, such as vandals below:\nDangling When destructive, researchers have learned that vandals are more likely to be in groups.\nIn addition, a dangling modifier may fall at the end of a sentence:\nDangling The vandalism was visible passing the building.\nRevising dangling modifiers\nRevise most dangling modifiers in one of two ways, depending on what you want to\nemphasize in the sentence.\nChange the subject of the main clause to a word the modifier properly describes:\nDangling To express themselves, graffiti decorate walls.\nRevised To express themselves, some youths decorate walls with graffiti.\nRewrite the dangling modifier as a complete clause with its own stated subject and verb:\nRevised Because some youths need to express themselves, graffiti decorate walls.\nEXERCISE 21.6 Revising: Dangling modifiers\nRevise the following sentences to eliminate any dangling modifiers. Each item has more than one possible\nanswer. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nDriving north, the vegetation became increasingly sparse.\nDriving north, we noticed that the vegetation became increasingly sparse.\nAs we drove north, the vegetation became increasingly sparse.\n1. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nBob demanded a hearing from the faculty.
