(You can do this\nexercise online at ablongman.com/\nlittlebrown.)\nCentral American tungara frogs silence several nights a week their mating croaks. When not\ncroaking, the chance that the frogs will be eaten by predators is reduced. The frogs seem to fully believe\nin ―safety in numbers.‖ They more than likely will croak along with a large group rather than by\nthemselves. By forgoing croaking on some nights, the frogs’ behavior prevents the species from\n―croaking.‖\nNote See page 381 for an exercise involving misplaced and dangling modifiers along with\nother sentence errors.\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp21.doc - 8\nVisit the companion Web site for more help and additional exercises on\nmisplaced and dangling modifiers.\nNoun used as\nadjective\nDeterminer\nOpinion\nSize or\nshape\nColor\nOrigin\nMaterial\nNoun\nIdentifying and revising dangling modifiers\nIdentify the modifier’s subject. If the modifier lacks a stated subject (as when in diapers does),\nidentify what the modifier describes.\nCompare the subject of the modifier and the subject of the sentence. Verify that what the modifier\ndescribes is in fact the subject of the main clause. If it is not, the modifier probably dangles.\nRevise as needed. Either (a) recast the dangling modifier with a stated subject of its own, or (b)\nchange the subject of the main clause to be what the modifier describes.\nmodifier subject\nDangling When in diapers, my mother remarried.\nRevision a When I was in diapers, my mother remarried.\nRevision b When in diapers, I attended my mother’s second wedding.\n\nFOWLcp22.doc - 1\n22\nCHAPTER\nMixed and Incomplete Sentences\nMixed Sentences\nA mixed sentence contains two or more parts that are incompatible—that is, the parts do not\nfit together. The misfit may be in grammar or in meaning.\nNote Grammar and style checkers may recognize a simple mixed construction such as reason\nis because, but they will fail to flag most mixed sentences.\nUntangle sentences that are mixed in grammar.\nSentences mixed in grammar combine two or more incompatible grammatical structures.\nMake sure subject and verb fit together grammatically.\nA mixed sentence may occur when you start a sentence with one plan and end it with another:\nmodifier (prepositional phrase) verb\nMixed By paying more attention to impressions than facts leads us to misjudge others.\nmodifier (prepositional phrase) subject +\nRevised By paying more attention to impressions than facts, we mis-\nverb\njudge others.\nsubject (gerund phrase) verb\nRevised Paying more attention to impressions than facts leads us to misjudge others.\nsubject modifier (adjective clause)\nMixed The fact that someone may be considered guilty just for asso-\nciating with someone guilty.\nsubject verb\nRevised The fact is that someone may be considered guilty just for associating with someone guilty.\nsubject verb\nRevised Someone may be considered guilty just for associating with someone guilty.\nIn some mixed sentences the grammar is so jumbled that the writer has little choice but to start\nover:\nMixed My long-range goal is through law school and government work I hope to help people deal\nwith those problems we all deal with more effectively.\nPossible My long-range goal is to go to law school and then work in government so that I can help\npeople deal more effectively with problems we all face.\nMixed sentences are especially likely on a computer when you connect parts of two sentences\nor rewrite half a sentence but not the other half. Mixed sentences may also occur when you don’t\nfocus your sentences on the subject and verb so that these elements carry the principal meaning.\n(See pp. 384–86.) If you need help identifying the subject and verb, see pages 233–36.\n\nFOWLcp22.doc - 2\nState parts of sentences, such as subjects,\nonly once.\nIn some languages other than English, certain parts of sentences may be repeated. These\ninclude the subject in any kind of clause or an object or adverb in an adjective clause. In English,\nhowever, these parts are stated only once in a clause.\nRepetition of subject\nYou may be tempted to restate a subject as a pronoun before the verb. But the subject needs\nstating only once in its clause.\nFaulty The liquid it reached a temperature of 180°F.\nRevised The liquid reached a temperature of 180°F.\nFaulty Gases in the liquid they escaped.\nRevised Gases in the liquid escaped.\nRepetition in an adjective clause\nAdjective clauses begin with who, whom, whose, which, that, where, and when (see p. 254).\nThe beginning word replaces another word: the subject (He is the person who called), an object of\na verb or preposition (He is the person whom I mentioned), or a preposition and pronoun (He\nknows the office where [in which] the conference will occur).\nDo not state the word that who, whom, and so on replace in an adjective clause:\nFaulty The technician whom the test depended on her was burned. [Whom should replace her.]\nRevised The technician whom the test depended on was burned.\nAdjective clauses beginning with where or when do not need an adverb such as there or then:\nFaulty Gases escaped at a moment when the technician was un-prepared then.\nRevised Gases escaped at a moment when the technician was un-prepared.\nMatch subjects and predicates in meaning.\nIn a sentence with mixed meaning, the subject is said to be or do something it cannot logically\nbe or do. Such a mixture is sometimes called faulty predication because the predicate conflicts\nwith the subject.\nIllogical equation with be\nWhen a form of be connects a subject and a word that describes the subject (a complement),\nthe subject and complement must be logically related:\nMixed A compromise between the city and the country would be the ideal place to live.\nRevised A community that offered the best qualities of both city and country would be the ideal place\nto live.\nIs when, is where\nDefinitions require nouns on both sides of be. Definition clauses beginning with when or\nwhere are common in speech but should be avoided in writing:\nMixed An examination is when you are tested on what you know.\nRevised An examination is a test of what you know.\nReason is because\nThe commonly heard construction reason is because is redundant since because means ―for\nthe reason that‖:\nMixed The reason the temple requests donations is because the school needs expansion.\n\nFOWLcp22.doc - 3\nRevised The reason the temple requests donations is that the school needs expansion.\nRevised The temple requests donations because the school needs ex-pansion.\nOther mixed meanings\nMismatched subjects and predicates are not confined to sentences with be:\nMixed The use of emission controls was created to reduce air pollution.\nRevised Emission controls were created to reduce air pollution.\nEXERCISE 22.1 Revising: Sentences mixed in grammar or meaning\nRevise the following sentences so that their parts fit together both in grammar and in meaning. Each item\nhas more than one possible answer. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nWhen they found out how expensive pianos are is why they were discouraged.\nThey were discouraged because they found out how expensive pianos are.\nWhen they found out how expensive pianos are, they were discouraged.\n1. A hurricane is when the winds in a tropical depression rotate counterclockwise at more than seventy-\nfour miles per hour.\n2. Because hurricanes can destroy so many lives and so much property is why people fear them.\n3. Through high winds, storm surge, floods, and tornadoes is how a hurricane can kill\nthousands of people.\n4. Many scientists observe that hurricanes in recent years they have become more ferocious and\ndestructive.\n5. However, in the last half-century, with improved communications systems and weather satellites\nhave made hurricanes less deadly.\nEXERCISE 22.2 Revising: Repeated sentence parts\nRevise the following sentences to eliminate any unnecessary repetition of sentence parts. (You can do\nthis exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nOver 79 percent of Americans they have heard of global warming.\nOver 79 percent of Americans have heard of global warming.\n1. Global warming it is caused by the gradual erosion of the ozone layer that protects the earth from the\nsun.\n2. Scientists who study this problem they say that the primary causes of erosion are the use of fossil\nfuels and the reduction of forests.\n3. Many nonscientists they mistakenly believe that aerosol spray cans are the primary cause of erosion.\n4. One scientist whom others respect him argues that Americans have effectively reduced their use of\naerosol sprays.\n5. He argues that we will stop global warming only when the public learns the real causes then.\nIncomplete Sentences\nThe most serious kind of incomplete sentence is the fragment (see Chapter 17). But sentences\nare also incomplete when they omit one or more words needed for clarity.\nNote Grammar and style checkers will not flag most kinds of incomplete sentences discussed\nin this section.\n\nFOWLcp22.doc - 4\nOmissions from compound constructions should\nbe consistent with grammar or idiom.\nIn both speech and writing, we commonly omit words not necessary for meaning, such as\nthose in brackets in the following examples. Notice that all the sentences contain compound\nconstructions (see p. 258):\nBy 2010 automobile-emission standards will be tougher, and by 2015 [automobile-emission standards\nwill be] tougher still.\nSome cars will run on electricity and some [will run] on methane or another alternative fuel.\nEnvironmentalists have hopes for alternative fuels and [for] public trans-portation.\nSuch omissions are possible only when you omit words that are common to all the parts of a\ncompound construction. When the parts differ in either grammar or idiom, all words must be\nincluded in all parts:\nOne new car gets eighty miles per gallon of gasoline; some old cars get as little as five miles per gallon.\n[One verb is singular, the other plural.]\nEnvironmentalists were invited to submit proposals and were eager to do so. [Each were has a different\ngrammatical function: the first is a helping verb; the second is a linking verb.]\nThey believe in and work for fuel conservation. [Idiom requires different prepositions with believe and\nwork.]\nIn the sentence My brother and friend moved to Dallas, the omission of my before friend\nindicates that brother and friend are the same person. If two different persons are meant, the\nmodifier or article must be repeated: My brother and my friend moved to Dallas.\n(See pp. 523–25 for a list of English idioms with prepositions and pp. 405–08 for a discussion\nof grammatical parallelism.)\nAll comparisons should be complete and logical.\nComparisons make statements about the relation between two or more things, as in Dogs are\nmore intelligent than cats.\nState a comparison fully enough to ensure clarity.\nUnclear Automakers worry about their industry more than environmentalists.\nClear Automakers worry about their industry more than environmentalists do.\nClear Automakers worry about their industry more than they worry about environmentalists.\nThe items being compared should in fact be comparable.\nIllogical The cost of an electric car is greater than a gasoline-\npowered car. [Illogically compares a cost and a car.]\nRevised The cost of an electric car is greater than the cost of [or that of] a gasoline-powered car.\nUse any or any other appropriately in comparisons.\nComparing a person or thing with all others in the same group creates two units: (1) the\nindividual person or thing and (2) all other persons or things in the group. The two units need to be\ndistinguished:\nIllogical Los Angeles is larger than any city in California. [Since Los Angeles is itself a city in\nCalifornia, the sentence seems to say that Los Angeles is larger than itself.]\nLogical Los Angeles is larger than any other city in California.\nComparing a person or thing with the members of a different group assumes separate units to\nbegin with. The two units do not need to be distinguished with other:\n\nFOWLcp22.doc - 5\nIllogical Los Angeles is larger than any other city in Canada. [The cities in Canada constitute a group\nto which Los Angeles does not belong.]\nLogical Los Angeles is larger than any city in Canada.\nComparisons should state what is being compared.\nBrand X gets clothes whiter. [Whiter than what?]\nBrand Y is so much better. [Better than what?]\nInclude all needed prepositions, articles,\nand other words.\nIn haste or carelessness we sometimes omit small words such as articles and prepositions that\nare needed for clarity:\nIncomplete Regular payroll deductions are a type painless savings. You hardly notice missing\namounts, and after period of years the contributions can add a large total.\nRevised Regular payroll deductions are a type of painless savings. You hardly notice the missing\namounts, and after a period of years the contributions can add up to a large total.\nBe careful not to omit that when the omission is confusing:\nIncomplete The personnel director expects many employees will benefit from the plan. [Many\nemployees seems to be the object of expects.]\nRevised The personnel director expects that many employees will benefit from the plan.\nAttentive proofreading is the best insurance against the kinds of omissions described in this\nsection. Proofread all your papers carefully. See page 63 for tips.\nIf your native language or dialect is not standard American English, you may have difficulty\nknowing when to use the English articles a, an, and the. For guidelines on using articles, see pages\n326–30.\nEXERCISE 22.3 REVISING: INCOMPLETE SENTENCES\nRevise the following sentences so that they are complete, logical, and clear. Some items have more than\none possible answer. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nOur house is closer to the bank than the subway stop.\nOur house is closer to the bank than it is to the subway stop.\nOur house is closer to the bank than the subway stop is.\n1. The first ice cream, eaten in China in about 2000 BC, was more lumpy than the modern era.\n2. The Chinese made their ice cream of milk, spices, and overcooked rice and packed in snow to\nsolidify.\n3. In the fourteenth century ice milk and fruit ices appeared in Italy and the tables of the wealthy.\n4. At her wedding in 1533 to the king of France, Catherine de Médicis offered more flavors of fruit ices\nthan any hostess offered.\n5. Modern sherbets resemble her ices; modern ice cream her soft dessert of thick, sweetened cream.\nEXERCISE 22.4 Revising: Mixed and incomplete sentences\nRevise the following paragraph to eliminate mixed or incomplete constructions. (You can do this exercise\nonline at ablongman.com/\nlittlebrown.)\nThe Hancock Tower in Boston is thin mirror-glass slab that rises almost eight hundred feet. When it\nwas being constructed was when its windows began cracking, and some fell crashing to the ground. In\norder to minimize risks is why the architects and owners replaced over a third the huge windows with\nplywood until the problem could be found and solved. With its plywood sheath, the building was\nhomelier than any skyscraper, the butt of many jokes. Eventually, however, it was discovered that the\nreason the windows cracked was because joint between the double panes of glass was too rigid. The\nsolution of thicker single-pane windows was installed, and the silly plywood building crystallized into\nreflective jewel.\n\nFOWLcp22.doc - 6\nEXERCISE ON CHAPTERS 17–22 Revising: Clear sentences\nClarify meaning in the following paragraphs by revising sentence fragments, comma splices, fused\nsentences, problems with pronoun reference, awkward shifts, misplaced and dangling modifiers, and\nmixed and incomplete sentences. Most errors can be corrected in more\nthan one way. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/\nlittlebrown.)\nMany people who are physically challenged. They have accomplished much. Which proves that they\nare not ―handicapped.‖ Confined to wheelchairs, successful careers have been forged by Bob Sampson\nand Stephen Hawking. Despite Sampson’s muscular dystrophy, he has earned a law degree he has also\nworked for United Airlines for more than thirty years. Stephen Hawking most famous for his book A Brief\nHistory of Time. Unable to speak, Hawking’s voice synthesizer allows him to dictate his books and\nconduct public lectures. And teach mathematics classes at Cambridge University.\nFranklin D. Roosevelt, Ann Adams, and Itzhak Perlman all refused let polio destroy their lives.\nIndeed, Roosevelt led the United States during two of the worst periods of its history as President. The\nGreat Depression and World War II. Reassured by his strong, firm voice, Roosevelt inspired hope and\ndetermination in the American people. Ann Adams, who was talented in art before polio paralyzed her,\nknew she had to continue to be one. Having retrained herself to draw with a pencil grasped in her teeth.\nShe produces sketches of children and pets. That were turned into greeting cards. The profits from the\ncards sustained her. Roosevelt and Adams were stricken with polio when they were adults; Itzhak\nPerlman when a child. He was unable to play sports, instead he studied the violin, now many think he is\ngreater than any violinist in the world.\nLike Perlman, many physically challenged individuals turn to the arts. Perhaps the reason is because\nthe joy of artistic achievement compensates for other pleasures they cannot experience. Stevie Wonder,\nJosé Feliciano, and Andrea Bocelli all express, through their music, their souls. Although unable to see\nphysically, their music reveals truly how well they see. Hearing impairment struck Ludwig van Beethoven\nand Marlee Matlin it did not stop them from developing their talents. Already a successful composer,\nmany of Beethoven’s most powerful pieces were written after he became deaf. Similarly, Matlin has had\nexcellent acting roles in movies, plays, and television programs, indeed she won an Oscar for Children of\na Lesser God. She encourages others to develop their ability, and many hearing-impaired actors have\nbeen inspired by her.\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp22.doc - 7\nVisit the companion Web site for more help and an additional exercise on\nmixed and incomplete sentences.\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 1\n5\nPART\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 2\nEffective Sentences\n23\nCHAPTER\nEmphasizing Ideas\n24\nCHAPTER\nUsing Coordination\nand Subordination\n25\nCHAPTER\nUsing Parallelism\n26\nCHAPTER\nAchieving Variety\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 3\n23\nCHAPTER\nEmphasizing Ideas\nWhen you emphasize the main ideas in your sentences, you hold and channel readers’\nattention.\nNote Many grammar and style checkers can spot some problems with emphasis, such as nouns\nmade from verbs, passive voice, wordy phrases, and long sentences that may also be flabby and\nunemphatic. However, the checkers cannot help you identify the important ideas in your sentences\nor whether those ideas receive appropriate emphasis.\n23a Using subjects and verbs effectively\nThe heart of every sentence is its subject, which usually names the actor, and its verb, which\nusually specifies the subject’s action: Children [subject] grow [verb]. When these elements do not\nidentify the sentence’s key actor and action, readers must find that information elsewhere and the\nsentence may be wordy and unemphatic.\nIn the following sentences, the subjects and verbs are underlined.\nUnemphatic The intention of the company was to expand its workforce. A proposal was also made to\ndiversify the backgrounds and abilities of employees.\nThese sentences are unemphatic because their key ideas do not appear in their subjects and verbs.\nRevised, the sentences are not only clearer but more concise:\nRevised The company intended to expand its workforce. It also proposed to diversify the\nbackgrounds and abilities of employees.\nThe constructions below usually drain meaning from a sentence’s subject and verb.\nNouns made from verbs\nNouns made from verbs can obscure the key actions of sentences and add words. These nouns\ninclude intention (from intend), proposal (from propose), decision (from decide), expectation\n(from expect), persistence (from persist), argument (from argue), and inclu-sion (from include).\nUnemphatic After the company made a decision to hire more disabled workers, its next step was the\nconstruction of wheelchair ramps and other facilities.\nRevised After the company decided to hire more disabled workers, it next constructed wheelchair\nramps and other facilities.\nWeak verbs\nWeak verbs, such as made and was in the unemphatic sentence above, tend to stall sentences\njust where they should be moving and often bury key actions:\nUnemphatic The company is now the leader among businesses in complying with the 1990\nAmericans with Disabilities Act. Its officers make speeches on the act to business groups.\nRevised The company now leads other businesses in complying with the 1990 Americans with\nDisabilities Act. Its officers speak on the act to business groups.\nPassive voice\nVerbs in the passive voice state actions received by, not performed by,\ntheir subjects. Thus the passive de-emphasizes the true actor of the\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 4\nsentence, sometimes omitting it entirely. Generally, prefer the active voice,\nin which the subject performs the verb’s action. (See also pp. 302–03.)\nUnemphatic The 1990 law is seen by most businesses as fair, but the costs of complying have\nsometimes been exaggerated.\nRevised Most businesses see the 1990 law as fair, but some opponents have exaggerated the\ncosts of complying.\nEXERCISE 23.1 Revising: Emphasis of subjects and verbs\nRewrite the following sentences so that their subjects and verbs identify their key actors and actions. (You\ncan do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nThe issue of students making a competition over grades is a reason why their focus on learning may\nbe lost.\nStudents who compete over grades may lose their focus on learning.\n1. The work of many heroes was crucial in helping to emancipate the slaves.\n2. The contribution of Harriet Tubman, an escaped slave herself, in-cluded the guidance of hundreds of\nother slaves to freedom on the Underground Railroad.\n3. A return to slavery was risked by Tubman or possibly death.\n4. During the Civil War she was also a carrier of information from the South to the North.\n5. After the war needy former slaves were helped by Tubman’s raising of money for refugees.\n23b Using sentence beginnings and endings\nReaders automatically seek a writer’s principal meaning in the main clause of a sentence—\nessentially, in the subject that names the actor and the verb that usually specifies the action (see the\npreceding pages). Thus you can help readers understand your intended meaning by controlling the\ninformation in your subjects and the relation of the main clause to any modifiers attached to it.\nOld and new information\nGenerally, readers expect the beginning of a sentence to contain information that they already\nknow or that you have already\nintroduced. They then look to the sentence ending for new information. In the unemphatic passage\nbelow, the second and third sentences both begin with new topics, while the old topics appear at\nthe ends of the sentences. The pattern of the passage is ARB. CRB. DRA.\nUnemphatic Education almost means controversy these days, with rising costs and constant\ncomplaints about its inade-quacies. But the value of schooling should not be ob-scured by the\ncontroversy. The single best means of economic advancement, despite its shortcomings, re-mains\neducation.\nIn the more emphatic revision, old information begins each sentence and new information ends the\nsentence. The passage follows the pattern ARB. BRC. ARD.\nRevised Education almost means controversy these days, with rising costs and constant\ncomplaints about its inadequacies. But the controversy should not obscure the value of schooling.\nEducation remains, despite its short-comings, the single best means of economic advancement.\nCumulative and periodic sentences\nYou can call attention to information by placing it first or last in a sentence, reserving the\nmiddle for incidentals:\nUnemphatic Education remains the single best means of economic advancement, despite its\nshortcomings. [Emphasizes shortcomings.]\nRevised Despite its shortcomings, education remains the single best means of economic\nadvancement. [Emphasizes advancement more than shortcomings.]\nRevised Education remains, despite its shortcomings, the single best means of economic\nadvancement. [De-emphasizes shortcomings.]\nMany sentences begin with the main clause and then add more modifiers to explain, amplify,\nor illustrate it. Such sentences are called cumulative (because they accumulate information as they\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 5\nproceed) or loose (because they are not tightly structured). They parallel the way we naturally\nthink.\nCumulative Education has no equal in opening minds, instilling values, and creating opportunities.\nCumulative Most of the Great American Desert is made up of bare rock, rugged cliffs, mesas,\ncanyons, mountains, separated from one another by broad flat basins covered with sunbaked mud and\nalkali, supporting a sparse and measured growth of sagebrush or creosote or saltbush, depending on\nlocation and elevation.\n—Edward Abbey\nThe opposite kind of sentence, called periodic, saves the main clause until just before the end\n(the period) of the sentence. Everything before the main clause points toward it.\nPeriodic In opening minds, instilling values, and creating opportunities, education has no equal.\nPeriodic With people from all over the world—Korean grocers, Jamaican cricket players, Vietnamese\nfishers, Haitian cabdrivers, Chinese doctors—the American mosaic is continually changing.\nThe periodic sentence creates suspense for readers by reserving important information for the\nend. But readers should already have an idea of the sentence’s subject—because it was discussed or\nintroduced in the preceding sentence—so that they know what the opening modifiers describe. A\nvariation of the periodic sentence names the subject at the beginning, follows it with a modifier,\nand then completes the main clause:\nDick Hayne, who works in jeans and loafers and likes to let a question cure in the air for a while before\nanswering it, bears all the markings of what his generation used to call a laid-back kind of guy.\n—George Rush\nEXERCISE 23.2 Sentence combining: Beginnings and endings\nLocate the main idea in each group of sentences below. Then combine each group into a single sentence\nthat emphasizes that idea by placing it at the beginning or the end. For sentences 2–5, determine the\nposition of the main idea by considering its relation to the previous sentences: if the main idea picks up a\ntopic that’s already been introduced, place it at the beginning; if it adds new information, place it at the\nend. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nThe storm blew roofs off buildings. It caused extensive damage. It knocked down many trees. It\nsevered power lines.\nMain idea at beginning: The storm caused extensive damage, blowing roofs off buildings, knocking\ndown many trees, and severing power lines.\nMain idea at end: Blowing roofs off buildings, knocking down many trees, and severing power lines,\nthe storm caused extensive damage.\n1. Pat Taylor strode into the room. The room was packed. He greeted students called ―Taylor’s Kids.‖\nHe nodded to their parents and teachers.\n2. This was a wealthy Louisiana oilman. He had promised his ―Kids‖ free college educations. He was\ndetermined to make higher education available to all qualified but disadvantaged students.\n3. The students welcomed Taylor. Their voices joined in singing. They sang ―You Are the Wind\nBeneath My Wings.‖ Their faces beamed with hope. Their eyes flashed with self-confidence.\n4. The students had thought a college education was beyond their dreams. It seemed too costly. It\nseemed too demanding.\n5. Taylor had to ease the costs and the demands of getting to college. He created a bold plan. The plan\nconsisted of scholarships, tutoring, and counseling.\n23c Arranging parallel elements effectively\nSeries\nWith parallelism, you use similar grammatical structures for ideas linked by and, but, and\nsimilar words: Blustery winds and upturned leaves often signal thunderstorms. (See Chapter 25.)\nIn addition, you should arrange the parallel ideas in order of their importance:\nUnemphatic The storm ripped the roofs off several buildings, killed ten people, and knocked down\nmany trees in town. [Buries the most serious damage—deaths—in the mid-dle.]\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 6\nEmphatic The storm knocked down many trees in town, ripped the roofs off several buildings, and\nkilled ten people. [Arranges items in order of increasing importance.]\nYou may want to use an unexpected item at the end of a series for humor or for another\nspecial effect:\nEarly to bed and early to rise makes a man healthy, wealthy, and dead.\n—James Thurber\nBut be careful not to use such a series carelessly. The following series seems thoughtlessly random\nrather than intentionally humorous:\nUnemphatic The painting has subdued tone, intense feeling, and a length of about three feet.\nEmphatic The painting, about three feet long, has subdued tone and intense feeling.\nBalanced sentences\nA sentence is balanced when its clauses are parallel—that is, matched in grammatical\nstructure (Chapter 25). When used carefully, balanced sentences can be especially effective in\nalerting readers to a strong comparison between two ideas. Read the following examples aloud to\nhear their rhythm.\nThe fickleness of the women I love is equalled only by the infernal constancy of the women who love me.\n—George Bernard Shaw\nIn a pure balanced sentence two main clauses are exactly parallel: they match item for item.\nScratch a lover, and find a foe. —Dorothy Parker\nBut the term is commonly applied to sentences that are only approximately parallel or that have\nonly some parallel parts:\nIf thought corrupts language, language can also corrupt thought.\n—George Orwell\nAs the traveler who has once been from home is wiser than he who has never left his own doorstep, so a\nknowledge of one other culture should sharpen our ability to scrutinize more steadily, to appreciate more\nlovingly, our own. —Margaret Mead\nEXERCISE 23.3 Revising: Series and balanced elements\nRevise the following sentences so that elements in a series or balanced elements are arranged to give\nmaximum emphasis to main ideas. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nThe campers were stranded without matches, without food or water, and without a tent.\nThe campers were stranded without matches, without a tent, and without food or water.\n1. Remembering her days as a ―conductor‖ on the Underground Railroad made Harriet Tubman proud,\nbut she got angry when she remembered her years as a slave.\n2. Tubman wanted freedom regardless of personal danger, whereas for her husband, John, personal\nsafety was more important than freedom.\n3. Tubman proved her fearlessness in many ways: she led hundreds of other slaves to freedom, she was\na spy for the North during the Civil War, and she disobeyed John’s order not to run away.\n4. To conduct slaves north to freedom, Tubman risked being re-turned to slavery, being hanged for a\nhuge reward, and being caught by Southern patrollers.\n5. After the war Tubman worked tirelessly for civil rights and women’s suffrage; raising money for\nhomes for needy former slaves was something else she did.\n23d Repeating ideas\nRepetition of words and phrases often clutters and weakens sentences, as discussed on pages\n532–33. But carefully planned repetition can be an effective means of emphasis. Such repetition\noften combines with parallelism. It may occur in a series of sentences (see p. 83) or in a series of\nwords, phrases, or clauses within a sentence, as in the following examples:\nThere is something uneasy in the Los Angeles air this afternoon, some unnatural stillness, some tension.\n—Joan Didion\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 7\nWe have the tools, all the tools—we are suffocating in tools—but we cannot find the actual wood to work\nor even the actual hand to work it.\n—Archibald MacLeish\n23e Separating ideas\nWhen you save important information for the end of a sentence, you can emphasize it even\nmore by setting it off from the rest of the sentence, as in the second example below:\nMothers and housewives are the only workers who do not have regular time off, so they are the great\nvacationless class.\nMothers and housewives are the only workers who do not have regular time off. They are the great\nvacationless class.\n—Anne Morrow Lindbergh\nYou can vary the degree of emphasis by varying the extent to which you separate one idea\nfrom the others. A semicolon provides more separation than a comma, and a period provides still\nmore separation. Compare the following sentences:\nMost of the reading which is praised for itself is neither literary nor intellectual, but narcotic.\nMost of the reading which is praised for itself is neither literary nor intellectual; it is narcotic.\nMost of the reading which is praised for itself is neither literary nor intellectual. It is narcotic. —Donald\nHall\nSometimes a dash or a pair of dashes will isolate and thus emphasize a part of a statement (see\nalso pp. 480–81):\nHis schemes were always elaborate, ingenious, and exciting—and wholly impractical.\nAthletics—that is, winning athletics—has become a profitable university operation.\nEXERCISE 23.4 Emphasizing with repetition or separation\nEmphasize the main idea in each of the following sentences or groups of sentences by following the\ninstructions in parentheses: either\ncombine sentences so that parallelism and repetition stress the main\nidea, or place the main idea in a separate sentence. Each item has\nmore than one possible answer. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nI try to listen to other people’s opinions. When my mind is closed, I find that other opinions open it.\nAnd they can change my mind when it is wrong. (Parallelism and repetition.)\nI try to listen to other people’s opinions, for they can open my mind when it is closed and they can\nchange my mind when it is wrong.\n1. One of the few worthwhile habits is daily reading. One can read for information. One can read for\nentertainment. Reading can give one a broader view of the world. (Parallelism and repetition.)\n2. Reading introduces new words. One encounters unfamiliar styles of expression through reading.\n(Parallelism and repetition.)\n3. Students who read a great deal will more likely write vividly, coherently, and grammatically, for they\nwill have learned from other authors. (Separation.)\n4. Reading gives knowledge. One gets knowledge about other cultures. One will know about history\nand current events. One gains information about human nature. (Parallelism and repetition.)\n5. As a result of reading, writers have more resources and more flexibility, and thus reading creates\nbetter writers. (Separation.)\n23f Being concise\nConciseness—brevity of expression—aids emphasis no matter what the sentence structure.\nUnnecessary words detract from necessary words. They clutter sentences and obscure ideas.\nWeak In my opinion the competition in the area of grades is distracting. It distracts many students\nfrom their goal, which is to obtain an education that is good. There seems to be a belief among a few\nstudents that grades are more important than what is measured by them.\nEmphatic The competition for grades distracts many students from their goal of obtaining a good\neducation. A few students seem to believe that grades are more important than what they measure.\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 8\nTechniques for tightening sentences are listed in the box opposite. Some of these techniques\nappear earlier in this chapter. All of them are covered in Chapter 39 on writing concisely.\nEXERCISE 23.5 REVISING: CONCISENESS\nRevise the following sentences to make them more emphatic by eliminating wordiness. (You can do this\nexercise online at ablongman.com/ littlebrown.)\nExample:\nThe problem in this particular situation is that we owe more money than we can afford under present\ncircumstances.\nThe problem is that we owe more money than we can afford.\n1. As far as I am concerned, customers who are dining out in restaurants in our country must be wary of\nsuggestive selling, so to speak.\n2. In suggestive selling, diners are asked by the waiter to buy additional menu selections in addition to\nwhat was ordered by them.\n3. For each item on the menu, there is another food that will naturally complement it.\n4. For example, customers will be presented with the question of whether they want to order french fries\nalong with a sandwich or whether they want to order a salad along with a steak dinner.\n5. Due to the fact that customers often give in to suggestive selling, they often find that their restaurant\nmeals are more costly than they had intended to pay.\nEXERCISE 23.6 Revising: Emphasizing ideas\nDrawing on the advice in this chapter, rewrite the following paragraph to emphasize main ideas and to de-\nemphasize less important information. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nIn preparing pasta, there is a requirement for common sense and imagination rather than for\ncomplicated recipes. The key to success in this area is fresh ingredients for the sauce and perfectly cooked\npasta. The sauce may be made with just about any fresh fish, meat, cheese, herb, or vegetable. As for the\npasta itself, it may be dried or fresh, although fresh pasta is usually more delicate and flavorful, as many\nexperienced cooks find. Dried pasta is fine with zesty sauces; with light oil and cream sauces fresh pasta\nis the best choice. There is a difference in the cooking time for dried and fresh pasta, with dried pasta\ntaking longer. It is important that the cook follow the package directions and that the pasta be tested\nbefore the cooking time is up. The pasta is done when the texture is neither tough nor mushy but al dente,\nor ―firm to the bite,‖ according to the Italians, who ought to know.\nNote See page 420 for an exercise involving emphasis along with parallelism and other\ntechniques for effective sentences.\n24\nCHAPTER\nUsing Coordination\nand Subordination\nWhen clearly written, your sentences show the relations between ideas and stress the more\nimportant ideas over the lesser ones. Two techniques can help you achieve such clarity:\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 9\nCoordination shows that two or more elements in a sentence are equally important in\nmeaning. You signal coordination with words such as and, but, and or.\nequally important\nCar insurance is costly, but health insurance seems a luxury.\nSubordination shows that some elements in a sentence are less important than other\nelements for your meaning. Usually, the main idea appears in the main clause, and\nsupporting information appears in single words, phrases, and subordinate clauses.\nless important more important\n(subordinate clause) (main clause)\nBecause accidents and thefts occur frequently, car insurance is costly.\nNote Grammar and style checkers may spot some errors in punctuating coordinated and\nsubordinated elements, and they can flag long sentences that may contain excessive coordination or\nsubordination. But otherwise they provide little help because they cannot recognize the relations\namong ideas in sentences.\n24a Coordinating to relate equal ideas\nBy linking equally important information, you can emphasize the relations for readers.\nCompare the passages below:\nString of simple sentences\nWe should not rely so heavily on oil. Coal and uranium are also overused. We have a substantial energy\nresource in the moving wa-ters of our rivers. Smaller streams add to the total volume of water. The\nresource renews itself. Oil and coal are irreplaceable. Uranium is also irreplaceable. The cost of water\ndoes not increase much over time. The costs of coal, oil, and uranium rise dramatically.\nIdeas coordinated\nWe should not rely so heavily on oil, coal, and uranium, for we have a substantial energy resource in the\nmoving waters of our rivers and streams. Oil, coal, and uranium are irreplaceable and thus subject to\ndramatic cost increases; water, however, is self-renewing and more stable in cost.\nThe information in both passages is essentially the same, but the second is shorter and\nconsiderably easier to read and understand because it links coordinate ideas with the\nunderlined words.\nPunctuating coordinated words, phrases, and clauses\nMost coordinated words, phrases, and subordinate clauses are not punctuated with commas\n(see p. 448). The exceptions are items in a series and coordinate adjectives:\nWe rely heavily on coal, oil, and uranium. [A series; see p. 441.]\nDirty, unhealthy air is one result. [Coordinate adjectives; see p. 442.]\nIn a sentence consisting of two main clauses, punctuation depends on whether a coordinating\nconjunction, a conjunctive adverb, or no connecting word links the clauses:\nOil is irreplaceable, but water is self-renewing. [See p. 432.]\nOil is irreplaceable; however, water is self-renewing. [See p. 455.]\nOil is irreplaceable; water is self-renewing. [See p. 453.]\n1 Using coordination effectively\nA string of coordinated elements—especially main clauses—creates the same effect as a string\nof simple sentences: it obscures the relative importance of ideas and details.\nExcessive The weeks leading up to the resignation of President\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 10\ncoordination Richard Nixon were eventful, and the Supreme Court and the Congress closed in on\nhim, and the Senate Judiciary Committee voted to begin impeachment proceedings, and finally the\nPresident resigned on August 9, 1974.\nSuch a passage needs editing to stress the important points (underlined below) and to de-emphasize\nthe less important information:\nRevised The weeks leading up to the resignation of President Richard Nixon were eventful, as\nthe Supreme Court and the Congress closed in on him and the Senate Judiciary Committee voted to begin\nimpeachment proceedings. Finally, the President resigned on August 9, 1974.\n2 Coordinating logically\nCoordinated sentence elements should be logically equal and related, and the relation between\nthem should be the one expressed by the connecting word. If either principle is violated, the result\nis faulty coordination.\nFaulty John Stuart Mill was a nineteenth-century utilitarian, and he believed that actions should be\njudged by their usefulness or by the happiness they cause. [The two clauses are not separate and equal:\nthe second expands on the first by explaining what a utilitarian such as Mill believed.]\nRevised John Stuart Mill, a nineteenth-century utilitarian, believed that actions should be judged by\ntheir usefulness or by the happiness they cause.\nFaulty Mill is recognized as a utilitarian, and he did not found the utilitarian school of philosophy.\n[The two clauses seem to contrast, requiring but or yet between them.]\nRevised Mill is recognized as a utilitarian, but he did not found the utilitarian school of philosophy.\nEXERCISE 24.1 Sentence combining: Coordination\nCombine sentences in the following passages to coordinate related ideas in the ways that seem most\neffective to you. You will have to supply coordinating conjunctions or conjunctive adverbs and the\nappropriate punctuation. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman\n.com/littlebrown.)\n1. Many chronic misspellers do not have the time to master spelling rules. They may not have the\nmotivation. They may rely on dic-\ntionaries to catch misspellings. Most dictionaries list words under their correct spellings. One kind of\ndictionary is designed for chronic misspellers. It lists each word under its common mis-spellings. It\nthen provides the correct spelling. It also provides the definition.\n2. Henry Hudson was an English explorer. He captained ships for the Dutch East India Company. On a\nvoyage in 1610 he passed by Greenland. He sailed into a great bay in today’s northern Canada. He\nthought he and his sailors could winter there. The cold was terrible. Food ran out. The sailors\nmutinied. The sailors cast Hudson adrift in a small boat. Eight others were also in the boat. Hudson\nand his companions perished.\nEXERCISE 24.2 Revising: Excessive or faulty coordination\nRevise the following sentences to eliminate excessive or faulty coordination. Relate ideas effectively by\nadding or subordinating information or by forming more than one sentence. Each item has more than one\npossible answer. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/ littlebrown.)\nExample:\nMy dog barks, and I have to move out of my apartment.\nBecause my dog’s barking disturbs my neighbors, I have to move out of my apartment.\n1. Often soldiers admired their commanding officers, and they gave them nicknames, and these names\nfrequently contained the word ―old,‖ but not all of the commanders were old.\n2. General Thomas ―Stonewall‖ Jackson was also called ―Old Jack,‖ and he was not yet forty years old.\n3. Another Southern general in the Civil War was called ―Old Pete,‖ and his full name was James\nLongstreet.\n4. The Union general Henry W. Halleck had a reputation as a good military strategist, and he was an\nexpert on the work of a French military authority, Henri Jomini, and Halleck was called ―Old\nBrains.‖\n5. General William Henry Harrison won the Battle of Tippecanoe, and he received the nickname ―Old\nTippecanoe,‖ and he used the name in his presidential campaign slogan ―Tippecanoe and Tyler,\nToo,‖ and he won the election in 1840, but he died of pneumonia a month after taking office.\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 11\n24b Subordinating to distinguish main ideas\nWith subordination you use words or word groups to indicate that some ideas in a sentence\nare less important than the idea in the main clause. In the following sentence, it is difficult to tell\nwhat is most important:\nExcessive Computer prices have dropped, and production costs\ncoordination have dropped more slowly, and computer manufacturers have had to contend with\nshrinking profits.\nThe following revision places the point of the sentence (shrinking profits) in the main clause and\nreduces the rest of the information to a modifier (underlined):\nRevised Because production costs have dropped more slowly than prices, computer\nmanufacturers have had to contend with shrinking profits.\nNo rules can specify what information in a sentence you should make primary and what you\nshould subordinate; the decision will depend on your meaning. But, in general, you should\nconsider using subordinate structures for details of time, cause, condition, concession, purpose, and\nidentification (size, location, and the like). You can subordinate information with the structures\nlisted in the box opposite.\nIn general, the shorter a subordinate structure is, the less emphasis it has. The following\nexamples show how subordinate structures may convey various meanings with various weights.\n(Some appropriate subordinating words for each meaning appear in parentheses.)\nSpace or time (after, before, since, until, when, while; at, in, on, until)\nThe mine explosion killed six workers. The owners adopted safety measures.\nAfter the mine explosion killed six workers, the owners adopted safety measures. [Subordinate clause.]\nAfter six deaths in a mine explosion, the owners adopted safety measures. [Prepositional phrases.]\nCause or effect (as, because, since, so that; because of, due to)\nJones had been without work for six months. He was having trouble paying his bills.\nBecause Jones had been without work for six months, he was having trouble paying his bills.\n[Subordinate clause.]\nHaving been jobless for six months, Jones could not pay his bills. [Verbal phrase.]\nCondition (if, provided, since, unless, whenever; with, without)\nForecasters predict a mild winter. Farmers hope for an early spring.\nWhenever forecasters predict a mild winter, farmers hope for an early spring. [Subordinate clause.]\nWith forecasts for a mild winter, farmers hope for an early spring. [Prepositional phrase.]\nConcession (although, as if, even though, though; despite, except for, in spite of)\nThe horse looked gentle. It proved hard to manage.\nAlthough the horse looked gentle, it proved hard to manage. [Subordinate clause.]\nThe horse, a gentle-looking animal, proved hard to manage. [Appositive.]\nThe gentle-looking horse proved hard to manage. [Single word.]\nPurpose (in order that, so that, that; for, toward)\nCongress passed new immigration laws. Many Vietnamese refugees could enter the United States.\nCongress passed new immigration laws so that many Vietnamese refugees could enter the United States.\n[Subordinate clause.]\nCongress passed new immigration laws, permitting many Vietnamese refugees to enter the United States.\n[Verbal phrase.]\nIdentification (that, when, where, which, who; by, from, of)\nOld barns are common in New England. They are often painted red.\nOld barns, which are often painted red, are common in New England. [Subordinate clause.]\nOld barns, often painted red, are common in New England. [Verbal phrase.]\nOld red barns are common in New England. [Single word.]\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 12\nPunctuating subordinate constructions\nA modifying word, phrase, or clause that introduces a sentence is usually set off from the rest\nof the sentence with a comma (see\np. 433):\nUnfortunately, the bank failed.\nIn a little over six months, the bank became insolvent.\nWhen the bank failed, many reporters investigated.\nA modifier that interrupts or concludes a main clause is not set off with punctuation when it is\nessential to the meaning of a word or words in the clause (see p. 435):\nOne article about the bank failure won a prize.\nThe article that won the prize appeared in the local newspaper.\nThe reporter wrote the article because the bank failure affected many residents of the town.\nWhen an interrupting or concluding modifier is not essential to meaning, but simply adds\ninformation to the sentence, it is set off with punctuation, usually a comma or commas (see p. 435):\nThe bank, over forty years old, never reopened after its doors were closed.\nThe bank managers, who were cleared of any wrongdoing, all found new jobs.\nSome customers of the bank never recovered all their money, though most of them tried to do so.\nLike a modifier, an appositive is set off with punctuation (usually a comma or commas) only\nwhen it is not essential to the meaning of the word it refers to (see p. 437):\nThe bank, First City, was the oldest in town.\nOur newspaper, the Chronicle, was one of several reporting the story.\nA dash or dashes may also be used to set off a nonessential appositive, particularly when it contains\ncommas (see p. 480). A concluding appositive is sometimes set off with a colon (see p. 477).\n1 Subordinating logically\nUse subordination only for the less important information in a sentence. Faulty\nsubordination reverses the dependent relation the reader expects:\nFaulty Ms. Angelo was in her first year of teaching, although she was a better instructor than\nothers with many years of experience. [The sentence suggests that Angelo’s in-experience is the main\nidea, whereas the writer meant to stress her skill despite her inexperience.]\nRevised Although Ms. Angelo was in her first year of teaching, she was a better instructor than\nothers with many years of experience.\n2 Using subordination effectively\nSubordination can do much to organize and emphasize information. But it loses that power\nwhen you try to cram too much loosely related detail into one long sentence:\nOverloaded The boats that were moored at the dock when the hurricane, which was one of the worst\nin three decades, struck were ripped from their moorings, because the owners had not been adequately\nprepared, since the weather service had predicted that the storm would blow out to sea, which storms do\nat this time of year.\nSuch sentences usually have more than one idea that deserves a main clause, so they are best\nrevised by sorting their details into more than one sentence:\nRevised Struck by one of the worst hurricanes in three decades, the boats at the dock were ripped from\ntheir moorings. The owners were unprepared because the weather service had said that storms\nat this time of year blow out to sea.\nA common form of excessive subordination occurs with a string of adjective clauses, each\nbeginning with which, who, or that:\nStringy The company opened a new plant outside Louisville, which is in Kentucky and which is on\nthe Ohio River, which forms the border between Kentucky and Ohio.\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 13\nTo revise such sentences, recast some of the subordinate clauses as other kinds of modifying\nstructures:\nRevised The company opened a new plant outside Louisville, Ken-tucky, a city across the Ohio River\nfrom Ohio.\nEXERCISE 24.3 Sentence combining: Subordination\nCombine each of the following pairs of sentences twice, each time using one of the subordinate structures\nin parentheses to make a single sentence. It consists of hot and irritating chemicals. (Phrase beginning\nconsisting. Phrase beginning of.)\n3. The spray’s two chemicals are stored separately in the beetle’s body and mixed in the spraying gland.\nThe chemicals resemble\na nerve-gas weapon. (Phrase beginning stored. Clause beginning which.)\n4. The tip of the beetle’s abdomen sprays the chemicals. The tip revolves like a turret on a World War II\nbomber. (Phrase beginning revolving. Phrase beginning spraying.)\n5. The beetle defeats most of its enemies. It is still eaten by spiders and birds. (Clause beginning\nalthough. Phrase beginning except.)\nEXERCISE 24.4 Revising: Subordination\nRewrite the following paragraph in the way you think most effective to subordinate the less important\nideas to the more important ones. Use subordinate clauses, phrases, and single words as you think\nappropriate. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nFewer students today are majoring in the liberal arts. I mean by ―liberal arts‖ such subjects as\nhistory, English, and the social sciences. Students think a liberal arts degree will not help them get jobs.\nThey are wrong. They may not get practical, job-related experience from the liberal arts, but they will get\na broad education, and it will never again be available to them. Many employers look for more than a\ntechnical, professional education. They think such an education can make an employee’s views too\nnarrow. The employers want open-minded employees. They want employees to think about problems\nfrom many angles. The liberal arts curriculum instills such flexibility. The flexibility is vital to the health\nof our society.\nEXERCISE 24.5 Revising: Faulty or excessive subordination\nRevise the following sentences to eliminate faulty or excessive subordination. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nTerrified to return home, he had driven his mother’s car into a cornfield.\nHaving driven his mother’s car into a cornfield, he was terrified to return home.\n1. Genaro González is blessed with great writing talent, which means that his novel Rainbow’s End and\nhis story collection Only Sons have been published.\n2. He loves to write, although he has also earned a doctorate in psychology.\n3. His first story, which reflects his consciousness of his Aztec heritage and place in the world, is titled\n―Un Hijo del Sol.‖\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 14\n4. González, who writes equally well in English and Spanish, received a large fellowship that enabled\nhim to take a leave of absence from the University of Texas–Pan American, where he teaches\npsychology, so that he could write without worrying about an income.\n5. González wrote the first version of ―Un Hijo del Sol‖ while he was a sophomore at Pan American,\nwhich is in the Rio Grande valley of southern Texas, which González calls ―el Valle‖ in the story.\n24c Choosing clear connectors\nMost connecting words signal specific and unambiguous re-\nlations; for instance, but clearly indicates contrast, and because clearly indicates cause. A few\nconnectors, however, require careful use, either because they are ambiguous in many contexts or\nbecause they are often misused.\n1 Using as and while clearly\nThe subordinating conjunction as can indicate several relations, including comparison and\ntime:\nComparison The technicians work quickly, as they are required to do.\nTime One shift starts as the other stops.\nAvoid using as to indicate cause. It is unclear.\nUnclear As the experiment was occurring, the laboratory was sealed. [Time or cause intended?]\nRevised When the experiment was occurring, the laboratory was sealed. [Time.]\nRevised Because the experiment was occurring, the laboratory was sealed. [Cause.]\nThe subordinating conjunction while can indicate either time or concession. Unless the\ncontext makes the meaning of while unmistakably clear, choose a more exact connector:\nUnclear While technicians work in the next room, they cannot hear the noise. [Time or\nconcession intended?]\nRevised When technicians work in the next room, they cannot hear the noise. [Time.]\nRevised Although technicians work in the next room, they cannot hear the noise. [Concession.]\n2 Using as and like correctly\nThe use of as as a substitute for whether or that is considered nonstandard (it does not\nconform to spoken and written standard English):\nNonstandard They are not sure as the study succeeded.\nRevised They are not sure whether [or that] the study succeeded.\nAlthough the preposition like is often used as a conjunction in informal speech and in\nadvertising (Dirt-Away works like a soap should), writing generally requires the conjunction as, as\nif, as though, or that:\nSpeech It seemed like it did succeed.\nWriting It seemed as if [or as though or that] it did succeed.\nEXERCISE 24.6 Revising: Coordination and subordination\nThe following paragraph consists entirely of simple sentences. Use coordination and subordination to\ncombine sentences in the ways you think most effective to emphasize main ideas. (You can do this\nexercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nSir Walter Raleigh personified the Elizabethan Age. That was the period of Elizabeth I’s rule of\nEngland. The period occurred in the last half of the sixteenth century. Raleigh was a courtier and poet. He\nwas also an explorer and entrepreneur. Supposedly, he gained Queen\nElizabeth’s favor. He did this by throwing his cloak beneath her feet at the right moment. She was just\nabout to step over a puddle. There is no evidence for this story. It does illustrate Raleigh’s dramatic and\ndynamic personality. His energy drew others to him. He was one of\nElizabeth’s favorites. She supported him. She also dispensed favors to him. However, he lost his queen’s\ngoodwill. Without her permission he seduced one of her maids of honor. He eventually married the maid\nof honor. Elizabeth died. Then her successor imprisoned Raleigh in the Tower of London. Her successor\nwas James I. The king falsely charged Raleigh with treason. Raleigh was released after thirteen years. He\nwas arrested again two years later on the old treason charges. At the age of sixty-six he was beheaded.\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 15\nNote See page 420 for an exercise involving coordination and subordination along with\nparallelism and other techniques for effective sentences.\n25\nCHAPTER\nUsing Parallelism\nParallelism is a similarity of grammatical form between two or more elements.\nThe air is dirtied by factories belching smoke\nand cars spewing exhaust.\nParallel structure reinforces and highlights a close relation between compound sentence elements,\nwhether words, phrases, or clauses.\nThe principle underlying parallelism is that form should reflect meaning: since the parts of\ncompound constructions have the same function and importance, they should have the same\ngrammatical form.\nNote A grammar and style checker cannot recognize problems with parallelism because it\ncannot recognize the relations among ideas.\n25a Using parallelism for coordinate elements\nUse parallelism in all the situations illustrated in the box on the facing page.\nNote Parallel elements match each other in structure, but they do not always match word for\nword:\nThe pioneers passedthrough the town\nand into the vast, unpopulated desert.\n1 Using parallelism for elements linked by coordinating conjunctions\nThe coordinating conjunctions and, but, or, nor, and yet always signal a need for parallelism:\nThe industrial base was shifting and shrinking.\nPoliticians rarely acknowledged the problem or proposed alternatives.\nIndustrial workers were understandably disturbed that they were losing their jobs and that no one seemed\nto care.\nIf sentence elements linked by coordinating conjunctions are not parallel in structure, the\nresulting sentence will be awkward and distracting:\nNonparallel Three reasons why steel companies kept losing money were that their plants were\ninefficient, high labor costs, and foreign competition was increasing.\nRevised Three reasons why steel companies kept losing money were inefficient plants, high labor\ncosts, and increasing foreign competition.\nAll the words required by idiom or grammar must be stated in compound\nconstructions (see also p. 258).\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 16\nNonparallel Given training, workers can acquire the skills and inter-est in other jobs. [Skills and\ninterest require different prepositions, so both must be stated.]\nRevised Given training, workers can acquire the skills for and interest in other jobs.\nOften, the same word must be repeated to avoid confusion:\nConfusing Thoreau stood up for his principles by not paying his taxes and spending a night in jail.\n[Did he spend a night in jail or not?]\nRevised Thoreau stood up for his principles by not paying his taxes and by spending a night in\njail.\nBe sure that clauses beginning who or which are coordinated only with other who or which\nclauses, even when the pronoun is not repeated:\nNonparallel Thoreau is the nineteenth-century essayist who retired to the woods and he wrote about\nnature.\nRevised Thoreau is the nineteenth-century essayist who retired to the woods and [who] wrote\nabout nature.\n2 Using parallelism for elements linked by correlative conjunctions\nCorrelative conjunctions are pairs of connectors. For example:\nboth . . . and neither . . . nor not only . . . but also\neither . . . or not . . . but whether . . . or\nCorrelative conjuctions stress equality and balance and thus emphasize the relation between\nelements, even long phrases and clauses. The elements should be parallel to confirm their relation:\nIt is not a tax bill but a tax relief bill, providing relief not for the needy but for the greedy. —Franklin\nDelano Roosevelt\nAt the end of the novel, Huck Finn both rejects society’s values by turning down money and a home and\naffirms his own values by setting out for ―the territory.‖\nMost errors in parallelism with correlative conjunctions occur when the element after the\nsecond connector does not match the element after the first connector.\nNonparallel Mark Twain refused either to ignore the moral blindness of his society or spare the\nreader’s sensibilities. [To follows either, so it must also follow or.]\nRevised Mark Twain refused either to ignore the moral blindness of his society or to spare the\nreader’s sensibilities.\nNonparallel Huck Finn learns not only that human beings have an enormous capacity for folly but\nalso enormous dignity. [The first element includes that human beings have; the second element does not.]\nRevised Huck Finn learns that human beings have not only an enormous capacity for folly but\nalso enormous dignity.\n3 Using parallelism for elements being compared or contrasted\nElements being compared or contrasted should ordinarily be cast in the same grammatical\nform.\nIt is better to live rich than to die rich. —Samuel Johnson\nWeak The study found that most welfare recipients wanted to work rather than handouts.\nRevised The study found that most welfare recipients wanted work rather than handouts.\nRevised The study found that most welfare recipients wanted to work rather than to accept\nhandouts.\n4 Using parallelism for lists, outlines, or headings\nThe elements of a list or outline that divides a larger subject are coordinate and should be\nparallel in structure. Parallelism is essential in the headings that divide a paper into sections (see p.\n119) and in a formal topic outline (see p. 37).\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 17\nFaulty\nChanges in Renaissance\nEngland\n1. Extension of trade routes\n2. Merchant class became more\npowerful\n3. The death of feudalism\n4. Upsurging of the arts\n5. The sciences were\nencouraged\n6. Religious quarrels began\nEXERCISE 25.1 Identifying parallel elements\nIdentify the parallel elements in the following sentences. Eating an animal has not always been an automatic or an everyday affair; it has tended to be done on\nsolemn occasions and for a special treat. —Margaret Visser\n2. Add\nor delete words or rephrase\nas necessary. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/\nlittlebrown.)\nExample:\nAfter emptying her bag, searching the apartment, and she called the library, Jennifer realized she had\nlost the book.\nAfter emptying her bag, searching the apartment, and calling the library, Jennifer realized she had\nlost the book.\n1. The ancient Greeks celebrated four athletic contests: the Olympic Games at Olympia, the Isthmian\nGames were held near Corinth, at Delphi the Pythian Games, and the Nemean Games were sponsored\nby the people of Cleonae.\n2. Each day of the games consisted of either athletic events or holding ceremonies and sacrifices to the\ngods.\n3. In the years between the games, competitors were taught wrestling, javelin throwing, and how to box.\n4. Competitors participated in running sprints, spectacular chariot and horse races, and running long\ndistances while wearing full armor.\n5. The purpose of such events was developing physical strength, demonstrating skill and endurance,\nand to sharpen the skills needed for war.\n6. Events were held for both men and for boys.\n7. At the Olympic Games the spectators cheered their favorites to victory, attended sacrifices to the\ngods, and they feasted on the meat not burned in offerings.\n8. The athletes competed less to achieve great wealth than for gaining honor both for themselves and\ntheir cities.\n9. Of course, exceptional athletes received financial support from patrons, poems and statues by\nadmiring artists, and they even got lavish living quarters from their sponsoring cities.\n10. With the medal counts and flag ceremonies, today’s Olympians sometimes seem to be proving their\ncountries’ superiority more than to demonstrate individual talent.\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 18\n25b Using parallelism to increase coherence\nEffective parallelism will enable you to combine in a single, well-ordered sentence related\nideas that you might have expressed in separate sentences. Compare the following three sentences\nwith the original single sentence written by H. L. Mencken:\nSlang originates in the effort of ingenious individuals to make the language more pungent and\npicturesque. They increase the store of terse and striking words or widen the boundaries of metaphor.\nThus a vocabulary for new shades and differences in meaning is provided by slang.\nSlang originates in the effort of ingenious individuals to make the language more pungent and\npicturesque—to increase the store of terse and striking words, to widen the boundaries of metaphor, and\nto provide a vocabulary for new shades and differences in meaning.\n—H. L. Mencken\nParallel structure works as well to emphasize the connections among related sentences in a\nparagraph:\nLewis Mumford stands high in the company of this century’s sages. A scholar of cosmic cultural reach\nand conspicuous public conscience,\na distinguished critic of life, arts, and letters, an unequaled observer of cities and civilizations, he is\nsecure in the modern pantheon of great men. He is also an enigma and an anachronism. A legend of epic\nproportions in intellectual and academic circles, he is surprisingly little known to the public. —Ada\nLouise Huxtable\nHere, Huxtable tightly binds her sentences with two layers of parallelism: the subject-verb patterns\nof all four sentences (italic and underlined) and the appositives of the second and fourth sentences\n(underlined). (See pp. 83–84 for another illustration of parallelism among sentences.)\nEXERCISE 25.3 Sentence combining: Parallelism\nCombine each group of sentences below into one concise sentence in which parallel elements appear in\nparallel structures. You will have to add, delete, change, and rearrange words. Each item has more than\none possible answer. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman .com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nThe new process works smoothly. It is efficient, too.\nThe new process works smoothly and efficiently.\n1. People can develop post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD). They develop it after experiencing a\ndangerous situation. They will also have felt fear for their survival.\n2.
