Many people consider the great white not\nonly swift and powerful but also to be a cunning and cruel predator on humans. However, scientists claim\nthat the great white attacks humans not by choice but as a result of chance. To a shark, our behavior in\nthe water is similar to that of porpoises, seals, and sea lions—the shark’s favorite foods. These sea\nmammals are both agile enough and can move fast enough to evade the shark. Thus the shark must attack\nwith swiftness and noiselessly to surprise the prey and giving it little chance to escape. Humans become\nthe shark’s victims not because the shark has any preference or hatred of humans but because humans can\nneither outswim nor can they outmaneuver the shark. If the fish were truly a cruel human-eater, it would\nprolong the terror of its attacks, perhaps by circling or bumping into its intended victims before they were\nattacked.\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 19\nNote See page 420 for an exercise involving parallelism along with other techniques for\neffective sentences.\n26\nCHAPTER\nAchieving Variety\nIn a paragraph or an essay, each sentence stands in relation to those before and after it. To\nmake sentences work together effectively, you need to vary their length, structure, and word order\nto reflect the importance and complexity of ideas. Variety sometimes takes care of itself, but you\ncan practice established techniques for achieving varied sentences:\nA series of similar sentences will prove monotonous and ineffective, as the following passage\nillustrates.\nUlysses S. Grant and Robert E. Lee met on April 9, 1865. Their meeting place was the parlor of a modest\nhouse at Appomattox Court House, Virginia. They met to work out the terms for the surrender of Lee’s\nArmy of Northern Virginia. One great chapter of American life ended with their meeting, and another\nbegan. Grant and Lee were bringing the Civil War to its virtual finish. Other armies still had to surrender,\nand the fugitive Confederate government would struggle desperately and vainly. It would try to find some\nway to go on living with its chief support gone. Grant and Lee had signed the papers, however, and it was\nall over in effect.\nThese eight sentences are all between twelve and sixteen words long (counting initials and dates),\nthey are about equally detailed, and they all begin with the subject. We get a sense of names, dates,\nand events but no immediate sense of how they relate or what is most important.\nNow compare the preceding passage with the actual passage written by Bruce Catton. Here the\nfour sentences range from eleven to fifty-five words, and only one sentence begins with its subject:\nWhen Ulysses S. Grant and Robert E. Lee met in the parlor of a modest\nhouse at Appo-mattox Court House, Virginia, on April 9, 1865, to work out the\nterms for the surrender of Lee’s Army of Northern Virginia, a great chapter in\nAmerican life came to a close, and a great new chapter began.\nThese men were bringing the Civil War to its virtual finish. To be sure,\nother armies had yet to surrender, and for a few days the fugitive Confederate\ngovernment would struggle desperately and vainly, trying to find some way to\ngo on living now that its chief support was gone. But in effect it was all over\nwhen Grant and Lee signed the papers.\n—Bruce Catton, ―Grant and Lee‖\nThe rest of this chapter suggests how you can vary your sentences for the kind of interest and\nclarity achieved by Catton.\nNote Some grammar and style checkers will flag long sentences, and you can check for\nappropriate variety in a series of such sentences. But generally these programs cannot help you see\nwhere variety may be needed because they cannot recognize the relative importance and complexity\nof your ideas.\n26a Varying sentence length and structure\nThe sentences of a stylistically effective essay will vary most obviously in their length and the\narrangement of main clauses and modifiers. The variation in length and structure makes writing\nboth readable and clear.\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 20\n1 Varying length\nIn most contemporary writing, sentences vary from about ten to about forty words. When\nsentences are all at one extreme or the other, readers may have difficulty focusing on main ideas\nand seeing the relations among them:\nLong sentences. If most of your sentences contain thirty-five words or more, your main ideas\nmay not stand out from the details that support them. Break some of the long sentences into\nshorter, simpler ones.\nShort sentences. If most of your sentences contain fewer than ten or fifteen words, all your\nideas may seem equally important and the links between them may not be clear. Try\ncombining them with coordination (p. 395) and subordination (p. 398) to show relationships\nand to stress main ideas over supporting information.\n2 Rewriting strings of brief and simple sentences\nA series of brief and simple sentences is both monotonous and hard to understand because it\nforces the reader to sort out relations among ideas. If you find that you depend on brief, simple\nsentences, work to increase variety by combining some of them into longer units that emphasize\nand link important ideas while de-emphasizing incidental information. (See Chapter 24.)\nThe following examples show how a string of simple sentences can be revised into an\neffective piece of writing:\nMonotonous The moon is now drifting away from the earth. It moves away at the rate of about one\ninch a year. This movement is lengthening our days. They increase a thousandth of a\nsecond every century. Forty-seven of our present days will someday make up a month.\nWe might eventually lose the moon altogether. Such great planetary movement rightly\nconcerns astronomers, but it need not worry us. It will take 50 million years.\nRevised The moon is now drifting away from the earth about one inch a year. At a thousandth\nof a second every century, this movement is lengthening our days. Forty-seven of our\npresent days will someday make up a month, if we don’t eventually lose the moon\naltogether. Such great planetary movement rightly concerns astronomers, but it need\nnot worry us. It will take 50 million years.\nIn the revision, underlining indicates subordinate structures that were simple sentences in the\noriginal. With five sentences instead of the original eight, the revision emphasizes the moon’s\nmovement, our lengthening days, and the enormous span of time involved.\n3 Rewriting strings of compound sentences\nCompound sentences are usually just simple sentences linked with conjunctions. Thus a series\nof them will be as weak as a series of brief, simple sentences, especially if the clauses of the\ncompound sentences are all about the same length:\nMonotonous Physical illness may involve more than the body, for the mind may also be affected.\nDisorientation is common among sick people, but they are often unaware of it. They\nmay reason abnormally, or they may be-have immaturely.\nRevised Physical illness may involve the mind as well as the body. Though often unaware of it,\nsick people are com-monly disoriented. They may reason abnormally or be-have\nimmaturely.\nThe first passage creates a seesaw effect. The revision, with some main clauses shortened or\nchanged into modifiers (underlined), is both clearer and more emphatic. (See p. 396 for more on\navoiding excessive coordination.)\nEXERCISE 26.1 Revising: Varied sentence structures\nRewrite the following paragraph to increase variety so that important ideas receive greater emphasis than\nsupporting information does. You will have to change some main clauses into modifiers and then\ncombine and reposition the modifiers and the remaining main clauses. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nCharlotte Perkins Gilman was a leading intellectual in the women’s movement during the first\ndecades of the twentieth century. She wrote Women and Economics. This book challenged Victorian\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 21\nassumptions about differences between the sexes, and it explored the economic roots of women’s\noppression. Gilman wrote little about gaining the vote for women, but many feminists were then\npreoccupied with this issue, and historians have since focused their analyses on this issue. As a result,\nGilman’s contribution to today’s women’s movement has often been overlooked.\n26b\nVarying sentence beginnings\nAn English sentence often begins with its subject, which gen-\nerally captures old information from a preceding sentence (see\npp. 386–87):\nThe defendant’s lawyer was determined to break the prosecution’s witness. She relentlessly cross-\nexamined the stubborn witness for a week.\nHowever, an unbroken sequence of sentences beginning with the subject quickly becomes\nmonotonous, as shown by the unvaried passage on Grant and Lee that opened this chapter (p. 413).\nYou can vary this subject-first pattern by adding modifiers or other elements before the subject.\nNote The final arrangement of sentence elements should al-ways depend on two concerns: the\nrelation of a sentence to those preceding and following it and the emphasis required by your\nmeaning.\nAdverb modifiers\nAdverbs modify verbs, adjectives, other adverbs, and whole clauses. They can often fall in a\nvariety of spots in a sentence. Consider these different emphases:\nFor a week, the defendant’s lawyer relentlessly cross-examined the stubborn witness.\nRelentlessly, the defendant’s lawyer cross-examined the stubborn witness for a week.\nRelentlessly, for a week, the defendant’s lawyer cross-examined the stubborn witness.\nNotice that the last sentence, with both modifiers at the beginning, is periodic and thus highly\nemphatic (see p. 388).\nIn standard American English, placing certain adverb modifiers at the beginning of a sentence\nrequires you to change the normal subject-verb order as well. The most common of these modifiers\nare negatives, including seldom, rarely, in no case, not since, and not until.\nadverb subject verb phrase\nFaulty Seldom a witness has held the stand for so long.\nhelping main\nadverb verb subject verb\nRevised Seldom has a witness held the stand for so long.\nAdjective modifiers\nAdjectives, modifying nouns and pronouns, may include participles and participial phrases, as\nin flying geese or money well spent (see pp. 247–50). These modifiers may sometimes fall at the\nbeginning of a sentence to postpone the subject:\nThe witness was exhausted from his testimony, and he did not\ncooperate.\nExhausted from his testimony, the witness did not cooperate.\nCoordinating conjunctions and transitional expressions\nWhen the relation between two successive sentences demands, you may begin the second with\na coordinating conjunction such as and or but (p. 259) or with a transitional expression such as\nfirst, for instance, however, or therefore (pp. 86–87).\nThe witness had expected to be dismissed after his first long day of cross-examination. But he was not.\nThe price of clothes has risen astronomically in recent years. For example, a cheap cotton shirt that once\ncost $6 now costs $25.\nOccasional expletive constructions\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 22\nAn expletive construction—it or there plus a form of be—may occasionally be useful to delay\nand thus emphasize the subject of the sentence:\nHis judgment seems questionable, not his desire.\nIt is his judgment that seems questionable, not his desire.\nHowever, expletive constructions are more likely to flatten writing by adding extra words. You\nshould use them rarely, only when you can justify doing so. (See also p. 534.)\nEXERCISE 26.2 Revising: Varied sentence beginnings\nFollow the instructions in parentheses to revise each group of sentences below: either create a single\nsentence that begins with an adverb or adjective modifier, or make one sentence begin with an\nappropriate connector. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/ littlebrown.)\nExample:\nThe Seabird took first place. It moved quickly in the wind. (One sen-tence with adjective modifier\nbeginning moving.)\nMoving quickly in the wind, the Seabird took first place.\n1. Some people are champion procrastinators. They seldom complete their work on time. (Two\nsentences with transitional expression.)\n2. Procrastinators may fear criticism. They may fear rejection. They will delay completing an\nassignment. (One sentence with adverb modifier beginning if.)\n3. Procrastinators often desire to please a boss or a teacher. They fear failure so much that they cannot\ndo the work. (Two sentences with coordinating conjunction.)\n4. Procrastination seems a hopeless habit. It is conquerable. (One sentence with adverb modifier\nbeginning although.)\n5. Teachers or employers can be helpful. They can encourage procrastinators. They can give\nprocrastinators the confidence to do good work on time. (One sentence with adjective modifier\nbeginning helpfully encouraging.)\nEXERCISE 26.3 Revising: Varied sentence beginnings\nRevise the following paragraph to vary sentence beginnings by using each of the following at least once:\nan adverb modifier, an adjective modifier, a coordinating conjunction, and a transitional expression. (You\ncan do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nScientists in Egypt dug up 40-million-year-old fossil bones. They had evidence of primitive whales.\nThe whale ancestors are called mesonychids. They were small, furry land mammals with four legs. These\nlimbs were complete with kneecaps, ankles, and little toes.\nGigantic modern whales have tiny hind legs inside their bodies and flippers instead of front legs.\nScientists are certain that these two very different creatures share the same family tree.\n26c Inverting the normal word order\nThe word order of subject, verb, and object or complement is strongly fixed in English (see\npp. 238–41). Thus an inverted sentence can be emphatic:\nVoters once had some faith in politicians, and they were fond of in-cumbents. But now all politicians,\nespecially incumbents, voters seem to detest. [The object all politicians precedes the verb detests.]\nInverting the normal order of subject, verb, and complement can be useful in two successive\nsentences when the second expands on the first:\nCritics have not been kind to Presidents who have tried to apply the ways of private business to public\naffairs. Particularly explicit was the curt verdict of one critic of President Hoover: Mr. Hoover was never\nPresident of the United States; he was four years chairman of the board.\n—Adapted from Emmet John Hughes,\n―The Presidency vs. Jimmy Carter‖\nInverted sentences used without need are artificial. Avoid descriptive sentences such as Up\ncame Ben and down went Katie’s spirits.\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 23\n26d Mixing types of sentences\nMost written sentences make statements. Occasionally, however, questions, commands, or\nexclamations may enhance variety.\nQuestions may set the direction of a paragraph, as in What does a detective do? or How is the\npercentage of unemployed workers calculated? More often, though, the questions used in\nexposition or\nargument do not require answers but simply emphasize ideas that readers can be expected to agree\nwith. Such rhetorical questions are illustrated in the following passage:\nAnother word that has ceased to have meaning due to overuse is at-tractive. Attractive has become verbal\nchaff. Who, by some stretch of language and imagination, cannot be described as attractive? And just\nwhat is it that attractive individuals are attracting? —Diane White\nCommands occur frequently in an explanation of a process, particularly in directions, as this\npassage on freewriting illustrates:\nThe idea is simply to write for ten minutes (later on, perhaps fifteen or\ntwenty). Don’t stop for anything. Go quickly, without rushing. Never stop\nto look back, to cross something out, to wonder how to spell something, to\nwonder what word or thought to use, or to think about what you are doing.\n—Peter Elbow\nNotice that the authors of these examples use questions and commands to achieve some\nspecial purpose. Variety occurs because a particular sentence type is effective for the context, not\nbecause the writer set out to achieve variety for its own sake.\nEXERCISE 26.4 Writing varied sentences\nImagine that you are writing an essay on a transportation problem at your school. Practice varying\nsentences by composing a sentence or passage to serve each purpose listed below. (You can do this\nexercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\n1. Write a question that could open the essay.\n2. Write a command that could open the essay.\n3. Write an exclamation that could open the essay.\n4. For the body of the essay, write an appropriately varied paragraph of at least five sentences, including\nat least one short and one long sentence beginning with the subject; at least one sentence beginning\nwith an adverb modifier; at least one sentence beginning with a coordinating conjunction or\ntransitional expression; and one rhetorical question or command.\nEXERCISE 26.5 Analyzing variety\nExamine the following paragraph for sentence variety. By analyzing your own response to each sentence,\ntry to explain why the author wrote each short or long sentence, each cumulative or periodic sentence,\neach sentence beginning with its subject or beginning some other way, and each question. (You can do\nthis exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nThat night in my rented room, while letting the hot water run over my can of pork and beans in the\nsink, I opened [H. L. Mencken’s] A Book of Prefaces and began to read. I was jarred and shocked by the\nstyle, the clear, clean, sweeping sentences. Why did he write like that? And how did one write like that? I\npictured the man as a raging demon, slashing with his pen, consumed with hate, denouncing everything\nAmerican, extolling everything European or German, laughing at the weaknesses of people, mocking\nGod, authority. What was this? I stood up, trying to realize what reality lay behind the meaning of the\nwords. Yes, this man was fighting, fighting with words. He was using words as a weapon, using them as\none would use a club. Could words be weapons? Well, yes, for here they were. Then, maybe, perhaps, I\ncould use them as a weapon? No. It frightened me. I read on and what amazed me was not what he said,\nbut how on earth anybody had the courage to say it. —Richard Wright, Black Boy\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 24\nEXERCISE 26.6 Revising: Variety\nThe following paragraph consists entirely of simple sentences that begin with their subjects. As\nappropriate, use the techniques discussed in this chapter to vary sentences. Your goal is to make the\nparagraph more readable and make its important ideas stand out clearly. You will have to delete, add,\nchange, and rearrange words. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nThe Italian volcano Vesuvius had been dormant for many years. It then exploded on August 24 in\nthe year AD 79. The ash, pumice, and mud from the volcano buried two busy towns. Herculaneum is one.\nThe more famous is Pompeii. Both towns lay undiscovered for many centuries. Herculaneum and\nPompeii were discovered in 1709 and 1748, respectively. The excavation of Pompeii was the more\nsystematic. It was the occasion for initiating modern methods of conservation and restoration.\nHerculaneum was simply looted of its most valuable finds. It was then left to disintegrate. Pompeii\nappears much as it did before the eruption. A luxurious house opens onto a lush central garden. An\nelection poster decorates a wall. A dining table is set for breakfast.\nEXERCISE ON CHAPTERS 23–26 Revising: Effective sentences\nRevise the paragraphs below to emphasize main ideas, de-emphasize supporting information, and achieve\na pleasing, clear variety in sentences. As appropriate, employ the techniques discussed in Chapters 23–26,\nsuch as using subjects and verbs effectively, subordinating and coordinating, creating parallelism, and\nvarying sentence beginnings. Edit the finished product for punctuation. (You can do this exercise online\nat ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nModern Americans owe many debts to Native Americans. Several pleasures are among the debts.\nNative Americans originated two fine junk foods. They discovered popcorn. Potato chips were also one of\ntheir contributions.\nThe introduction of popcorn to the European settlers came from Native Americans. Massasoit\nprovided popcorn at the first Thanksgiving feast. The Aztecs offered popcorn to the Spanish explorer\nHernando Cortés. The Aztecs wore popcorn necklaces. So did the natives of the West Indies. There were\nthree ways that the Native Americans popped the corn. First, they roasted an ear over fire. The ear was\nskewered on a stick. They ate only some of the popcorn. They ate the corn that fell outside the flames.\nSecond, they scraped the corn off the cob. The kernels would be thrown into the fire. Of course, the fire\nhad to be low. Then the popped kernels that did not fall into the fire were eaten. The third method was the\nmost sophisticated. It involved a shallow pottery vessel. It contained sand. The vessel was heated. The\nsand soon got hot. Corn kernels were stirred in. They popped to the surface of the sand and were eaten.\nA Native American chef was responsible for devising the crunchy potato chip. His name was\nGeorge Crum. In 1853 Crum was cooking at Moon Lake Lodge. The lodge was in Saratoga Springs, New\nYork. Complaints were sent in by a customer. The man thought Crum’s french-fried potatoes were too\nthick. Crum tried a thinner batch. These were also unsuitable. Crum became frustrated. He deliberately\nmade the potatoes thin and crisp. They could not be cut with a knife and fork. Crum’s joke backfired. The\ncustomer raved about the potato chips. The chips were named Saratoga Chips. Soon they appeared on the\nlodge’s menu. They also appeared throughout New England. Crum later opened his own restaurant. Of\ncourse, he offered potato chips.\nNow all Americans munch popcorn in movies. They crunch po-tato chips at parties. They gorge on\nboth when alone and bored. They can be grateful to Native Americans for these guilty pleasures.\nWays to emphasize ideas\nUse the subjects and verbs of sentences to state key actors and actions (below).\nUse the beginnings and endings of sentences to pace and stress information (p. 386).\nArrange series items in order of increasing importance (p. 387).\nUse an occasional balanced sentence (p. 389).\nCarefully repeat key words and phrases (p. 390).\nSet off important ideas with punctuation (p. 391).\nWrite concisely (p. 392).\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 25\nVisit the companion Web site for more help and additional exercises on emphasis.\nWays to achieve conciseness\nMake the subject and verb of each sentence identify its actor and action (pp. 384, 529):\nAvoid nouns made from verbs.\nUse strong verbs.\nRewrite the passive voice as active.\nCut or shorten empty words or phrases (p. 531):\nShorten filler phrases, such as by virtue of the fact that.\nCut all-purpose words, such as area, factor.\nCut unneeded qualifiers, such as in my opinion, for the most part.\nCut unnecessary repetition (p. 532).\nReduce clauses to phrases and phrases to single words (p. 534).\nAvoid constructions beginning with there is or it is (p. 534).\nCombine sentences (p. 535).\nCut or rewrite jargon (p. 535).\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 26\nVisit the companion Web site for more help and additional exercises on coordination and subordination.\nCoordinating to relate equal ideas\nWays to coordinate information in sentences\nLink main clauses with a comma and a coordinating conjunction: and, but, or, nor, for, so, yet (p.\n432).\nIndependence Hall in Philadelphia is now restored, but fifty years ago it was in bad shape.\nRelate main clauses with a semicolon alone or a semicolon and a conjunctive adverb: however,\nindeed, thus, etc. (pp. 453, 455).\nThe building was standing; however, it suffered from neglect.\nWithin clauses, link words and phrases with a coordinating conjunction: and, but, or, nor (p.\n259).\nThe people and officials of the nation were indifferent to Independence Hall or took it for granted.\nLink main clauses, words, or phrases with a correlative conjunction: both . . . and, not only . . .\nbut also, etc. (p. 260).\nPeople not only took the building for granted but also neglected it.\nWays to subordinate information in sentences\nUse a subordinate clause beginning with a subordinating conjunction: although, because, if,\nwhereas, etc. (p. 253).\nAlthough some citizens had tried to rescue Independence Hall, they had not gained substantial public\nsupport.\nUse a subordinate clause beginning with a relative pronoun: who, whoever, which, that (p. 254).\nThe first strong step was taken by the federal government, which made the building a national\nmonument.\nUse a phrase (p. 244).\nLike most national monuments, Independence Hall is protected by the National Park Service.\n[Prepositional phrase.]\nProtecting many popular tourist sites, the service is a highly visible gov-ernment agency. [Verbal\nphrase.]\nUse an appositive (p. 257).\nThe National Park Service, a branch of the Department of Interior, also runs Yosemite and other\nwilderness parks.\nUse a modifying word.\nAt the red brick Independence Hall, park rangers give guided tours and protect the irreplaceable\nbuilding from vandalism.\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 27\nVisit the companion Web site for more help and additional exercises on parallelism.\nPatterns of parallelism\nUse parallel structures for elements connected by coordinating conjunctions (and, but, or, etc.)\nor correlative conjunctions (both . . . and, neither . . . nor, etc.):\nIn 1988 a Greek cyclist, backed up by engineers,\nphysiologists,\nand athletes,\nbroke the world’s record for human flight\nwith neither a boost\nnor a motor.\nUse parallel structures for elements being compared or contrasted:\nPedal power\nrather than horse power\npropelled the plane.\nUse parallel structures for lists, outlines, or headings:\nThe four-hour flight was successful because\n(1) the cyclist was very fit,\n(2) he flew a straight course over water,\nand (3) he kept the aircraft near the water’s surface.\nImproved\nChanges in Renaissance\nEngland\n1. Extension of trade routes\n2. Increased power of the\nmerchant class\n3. Death of feudalism\n4. Upsurge of the arts\n5. Encouragement of the\nsciences\n6. Rise of religious quarrels\nWays to achieve variety among sentences\nVary the length and structure of sentences so that important ideas stand out (opposite).\nVary the beginnings of sentences with modifiers, transitional words and expressions, and occasional\nexpletive constructions (p. 415).\nOccasionally, invert the normal order of subject, verb, and object or complement (p. 418).\nOccasionally, use a command, question, or exclamation (p. 418).\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp23-26.doc - 28\nVisit the companion Web site for more help and an additional exercise on sentence variety.\nSentence length and structure\nSuspenseful periodic sentence (p. 388) focuses\nattention on meeting; details of place, time, and cause are in opening subordinate\nclause\nShort sentence sums up\nCumulative sentence (p. 387) reflects linger-ing obstacles to peace\nShort final sentence indicates futility of further struggle\n\nFOWLcp27-29.doc - 1\n6\nPART\n\nFOWLcp27-29.doc - 2\nPunctuation\n27\nCHAPTER\nEnd Punctuation\n28\nCHAPTER\nThe Comma\n29\nCHAPTER\nThe Semicolon\n30\nCHAPTER\nThe Apostrophe\n31\nCHAPTER\nQuotation Marks\n32\nCHAPTER\nOther Punctuation Marks\n\nFOWLcp27-29.doc - 3\n27\nCHAPTER\nEnd Punctuation\nEnd punctuation marks—the period, the question mark, and the exclamation point—signal the\nends of sentences.\nNote A grammar checker may flag missing question marks after direct questions or incorrect\ncombinations of marks (such as a question mark and a period at the end of a sentence), but it\ncannot do much else.\n27a Use periods after most sentences and with some\nabbreviations.\n1 Use a period to end a statement, mild command,\nor indirect question.\nStatements\nThese are exciting and trying times.\nThe airline went bankrupt.\nMild commands\nPlease do not smoke.\nThink of the possibilities.\nIf you are unsure whether to use an exclamation point or a period after a command, use a\nperiod. The exclamation point should be used only rarely (see p. 429).\nAn indirect question reports what someone has asked but not in the form or exact words of\nthe original:\nIndirect questions\nStudents sometimes wonder whether their teachers read the papers they write.\nAbused children eventually stop asking why they are being punished.\nIn standard American English, an indirect question uses the wording and\nsubject-verb order of a statement: The reporter asked why the negotiations\nfailed, not why did the negotiations fail.\n2 Use periods with some abbreviations.\nUse periods with abbreviations that consist of or end in small letters. Otherwise, omit periods\nfrom abbreviations.\nDr. Mr., Mrs. e.g. Feb. ft.\nSt. Ms. i.e. p. a.m., p.m.\nPhD BC, BCE USA IBM AM, PM\nBA AD, CE US USMC AIDS\nNote When a sentence ends in an abbreviation with a period, don’t add a second period: My\nfirst class is at 8 a.m.\nSee also pages 500–03 on uses of abbreviations in writing.\n\nFOWLcp27-29.doc - 4\nEXERCISE 27.1 Revising: Periods\nRevise the following sentences so that periods are used correctly. (See\nopposite.)\nQuestions in a series are each followed by a question mark:\nThe officer asked how many times the suspect had been arrested.\nThree times? Four times? More than that?\nThe use of capital letters for questions in a series is optional (see p. 491).\nNote Question marks are never combined with other question marks, exclamation points,\nperiods, or commas:\nFaulty ―What is the point?,‖ readers ask.\nRevised ―What is the point?‖ readers ask.\n2 Use a question mark within parentheses to indicate doubt about a number or date.\nThe Greek philosopher Socrates was born in 470 (?) BC and died in 399 BC from drinking poison after\nhaving been condemned to death.\nNote Don’t use a question mark within parentheses to express sarcasm or irony. Express these\nattitudes through sentence structure and word choice. (See Chapters 23 and 38.)\nFaulty Stern’s friendliness (?) bothered Crane.\nRevised Stern’s insincerity bothered Crane.\nEXERCISE 27.2 Revising: Question marks\nRevise the following sentences so that question marks (along with other punctuation marks) are used\ncorrectly. We must not lose this election!\nCome here immediately!\nFollow mild interjections and commands with commas or periods, as appropriate:\nNo, the response was not terrific.!\nTo prolong your car’s life, change its oil regularly.\nUse exclamation points sparingly, not to express sarcasm, irony, or\namazement. Rely on sentence structure and word choice to express these\nattitudes. (See Chapters 23 and 38.)\nFaulty After traveling 4.4 billion miles through space, Voyager 2 was off-target by 21 miles (!).\nRevised After traveling 4.4 billion miles through space, Voyager 2 was off-target by a mere 21 miles.\nRelying on the exclamation point for emphasis is like crying wolf: the mark loses its power to\nimpress the reader. Frequent exclamation points can also make writing sound overemotional:\nOverused exclamation points\nOur city government is a mess! After just six months in office, the mayor has had to fire four city\nofficials! In the same period the city councilors have done nothing but argue! And city services decline\nwith each passing day!\nNote Exclamation points are never combined with other exclamation points, question marks,\nperiods, or commas:\nFaulty ―This will not be endured!,‖ he roared.\nRevised ―This will not be endured!‖ he roared.\nEXERCISE 27.3 Revising: Exclamation points\nRevise the following sentences so that exclamation points (along with other punctuation marks) are used\ncorrectly. Let me tell you about it.\nEXERCISE 27.4 Revising: End punctuation\nInsert appropriate punctuation (periods, question marks, or exclamation points) where needed in the\nfollowing paragraph. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nWhen visitors first arrive in Hawaii, they often encounter an unexpected language barrier Standard\nEnglish is the language of business and government, but many of the people speak Pidgin English Instead\nof an excited ―Aloha‖ the visitors may be greeted with an excited\nPidgin ―Howzit‖ or asked if they know ―how fo’ find one good hotel‖ Many Hawaiians question whether\nPidgin will hold children back because it prevents communication with the haoles, or Caucasians, who\nrun businesses Yet many others feel that Pidgin is a last defense of ethnic diversity on the islands To\nthose who want to make standard\nEnglish the official language of the state, these Hawaiians may respond, ―Just ’cause I speak Pidgin no\nmean I dumb‖ They may ask, ―Why you no listen‖ or, in standard English, ―Why don’t you listen‖\n\nFOWLcp27-29.doc - 6\nNote See page 488 for a punctuation exercise combining periods with other marks of\npunctuation.\n28\nCHAPTER\nThe Comma\nCommas usually function within sentences to separate elements (see the box on the next\npage). Omitting needed commas or inserting needless ones can confuse the reader:\nComma Though very tall Abraham Lincoln was not an overbearing\nneeded man.\nRevised Though very tall, Abraham Lincoln was not an overbearing man.\nUnneeded The hectic pace of Beirut, broke suddenly into frightening\ncommas chaos when the city became, the focus of civil war.\nRevised The hectic pace of Beirut broke suddenly into frightening chaos when the city became the\nfocus of civil war.\nNote Grammar and style checkers will ignore many comma er-rors. For example, a checker\nfailed to catch the missing commas\nin The boat ran aground and we were stranded and in We cooked lasagna spinach and apple pie.\nAt the same time the checker overlooked the misused commas in The trip was short but, the\nweather was perfect and The travelers were tempted by, the many shops, and varied restaurants.\n28a Use a comma before and, but, or another\ncoordinating conjunction linking main clauses.\nThe coordinating conjunctions are and, but, or, nor, for, so, and yet. When these link words or\nphrases, do not use a comma: Dugain plays and sings Irish and English folk songs. However, do\nuse a comma when a coordinating conjunction joins main clauses. A main clause has a subject and\na predicate (but no subordinating word at the beginning) and makes a complete statement (see p.\n252).\nCaffeine can keep coffee drinkers alert, and it may elevate their mood.\nCaffeine was once thought to be safe, but now researchers warn of harmful effects.\nCoffee drinkers may suffer sleeplessness, for the drug acts as a stimulant to the nervous system.\nNote Do not add a comma after a coordinating conjunction between main clauses (see also pp.\n448–49):\nNot Caffeine increases the heart rate, and, it constricts blood vessels.\nBut Caffeine increases the heart rate, and it constricts blood vessels.\nExceptions When the main clauses in a sentence are very long or grammatically complicated,\nor when they contain internal punctuation, a semicolon before the coordinating conjunction will\nclarify the division between clauses (see p. 457):\n\nFOWLcp27-29.doc - 7\nCaffeine may increase alertness, elevate mood, and provide energy; but it may also cause irritability,\nanxiety, stomach pains, and other ills.\nWhen main clauses are very short and closely related in meaning, you may omit the comma\nbetween them as long as the resulting sentence is clear:\nCaffeine helps but it also hurts.\nIf you are in doubt about whether to use a comma in such a sentence, use it. It will always be\ncorrect.\nEXERCISE 28.1 Punctuating linked main clauses\nInsert a comma before each coordinating conjunction that links main clauses in the following sentences.\n(You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nI would have attended the concert and the reception but I had to baby-sit for my niece.\nI would have attended the concert and the reception, but I had to baby-sit for my niece.\n1. Parents once automatically gave their children the father’s surname but some no longer\ndo.\n2. Instead, they bestow the mother’s name for they believe that the mother’s importance should be\nrecognized.\n3. The child’s surname may be just the mother’s or it may link the mother’s and the father’s with a\nhyphen.\n4. Sometimes the first and third children will have the mother’s surname and the second child will have\nthe father’s.\n5. Occasionally the mother and father combine parts of their names and a new hybrid surname is born.\nEXERCISE 28.2 Sentence combining: Linked main clauses\nCombine each group of sentences below into one sentence that contains only two main clauses connected\nby the coordinating conjunction in parentheses. Separate the main clauses with a comma. You will have\nto add, delete, and rearrange words. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nThe circus had come to town. The children wanted to see it. Their parents wanted to see it. (and)\nThe circus had come to town, and the children and their parents wanted to see it.\n1. Parents were once legally required to bestow the father’s surname on their children. These laws have\nbeen contested in court. They have been found invalid. (but)\n2. Parents may now give their children any surname they choose. The arguments for bestowing the\nmother’s surname are often strong. They are often convincing. (and)\n3. Critics sometimes question the effects of unusual surnames on children. They wonder how confusing\nthe new surnames will be. They wonder how fleeting the surnames will be. (or)\n4. Children with surnames different from their parents’ may suffer embarrassment. They may suffer\nidentity problems. Giving children their father’s surname is still very much the norm. (for)\n5. Hyphenated names are awkward. They are also difficult to pass on. Some observers think they will\ndie out in the next generation. Or they may die out before. (so)\n28b Use a comma to set off most introductory elements.\nAn introductory element modifies a word or words in the main clause that follows. These\nelements are usually set off from the rest of the sentence with a comma:\nSubordinate clause (p. 252)\nEven when identical twins are raised apart, they grow up very like each other.\nBecause they are similar, such twins interest scientists.\nVerbal or verbal phrase (p. 247)\nExplaining the similarity, some researchers claim that one’s genes are one’s destiny.\nConcerned, other researchers deny the claim.\n\nFOWLcp27-29.doc - 8\nPrepositional phrase (p. 245)\nIn a debate that has lasted centuries, scientists use identical twins to argue for or against genetic destiny.\nTransitional or parenthetical expression (pp. 85–86)\nOf course, scientists can now look directly at the genes themselves.\nThe comma may be omitted after short introductory elements if its omission does not create\nconfusion. (If you are in doubt, however, the comma is always correct.)\nClear In a hundred years genetics may no longer be a mystery.\nConfusing Despite intensive research scientists still have more questions than answers.\nClear Despite intensive research, scientists still have more questions than answers.\nNote Take care to distinguish -ing words used as modifiers from -ing words used as subjects,\nas shown in the following examples. The former almost always take a comma; the latter never do.\nmodifier subject verb\nStudying identical twins, geneticists learn about inheritance.\nsubject verb\nStudying identical twins helps geneticists learn about inheritance.\nEXERCISE 28.3 Punctuating introductory elements\nInsert commas where needed after introductory elements in the following sentences. If a sentence is\npunctuated correctly as given, mark the number preceding it. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/ littlebrown.)\nExample:\nAfter the new library opened the old one became a student union.\nAfter the new library opened, the old one became a student union.\n1. Moving in a fluid mass is typical of flocks of birds and schools of fish.\n2. Because it is sudden and apparently well coordinated the movement of flocks and schools has\nseemed to be directed by a leader.\n3. However new studies have discovered that flocks and schools are leaderless.\n4. When each bird or fish senses a predator it follows individual rules for fleeing.\n5. Multiplied over hundreds of individuals these responses look as if they have been choreographed.\nEXERCISE 28.4 Sentence combining: Introductory elements\nCombine each pair of sentences below into one sentence that begins with an introductory phrase or clause\nas specified in parentheses. Follow the introductory element with a comma. You will have to add, delete,\nchange, and rearrange words. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nThe girl was humming to herself. She walked upstairs. (Phrase beginning Humming.)\nHumming to herself, the girl walked upstairs.\n1. Scientists have made an effort to explain the mysteries of flocks and schools. They have proposed\nbizarre magnetic fields and telepathy. (Phrase beginning In.)\n2. Scientists developed computer models. They have abandoned earlier explanations. (Clause beginning\nSince.)\n3. The movement of a flock or school starts with each individual. It is rapidly and perhaps\nautomatically coordinated among individuals. (Phrase beginning Starting.)\n4. One zoologist observes that human beings seek coherent patterns. He suggests that investigators saw\npurpose in the movement of flocks and schools where none existed. (Phrase begin- ning Observing.)\n5. One may want to study the movement of flocks or schools. Then one must abandon a search for\npurpose or design. (Phrase beginning To.)\n28c Use a comma or commas to set off nonessential\nelements.\nCommas around part of a sentence often signal that the element is not essential to the meaning\nof the sentence:\n\nFOWLcp27-29.doc - 9\nNonessential element\nThe company, which is located in Oklahoma, has a good reputation.\nThis nonessential element may modify or rename the word it refers to (company in the example), but it\ndoes not limit the word to a particular individual or group. (Because it does not restrict meaning, a\nnonessential element is also called a nonrestrictive element.) Non-essential elements are not\nessential, but punctuation is.\nIn contrast, an essential (or restrictive) element does limit the word it refers to:\nEssential element\nThe company rewards employees who work hard.\nIn this example the underlined essential element cannot be omitted without leaving the meaning of\nemployees too general. Because it is essential, such an element is not set off with commas. The\nelement is essential, but punctuation is not.\nMeaning and context\nThe same element in the same sentence may be essential or nonessential depending on your\nintended meaning and the context in which the sentence appears. For example, look at the second\nsentence in each of the following passages:\nEssential\nNot all the bands were equally well received, however. The band\nplaying old music held the audience’s attention. The other groups created much less excitement. [Playing\nold music identifies a particular band.]\nNonessential\nA new band called Fats made its debut on Saturday night. The band, playing old music, held the\naudience’s attention. If this performance is typical, the group has a bright future. [Playing old music adds\ninformation about a band already named.]\nPunctuation of interrupting nonessential elements\nWhen a nonessential element falls in the middle of a sentence, be sure to set it off with a pair\nof commas, one before and one after the element. Dashes or parentheses may also set off\nnonessential elements (see pp. 480 and 482).\n1 Use a comma or commas to set off nonessential clauses and phrases.\nClauses and phrases serving as adjectives and adverbs may be either nonessential or essential.\nIn the following examples the underlined clauses and phrases are nonessential: they could be\nomitted without changing the meaning of the words they modify.\nNonessential\nElizabeth Blackwell was the first woman to graduate from an American medical school, in 1849.\nShe was a medical pioneer, helping to found the first medical college for women.\nShe taught at the school, which was affiliated with the New York Infirmary.\nBlackwell, who published books and papers on medicine, practiced pediatrics and gynecology.\nShe moved to England in 1869, when she was forty-eight.\nNote Most adverb clauses are essential because they describe conditions necessary to the main\nclause. They are set off by a comma only when they introduce sentences (see p. 433) and when they\nare truly nonessential, adding incidental information (as in the last example above) or expressing a\ncontrast beginning although, even though, though, whereas, and the like.\nIn the following sentences, the underlined elements limit the meaning of the words they\nmodify. Removing the elements would leave the meaning too general.\nEssential\nThe history of aspirin began with the ancient Greeks.\nPhysicians who sought to relieve their patients’ painsPhysicians recommended chewing willow bark.\nWillow bark contains a chemical that is similar to aspirin.\n\nFOWLcp27-29.doc - 10\nNote Whereas both nonessential and essential clauses may be-gin with which, only essential\nclauses begin with that. Some writers prefer that exclusively for essential clauses and which\nexclusively for nonessential clauses. See the Glossary of Usage, page 879, for advice on the use of\nthat and which.\n2 Use a comma or commas to set off nonessential appositives.\nAn appositive is a noun or noun substitute that renames another noun just before it. (See p.\n257.) Many appositives are non-\nessential; thus they are set off, usually with commas.\nNonessential\nToni Morrison’s fifth novel, Beloved, won the Pulitzer Prize in 1988.\nMorrison, a native of Ohio, won the Nobel Prize in 1993.\nTake care not to set off essential appositives; like other essential elements, they limit or define\nthe word to which they refer.\nEssential\nMorrison’s novel The Bluest Eyeis about an African American girl who longs for blue eyes.\nThe critic Michiko Kakutani says that Morrison’s work ―stands radi-antly on its own as an American\nepic.‖\n3 Use a comma or commas to set off transitional or parenthetical expressions.\nTransitional expressions\nTransitional expressions form links between ideas. They include conjunctive adverbs such as\nhowever and moreover as well as other words or phrases such as for example and of course. (See\npp. 85–86 for a list of transitional words and phrases.) Transitional expressions are nonessential, so\nset them off with a comma or commas:\nAmerican workers, for example, receive fewer holidays than European workers do.\nWhen a transitional expression links main clauses, precede it with a semicolon and follow it with a\ncomma. (See p. 455.)\nEuropean workers often have long paid vacations; indeed, they may receive a full month.\nNote The conjunctions and, but, and yet are sometimes used as transitional expressions but are\nnot followed by commas (see p. 448). Nor are commas required after some transitional expressions\nthat we read without pauses, such as also, hence, next, now, and thus. A few transitional\nexpressions, notably therefore and instead, do not need commas when they fall inside or at the ends\nof clauses.\nAmerican workers thus put in more work days. But the days themselves may be shorter.\nParenthetical expressions\nParenthetical expressions provide comments, explanations,\ndigressions, or other supplementary information not essential to meaning—for example,\nfortunately, unfortunately, all things considered, to be frank, in other words. Set parenthetical\nexpressions off with commas:\nFew people would know, or even guess, the most celebrated holiday on earth.\nThat holiday is, surprisingly, New Year’s Day.\n(Dashes and parentheses may also set off parenthetical expressions. See pp. 480 and 482,\nrespectively.)\n\nFOWLcp27-29.doc - 11\n4 Use a comma or commas to set off yes and no, tag questions, words of direct address,\nand mild interjections.\nYes and no\nYes, the editorial did have a point.\nNo, that can never be.\nTag questions\nJones should be allowed to vote, should he not?\nThey don’t stop to consider others, do they?\nDirect address\nCody, please bring me the newspaper.\nWith all due respect, sir, I will not do that.\nMild interjections\nWell, you will never know who did it.\nOh, they forgot all about the baby.\n(You may want to use an exclamation point to set off a forceful interjection. See p. 428.)\nEXERCISE 28.5 Punctuating essential and nonessential elements\nInsert commas in the following sentences to set off nonessential\nelements, and delete any commas that incorrectly set off essential elements. If a sentence is correct as\ngiven, mark the number preceding it. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nOur language has adopted the words, garage and fanfare, from the French.\nOur language has adopted the words garage and fanfare from the French.\n1. Italians insist that Marco Polo the thirteenth-century explorer did not import pasta from China.\n2. Pasta which consists of flour and water and often egg existed in Italy long before Marco Polo left for\nhis travels.\n3. A historian who studied pasta says that it originated in the Middle East in the fifth century.\n4. Most Italians dispute this account although their evidence is shaky.\n5. Wherever pasta originated, the Italians are now the undisputed masters, in making and cooking it.\n6. Marcella Hazan, who has written several books on Italian cooking, insists that homemade and hand-\nrolled pasta is the best.\n7. Most cooks must buy dried pasta lacking the time to make their own.\n8. The finest pasta is made from semolina, a flour from hard durum wheat.\n9. Pasta manufacturers choose hard durum wheat, because it makes firmer cooked pasta than common\nwheat does.\n10. Pasta, made from common wheat, tends to get soggy in boiling water.\nEXERCISE 28.6 Sentence combining: Essential and nonessential elements\nCombine each pair of sentences below into one sentence that uses the element described in parentheses.\nInsert commas as appropriate. You will have to add, delete, change, and rearrange words. Some items\nhave more than one possible answer. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nMr. Ward’s oldest sister helped keep him alive. She was a nurse in the hospital. (Nonessential clause\nbeginning who.)\nMr. Ward’s oldest sister, who was a nurse in the hospital, helped keep him alive.\n1. American colonists first imported pasta from the English. The English had discovered it as tourists in\nItaly. (Nonessential clause beginning who.)\n2. The English returned from their grand tours of Italy. They were called macaronis because of their\nfancy airs. (Essential phrase beginning returning.)\n3. A hair style was also called macaroni. It had elaborate curls. (Essential phrase beginning with.)\n4. The song ―Yankee Doodle‖ refers to this hairdo. It reports that Yankee Doodle ―stuck a feather in his\ncap and called it macaroni.‖ (Essential clause beginning when.)\n\nFOWLcp27-29.doc - 12\n5. (Nonessential appositive beginning a creation.)\n28d Use a comma or commas to set off absolute phrases.\nAn absolute phrase modifies a whole main clause rather than any word in the clause, and it\nusually consists of at least a participle (such as done or having torn) and its subject (a noun or\npronoun). (See p. 251.) Absolute phrases can occur at almost any point in the sentence, and they\nare always set off by a comma or commas:\nHousehold recycling having succeeded, the city now wants to extend the program to businesses.\nMany businesses, their profits already squeezed, resist recycling.\n28e Use a comma or commas to set off phrases\nexpressing contrast.\nThe essay needs less wit, more pith.\nThe substance, not the style, is important.\nSubstance, unlike style, cannot be faked.\nNote Writers often omit commas around contrasting phrases beginning\nwith but: A full but hazy moon shone down.\nEXERCISE 28.7 Punctuating absolute phrases and phrases of contrast\nInsert commas in the following sentences to set off absolute phrases and phrases of contrast. (You can do\nthis exercise online at ablongman\n.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nThe recording contract was canceled the band having broken up.\nThe recording contract was canceled, the band having broken up.\n1. Prices having risen rapidly the government debated a price freeze.\n2. A price freeze unlike a rise in interest rates seemed a sure solution.\n3. The President would have to persuade businesses to accept a price freeze his methods depending on\ntheir resistance.\n4. No doubt the President his advisers having urged it would first try a patriotic appeal.\n5. The President not his advisers insisted on negotiations with businesses.\n28f Use commas between items in a series and\nbetween coordinate adjectives.\n1 Use commas between words, phrases, or clauses forming\na series.\nPlace commas between all elements of a series—that is, three or more items of equal\nimportance:\nAnna Spingle married at the age of seventeen, had three children by twenty-one, and divorced at twenty-\ntwo.\nShe worked as a cook, a baby-sitter, and a crossing guard.\nSome writers omit the comma before the coordinating conjunction in a series (Breakfast consisted\nof coffee, eggs and kippers). But the\nfinal comma is never wrong, and it always helps the reader see the last two items as separate:\n\nFOWLcp27-29.doc - 13\nConfusing Spingle’s new job involves typing, filing and answering correspondence.\nClear Spingle’s new job involves typing, filing, and answering correspondence.\nException When items in a series are long and grammatically complicated, they may be\nseparated by semicolons. When the items contain commas, they must be separated by semicolons.\n(See p. 257.)\n2 Use commas between two or more adjectives that equally modify the same word.\nWhen two or more adjectives modify the same word equally, they are said to be coordinate.\nThe adjectives may be separated either by and or by a comma, as in the following examples.\nSpingle’s scratched and dented car is old, but it gets her to work.\nShe has dreams of a sleek, shiny car.\nAdjectives are not coordinate—and should not be separated by commas—when the one nearer the\nnoun is more closely related to the noun in meaning. In each of the next examples, the second\nadjective and the noun form a unit that is modified by the first adjective:\nSpingle’s children work at various part-time jobs.\nThey all expect to go to a nearby community college.\nSee the box above for a test to use in punctuating adjectives.\nNote Numbers are not coordinate with other adjectives:\nFaulty Spingle has three, teenaged children.\nRevised Spingle has three teenaged children.\nDo not use a comma between the final adjective and the noun:\nFaulty The children hope to achieve good, well-paying, jobs.\nRevised The children hope to achieve good, well-paying jobs.\nEXERCISE 28.8 Punctuating series and coordinate adjectives\nInsert commas in the following sentences to separate coordinate adjectives or elements in series. Mark the\nnumber preceding each sentence whose punctuation is already correct. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nQuiet by day, the club became a noisy smoky dive at night.\nQuiet by day, the club became a noisy, smoky dive at night.\n1. Shoes with high heels originated to protect feet from the mud garbage and animal waste in the\nstreets.\n2. The first known high heels worn strictly for fashion appeared in the sixteenth century.\n3. The heels were worn by men and made of colorful silk brocades soft suedes or smooth leathers.\n4. High-heeled shoes received a boost when the short powerful King Louis XIV of France began\nwearing them.\n5. Eventually only wealthy fashionable French women wore high heels.\n28g Use commas according to convention in dates,\naddresses, place names, and long numbers.\nUse commas to separate most parts of dates, addresses, and place names: June 20, 1950; 24\nFifth Avenue, Suite 601; Cairo, Illi-nois. Within a sentence, any element preceded by a comma\nshould be followed by a comma as well, as in the examples below:\nDates\nJuly 4, 1776, is the date the Declaration of Independence was signed.\nThe bombing of Pearl Harbor on Sunday, December 7, 1941, prompted American entry into World War\nII.\n\nFOWLcp27-29.doc - 14\nDo not use commas between the parts of a date in inverted order: Their anniversary on 15\nDecember 2005 was their fiftieth. You need\nnot use commas in dates consisting of a month or season and a year: For the United States the war\nended in August 1945.\nAddresses and place names\nColumbus, Ohio, is the state capital and the location of Ohio State University.\nThe population of Garden City, Long Island, New York, is 30,000.\nUse the address 220 Cornell Road, Woodside, California 94062, for all correspondence.\nDo not use a comma between a state and a zip code.\nLong numbers\nUse the comma to separate the figures in long numbers into groups of three, counting from the\nright. With numbers of four digits, the comma is optional.\nA kilometer is 3,281 feet [or 3281 feet].\nThe new assembly plant cost $7,535,000 to design and build.\nUsage in American English differs from that in some other languages and\ndialects, which use a period, not a comma, to separate the figures in long\nnumbers.\nEXERCISE 28.9 Punctuating dates, addresses, place names, numbers\nInsert commas as needed in the following sentences.\nExample:\nThe house cost $27000 fifteen years ago.\nThe house cost $27,000 fifteen years ago.\n1. The festival will hold a benefit dinner and performance on March 10 2006 in Asheville.\n2. The organizers hope to raise more than $100000 from donations and ticket sales.\n3. Performers are expected from as far away as Milan Italy and Kyoto Japan.\n4. All inquiries sent to Mozart Festival PO Box 725 Asheville North Carolina 28803 will receive a\nquick response.\n5. The deadline for ordering tickets by mail is Monday December 3 2005.\n28h Use commas with quotations according to\nstandard practice.\nThe words he said, she writes, and so on identify the source of a quotation. These signal\nphrases may come before, after, or in the middle of the quotation. A signal phrase must always be\nseparated from the quotation by punctuation, usually a comma or commas.\nNote Additional issues with quotations are discussed elsewhere in this book:\nUsing quotation marks conventionally, pages 468–75.\nChoosing and transcribing quotations from sources, pages 620–21.\nIntegrating source material into your text, pages 623–28.\nAcknowledging the sources of quotations to avoid plagiarism, pages 633–34 and 637–38.\nFormatting long prose quotations and poetry quotations in MLA style, pages 688–89;\nChicago style, page 776; and APA style, pages 802–03.\n1 Ordinarily, use a comma with a signal phrase before or after a quotation.\nEleanor Roosevelt said, ―You must do the thing you think you cannot do.‖\n―Knowledge is power,‖ writes Francis Bacon.\nExceptions Do not use a comma when a signal phrase follows a quotation ending in an\nexclamation point or a question mark:\n\nFOWLcp27-29.doc - 15\n―Claude!‖ Mrs. Harrison called.\n―Why must I come home?‖ he asked.\nDo not use commas with a quotation introduced by that or with a quotation that is integrated into\nyour sentence structure:\nJames Baldwin insists that ―one must never, in one’s life, accept . . . injustices as commonplace.‖\nBaldwin thought that the violence of a riot ―had been devised as a corrective‖ to his own violence.\nUse a colon instead of a comma between a signal phrase and a quotation when the signal phrase is\nactually a complete sentence and the quotation is very formal or longer than a sentence.\nThe Bill of Rights is unambiguous: ―Congress shall make no law re-specting an establishment of religion,\nor prohibiting the free exercise thereof.‖\n2 With an interrupted quotation, precede the signal phrase with a comma and follow it\nwith the punctuation required by the quotation.\nQuotation\n―The shore has a dual nature, changing with the swing of the tides.‖\nSignal phrase\n―The shore has a dual nature,‖ observes Rachel Carson, ―changing with the swing of the tides.‖ [The\nsignal phrase interrupts the quotation at a comma and thus ends with a comma.]\nQuotation\n―However mean your life is, meet it and live it; do not shun it and call it hard names.‖\nSignal phrase\n―However mean your life is, meet it and live it,‖ Thoreau advises in Walden; ―do not shun it and call it\nhard names.‖ [The signal phrase interrupts the quotation at a semicolon and thus ends with a semicolon.]\nQuotation\n―This is the faith with which I return to the South. With this new faith we will be able to hew out of the\nmountain of despair a stone of hope.‖\nSignal phrase\n―This is the faith with which I return to the South,‖ Martin Luther King, Jr., proclaimed. ―With this new\nfaith we will be able to hew out of the mountain of despair a stone of hope.‖ [The signal phrase interrupts\nthe quotation at the end of a sentence and thus ends with a period.]\nNote Using a comma instead of a semicolon or a period after the Thoreau and King signal\nphrases would result in the error called a comma splice: two main clauses separated only by a\ncomma. (See pp. 342–47.)\n3 Place commas that follow quotations within quotation marks.\n―Death is not the greatest loss in lifem,” claims Norman Cousins.\n―The greatest lossm,” Cousins says, ―is what dies inside us while we live.‖\nEXERCISE 28.10 Punctuating quotations\nInsert commas or semicolons in the following sentences to correct punctuation with quotations. Mark the\nnumber preceding any sentence whose punctuation is already correct. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nThe shoplifter declared ―I didn’t steal anything.‖\nThe shoplifter declared, ―I didn’t steal anything.‖\n1. The writer and writing teacher Peter Elbow proposes an ―open-ended writing process‖ that ―can\nchange you, not just your words.‖\n\nFOWLcp27-29.doc - 16\n2. ―I think of the open-ended writing process as a voyage in two stages‖ Elbow says.\n3. ―The sea voyage is a process of divergence, branching, proliferation, and confusion‖ Elbow\ncontinues ―the coming to land is a process of convergence, pruning, centralizing, and clarifying.‖\n4. ―Keep up one session of writing long enough to get loosened up and tired‖ advises Elbow ―long\nenough in fact to make a bit of a voyage.‖\n5. ―In coming to new land‖ Elbow says ―you develop a new conception of what you are writing about.‖\n28i Use commas to prevent misreading.\nIn some sentences words may run together in unintended and confusing ways unless a comma\nseparates them:\nConfusing Soon after the business closed its doors.\nClear Soon after, the business closed its doors.\nAlways check whether a comma added to prevent misreading might cause some other confusion or error.\nIn the first example below, the comma prevents pasta and places from running into each other as\npasta places, but it separates the subject (historian) and the verb (places). The revision solves both\nproblems.\nFaulty A historian who studied pasta, places its origin in the Middle East.\nRevised A historian who studied pasta says that it originated in the Middle East.\nEXERCISE 28.11 Punctuating to prevent misreading\nInsert commas in the following sentences to prevent misreading. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nTo Laura Ann symbolized decadence.\nTo Laura, Ann symbolized decadence.\n1. Though happy people still have moments of self-doubt.\n2. In research subjects have reported themselves to be generally happy people.\n3. Among those who have life has included sufferings as well as joys.\n4. Of fifty eight subjects reported bouts of serious depression.\n5. For half the preceding year had included at least one personal\ncrisis.\n28j Use commas only where required.\nCommas can make sentences choppy and even confusing if they are used more often than\nneeded. The main misuses of commas are summarized in the box opposite.\n1 Delete any comma after a subject or a verb.\nCommas interrupt the movement from subject to verb to object or complement, as in the\nfollowing faulty examples.\nFaulty The returning soldiers, received a warmer welcome than they expected. [Separation of subject\nand verb.]\nRevised The returning soldiers received a warmer welcome than they expected.\nFaulty They had chosen, to fight for their country. [Separation of verb chosen and object to fight.]\nRevised They had chosen to fight for their country.\nException Use commas between subject, verb, and object or complement only when other\nwords between these elements require punctuation:\n\nFOWLcp27-29.doc - 17\nAmericans, who are preoccupied with other sports, have only re-cently developed an interest in\nprofessional soccer. [Commas set off a nonessential clause.]\n2 Delete any comma that separates a pair of words,\nphrases, or subordinate clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction.\nWhen linking elements with and, or, or another coordinating conjunction, do not use a comma\nunless the elements are main clauses (see p. 432):\nFaulty Banks could, and should help older people manage their money. [Compound helping verb.]\nRevised Banks could and should help older people manage their money.\nFaulty Older people need special assistance because they live on fixed incomes, and because they are\nnot familiar with new accounts, and rates. [Compound subordinate clauses be-cause . . .\nbecause and compound object of preposition with.]\nRevised Older people need special assistance because they live on fixed incomes and because they are\nnot familiar with new accounts and rates.\nFaulty Banks, and community groups can assist the elderly, and eliminate the confusion they often\nfeel. [Compound subject and compound predicate.]\nRevised Banks and community groups can assist the elderly and eliminate the confusion they often\nfeel.\n3 Delete any comma after a conjunction.\nThe coordinating conjunctions (and, but, and so on) and the subordinating conjunctions\n(although, because, and so on) are not followed by commas:\nFaulty Parents of adolescents notice increased conflict at puberty, and, they complain of bickering.\nRevised Parents of adolescents notice increased conflict at puberty, and they complain of bickering.\nFaulty Although, other primates leave the family at adolescence, humans do not.\nRevised Although other primates leave the family at adolescence, humans do not.\n4 Delete any commas that set off essential elements.\nCommas do not set off an essential element, which limits the meaning of the word to which it\nrefers (see p. 435):\nFaulty Hawthorne’s work, The Scarlet Letter, was the first major American novel. [The title is\nessential to distinguish the novel from the rest of Hawthorne’s work.]\nRevised Hawthorne’s work The Scarlet Letter was the first major American novel.\nFaulty The symbols, that Hawthorne uses, have influenced other novelists. [The clause identifies\nwhich symbols have been influential.]\nRevised The symbols that Hawthorne uses have influenced other novelists.\nQuoted or italicized words are essential appositives when they limit the word they refer to (see\np. 437). Do not use commas around an essential appositive:\nFaulty James Joyce’s short story, ―The Dead,‖ was made into an affecting film. [The commas imply\nwrongly that Joyce wrote only one story.]\nRevised James Joyce’s short story ―The Dead‖ was made into an affecting film.\nFaulty The word, open, can be either a verb or an adjective.\nRevised The word open can be either a verb or an adjective.\nThe following sentence requires commas because the quoted\ntitle is a nonessential appositive:\nHer only poem about death, ―Mourning,‖ was printed in The New Yorker.\n\nFOWLcp27-29.doc - 18\n5 Delete any comma before or after a series unless a rule requires it.\nCommas separate the items within a series (p. 441) but do not separate the series from the rest\nof the sentence:\nFaulty The skills of, hunting, herding, and agriculture, sustained the Native Americans.\nRevised The skills of hunting, herding, and agriculture sustained the Native Americans.\nIn the sentence below, the commas around the series are appropriate because the series is a\nnonessential appositive (p. 437):\nThe four major television networks, ABC, CBS, Fox, and NBC, face fierce competition from the cable\nnetworks.\nHowever, many writers prefer to use dashes rather than commas to set off series functioning as\nappositives (see p. 480).\n6 Delete any comma setting off an indirect quotation.\nFaulty The report concluded, that dieting could be more dangerous than overeating.\nRevised The report concluded that dieting could be more dangerous than overeating.\nEXERCISE 28.12 Revising: Needless or misused commas\nRevise the following sentences to eliminate needless or misused commas. Mark the number preceding\neach sentence that is already punctuated correctly. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/\nlittlebrown.)\nExample:\nThe portrait of the founder, that hung in the dining hall, was stolen by pranksters.\nThe portrait of the founder that hung in the dining hall was stolen by pranksters.\n1. Nearly 32 million US residents, speak a first language other than English.\n2. After English the languages most commonly spoken in the United States are, Spanish, French, and\nGerman.\n3. Almost 75 percent of the people, who speak foreign languages, used the words, ―good‖ or ―very\ngood,‖ when judging their proficiency in English.\n4. Recent immigrants, especially those speaking Spanish, Chinese, and Korean, tended to judge their\nEnglish more harshly.\n5. The states with the highest proportion of foreign language speakers, are New Mexico, and California.\nEXERCISE 28.13 Revising: Commas\nInsert commas in the following paragraphs wherever they are needed, and eliminate any misused or\nneedless commas. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nEllis Island New York reopened for business in 1990 but now the customers are tourists not\nimmigrants. This spot which lies in New York Harbor was the first American soil seen, or touched by\nmany of the nation’s immigrants. Though other places also served as ports of entry for foreigners none\nhas the symbolic power of, Ellis Island. Between its opening in 1892 and its closing in 1954, over 20\nmillion people about two-thirds of all immigrants were detained there before taking up their new lives in\nthe United States. Ellis Island processed over 2000 newcomers a day when immigration was at its peak\nbetween 1900 and 1920.\nAs the end of a long voyage and the introduction to the New World Ellis Island must have left\nsomething to be desired. The ―huddled masses‖ as the Statue of Liberty calls them indeed were huddled.\nNew arrivals were herded about kept standing in lines for hours or days yelled at and abused. Assigned\nnumbers they submitted their bodies to the pokings and proddings of the silent nurses and doctors, who\nwere charged with ferreting out the slightest sign of sickness, disability or insanity. That test having been\npassed the immigrants faced interrogation by an official through an interpreter. Those, with names\ndeemed inconveniently long or difficult to pronounce, often found themselves permanently labeled with\nabbreviations, of their names, or with the names, of their hometowns. But, millions survived the\nexamination humiliation and confusion, to take the last short boat ride to New York City. For many of\nthem and especially for their descendants Ellis Island eventually became not a nightmare but the place\nwhere life began.\n\nFOWLcp27-29.doc - 19\nNote See page 488 for a punctuation exercise combining commas with other marks of\npunctuation.\n29\nCHAPTER\nThe Semicolon\nThe semicolon separates equal and balanced sentence elements, usually main clauses (opposite\nthrough p. 456), sometimes items in series (p. 457).\nNote A grammar and style checker can spot a few errors in the use of semicolons. For\nexample, a checker suggested using a semicolon after perfect in The set was perfect, the director\nhad planned every detail, thus correcting a comma splice. But it missed the in-correct semicolon in\nThe set was perfect; deserted streets, dark houses, and gloomy mist (a colon would be correct; see\np. 477).\n29a Use a semicolon between main clauses not joined\nby and, but, or another coordinating conjunction.\nMain clauses contain a subject and a predicate and do not begin with a subordinating word\n(see p. 252). When you join two main clauses in a sentence, you have two primary options for\nseparating them:\nInsert a comma and a coordinating conjunction: and, but, or, nor, for, so, yet. (See p. 432.)\nThe drug does little to relieve symptoms, and it\ncan have side effects.\nInsert a semicolon:\nThe side effects are not minor; some leave the\npatient quite ill.\nNote If you do not link main clauses with a coordinating conjunction and you separate them\nonly with a comma or with no punctuation at all, you will produce a comma splice or a fused\nsentence. (See Chapter 18.)\nEXERCISE 29.1 Punctuating between main clauses\nInsert semicolons to separate main clauses in the following sentences. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nOne man at the auction bid prudently another spent his bank account.\nOne man at the auction bid prudently; another spent his bank account.\n1. More and more musicians are playing computerized instruments more and more listeners are\nworrying about the future of acoustic instruments.\n2. The computer is not the first new technology in music the pipe organ and saxophone were also\ntechnological breakthroughs in their day.\n\nFOWLcp27-29.doc - 20\n3. Musicians have always experimented with new technology audi-ences have always resisted the\nexperiments.\n4. Most computer musicians are not merely following the latest fad they are discovering new sounds\nand new ways to manipulate sound.\n5. Few musicians have abandoned acoustic instruments most value acoustic sounds as much as\nelectronic sounds.\nEXERCISE 29.2 Sentence combining: Related main clauses\nCombine each set of three sentences below into one sentence containing only two main clauses, and insert\na semicolon between the clauses. You will have to add, delete, change, and rearrange words. Most items\nhave more than one possible answer. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nThe painter Andrew Wyeth is widely admired. He is not universally admired. Some critics view his\nwork as sentimental.\nThe painter Andrew Wyeth is widely but not universally admired; some\ncritics view his work as sentimental.\n1. Electronic instruments are prevalent in jazz. They are also prevalent in rock music. They are less\ncommon in classical music.\n2. Jazz and rock change rapidly. They nourish experimentation. They nourish improvisation.\n3. Traditional classical music does not change. Its notes and instrumentation were established by a\ncomposer. The composer was writing decades or centuries ago.\n4. Contemporary classical music not only can draw on tradition. It also can respond to innovations.\nThese are innovations such as jazz rhythms and electronic sounds.\n5. Much contemporary electronic music is more than just one type of music. It is more than just jazz,\nrock, or classical. It is a fusion of all three.\n29b Use a semicolon between main clauses related\nby however,for example, and so on.\nTwo kinds of words can relate main clauses: conjunctive adverbs, such as consequently,\nhence, however, indeed, and thus (see p. 261), and other transitional expressions, such as even so,\nfor example, and of course (see pp. 85–86). When either of these connects two main clauses, the\nclauses should be separated by a semicolon:\nAn American immigrant, Levi Strauss, invented blue jeans in the 1860s; eventually, his product clothed\nworking men throughout the West.\nThe position of the semicolon between main clauses never changes, but the conjunctive\nadverb or transitional expression may move around within a clause. The adverb or expression is\nusually set off with a comma or commas (see p. 436):\nBlue jeans have become fashionable all over the world; however, the American originators still wear\nmore jeans than anyone else.\nBlue jeans have become fashionable all over the world; the American originators, however, still wear\nmore jeans than anyone else.\nIts mobility distinguishes a conjunctive adverb or transitional expression from other connecting\nwords, such as coordinating and subordinating conjunctions. See pages 261–62 on this distinction.\nNote If you use a comma or no punctuation at all between main clauses connected by a\nconjunctive adverb or transitional expression, you will produce a comma splice or a fused sentence.\n(See Chapter 18.)\nEXERCISE 29.3 Punctuating main clauses related by conjunctive adverbs or transitional\nexpressions\nInsert a semicolon in each of the following sentences to separate main clauses related by a conjunctive\nadverb or transitional expression. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\n\nFOWLcp27-29.doc - 21\nExample:\nHe knew that tickets for the concert would be scarce therefore he arrived at the box office hours\nbefore it opened.\nHe knew that tickets for the concert would be scarce; therefore, he\narrived at the box office hours before it opened.\n1. Music is a form of communication like language the basic elements however are not letters but notes.\n2. A computer’s ability to process music depends on what software it can run it must moreover be\nconnected to a system that converts electrical vibration into sound.\n4. Computers and their sound systems can produce many different sounds indeed the number of\npossible sounds is infinite.\n5. You will have to add, delete, change, and rearrange words. Each item has more\nthan one possible answer. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nThe Albanians censored their news. We got little news from them. And\nwhat we got was unreliable. (therefore)\nThe Albanians censored their news; therefore, the little news we got\nfrom them was unreliable.\n1. Most music computers are too expensive for the average consumer. Digital keyboard\ninstruments can be inexpensive. They are widely available. (however)\n2. Inside the keyboard is a small computer. The computer controls a sound synthesizer.
