All academic disciplines use abbreviations in\nsource citations, and much technical writing, such as in the sciences and\nengineering, uses many abbreviations in the document text. For the\nrequirements of the discipline you are writing in, consult one of the style\nguides listed on pages 764 (humanities), 784 (social sciences), and 812\n(natural and applied sciences).\nUsage varies, but writers increasingly omit periods from abbreviations that consist of or end in\ncapital letters: US, BA, USMC, PhD. See page 427 on punctuating abbreviations.\nNote A grammar and style checker may flag some abbreviations, such as ft. (for foot) and st.\n(for street). A spelling checker will flag abbreviations it does not recognize. But neither checker\ncan tell you whether an abbreviation is appropriate for your writing situation or will be clear to\nyour readers.\nUse standard abbreviations for titles immediately\nbefore and after proper names.\nBefore the name After the name\nDr. James Hsu James Hsu, MD\nMr., Mrs., Ms., Hon., St., DDS, DVM, PhD, EdD,\nRev., Msgr., Gen. OSB, SJ, Sr., Jr.\nUse abbreviations such as Rev., Hon., Prof., Rep., Sen., Dr., and St. (for Saint) only if they\nappear with a proper name. Spell them out in the absence of a proper name:\nNot We learned to trust the Dr.\nBut We learned to trust the doctor.\nOr We learned to trust Dr. Kaplan.\nThe abbreviations for academic degrees—PhD, MA, BA, and the like—may be used without a\nproper name: My brother took seven years to get his PhD. It will probably take me just as long to\nearn my BA.\nFamiliar abbreviations and acronyms are\nacceptable in most writing.\nAn acronym is an abbreviation that spells a pronounceable word, such as WHO, NATO, and\nAIDS. These and other abbreviations that use initials are acceptable in most writing as long as\nthey are familiar to readers. Abbreviations of two or more words written in all capital letters may be\nwritten without periods (see p. 427):\nInstitutions LSU, UCLA, TCU\nOrganizations CIA, FBI, YMCA, AFL-CIO\nCorporations IBM, CBS, ITT\nPeople JFK, LBJ, FDR\nCountries US, USA\n\nFOWLcp33-36.doc - 10\nNote If a name or term (such as operating room) appears often in a piece of writing, then its\nabbreviation (OR) can cut down on extra words. Spell out the full term at its first appearance, give\nits abbreviation in parentheses, and use the abbreviation from then\non.\nUse BC, BCE, AD, CE, AM, PM, no., and $ only with\nspecific dates and numbers.\n44 BC 44 BCE 8:05 PM (or p.m.) no. 36 (or No. 36)\nAD 1492 1492 CE 11:26 AM (or a.m.) $7.41\nNot Hospital routine is easier to follow in the AM than in the PM.\nBut Hospital routine is easier to follow in the morning than in the afternoon or evening.\nNote The abbreviation BC (―before Christ‖) always follows a date, whereas AD (anno Domini,\nLatin for ―in the year of the Lord‖) precedes a date. Increasingly, these abbreviations are being re-\nplaced by BCE (―before the common era‖) and CE (―common era‖), respectively. Both follow the\ndate.\nGenerally, reserve Latin abbreviations for source\ncitations and comments in parentheses.\nThe following common Latin abbreviations are generally not italicized or underlined.\ni.e. id est: that is\ncf. confer: compare\ne.g. exempli gratia: for example\net al. et alii: and others\netc. et cetera: and so forth\nNB nota bene: note well\nHe said he would be gone a fortnight (i.e., two weeks).\nTrees, too, are susceptible to disease (e.g., Dutch elm disease).\nBloom et al., editors, Anthology of Light Verse\nSome writers avoid these abbreviations in formal writing, even within parentheses.\nUse Inc., Bros., Co., or & (for and) only in official\nnames of business firms.\nNot The Santini bros. operate a large moving firm in New York City.\nBut The Santini brothers operate a large moving firm in New York City.\nOr Santini Bros. is a large moving firm in New York City.\nNot We read about the Hardy Boys & Nancy Drew.\nBut We read about the Hardy Boys and Nancy Drew.\nSpell out most units of measurement and names of\nplaces, calendar designations, people, and courses.\nIn most academic, general, and business writing, certain words should always be spelled out.\n(In source citations and technical writing, however, these words are more often abbreviated.)\nUnits of measurement\nThe dog is thirty inches [not in.] high.\nThe building is 150 feet [not ft.] tall.\n\nFOWLcp33-36.doc - 11\nException Long phrases such as miles per hour (m.p.h.) or cycles per second (c.p.s.) are\nusually abbreviated, with or without periods: The speed limit on that road was once 75 m.p.h. [or\nmph].\nGeographical names\nThe publisher is in Massachusetts [not Mass. or MA].\nHe came from Auckland, New Zealand [not NZ].\nShe lived on Morrissey Boulevard [not Blvd.].\nExceptions The United States is often referred to as the USA or the US. In writing of the US\ncapital, use the abbreviation DC for District of Columbia when it follows the city’s name:\nWashington, DC.\nNames of days, months, and holidays\nThe truce was signed on Tuesday [not Tues.], April [not Apr.] 16.\nThe Christmas [not Xmas] holidays are uneventful.\nNames of people\nVirginia [not Va.] Woolf was British.\nRobert [not Robt.] Frost wrote accessible poems.\nCourses of instruction\nI’m majoring in political science [not poli. sci.].\nEconomics [not Econ.] is a difficult course.\nEXERCISE 35.1 Revising: Abbreviations\nRevise the following sentences as needed to correct inappropriate use of abbreviations for nontechnical\nwriting. But\nthey can’t tell you whether numerals or spelled-out numbers are\nappropriate for your writing situation.\nUse numerals according to standard practice in\nthe field you are writing in.\nAlways use numerals for numbers that require more than two words to spell out:\nThe leap year has 366 days.\nThe population of Minot, North Dakota, is about 32,800.\nIn nontechnical academic writing, spell out numbers of one or two words:\nTwelve nations signed the treaty.\nThe ball game drew forty-two thousand people. [A hyphenated num-ber may be considered one word.]\nIn much business writing, use numerals for all numbers over ten (five reasons, 11\nparticipants). In technical academic and business writing, such as in science and engineering, use\nnumerals for all numbers over ten, and use numerals for zero through nine when they refer to exact\nmeasurements (2 liters, 1 hour). (Consult one of the style guides listed on pp. 784 and 812 for more\ndetails.)\nNote Use a combination of numerals and words for round numbers over a million: 26 million,\n2.45 billion. And use either all numerals or all words when several numbers appear together in a\npassage, even if convention would require a mixture:\nInconsistent The satellite Galatea is about twenty-six thousand miles from Neptune. It is 110 miles in\ndiameter and orbits Neptune in just over ten hours.\nRevised The satellite Galatea is about 26,000 miles from Neptune. It is 110 miles in diameter and\norbits Neptune in just over 10 hours.\nIn American English a comma separates the numerals in long numbers (26,000), and a period\nfunctions as a decimal point (2.06).\nUse numerals according to convention for dates,\naddresses, and other information.\nEven when a number requires one or two words to spell out, we conventionally use numerals\nin the following situations:\nDays and years\nJune 18, 2000 AD 12 456 BCE 1999\nException The day of a month may be expressed in words when it is not followed by a year\n(June fifth; October first).\n\nFOWLcp33-36.doc - 13\nPages, chapters, volumes, Decimals, percentages, and\nacts, scenes, lines fractions\nChapter 9, page 123 22.5\nHamlet, act 5, scene 3, 48% (or 48 percent)\nlines 35–40 31⁄2\nAddresses Scores and statistics\nRD 2 21 to 7\n419 Stonewall Street a mean of 26\nWashington, DC 20036 a ratio of 8 to 1\nExact amounts of money The time of day\n$4.50 9:00 AM\n$3.5 million (or $3,500,000) 2:30 PM\nExceptions Round dollar or cent amounts of only a few words may be expressed in words:\nseventeen dollars; fifteen hundred dollars; sixty cents. When the word o’clock is used for the time\nof day, also express the number in words: two o’clock (not 2 o’clock).\nAlways spell out numbers that begin sentences.\nFor clarity, spell out any number that begins a sentence. If the number requires more than two\nwords, reword the sentence so that the number falls later and can be expressed as a numeral:\nNot 3.7 billion people live in Asia.\nBut The population of Asia is 3.7 billion.\nEXERCISE 36.1 Revising: Numbers\nRevise the following sentences so that numbers are used appropriately for nontechnical writing. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nAccording to many sources—e.g., the Cambridge Ancient History and Gardiner’s Egypt of the\nPharaohs—the ancient egyptians devoted much attention to making Life more convenient and pleasurable\nfor themselves.\nOur word pharaoh for the ancient egyptian rulers comes from the egyptian word pr’o, meaning\n―great house.‖ Indeed, the egyptians placed great emphasis on family residences, adding small bedrms. as\n\nFOWLcp33-36.doc - 14\nearly as 3500 yrs. bce. Feeling that only those who smelled sweet could be attractive, the egyptians made\ndeodorants from perfumed oils, e.g., cinnamon and citrus.\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp33-36.doc - Look, for example, at the\nunderlined words in these two sentences:\nSome patients decide to bag counseling because their shrinks seem strung out.\nSome patients decide to abandon counseling because their therapists seem disturbed.\nThe first sentence might be addressed to friends in casual conversation. The second is more suitable\nfor an academic audience.\nThe more formal language of the second example is typical of standard American English.\nThis is the dialect of English normally expected and used in school, business, government, the\nprofessions, and the communications media. (For more on its role in academic writing, see pp.\n132–33.)\nThe vocabulary of standard American English is huge, allowing expression of an infinite range\nof ideas and feelings; but it does exclude words that only some groups of people use, understand,\nor find inoffensive. Some of those more limited vocabularies should be avoided altogether; others\nshould be used cautiously and in special situations, as when aiming for a special effect with an\naudience you know will appreciate it. Whenever you doubt a word’s status, consult a dictionary\n(see p. 536).\nNote Many grammar and style checkers can be set to flag potentially inappropriate words (see\np. 61), such as nonstandard dialect, slang, colloquialisms, and gender-specific terms (manmade,\nmailman). However, the checker can flag only words listed in its diction-ary of questionable words.\nFor example, a checker flagged business- man as potentially sexist in A successful businessman\nputs clients first, but the checker did not flag his in A successful businessperson listens to his\nclients. If you use a checker to review your language, you’ll need to determine whether a flagged\nword is or is not appropriate for your writing situation.\n37a Revising nonstandard dialect\nLike many countries, the United States consists of scores of regional, social, and ethnic groups\nwith their own distinct dialects, or versions of English. Standard American English is one of these\ndialects, and so are Black English, Appalachian English, Creole, and the English of coastal Maine.\nAll the dialects of English share many features, but each also has its own vocabulary,\npronunciation, and grammar.\nIf you speak a dialect of English besides standard American English, be careful about using\nyour dialect in situations where standard English is the norm, such as in academic or public\nwriting. Dialects are not wrong in themselves, but forms imported from one dialect into another\nmay still be perceived as unclear or incorrect. When you know standard English is expected in your\nwriting, edit to eliminate expressions in your dialect that you know (or have been told) differ from\nstandard English. These expressions may\ninclude theirselves, hisn, them books, and others labeled ―non-\nstandard‖ by a dictionary. They may also include verb forms, as\ndiscussed on pages 278–92. For help identifying and editing nonstandard language, see ― Guide‖\ninside the back cover of this book.\nYour participation in the community of standard English does not require you to abandon\nyour own dialect. You may want to use it in writing you do for yourself, such as journals, notes,\nand drafts, which should be composed as freely as possible. You may want to quote it in an\nacademic paper, as when analyzing or reporting conversation in dialect. And, of course, you will\nwant to use it with others who speak it.\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 4\n37b Using regionalisms only when appropriate\nRegionalisms are expressions or pronunciations peculiar to a particular area. Southerners may\nsay they reckon, meaning ―think‖ or ―suppose.‖ People in Maine invite their Boston friends to\ncome down rather than up (north) to visit. New Yorkers stand on rather than in line for a movie.\nRegional expressions are appropriate in writing addressed to local readers and may lend\nrealism to regional description, but they should be avoided in writing intended for a general\naudience.\n37c Using slang only when appropriate\nAll groups of people—from musicians and computer scientists to vegetarians and golfers—\ncreate novel and colorful expressions called slang. The following quotation, for instance, is from\nan essay on the slang of ―skaters‖ (skateboarders):\nCurtis slashed ultra-punk crunchers on his longboard, while the Rube-man flailed his usual Gumbyness\non tweaked frontsides and lofty fakie ollies.—Miles Orkin, ―Mucho Slingage by the Pool‖\nAmong those who understand it, slang may be vivid and forceful. It often occurs in dialog,\nand an occasional slang expression can enliven an informal essay. Some slang, such as dropout\n(She was a high school dropout), has proved so useful that it has passed into the general\nvocabulary.\nBut most slang is too flippant and imprecise for effective communication, and it is generally\ninappropriate for college or business writing. Notice the gain in seriousness and precision achieved\nin the following revision:\nSlang Many students start out pretty together but then get weird.\nRevised Many students start out with clear goals but then lose their direction.\n37d Using colloquial language only when appropriate\nColloquial language designates the words and expressions appropriate to everyday spoken\nlanguage. Regardless of our backgrounds and how we live, we all try to get along with each other.\nWe play with kids, go crazy for something, and in our worst moments try to get back at someone\nwho has made us do the dirty work.\nWhen you write informally, colloquial language may be appropriate to achieve the casual,\nrelaxed effect of conversation. An occasional colloquial word dropped into otherwise more formal\nwriting can also help you achieve a desired emphasis. But colloquial language does not provide the\nexactness needed in more formal college, public, and professional writing. In such writing you\nshould generally avoid any words and expressions labeled ―informal‖ or ―colloquial‖ in your\ndictionary. Take special care to avoid mixed diction, a combination of standard and colloquial\nwords:\nMixed According to a Native American myth, the Great Creator had\ndiction a dog hanging around with him when he created the earth.\nRevised According to a Native American myth, the Great Creator was accompanied by a dog when he\ncreated the earth.\n37e Revising neologisms\nNeologisms are words created (or coined) so recently that they have not come into established\nuse. An example is prequel (made up of pre-, meaning ―before,‖ and the ending of sequel), a movie\nor book that takes the story of an existing movie or book back in time. Some neologisms do\nbecome accepted as part of our general vocabulary—motel, coined from motor and hotel, is an\nexample. But most neologisms pass quickly from the language. Unless such words serve a special\npurpose in your writing and are sure to be understood by your readers, you should avoid them.\n37f Using technical words with care\nAll disciplines and professions rely on special words or give common words special meanings.\nChemists speak of esters and phosphatides, geographers and mapmakers refer to isobars and\nisotherms, and literary critics write about motifs and subtexts. Such technical language allows\nspecialists to communicate precisely and economically with other specialists who share their\nvocabulary. But without explanation these words are meaningless to nonspecialists. When you are\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 5\nwriting for nonspecialists, avoid unnecessary technical terms and carefully define terms you must\nuse.\n37g Revising indirect or pretentious writing\nIn most writing, small, plain, and direct words are preferable to big, showy, or evasive words.\nAvoid euphemisms, double talk, and pretentious writing.\nA euphemism is a presumably inoffensive word that a writer or speaker substitutes for a word\ndeemed potentially offensive or too blunt, such as passed away for ―died.‖ Euphemisms appear\nwhenever a writer or speaker wants to bury the truth, as when a governor mentions the negative\ngrowth (meaning ―decline‖) in her state. Use euphemisms only when you know that blunt, truthful\nwords would needlessly hurt or offend members of your audience.\nA kind of euphemism that deliberately evades the truth is double talk (also called\ndoublespeak or weasel words): language intended to confuse or to be misunderstood. Today\ndouble talk is unfortunately common in politics and advertising—the revenue enhancement that is\nreally a tax, the biodegradable bags that last dec-ades. Double talk has no place in honest writing.\nEuphemism and sometimes double talk seem to keep company with fancy writing. Any writing\nthat is more elaborate than its subject requires will sound pretentious—that is, excessively showy.\nChoose your words for their exactness and economy. The big, ornate word may be tempting, but\npass it up. Your readers will be grateful.\nPretentious To perpetuate our endeavor of providing funds for our elderly citizens as we do at the\npresent moment, we will face the exigency of enhanced contributions from all our citizens.\nRevised We cannot continue to fund Social Security and Medi-care for the elderly unless we raise\ntaxes.\n37h Revising sexist and other biased language\nEven when we do not mean it to, our language can reflect and perpetuate hurtful prejudices\ntoward groups of people, especially racial, ethnic, religious, age, and sexual groups. Such biased\nlanguage can be obvious—words such as nigger, whitey, mick, kike, fag, dyke, and broad. But it\ncan also be subtle, generalizing about groups in ways that may be familiar but that are also\ninaccurate or unfair. For instance, people with physical disabilities are as varied a group as any\nother: the only thing they have in common is some form of impairment. To assume that people with\ndisabilities share certain attitudes (shyness, helplessness, victimization, whatever) is to disregard\nthe uniqueness of each person.\nBiased language reflects poorly on the user, not on the person or persons whom it\nmischaracterizes or insults. Unbiased language does not submit to false generalizations. It treats\npeople as individuals and labels groups as they wish to be labeled.\n1 Avoiding stereotypes of race, ethnicity, religion, age, and other characteristics\nA stereotype is a generalization based on poor evidence, a kind of formula for understanding\nand judging people simply because of their membership in a group:\nMen are uncommunicative.\nWomen are emotional.\nLiberals want to raise taxes.\nConservatives are affluent.\nAt best, stereotypes betray an uncritical writer, one who is not think-ing beyond notions received\nfrom others. Worse, they betray a writer who does not mind hurting others or even wants to hurt\nothers.\nIn your writing, be alert for any general statements about people based on only one or a few\ncharacteristics. Be especially cautious about substituting such statements for the evidence you\nshould be providing instead.\nStereotype Elderly drivers should have their licenses limited to daytime driving. [Implies that all elderly\npeople are poor night drivers.]\nRevised Drivers with impaired night vision should have their licenses limited to daytime driving.\nSome stereotypes have become part of the language, but they are still potentially offensive.\nStereotype The administrators are too blind to see the need for a new gymnasium.\nRevised The administrators do not understand the need for a new gymnasium.\n2 Avoiding sexist language\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 6\nAmong the most subtle and persistent biased language is that expressing narrow ideas about\nmen’s and women’s roles, position, and value in society. This sexist language distinguishes\nneedlessly between men and women in such matters as occupation, ability, behavior, temperament,\nand maturity. Like other stereotypes, it can wound or irritate readers, and it indicates the writer’s\nthoughtlessness or unfairness. The following box suggests some ways of eliminating sexist\nlanguage.\nForms of address vary widely from culture to culture. In some cultures, for instance, one\nshows respect by referring to all older women as if they were married, using the equivalent of the\ntitle Mrs. Usage in the United States is changing toward making no assumptions about marital\nstatus, rank, or other characteristics—for instance, using the title Ms. for a woman unless she is\nknown to prefer Mrs. or Miss.\n3 Using appropriate labels\nWe often need to label groups: swimmers, politicians, mothers, Christians, westerners,\nstudents. But labels can be shorthand stereotypes that slight the person labeled and ignore the\npreferences of the group members themselves. Showing sensitivity when applying labels reveals\nyou to be alert to readers’ needs and concerns. Although sometimes dismissed as ―political\ncorrectness,‖ such sensitivity hurts no one and helps gain your readers’ trust and respect.\nAvoid labels that (intentionally or not) disparage the person or group you refer to. A\nperson with emotional problems is not a mental patient. A person with cancer is not a cancer\nvictim. A person using a wheelchair is not wheelchair-bound.\nUse names for racial, ethnic, and other groups that reflect the preferences of each group’s\nmembers, or at least many of them. Examples of current preferences include African\nAmerican or black, latino/latina (for Americans and American immigrants of Spanish-\nspeaking descent), and people with disabilities (rather than the handicapped). But labels\nchange often. To learn how a group’s members wish to be labeled, ask them directly, attend to\nusage in reputable periodicals, or check a recent dictionary.\nA helpful reference for appropriate labels is Guidelines for Bias-Free Writing, by Marilyn\nSchwartz and the Task Force on Bias-Free Language of the Association of American University\nPresses.\nEXERCISE 37.1 Revising: Appropriate words\nRewrite the following sentences as needed for standard American English. Consult a dictionary to\ndetermine whether particular words are appropriate and to find suitable substitutes. (You can do this\nexercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nIf negotiators get hyper during contract discussions, they may mess up chances for a settlement.\nIf negotiators become excited or upset during contract discussions, they may harm chances for a\nsettlement.\n1. The disease gets around primarily by sexual intercourse, ex-change of bodily fluids, shared needles,\nand blood transfusions.\n3. Those who think the disease is limited to homos, druggies, and foreigners are quite mistaken.\n4. Stats suggest that in the United States one in every five hundred college kids carries the virus.\n5.
