Instead, he has the necessity for all the compassion, medical care, and\nfinancial assistance due those who are in the extremity of illness.\n6. An AIDS victim often sees a team of doctors or a single doctor with a specialized practice.\n7. The doctor may help his patients by obtaining social services for them as well as by providing\nmedical care.\n8. The AIDS sufferer who loses his job may need public assistance.\n9. For someone who is very ill, a full-time nurse may be necessary. She can administer medications and\nmake the sick person as comfortable as possible.\n10. Some people with AIDS have insurance, but others lack the bread for premiums.\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 7\nVisit the companion Web site for more help and additional exercises on appropriate language.\nLanguage in academic and public writing\nAlways appropriate\nStandard American English\nSometimes appropriate\nRegional words and expressions Neologisms\nSlang Technical language\nColloquial language Euphemisms\nRarely or never appropriate\nNonstandard dialect Biased language: sexist, racist,\nDouble talk ethnocentric, etc.\nEliminating sexist language\nAvoid demeaning and patronizing language—for instance, identifying women and men differently\nor trivializing either gender:\nSexist Dr. Keith Kim and Lydia Hawkins wrote the article.\nRevised Dr. Keith Kim and Dr. Lydia Hawkins wrote the article.\nRevised Keith Kim and Lydia Hawkins wrote the article.\nSexist Ladies are entering formerly male occupations.\nRevised Women are entering formerly male occupations.\nAvoid occupational or social stereotypes, assuming that a role or profession is exclusively male or\nfemale:\nSexist The considerate doctor commends a nurse when she provides his patients with good care.\nRevised The considerate doctor commends a nurse who provides good care for patients.\nAvoid referring needlessly to gender:\nSexist Marie Curie, a woman chemist, discovered radium.\nRevised Marie Curie, a chemist, discovered radium.\nSexist The patients were tended by a male nurse.\nRevised The patients were tended by a nurse.\nAvoid using man or words containing man to refer to all human beings. Here are a few\nalternatives:\nbusinessman businessperson\nchairman chair, chairperson\ncongressman representative in Congress, legislator\ncraftsman craftsperson, artisan\nlayman layperson\nmankind humankind, humanity, human beings, people\npoliceman police officer\nsalesman salesperson, sales representative\nSexist Man has not reached the limits of social justice.\nRevised Humankind [or Humanity] has not reached the limits of so-cial justice.\nSexist The furniture consists of manmade materials.\nRevised The furniture consists of synthetic materials.\nAvoid the generic he, the male pronoun used to refer to both genders. (See also pp. 315–16.)\nSexist The newborn child explores his world.\nRevised Newborn children explore their world. [Use the plural for the pronoun and the word it\nrefers to.]\nRevised The newborn child explores the world. [Avoid the pronoun altogether.]\nRevised The newborn child explores his or her world. [Substitute male and female pronouns.]\nUse the last option sparingly—only once in a group of sentences and only to stress the singular\nindividual.\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 8\n38\nCHAPTER\nUsing Exact Language\nTo write clearly and effectively, you will want to find the words that fit your meaning exactly\nand convey your attitude precisely. Don’t worry too much about choosing exact words while you\nare drafting an essay. If the right word doesn’t come to you, leave a blank. Revision (p. 48) or\nediting (p. 58) is the stage to consider tone, specificity, and precision.\nNote A grammar and style checker can provide some help with inexact language. For instance,\nyou can set it to flag commonly confused words (such as continuous/continual), misused\nprepositions in idioms (such as accuse for instead of accuse of), and clichés. (See p. 61 on setting a\nchecker.) But the checker can flag only words stored in its dictionary. It can’t help you at all with\ninappropriate connotation, excessive abstraction, or other problems discussed in this chapter.\n38a Using the right word for your meaning\nPrecisely expressing your meaning requires understanding both the denotations and the\nconnotations of words. A word’s de-notation is the thing or idea it refers to, the meaning listed in\nthe dictionary without reference to the emotional associations it may arouse in a reader. Using\nwords according to their established denotations is essential if readers are to grasp your meaning.\nHere are a few guidelines:\nBecome acquainted with a dictionary. Consult it whenever you are unsure of a word’s\nmeaning.\nDistinguish between similar-sounding words that have widely different denotations:\nInexact Older people often suffer infirmaries [places for the sick].\nExact Older people often suffer infirmities [disabilities].\nSome words, called homonyms (from the Greek meaning ―same name‖), sound exactly alike\nbut differ in meaning: for example, principal/principle or rain/reign/rein. (See pp. 543–44 for\na list of commonly confused homonyms.)\nDistinguish between words with related but distinct denotations:\nInexact Television commercials continuously [unceasingly] interrupt programming.\nExact Television commercials continually [regularly] interrupt programming.\nIn addition to their emotion-free denotations, many words also carry associations with specific\nfeelings. These connotations can shape readers’ responses and are thus a powerful tool for writers.\n(At the same time they are a potential snare for readers. See p. 189.) Some connotations are\npersonal: the word dog, for instance, may have negative connotations for the letter carrier who has\nbeen bitten three times. Usually, though, people agree about connotations. The following word\npairs are just a few of many that have related denotations but very different connotations:\npride: sense of self-worth\nvanity: excessive regard for oneself\nfirm: steady, unchanging, unyielding\nstubborn: unreasonable, bullheaded\nenthusiasm: excitement\nmania: excessive interest or desire\nUnderstanding connotation is especially important in choosing among synonyms, words with\napproximately, but often not exactly, the same meanings. For instance, cry and weep both denote\nthe shedding of tears, but cry more than weep connotes a sobbing sound accompanying the tears.\nSob itself connotes broken, gasping crying, with tears, whereas wail connotes sustained sound,\nperhaps without tears.\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 9\nSeveral resources can help you track down words with the exact connotations you want:\nA standard dictionary distinguishes among synonyms. See page 539 for an example.\nA dictionary of synonyms lists and defines synonyms in groups. See page 538 for a title.\nA thesaurus lists synonyms but does not distinguish among them. See page 538 for a title.\nNote Because a thesaurus lacks definitions, it can only suggest possibilities. You will still need\na dictionary to discover words’ exact denotations and connotations.\nEXERCISE 38.1 Revising: Denotation\nRevise any underlined word below that is not used according to its established denotation. Circle any\nword used correctly. Consult a diction-ary if you are uncertain of a word’s precise meaning. (You can do\nthis exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nSam and Dave are going to Bermuda and Hauppauge, respect-fully, for spring vacation.\nSam and Dave are going to Bermuda and Hauppauge, respec-tively, for spring vacation.\n1. Maxine Hong Kingston was rewarded many prizes for her first two books, The Woman Warrior and\nChina Men.\n2. Kingston sites her mother’s tales about ancestors and ancient Chinese customs as the sources of these\nmemoirs.\n3. In her childhood Kingston was greatly effected by her mother’s tale about a pregnant aunt who was\nostracized by villagers.\n4. The aunt gained avengeance by drowning herself in the village’s water supply.\n5. Kingston decided to make her nameless relative infamous by giving her immortality in The Woman\nWarrior.\nEXERCISE 38.2 Considering the connotations of words\nFill the blank in each sentence below with the most appropriate word from the list in parentheses. Consult\na dictionary to be sure of your choice. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nCHANNEL 5 OSHU THE WINNER BEFORE THE POLLS CLOSED. (ADVERTISED, DECLARED,\nBROADCAST, PROMULGATED)\nChannel 5 declared Oshu the winner before the polls closed.\n1. AIDS is a serious health . (problem, worry, difficulty, plight)\n2. Once the virus has entered the blood system, it T-cells. (murders, destroys, slaughters, executes)\n3. The of T-cells is to combat infections. (ambition, function, aim, goal)\n4. Without enough T-cells, the body is nearly against infections. (defenseless, hopeless, desperate)\n5. To prevent exposure to the disease, one should be especially in sexual relationships. (chary,\ncircumspect, cautious, calculating)\n38b Balancing the abstract and concrete, the general\nand specific\nTo understand a subject as you understand it, your readers need ample guidance from your\nwords. When you describe a building as beautiful and nothing more, you force readers to provide\ntheir own ideas of what makes a building beautiful. If readers bother (and they may not), they\nsurely will not conjure up the image you had in mind. Use words to tell readers what you want\nthem to know, that the beautiful building is a sleek, silver skyscraper with blue-tinted windows, for\ninstance, or a Victorian brick courthouse with tall, arched windows.\nClear, exact writing balances abstract and general words, which outline ideas and objects, with\nconcrete and specific words, which sharpen and solidify.\nAbstract words name qualities and ideas: beauty, inflation, management, culture, liberal.\nConcrete words name things we can know by our five senses of sight, hearing, touch, taste,\nand smell: sleek, humming, brick, bitter, musty.\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 10\nGeneral words name classes or groups of things, such as buildings, weather, or birds, and\ninclude all the varieties of the class. Specific words limit a general class, such as buildings, by\nnaming a variety, such as skyscraper, Victorian courthouse, or hut.\nNote that general and specific are relative terms: the same word may be more general than some\nwords but more specific than others.\nGeneral\nweather bird\nrain parrot\ndownpour cockatoo\nsudden downpour my pet cockatoo Moyshe\nSpecific\nAbstract and general words are useful in the broad statements that set the course for your\nwriting:\nThe wild horse in America has a romantic history.\nWe must be free from government interference in our affairs.\nRelations between the sexes today are only a little more relaxed than they were in the past.\nBut the sentences following these would have to develop the ideas with concrete and specific\ndetails. When your meaning calls for an abstract or general word, make sure you define it, explain\nit, and narrow it. Look at how concrete and specific information turns vague sentences into exact\nones in the examples below:\nVague The size of his hands made his smallness real. [How big were\nhis hands? How small was he?]\nExact Not until I saw his delicate, doll-like hands did I realize that he\nstood a full head shorter than most other men.\nVague The long flood caused a lot of awful destruction in the town.\n[How long did the flood last? What destruction did it cause?\nWhy was the destruction awful?]\nExact The flood waters, which rose swiftly and then stayed stub-\nbornly high for days, killed at least six townspeople and made\nlife a misery for the hundreds who had to evacuate their ruined\nhomes and stores.\nNote You can use your computer’s Find function to help you find and revise abstract and\ngeneral words that you tend to overuse. Examples of such words might include nice, interesting,\nthings, very, good, a lot, a little, and some.\nEXERCISE 38.3 Revising: Concrete and specific words\nMake the following paragraph vivid by expanding the sentences with appropriate details of your own\nchoosing. Substitute concrete and specific words for the abstract and general ones that are underlined.\n(You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nI remember clearly how awful I felt the first time I attended Mrs. Murphy’s second-grade class. I had\nrecently moved from a small town in Missouri to a crowded suburb of Chicago. My new school looked\nbig from the outside and seemed dark inside as I walked down the long corridor toward the classroom.\nThe class was noisy as I neared the door; but when I entered, everyone became quiet and looked at me. I\nfelt uncomfortable and wanted a place to hide. However, in a loud voice Mrs. Murphy directed me to the\nfront of the room to introduce myself.\nEXERCISE 38.4 Using concrete and specific words\nFor each abstract or general word below, give at least two other words or phrases that illustrate increasing\nspecificity or concreteness. Consult a dictionary as needed. Use the most specific or concrete word from\neach group in a sentence of your own. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nawake, watchful, vigilant\nVigilant guards patrol the buildings.\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 11\n1. fabric 6. green 11. teacher\n2. delicious 7. walk (verb) 12. nice\n3. car 8. flower 13. virtue\n4. narrow-minded 9. serious 14. angry\n5. reach (verb) 10. pretty 15. crime\n38c Using idioms\nIdioms are expressions in any language whose meanings cannot be determined simply from\nthe words in them or whose component words cannot be predicted by any rule of grammar; often,\nthey violate conventional grammar. Examples of English idioms include put up with, plug away at,\nand make off with.\nIdiomatic combinations of verbs or adjectives and prepositions can be\nconfusing for both native and nonnative speakers of English. A number of\nthese pairings are listed in the box below.\nIf you are learning standard American English, you are justified in stumbling over its\nprepositions: their meanings can shift depending on context, and they have many idiomatic uses. In\nmastering the prepositions of standard English, you probably can’t avoid memorization. But you\ncan help yourself by memorizing related groups, such as the following.\nAt, in, or on in expressions of time\nUse at before actual clock time: at 8:30.\nUse in before a month, year, century, or period: in April, in 2007, in the twenty-first century,\nin the next month.\nUse on before a day or date: on Tuesday, on August 3, on Labor Day.\nAt, in, or on in expressions of place\nUse at before a specific place or address: at the school, at 511 Iris Street.\nUse in before a place with limits or before a city, state, country, or continent: in the house, in a\nbox, in Oklahoma City, in China, in Asia.\nUse on to mean ―supported by‖ or ―touching the surface of‖: on the table, on Iris Street, on\npage 150.\nFor or since in expressions of time\nUse for before a period of time: for an hour, for two years.\nUse since before a specific point in time: since 1999, since Friday.\nA dictionary of English as a second language is the best source for the meanings of\nprepositions; see the recommendations on page 537. In addition, some references focus on\nprepositions. See, for\ninstance, volume 1 (Verbs with Prepositions and Particles) of the Ox-ford Dictionary of Current\nIdiomatic English.\nEXERCISE 38.5 Using prepositions in idioms\nInsert the preposition that correctly completes each idiom in the following sentences. Consult the box on\nthe previous page or a diction-ary as needed. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/\nlittlebrown.)\nExample:\nI disagree many feminists who say women should not be homemakers.\nI disagree with many feminists who say women should not be homemakers.\n1. As Mark and Lana waited the justice of the peace, they seemed oblivious the other people in the\nlobby.\n2. But Mark inferred Lana’s glance at a handsome man that she was no longer occupied him alone.\n3. Angry Lana, Mark charged her not loving him enough to get married.\n4. Impatient Mark’s childish behavior, Lana disagreed his interpretation of her glance.\n5. They decided that if they could differ so violently a minor incident, they should part each other.\n38d Using figurative language\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 12\nFigurative language (or a figure of speech) departs from the literal meanings (the\ndenotations) of words, usually by comparing very different ideas or objects:\nLiteral As I try to write, I can think of nothing to say.\nFigurative As I try to write, my mind is a slab of black slate.\nImaginatively and carefully used, figurative language can capture meaning more precisely and\nfeelingly than literal language.\nFigurative language is commonplace in speech. Having slept like a log, you may get up to find\nit raining cats and dogs. But the rapid exchange of speech leaves little time for inventiveness, and\nmost figures of daily conversation, like those above, are worn and hackneyed. Writing gives you\ntime to reject the tired figure and to search out fresh, concrete words and phrases.\nThe two most common figures of speech are the simile and the metaphor. Both compare two\nthings of different classes, often one abstract and the other concrete. A simile makes the\ncomparison explicit and usually begins with like or as:\nWhenever we grow, we tend to feel it, as a young seed must feel the weight and inertia of the earth when\nit seeks to break out of its shell on its way to becoming a plant. —Alice Walker\nTo hold America in one’s thoughts is like holding a love letter in one’s\nhand—it has so special a meaning. —E. B. White\nInstead of stating a comparison, a metaphor implies it, omitting such words as like or as:\nI cannot and will not cut my conscience to fit this year’s fashions.\n—Lillian Hellman\nA school is a hopper into which children are heaved while they are young and tender; therein they are\npressed into certain standard shapes and covered from head to heels with official rubber stamps.\n—H. L. Mencken\nTwo other figures of speech are personification and hyperbole. Personification treats ideas\nand objects as if they were human:\nThe economy consumes my money and gives me little in return.\nI could hear the whisper of snowflakes, nudging each other as they fell.\nHyperbole deliberately exaggerates:\nShe appeared in a mile of billowing chiffon, flashing a rhinestone as big as an ostrich egg.\nHe yelled so loud that his voice carried to the next county.\nTo be successful, figurative language must be fresh and unstrained, calling attention not to\nitself but to the writer’s meaning. One kind of figurative language gone wrong is the mixed\nmetaphor, in which the writer combines two or more incompatible figures. Since metaphors often\ngenerate visual images in the reader’s mind, a mixed metaphor can be laughable:\nMixed Various thorny problems that we try to sweep under the rug\ncontinue to bob up all the same.\nTo revise a mixed metaphor, follow through consistently with just one image:\nImproved Various thorny problems that we try to weed out continue to thrive all the same.\nEXERCISE 38.6 Analyzing figurative language\nIdentify each figure of speech in the following sentences as a simile or a metaphor, and analyze how it\nadds to the writer’s meaning. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\n1. A distant airplane, a delta wing out of nightmare, made a gliding shadow on the creek’s bottom that\nlooked like a stingray crossing upstream. —Annie Dillard\n2. Her roots ran deep into the earth, and from those roots she drew strength enough to hold still against\nall the forces of chance and disorder. —N. Scott Momaday\n3. As a member of the winning team (the graduating class of 1940) I had outdistanced unpleasant\nsensations by miles. I was headed for the freedom of open fields.—Maya Angelou\n4. All artists quiver under the lash of adverse criticism.\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 13\n—Catherine Drinker Bowen\n5. Every writer, in a roomful of writers, wants to be the best, and the judge, or umpire, or referee is soon\noverwhelmed and shouted down like a chickadee trying to take charge of a caucus of crows.\n—James Thurber\nEXERCISE 38.7 USING FIGURATIVE LANGUAGE\nInvent appropriate figurative language of your own (simile, meta-phor, hyperbole, or personification) to\ndescribe each scene or quality below, and use the figure in a sentence. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nThe attraction of a lake on a hot day\nThe small waves like fingers beckoned us irresistibly.\n1. The sound of a kindergarten classroom\n2. People waiting in line to buy tickets to a rock concert\n3. The politeness of strangers meeting for the first time\n4. A streetlight seen through dense fog\n5. The effect of watching television for ten hours straight\n38e Using fresh, not trite, expressions\nTrite expressions, or clichés, are phrases so old and so often repeated that they have become\nstale. They include the following:\nacid test beyond the shadow of a doubt\nadd insult to injury brought back to reality\nbetter late than never cold, hard facts\ncool as a cucumber\ncrushing blow\neasier said than done\nface the music\nflat as a pancake\ngreen with envy\nhard as a rock\nheavy as lead\nhit the nail on the head\nhour of need\nladder of success\nmoving experience\nneedle in a haystack\nBesides these old phrases, stale writing may also depend on fashionable words that are losing their\neffect: for instance, lifestyle, enhance, excellent, fantastic, and caring.\nMany of these expressions were once fresh and forceful, but constant use has dulled them.\nThey, in turn, will dull your writing by suggesting that you have not thought about what you are\nsaying and have resorted to the easiest phrase.\nClichés may slide into your drafts while you are trying to express your meaning. In editing,\nthen, be wary of any expression you have heard or used before. Substitute fresh words of your own\nor restate the idea in plain language.\nTrite A healthful lifestyle enhances your ability to go for the gold, allows you to enjoy life to the\nfullest, and helps you live to a ripe old age.\nRevised Living healthfully helps you perform well, enjoy life thor-oughly, and live long.\nEXERCISE 38.8 Revising: Trite expressions\nRevise the following sentences to eliminate trite expressions. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nThe basketball team had almost seized victory, but it faced the test of truth in the last quarter of the\ngame.\nThe basketball team seemed about to win, but the real test came in the last quarter of the game.\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 14\n1. The disastrous consequences of the war have shaken the small nation to its roots.\n2. Prices for food have shot sky high, and citizens have sneaking suspicions that others are\nmaking a killing on the black market.\n3. Medical supplies are so few and far between that even civilians who are as sick as dogs cannot get\ntreatment.\n4. With most men fighting or injured or killed, women have had to bite the bullet and bear the men’s\nburden in farming and manufacturing.\n5. Last but not least, the war’s heavy drain on the nation’s pocketbook has left the economy in a\nshambles.\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 15\nVisit the companion Web site for more help and additional exercises on exact language.\nIdioms with prepositions\nabide by a rule\nabide in a place or state\naccords with\naccording to\naccuse of a crime\nadapt from a source\nadapt to a situation\nafraid of\nagree on a plan as a group\nagree to someone else’s plan\nagree with a person\nangry with\naware of\nbased on\ncapable of\ncertain of\ncharge for a purchase\ncharge with a crime\nconcur in an opinion\nconcur with a person\ncontend for a principle\ncontend with a person\ndependent on\ndiffer about or over a question\ndiffer from in some quality\ndiffer with a person\ndisappointed by or in a person\ndisappointed in or with a thing\nfamiliar with\nidentical with or to\nimpatient for a raise\nimpatient with a person\nindependent of\ninfer from\ninferior to\ninvolved in a task\ninvolved with a person\noblivious of or to one’s surroundings\noblivious of something forgotten\noccupied by a person\noccupied in study\noccupied with a thing\nopposed to\npart from a person\npart with a possession\nprior to\nproud of\nrelated to\nrewarded by the judge\nrewarded for something done\nrewarded with a gift\nsimilar to\nsuperior to\nwait at a place\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 16\nwait for a train, a person\nwait on a customer\npoint with pride\nripe old age\nshoulder the burden\nsmart as a whip\nsneaking suspicion\nsober as a judge\nstand in awe\nstrong as an ox\nthin as a rail\ntired but happy\ntried and true\nuntimely death\nwise as an owl\n39\nCHAPTER\nWriting Concisely\nConcise writing makes every word count. Conciseness is not the same as mere brevity: detail\nand originality should not be cut along with needless words. Rather, the length of an expression\nshould be appropriate to the thought.\nYou may find yourself writing wordily when you are unsure\nof your subject or when your thoughts are tangled. It’s fine, even necessary, to stumble and grope\nwhile drafting. But you should straighten out your ideas and eliminate wordiness during revision\nand editing.\nNote Any grammar and style checker will identify at least some wordy structures, such as\nrepeated words, weak verbs, passive voice, and there is and it is constructions. But a checker can’t\nidentify all potentially wordy structures, nor can it tell you whether a structure is appropriate for\nyour ideas.\nAs you’ll see in the examples that follow, wordiness is not a problem of incorrect grammar. A\nsentence may be perfectly grammatical but still contain unneeded words that interfere with your\nidea.\n39a Focusing on the subject and verb\nUsing the subjects and verbs of your sentences for the key actors and actions will reduce\nwords and emphasize important ideas. (See pp. 384–86 for more on this topic.)\nWordy The reason why most of the country shifts to daylight savings time is that winter days are\nmuch shorter than summer days.\nConcise Most of the country shifts to daylight savings time because winter days are much shorter than\nsummer days.\nFocusing on subjects and verbs will also help you avoid several other causes of wordiness\n(also discussed further on pp. 384–86):\nNouns made from verbs\nWordy The occurrence of the winter solstice, the shortest day of the year, is an event occurring about\nDecember 22.\nConcise The winter solstice, the shortest day of the year, occurs about December 22.\nWeak verbs\nWordy The earth’s axis has a tilt as the planet is in orbit around the sun so that the northern and\nsouthern hemispheres are alternately in alignment toward the sun.\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 17\nConcise The earth’s axis tilts as the planet orbits the sun so that the northern and southern\nhemispheres alternately align toward the sun.\nPassive voice\nWordy During its winter the northern hemisphere is tilted farthest away from the sun, so the nights\nare made longer and the days are made shorter.\nConcise During its winter the northern hemisphere tilts away from the\nsun, making the nights longer and the days shorter.\nSee also pages 302–03 on changing the passive voice to the active voice, as in the example\nabove.\n39b Cutting or shortening empty words and phrases\nEmpty words and phrases walk in place, gaining little or nothing in meaning. When you cut or\nshorten them, your writing will move faster and work harder.\nMany empty phrases can be cut entirely:\nall things considered in a manner of speaking\nas far as I’m concerned in my opinion\nfor all intents and purposes last but not least\nfor the most part more or less\nWordy As far as I am concerned, discrimination against women still exists in medicine for all intents\nand purposes.\nRevised Discrimination against women still exists in medicine.\nOther empty words can be cut along with some words around them:\nangle character kind situation\narea element manner thing\naspect factor nature type\ncase field\nWordy The type of large expenditures on advertising that manufacturers must make is a very\nimportant aspect of the cost of detergents.\nConcise Manufacturers’ large advertising expenditures increase the cost of detergents.\nStill other empty phrases can be reduced from several words to a single word:\nFor Substitute\nat all times always\nat the present time now\nat this point in time now\nin today’s society now\nin the nature of like\nfor the purpose of for\nin order to to\nuntil such time as until\nFor Substitute\nfor the reason that because\ndue to the fact that because\nbecause of the fact that because\nby virtue of the fact that because\ndespite the fact that although\nin the event that if\nby means of by\nin the final analysis finally\nWordy At this point in time, the software is expensive due to the fact that it has no competition.\nRevised The software is expensive now because it has no competition.\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 18\nEXERCISE 39.1 Revising: Subjects and verbs; empty words\nand phrases\nRevise the following sentences to achieve conciseness by focusing on subjects and verbs and by cutting or\nreducing empty words and phrases. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.) See\nalso page 386 for an additional exercise in focusing on subjects and verbs.\nExample:\nI made college my destination because of many factors, but most of all because of the fact that I want\na career in medicine.\nI came to college mainly because I want a career in medicine.\n1. Gerrymandering refers to a situation in which the lines of a voting district are redrawn so that a\nparticular party or ethnic group has benefits.\n2. The name is a reference to the fact that Elbridge Gerry, the governor of Massachusetts in 1812,\nredrew voting districts in Essex County.\n3. On the map one new district was seen to resemble something in the nature of a salamander.\n4. Upon seeing the map, a man who was for all intents and purposes a critic of Governor Gerry’s\nadministration cried out, ―Gerrymander!‖\n5. At the present time, changes may be made in the character of a district’s voting pattern by a political\ngroup by gerrymandering to achieve the exclusion of rival groups’ supporters.\n39c Cutting unnecessary repetition\nPlanned repetition and restatement can make writing more coherent (p. 83) or emphatic (pp.\n390–91). But unnecessary repetition weakens sentences:\nWordy Many unskilled workers without training in a particular job are unemployed and do not\nhave any work.\nConcise Many unskilled workers are unemployed.\nThe use of one word two different ways within a sentence is confusing:\nConfusing Preschool instructors play a role in the child’s understanding of male and female roles.\nClear Preschool instructors contribute to the child’s understanding of male and female roles.\nThe simplest kind of useless repetition is the phrase that says the same thing twice. In the\nfollowing examples, the unneeded words are underlined:\nbiography of his life habitual custom\ncircle around important [basic] essentials\nconsensus of opinion large in size\ncontinue on puzzling in nature\ncooperate together repeat again\nfew in number return again\nfinal completion revert back\nfrank and honest exchange square [round] in shape\nthe future to come surrounding circumstances\nPhrases like those above are redundant because the main word already implies the underlined\nword or words. The repetition is not emphatic but tedious. A dictionary will tell you what meanings\na word implies. Assassinate, for instance, means ―mur-der someone well known,‖ so the following\nsentence is redundant: Julius Caesar was assassinated and killed.\nEXERCISE 39.2 Revising: Unnecessary repetition\nRevise the following sentences to achieve conciseness. Concentrate on eliminating repetition and\nredundancy. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nBecause the circumstances surrounding the cancellation of classes were murky and unclear, the\neditor of the student newspaper assigned a staff reporter to investigate and file a report on the\ncircumstances.\nBecause the circumstances leading to the cancellation of classes were unclear, the editor of the\nstudent newspaper assigned a staffer to investigate and report the story.\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 19\n1. Some Vietnam veterans coming back to the United States after their tours of duty in Vietnam had\nproblems readjusting again to life in America.\n2. Afflicted with post-traumatic stress disorder, a psychological disorder that sometimes arises after a\ntrauma, some veterans had psychological problems that caused them to have trouble holding jobs and\nmaintaining relationships.\n3. Some who used to use drugs in Vietnam could not break their drug habits after they returned back to\nthe United States.\n4. The few veterans who committed crimes and violent acts gained so much notoriety and fame that\nmany Americans thought all veterans were crazy, insane maniacs.\n5. As a result of such stereotyping of Vietnam-era veterans, veterans are included in the same\nantidiscrimination laws that protect other victims of discrimination.\n39d Reducing clauses to phrases, phrases to\nsingle words\nModifiers—subordinate clauses, phrases, and single words—can be expanded or contracted\ndepending on the emphasis you want to achieve. (See pp. 254–58 on phrases and clauses and 398–\n401 on working with modifiers.) When editing your sentences, consider whether any modifiers can\nbe reduced without loss of emphasis or clarity:\nWordy The Channel Tunnel, which runs between Britain and France, bores through a bed of solid\nchalk that is twenty-three miles across.\nConcise The Channel Tunnel between Britain and France bores through twenty-three miles of solid\nchalk.\n39e Eliminating there is and it is constructions\nYou can postpone the sentence subject with the words there is (there are, there was, there\nwere) and it is (it was). (See p. 264.) These constructions can be useful to emphasize the subject (as\nwhen introducing it for the first time) or to indicate a change in direction. But often they just add\nwords and create limp substitutes for more vigorous sentences:\nWordy There were delays and cost overruns that plagued construction of the Channel Tunnel. It is the\nexpectation of investors to earn profits at last, now that there are trains passing daily through the tunnel.\nConcise Delays and cost overruns plagued construction of the Channel Tunnel. Investors expect to\nearn profits at last, now that trains pass daily through the tunnel.\n39f Combining sentences\nOften the information in two or more sentences can be combined into one tight sentence:\nWordy An unexpected problem with the Channel Tunnel is stowaways. The stowaways are\nmostly illegal immigrants. They are trying to smuggle themselves into England. They cling to train roofs\nand undercarriages.\nConcise An unexpected problem with the Channel Tunnel is stowaways, mostly\nillegal immigrants who are trying to smuggle themselves into England by clinging to\ntrain roofs and undercarriages.\nA number of exercises in this handbook give you practice in sentence combining. For a list,\nsee ―Sentence combining‖ in the Index.\n39g Rewriting jargon\nJargon can refer to the special vocabulary of any discipline or profession (see p. 513). But it\nhas also come to describe vague,\ninflated language that is overcomplicated, even incomprehensible. When it comes from government\nor business, we call it bureaucratese.\nJargon The weekly social gatherings stimulate networking by members of management from\nvarious divisions, with the aim of developing contacts and maximizing the flow of creative information.\nTranslation The weekly parties give managers from different divisions a chance to meet and to share\nideas.\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 20\nEXERCISE 39.3 Revising: Conciseness\nRewrite each passage below into a single concise sentence, using the techniques described in this chapter.\n(You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nExample:\nHe was taking some exercise in the park. Then several thugs were suddenly ahead of him in his path.\nHe was exercising [or jogging or strolling] in the park when several thugs suddenly loomed in his\npath.\n1. Chewing gum was originally introduced to the United States by Antonio López de Santa Anna. He\nwas the Mexican general.\n2. After he had been defeated by the Texans in 1845, the general, who was exiled, made the choice to\nsettle in New York.\n3. A piece of chicle had been stashed by the general in his baggage. Chicle is the dried milky sap of the\nMexican sapodilla tree.\n4. There was more of this resin brought into the country by Santa Anna’s friend Thomas Adams.\nAdams had a plan to make rubber.\n5. The plan failed. Then the occasion arose for Adams to get a much more successful idea on the basis\nof the use to which the resin was put by General Santa Anna. That is, Adams decided to make a gum\nthat could be chewed.\nEXERCISE 39.4 Revising: Conciseness\nMake the following passage as concise as possible. Be merciless. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nAt the end of a lengthy line of reasoning, he came to the conclusion that the situation with\ncarcinogens [cancer-causing substances] should be regarded as similar to the situation with the\nautomobile. Instead of giving in to an irrational fear of cancer, we should consider all aspects of the\nproblem in a balanced and dispassionate frame of mind, making a total of the benefits received from\npotential carcinogens (plastics, pesticides, and other similar products) and measuring said total against\nthe damage done by such products. This is the nature of most discussions about the automobile. Instead\nof responding irrationally to the visual, aural, and air pollution caused by automobiles, we have decided to\nlive with them (while simultaneously working to improve on them) for the benefits brought to society as a\nwhole.\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 21\nVisit the companion Web site for more help and additional exercises on writing concisely.\nWays to achieve conciseness\nWordy (87 words)\nThe highly pressured nature of critical-care nursing is due to the fact that\nthe patients have life-threatening illnesses. Critical-care nurses must have\npossession of steady nerves to care for patients who are critically ill and\nvery sick. The nurses must also have posses- sion of interpersonal skills.\nThey must also have medical skills. It is considered by most health-care\nprofessionals that these nurses\nare essential if there is to be improvement of patients who are now in\ncritical care from that status to the status of intermediate care.\nConcise (37 words)\nCritical-care nursing is highly pressured because the patients have life-threatening illnesses. Critical-care\nnurses must possess steady nerves and interpersonal and medical skills. Most health-care professionals\nconsider these nurses essential if patients are to improve to intermediate care.\nFocus on subject and verb, and cut or shorten empty words and phrases.\nAvoid nouns made from verbs.\nCut unneeded repetition.\nCombine sentences.\nChange passive voice to active voice.\nEliminate there is constructions.\nCut unneeded repetition, and reduce clauses and phrases.\n40\nCHAPTER\nUsing Dictionaries\nA dictionary can answer most questions about words. This chapter shows you how to choose a\ndictionary that suits your purpose and how to read a dictionary without difficulty.\n40a Choosing a dictionary\n1 Abridged dictionaries\nAbridged dictionaries are the most practical for everyday use. Often called desk dictionaries\nbecause of their convenient size, they usually list 150,000 to 200,000 words and concentrate on\nfairly common words and meanings.\nThe American Heritage College Dictionary\nMerriam-Webster’s Collegiate Dictionary\nThe Random House Webster’s College Dictionary\nWebster’s New World College Dictionary\nMost of these dictionaries are available both in print and on CD-ROM, and some are available\nonline (visit ablongman.com/\nlittlebrown for links). Your computer may include a dictionary that you can customize with words\nor meanings it does not cover. With an electronic dictionary, as with a print dictionary, you can\nlook up words as you write, checking spellings, meanings, synonyms, and other information.\nIf English is not your first language, you probably should have a dictionary prepared\nespecially for ESL students in addition to one of the dictionaries listed above. The dictionaries\nlisted below give much more information on such matters as count versus noncount nouns,\nprepositions with verbs and adjectives, and other concerns of ESL students.\nCOBUILD English Language Dictionary\nLongman Dictionary of Contemporary English. Longman Dictionary of American English is the\nAmerican version.\nOxford Advanced Learner’s Dictionary. Oxford Dictionary of American English is the American version.\n2 Unabridged dictionaries\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 22\nUnabridged dictionaries are the most scholarly and comprehensive of all dictionaries,\nsometimes consisting of many volumes. They emphasize the history of words and the variety of\ntheir uses. An unabridged dictionary is useful when you are studying a word in depth, reading or\nwriting about the literature of another century, or looking for a quotation containing a particular\nword. The following unabridged dictionaries are available at most libraries.\nThe Oxford English Dictionary, 20 volumes\nThe Random House Webster’s Unabridged Dictionary\nWebster’s Third New International Dictionary of the English Language\n3 Special dictionaries\nSpecial dictionaries limit their attention to a single class of word (for example, slang,\nengineering terms, abbreviations), to a single kind of information (synonyms, usage, word origins),\nor to a specific subject (African American culture, biography, history). (See Chapters 50–53 for\nlists of subject dictionaries in various academic disciplines.)\nFor guidance on English usage\nUsage guides provide help with commonly confused and misused words, phrases, idioms, and\nother matters:\nR. W. Burchfield, The New Fowler’s Modern English Usage\nBryan A. Garner, A Dictionary of Modern American Usage\nPractical English Usage, by Michael Swan, is a usage guide prepared especially for\nnonnative speakers of English.\nFor the origins of words\nDictionaries of etymology, or word history, explain how words have evolved:\nCharles T. Onions et al., eds., Oxford Dictionary of English Etymology\nEric Partridge, Origins: A Short Etymological Dictionary of Modern English\nFor information on slang\nDictionaries of slang explain the histories and meanings of conversational expressions. They\ncan make entertaining reading.\nJ. E. Lighter, ed., Historical Dictionary of American Slang\nHarold Wentworth and Stuart Berg Flexner, Dictionary of American Slang\nFor information about synonyms\nA thesaurus like Roget’s provides extensive lists of words with related meanings. A dictionary\nof synonyms like Webster’s contains discussions and illustrations of shades of meaning.\nRobert L. Chapman, ed., Roget’s International Thesaurus\nWebster’s New Dictionary of Synonyms\nMany electronic dictionaries include a thesaurus as well, and some thesauruses are available\nindependently on CD-ROM or online. (Visit ablongman.com/littlebrown for links.) With an online\nthesaurus, you can easily find synonyms and insert them into your documents. Take care with any\nthesaurus, ensuring that you know the meaning of a synonym before you use it.\n40b Working with a dictionary’s contents\nDictionaries use abbreviations and symbols to squeeze a lot of information into a relatively\nsmall book. This system of condensed information may at first seem difficult to read. But all\ndictionaries include in their opening pages detailed information on the arrangement of entries,\npronunciation symbols, and abbreviations.\nHere is a fairly typical entry, from Merriam-Webster’s Collegiate Dictionary. The labeled\nparts are discussed on the pages that follow.\nSpelling and word division\nThe small initial letters for reckon indicate that it is not normally capitalized. (In contrast,\nFranklin stove is capitalized in Merriam-Webster’s because Franklin is a proper noun.)\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 23\nThe centered period in reck·on shows the division into syllables. If you are breaking a word at\nthe end of a line, follow the dictionary’s division of the word into syllables. For a hyphenated\ncompound word, such as cross-question, a dictionary shows the hyphen as part of the spelling:\ncross-ques·tion.\nDictionaries provide any acceptable variant spellings of a word at the beginning of an entry.\nFor the word dexterous, Merriam-\nWebster’s has ―dex·ter·ous or dex·trous.‖\nPronunciation\nIn Merriam-Webster’s the pronunciation appears between reversed slashes (\\\\). The stressed\nsyllable is preceded by an accent mark ('re-k@n).\nDictionaries use symbols to indicate how to pronounce a word because the alphabet itself does\nnot record all the sounds in the language. (Listen, for example, to the different sounds of a in only\nthree words: far, make, and answer.) Most dictionaries provide a key to the pronunciation symbols\nat least every other page.\nGrammatical functions and forms\nDictionaries give helpful information about a word’s functions and forms. The Merriam-\nWebster’s entry for reckon shows the word to be a verb (vb), with the past tense and past participle\nreckoned and the present participle reckoning, and with both transitive (vt) and intransitive (vi)\nmeanings. (For the definitions of these terms, see p. 238.)\nMost dictionaries provide not only the principal forms of regular and irregular verbs but also\nthe plural forms of irregular nouns and the -er and -est forms of adjectives and adverbs. An\nadjective or adverb without -er and -est forms in the dictionary requires the addition of more and\nmost to show comparison (see pp. 322–23).\nEtymology\nDictionaries provide the etymology of a word (its history) to indicate its origin and the\nevolution of its meanings and forms. The dictionary can compress much information about a word\ninto\na small space through symbols, abbreviations, and different type fonts. An explanation of these\nsystems appears in the dictionary’s opening pages. Merriam-Webster’s traces reckon back most\nrecently to Middle English (ME) and then further back to Old English (OE). The notation ―(13c)‖\nbefore the first definition indicates that the first recorded use of reckon to mean ―count‖ occurred in\nthe thirteenth century. When seeking the etymology of a word, be sure to read the entire history,\nnot just the most recent event.\nMeanings\nDictionaries divide the general meaning of a word into particular meanings on the basis of\nhow the word is or has been actually used. They arrange a word’s meanings differently, either in\norder of their appearance in the language, earliest first, or in order of their frequency of use, most\ncommon first. (Merriam-Webster’s follows the former practice.) To learn your dictionary’s\narrangement, consult its opening pages. Then read through a word’s entire entry before settling on\nthe meaning that fits the context of what you’re reading or writing.\nThe Merriam-Webster’s entry for reckon ends with two uses of the word in idiomatic\nexpressions (reckon with and reckon without). These phrases are defined because, as with all\nidioms, their meanings cannot be inferred simply from the words they consist of (see p. 523).\nLabels\nDictionaries apply labels to words or to particular meanings that have a special status or use.\nStyle labels restrict a word or one of its meanings to a particular level of usage:\nSlang: words or meanings inappropriate in writing except for a special effect, such as crumb\nfor ―a worthless or despicable person.‖\nInformal or colloquial: words or meanings appropriate for informal writing but not formal\nwriting, such as great to mean ―very good,‖ as in a great movie.\nNonstandard or substandard: words or meanings inappropriate for standard American\nEnglish, such as ain’t.\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 24\nVulgar or vulgar slang: words or meanings considered offensive in speech and writing, such\nas profanity.\nPoetic or literary: words or meanings used only in poetry or the most formal writing, such as\neve for evening and o’er for over.\nSubject labels tell us that a word or one of its meanings has a special use in a discipline or\nprofession. In its entry for relaxation, for instance, American Heritage presents specialized\nmeanings with the subject labels physiology, physics, and mathematics.\nRegion labels indicate that a particular spelling, pronunciation, or meaning of a word is not\nnational but limited to an area. A regional difference may be indicated by the label dialect.\nMerriam-Webster’s labels as dialect (dial) the uses of reckon to mean ―suppose‖ or ―think‖ (as in I\nreckon I’ll do that). More specific region labels may designate areas of the United States or other\ncountries.\nTime labels indicate words or their meanings that the language, in evolving, has discarded.\nObsolete designates words or mean-ings that are no longer used; archaic designates words or\nmeanings that are out of date but are used occasionally.\nSee pages 510–17 for further discussion of levels of usage and their appropriateness in your\nwriting.\nSynonyms\nSynonyms are words whose meanings are approximately the same, such as small and little.\nMerriam-Webster’s defines reckon with some words in small capital letters (COUNT, ESTIMATE,\nCONSIDER, and so on). These are both synonyms and cross-references, in that each word may be\nlooked up in its alphabetical place. Some diction-aries devote separate paragraphs to words with\nmany synonyms.\nIllustrative quotations\nDictionaries are made by collecting quotations showing actual uses of words in all kinds of\nspeech and writing. Some of these quotations, or others that the dictionary makers invent, may\nappear in the dictionary’s entries as illustrations of how a word may be used. Five such quotations\nillustrate uses of reckon in the Merriam-Webster’s entry (in these quotations, ~ stands for the word\nbeing illustrated).\nEXERCISE 40.1 Using a dictionary\nConsult your dictionary on five of the following words. First find out whether your dictionary lists the\noldest or the most common meanings first in its entries. Then, for each word, write down (a) the division\ninto syllables, (b) the pronunciation, (c) the grammatical functions and forms, (d) the etymology, (e) each\nmeaning, and (f) any special uses\nindicated by labels. Finally, use the word in at least two sentences of your own. (You can do this exercise\nonline at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\n1. depreciation 4. manifest 7. potlatch 10. toxic\n2. secretary 5. assassin 8. plain (adj.) 11. steal\n3. grammar 6. astrology 9. ceremony 12. obelisk\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 25\nVisit the companion Web site for links to online dictionaries and other language resources.\nDictionary entry for reckon\n41\nCHAPTER\nSpelling and\nthe Hyphen\nEnglish spelling is difficult, even for some very experienced and competent writers. You can\ntrain yourself to spell better, and this chapter will help you. But you can also improve instantly by\nacquiring three habits:\nCarefully proofread all of your writing.\nBe suspicious of your spellings.\nCheck a dictionary every time you doubt a spelling.\nNote A spelling checker can help you find and track spelling errors in your papers. But its\nusefulness is limited, mainly because it can’t spot the common error of confusing words with\nsimilar spellings, such as now/not, to/too, their/they’re/there, and principal/ principle. See pages\n60–61 for more on spelling checkers.\n41a Recognizing typical spelling problems\nSpelling well involves recognizing situations that commonly lead to misspelling:\npronunciation can mislead you in several ways; different forms of the same word may have\ndifferent spellings; and some words have more than one acceptable spelling.\n1 Being wary of pronunciation\nIn English, unlike some other languages, pronunciation of words is an unreliable guide to their\nspelling. The same letter or combination of letters may have different sounds in different words.\n(Say aloud these different ways of pronouncing the letters ough: tough, dough, cough, through,\nbough.) In addition, some words contain letters that are not pronounced clearly or at all, such as the\ned in asked, the silent e in swipe, or the unpronounced gh in tight.\nPronunciation is a particularly unreliable guide in spelling homonyms, words pronounced the\nsame though they have different spellings and meanings: great/grate, to/too/two. Some commonly\nconfused homonyms and near-homonyms, such as accept/except, are listed below. (See p. 551 for\ntips on how to use spelling lists.)\n2 Distinguishing between different forms of the same word\nSpelling problems may occur when forms of the same word have different spellings, as in the\nfollowing examples.\nVerbs and nouns\nVerb Noun Verb Noun\nadvise advice enter entrance\ndescribe description marry marriage\nspeak speech omit omission\nNouns and adjectives\nNoun Adjective Noun Adjective\ncomedy comic height high\ncourtesy courteous Britain British\ngenerosity generous\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 26\nIrregular verbs\nbegin, began, begun know, knew, known\nbreak, broke, broken ring, rang, rung\nIrregular nouns\nchild, children shelf, shelves\ngoose, geese tooth, teeth\nmouse, mice woman, women\nOther differences\nfour, forty thief, theft\n3 Using preferred spellings\nMany words have variant spellings as well as preferred spell-ings. Often the variant spellings\nlisted in an American dictionary are British spellings.\nAmerican British\ncolor, humor colour, humour\ntheater, center theatre, centre\ncanceled, traveled cancelled, travelled\njudgment judgement\nrealize realise\n41b Following spelling rules\nMisspelling is often a matter of misspelling a syllable rather than the whole word. The\nfollowing general rules focus on troublesome syllables, with notes for the occasional exceptions.\n1 Distinguishing between ie and ei\nWords like believe and receive sound alike in the second syllable, but the syllable is spelled\ndifferently. Use the familiar jingle to distinguish between ie and ei:\nI before e, except after c, or when pronounced ―ay‖ as in neighbor and\nweigh.\ni before e believe bier hygiene\ngrief thief friend\nei after c ceiling conceive perceive\nreceive deceit conceit\nei sounded neighbor weight eight\nas ―ay‖ sleigh freight vein\nExceptions In some words an ei combination neither follows c nor is pronounced ―ay.‖ These\nwords include either, neither, foreign, forfeit, height, leisure, weird, seize, and seizure. This\nsentence might help you remember some of them: The weird foreigner neither seizes leisure nor\nforfeits height.\nEXERCISE 41.1 Distinguishing between ie and ei\nInsert ie or ei in the words below. Check doubtful spellings in a diction-ary. (You can do this exercise\nonline at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\n1. br f 5. for gn 9. l surely 13. h ght\n2. dec ve 6. pr st 10. ach ve 14. fr ght\n3. rec pt 7. gr vance 11. pat nce 15. f nt\n4. s ze 8. f nd 12. p rce 16. s ve\n2 Keeping or dropping a final e\nMany words end with an unpronounced or silent e: move, brave, late, rinse. Drop the final e\nwhen adding an ending that begins with a vowel:\nadvise + able = advisable surprise + ing = surprising\nforce + ible = forcible guide + ance = guidance\nKeep the final, silent e when adding an ending that begins with a consonant:\nbattle + ment = battlement care + ful = careful\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 27\naccurate + ly = accurately like + ness = likeness\nExceptions The silent e is sometimes retained before an ending beginning with a vowel. It is\nkept when dye becomes dyeing, to avoid confusion with dying. It is kept to prevent\nmispronunciation of words like shoeing (not shoing) and mileage (not milage). And the final e\nis often retained after a soft c or g, to keep the sound of the consonant soft rather than hard:\ncourageous changeable noticeable\noutrageous manageable embraceable\nThe silent e is also sometimes dropped before an ending beginning with a consonant, when the e is\npreceded by another vowel:\nargue + ment = argument\ndue + ly = duly\ntrue + ly = truly\nEXERCISE 41.2 Keeping or dropping a final e\nCombine the following words and endings, keeping or dropping a final e as necessary to make correctly\nspelled words. Check doubtful spellings in a dictionary. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman\n.com/littlebrown.)\n1. malice + ious 4. retire + ment 7. note + able\n2. love + able 5. sue + ing 8. battle + ing\n3. service + able 6. virtue + ous 9. suspense + ion\n3 Keeping or dropping a final y\nWords ending in y often change their spelling when an ending is added to them. Change the\nfinal y to an i when it follows a consonant:\nbeauty, beauties worry, worried supply, supplies\nfolly, follies merry, merrier deputy, deputize\nBut keep the y when it follows a vowel, when the ending is -ing, or when it ends a proper name:\nday, days cry, crying May, Mays\nobey, obeyed study, studying Minsky, Minskys\nEXERCISE 41.3 Keeping or dropping a final y\nCombine the following words and endings, changing or keeping a\nfinal y as necessary to make correctly spelled words. Check doubt-\nful spellings in a dictionary. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\n1. imply + s 4. delay + ing 7. solidify +\ns\n2. messy + er 5. defy + ance 8. Murphy + s\n3. apply + ing 6. say + s9. supply + ed\n4 Doubling consonants\nWhether to double a word’s final consonant depends first on the number of syllables in the\nword. In one-syllable words, double the final consonant when a single vowel precedes the final\nconsonant. Otherwise, don’t double the consonant.\nslap, slapping pair, paired\ntip, tipping park, parking\nIn words of more than one syllable, double the final consonant when a single vowel precedes\nthe final consonant and the consonant ends a stressed syllable once the ending is added. Otherwise,\ndon’t double the consonant.\nrefer, referring refer, reference\nbegin, beginning relent, relented\noccur, occurrence despair, despairing\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 28\nEXERCISE 41.4 Doubling consonants\nCombine the following words and endings, doubling final consonants as necessary to make correctly\nspelled words. Check doubtful spellings in a dictionary. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/\nlittlebrown.)\n1. repair + ing 4. shop + ed 7. drip + ing\n2. admit + ance 5. conceal + ed 8. declaim + ed\n3. benefit + ed 6. allot + ed 9. parallel + ing\n5 Attaching prefixes\nAdding a prefix such as dis, mis, and un does not change the spelling of a word. When adding\na prefix, do not drop a letter from or add a letter to the original word:\nuneasy anti-intellectual defuse misstate\nunnecessary disappoint de-emphasize misspell\nantifreeze dissatisfied misinform\n(See also p. 555 for when to use hyphens with prefixes: prehistory versus ex-student.)\n6 Forming plurals\nNouns\nMost nouns form plurals by adding s to the singular form:\nboy, boys table, tables\ncarnival, carnivals Murphy, Murphys\nSome nouns ending in f or fe form the plural by changing the ending to ve before adding s:\nleaf, leaves wife, wives\nlife, lives yourself, yourselves\nSingular nouns ending in s, sh, ch, or x form the plural by adding es:\nkiss, kisses church, churches\nwish, wishes Jones, Joneses\n(Notice that verbs ending in s, sh, ch, or x form the third-person singular in the same way. Taxes\nand lurches are examples.)\nNouns ending in o preceded by a vowel usually form the plural by adding s:\nratio, ratios zoo, zoos\nNouns ending in o preceded by a consonant usually form the plural by adding es:\nhero, heroes tomato, tomatoes\nExceptions Some very common nouns form irregular plurals:\nchild, children man, men\nmouse, mice woman, women\nSome English nouns that were originally Italian, Greek, Latin, or French form the plural according\nto their original language:\nanalysis, analyses datum, data\nbasis, bases medium, media\nbeau, beaux phenomenon, phenomena\ncrisis, crises piano, pianos\ncriterion, criteria thesis, theses\nA few such nouns may form irregular or regular plurals: for instance, index, indices, indexes;\ncurriculum, curricula, curriculums. The regular plural is more contemporary.\nNoncount nouns do not form plurals, either regularly (with an added s) or irregularly.\nExamples of noncount nouns include equipment, courage, and wealth. (See p. 327.)\nCompound nouns\nForm plurals of compound nouns in one of two ways. Add s to the last word when the\ncomponent words are roughly equal in importance, whether or not they are hyphenated:\ncity-states breakthroughs\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 29\npainter-sculptors bucket seats\nAdd s to a noun combined with other parts of speech:\nfathers-in-law passersby\nNote, however, that most modern dictionaries give the plural of spoonful as spoonfuls.\nEXERCISE 41.5 Forming plurals\nMake the correct plural of each of the following singular words. Check doubtful spellings in a dictionary.\n(You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\n1. pile 5. mile per hour 9. Bales 13. thief\n2. donkey 6. box 10. cupful 14. goose\n3. beach 7. switch 11. libretto 15. hiss\n4. summary 8. sister-in-law 12. video 16. appendix\n41c Developing spelling skills\nThe following techniques can help you improve your spelling. In addition,\ndo not overrely on your computer’s spelling checker (see pp. 60–61).\n1 Editing and proofreading carefully\nIf spelling is a problem for you, give it high priority while editing your writing (p. 48) and\nagain while proofreading, your last chance to catch misspelled words (p. 58). Reading a draft\nbackward, word by word, can help you spot mistakes such as switched or omitted letters in words\nyou know. Because the procedure forces you to consider each word in isolation, it can also\nhighlight spellings you may be less sure of. A sense of uncertainty is crucial in spotting and\ncorrecting spelling errors, even for good spellers who make relatively few errors. Listen to your\nown uncertainty, and let it lead you to the dictionary.\n2 Using a dictionary\nHow can you look up a word you can’t spell? Start by guessing at the spelling and looking up\nyour guess. If that doesn’t work, pronounce the word aloud to come up with other possible\nspellings, and look them up. Unless the word is too specialized to be included in your dictionary,\ntrial and error will eventually pay off.\nIf you’re using a spelling checker, it may do the guessing for you by providing several choices\nfor misspelled words. But you may still need to check a dictionary to verify your choice.\n3 Pronouncing carefully\nCareful pronunciation is not always a reliable guide to spelling (see p. 543), but it can keep\nyou from misspelling words that are often mispronounced. For example:\nathletics (not atheletics) laboratory (not labratory)\ndisastrous (not disasterous) library (not libary)\nenvironment (not envirnment) lightning (not lightening)\nfrustrate (not fustrate) mischievous (not mischievious)\ngovernment (not goverment) nuclear (not nucular)\nheight (not heighth) recognize (not reconize)\nhistory (not histry) representative (not representive)\nirrelevant (not irrevelant) strictly (not stricly)\n4 Tracking and analyzing your errors\nKeep a list of the words marked ―misspelled‖ or ―spelling‖ or ―sp‖ in your papers. This list\nwill contain hints about your particular spelling problems, such as confusing affect and effect or\nforming plurals incorrectly. (If you need help analyzing the list, consult your writing instructor.)\nThe list will also provide a personalized study guide, a focus for your efforts to spell better.\n5 Using mnemonics\nMnemonics (pronounced with an initial n sound) are techniques for assisting your memory.\nThe er in letter and paper can remind you that stationery (meaning ―writing paper‖) has an er near\nthe end; stationary with an a means ―standing in place.‖ Or the word dome with its long o sound\ncan remind you that the building in which the legislature meets is spelled capitol, with an o. The\ncapital city is spelled with al like Albany, the capital of New York. If you identify the words you\nhave trouble spelling, you can think of your own mnemonics, which may work better for you than\nsomeone else’s.\n6 Studying spelling lists\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 30\nLearning to spell commonly misspelled words will reduce your spelling errors. For general\nimprovement, work with the following list of commonly misspelled words. Study only six or seven\nwords at a time. If you are unsure of the meaning of a word, look it up in a dictionary and try using\nit in a sentence. Pronounce the word out loud, syllable by syllable, and write the word out. (The list\nof similar-sounding words on pp. 543–44 should be considered an extension of the one below.)\n41d Using the hyphen to form or divide words\nThe hyphen (-) is a mark of punctuation used either to form words or to divide them at the\nends of lines.\n1 Forming compound adjectives\nWhen two or more words serve together as a single modifier before a noun, a hyphen or\nhyphens form the modifying words clearly into a unit:\nShe is a well-known actor.\nThe conclusions are based on out-of-date statistics.\nSome Spanish-speaking students work as translators.\nWhen the same compound adjectives follow the noun, hyphens are unnecessary and are usually left\nout.\nThe actor is well known.\nThe statistics were out of date.\nMany students are Spanish speaking.\nHyphens are also unnecessary in compound modifiers containing an -ly adverb, even when these fall\nbefore the noun: clearly defined terms; swiftly moving train.\nWhen part of a compound adjective appears only once in two or more parallel compound\nadjectives, hyphens indicate which words the reader should mentally join with the missing part:\nSchool-aged children should have eight- or nine-o’clock bedtimes.\n2 Writing fractions and compound numbers\nHyphens join the numerator and denominator of fractions and the parts of the whole numbers\ntwenty-one to ninety-nine:\nthree-fourths twenty-four\none-half eighty-seven\n3 Forming coined compounds\nWriters sometimes create (coin) temporary compounds and join the words with hyphens:\nMuhammad Ali gave his opponent a come-and-get-me look.\n4 Attaching some prefixes and suffixes\nDo not use hyphens with prefixes except as follows:\nWith the prefixes self-, all-, and ex-: self -control, all -inclusive,\nm m\nex-student.\nm\nWith a prefix before a capitalized word: un -American.\nm\nWith a capital letter before a word: T -shirt.\nm\nTo prevent misreading: de-emphasize, anti -intellectual.\nm m\nThe only suffix that regularly requires a hyphen is -elect, as in presiden t-elect.\nm\n5 Eliminating confusion\nIf you wrote the sentence Doonesbury is a comic strip character, the reader might stumble\nbriefly over your meaning. Is Doonesbury a character in a comic strip or a comic (funny) character\nwho strips? A hyphen would prevent any possible confusion: Doonesbury is a comi c-strip\nm\ncharacter.\nAdding prefixes to words can sometimes create ambiguity. Recreation (creation with the\nprefix re-) could mean either ―a new creation‖ or ―diverting, pleasurable activity.‖ The use of a\nhyphen, re-creation, limits the word to the first meaning. Without a hyphen the word suggests the\nm\nsecond meaning.\n6 Dividing words at the ends of lines\nYou can avoid occasional short lines in your documents by dividing some words between the\nend of one line and the beginning of the next. On a word processor, you can set the program to\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 31\ndivide words automatically at appropriate breaks (in the Tools menu, select Language and then\nHyphenation). To divide words manually, follow these guidelines:\nDivide words only between syllables—for instance, win-dows, not wi-ndows. Check a\ndictionary for correct syllable breaks.\nNever divide a one-syllable word.\nLeave at least two letters on the first line and three on the second line. If a word cannot be\ndivided to follow this rule (for instance, a-bus-er), don’t divide it.\nBreak an electronic address only after a slash. Do not hyphenate, because readers may\npreceive any added hyphens as part of the address.\nEXERCISE 41.6 Using hyphens in compound words\nInsert hyphens as needed in the following compounds. Mark all compounds that are correct as given.\nConsult a dictionary as needed. V shaped\n5. seven eighths 10. postwar 14. reeducate\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc - 32\nVisit the companion Web site for more help and an additional exercise on spelling and the hyphen.\nWords commonly confused\naccept (to receive)\nexcept (other than)\naffect (to have an influence on)\neffect (result)\nall ready (prepared)\nalready (by this time)\nallude (to refer to indirectly)\nelude (to avoid)\nallusion (indirect reference)\nillusion (erroneous belief or\nperception)\nascent (a movement up)\nassent (agreement)\nbare (unclothed)\nbear (to carry, or an animal)\nboard (a plane of wood)\nbored (uninterested)\nborn (brought into life)\nborne (carried)\nbrake (stop)\nbreak (smash)\nbuy (purchase)\nby (next to)\ncapital (the seat of a government)\ncapitol (the building where a\nlegislature meets)\ncite (to quote an authority)\nsight (the ability to see)\nsite (a place)\ndesert (to abandon)\ndessert (after-dinner course)\ndiscreet (reserved, respectful)\ndiscrete (individual or distinct)\nelicit (to bring out)\nillicit (illegal)\neminent (well known)\nimminent (soon to happen)\nfair (average, or lovely)\nfare (a fee for transportation)\nforth (forward)\nfourth (after third)\ngorilla (a large primate)\nguerrilla (a kind of soldier)\nhear (to perceive by ear)\nhere (in this place)\nheard (past tense of hear)\nherd (a group of animals)\nhole (an opening)\nwhole (complete)\nits (possessive of it)\nit’s (contraction of it is or it has)\nlead (heavy metal)\nled (past tense of lead)\n\nFOWLcp37-41.doc -
