42\nyacht\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 1\n9\nPART\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 2\nResearch\nWriting\n42\nCHAPTER\nPlanning a Research Project\n43\nCHAPTER\nFinding Sources\n44\nCHAPTER\nWorking with Sources\n45\nCHAPTER\nAvoiding Plagiarism and\nDocumenting Sources\n46\nCHAPTER\nWriting the Paper\n47\nCHAPTER\nUsing MLA Documentation\nand Format\n48\nCHAPTER\nTwo Research Papers in\nMLA Style\n42\nCHAPTER\nPlanning a\nResearch Project\nIf you’ve ever watched a TV or movie detective pursue a culprit, you know that research can\nbe exciting. When an investigator has a goal in sight, the seemingly mundane work of digging\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 3\nthrough files, interviewing witnesses, and piecing together clues becomes a concentrated and\nenthusiastic search.\nThis same excitement can be yours as you conduct research in school. Honest and inquisitive\nresearch writing does demand close attention to details, but the work will not be tedious if you see\nthe details as steadily contributing to discoveries about yourself and the world around you. As you\nconsider what others have to say about your subject and build on that to create new knowledge, you\nwill become an expert in your own right. You will have a significant, in-depth understanding of a\nsubject you care about, and you will communicate that understanding to others.\nThrough research writing, you will also learn skills that will help you in school, in work, and\nin life:\nUsing the library and the Internet for research, carefully re-cording information on where\nyou find sources.\nAnalyzing and evaluating others’ work.\nDrawing on others’ work to form, support, and extend your own opinions.\nPracticing intellectual integrity by presenting others’ work accurately and acknowledging\nyour sources fully.\nYour investigation will be influenced by whether you are expected mainly to report, to\ninterpret, or to analyze sources.\nIn reporting, you survey, organize, and objectively present the available evidence about a\ntopic.\nIn interpreting, you examine a range of views on a topic in order to answer a question with\nyour own conclusions.\nIn analyzing, you attempt to solve a problem or answer a question through critical thinking\nabout texts such as scholarly or literary works. (In this context, analysis stands for the entire\nprocess of critical reading and writing. See pp. 150–63.)\nThroughout Chapters 42–46, we will follow the development of research papers by two\nstudents, Edward Begay and Vanessa Haley. Begay’s work, emphasizing interpretation, receives\nsomewhat more attention; Haley’s work, emphasizing analysis, enters the discussion whenever her\nprocess differed significantly from Begay’s. Both students’ final papers appear in Chapter 48.\n42a Starting out\nWith its diverse and overlapping activities, research writing\ndemands more planning than other kinds of writing. A thoughtful plan and systematic procedures\nwill help you anticipate your needs, schedule your time, and complete the work successfully.\n1 Preparing a schedule\nAs soon as you receive an assignment for a research project, you can begin developing a\nstrategy for completing it. The first step should be making a schedule that apportions the available\ntime to the necessary work. A possible schedule appears on the next page. In it the research-writing\nprocess corresponds to the general writing process discussed in Chapters 1–3: planning or\ndeveloping (steps 1–9), drafting (step 10), and revising and editing (step 11), plus the additional\nimportant stage of documenting the sources you use (steps 12–13).\n2 Keeping a research journal\nTo keep track of your activities and ideas during research, maintain a research journal\nthroughout the process. (See pp. 17 and 154 on journal keeping.) Make your journal portable so\nthat you can carry it with you conveniently. Many researchers use paper notebooks or handheld or\nnotebook computers. Some schools offer students Web log space for recording research and other\nlearning activities.\nIn the research journal’s dated entries, you can keep a record of sources you consult, the leads\nyou want to pursue, any dead ends you reach, and, most important, your thoughts about sources,\nleads, dead ends, new directions, relationships, and anything else that strikes you. You will\nprobably find that the very act of writing in your journal opens your mind and clarifies your\nthinking, making your research increasingly productive and rewarding.\nNote The research journal is the place to track and develop your own ideas. To avoid mixing\nup your thoughts and those of oth-ers, keep separate notes on what your sources actually say, using\none of the methods discussed on pages 615–17.\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 4\n42b Finding a researchable subject and question\nBefore reading this section, review the suggestions given on pages 6–9 for finding and\nlimiting an essay subject. Generally, the same procedure applies to writing any kind of research\npaper: begin with an assigned subject or one that interests you (perhaps one you’ve already written\nabout without benefit of research), and then narrow the subject to manageable size by asking\nquestions about it. However, selecting and limiting a subject for a research paper can present\nspecial opportunities and problems.\n1 Choosing an appropriate subject\nA subject for a research paper has four primary requirements, each with corresponding pitfalls:\n1. Ample sources of information are available on the subject. Other researchers should have\nhad a chance to produce evidence on the subject, weigh the evidence, and publish their\nconclusions. And the sources should be accessible.\nAvoid very recent subjects, such as a new medical discovery or a breaking story in today’s\nnewspaper.\n2. The subject encourages research in the kinds and number of sources required by the\nassignment.\nAvoid (a) subjects that depend entirely on personal opinion and experience, such as the\nvirtues of your hobby; and (b) subjects that require research in only one source, such as a\nstraight\nfactual biography or a how-to like ―Making Lenses for Eyeglasses.‖ (An exception to b is a\npaper in which you analyze a single work such as a novel or painting.)\n3. The subject will lead you to an assessment of sources and to defensible conclusions. Even\nwhen a research paper is intended to persuade, the success of the argument will depend on the\nbal-anced presentation of all significant points of view.\nAvoid controversial subjects that rest entirely on belief or prejudice, such as when human life\nbegins or why women (or men) are superior. Though these subjects may certainly be disputed,\nyour own preconceptions could slant your research or conclusions. Further, your readers are\nunlikely to be swayed from their own beliefs.\n4. The subject suits the length of paper assigned and the time given for research and\nwriting.\nAvoid broad subjects that have too many sources to survey adequately, such as a major event\nin history or the collected works of a poet.\n2 Posing a research question\nAsking a question about your subject can give direction to your research by focusing your\nthinking on a particular approach. You can even begin asking questions before you find your\nspecific subject, as a way of opening avenues you may not have considered.\nThe students Edward Begay and Vanessa Haley arrived at their research questions by slightly\ndifferent paths. For a composition course, Begay’s instructor assigned an interpretation with a\npersuasive purpose but left the selection of subject to the student. Begay had recently read several\nnewspaper articles on a subject that intrigued him: how computers will alter the future. Taking this\nbroad subject as his starting point, he used clustering (see p. 22) to pursue some ideas. While\ngenerating the cluster diagram on the facing page, Begay found himself giving the most thought to\nthe Internet. He posed questions until a cluster about access to the Internet led him to a question\nthat seemed interesting and significant: Will the poor be excluded from the Internet?\nIn developing a topic and question for an analysis paper assigned in a composition course,\nVanessa Haley followed a somewhat different procedure. Instead of starting with a general subject,\nas Begay did, Haley began by looking for an unresolved question, an interesting problem, or a\ndisagreement among the experts in some field of study. She had recently been reading an anthology\nof writings on the environment, and she had been disturbed by how many naturalists and\nenvironmentalists view human beings not as part of ―nature‖ but as something separate from it,\nusually as its destroyer. In her journal, Haley wrote this entry:\nMany writers see nature as a place for humans to retreat to, or a wonderful thing that humans are ruining.\nHumans aren’t considered natural themselves—human civilization isn’t considered natural. Human civ. is\n―anti-natural.‖ Isn’t such a separation unrealistic and damaging? We are natural. We’re here to stay, and\nwe’re not going back to the Stone Age, so we’d better focus on the connections between ―us‖ and ―it‖\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 5\n(nature) rather than just the differences. Dillard seems to do this—seems to connect human and natural\nworlds. People are neither better nor worse than nature but just bound up in it. ―Nature is as careless as it\nis bountiful, and . . . with that extravagance goes a crushing waste that will one day include our own\ncheap lives‖ (―Fecundity‖).\nAt the end of this entry, Haley refers to and quotes the writer Annie Dillard, one of the authors\nrepresented in the anthology. Haley decided to explore Dillard’s views further by reading and\nanalyzing more of her work. Haley’s opening question for investigation, then, was How does Annie\nDillard see the place of humanity in nature?\nEXERCISE 42.1 Finding a topic and question\nChoose three of the following subjects (or three subjects of your own), and narrow each one to at least one\nsubject and question suitable for beginning work on a research paper. (This exercise can be the first step\nin a research-writing project that continues through Chapters 42–46.)\n1. Bilingual education\n2. National security and civil\nrights\n3. Distribution of music by con-\nventional versus electronic\nmeans\n4. Dance in America\n5. The history of women’s\nsuffrage\n6. Genetically modified foods\n7. Immigrants in the United States\n8. Space exploration\n9. Business espionage\n10. The effect of television\non professional sports\n11. Child abuse\n12. African Americans and\ncivil rights\n13. Successes in cancer research\n14. Computer piracy\n15. The European exploration\nof North America before\nColumbus\n16. Hazardous substances in the workplace\n17. Television evangelism\n18. Science fiction\n19. Treatment or prevention of AIDS in the United States or Africa\n20. Water pollution\n21. Women writers\n22. Campaign financing\n23. Comic film actors\n24. An unsolved crime\n25. Alternative fuels\n26. Male and female heroes in modern fiction\n27. Computers and the privacy of the individual\n28. Gothic or romance novels in the nineteenth and twenty-first centuries\n29. The social responsibility of business\n30. Stem-cell research\n42c Developing a research strategy\nBefore you start looking for sources, consider what you already know about your subject and\nwhere you are likely to find information on it.\n1 Tapping into your own knowledge\nDiscovering what you already know about your subject will guide you in discovering what\nyou don’t know. Take some time to spell out facts you have learned, opinions you have heard or\nread elsewhere, and of course your own opinions. Use one of the discovery techniques discussed in\nChapter 2 to explore and develop your ideas: keeping a journal, observing your surroundings,\nfreewriting, list making, clustering, asking the journalist’s questions, using the patterns of\ndevelopment, or thinking critically.\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 6\nWhen you’ve explored your thoughts, make a list of questions for which you don’t have\nanswers, whether factual (What is the distribution of computers among affluent and poor schools?)\nor more open-ended (Are computers a positive force in education?). These questions will give you\nclues about the sources you need to look for first.\n2 Setting goals for sources\nFor many research projects, you’ll want to consult a mix of sources, as described on the\nfollowing pages. You may start by seeking the outlines of your topic—the range and depth of\nopinions about it—in reference works and articles in popular periodicals or through a Web search.\nThen, as you refine your views and your research question, you’ll move on to more specialized\nsources, such as scholarly books and periodicals and your own interviews or surveys. (See pp. 575–\n97 for more on each kind of source.)\nLibrary and Internet sources\nThe print and electronic sources available through your library—mainly reference works,\nperiodicals, and books—have two big advantages over most of what you’ll find on the Internet:\nthey are cataloged and indexed for easy retrieval; and they are generally reliable, having been\nscreened first by their publishers and then by the library’s staff. In contrast, the Internet’s retrieval\nsystems are more difficult to use effectively, and Internet sources tend to be less reliable because\nmost do not pass through any screening before being posted. (There are many exceptions, such as\nonline scholarly journals and reference works. But these sources are generally available through\nyour library’s Web site as well.)\nMost instructors expect research writers to consult sources found in and through the library,\nincluding print sources. But most will accept Internet sources, too, if you have used them\njudiciously. Even with its disadvantages, the Internet can be a valuable resource for primary\nsources, current information, and a diversity of views. For guidelines on evaluating both library and\nInternet sources, see pages 599–609.\nPrimary and secondary sources\nAs much as possible, you should rely on primary sources, or firsthand accounts: historical\ndocuments (letters, speeches, and so on), eyewitness reports, works of literature, reports on\nexperiments or surveys conducted by the writer, or your own interviews, experiments, observations,\nor correspondence.\nIn contrast, secondary sources report and analyze information drawn from other sources\n(often primary ones): a reporter’s summary of a controversial issue, a historian’s account of a\nbattle, a critic’s reading of a poem, a physicist’s evaluation of several studies. Secondary sources\nmay contain helpful summaries and interpretations that direct, support, and extend your own\nthinking. However, most research-writing assignments expect your ideas to go beyond those in\nsuch sources.\nScholarly and popular sources\nThe scholarship of acknowledged experts is essential for depth, authority, and specificity.\nMost instructors expect you to emphasize scholarly sources in your research. But the general-\ninterest views and information of popular sources can help you apply more scholarly approaches to\ndaily life.\nCheck the title. Is it technical, or does it use a general vocabulary?\nCheck the publisher. Is it a scholarly journal (such as Education Forum) or a publisher of\nscholarly books (such as Harvard University Press), or is it a popular magazine (such as Time\nor Newsweek) or a publisher of popular books (such as Random House)?\nCheck the length of periodical articles. Scholarly articles are generally much longer than\nmagazine and newspaper articles.\nCheck the author. Have you seen the name elsewhere, which might suggest that the author is\nan expert?\nCheck the electronic address. Addresses, or URLs, for Internet sources often include an\nabbreviation that tells you something about the origin of the source: edu means the source\ncomes from an educational institution, gov from a government body, org from a nonprofit\norganization, com from a commercial organization such as a corporation. The abbreviation is\nnot a firm guide to the kind of source—edu sites, for instance, may include student papers and\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 7\nWeb logs as well as works by scholars—but it can indicate the context. (See pp. 592–93 for\nmore on types of online sources.)\nOlder and newer sources\nCheck the publication date. For most subjects a combination of older, established sources\n(such as books) and current sources (such as newspaper articles, interviews, or Web sites) will\nprovide both background and up-to-date information. Only historical subjects or very current\nsubjects like Edward Begay’s (the Internet) require an emphasis on one extreme or another.\nImpartial and biased sources\nSeek a range of viewpoints. Sources that attempt to be impartial can offer trustworthy facts\nand an overview of your subject. Sources with clear biases can offer a diversity of opinion. Of\ncourse, to discover bias, you may have to read the source carefully (see\np. 601); but even a bibliographical listing can be informative.\nCheck the title. It may reveal something about point of view. (Consider these contrasting\ntitles uncovered by Edward Begay: ―Computer Literacy and Ideology‖ versus ―The Process of\nIn-troducing Internet-Based Classroom Projects and the Role of School Librarians.‖)\nCheck the author. You may have heard of the author before as a respected researcher (thus\nmore likely to be objective) or as a leading proponent of a certain view (less likely to be\nobjective).\nNote Internet sources must be approached with particular care. See pages 602–09.\nSources with helpful features\nDepending on your topic and how far along your research is, you may want to look for sources\nwith features such as illustrations (which can clarify important concepts), bibliographies (which can\ndirect you to other sources), and indexes (which can help you develop keywords for electronic\nsearches; see pp. 573–75).\nEXERCISE 42.2 DEVELOPING A RESEARCH STRATEGY\nFollowing the suggestions on page 564, write what you already know about the topic you selected in\nExercise 42.1 (pp. 563–64), and then frame some questions for which you’ll need to find answers. Also in\nwriting, consider the kinds of sources you’ll probably need to consult, using the categories given on the\npreceding pages.\n42d Making a working, annotated bibliography\nWhen you begin searching for sources, it may be tempting to pursue each possibility as you\ncome across it. But that approach would prove inefficient and probably ineffective. Instead, you’ll\nwant to find out the full range of sources available—from scholarly and popular articles to books\nand Web sites—and then decide on a good number to consult. For a paper of 1800 to 2500 words,\ntry for ten to thirty promising titles as a start.\nTo keep track of where sources are, compile a working bibliography, as you uncover\npossibilities. Record the information for a source as soon as you think you may want to use it,\nfollowing the guidelines on pages 568–69. Then you’ll be able to find the source when you’re\nready to consult it, and you’ll have the information needed to cite the source in your paper.\nMany instructors ask student to prepare an annotated bibliography that records not only where\nsources are but also what they are. Suggestions for compiling an annotated bibliography appear on\npages 570 and 600.\n1 Tracking source information\nYou have several options for making a working bibliography:\nCopy source information by hand on note cards (usually 3\" u 5\"), one source to a card.\nWith this system, you can record source information no matter where you are, as long as you\nhave a pen or pencil, and you can easily add, delete, and rearrange sources. Some instructors\nrequire that a working bibliography be submitted on note cards.\nCreate the working bibliography on your word processor or handheld computer, either\ntyping the information yourself\nor downloading it from online indexes, catalogs, and other sources. You’ll be able to sort\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 8\nsources by subject or by author and to copy bibliographic data into your paper when you\nprepare final source citations.\nCombine the two systems: print and cut up computer-generated source listings, and then\npaste or tape each source on a note card.\nWhatever system you use, be sure to record all the information you will need. A downloaded\nlisting, for instance, may look complete but lack the name of the database and other crucial\ninformation.\nThe two records below come from Edward Begay’s working bibliography. The first, for a\nbook, Begay handwrote on a note card. The second, for a magazine article, Begay downloaded\nfrom a subscription service, printed out, and then annotated with information he knew he would\nneed.\n2 Recording source information\nWhen you turn in your paper, you will be expected to attach a list of the sources you have\nused. So that readers can check or follow up on your sources, your list must include all the\ninformation needed to find the sources, in a format readers can understand. The box below shows\nthe information you should record for each type of source so that you will not have to retrace your\nsteps later. See later pages for illustrations of how to locate this information for books (660),\njournal articles (667), newspaper articles (669), Web sites (675), and sources you find through\nsubscription databases (672).\nNote Whenever possible, record source information in the correct format for the\ndocumentation style you will be using. Then you will be less likely to omit needed information or\nto confuse dates, numbers, and other data when it’s time to write your citations. This book\ndescribes four styles: MLA (p. 647), Chicago (p. 764), APA (p. 784), and CSE (p. 812). For others,\nconsult one of the guides listed on pages 784 and 812. (Begay’s first card on p. 568 follows MLA\nstyle.) See also page 638for tips on using bibliography software to format source information.\n3 Annotating your bibliography\nAnnotating a working bibliography converts a simple list into a tool for assessing sources.\nWhen you discover a possible source, record not only its publication information but also the\nfollowing:\nWhat you know about the source’s content. Periodical databases and book catalogs\ngenerally include abstracts or summaries of sources that can help you with this part of the\nannotation.\nHow you think the source may be helpful in your research. Does it offer expert opinion,\nstatistics, an important example, or a range of views? Does it place your subject in a historical,\nsocial, or economic context?\nTaking time to prepare thorough annotations can help you discover gaps that may remain in\nyour sources and will later help you decide which sources to pursue in depth. Edward Begay\nannotated a bibliography entry on his computer with a summary and a note on the source features\nhe thought would be most useful to him:\nAs you become more familiar with your sources, you can use your initial annotated\nbibliography to record your evaluation of them and more detailed thoughts on how they fit into\nyour research. See page 600 on this later stage.\nEXERCISE 42.3 Compiling an annotated working bibliography\nPrepare an annotated working bibliography of at least ten sources for a research paper on one of the\nfollowing people or on someone of your own choosing. Begin by limiting the subject to a manageable\nsize, posing a question about a particular characteristic or achievement of the person. Then consult\nreference works, periodical indexes, the library’s book catalog, and the Web. (See pp. 575–91 for more on\nthese resources.) For each source, record complete publication information as well as a summary and a\nnote on the source’s potential use.\n1. Steven Jobs (a founder of Apple Computer), or another business entrepreneur\n2. Ruth Bader Ginsburg, or another Supreme Court justice\n3. Emily Dickinson, or another writer\n4. Shaquille O’Neal, or another sports figure\n5. Isamu Noguchi, or another artist\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 9\nVisit the companion Web site for more help and an additional exercise on\nplanning a research project.\nStarting out\nPlanning a research project\nScheduling steps in research writing\n(See the pages in parentheses for discussion of the steps.)\nComplete\nby:\n1. Setting a schedule and beginning a research journal (previ-ous page)\n2.Finding a researchable subject and question (below)\n3. Developing a research strategy (p. 564)\n4.
