Preparing and proofreading the final manuscript (p. 646)\nFinal paper due\nEach segment marked off by a horizontal line will occupy roughly one-fourth of the total time. The most\nunpredictable segments are the first two, so get started early enough to accommodate the unexpected.\nYou can download this schedule from ablongman.com/littlebrown. Use a duplicate to plan and time the\nspecific steps of each research project you work on.\nFinding a subject and question\nChecklist for a good research subject\n1. Published sources are ample: the subject is not so recent that other re-searchers will still be\ndiscovering it.\n2. Sources are diverse: the subject is neither wholly personal nor\nwholly factual.\n3. Sources can be assessed objectively: the subject is not solely a\nmatter of belief or prejudice.\n4. Sources can be examined thoroughly in the assigned time and\nlength: the subject is not too broad.\nPlanning a research project\nClustering for a research question\nFinding a subject and question\nPlanning a research project\nDeveloping a research strategy\nPlanning a research project\nMaking a working, annotated bibliography\nPlanning a research project\nBasic entries for a working bibliography\nMaking a working, annotated bibliography\nInformation for a working bibliography\nFor books\nLibrary call number\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 10\nName(s) of author(s), editor(s), translator(s), or others listed\nTitle and subtitle\nPublication data:\nPlace of publication\nPublisher’s name\nDate of publication\nOther important data, such as\nedition or volume number\nFor periodical articles\nName(s) of author(s)\nTitle and subtitle of article\nTitle of periodical\nPublication data:\nVolume number and issue number (if any) in which\narticle appears\nDate of issue\nPage numbers on which\narticle appears\nFor electronic sources\nName(s) of author(s)\nTitle and subtitle\nPublication data for books and articles (see above)\nDate of release, online posting,\nor latest revision\nYou can download these lists from ablongman.com/littlebrown. Copy the appropriate list for each source\nyou’re using, and fill in the required in-formation.\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 11\nMedium (online, CD-ROM, etc.)\nFormat of online source (Web site, Web page, e-mail, etc.)\nDate you consulted the source\nComplete URL (unless source was obtained through a subscription service and has no\nusable address)\nFor source obtained through a subscription service:\nName of database\nName of service\nElectronic address of the service home page or search terms used to reach the source\nFor other sources\nName(s) of author(s), creator(s), or others listed, such as a government department, recording artist, or\nphotographer\nTitle of work\nFormat, such as unpublished letter, live performance, or photograph\nPublication or production data:\nPublication title\nPublisher’s or producer’s name\nDate of publication, release, or production\nIdentifying numbers (if any)\nPlanning a research project\nEntry for an annotated working bibliography\nPublication and access information for source\nUnited States. Dept. of Education. National Center for Education Statistics.\nInternet Access in US Public Schools and Classrooms. 24 Feb. 2005. 12 Mar.\n2005 <http://nces.ed.gov/\npubsearch/pubsinfo.asp?pubid=2005015>.\nReport on the annual NCES survey of the quantity and quality\nof technology used in K-12 classrooms. Includes important\nstatistics on trends—student-to-computer ratios, teacher training, computer availability to students in different socio-\neconomic brackets.\nSummary of source\nIdeas on use of source\nSearching electronically\n43\nCHAPTER\nFinding Sources\nOnce you have discovered a research subject and question, have developed a research strategy,\nand know how to make an annotated working bibliography, you’re ready to find sources. This\nchapter discusses electronic searches and the kinds of sources, both print and electronic, that are\navailable to you.\nNote If you require sources that are not available from your own library, you may be able to\nobtain them from another library by mail, fax, or computer. Ask your librarian for help, and plan\nahead: interlibrary loans can take a week or longer.\n43a Searching electronically\n1 Beginning with your library’s Web site\nAs you conduct research, the World Wide Web will be your gateway to ideas and information.\nAlways start with the Web site of your library, not with a public search engine such as Google.\n(Google Scholar, a new tool that searches for scholarly articles, is discussed on p. 583.) The\nlibrary’s site will lead you to vast resources, includ-ing books, periodical articles, and reference\nworks. More important, every source you find on the library’s site will have passed through filters\nto ensure its value. A scholarly journal article, for instance, undergoes at least three successive\nreviews: first, subject-matter experts deem it worth publishing in the journal; next, a database\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 12\nvendor deems the journal worth including in the database; and finally, your school’s librarians\ndeem the database worth subscribing to.\nGoogle and other search engines may seem more user-friendly than the library’s Web site and\nmay seem to return plenty of sources for you to work with. Many of the sources may indeed be\nreliable and relevant to your research, but many more will not be. In the end, a library Web search\nwill be more efficient and more effective than a direct Web search. (For help with evaluating\nsources from any resource, see pp. 599–609.)\nNote Start with your library’s Web site, but don’t stop there. Many books, periodicals, and\nother excellent sources are available only on library shelves, not online, and most instructors expect\nresearch papers to be built to some extent on these resources. When you spot promising print\nsources while browsing a library’s online databases, make records of them and then look them up at\nthe library. You can also browse the bookshelves to discover sources (see p. 580).\n2 Anticipating the kinds of electronic sources\nYour school’s library and the Web offer several kinds of electronic resources that are suitable\nfor academic research. For more on searching these resources, see pages 575–92.\nThe library’s catalog of holdings is a database that lists all the resources that the library\nowns or subscribes to: books, journals, magazines, newspapers, reference works, and more.\nThe catalog may also include the holdings of other school libraries nearby or in your state.\nOnline databases include indexes, bibliographies, and other reference works. They are your\nmain route to articles in periodicals, providing publication information, summaries, and often\nfull text (see below). Your library subscribes to the databases and makes them available\nthrough its Web site. (You may also discover databases directly on the Web, but, again, the\nlibrary is the more productive starting place.)\nDatabases on CD-ROM include the same information as online databases, but they must be\nread at a library computer terminal. Increasingly, libraries are providing CD-ROM databases\nthrough their Web sites or are moving away from CD-ROMs in favor of online databases.\nFull-text resources contain the entire contents of articles, book chapters, even whole books.\nThe library’s databases provide access to the full text of many listed sources. In addition,\nmany Web sites, such as those for government agencies, offer the full text of articles, reports,\nand other publications.\n3 Using keywords\nOnce you determine what resources are available, you should plan your search. Careful\nplanning is essential: a too-casual search can miss helpful sources while returning hundreds, even\nthousands, of irrelevant sources.\nProbably the most important element in planning a search is to develop keywords, or\ndescriptors, that name your subject for databases and Web search engines.\nDatabases vs. the Web\nTo develop keywords it helps to understand an important difference in how library databases\nand the open Web work:\nA database indexes sources by authors, titles, publication years, and its own subject\nheadings. The subject headings reflect the database’s directory of terms and are assigned by\npeople who have read the sources. You can find these subject headings by using your own\nkeywords until you locate a promising source. The information for the source will list the\nheadings under which the database indexes it and other sources like it. (See p. 585 for an\nillustration.) You can then use those headings for further searches.\nA Web search engine seeks your keywords in the titles and texts of sites. The process is\nentirely electronic, so the results from a search engine depend on how well your keywords\ndescribe your subject and anticipate the words used in sources. If you describe your subject\ntoo broadly or describe it specifically but don’t match the vocabulary in relevant sources, your\nsearch will turn up few relevant sources and probably many that aren’t relevant.\nKeyword refinement\nEvery database and search engine provides a system that you can use to refine your keywords\nfor a productive search. The basic operations appear in the box below, but resources do differ. For\ninstance, some assume that AND should link two or more keywords, while others provide options\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 13\nspecifying ―Must contain all the words,‖ ―May contain any of the words,‖ and other equivalents for\nthe operations in the box. You can learn a search engine’s system by consulting its Advanced\nSearch page.\nTrial and error\nYou will probably have to use trial and error in developing your keywords. Because different\ndatabases have different directories and many search tools have no directory at all, you should\ncount\non occasionally running dry (turning up few or no sources) or hitting uncontrollable gushers\n(turning up hundreds or thousands of mostly irrelevant sources). But the process is not busywork—\nfar from it. Besides leading you eventually to worthwhile sources, it can also teach you a great deal\nabout your subject: how you can or should narrow it, how it is and is not described by others, what\nothers consider interesting or debatable about it, and what the major arguments are.\n43b Finding reference works\nReference works, often available online or on CD-ROM, include encyclopedias, dictionaries,\ndigests, bibliographies, indexes, atlases, and handbooks. Your research must go beyond reference\nworks; indeed, many instructors discourage students from relying on such sources for ideas and\ninformation in final papers. But reference works can help you get started:\nThey can help you decide whether your subject interests you and meets the requirements\nfor a research paper (p. 561).\nThey can direct you to more detailed sources on your subject.\nThey can help you refine keywords for electronic searches, giv-ing you the terminology of\nthe field you’re researching.\nThey can identify the main debates in a field and the proponents of each side.\nEdward Begay’s use of reference works illustrates how helpful such sources can be as a\nstarting point, even for a topic as current as the Internet. Begay first consulted Bibliographic Guide\nto the History of Computing, Computers, and the Information Processing Industry (to get some\nbackground on computers and the Internet) and Encyclopedia of Sociology (to explore the concept\nof equality in education).\nThe following lists give the types of reference works. Ask a reference librarian if you’re\nunsure of where to start. The librarian can also advise you which sources are available through the\nlibrary’s Web site, in print or on CD-ROM at the library, or directly via the Web.\nGeneral encyclopedias\nGeneral encyclopedias give brief overviews and bibliographies. Covering all fields, they are\nconvenient but very limited.\nAcademic American Encyclopedia\nCollier’s Encyclopedia\nThe Columbia Encyclopedia\nEncyclopedia Americana\nEncyclopaedia Britannica\nEncyclopedia International\nRandom House Encyclopedia\nSpecialized encyclopedias, dictionaries, bibliographies\nA specialized encyclopedia, dictionary, or bibliography generally covers a single field or\nsubject. These works will give you more detailed and more technical information than a general\nreference work will, and many of them (especially bibliographies) will direct you to particular\nbooks and articles on your subject. For information on specialized reference works in various\ndisciplines, consult lists elsewhere in this book:\nLiterature (p. 744)\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 14\nOther humanities, including history, the arts, philosophy, and religion (p. 761)\nSocial sciences, such as business and economics, criminal justice, education, and psychology\n(p. 781)\nNatural and applied sciences, such as biology, engineering, mathematics, and physics (p.\n810)\nTwo general references provide a wide range of information in many fields:\nEssay and General Literature Index. Lists tens of thousands of articles and essays that appear in books\n(rather than periodicals) and that might not be listed elsewhere.\nOxford Reference Online. A compilation of general and specialized dictionaries, encyclopedias, and other\nsources.\nUnabridged dictionaries and special dictionaries on language\nUnabridged dictionaries are more comprehensive than college or abridged dictionaries.\nSpecial dictionaries give authoritative information on aspects of language. (See Chapter 40 for\nmore on the kinds of dictionaries and how to use them.)\nUnabridged dictionaries\nA Dictionary of American English on Historical Principles\nThe Oxford English Dictionary\nWebster’s Third New International Dictionary of the English Language\nSpecial dictionaries\nR. W. Burchfield, ed., The New Fowler’s Modern English Usage\nFrederic G. Cassidy et al., eds., Dictionary of American Regional English\nRobert L. Chapman, ed., Roget’s International Thesaurus\nBryan A. Garner, A Dictionary of Modern American Usage\nJ. E. Lighter, ed., Historical Dictionary of American Slang\nCharles T. Onions et al., eds., The Oxford Dictionary of English Etymology\nBiographical reference works\nIf you want to learn about someone’s life, achievements, credentials, or position, or if you\nwant to learn the significance of a name you’ve come across, consult one of the reference works\nbelow.\nAmerican Men and Women of Science\nContemporary Authors\nCurrent Biography\nDictionary of American Biography\nDictionary of American Negro Biography\nDictionary of Literary Biography\nDictionary of National Biography (British)\nDictionary of Scientific Biography\nMarquis Who’s Who (nearly twenty publications organized by geography, profession, and other\ncategories)\nNotable American Women\nWebster’s New Biographical Dictionary\nWorld Authors\nAtlases and gazetteers\nAtlases are bound collections of maps; gazetteers are geographical dictionaries.\nBritannica Atlas\nCosmopolitan World Atlas\nNational Atlas of the United States of America\nNational Geographic Atlas of the World\nTimes Atlas of the World\nTimes Atlas of World History\nWebster’s New Geographical Dictionary\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 15\nAlmanacs and yearbooks\nBoth almanacs and yearbooks are annual compilations of facts. Yearbooks\nrecord information about the previous year in a country, field, or other\nsubject. Almanacs give facts about a variety of fields.\nAmericana Annual\nBritannica Book of the Year\nFacts on File Yearbook\nInformation Please Almanac\nUS Bureau of the Census, Statistical Abstract of the United States\nWorld Almanac and Book of Facts\n43c Finding books\nBooks can provide background information, subject surveys, popular views of culture,\nstatements of scholarly theory, research results—in short, a broad range of secondary and primary\nsources (see p. 565).\n1 Using the library catalog\nYour library’s catalog is searchable at terminals in the library and via the library’s Web site.\nWith WorldCat, available through most libraries, you can also search for specific books in the\ncatalogs of tens of thousands of libraries worldwide. You may be able to borrow books from\nanother library in your area. Ask a librarian for assistance with remote searches and loans. And\nallow time for a book to be sent after your request.\nAll book catalogs contain similar information, though it may be organized differently from\none library to the next. By far the most widely used format derives from the Library of Congress\ncataloging system and includes author, title, publisher, date of publication, description (number of\npages, size, and other data), subject headings the book is listed under, and the library’s call number\n(which directs you to the book’s location in the stacks). See page 581 for a complete book record\nshowing all this information.\n2 Using a search strategy\nUnless you seek a specific author or title, you’ll want to find books with a subject or keyword\nsearch of the library’s catalog. In a subject search, you start with headings found in Library of\nCongress Subject Headings (LCSH), the massive directory under which the Library of Congress\ncatalogs books. In a keyword search, you start with your own description of your subject as a way\nof locating appropriate LCSH headings.\nThe keyword approach is illustrated by part of Edward Begay’s catalog search, shown below\nand on the next page. In his initial keyword search (screen 1), Begay used Internet and access. The\nsearch returned several books (screen 2), including a promising one titled Social Consequences of\nInternet Use. The complete record for this book (screen 3) showed that it was indexed under three\nLCSH subject headings: Internet—Social aspects—United States; Digital divide —United States;\nand Telecommunication—Social aspects—United States. Begay then used these headings to search\nthe catalog further.\nNote In addition to searching the book catalog, you can also browse the library shelves. The\nfirst part of the call number for a promising title (HM851 in the full record opposite) will lead you\nto other books on the same subject that a catalog search might miss.\n3 Using references to books\nTwo types of references can help you identify general books that have information about your\ntopic:\nPublishing bibliographies tell whether a book is still in print, whether a paperback edition is\navailable, what books were published on a certain topic in a certain year, and so on:\nBooks in Print. Books indexed by author, title, and subject.\nCumulative Book Index\nYou might, for example, want to know if the author of an encyclopedia article has published\nany relevant books since the date of the encyclopedia. You could look up the author’s name in\nthe latest Books in Print to find out.\nReview indexes list published reviews of books and can help you evaluate whether a book is\nrelevant to your subject:\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 16\nBook Review Digest. Summarizes and indexes reviews of books.\nBook Review Index\nCurrent Book Review Citations\nFor specialized review indexes, see pages 783 (social sciences) and 811 (natural and applied\nsciences).\n43d Finding periodicals\nPeriodicals include newspapers, journals, and magazines. News-papers, the easiest to\nrecognize, are useful for detailed accounts of past and current events. Journals and magazines can\nbe harder to distinguish, but their differences are important. Most college instructors expect\nstudents’ research to rely more on journals than on magazines.\nJournals\nExamples: American Anthropologist, Journal of Black Studies, Journal of Chemical Education\nAvailable mainly through college and university libraries.\nArticles are intended to advance knowledge in a particular field.\nWriters and readers are specialists in the field.\nArticles always include source\ncitations.\nArticles are usually long, ten\npages or more\nAppearance is bland, with black-only type, little or no decoration, and only illustrations that directly\namplify the text, such as graphs.\nIssues may appear quarterly or\nless often.\nIssues may be paged separately (like a magazine) or may be paged sequentially throughout an annual\nvolume, so that issue number 3 (the third issue of the year) could open on page 327. (The method of\npagination affects source citations. See p. 666.)\nMagazines\nExamples: The New Yorker, Time, Rolling Stone, People\nAvailable in public libraries, on newsstands, and in bookstores.\nArticles are intended to express opinion, inform, or entertain.\nWriters may or may not be specialists in their subjects. Readers are members of the general public or a\nsubgroup with a particular interest.\nArticles rarely include source\ncitations.\nArticles are usually short, fewer than ten pages\nAppearance varies but is generally lively, with color, decoration (headings, sidebars, and other elements),\nand illustrations (drawings, photographs).\nIssues may appear weekly, bi-weekly, or monthly.\nIssues are paged separately,\neach beginning on page 1.\n1 Using periodical databases\nPeriodical databases index the articles in journals, magazines, and newspapers. Often, these\ndatabases include abstracts, or summaries, of the articles, and they may offer the full texts of the\narticles as well. Your library subscribes to many periodical databases and to services that offer\nmultiple databases. (See p. 586 for a list.) Most databases and services will be searchable through\nthe library’s Web site.\nNote The search engine Google is developing Google Scholar, an engine at\nscholar.google.com that seeks out scholarly articles. Al-though it could eventually prove a valuable\nresearch tool, at this point Google Scholar produces results that are far from complete and include\nmore from science and engineering than from the hu-manities and social sciences. Your library\nprobably subscribes to most of the periodicals searched by Google Scholar, so begin there.\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 17\nSelection of databases\nTo decide which databases to consult, you’ll need to consider what you’re looking for:\nHow broadly and deeply should you search? Periodical databases vary widely in what they\nindex. Some, such as ProQuest Research Library, cover many subjects but don’t index the full\nrange of periodicals in each subject. Others, such as Historical Abstracts, cover a single\nsubject but then include most of the available periodicals and other resources as well. If your\nsubject ranges across disciplines, as Edward Begay’s Internet subject does, then start with a\nbroad database. If your subject focuses on a particular discipline, then start with a narrower\ndatabase.\nWhich databases most likely include the kinds of resources you need? The Web sites of\nmost libraries allow you to narrow a database search to a particular kind of periodical (such as\nnewspapers or journals) or to a particular discipline. You can then discover each database’s\nfocus by checking the description of the database (sometimes labeled ―Help‖ or ―Guide‖) or\nthe list of indexed resources (sometimes labeled ―Publications‖ or ―Index‖). The description\nwill also tell you the time period the database covers, so you’ll know whether you also need to\nconsult older print indexes at the library.\nDatabase searches\nWhen you first search a database, use your own keywords to\nlocate sources. The general procedure is discussed on page 573. Your goal is to find at least one\nsource that seems just right for your subject, so that you can then see what subject headings the\ndatabase itself uses for such sources. Using one or more of those headings will focus and speed\nyour search.\nEdward Begay first searched EBSCOhost Academic Search Elite, a broad database covering\nnearly 3500 periodicals. His keywords Internet AND access returned more than 2000 articles, and\nnone\non the first page dealt with his subject. He then tried Internet AND access AND education, getting\nfewer returns but still nothing us-\nable on the first page. He stopped there to brainstorm other terms that might work better, some of\nthem synonyms and some narrower:\nInternet access education\nWorld Wide Web use schools\nWeb equality public schools\ncomputers poverty\ntechnology\nThe three screen shots here show Begay’s use of one combination of these terms, Internet\nAND equality AND education. The returns (screen 2) included one source clearly related to\nBegay’s subject. The full record for the source (screen 3) provided an abstract of the source along\nwith the database’s headings for the article. Begay had seen one of these headings, DIGITAL\ndivide, in his background research, but he had thought the term might be too current to produce\ngood results. Now he tried it on Academic Search Elite and found dozens of additional source\npossibilities. To broaden his search, he also tried some of the subject headings for these other\nsources, such as Educational technology and Technology—sociological aspects. When he thought\nhe had found a good range of possible sources, including government publications as well as\nscholarly and popular articles, he moved on to other databases.\nMany databases allow you to limit your search to so-called peer-reviewed or refereed\njournals—that is, scholarly journals whose articles have been reviewed before publication by\nexperts in the field and then revised by the author. (Begay’s initial search of EBSCOhost Academic\nSearch Elite included this limitation, as shown in screen 1.) Limiting your search to peer-reviewed\njournals can help you navigate huge databases that might otherwise return scores of unusable\narticles from other kinds of periodicals.\nNote As you follow leads in online indexes, it’s easy to lose track of what database you’re using.\nYou’ll need this information for any article you obtain online, so make sure you have it. If you\nprint search records or e-mail them to yourself, the database information will appear on the\nrecord along with other bibliographic information (see screen 3 above).\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 18\nThe use of abstracts\nThe full article record on the previous page shows a key feature of many databases’ periodical\nlistings: an abstract that summarizes the article. Describing research methods, conclusions, and\nother information, an abstract can tell you whether you want to pursue an article and thus save you\ntime. However, the abstract cannot replace the actual article. If you want to use the work as a\nsource, you must consult the full text, as described on the facing page.\nHelpful databases\nThe list below includes databases to which academic librar-\nies commonly subscribe. Some of these databases—for instance, ProQuest Research Library and\nEBSCOhost Academic Search—cover much the same material, so your library may subscribe to\nseveral of them but not all.\nNote More specific lists of indexes for academic disciplines\nappear later in this book: see pages 745 (literature), 762 (other humanities), 782–83 (social\nsciences), and 810 (natural and applied sciences).\nEBSCOhost Academic Search. A periodical index covering magazines and journals in the social sciences,\nsciences, arts, and humanities. Many articles are available full-text.\nFirstSearch. A Web interface to more than sixty databases, including WorldCat, Books in Print,\nDissertation Abstracts, Wilson Select, Article-First, Biography Index, Book Review Index, Education\nAbstracts, ERIC, Medline, PAIS, and many more.\nInfoTrac Expanded Academic. The Gale Group’s general periodical index covering the social sciences,\nsciences, arts, and humanities as well as national news periodicals. It includes full-text articles.\nJSTOR. Full-text articles from older volumes of several hundred journals in the sciences, social sciences,\narts, humanities, and business.\nLexisNexis Academic. An index of news and business, legal, and reference information, with full-text\narticles. LexisNexis includes international, national, and regional newspapers, newsmagazines, legal\nand business publications, and court cases.\nNineteenth-Century Masterfile. Perhaps the only electronic database for materials from the nineteenth\ncentury, including Poole’s Index to Periodical Literature (1802–1906), W. T. Stead’s Index to\nPeriodicals (1890–1902), and indexes to books and journals.\nProQuest Research Library. A periodical index covering the sciences, social sciences, arts, and\nhumanities, including many full-text articles.\nWilson Databases. A collection of indexes, often provided in a package, including Business Periodicals\nIndex, Education Index, General Science Index, Humanities Index, Readers’ Guide to Periodical\nLiterature, and Social Sciences Index.\nWhile using databases like those above, you may want to consult a citation index to see what\nhas been written about an article or book, as when one scholar comments on the work of another.\nThe three main citation indexes are Arts and Humanities Citation Index, Social Science Citation\nIndex, and Science Citation Index. All three are available on the database ISI Web of Science,\nwhich many libraries subscribe to.\n2 Locating the articles in periodicals\nMany article listings you find will include or link directly to the full text of the article, which\nyou’ll be able to read online and print or e-mail to yourself. If the full text is not available online,\nyou’ll need to consult the periodical itself. Usually the article listing will also say whether the\nperiodical is available in your library. If you don’t see this information, then consult the library’s\nlist of periodicals (often called serials) either in the main catalog or in a separate catalog.\nYour library probably holds recent issues of periodicals in the periodicals room. Back issues\nare usually stored elsewhere, in one of three forms:\nIn bound volumes\nOn microfilm, a filmstrip showing pages side by side\nOn microfiche, a sheet of film with pages arranged in rows and columns.\nConsulting periodicals stored on microfilm or microfiche requires using a special machine, or\n―reader,‖ with which you locate the page and project it on a screen. (Some readers are also attached\nto scanners or photocopiers.) Any member of the library’s staff will show you how to operate the\nreader.\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 19\nIf the periodical you seek is not available in your library, you may be able to obtain the article\nby interlibrary loan. The article may arrive online, by mail, or by fax, and there may be a fee for the\nservice. Even electronic loans can sometimes take a week or more, so place your order early.\nNote Many periodicals are available both online and in print, and most documentation styles\nrequire you to specify which version you consulted. For scholarly journals, the two versions are\nlikely to be identical in content though not in format: for in-stance, the online version may not\ninclude the print version’s page breaks and numbers. (However, a full-text version in PDF format\nreproduces the actual pages of the print journal. To open PDF files, you may need to download a\nprogram called Acrobat Reader.) For newspapers and magazines, the two versions often differ\nmore: for instance, the online version may be shorter or longer than the print version, may contain\nfewer illustrations, or may include links to other resources.\n43e Finding sources on the Web\nAs an academic researcher, you enter the World Wide Web in two ways: through your\nlibrary’s Web site, and through public search engines such as Yahoo! and Google. The library\nentrance, covered in the preceding sections, is your main path to the books and periodicals that, for\nmost subjects, should make up most of your sources. The public entrance, discussed here, can lead\nto a wealth of information and ideas, but it also has a number of disadvantages:\nThe Web is a wide-open network. Anyone with the right hardware and software can place\ninformation on the Internet, and even a carefully conceived search can turn up sources with\nwidely varying reliability: journal articles, government documents, scholarly data, term papers\nwritten by high school students, sales pitches masked as objective reports, wild theories. You\nmust be especially diligent about evaluating Internet sources (see pp. 602–09).\nThe Web changes constantly. No search engine can keep up with the Web’s daily additions\nand deletions, and a source you find today may be different or gone tomorrow. You should not\nput off consulting an online source that you think you may want to use. If it seems appropriate\nfor your needs, take notes from it or (if the source allows) download it to your own computer.\nBe sure to record complete source information at the same time (see p. 569 for a list of what to\nrecord).\nThe Web provides limited information on the past. Sources dat-ing from before the 1980s\nor even more recently probably will not appear on the Web.\nThe Web is not all-inclusive. Most books and many periodicals are available only via the\nlibrary, not directly via the Web.\nClearly, the Web warrants cautious use. It should not be the only resource you work with.\n1 Using a search engine\nTo find sources on the Web, you use a search engine that catalogs Web sites in a series of\ndirectories and conducts keyword searches. Generally, use a directory when you haven’t yet refined\nyour topic or you want a general overview. Use keywords when you have refined your topic and\nyou seek specific information.\nCurrent search engines\nDozens of search engines are available. The box opposite lists the currently most popular\nengines. (To reach any of them, enter its URL in the Address or Location field of your Web\nbrowser.)\nNote For a good range of reliable sources, try out more than a single search engine, perhaps as\nmany as four or five. No search en-gine can catalog the entire Web—indeed, even the most\npowerful engine may not include half the sites available at any given time, and most engines\ninclude only a fifth or less. In addition, most search engines accept paid placements, giving higher\nbilling to sites that pay a fee. These so-called sponsored links are usually marked as such, but they\ncan compromise a search engine’s method for ar-ranging sites in response to your keywords.\nA sample search engine\nThe screen shot on the following page from Google shows the features common to most\nsearch engines. Any search engine’s Advanced Search option allows you to customize your search\n(for instance, by selecting a date range, a language, or a number of results to see) and to limit or\nexpand your keywords (for instance, by using AND, NOT, and other operators). It may also tell you\nhow the search engine determines the order in which it presents results. (Criteria include the\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 20\nnumber of times your keywords appear on a site, whether the terms appear in the site’s title or\naddress, and, in Google’s case, which other sites link to the site.)\nSearch records\nYour Web browser includes functions that allow you to keep track of Web sources and your\nsearch:\nFavorites or Bookmarks save site addresses as links. Click one of these terms near the top of\nthe browser screen to add a site you want to return to. A favorite or bookmark remains on file\nuntil you delete it.\nHistory records the sites you visited over a certain period, such as a day, a week, or a\nmonth. (You can set the period from the browser’s Tools menu.) After the period elapses, the\nhistory is deleted. If you forgot to bookmark a site, click History or Go to recover the site from\nthe search history.\nNote If you do Web research on a public computer, such as in a lab at school, your favorites\nand history probably will not be saved from one day to the next. To track sources that you may\nwant to return to, copy the site URLs and e-mail them to yourself.\n2 Following a sample Web search\nFor his initial search of the Web, Edward Begay started with the keywords digital divide on\nGoogle. But the search returned more than 6.6 million items, as shown on screen 1 opposite.\nAlthough some sources on the first page looked promising, Begay realized he had to alter his\nstrategy to get more focused results. Following the same procedure he used with a periodical\ndatabase, Begay experimented with combinations of synonyms and narrower terms. The keywords\n―digital divide‖ ―public education‖ US did refine the search but still produced 31,000 results.\nFrom Google’s Advanced Search help, Begay learned that he could specify what he wanted to\nsee in the URLs of sources. Adding site:.gov limited the results to government publications, whose\nURLs end in gov. With ―digital divide‖ ―public education‖ US site:.gov, Begay received 547\nresults (see screen 2). Although the number was still large, the government origin combined with\nGoogle’s criteria for ranking sources gave Begay confidence that the first fifty or so would serve\nhis needs. He continued to limit the search by replacing site:.gov with site:.edu (educational\ninstitutions), site:.org (nonprofit organizations), and site:.com (commercial organizations).\nKnowing that no search engine catalogs every Web site, Begay also tried his successful\nkeywords at two other engines, AlltheWeb and Dogpile. The AlltheWeb search turned up an\nadditional promising site among the first thirty. The Dogpile search, which worked through\nmultiple search engines, returned more than a thousand items, including many irrelevant ones but\nalso (among the first thirty) one more possible source.\nBegay’s Web search illustrates the trial-and-error approach required to refine keywords so that\nthey locate worthwhile sources. Almost any successful Web search will require similar persistence\nand patience.\n43f Finding other online sources\nSeveral online sources can put you directly in touch with experts and others whose ideas and\ninformation may inform your research. Because these sources, like Web sites, are unfiltered, you\nmust always evaluate them carefully. (See pp. 607–09.)\n1 Using electronic mail\nAs a research tool, e-mail allows you to communicate with others who are interested in your\ntopic. You may, for instance, carry on an e-mail conversation with a teacher at your school or\ninterview an expert in another state to follow up on a scholarly article he or she published. (See pp.\n826–29 on using e-mail.)\n2 Using discussion lists\nA discussion list (sometimes called a listserv or just a list) uses e-mail to connect individuals\nwho are interested in a common subject, often with a scholarly or technical focus. By sending a\nquestion to an appropriate list, you may be able to reach scores of people who know something\nabout your topic. For an index of discussion lists, see tile.net/lists.\nWhen conducting research on a discussion list, follow the guide-lines for e-mail etiquette on\npages 828–29 as well as these:\nLurk for a while—reading without posting messages. Make sure the discussion is relevant to\nyour topic, and get a sense of how the group interacts.\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 21\nDon’t ask for information you can find elsewhere. Most list members are glad to help with\nlegitimate questions but resent messages that rehash familiar debates or that ask them to do\nsomeone else’s work.\nEvaluate messages carefully. Many list subscribers are passionate experts with fair-minded\napproaches to their topics, but almost anyone with an Internet connection can post a message\nto a list. See pages 607–09 on evaluating online sources.\n3 Using Web forums and newsgroups\nWeb forums and newsgroups are more open and less scholarly than discussion lists, so their\nmessages require even more diligent evaluation. Web forums allow participants to join a\nconversation simply by selecting a link on a Web page. For a directory of forums, see\ndelphiforums.com. Newsgroups are organized under subject headings such as soc for social issues\nand biz for business. For a directory of newsgroups, see groups.google.com.\n4 Using Web logs\nWeb logs, or blogs, are personal sites on which an author posts time-stamped comments,\ngenerally centering on a common theme, in a format that allows readers to respond to the author\nand to one another. You can find directories of blogs at bloglines.com and\nblogwise.com. See also pages 830–32 for a discussion and example of communicating via blogs.\nLike all other online media discussed in this section, Web logs consulted as potential sources\nmust be evaluated carefully. Some are reliable sources of opinion, news, or evolving scholarship,\nand many refer to worthy books, articles, Web sites, and other resources. But lots of blogs are little\nmore than outlets for their authors’ gripes and prejudices. See pages 607–09 for tips on telling the\ngood from the bad.\n5 Using synchronous communication\nSynchronous (or simultaneous) communication allows conversations in real time, the way\nwe talk on the phone. Synchronous programs include instant-messaging applications, Web\ncourseware, Internet relay chat (IRC), and virtual environments called MOOs.\nSynchronous communication can be used to conduct interviews or hold debates. Your\ninstructor may ask you to use it for your coursework or research and will provide the software and\ninstructions to get started. You can also find out more about synchronous communication at\ndu.org/cybercomp.html or internet101.org/ chat.html.\n43g Finding government publications\nGovernment publications provide a vast array of data compilations, reports, policy statements,\npublic records, and other historical and contemporary information. For US government\npublications, by far the most numerous, consult the Government Printing Office’s GPO Access at\ngpoaccess.gov/index.html. Many federal, state, and local government agencies post important\npublications—legislation, reports, press releases—on their own Web sites. You can find lists of\nsites for various federal agencies by using the keywords United States federal government with any\nsearch engine. Edward Begay took this approach to find statistics from the Department of\nCommerce on computer use by people of various incomes and races (see the screen shot below).\nBesides what’s available online, your library will have a large collection of printed\ngovernment publications only if it is a depository library (that is, designated to receive such\ndocuments). If yours is a depository library, ask a librarian to help you locate the documents you\nseek.\n43h Finding images\nPages 225–29 discuss the use of images to support an argument. To find images, you have a\nnumber of options. (The Web links in the following lists are available online at ablongman.com/\nlittlebrown.)\nScout for images while reading print or online sources. Your sources may include charts,\ngraphs, photographs, and other images that can support your ideas. When you find an image\nyou may want to use, photocopy or download it so you’ll have it available later.\nCreate your own images, such as photographs or charts. See pages 120–21 for suggestions\non graphics software programs.\nUse an image search engine. Google, Yahoo!, AlltheWeb, and some other search engines\nconduct specialized image searches. They can find scores of images, but the results may be\ninaccurate or incomplete because the sources surveyed often do not include descriptions of the\nimages. (The engines search file names and any text accompanying the images.)\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 22\nUse a public image database. The following sites generally conduct accurate searches\nbecause their images are filed with information such as a description of the image, the artist’s\nname, and the image’s date:\nAdflip (adflip.com). Historical and contemporary print advertisements.\nDuke University, Ad*Access (scriptorium.lib.duke.edu/adaccess). Print ad-vertisements spanning 1911–\n55.\nLibrary of Congress, American Memory (memory.loc.gov/ammem). Maps, photographs, and prints\ndocumenting the American experience.\nLibrary of Congress, Prints and Photographs Online Catalog (loc.gov/rr/ print/catalog.html). Images\nfrom the library’s collection, including those available through American Memory.\nNew York Public Library Digital Gallery (digitalgallery.nypl.org/nypldigital). Maps, drawings,\nphotographs, and paintings from the library’s collection.\nPolitical Cartoons (politicalcartoons.com). Cartoons on contemporary is-sues and events.\nUse a public image directory. The following sites collect links to image sources:\nArt Source (ilpi.com/artsource/general.html). Sources on art and architecture.\nARTstor (artstor.org). Museum collections and a database of images typ-ically used in art history courses.\nMuseum Computer Network (mcn.edu/resources/sitesonline.htm). Museum collections.\nWashington State University, Popular Culture. Resources for Critical Anal-ysis\n(wsu.edu/%7Eamerstu/pop/tvrguide.html). Sources on advertising, fashion, magazines, toys, and\nother artifacts of popular culture.\nYale University Arts Library, Image Resources (www.library.yale.edu/art/ imageresources.html).\nSources on the visual and performing arts.\nUse a subscription database. Your library may subscribe to the following resources:\nAssociated Press, AccuNet/AP Multimedia Archives. Historical and contemporary news images.\nGrove Art Online. Art images and links to museum sites.\nMany images you find will be available at no charge for copying or downloading, but some\nsources do charge a fee for use. Before paying for an image, check with a librarian to see if it is\navailable elsewhere for free.\nNote You must cite every image source fully in your paper, just as you cite text sources, with\nauthor, title, and publication information. In addition, some sources will require that you seek\npermission from the copyright holder, either the source itself or a third party such as a\nphotographer. Permission is especially likely to be required if you are submitting your paper on the\npublic Web. See pages 635–37 for more about online publication.\n43i Generating your own sources\nMany of the sources you consult for a research project—and most of the resources of the\nlibrary—are likely to be secondary sources whose authors draw their information from other\nauthors. However, academic writing will also require you to consult primary sources and to\nconduct primary research for information of your own. In many papers this primary research will be\nthe sole basis for your writing, as when you analyze a poem or report on an experiment you\nconducted. In other papers you will be expected to use your research to support, extend, or refute\nthe ideas of others.\nChapters 49–53 discuss the textual analyses, surveys, experiments, and other primary sources\nyou may use in writing for various academic disciplines. One primary source not covered there is\nthe personal interview with an expert in the topic you are researching. Because of the give-and-take\nof an interview, you can obtain answers to questions precisely geared to your topic, and you can\nfollow up on points of confusion and unexpected leads. In addition, quotations and paraphrases\nfrom an interview can give your paper immediacy and authority. Edward Begay used just such an\ninterview in his paper about the Internet (see p. 704).\nYou can conduct an interview in person, over the telephone, or online using electronic mail or\na form of synchronous communication. A personal interview is preferable if you can arrange it\nbecause you can see the person’s expressions and gestures as well as hear his or her tone and\nwords. But telephone and online interviews allow you to consult someone who resists a personal\ninterview or who lives far away from you, while still retaining most advantages of interaction.\nA few precautions will help you get the maximum information from an interview with the\nminimum disruption to the person you are interviewing:\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 23\nChoose your interviewee carefully. If you do not already know whom to consult for an\ninterview, ask a teacher in the field or do some telephone or library research. Likely sources,\ndepending on your topic, are those who have written about your topic or something closely\nrelated, officials in government, businesspeople, even a relative, if he or she is an expert in\nyour topic because of experience, scholarship, or both.\nCall or write for an appointment. Tell the person exactly why you are calling, what you\nwant to discuss, and how long you expect the interview to take. Be true to your word on all\npoints.\nPrepare a list of open-ended questions to ask—perhaps ten or twelve for a one-hour\ninterview. Plan on doing some research for these questions to discover background on the\nissues and your subject’s published views on the issues.\nGive your subject time to consider your questions. Don’t rush into silences with more\nquestions.\nPay attention to your subject’s answers so that you can ask appropriate follow-up questions\nand pick up on unexpected but worthwhile points.\nTake care in interpreting answers, especially if you are online and thus can’t depend on\nfacial expressions, gestures, and tone of voice to convey the subject’s attitudes. Ask for\nclarification when you need it.\nKeep thorough notes. Take notes during an in-person or telephone interview, or tape-record\nthe interview, if you have the equipment and your subject agrees. For online interviews, save\nthe discussion in a file of its own.\nVerify quotations. Before you quote your subject in your paper, check with him or her to\nensure that the quotations are accurate.\nSend a thank-you note immediately after the interview. Promise your subject a copy of\nyour finished paper, and send the paper promptly.\nEXERCISE 43.1 Using the library\nTo become familiar with the research sources available through your\nlibrary, visit both the library and its Web site for answers to the following questions. Ask a librarian for\nhelp whenever necessary. (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\n1. Which resources does the library include on its Web site? Which resources require a visit to the\nlibrary?\n2. Where are reference books stored in the library? How are they cataloged and arranged? Which ones\nare available through the Web site or on CD-ROM? Where and in what format(s) are (a) Contem-\nporary Authors, (b) Encyclopaedia Britannica, and (c) MLA International Bibliography of Books\nand Articles on the Modern Languages and Literatures?\n3. Where is the catalog of the library’s periodicals? Where and in what format(s) does the library have\ncurrent and back issues of the following periodicals: (a) the New York Times, (b) Harper’s magazine,\nand (c) Journal of Social Psychology?\n4. What tools does the library’s Web site offer for finding periodical databases that are appropriate for a\nparticular research subject?\n5. Research the focus and indexed publications of two periodical data-bases, such as InfoTrac, JSTOR,\nLexisNexis, PAIS, or ProQuest Research Library. What disciplines does each database seem most\nsuited for?\n6. Does the book catalog cover all of the library’s book holdings? If not, which books are not included,\nand where are they cataloged?\n7. What are the library call numbers of the following books: (a) The Power Broker, by Robert Caro; (b)\nHeart of Darkness, by Joseph Conrad; and (c) The Hero with a Thousand Faces, by Joseph\nCampbell?\nEXERCISE 43.2 Finding library sources\nLocate at least six promising articles and books for the subject you began working on in the previous\nchapter (Exercise 42.1, p. 563, and Exercise 42.2, p. 567). Consider the sources ―promising‖ if they seem\ndirectly to address your central research question. Following the guide- lines on pages 567–70, make an\nannotated working bibliography of the sources. Be sure to include all the information you will need to\nacknowledge the sources in your final paper.\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 24\nEXERCISE 43.3 Finding Web sources\nUse at least two Web search engines to locate six or seven promising sources for your research project.\nBegin by developing a list of keywords that can be used to query one of the search engines (see pp. 574–\n75). Check the Advanced Search option of the engine if you need to narrow your search. Then try your\nrefined keywords on the other search engine as well. How do the results differ? What keyword strategies\nworked best for finding relevant information? Add promising sources to your annotated working\nbibliography.\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 25\nVisit the companion Web site for more help and additional exercises on\nfinding sources, including searching electronically.\nA tip for researchers\nTake advantage of two valuable resources offered by your library:\nAn orientation, which introduces the library’s resources and explains how to reach and use the Web\nsite and the print holdings.\nReference librarians, whose job it is to help you and others navigate the library’s resources. Even\nvery experienced researchers often consult reference librarians.\nWays to refine keywords\nMost databases and many search engines work with Boolean operators, terms or symbols that allow you\nto expand or limit your keywords and thus your search.\nUse AND or + to narrow the search by including only sources that use all the given words. The\nkeywords Internet AND access request only the sources in the shaded area:\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 26\nUse NOT or – (“minus”) to narrow the search by excluding irrelevant words. Internet AND access\nNOT provider excludes sources that use the word provider:\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 27\nUse OR to broaden the search by giving alternate keywords. Inter-net AND access OR use allows\nfor sources that use a synonym for\naccess:\nUse parentheses or quotation marks to form search phrases. For\ninstance, (Internet access) requests the exact phrase, not the separate words. Only sources using\nInternet access would turn up.\nUse NEAR to narrow the search by requiring the keywords to be close to each other—for instance,\nInternet NEAR education. Depending on the resource you’re using, the words could be directly next\nto each other or many words apart. Some resources use WITHIN so that you can specify the exact\nnumber of words apart—for instance, Internet WITHIN 10 education.\nUse wild cards to permit different versions of the same word. In child*, for instance, the wild card\n* indicates that sources may include child, children, childcare, childhood, childish, childlike, and\nchildproof. The example suggests that you have to consider all the variations allowed by a wild card\nand whether it opens up your search too much. If you seek only two or three from many variations,\nyou may be better off using OR: child OR children. (Note that some systems use ?, :, or + for a wild\ncard instead of *.)\nBe sure to spell your keywords correctly. Some search tools will look for close matches or\napproximations, but correct spelling gives you the best chance of finding relevant sources.\nGuide to research sources\nReference works: helpful for summaries of topics and information for further research\nGeneral encyclopedias 577\nSpecialized encyclopedias, dictionaries, bibliographies 577\nUnabridged dictionaries and special dictionaries on language 578\nBiographical reference works 578\nAtlases and gazetteers 578\nAlmanacs and yearbooks 579\nGeneral books: literary works, nonfiction surveys, in-depth studies, and other materials, available for\ncirculation\nThe library catalog 579\nSearch strategy 579\nReferences to books 581\nPeriodicals: magazines, journals, and newspapers, containing detailed and current information\nPeriodical databases 582\nAbstracts 586\nHelpful databases 586\nLocating articles 587\nThe Web: a network of computers providing access to libraries, publications, organizations,\ngovernments, and individuals\nSearch engines 588\nSample search 589\nOther online sources:\nElectronic mail 592\nDiscussion lists 592\nWeb forums and newsgroups 593\nWeb logs 593\nSynchronous communication 593\nGovernment publications: practical advice, raw data, reports, and other information 594\nImages: photographs, paintings, graphs, and other illustrations 594\nYour own sources: interviews, surveys, and other primary sources you create 596\n1.
