Full article record with abstract\nArticle title\nAuthor\nJournal and publication information\nDatabase subject headings\nAbstract\nDatabase\nWeb search engines\nThe features of search engines change often, and new ones appear constantly. For the latest on search\nengines, see the links collected by Search Engine Watch at searchenginewatch.com/links.\nDirectories that review sites\nBUBL Link (bubl.ac.uk)\nInternet Public Library (ipl.org/div/subject)\nInternet Scout Project (scout.wisc.edu/archives)\nLibrarians’ Index to the Internet (lii.org)\nThe most advanced and efficient engines\nAlltheWeb (alltheweb.com)\nOne of the fastest and most comprehensive engines, AlltheWeb updates its database frequently so that it\nreturns more of the Web’s most recent sites. It allows searches for news, pictures, and audio and video\nfiles.\nGoogle (google.com)\nAlso fast and comprehensive, Google ranks a site based not only on its content but also on the other sites\nthat are linked to it, thus providing a measure of a site’s usefulness. Google also allows searches for news,\ndiscussion groups, and images.\nOther engines\nAltaVista (altavista.com)\nAsk Jeeves (ask.com)\nDogpile (dogpile.com)\nExcite (excite.com)\nLycos (lycos.com)\nMetaCrawler (metacrawler.com)\nYahoo! (yahoo.com)\nGoogle home page\nSaved sites\nHistory: record\nof sites visited\nSpecific collections to search\nSearch help\nKeywords\n1. First Google search results\n\nFOWLcp42-43.doc - 29\nKeywords used for search\nNumber of results\n2. Google results with refined keywords\nKeywords used for search\nNumber of results\nChart from a government publication\nGovernment Web sites can be sources for data, policy, consumer advice, and other\ninformation.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 1\n44\nCHAPTER\nWorking with Sources\nThe previous chapters helped you lay the groundwork for a research project. This chapter\ntakes you into the most personal, most intensive, and most rewarding part of research writing: using\nthe sources you‘ve found to extend and support your own ideas, to make your subject your own. As\nbefore, the work of Edward Begay (on access to the Internet) and Vanessa Haley (on the work of\nAnnie Dillard) will illustrate the activity and thought that go into research writing.\nMaking a subject your own requires thinking critically about sources and developing\nindependent ideas. These goals may at first be uncomfortable if your native culture emphasizes\nunderstanding and respecting established authority more than questioning and enlarging it. This\nchapter offers guidance in working with sources so that you can become an expert in your own\nright and convincingly convey your expertise to others.\nEvaluating sources\nGaining an overview\nOnce you have a satisfactory working bibliography, you want to get a sense of your sources‘\nusefulness and value.\nLook first at sources that seem most likely to define your subject. Edward Begay initially\nconsulted a government report on computer use by different segments of society. Vanessa\nHaley turned first to a book by Annie Dillard, Haley‘s subject. (For a paper like Haley‘s that\nanalyzes a writer‘s work, that work is always the starting point.)\nScan sources to gauge the kind and extent of their ideas and information. Don‘t get\nbogged down collecting information at this point. Instead, ensure that your sources are\nappropriately detailed and cover the full range of your subject—that together they promise to\nhelp you answer your research question.\nUpdate your annotated bibliography with assessments of sources. As you take the measure\nof sources, add your evaluations to your working bibliography. The writing will help you\ndifferentiate sources and start you on building connections among them. In each annotation,\nsummarize the purpose, main argument, and any apparent bias of the source. Also note any\nquestions you have about it. Here is an example from Edward Begay‘s evolving bibliography,\nadding to the entry on page 570.\nJudging relevance and reliability\nNot all the sources you find will prove worthwhile: some may be irrelevant to your subject,\nand others may be unreliable. Gauging the relevance and reliability of sources is the essential task\nof evaluating them. If you haven‘t already done so, read this book‘s Chapters 8 and 9 on critical\nreading. They provide a foundation for answering the questions in the box opposite.\nNote In evaluating sources, you need to consider how they came to you. The sources you find\nthrough the library, both print and online, have been previewed for you by their publishers and by\nthe library‘s staff. They still require your critical reading, but you can have some confidence in the\ninformation they contain. With online sources you reach directly, however, you cannot assume\nsimilar previewing, so your critical reading must be especially rigorous. Special tips for evaluating\nWeb sites and other online sources appear on the following pages.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 2\nEvaluating a Web site\nTo a great extent, the same critical reading that helps you evaluate library sources will help\nyou evaluate Web sites. But most Web sites have not undergone prior screening by editors and\nlibrarians. On your own, you must distinguish scholarship from corporate promotion, valid data\nfrom invented statistics, well-founded opinion from clever propaganda.\nThe following strategy, summarized in the box below, can help you make such distinctions.\nWe‘ll apply the strategy to the Web site shown on the next several pages, Global Warming\nInformation Center, which turned up in a search for views and data on global warming.\nNote To evaluate a Web document, you‘ll often need to travel to the site‘s home page to\ndiscover the author or sponsoring organization, date of publication, and other relevant information.\nThe page you‘re reading may include a link to the home page. If it doesn‘t, you can find it by\nediting the URL in the Address or Location field of your browser. Working backward, delete the\nend of the URL up to the last slash and hit Enter. Repeat this step until you reach the home page.\nThere you may also find a menu option, often labeled ―About,‖ that will lead you to a description\nof the site‘s author or sponsor.\nDetermine the type of site.\nWhen you search the Web, you‘re likely to encounter various types of sites. Although they\noverlap—a primarily informational site may include scholarship as well—the types can usually be\nidentified by their content and purposes. Here are the main types:\nSites focusing on scholarship: These sites have a knowledge-building interest and include\nresearch reports with supporting data and extensive documentation of scholarly sources. The\nURLs of the sites generally end in edu (originating from an educational institution), org (a\nnonprofit organization), or gov (a government department or agency). Such sites are more\nlikely to be reliable than the others described below.\nSites with an informational purpose: Individuals, nonprofit organizations, schools,\ncorporations, and government bodies all produce sites intended to centralize information on\nsubjects as diverse as astronomy, hip-hop music, computer architecture, and zoo design. The\nsites‘ URLs may end in edu, org, gov, or com (originating from a commercial organization).\nSuch sites generally do not have the knowledge-building focus of scholarly sites and may omit\nsupporting data and documentation, but they can provide useful information and often include\nlinks to scholarly and other sources.\nSites focusing on advocacy: Many sites present the views of individuals or organizations that\nadvocate certain policies or actions. Their URLs usually end in org, but they may end in edu\nor com. Some advocacy sites include serious, well-documented research to support their\npositions, but others select or distort evidence.\nSites with a commercial purpose: Corporations and other busi-nesses maintain Web sites to\nexplain themselves, promote them-selves, or sell goods and services. The URLs of commercial\nsites end in com. Although the information on such a site furthers the sponsor‘s profit-making\npurpose, it can include reliable data.\nPersonal sites: The sites maintained by individuals range from diaries of a family‘s travels to\nopinions on political issues to reports on evolving scholarship. The sites‘ URLs usually end in\ncom or edu. Personal sites are only as reliable as their authors, but some do provide valuable\neyewitness accounts, links to worthy sources, and other usable information. A particular kind\nof personal site, the Web log, is discussed on pages 607–09.\nThe following home page of the Global Warming Information Center\ngives some information that can be used to tell what type of site it is:\nIdentify the author or sponsor.\nA reputable site will list the author or group responsible for the site and will provide\ninformation or a link for contacting the author or group. If none of this information is provided,\nyou should not use the source. If you have only the author or group name, you may be able to\ndiscover more in a biographical dictionary, through a keyword search, or in your other sources.\nAs screen 1 shows, the Global Warming site names its sponsor right up front: the John P.\nMcGovern M.D. Center for Environmental and Regulatory Affairs. The bottom of this home page\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 3\ngives links to information about the McGovern Center and its parent organization, the National\nCenter for Public Policy Research. Their names imply that both groups are involved in research, so\nthe site does indeed seem to be informational or possibly even scholarly.\nGauge purpose.\nA Web site‘s purpose determines what ideas and information it offers. Inferring that purpose\ntells you how to interpret what you see on the site. If a site is intended to sell a product or an\nopinion, it will likely emphasize favorable ideas and information while ignoring or even distorting\nwhat is unfavorable. In contrast, if a site is intended to build knowledge—for instance, a scholarly\nproject or journal—it will likely acknowledge diverse views and evidence.\nDetermining the purpose of a site often requires looking beyond the first page and beneath the\nsurface of words and images. The elements of the Global Warming site—the title, the green color,\nthe photo of a child carrying a globe through a field of grass—suggest an environmentalist purpose\nof informing readers about the theory and consequences of rising earth temperatures caused by\npollution. The site‘s purpose is actually different, though. The home- page links lead to statements\nabout the aims of the McGovern Center and its parent, the National Center. Screen 2 shows the\nMcGovern Center statement.\nThe National Center‘s purpose statement expands the McGovern Center‘s:\nThe National Center for Public Policy Research is a communications and research foundation supportive\nof a strong national defense and dedicated to providing free market solutions to today‘s public policy\nproblems. We believe that the principles of a free market, individual liberty and personal responsibility\nprovide the greatest hope for meeting the challenges facing America in the 21st century.\nThese two statements imply that the purpose of the McGovern Center‘s Global Warming site is to\ninform readers about the evidence against global warming in the interest of reducing or overturning\nenvironmental regulations.\nConsider context.\nYour evaluation of a Web site should be informed by considerations outside the site itself.\nChief among these is your own knowledge: What do you already know about the site‘s subject and\nthe prevailing views of it? Where does this site seem to fit into that picture? What can you learn\nfrom this site that you don‘t already know?\nIn addition, you can follow some of the site‘s links to see how they support, or don‘t support,\nthe site‘s credibility. For instance, links to scholarly sources lend authority to a site—but only if the\nscholarly sources actually relate to and back up the site‘s claims.\nThe Global Warming site has a clear anti-regulatory bias, but this view is a significant one in\nthe debates over global warming, that is, the bias does not necessarily disqualify the site as a source\non global warming. The question is how reliable its information is: does it come from trustworthy,\nless biased sources? All the site‘s links lead to publications of the McGovern Center or the\nNational Center, so the question can be answered only by looking more deeply at these\npublications.\nLook at presentation.\nConsidering both the look of a site and the way it‘s written can illuminate its intentions and\nreliability. Are the site‘s elements all functional and well integrated, or is the site cluttered with\nirrelevant material and graphics? Does the site seem carefully constructed and well maintained, or\nis it sloppy and outdated? Does the design reflect the apparent purpose of the site, or does it\nundercut or conceal that purpose in some way? Is the text clearly written, or is it difficult to\nunderstand? Is it error-free, or does it contain typos and grammatical errors?\nAt first glance, as noted earlier, the Global Warming site casts a pro-environmentalist image\nthat turns out not to coincide with its purpose. Otherwise, the site is cleanly designed, with minimal\nelements laid out clearly. The text on other pages is straightfor- ward and readable. Together,\ndesign and readability indicate that the sponsor takes its purpose seriously and has thought out its\npresentation.\nAnalyze content.\nWith information about a site‘s author, purpose, and context, you‘re in a position to evaluate\nits content. Are the ideas and information slanted and, if so, in what direction? Are the views and\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 4\ndata authoritative, or do you need to balance them—or even reject them? These questions require\nclose reading of both the text and its sources.\nThe Global Warming site links to a wealth of reports and prominently features ―Questions and\nAnswers on Global Warming.‖ The screen shots opposite show two of the items from this page and\nthe footnotes citing sources for the answers. The source mix in the footnotes is similar in the other\npublications found through the Global Warming site. Scholars do disagree over whether the earth‘s\ntemperatures are rising significantly, whether human-made pollution is an important cause, how\nserious the consequences may be, and how to solve the problem. However, the Global Warming\nsite does not offer or refer to the scholarly research. As a result, the claims and evidence must be\nviewed suspiciously and probably rejected for use in a research paper. A usable source need not be\nless biased, but it must be more substantial.\nEvaluating other online sources\nWeb logs and the postings to online discussions require the same critical scrutiny as Web sites\ndo. Web logs can be sources of in-depth information and informed opinion, but they can also be\nvirtually useless. Web forums and newsgroups are similarly suspect. Even if a reliable blog or\ndiscussion-group message provides very current information or eyewitness testimony, it will not\nhave the authority of a scholarly publication. An e-mail discussion list may be more trustworthy if\nits subscribers are professionals in the field, but you will still find wrong or misleading data and\nskewed opinions.\nUse the following strategy for evaluating blogs and messages in online discussions.\nIdentify the author.\nChecking out the author of a blog or online posting can help you judge the reliability of the\nmessage. If the author uses a screen name, write directly to him or her requesting full name and\ncredentials. Do not use the message as a source if the author fails to respond. Once you know an\nauthor‘s name, you may be able to obtain background information from a keyword search of the\nWeb or a biographical dictionary.\nYou can also get a sense of the interests and biases of an author by tracking down his or her\nother writing. For a blog, check whether the author cites or links to other publications. For a\nnewsgroup message, look for a feature that allows you to find other messages by the author of any\nposting. For a discussion list or Web forum, use the group‘s archive to locate other messages by a\nparticular author.\nAnalyze the author’s purpose.\nAs with Web sites, you can use cues in the author‘s writing to figure out why he or she is\nwriting and thus how to position the blog or message among your other sources. The claims, use\n(or not) of evidence, and treatment of opposing views all convey the author‘s stand on the subject\nand general fairness.\nConsider the context.\nWeb logs and discussion-group postings are often difficult to evaluate in isolation. Looking\noutside a particular contribution to the responses of others will give you a sense of how the\nauthor‘s view is regarded. On a blog, look at the comments others have posted. Do the same with\ndiscussion-group messages, going back to the initial posting in the discussion thread and reading\nforward.\nAnalyze content.\nA reliable source will offer evidence for claims and sources for evidence. If you don‘t see such\nsupporting information, ask the author for it. (If he or she fails to respond, don‘t use the source.)\nThen verify the sources with your own research: are they reputable?\nThe tone of the writing can also be a clue to its purpose and reliability. Blogs and online\ndiscussions tend to be more informal and often more heated than other kinds of dialog, but look\naskance at writing that‘s contemptuous, dismissive, or shrill.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 5\nCompare with other sources.\nAlways consider blogs and discussion-group messages in comparison to other sources so that\nyou can distinguish singular, untested views from more mainstream views that have been subject to\nverification. Don‘t assume that a blog author‘s information and opinions are mainstream just\nbecause you see them on other blogs. The technology allows content to be picked up instantly on\nother blogs, so widespread distribution indicates only popularity, not reliability.\nBe wary of blogs or messages that reproduce periodical articles, reports, or other publications.\nTry to locate the original version of the publication to be sure it has been reproduced fully and\naccurately, not quoted selectively or distorted. If you can‘t locate the original version, then don‘t\nuse the publication as a source.\nEXERCISE 44.1 Evaluating a source\nImagine that you are researching a paper on the advertising techniques that are designed to persuade\nconsumers to buy products. You have listed the following book in your working bibliography:\nVance Packard, The Hidden Persuaders, revised edition, 1981.\nOn your own or with your classmates (as your instructor wishes), obtain this book from a library and\nevaluate it as a source for your paper. Use the guidelines on page 601. (You can do this exercise on-line at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nEXERCISE 44.2 Evaluating Web sites\nFind and evaluate three Web sites: a commercial site, such as Microsoft‘s or Apple‘s; a site for a\nnonprofit organization, such as the American Medical Association or Greenpeace; and the personal site of\nan individual. What do you know or can you infer about each site‘s sponsor or author? What seems to be\nthe site‘s purpose or purposes? What do the site‘s links contribute? How effective is the site‘s design?\nHow reliable do you judge the site‘s information to be? How do the three types of sites differ in these\nrespects?\nEXERCISE 44.3 Evaluating a Web log\nVisit bloglines.com or blogwise.com to find a Web log on a controversial subject such as stem-cell\nresearch or online sharing of music files. Who is responsible for the blog? What can you tell about its\npurpose? How reliable do you judge its ideas and information to be?\nEXERCISE 44.4 Evaluating an online discussion\nUsing groups.google.com, locate a newsgroup on a subject that interests you. (If you already participate\nin an online discussion group, you can use it instead.) Pick one series of at least ten related messages on a\nsingle topic. Write a brief summary of each message (see pp. 140–42 on summarizing). Then analyze and\nsynthesize the messages to develop a one- or two-paragraph evaluation of the discussion. Which messages\nseem reliable? Which don‘t? Why?\nSynthesizing sources\nWhen you begin to locate the differences and similarities among sources, you move into the\nmost significant part of research writing: forging relationships for your own purpose. This\nsynthesis, an essential step in critical reading (pp. 161–62), continues through the drafting and\nrevision of a research paper. As you infer connections—say, between one writer‘s ideas and\nanother‘s or between two works by the same author—you create new knowledge.\nAll kinds of connections may occur to you as you work with sources. Edward Begay,\nresearching the potential accessibility of the Internet to both the affluent and the poor, found data in\none source to support another source‘s assertions about a technological gap between private and\npublic schools. He also uncovered a central disagreement among sources over whether the Internet\nwould prove a boon or an obstruction to education. Vanessa Haley, writing about Annie Dillard,\nsought and found similarities in Dillard‘s ideas about the place of humanity in nature, ideas\nexpressed in varying contexts throughout Dillard‘s best-known book. Haley also discovered that\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 6\nher view of Dillard was partly supported by some of the critics she consulted but not supported by\nothers. She knew she would have to take account of these divergent views in her paper.\nYour synthesis of sources will grow more detailed and sophisticated as you proceed through\nthe research-writing process. Unless, like Vanessa Haley, you are analyzing primary sources such\nas the works of a writer, at first read your sources quickly and selectively to obtain an overview of\nyour subject and a sense of how the sources approach it. Instead of taking detailed notes about what\nsources say, record your own ideas about the sources in your research journal (p. 559) or your\nannotated bibliography (p. 600).\nRespond to sources.\nWrite down what your sources make you think. Do you agree or disagree\nwith the author? Do you find his or her views narrow, or do they open up\nnew approaches for you? Is there anything in the source that you need to\nresearch further before you can understand it? Does the source prompt\nquestions that you should keep in mind while reading other sources?\nConnect sources.\nWhen you notice a link between sources, jot it down. Do two sources differ in their theories or\ntheir interpretations of facts? Does one source illuminate another—perhaps commenting or\nclarifying or supplying additional data? Do two or more sources report studies that support a theory\nyou‘ve read about or an idea of your own?\nHeed your own insights.\nApart from ideas prompted by your sources, you are sure to come up with independent\nthoughts: a conviction, a point of confusion that suddenly becomes clear, a question you haven‘t\nseen anyone else ask. These insights may occur at unexpected times, so it‘s good practice to keep a\nnotebook or computer handy to record them.\nUse sources to support your own ideas.\nAs your research proceeds, the responses, connections, and insights you form through\nsynthesis will lead you to answer your starting research question with a statement of your thesis\n(see p. 639). They will also lead you to the main ideas supporting your thesis—conclusions you\nhave drawn from your synthesis of sources, forming the main divisions of your paper. When\ndrafting the paper, make sure each paragraph focuses on an idea of your own, with the support for\nthe idea coming from your sources. In this way, your paper will synthesize others‘ work into\nsomething wholly your own.\nEXERCISE 44.5 Synthesizing sources\nThe following three passages address the same issue, the legalization of drugs. What similarities do you\nsee in the authors‘ ideas? What differences? Write a paragraph of your own in which you use these\nauthors‘ views as a point of departure for your own view about drug legalization. (You can do this\nexercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nPerhaps the most unfortunate victims of drug prohibition laws have been the residents of America‘s\nghettos. These laws have proved largely futile in deterring ghetto-dwellers from becoming drug abusers,\nbut they do account for much of what ghetto residents identify as the drug problem. Aggressive, gun-\ntoting drug dealers often upset law-abiding residents far more than do addicts nodding out in doorways.\nMeanwhile other residents perceive the drug dealers as heroes and successful role models. They‘re\nsymbols of success to children who see no other options. At the same time the increasingly harsh criminal\npenalties imposed on adult drug dealers have led drug traffickers to recruit juveniles. Where once children\nstarted dealing drugs only after they had been using them for a few years, today the sequence is often\nreversed. Many children start using drugs only after working for older drug dealers for a while.\nLegalization of drugs, like legalization of alcohol in the early 1930s, would drive the drug-dealing\nbusiness off the streets and out of apartment buildings and into government-regulated, tax-paying stores.\nIt also would force many of the gun-toting dealers out of the business and convert others into legitimate\nbusinessmen.\n—Ethan A. Nadelmann, ―Shooting Up‖\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 7\nStatistics argue against legalization. The University of Michigan conducts an annual survey of\ntwelfth graders, asking the students about their drug consumption. In 1980, 56.4 percent of those polled\nsaid they had used marijuana in the past twelve months, whereas in 2004 only 45.7 percent had done so.\nCocaine use was also reduced in the same period (22.6 percent to 15.4 percent). At the same time, twelve-\nmonth use of legally available drugs—alcohol and nicotine-containing cigarettes—remained fairly steady\naround 75 percent and 55 percent, respectively. The numbers of illegal drug users haven‘t declined nearly\nenough: those teenaged marijuana and cocaine users are still vulnerable to addiction and even death, and\nthey threaten to infect their impressionable peers. But clearly the prohibition of illegal drugs has helped,\nwhile the legal status of alcohol and cigarettes has not made them less popular.\n—Sylvia Runkle, ―The Case Against Legalization‖\nI have to laugh at the debate over what to do about the drug problem. Everyone is running around\noffering solutions—from making drug use a more serious criminal offense to legalizing it. But there isn‘t\na real solution. I know that. I used and abused drugs, and people, and society, for two decades. Nothing\nworked to get me to stop all that behavior except just plain being sick and tired. Nothing. Not threats, not\nten-plus years in prison, not anything that was said to me. I used until I got through. Period. And that‘s\nwhen you‘ll win the war. When all the dope fiends are done. Not a minute before.\n—Michael W. Posey, ―I Did Drugs Until\nThey Wore Me Out. Then I Stopped.‖\nEXERCISE 44.6 Evaluating and synthesizing sources\nLook up the sources in the working bibliography you made in Exercises 43.2 and 43.3 (p. 598). Evaluate\nthe sources for their relevance and reliability. If the sources seem unreliable or don‘t seem to give you\nwhat you need, expand your working bibliography and evaluate the new sources. In your research journal\nor annotated bibliography, write down your responses to sources, the connections you perceive among\nsources, and other original ideas that occur to you.\nMining and interacting with sources\nWhen you have decided which sources to pursue, you may be ready to gather information, or\nyou may want to step back and get your bearings. Your choice will depend mainly on how familiar\nyou are with the main issues of your subject and whether you have formed a central idea about it.\nWhen you know what you’re looking for, proceed with information gathering. If you‘ve\nformed a good idea of your thesis and a sense of the subjects you will cover, you‘ll have a\nframework in which to use sources.\nWhen you’re uncertain of your direction, stop to draft a thesis statement and create an\noutline. These steps (discussed on pp. 639–43) can help when you‘re attracted to several\ndifferent\ntheses or when you don‘t see how the various areas of your subject relate.\nThe following sections discuss a way of reading sources and methods of gathering and\norganizing information from sources.\nSkimming and then reading\nThe most efficient method of reading secondary sources during research is skimming, reading\nquickly to look for pertinent information. (Primary sources usually need to be read more carefully,\nespecially when they are the focus of your paper.) Follow these guide-lines for skimming:\nRead with a specific question in mind, not randomly in hopes of hitting something\nworthwhile.\nConsult reference aids—tables of contents, menus, indexes, or headings—to find what you\nwant.\nConcentrate on headings and main ideas, skipping material unrelated to the specific\nquestion you are researching.\nWhen you find something relevant, read slowly and carefully to achieve a clear understanding\nof what the author is saying and to interpret and evaluate the material in the context of your own\nand others‘ opinions.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 8\nGathering and organizing information\nBefore you begin mining your sources, decide on categories into which your subject can be\ndivided. (If you have previously outlined your preliminary ideas, use outline headings for these\ncategories.) Edward Begay, for instance, divided his general subject of Internet access into these\ncategories:\nHistory of the Internet\nTraditional vs. innovative models of education\nDifferences between rich and poor schools\nTraining of Internet users\nCosts of hooking up to the Internet\nInternet and economic inequality\nRole of librarians and teachers in Internet use\nRole of businesses in Internet use\nRole of government in Internet use\nHeadings for your categories will go at the top of each note to cue you about its content.\nResearchers vary in their methods for working with sources, but all methods share the same\ngoals:\nKeep accurate records of what sources say. Accuracy helps prevent misrepresentation and\nplagiarism.\nKeep accurate records of how to find sources. These records are essential for retracing steps\nand for citing sources in the final paper. (See pp. 567–70 on recording information in a\nworking bibliography.)\nInteract with sources. Information gathering is not mechanical but critical, leading to an\nunderstanding of sources, the relationships among them, and their support for one‘s own\nideas.\nYou can accomplish these goals by taking handwritten notes, typing notes into a computer,\nannotating photocopies or printouts of sources, or annotating downloaded documents. On any\ngiven project, you may use all the methods. Each has advantages and disadvantages.\nNote Whichever method you use, take two steps with every note, photocopy, printout, or\ndownloaded document:\nKey the material to your outline so that you know where it belongs.\nRecord the source’s complete publication information, or use the author‘s name as a\nshorthand reference to a list where you keep complete publication information.\nHandwritten notes\nThe traditional method of writing notes on 49 ¥ 69 cards will prove useful when you come\nacross a source with no computer or photocopier handy or when you need to record something\nbrief. Handwritten notes can be risky, though. It‘s easy to introduce errors as you work from source\nto note card. And it‘s possible to copy source language and then later mistake and use it as your\nown, thus plagiarizing the source. Always take care to make accurate notes and to place big\nquotation marks around any passage you quote. Some researchers reduce the risk of plagiarism by\nsummarizing\nor paraphrasing sources on notes instead of quoting them, as described on pages 617–20. Other\nresearchers delay this step until drafting because it increases the risk of error.\nNotes on computer\nTaking notes on a computer can streamline the path of source to note to paper because you can\nimport the notes into your draft as you write. Using word-processor files, you can label and sort\nnotes much as you would on note cards. (The examples on pages 618–22 illustrate one way of\nlabeling computer notes.)\nComputer notes have the same disadvantages as handwritten notes: the risk of introducing\nerrors and the risk of plagiarizing.\nAs with handwritten notes, strive for accuracy, and use quotation marks for quotations.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 9\nPhotocopies and printouts\nPhotocopying from print sources or printing out online sources each has distinct advantages\nfor researchers:\nBoth methods are convenient, particularly when sources must remain in the library (for\ninstance, bound periodicals and reference works) or when you find sources online (for\ninstance, full-text articles or trustworthy Web pages).\nBoth methods may be safer than handwritten or computer notes. Many researchers and\nteachers believe that photocopying or printing out sources reduces the risks of error and\nplagiarism. Each method requires manipulating source material only once, not twice, to use it\nin your paper. And with each method you see exactly what‘s quoted as you work the source\nmaterial into your draft.\nBut photocopies and printouts have disadvantages, too:\nThey can discourage interaction with sources by substituting busywork for thinking. You\nmust read photocopies and print-outs as critically as you would any other sources. Highlight\nor annotate the relevant passages with underlining, circles, and marginal notes about their\nsignificance for your subject. (See the example below.)\nSource records can get lost. You must take special care to note the publication information\nfor sources directly on a printout or photocopy. If you don‘t have this information for your\nfinal paper, you can‘t use the source.\nPhotocopies and printouts can encourage unselective quotation. When drafting from a\ncomplete source rather than notes on the source, you may be tempted to import whole blocks\nrather than the bits that advance and support your ideas. See page 620 for guidelines on\njudicious use of quotations.\nDownloads\nResearching online, you can usually download full-text articles, Web pages, discussion-group\nmessages, and other materials onto your computer. If you also take notes on your computer, you\ncan use it to organize much or all of your research and then, while drafting, import source\ninformation from one file into another.\nThese advantages of downloading are offset by clear disadvantages:\nDirectly importing source material creates a high risk of plagiarism. You must keep clear\nboundaries between your own ideas and words and those of others—perhaps by using\ndistinctive type fonts or colors. And make sure to record publication information as part of\nevery downloaded document.\nDownloading can discourage interaction with sources. Even more than with photocopies\nand printouts, you must make an effort with downloads to analyze and synthesize sources.\nMany researchers print out downloaded sources and mark up paper copies. Alternatively, you\ncan use your word processor‘s Comment and Highlight functions to annotate the electronic\nfile or simply type your own comments into the source. (But use a different color or font!)\nUsing summary, paraphrase, and quotation\nAs you take notes from sources or work source material into your draft, you can summarize,\nparaphrase, quote, or combine meth-ods. The choice should depend on why you are using a source.\nNote Summaries, paraphrases, and quotations all require source citations. A summary or\nparaphrase without a source citation or a quotation without quotation marks and a source\ncitation is\nplagiarism. (See pp. 629–37 for more on plagiarism.)\nSummarizing\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 10\nWhen you summarize, you condense an extended idea or argument into a sentence or more in\nyour own words. A full discussion of summary appears on pages 140–42, and you should read that\nsection if you have not already done so.\nSummary is most useful when you want to record the gist of\nan author‘s idea without the background or supporting evidence. Edward Begay summarized the\nfollowing quotation from one of his sources—Larry Irving, ―The Still-Yawning Divide,‖\nNewsweek, page\n64:\nInternet access is affecting our everyday lives in important ways, from how we shop to how we define the\nnotion of community. Yet the digital divide between the information haves and have-nots is still very\nwide, especially between households of high and low income and between whites and minorities.\nAlthough competition will continue to drive down the cost of technology, and corporations and\neducational institutions will continue to bring people online, the market, private enterprise, and local\ngovernments cannot seal the divide by themselves. The federal government must assume leadership in\nensuring that all Americans have the access and skills they need to participate fully in the digital age.\nIn the following one-sentence summary, Begay picks out the kernel of Irving‘s passage and\nexpresses it in his own words.\nParaphrasing\nWhen you paraphrase, you follow much more closely the author‘s original presentation, but\nyou still restate it in your own words. Paraphrase is most useful when you want to present or\nexamine an author‘s line of reasoning but don‘t feel the original words merit direct quotation.\nThe note below shows how Begay might have paraphrased the passage by Irving given on the\npreceding page.\nNotice how the paraphrase differs from the Irving passage in sentence structures and wording,\nexcept in the case of terms that lack synonyms such as government and market:\nIrving‘s words Begay‘s paraphrase\nInternet access is affecting our Significant areas of daily life\neveryday lives in important are changing because of\nways, from how we shop to online communication,\nhow we define the notion of\ncommunity.\nYet the digital divide between but many people, particularly\nthe information haves and the poor and nonwhite, are\nhave-nots is still very wide, being left behind by this change.\nespecially between households\nof high and low income and\nbetween whites and minorities.\nIrving‘s words Begay‘s paraphrase\nAlthough competition will Market forces, businesses, and\ncontinue to drive down the cost schools will help to narrow the\nof technology, and corporations gap,\nand educational institutions will\ncontinue to bring people online,\nthe market, private enterprise, but they can‘t close it\nand local governments cannot\nseal the divide by themselves.\nThe federal government must unless the US government takes\nassume leadership in ensuring the lead to see that everyone\nthat all Americans have the benefits.\naccess and skills they need to\nparticipate fully in the\ndigital age.\nFollow these guidelines when paraphrasing:\nRead the material several times to be sure you understand it.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 11\nRestate the main ideas in your own words and sentence structures. You need not put down\nin new words the whole passage or all the details. Select what is pertinent and restate only\nthat. If complete sentences seem too detailed or cumbersome, use phrases. Edward Begay\nmight have written the following more telegraphic paraphrase of the quotation by Irving.\nBe careful not to distort meaning. Don‘t change the source‘s emphasis or omit connecting\nwords, qualifiers, and other material whose absence will confuse you later or cause you to\nmisrepresent the source. (See also p. 622.)\nSee page 634 for examples of unacceptable (plagiarized) paraphrases.\nIf English is not your native language and you have difficulty paraphrasing the ideas in\nsources, try this: Before attempting a paraphrase, read the original passage several times. Then,\ninstead of ―translating‖ line by line, try to state the gist of the passage without looking at it. Check\nyour effort against the origi-\nnal to be sure you have captured the source author‘s meaning and emphasis without using his or\nher words and sentence structures. If you need a synonym for a word, look it up in a dictionary.\nQuoting\nDeciding when to quote\nYour notes from sources may include many quotations, especially if you rely on photocopies,\nprintouts, or downloads. Whether to use a quotation in your draft, instead of a summary or\nparaphrase, depends on whether the source is primary or secondary and on how important the exact\nwords are:\nQuote extensively when you are analyzing primary sources, such as literary works and\nhistorical documents. The quotations will often be both the target of your analysis and the\nchief support for your ideas. You may need to quote many brief passages, integrated into your\nsentences, and then comment on the quotations to clarify your analysis and win readers‘\nagreement with it. For examples, see Vanessa Haley‘s analysis of Annie Dillard‘s writing (p.\n725) and the three literary analyses in Chapter 50 (pp. 750, 752, and 756).\nQuote selectively when you are drawing on secondary sources. Favor summaries and\nparaphrases over quotations, and put every quotation to each test in the box below. Most\npapers of ten or so pages should not need more than two or three quotations that are longer\nthan a few lines each.\nTranscribing and using quotations\nWhen you quote a source, either in your notes or in your draft, take\nprecautions to avoid plagiarism or misrepresentaton of the source:\nCopy the material carefully. Take down the author‘s exact wording, spelling, capitalization,\nand punctuation.\nProofread every direct quotation at least twice.\nUse quotation marks around the quotation so that later you won‘t confuse it with a\nparaphrase or summary. Be sure to transfer the quotation marks into your draft as well, unless\nthe quotation is long. See later pages for handling long quotations in MLA style (688–89),\nChicago style (776), and APA style (802–03).\nUse brackets to add words for clarity or to change the capitalization of letters (see pp. 483,\n491).\nUse ellipsis marks to omit irrelevant words or sentences (see\np. 484).\nCite the source of the quotation in your draft. See pages 637–38 on documenting sources.\nFor a summary of conventions regarding quotations, see the chart on pages 469–70.\nThe note below shows how Edward Begay might have quoted part of the passage by Larry\nIrving on page 617, using ellipsis marks and brackets to make the quotation more concise.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 12\nCombining quotation, summary, and paraphrase\nUsing quotation in combination with summary or paraphrase can help you shape the material\nto suit your purposes (although you must be careful not to distort the author‘s meaning). The note\nbelow shows how Edward Begay might have used a combination of quotation and paraphrase to\nrecord the statement by Irving.\nUsing sources accurately and fairly\nIn borrowing from sources, you must represent the author‘s meaning exactly, without\ndistorting it. In the following inaccurate summary, the writer has stated a meaning exactly opposite\nthat of the original. The original quotation, from the artist Henri Matisse, appears in Jack D. Flam,\nMatisse on Art, page 148.\nOriginal For the artist creation begins with vision. To see is itself a creative operation, requiring an\neffort. Everything that we see in our daily life is more or less distorted by acquired habits, and this is\nperhaps more evident in an age like ours when cinema posters and magazines present us every day with a\nflood of ready-made images which are to the eye what prejudices are to the mind.\nInaccurate Matisse said that the artist can learn how to see cre-\nsummary atively by looking at posters and magazines (qtd. in Flam 148).\nThe revision below combines summary and quotation to represent the author‘s meaning exactly:\nAccurate Matisse said that the artist must overcome visual ―habits‖\nsummary and ―prejudices,‖ particularly those developed in re-\nsponse to images of popular culture (qtd. in Flam 148).\nEXERCISE 44.7 Summarizing and paraphrasing\nPrepare two source notes, one summarizing the entire following paragraph and the other paraphrasing the\nfirst four sentences (ending with the word autonomy). Use the note format illustrated in the preceding\nsection, omitting only the subject heading. (You can do this exercise online at\nablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nFederal organization [of the United States] has made it possible for the different states to deal with\nthe same problems in many different ways. One consequence of federalism, then, has been that people are\ntreated differently, by law, from state to state. The great strength of this system is that differences from\nstate to state in cultural preferences, moral standards, and levels of wealth can be accommodated. In\ncontrast to a unitary system in which the central government makes all important decisions (as in France),\nfederalism is a powerful arrangement for maximizing regional freedom and autonomy. The great\nweakness of our federal system, however, is that people in some states receive less than the best or the\nmost advanced or the least expensive services and policies that government can offer. The federal\ndilemma does not invite easy solutions, for the costs and benefits of the arrangement have tended to\nbalance out.\n—Peter K. Eisinger et al., American Politics, page 44\nEXERCISE 44.8 Combining summary, paraphrase,\nand direct quotation\nPrepare a source note that combines paraphrase or summary and direct quotation to state the main idea of\nthe passage below. Use the note format illustrated in the preceding section, omitting only the heading.\n(You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nMost speakers unconsciously duel even during seemingly casual conversations, as can often be\nobserved at social gatherings where they show less concern for exchanging information with other guests\nthan for asserting their own dominance. Their verbal dueling often employs very subtle weapons like\nmumbling, a hostile act which de-feats the listener‘s desire to understand what the speaker claims he is\ntrying to say (but is really not saying because he is mumbling!). Or the verbal dueler may keep talking\nafter someone has passed out of hearing range—which is often an aggressive challenge to the listener to\nreturn and acknowledge the dominance of the speaker.\n—Peter K. Farb, Word Play, page 107\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 13\nEXERCISE 44.9 Gathering information from sources\nContinuing from Exercise 44.6 (p. 613), as the next step in preparing a research paper, gather and\norganize the information from your sources. Mark every note, photocopy, printout, and download with the\nsource‘s publication information and a heading related to your paper. Annotate relevant passages of\nphotocopies, printouts, and downloads. For handwritten or computer notes, use direct quotation,\nsummary, or paraphrase as seems appropriate, being careful to avoid inaccuracy and plagiarism. (If you\nneed help recognizing plagiarism, see Chapter 45.)\nIntegrating sources into your text\nThe evidence of others‘ information and opinions should back up your conclusions. You don‘t\nwant to let your evidence overwhelm your own point of view and voice. The point of research\nwriting is to investigate and go beyond sources, to interpret them and use them to support your own\nindependent ideas.\nNote Integrating borrowed material into your sentences involves several conventions discussed\nelsewhere in this book:\nUsing commas to punctuate signal phrases, pages 444–46.\nPlacing other punctuation marks with quotation marks, pages 474–75.\nUsing brackets for changes in quotations, pages 483–84 and below.\nUsing ellipsis marks for omissions from quotations, pages 484–86.\nPunctuating and placing parenthetical citations, pages 654–56.\nSetting off long quotations from your text without quotation marks, pages 688–89 (MLA\nstyle), 776 (Chicago style), and 802–03 (APA style).\nIntroducing borrowed material\nWhen using a summary, paraphrase, or quotation, smooth the transition between your ideas\nand words and those of the source.\nNote The examples in this and the next section use the MLA style of source documentation\nand also present-tense verbs (such as disagrees). For specific variations in documentation and verb\ntense within the academic disciplines, see pages 627–28.\nLinks between borrowed material and your own sentences\nReaders will be distracted from your point if borrowed material does not fit into your\nsentence. In the passage below, the writer has not meshed the structures of her own and her\nsource‘s sentences:\nAwkward One editor disagrees with this view and ―a good reporter does not fail to separate opinions\nfrom facts‖ (Lyman 52).\nIn the following revision the writer adds words to integrate the quotation into her sentence:\nRevised One editor disagrees with this view, maintaining that ―a good reporter does not fail to\nseparate opinions from facts‖ (Lyman 52).\nAlterations of quotations\nTo mesh your own and your source‘s words, you may sometimes need to make a substitution\nor addition to the quotation, signaling your change with brackets:\nWords added\n―The tabloids [of England] are a journalistic case study in bad reporting,‖ claims Lyman (52).\nVerb form changed\nA bad reporter, Lyman implies, is one who ―[fails] to separate opinions from facts‖ (52). [The bracketed\nverb replaces fail in the original.]\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 14\nCapitalization changed\n―[T]o separate opinions from facts‖ is a goal of good reporting (Lyman 52). [In the original, to is not\ncapitalized.]\nNoun supplied for pronoun\nThe reliability of a news organization ―depends on [reporters‘] trustworthiness,‖ says Lyman (52). [The\nbracketed noun replaces their in the original.]\nInterpreting borrowed material\nEven when it does not conflict with your own sentence structure, borrowed material will be\nineffective if you merely dump it in readers‘ laps without explaining how you intend it to be\nunderstood. Reading the following passage, we must figure out for ourselves that the writer‘s\nsentence and the quotation state opposite points of view.\nDumped Many news editors and reporters maintain that it is impossible to keep personal opinions\nfrom influencing the selection and presentation of facts. ―True, news reporters, like everyone else, form\nimpressions of what they see and hear. However, a good reporter does not fail to separate opinions from\nfacts‖ (Lyman 52).\nIn the revision the underlined additions tell us how to interpret the quotation:\nRevised Many news editors and reporters maintain that it is impossible to keep personal opinions\nfrom influencing the selection and presentation of facts. Yet not all authorities agree with this view. One\neditor grants that ―news reporters, like everyone else, form impressions of what they see and hear.‖ But,\nhe insists, ―a good reporter does not fail to separate opinions from facts‖ (Lyman 52).\nSignal phrases\nIn the revised passage above, the words One editor grants and he insists\nare signal phrases: they tell readers who the source is and what to expect\nin the quotations that follow. Signal phrases usually contain (1) the source\nauthor‘s name (or a substitute for it, such as One editor and he) and (2) a\nverb that indicates the source author‘s attitude or approach to what he or\nshe says. In the preceding example, grants implies concession and insists\nimplies argument. The box on the next page includes a list of verbs for\nsignal phrases.\nVary your signal phrases to suit your interpretation of borrowed material and also to keep\nreaders‘ interest. A signal phrase may precede, interrupt, or follow the borrowed material:\nSignal phrase precedes\nLyman insists that ―a good reporter does not fail to separate opinions from facts‖ (52).\nSignal phrase interrupts\n―However,‖ Lyman insists, ―a good reporter does not fail to separate opinions from facts‖ (52).\nSignal phrase follows\n―[A] good reporter does not fail to separate opinions from facts,‖ Lyman insists (52).\nBackground information\nYou can add information to a signal phrase to inform readers why you are using a source. In\nmost cases, provide the author‘s name in the text, especially if the author is an expert or if readers\nwill recognize the name:\nAuthor named\nHarold Lyman grants that ―news reporters, like everyone else, form impressions of what they see and\nhear.‖ But, Lyman insists, ―a good reporter does not fail to separate opinions from facts‖ (52).\nIf the source title contributes information about the author or the context of the quotation, you can\nprovide it in the text:\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 15\nTitle given\nHarold Lyman, in his book The Conscience of the Journalist, grants that ―news reporters, like everyone\nelse, form impressions of what they see and hear.‖ But, Lyman insists, ―a good reporter does not fail to\nseparate opinions from facts‖ (52).\nFinally, if the quoted author‘s background and experience reinforce or clarify the quotation, you\ncan provide these credentials in the text:\nCredentials given\nHarold Lyman, a newspaper editor for more than forty years, grants that ―news reporters, like everyone\nelse, form impressions of what they see and hear.‖ But, Lyman insists, ―a good reporter does not fail to\nseparate opinions from facts‖ (52).\nYou need not always name the author, source, or credentials in your text. In fact, such\nintroductions may get in the way when you are simply establishing facts or weaving together facts\nand opinions from varied sources. In the following passage, the information is more important than\nthe source, so the name of the source is confined to a parenthetical acknowledgment:\nTo end the abuses of the British, many colonists were urging three actions: forming a united front,\nseceding from Britain, and taking control of their own international relations (Wills 325–36).\nFollowing discipline styles for integrating sources\nThe preceding guidelines for introducing and interpreting borrowed material apply generally\nacross academic disciplines, but there are differences in verb tenses and documentation style.\nEnglish and some other humanities\nWriters in English, foreign languages, and related disciplines use MLA style for documenting\nsources (see Chapter 47) and generally use the present tense of verbs in signal phrases. In\ndiscussing sources other than works of literature, the present perfect tense is also sometimes\nappropriate:\nLyman insists . . . [present].\nLyman has insisted . . . [present perfect].\nIn discussing works of literature, use only the present tense to describe both the work of the author\nand the action in the work:\nKate Chopin builds irony into every turn of ―The Story of an Hour.‖ For example, Mrs. Mallard, the\ncentral character, finds joy in the death of her husband, whom she loves, because she anticipates ―the long\nprocession of years that would belong to her absolutely‖ (23).\nAvoid shifting tenses in writing about literature. You can, for instance, shorten quotations to\navoid their past-tense verbs:\nShift Her freedom elevates her, so that ―she carried herself unwittingly like a goddess of victory‖\n(24).\nNo shift Her freedom elevates her, so that she walks ―unwittingly like a goddess of victory‖ (24).\nHistory and other humanities\nWriters in history, art history, philosophy, and related disciplines generally use the present\ntense or present perfect tense of verbs in signal phrases:\nLincoln persisted, as Haworth has noted, in ―feeling that events controlled him.‖3\nWhat Miller calls Lincoln‘s ―severe self-doubt‖6 undermined his effectiveness on at least two occasions.\nThe raised numbers after the quotations are part of the Chicago documentation style, used in\nhistory and other disciplines and discussed on pages 764–65.\nSocial and natural sciences\nWriters in the sciences generally use a verb‘s present tense just for reporting the results of a\nstudy (The data suggest . . .). Otherwise, they use a verb‘s past tense or present perfect tense in a\nsignal phrase, as when introducing an explanation, interpretation, or other commentary. (Thus\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 16\nwhen you are writing for the sciences, generally convert the list of signal-phrase verbs on p. 626\nfrom the present to the past or present perfect tense.)\nLin (1999) has suggested that preschooling may significantly affect children‘s academic performance\nthrough high school (pp. 22–23).\nIn an exhaustive survey of the literature published between 1990 and 2000, Walker (2001) found ―no\nproof, merely a weak correlation, linking place of residence and rate of illness‖ (p. 121).\nThese passages conform to APA documentation style, discussed on pages 784–800. APA\nstyle, or one quite similar to it, is also used in sociology, education, nursing, biology, and many\nother sciences.\nEXERCISE 44.10 Introducing and interpreting borrowed material\nDrawing on the ideas in the following paragraph and using examples from your own observations and\nexperiences, write a paragraph about anxiety. Integrate at least one direct quotation and one paraphrase\nfrom the following paragraph into your own sentences. In your paragraph identify the author by name and\ngive his credentials: he is a professor of psychiatry and a practicing psychoanalyst. (You can do this\nexercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nThere are so many ways in which man is different from all the lower forms of animals, and almost\nall of them make us uniquely susceptible to feelings of anxiousness. Our imagination and reasoning\npowers facilitate anxiety; the anxious feeling is precipitated not by an absolute impending threat—such as\nthe worry about an examination, a speech, travel—but rather by the symbolic and often unconscious\nrepresentations. We do not have to be experiencing a potential danger. We can experience something\nrelated to it. We can recall, through our incredible memories, the original symbolic sense of vulnerability\nin childhood and suffer the feeling attached to that. We can even forget the original memory and still be\nstuck with the emotion—which is then compounded by its seemingly irrational quality at this time. It is\nnot just the fear of death which pains us, but the anticipation of it; or the anniversary of a specific death;\nor a street, a hospital, a time of day, a color, a flower, a symbol associated with death.\n—Willard Gaylin, ―Feeling Anxious,‖ page 23\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 17\nVisit the companion Web site for more help and an additional exercise on\nworking with sources.\nAnnotated bibliography entry with assessment\nPublication and access information for source\nUnited States. Statistics confirmed in other\nstudies?\nSummary of source (from working bibliography)\nAssessment of source\nQuestion\nQuestions for evaluating sources\nFor online sources, supplement these questions with those on pages 602 and 608.\nRelevance\nDoes the source devote some attention to your subject? Check whether the source focuses on your\nsubject or covers it marginally, and compare the source‘s coverage to that in other sources.\nIs the source appropriately specialized for your needs? Check the source‘s treatment of a topic\nyou know something about, to ensure that it is neither too superficial nor too technical.\nIs the source up to date enough for your subject? Check the publication date. If your subject is\ncurrent, your sources should be, too.\nReliability\nWhere does the source come from? It matters whether you found the source through your library or\ndirectly on the Internet. (If the latter, see the following pages.) Check whether a library source is\npopular or scholarly. Scholarly sources, such as refereed journals and university press books, are\ngenerally deeper and more reliable.\nIs the author an expert in the field? The authors of scholarly publications tend to be experts. To\nverify expertise, check an author‘s credentials in a biography (if the source includes one), in a\nbiographical reference (see p. 578), or by a keyword search of the Web. Look for the author‘s other\npublications and for his or her job and any affiliation, such as teacher at a university, researcher with a\nnonprofit organization, author of general-interest books, or writer for popular magazines.\nWhat is the author‘s bias? Every author has a point of view that influences the selection and\ninterpretation of evidence. You may be able to learn about an author‘s bias from biographies, citation\nindexes (pp. 586–87), and review indexes (p. 581). But also look at the source itself. How do the\nauthor‘s ideas relate to those in other sources? What areas does the author emphasize, ignore, or\ndismiss? When you‘re aware of sources‘ biases, you can attempt to balance them.\nIs the source fair and reasonable? Even a strongly biased work should present sound reasoning,\nadequate evidence, and a fair picture of opposing views—all in an objective, calm tone. The absence\nof any of these qualities should raise a warning flag.\nIs the source well written? A logical organization and clear, error-free sentences indicate a careful\nauthor.\nYou can download these questions from ablongman.com/littlebrown. Use a copy of the file for each\nsource you are evaluating, providing written answers between the questions.\nQuestions for evaluating Web sites\nSupplement these questions with those on the previous page.\nWhat type of site are you viewing? What does the type lead\nyou to expect about the site‘s purpose and content?\nWho is the author or sponsor? How credible is the person or group responsible for the site?\nWhat is the purpose of the site? What does the site‘s author or sponsor intend to achieve?\nWhat does context tell you? What do you already know about the site‘s subject that can inform your\nevaluation? What kinds of support or other information do the site‘s links provide?\nWhat does presentation tell you? Is the site‘s design well thought out and effective? Is the writing\nclear and error-free?\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 18\nHow worthwhile is the content? Are the site‘s claims well supported by evidence? Is the evidence\nfrom reliable sources?\nYou can download these questions from ablongman.com/littlebrown. Although the average Internet access rate for\nincomes of $75,000 and greater is 77.7%, it ranges from 82% for those with a college\ndegree or more down to 51% for those with less than a high school education.\nLikewise, households with incomes between $15,000 and $34,999 had an average access\nrate of 28%, ranging from 46% for college or more down to 11% for less than high\nschool. The same wide disparities occur within education categories. For example,\namong households in which the householder had some schooling beyond high school but\nnot a college degree, home Internet access reached 76% in the over $75,000 income\ngroup but only 26% in the under $15,000 income group. Market forces, businesses, and schools can‘t close gap—\nUSgovt. must lead the way.\nTests for direct quotations from\nsecondary sources\nThe author’s original satisfies one of these requirements:\nThe language is unusually vivid, bold, or inventive.\nThe quotation cannot be paraphrased without distortion or loss of meaning.\nThe words themselves are at issue in your interpretation.\nThe quotation represents and emphasizes a body of opinion or the view of an important expert.\nThe quotation emphatically reinforces your own idea.\nThe quotation is an illustration, such as a graph, diagram, or table.\nThe quotation is as short as possible:\nIt includes only material relevant to your point.\nIt is edited to eliminate examples and other unneeded material. (For editing quotations, see the\nbulleted list on the facing page.)\nQuotation of source\nRole of government in Internet use\nIrving 64\n―Internet access is affecting our lives in important ways. . . . Yet the digital\ndivide . . . is still very wide, especially between households of high and low\nincome and between whites and minorities. . . . [T]he market, private enterprise, and local governments cannot seal the divide by\nthemselves. The federal government must assume leadership in ensuring that all Americans have the\naccess and skills they need to participate fully in the digital age.‖\nCombined paraphrase and quotation of source\nRole of government in Internet use\nIrving 64\nMany people—particularly the poor and nonwhite—are being left behind by\nInternet communication. Market forces, businesses, and schools can‘t close the gap unless the US government takes the lead ―in\nensuring that all Americans have the access and skills they need to participate fully in the digital age.‖\nVerbs for signal phrases\nUse verbs that convey information about source authors‘ attitudes or\napproaches. In the sentence Smith that the flood might have been\ndisastrous, filling the blank with observes, finds, or insists would create\ndifferent meanings.\nDisciplines vary in the tenses of verbs within signal phrases. The\nNote\nverbs listed below are in the present tense, typical of writing in the\nhumanities. But in the social and natural sciences, the past tense or pres-\nent perfect tense is more common. See page 625.\nAuthor is\nneutral\ncomments\ndescribes\nexplains\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 20\nillustrates\nnotes\nobserves\npoints out\nrecords\nrelates\nreports\nsays\nsees\nthinks\nwrites\nAuthor infers\nor suggests\nanalyzes\nasks\nassesses\nconcludes\nfinds\npredicts\nproposes\nreveals\nshows\nspeculates\nsuggests\nsupposes\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 21\nAuthor\nargues\nclaims\ncontends\ndefends\ndisagrees\nholds\ninsists\nmaintains\nAuthor agrees\nadmits\nagrees\nconcedes\nconcurs\ngrants\nAuthor is uneasy or disparaging\nbelittles\nbemoans\ncomplains\ncondemns\ndeplores\ndeprecates\nderides\nlaments\nwarns\n45\nCHAPTER\nAvoiding Plagiarism and\nDocumenting Sources\nThe knowledge building that is the focus of academic writing rests on participants‘ integrity in\nusing sources. This standard of integrity derives from the idea that the work of an author is his or\nher intellectual property: if you use that work, you must acknowledge the author‘s ownership. At\nthe same time, source acknowledgments tell readers what your own writing is based on, creating\nthe trust that knowledge building requires.\nPlagiarism (from a Latin word for ―kidnapper‖) is the presentation of someone else‘s ideas or\nwords as your own. Whether deliberate or accidental, plagiarism is a serious offense.\nDeliberate plagiarism:\nCopying or downloading a phrase, a sentence, or a longer passage from a source and passing it\noff as your own by omitting quotation marks and a source citation.\nSummarizing or paraphrasing someone else‘s ideas without acknowledging your debt in a\nsource citation.\nHanding in as your own work a paper you have bought, copied off the Web, had a friend write, or accepted\nfrom another student.\nAccidental plagiarism:\nForgetting to place quotation marks around another writer‘s words.\nCarelessly omitting a source citation for a paraphrase.\nOmitting a source citation for another‘s idea because you are unaware of the need to\nacknowledge the idea.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 22\nIn most schools a code of academic honesty calls for severe consequences for deliberate or\naccidental plagiarism: a failing grade, suspension from school, or even expulsion.\nThe way to avoid plagiarism is to acknowledge your sources by documenting them. This\nchapter discusses plagiarism and the Internet (below), shows how to distinguish what doesn‘t\nrequire acknowledgment from what does (pp. 632–34), covers issues particular to online sources\n(pp. 635–37), and provides an overview of source documentation (pp. 637–38).\nThe concept of intellectual property and thus the rules governing plagiarism are not universal.\nIn some other cultures, for instance, students may be encouraged to copy the words of scholars\nwithout acknowledgment, in order to demonstrate their mastery of or respect for the scholars‘ work.\nIn the United States, however, using an author‘s work without a source citation is considered theft.\nWhen in doubt about the guidelines in this chapter, ask your instructor for advice.\nCommitting and detecting plagiarism\non the Internet\nThe Internet has made it easier to plagiarize than ever before, but it has also made plagiarism\neasier to catch.\nEven honest students risk accidental plagiarism by downloading sources and importing\nportions into their drafts. Dishonest students may take advantage of the downloading process to\nsteal others‘ work. They may also use the term-paper businesses on the Web, which offer both\nready-made research and complete papers, usually for a fee. Paying for research or a paper does\nnot make it the buyer’s work. Anyone who submits someone else‘s work as his or her own is a\nplagiarist.\nStudents who plagiarize from the Internet deprive themselves of an education in honest\nresearch, and they also expose them-selves to detection. Teachers can use search engines to find\nspecific phrases or sentences anywhere on the Web, including among scholarly publications, all\nkinds of Web sites, and term-paper collections. They can search term-paper sites as easily as\nstudents can, look-\ning for similarities with papers they‘ve received. They can also use special detection software that\ncompares students‘ work with other work anywhere on the Internet, seeking matches as short as a\nfew words.\nSome instructors suggest that their students use plagiarism-\ndetection programs to verify that their own work does not include accidental plagiarism, at least not\nfrom the Internet. Links to such programs appear on this book‘s Web site at ablongman.com/\nlittlebrown.\nKnowing what you need not acknowledge\nTwo kinds of information do not have to be acknowledged in source citations: your own\nindependent material and common knowledge.\nUsing your independent material\nYour own independent material includes your thoughts, observations from experience,\ncompilations of facts, or experimental results, expressed in your words or format. For example, you\nmight offer a conclusion about crowd behavior based on watching crowds at concerts or draw a\ndiagram from information you gathered yourself. Though you generally should describe the basis\nfor your conclusions so that readers can evaluate your thinking, you need not cite sources for them.\nNote that someone else‘s ideas and facts are not yours, even when you express them entirely in\nyour own words and sentence structures. The ideas and facts require acknowledgment.\nUsing common knowledge\nCommon knowledge consists of the standard information of a field of study as well as folk\nliterature and commonsense observations.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 23\nStandard information includes the major facts of history, such as the dates during which\nCharlemagne ruled as emperor of Rome (800–14). It does not include interpretations of facts,\nsuch as a historian‘s opinion that Charlemagne was sometimes needlessly cruel in extending\nhis power.\nFolk literature, such as the fairy tale ―Snow White,‖ is popu-larly known and cannot be\ntraced to a particular writer. Literature traceable to a writer is not folk literature, even if it is\nvery familiar.\nCommonsense observations are things most people know, such as that inflation is most\ntroublesome for people with low and fixed incomes. A particular economist‘s idea about the\neffects of inflation on Chinese immigrants is not a commonsense observation.\nAs long as you express it in your own words and sentence structures, you may use common\nknowledge as your own.\nThe first time you come across an idea or a piece of information that you may want to use,\nrecord the publication information for the source. If in wider reading you repeatedly encounter the\nsame idea or information without cited sources, then you may assume it‘s common knowledge.\nDon‘t take unnecessary risks, however: a source citation for common knowledge is not wrong.\nKnowing what you must acknowledge\nYou must always acknowledge other people‘s independent material—that is, any facts or ideas\nthat are not common knowledge or your own. The source may be anything, including a book, an\narticle, a movie, an interview, a microfilmed document, a Web page, a newsgroup posting, or an\nopinion expressed on the radio. You must acknowledge summaries or paraphrases of ideas or facts\nas well as quotations of the language and format in which ideas or facts ap-pear: wording, sentence\nstructures, arrangement, and special graphics (such as a diagram).\nYou need to acknowledge another‘s material no matter how you use it, how much of it you\nuse, or how often you use it. Whether you are quoting a single important word, paraphrasing a\nsingle sentence, or summarizing three paragraphs, and whether you are using the source only once\nor a dozen times, you must acknowledge the original author every time. See pages 637–38 for how\nto acknowledge sources and pages 654–55 for where to place source citations in relation to cited\nmaterial.\nUsing copied language: Quotation marks\nand a source citation\nCopied words and sentence structure\nThe following example baldly plagiarizes the original quotation from Jessica Mitford‘s Kind\nand Usual Punishment, page 9. Without quotation marks or a source citation, the example matches\nMitford‘s wording (underlined) and closely parallels her sentence structure:\nOriginal ‖The character and mentality of the keepers may be of more importance in understanding\nprisons than the character and mentality of the kept.‖\nPlagiarism But the character of prison officials (the keepers) is more important in understanding\nprisons than the character of prisoners (the kept).\nTo avoid plagiarism, the writer can paraphrase and cite the source (see the next page) or use Mitford‘s\nactual words in quotation marks and with a source citation (here, in MLA style):\nRevision According to one critic of the penal system, “The char-\n(quotation) acter and mentality of the keepers may be of more importance in understanding prisons\nthan the character and mentality of the kept” (Mitford 9).\nChanged sentence structure but copied words\nEven with a source citation and with a different sentence structure, the next example is still\nplagiarism because it uses some of Mitford‘s words (underlined) without quotation marks:\nPlagiarism According to one critic of the penal system, the psychology of the kept may say less\nabout prisons than the psychology of the keepers (Mitford 9).\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 24\nRevision According to one critic of the penal system, the psy-\n(quotation) chology of “the kept” may say less about prisons than the psychology of “the keepers”\n(Mitford 9).\nUsing a paraphrase or summary: Your own words and sentence structure and a\nsource citation\nChanged sentence structure but copied words\nThe following example changes the sentence structure of the origi-nal Mitford quotation\nabove, but it still uses Mitford‘s words (underlined) without quotation marks and without a source\ncitation:\nPlagiarism In understanding prisons, we might focus less on the character and mentality of the kept\nthan on those of the keepers.\nTo avoid plagiarism, the writer can use quotation marks and cite the source or use his or her own\nwords and still cite the source (because the idea is Mitford‘s, not the writer‘s):\nRevision Mitford holds that we may be able to learn less about\n(paraphrase) prisons from the psychology of prisoners than from the psychology of prison officials\n(9).\nRevision We may understand prisons better if we focus on the\n(paraphrase) personalities and attitudes of the prison workers rather than those of the inmates\n(Mitford 9).\nChanged words but copied sentence structure\nIn the next example, the writer cites Mitford and does not use her words but still plagiarizes\nher sentence structure:\nPlagiarism One critic of the penal system maintains that the\npsychology of prison officials may be more informa-tive about prisons than the psychology of prisoners\n(Mitford 9).\nRevision One critic of the penal system maintains that we may\n(paraphrase) be able to learn less from the psychology of prisoners than from the psychology of\nprison officials (Mitford 9).\nEXERCISE 45.1 Recognizing plagiarism\nThe numbered items below show various attempts to quote or paraphrase the following passage. Carefully\ncompare each attempt with the original passage. Which attempts are plagiarized, inaccurate, or both, and\nwhich are acceptable? Why? (You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\nI would agree with the sociologists that psychiatric labeling is dangerous. Society can inflict terrible\nwounds by discrimination, and by confusing health with disease and disease with badness.\n—George E. Vaillant, Adaptation to Life, p. 361\n1. According to George Vaillant, society often inflicts wounds by using psychiatric labeling, confusing\nhealth, disease, and badness (361).\n2. According to George Vaillant, ―psychiatric labeling [such as ‗homo-sexual‘ or ‗schizophrenic‘] is\ndangerous. Society can inflict terrible wounds by . . . confusing health with disease and disease with\nbad-ness‖ (361).\n3. According to George Vaillant, when psychiatric labeling discriminates between health and disease or\nbetween disease and badness, it can inflict wounds on those labeled (361).\n4. Psychiatric labels can badly hurt those labeled, says George Vaillant, because they fail to distinguish\namong health, illness, and immor-ality (361).\n5. Labels such as ―homosexual‖ and ―schizophrenic‖ can be hurtful when they fail to distinguish among\nhealth, illness, and immorality.\n6. ―I would agree with the sociologists that society can inflict\nterrible wounds by discrimination, and by confusing health with disease\nand disease with badness‖ (Vaillant 361).\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 25\nAcknowledging online sources\nOnline sources are so accessible and so easy to download into your own documents that it may\nseem they are freely available, exempting you from the obligation to acknowledge them. They are\nnot. Acknowledging online sources is somewhat trickier than acknowledging print sources, but no\nless essential. Further, if you are publishing your work on the Web, you need to take account of\nsources‘ copyright restrictions as well.\nNote Ask your instructor for advice whenever you are unsure about citing an online source or\nseeking permission to use copyrighted material.\nAcknowledging online sources in an unpublished project\nWhen you use material from an online source in a print or online document to be distributed\njust to your class, your obligation to cite sources does not change: you must acknowledge someone\nelse‘s independent material in whatever form you find it. With online sources, that obligation can\npresent additional challenges:\nRecord complete publication information each time you consult an online source. Online\nsources may change from one day to the next or even disappear entirely. See page 569 for the\ninformation to record, such as the electronic address and the publication date. Without the\nproper information, you may not use the source.\nAcknowledge linked sites.
