If you use not only a Web site but also one or more of its linked\nsites, you must acknowledge the linked sites as well. The fact that one person has used a\nsecond person‘s work does not release you from the responsibility to cite the second work.\nSeek the author’s permission before using an e-mail message, discussion-group posting,\nor Web log contribution. Obtaining permission advises the author that his or her ideas are\nabout to be distributed more widely and lets the author verify that you have not\nmisrepresented the ideas. (See p. 608 for advice on tracing authors.)\nAcknowledging print and online sources\nin a Web composition\nWhen you use material from print or online sources in a composition for the Web, you must\nnot only acknowledge your sources, as discussed above, but take the additional precaution of\nobserving copyright restrictions.\nA Web site is a medium of publication just as a book or magazine is and so involves the same\nresponsibility to obtain reprint permission from copyright holders. The exception is a password-\nprotected site (such as a course site), which many copyright holders regard as private. You can find\ninformation about copyright holders and permissions on the copyright page of a print publication\n(following the\ntitle page) and on a page labeled something like ―Terms of Use‖ on a Web site. If you don‘t see an\nexplicit release for student use or publication on private Web sites, assume you must seek\npermission.\nThe legal convention of fair use allows an author to reprint a small portion of copyrighted\nmaterial without obtaining the copyright holder‘s permission, as long as the author acknowledges\nthe source. The online standards of fair use differ for print and online sources and are not fixed in\neither case. The guidelines below are conservative:\nPrint sources: Quote without permission fewer than fifty words from an article or fewer than\nthree hundred words from a book. You‘ll need permission to use any longer quotation from an\narticle or book; any quotation at all from a play, poem, or song; and any use of an entire\nwork—such as a photograph, chart, or other illustration.\nOnline sources: Quote without permission text that represents just a small portion of the\nwhole—say, forty words out of three hundred. Follow the preceding print guidelines for plays,\npoems, songs, and illustrations, adding multimedia elements (audio or video clips) to the list\nof works that require reprint permission for any use.\nLinks: You may need to seek permission to link your site to another one—for instance, if you\nrely on the linked site to provide substantial evidence for your claims or if you incorporate a\nlinked site‘s multimedia element (an image or a sound or video clip) into your site.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 26\nNote Although most online sources are copyrighted, much val-uable material is not: either the\ncreator does not claim copyright, or the copyright has lapsed so that the work is in the public\ndomain. The first category includes most government documents; the second includes most works\nby authors who have been dead at least fifty years. You do not need permission to reprint from such\na source, but you still must cite the source.\nDocumenting sources\nEvery time you borrow the words, facts, or ideas of others, you must document the source—\nthat is, supply a reference (or document) telling readers that you borrowed the material and where\nyou borrowed it from.\nEditors and teachers in most academic disciplines require special documentation formats (or\nstyles) in their scholarly journals and in students‘ papers. All the styles use a citation in the text that\nserves two purposes: it signals that material is borrowed, and it refers readers to detailed\ninformation about the source so that they can locate both the source and the place in the source\nwhere the borrowed material appears. The detailed source information appears either in footnotes\nor at the end of the paper.\nAside from these essential similarities, the disciplines‘ documentation styles differ markedly in\ncitation form, arrangement of source information, and other particulars. Each discipline‘s style\nreflects the needs of its practitioners for certain kinds of information presented in certain ways. For\ninstance, the currency of a source is important in the social and natural sciences, where studies\nbuild on and correct each other; thus in-text citations in these disciplines usually include a source‘s\ndate of publication. In the humanities, however, currency is less important, so in-text citations do\nnot include date of publication.\nThe disciplines‘ documentation formats are described in style guides listed elsewhere in this\nbook for the humanities (p. 764), the social sciences (p. 784), and the natural and applied sciences\n(p. 812). This book discusses and illustrates four common documentation styles:\nMLA style, used in English, foreign languages, and some other humanities (p. 647).\nChicago style, used in history, art history, philosophy, religion, and some other humanities (p.\n764).\nAPA style, used in psychology and some other social sciences (p. 784).\nCSE style, used in the biological and some other sciences (p. 812).\nAsk your instructor which style you should use. If no style is required, use the guide that‘s\nmost appropriate for the discipline in which you‘re writing. Do follow one system for citing\nsources—and one system only—so that you provide all the necessary information in a consistent\nformat.\nNote Bibliography software—Biblio, Refworks, Endnote, Pro-Cite, and others—can help you\nformat your source citations in the style of your choice. Always ask your instructors if you may use\nsuch software for your papers. The programs prompt you for needed information (author‘s name,\nbook title, and so on) and then arrange, capitalize, underline, and punctuate the information as\nrequired by the style. But no program can anticipate all the varieties of source information, nor can\nit substitute for your own care and attention in giving your sources complete acknowledgment\nusing the required form.\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 27\nVisit the companion Web site for more help and an additional exercise\non avoiding plagiarism and documenting sources.\nChecklist for avoiding plagiarism\nType of source\nAre you using\nyour own independent material,\ncommon knowledge, or\nsomeone else‘s independent material?\nYou must acknowledge someone else’s material.\nQuotations\nDo all quotations exactly match their sources? Check them.\nHave you inserted quotation marks around quotations that are run into your text?\nHave you shown omissions with ellipsis marks and additions with brackets?\nDoes every quotation have a source citation?\nParaphrases and summaries\nHave you used your own words and sentence structures for every paraphrase and summary? If not, use\nquotation marks around the original author‘s words.\nDoes every paraphrase and summary have a source citation?\nThe Web\nHave you obtained any necessary permission to use someone else‘s material on the Web?\nSource citations\nHave you acknowledged every use of someone else‘s material in the place where you use it?\nDoes your list of works cited include all the sources you have used?\nYou can download this checklist from ablongman.com/littlebrown. Working with a copy of the list,\nquestion every use you make of someone else‘s material.\n46\nCHAPTER\nWriting the Paper\nWriting a research paper begins when you seek a topic and continues as you evaluate and\ngather information from your sources. During research, you might even pause to draft paragraphs\nor sections that pull your sources together to support your ideas. At some point, though, you‘ll need\nto turn your attention to the whole paper—ensuring that it has a clear thesis (below), creating a\nstructure (next page), writing the complete draft (p. 643), revising and editing the paper (p. 645),\nand preparing the final draft (p. 646). To illustrate these stages, we continue to draw on Edward\nBegay‘s and Vanessa Haley‘s work.\nNote This chapter complements and extends the detailed discussion of the writing situation\nand the writing process in Chapters 1–3, which also include many tips for using a word processor.\nIf you haven‘t already done so, read Chapters 1–3 before this one.\nDeveloping a thesis statement\nPerhaps earlier in the research-writing process, but certainly once you have gathered\ninformation from your sources, you will want to express your central idea and perspective in a\nthesis statement of one or two sentences. (See pp. 27–31 if you need guidance on developing a\nthesis statement.) Drafting a thesis statement will help you see the overall picture and organize your\nnotes.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 28\nEdward Begay‘s and Vanessa Haley‘s work on their research papers illustrates how a thesis\nstatement evolves to become complete and specific. Before finishing his reading on access to the\nInternet, Begay wrote the following draft of a thesis statement:\nTentative thesis statement\nBecause of the cost of hooking up to the Internet and training people to use it, the nation faces the\npossibility of a widening gap between rich and poor in information, skills, and income.\nThis statement captured Begay‘s preliminary idea that the Internet poses a threat to equality. But\nwith further reading, Begay re-thought this idea: many of his sources mentioned Internet access\nthrough public schools and libraries, and he began to focus on these institutions as a solution to the\nproblem. The solution opened up new questions: How would schools and libraries have to change,\nwhat would the change cost, and who should pay for it? With more reading, Begay revised his\nthesis statement:\nRevised thesis statement\nGovernments and businesses must ensure that libraries and schools have the hardware, connections, and\ntraining capabilities needed for computer technology to make Americans more rather than less equal.\nFor Vanessa Haley, framing a thesis statement for her paper\non Annie Dillard required drawing together (synthesizing) Dillard‘s ideas about humanity and\nnature into a single statement of Haley‘s own. The first draft merely conveyed Haley‘s interest in\nDillard:\nTentative thesis statement\nUnlike many other nature writers, Dillard does not reinforce the separation between humanity and nature.\nHaley‘s revision stated her synthesis of Dillard‘s ideas:\nRevised thesis statement\nIn her encounters with nature, Dillard probes a spiritual as well as a physical identity between human\nbeings and nature that could help to heal the rift between them.\nEXERCISE 46.1 Developing a thesis statement\nDraft and revise a thesis statement for your developing research paper. Make sure the revised version\nspecifically asserts your main idea.\nCreating a structure\nBefore starting to draft your research paper, organize your ideas and information so that you\nknow the main divisions of your paper, the order you‘ll cover them in, and the important\nsupporting ideas for each division. The goal is to create a structure that presents your ideas in a\nsensible and persuasive sequence and that supports ideas at each level with enough explanation and\nevidence. Consult the discussion of organization on pages 32–42 if you need help distinguishing\ngeneral and specific information, arranging groups of information, or using a computer effectively\nfor developing a structure.\nArranging ideas and support\nCreating a structure for a research paper involves almost constant synthesis, the forging of\nrelationships among ideas (see p. 610). As you arrange and rearrange source information and your\nown thoughts, you find connections among ideas and determine which are most important, which\nare merely supportive, and which are not relevant at all.\nTo build a structure, follow these guidelines:\nArrange your source information in categories according to the subject headings you\nattached to each note, photocopy, or printout. Each category should correspond to a main\nsection of your paper: a key idea of your own that supports the thesis along with the evidence\nfor that idea.\nReview your research journal and annotated bibliography. Hunt for connections between\nsources, opinions of sources, and other thoughts that can help you organize your paper.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 29\nLook objectively at your categories. If some are skimpy, with little information, consider\nwhether you should drop the categories or conduct more research to fill them out. If most of\nyour information falls into one or two categories, consider whether the categories are too\nbroad and should be divided. (Does any of this rethinking affect your thesis statement? If so,\nrevise it accordingly.)\nWithin each category, distinguish between the main idea and the supporting ideas and\nevidence. Only the support should come from your sources. The main idea should be your\nown.\nUsing an outline\nAn outline can help you shape your research and also discover potential problems, such as\ninadequate support and overlapping or irrelevant ideas.\nInformal outline\nFor some research projects, you may find an informal outline sufficient: you list main points\nand supporting information in the order you expect to discuss them. Because of its informality,\nsuch an outline can help you try out different arrangements of material, even fairly early in the\nresearch process.\nEdward Begay experimented with an informal outline while examining his sources, in order to\nsee how his developing ideas might fit together:\nHistory of the Internet\nPacket-switching networks—UK, France\nARPANET—linked US Defense Dept., contractors, universities\nNetwork of networks—UNIX, NSFNET, and onward\nCommercial vs. public use\n1st users universities, libraries, govts.\nBusiness sees commercial uses\nPCs, modems increase home use\nAccess to Internet\nTech. skills needed\nProblems for equality, democracy\nExpense of going online\nImp. of Internet to democratic society\nLibraries & schools: sites for widespread access\nLibraries & schools need to adapt, find money to go online\nThis informal outline helped Begay decide not to continue researching the history of the Internet or\nthe conflicts between commercial and public use (the first two sections) because they seemed likely\nto overwhelm his central concern, equal access to the Internet (last section).\nFormal outline\nUnlike an informal outline, a formal outline arranges ideas tightly and in considerable detail,\nwith close attention to hierarchy and phrasing. The example below shows the formal outline‘s\nformat and schematic content:\nI. First main idea\nA. First subordinate idea\n1. First evidence for subordinate idea\na. First detail of evidence\nb. Second detail of evidence\n2. Second evidence for subordinate idea\nB. Second subordinate idea\nII. Second main idea\nIn this model, levels of headings correspond to levels of importance or detail in the paper:\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 30\nMain ideas (Roman numerals) are the major divisions of the paper. Each\ndivision covers a part of your thesis statement and will probably take a number of\nparagraphs to develop.\nSubordinate ideas (capital letters) are the building blocks. These ideas are your own and\nwill probably take a paragraph or two to develop. They may serve as the topic sentences of\nparagraphs.\nEvidence (Arabic numerals) and its details (small letters) support each idea. The evidence\nfrom one or more sources will occupy much of each body paragraph. (A fourth sublevel, if\nneeded, is labeled with Arabic numerals enclosed in parentheses.)\nEach level of the outline is indented farther than the level it supports. (Your word processor may be\nable to help you with labels and indentions.)\nA formal outline can help you decide not only what your main ideas are and how you will\narrange them but also how you will support them. Some of this information may not emerge until\nyou are drafting, however, so remain open to revising the outline as you proceed. And consider\nusing a formal outline as a revision tool as well, creating a map of your completed first draft to\ncheck and improve the structure (see p. 49).\nTo be an effective organizer for your thoughts or an effective revision tool, a formal outline\nshould be detailed and should adhere to several principles of logical arrangement, clarity, balance,\nand completeness. (See pp. 35–38 for examples and more details.)\nThe outline should indicate which ideas are primary and which are subordinate. A long,\nundivided list of parallel items probably needs to be subdivided.\nParallel headings should represent ideas of equal importance and generality. They should\nnot overlap one another.\nSingle sublevels should be avoided. They illogically imply that something is divided into\nonly one part.\nA formal outline is usually written either in phrases (a topic outline) or in sentences (a\nsentence outline). A complete topic outline is illustrated on page 36. A complete sentence outline\naccompanies Edward Begay‘s research paper on pages 692–93. Either is suitable for a research\npaper, though a sentence outline, because it requires complete statements, conveys more\ninformation.\nEXERCISE 46.2 Creating a structure\nContinuing from Exercise 46.1 (p. 640), arrange your notes into a structure. As specified by your\ninstructor, make an informal outline or a formal sentence or topic outline to guide the drafting of your\npaper.\nDrafting the paper\nBeginning a draft of what will be a relatively long and complicated paper can be difficult, so it\nmay help to remember that you do not have to proceed methodically from beginning to end.\nWorking section by section\nIn writing a first draft, remember that a primary reason for doing a research paper is learning\nhow to interpret and evaluate the evidence in sources, draw your own conclusions from the\nevidence, and weave the two together in a way that establishes your expertise in your subject. The\nweaving will be easier if you view each principal idea of your paper (each Roman numeral of your\noutline) as a unit. Usually these principal ideas require a block of paragraphs to develop.\nCompose the units of your paper as if each will stand alone (though of course you will pull the\nunits together before your draft is complete).\nBegin each unit by stating the idea, which should be a conclusion you have drawn from\nreading and responding to your sources.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 31\nFollow the statement with specific support from your sources: facts and examples;\nsummaries, paraphrases, or quotations of secondary sources; quotations of passages from\nprimary sources with your analysis.\nPresent fairly any disagreements among experts. Give the evidence that leads you to side\nwith one expert or another.\nTry to remain open to new interpretations or new arrangements of ideas that occur to\nyou.\nNote Proceeding in this way will help you avoid a common trap of research writing: allowing\nyour sources to control you, rather than vice versa. Make sure each unit of your paper centers on an\nidea of your own, not someone else‘s, and that your paragraphs are pointed toward demonstrating\nthat idea, not merely presenting sources.\nTracking source citations\nAs you draft your paper, insert the source of each summary, paraphrase, and quotation in\nparentheses in the text—for instance, ―(Irving 64),‖ referring to page 64 in a work by Irving. If you\nare conscientious about inserting these notes and carrying them through successive drafts, you will\nbe less likely to plagiarize accidentally and you will have little difficulty documenting your sources\nin the final paper.\nEXERCISE 46.3 Drafting your paper\nDraft the research paper you have been developing in Chapters 42–45. Before beginning the draft, study\nyour research journal and your source information. While drafting, follow your thesis statement and\noutline as closely as you need to, but stay open to new ideas, associations, and arrangements.\nRevising and editing the paper\nWhen you have written a first draft, take a break for at least a day so that you can gain some\nobjectivity about your work and can read the draft critically when you begin to revise.\nRevising\nAlways revise your draft first, satisfying yourself with the content and shape of the whole\nbefore trying to edit sentences and words. For revision, begin with the advice and checklist on\npages 48–54, and supplement with the checklist below.\nEditing\nWhen you complete your revision—and only then—you are ready to edit. If you do not\ncompose on a computer, copy or retype the new draft if possible so that you have a clean copy to\nwork on. If you do compose on a computer, you can edit directly on screen or print a clean copy.\n(Some writers find it easier to spot errors on paper than on screen.)\nFor editing, consult the advice and checklist on pages 58–62. Try to read the paper from the\npoint of view of someone who has not spent hours planning and researching but instead has come\nfresh to the paper. Look for lapses in sense, awkward passages, wordiness, poor transitions between\nideas and evidence, unnecessary repetition, wrong or misspelled words, errors in grammar,\npunctuation, or mechanics—in short, anything that is likely to interfere with a reader‘s\nunderstanding of your meaning.\nCompleting source citations\nBefore you prepare your final draft (next section), you must\ninsert final source citations into your text and prepare the list of sources for the end of the paper.\nSee pages 635–37 on documenting sources in various disciplines‘ styles.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 32\nEXERCISE 46.4 Revising and editing your paper\nUsing the revision and editing checklists on pages 51 and 58–59 and the checklist and pointers here,\nrevise and edit your research paper. Work to improve not only the presentation of ideas but also, if\nnecessary, the ideas themselves. Make sure you have provided an in-text citation for every summary,\nparaphrase, and direct quotation of a source and that your list of sources is complete.\nPreparing and proofreading the final draft\nPrepare the final draft of your paper when you have edited the text, added the source citations,\nand written the list of works cited. Most instructors expect research papers to be neatly typed with\nclear titling, double spacing, standard margins, and minimal handwritten corrections. Your\ninstructor may have additional requirements, suggested by the discipline in which you are writing.\nThis book explains four such document formats:\nMLA format, used in English, foreign languages, and some other humanities, pages 656 and\n687–89.See also Chapter 48 for the sample research papers of Edward Begay and Vanessa\nHaley.\nChicago format, used in history, art history, religion, philosophy, and some other humanities,\npages 765–66 and 775–77.\nAPA format, used in psychology and other social sciences, pages 788–90 and 800–03.\nCSE format, used in the natural and applied sciences, pages 813–15 and 820.\nIn any discipline, you can use a word processor to present your ideas effectively and\nattractively with readable type fonts, headings, illustrations, and other elements. See Chapter 5 for\nideas and examples.\nBefore you submit your paper, proofread it carefully for typographical errors, misspellings,\nand other errors. (See p. 63 for proofreading tips.) Unless the errors are very numerous (more than\nseveral on a page), you can correct them by whiting out or crossing out (neatly) and inserting the\ncorrection (neatly) in ink. Don‘t let the pressure of a deadline prevent you from proofreading, for\neven minor errors can impair clarity or annoy readers and thus negate some of the hard work you\nhave put into your project.\nEXERCISE 46.5 Preparing and proofreading your final draft\nPrepare the final draft of your research paper, following your instructor‘s requirements for document\nformat. If your instructor does not specify a format, follow the MLA guidelines on pages 687–89.\nProofread and correct the paper before submitting it.\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 33\nVisit the companion Web site for more help on writing and revising a research paper.\nTips for drafting a research paper\nReread the assignment to review your instructor’s criteria. Make sure you have all the materials\nyou‘ll need to meet the criteria.\nWrite a quick two- or three-paragraph summary of what the paper will be about. This writing\nwill get your juices flowing and give you a sense of direction. (Pretend you‘re writing to a friend if\nthat will help loosen you up.) A version of the material you generate in this way may eventually prove\nuseful for your paper‘s introduction or conclusion.\nStart with the section of the paper you feel most confident about. At first, skip any parts that scare\nyou or give you undue trouble, even the introduction.\nWork in chunks, one unit or principal idea at a time. Fit the sections together only after you begin\nto see the draft take shape.\nCenter each section on an idea of your own. Use source material to back up the idea.\nTake great care in working with source material. Integrate sources into your own ideas (p. 623),\nand do not plagiarize (p. 629).\nInsert source citations into the draft as you quote, paraphrase, or summarize. Use authors‘\nnames and page numbers.\nChecklist for revising a research paper\nAssignment\nHow does the draft satisfy all of the criteria stated in your instructor‗s assignment?\nThesis statement\nHow well does your thesis statement describe your subject and your perspective as they emerged during\ndrafting?\nStructure\n(Outlining your draft as suggested on p. 642 can help you see structure at a glance.)\nHow consistently does borrowed material illuminate and support—not lead and dominate—your own\nideas? How well is the importance of ideas reflected in the emphasis they receive? Will the arrangement\nof ideas be clear to readers?\nEvidence\nWhere might readers need more evidence in order to accept your ideas? Where might the evidence seem\nweak or irrelevant?\nReasonableness and clarity\nHow reasonable will readers find your arguments? (See pp. 202–06.) Where do you need to define terms\nor concepts that readers may not know or may dispute?\nYou can download this checklist from ablongman.com/littlebrown. Copy the checklist for each research\npaper, answering the questions in writing.\n47\nCHAPTER\nUsing MLA Documentation\nand Format\nEnglish, foreign languages, and some other humanities use the\ndocumentation style and document format of the Modern Language\nAssociation, detailed in the MLA Handbook for Writers of Research\nPapers, 6th ed. (2003).\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 34\nMLA documentation style employs brief parenthetical citations within the text that direct\nreaders to a list of works cited at the end of the text. A parenthetical citation might look like this:\nOnly one article mentions this discrepancy (Wolfe 62).\nThe name Wolfe directs readers to the article by Wolfe in the list of works cited, and the page\nnumber 62 specifies the page in the article on which the cited material appears.\nThis chapter describes MLA style: what to include in a parenthetical citation (below), where to\nplace citations (p. 654), when to use footnotes or endnotes in addition to parenthetical citations (p.\n656), how to create the list of works cited (p. 656), and how to format the entire paper (p. 687).\nUsing MLA in-text citations\nWriting parenthetical text citations\nIn-text citations of sources must include just enough information for the reader to locate the\nfollowing:\nThe source in your list of works cited.\nThe place in the source where the borrowed material appears.\nFor any kind of source, you can usually meet both these requirements by providing the author‘s last\nname and (if the source uses them) the page numbers where the material appears. The reader can\nfind the source in your list of works cited and find the borrowed material in the source itself.\nNote For most sources, you will provide the author‘s or authors‘ last names and a page\nreference. Do not include the title unless you are citing more than one work by exactly the same\nauthor(s) or the source has no listed author (models 8 and 9, p. 651). The examples below cite a\nbook to which neither of these exceptions applies:\nNot One textbook discusses the ‘‘ethical dilemmas in public relations\npractice’’ (Wilcox, Ault, and Agee, Public Relations 125).\nNot One textbook discusses the ‘‘ethical dilemmas in public relations\npractice’’ (Public Relations 125).\nBut One textbook discusses the ‘‘ethical dilemmas in public relations\npractice’’ (Wilcox, Ault, and Agee 125).\n1. Author not named in your text\nWhen you have not already named the author in your sentence, provide the author‘s last name\nand the page number(s), with no punctuation between them, in parentheses.\nOne researcher concludes that ―women impose a distinctive construction on moral problems, seeing moral\ndilemmas in terms of conflicting responsibilities‖ (Gilligan 105-06).\nSee models 5 and 6 for the forms to use when the source does not provide page numbers.\n2. Author named in your text\nIf the author‘s name is already given with the material you‘re citing, you need not repeat it in\nthe parenthetical citation. The citation gives just the page number(s).\nCarol Gilligan concludes that ―women impose a distinctive construction on moral problems, seeing moral\ndilemmas in terms of conflicting responsibilities‖ (105-06).\n3. A work with two or three authors\nIf the source has two or three authors, give all their last names in the text or in the citation.\nSeparate two authors‘ names with and:\nAs Frieden and Sagalyn observe, ―The poor and the minorities were the\nleading victims of highway and renewal programs‖ (29).\nAccording to one study, ―The poor and the minorities were the leading\nvictims of highway and renewal programs‖ (Frieden and Sagalyn 29).\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 35\nWith three authors, add commas and also and before the final name:\nThe textbook by Wilcox, Ault, and Agee discusses the ―ethical dilemmas\nin public relations practice‖ (125).\nOne textbook discusses the ―ethical dilemmas in public relations practice‖\n(Wilcox, Ault, and Agee 125).\n4. A work with more than three authors\nIf the source has more than three authors, you may list all their last names or use only the first\nauthor‘s name followed by et al. (the abbreviation for the Latin et alii, ―and others‖). The choice\ndepends on what you do in your list of works cited (see p. 659).\nIt took the combined forces of the Americans, Europeans, and Japanese to break the rebel siege of\nBeijing in 1900 (Lopez et al. 362).\nIt took the combined forces of the Americans, Europeans, and Japanese to\nbreak the rebel siege of Beijing in 1900 (Lopez, Blum, Cameron, and\nBarnes 362).\n5. A work with numbered paragraphs or screens\ninstead of pages\nSome electronic sources number each paragraph or screen instead of each page. In citing\npassages in these sources, give the paragraph or screen number(s) and distinguish them from page\nnumbers: after the author‘s name, put a comma, a space, and par. (one paragraph), pars. (more\nthan one paragraph), screen, or screens.\nTwins reared apart report similar feelings (Palfrey, pars. 6-7).\n6. An entire work or a work with no page\nor other reference numbers\nWhen you cite an entire work rather than a part of it, you may omit any page or paragraph\nnumber. Try to work the author‘s name into your text, in which case you will not need a\nparenthetical citation. But remember that the source must appear in the list of works cited.\nBoyd deals with the need to acknowledge and come to terms with our fear of nuclear technology.\nUse the same format when you cite a specific passage from a work with no page, paragraph, or\nother reference numbers, such as an online source.\nIf the author‘s name does not appear in your text, put it in a parenthetical citation.\nAlmost 20 percent of commercial banks have been audited for the practice\n(Friis).\n7. A multivolume work\nIf you consulted only one volume of a multivolume work, your list of works cited will say so\n(see model 14 on p. 653), and you can treat the volume as any book.\nIf you consulted more than one volume of a multivolume work, give the appropriate volume in\nyour text citation.\nAfter issuing the Emancipation Proclamation, Lincoln said, ―What I did, I did after very full deliberations,\nand under a very heavy and solemn sense of responsibility‖ (5: 438).\nThe number 5 indicates the volume from which the quotation was taken; the number 438 indicates\nthe page number in that volume. When the author‘s name appears in such a citation, place it before\nthe volume number with no punctuation: (Lincoln 5: 438).\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 36\nIf you are referring generally to an entire volume of a multi-\nvolume work and are not citing specific page numbers, add the abbreviation vol. before the\nvolume number as in (vol. 5) or (Lincoln, vol. 5) (note the comma after the author‘s name).\nThen readers will not misinterpret the volume number as a page number.\n8. A work by an author of two or more cited works\nIf your list of works cited includes two or more works by the same author, then your citation\nmust tell the reader which of the author‘s works you are referring to. Give the title either in the text\nor in a parenthetical citation. In a parenthetical citation, give the full title only if it is brief;\notherwise, shorten the title to the first one, two, or three main words (excluding A, An, or The).\nAt about age seven, children begin to use appropriate gestures with their stories (Gardner, Arts 144-45).\nThe full title of Gardner‘s book is The Arts and Human Development (see the works-cited entry on\np. 661). This shortened title is underlined because the source is a book.\n9. An anonymous work\nFor a work with no named author or editor (whether an individual or an organization), use a\nfull or shortened version of the\ntitle, as explained above. In your list of works cited, you alphabetize an anonymous work by the\nfirst main word of the title (see p.\n662), so the first word of a shortened title should be the same.\nThe following citation refers to an unsigned source titled ―The Right to Die.‖ This shortened title is\nplaced in quotation marks because the source is a periodical article.\nOne article notes that a death-row inmate may demand his own execution to achieve a fleeting notoriety\n(―Right‖).\n(A page number may be omitted for this source because the article is only one page. See the entry\nfor the article on p. 668.)\nIf two or more anonymous works have the same title, distinguish them with additional\ninformation in the text citation, such as the publication date.\n10. A government publication or a work with\na corporate author\nSome works list as author a government body, association, committee, company, or other\ngroup. Cite such a work by the organization‘s name. If the name is long, work it into the text to\navoid an intrusive parenthetical citation.\nA 2005 report by the Hawaii Department of Education predicts an increase\nin enrollments (6).\n11. An indirect source\nWhen you want to use a quotation that is already in quotation marks—indicating that the\nauthor you are reading is quoting someone else—try to find the original source and quote directly\nfrom it. If you can‘t find the original source, then your citation must indicate that your quotation of\nit is indirect. In the following citation, qtd. in (―quoted in‖) says that Davino was quoted by\nBoyd:\nGeorge Davino maintains that ―even small children have vivid ideas about nuclear energy‖ (qtd. in Boyd\n22).\nThe list of works cited then includes only Boyd (the work consulted), not Davino.\n12. A literary work\nNovels, plays, and poems are often available in many editions, so your instructor may ask you\nto provide information that will help readers find the passage you cite no matter what edition they\nconsult.\nNovels: The page number comes first, followed by a semicolon and then information on the\nappropriate part or chapter of the work.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 37\nToward the end of James‘s novel, Maggie suddenly feels ―the thick breath of the definite—which was the\nintimate, the immediate, the familiar, as she\nhadn‘t had them for so long‖ (535; pt. 6, ch. 41).\nPoems that are not divided into parts: You may omit the page number and supply the line\nnumber(s) for the quotation. To prevent confusion with page numbers, precede the numbers\nwith line or lines in the first citation; then use just the numbers.\nIn Shakespeare‘s Sonnet 73 the speaker identifies with the trees of late autumn, ―Bare ruined choirs,\nwhere late the sweet birds sang‖ (line 4). ―In me,‖ Shakespeare writes, ―thou seest the glowing of such fire\n/ That on the ashes of his youth doth lie . . .‖ (9-10).\n(See pp. 752–54 for a sample paper on a poem.)\nVerse plays and poems that are divided into parts: Omit a page number and cite the\nappropriate part—act (and scene, if any), canto, book, and so on—plus the line number(s).\nUse Arabic numerals for parts, including acts and scenes (3.4), unless your instructor\nspecifies Roman numerals (III.iv).\nLater in King Lear the disguised Edgar says, ―The prince of darkness is a gentleman‖ (3.4.147).\n(See pp. 756–59 for a sample paper on a verse play.)\nProse plays: Provide the page number followed by the act and scene, if any. See the reference\nto Death of a Salesman on page 655.\n13. The Bible\nWhen you cite passages of the Bible in parentheses, abbreviate the title of any book longer\nthan four letters—for instance, Gen. (Genesis), 1 Sam. (1 Samuel), Ps. (Psalms), Prov.\n(Proverbs), Matt. (Mat-thew), Rom. (Romans). Then give the chapter and verse(s) in Arabic\nnumerals.\nAccording to the Bible, at Babel God ―did . . . confound the language of all\nthe earth‖ (Gen. 11.9).\n14. An electronic source\nCite an electronic source as you would any other source: usually by author‘s name or, if there\nis no author, by title.\nBusiness forecasts for the fourth quarter tended to be optimistic (White 4).\nThis example cites a source with page numbers. For a source with paragraph or screen numbers or\nno numbering, see models 5 and 6 (p. 650).\n15. Two or more works in the same citation\nIf you use a single parenthetical citation to refer to more than one work, separate the\nreferences with a semicolon.\nTwo recent articles point out that a computer badly used can be less efficient than no computer at all\n(Gough and Hall 201; Richards 162).\nSince long citations in the text can distract the reader, you may choose to cite several or more\nworks in an endnote or footnote rather than in the text. See page 656.\nPositioning and punctuating parenthetical citations\nThe following guidelines will help you place and punctuate text citations to distinguish between\nyour and your sources‘ ideas and to make your own text readable. See also pages 623–28 on\nediting quotations and using signal phrases to integrate source material into your sentences.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 38\nWhere to place citations\nPosition text citations to accomplish two goals:\nMake it clear exactly where your borrowing begins and ends.\nKeep the citation as unobtrusive as possible.\nYou can accomplish both goals by placing the parenthetical citation at the end of the sentence\nelement containing the borrowed material. This sentence element may be a phrase or a clause, and\nit may begin, interrupt, or conclude the sentence. Usually, as in the following examples, the\nelement ends with a punctuation mark.\nThe inflation rate might climb as high as 30 percent (Kim 164), an\nincrease that could threaten the small nation‘s stability.\nThe inflation rate, which might climb as high as 30 percent (Kim 164),\ncould threaten the small nation‘s stability.\nThe small nation‘s stability could be threatened by its inflation rate, which,\none source predicts, might climb as high as 30 percent (Kim 164).\nIn the last example the addition of one source predicts clarifies that Kim is responsible only\nfor the inflation-rate prediction, not for the statement about stability.\nWhen your paraphrase or summary of a source runs longer than a sentence,\nclarify the boundaries by using the author‘s name in the first sentence and\nplacing the parenthetical citation at the end of the last sentence.\nJuliette Kim studied the effects of acutely high inflation in several South\nAmerican and African countries since World War II. She discovered that a\nmajor change in government accompanied or followed the inflationary\nperiod in 56 percent of cases (22-23).\nWhen you cite two or more sources in the same paragraph, position authors‘ names and\nparenthetical citations so that readers can see who said what. In the following example, the\nbeginnings and ends of sentences clearly mark the different sources:\nFor some time, schools have been using computers extensively for\ndrill-and-practice exercises, in which students repeat specific skills such as\nspelling words, using the multiplication facts, or, at a higher level, doing\nchemistry problems. But many education experts criticize such exercises\nfor boring students and failing to engage their critical thinking and\ncreativity. Jane M. Healy, a noted educational psychologist and teacher,\ntakes issue with ―interactive‖ software for children as well as drill-and-\npractice software, arguing that ―some of the most popular ‗educational‘\nsoftware . . . may be damaging to independent thinking, attention, and\nmotivation‖ (20). Another education expert, Harold Wenglinsky of the\nEducational Testing Service, found in a well-regarded 1998 study that\nfourth and eighth graders who used computers frequently, including for\ndrill and practice, actually did worse on tests than their peers who used\ncomputers less often (Does It Compute? 21). In a later article, Wenglinsky\nconcludes that ―the quantity of use matters far less than the quality of use.‖\nIn schools, he says, high-quality computer work, involving critical\nthinking, is still rare (―In Search‖ 17).\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 39\nHow to punctuate citations\nGenerally place a parenthetical citation before any punctuation required by your sentence. If\nthe borrowed material is a quotation, place the citation between the closing quotation mark and the\npunctuation:\nSpelling argues that during the 1970s American automobile manufacturers met consumer needs ―as well\nas could be expected‖ (26), but not everyone agrees with him.\nThe exception is a quotation ending in a question mark or exclamation point. Then use the\nappropriate punctuation inside the closing quotation mark, and follow the quotation with the text\ncitation and a period:\n―Of what use is genius,‖ Emerson asks, ―if the organ . . . cannot find a focal distance within the actual\nhorizon of human life?‖ (―Experience‖ 60). Mad\ngenius is no genius.\nWhen a citation appears at the end of a quotation set off from the text, place it one space after\nthe punctuation ending the quotation. Do not use additional punctuation with the citation or\nquotation marks around the quotation:\nIn Arthur Miller‘s Death of a Salesman, the most poignant defense of Willie Loman comes from his wife,\nLinda:\nHe‘s not the finest character that ever lived. But he‘s a human\nbeing, and a terrible thing is happening to him. So attention\nmust be paid. He‘s not to be allowed to fall into his grave like\nan old dog. Attention, attention must finally be paid to such a\nperson. (56; act 1)\n(This citation of a play includes the act number as well as the page number. See p. 653.)\nSee the two sample research papers starting on pages 690 and 725 for further examples of\nplacing parenthetical references in relation to summaries, paraphrases, and quotations.\nUsing footnotes or endnotes in special\ncircumstances\nOccasionally, you may want to use footnotes or endnotes in place of parenthetical citations. If\nyou need to refer to several sources at once, listing them in a long parenthetical citation could be\nintrusive. In that case, signal the citation with a numeral raised above the appropriate line of text\nand write a note with the same numeral to cite the sources:\nText At least five studies have confirmed these results.1\nNote 1 Abbott and Winger 266-68; Casner 27; Hoyenga 78-79;\nMarino 36; Tripp, Tripp, and Walk 179-83.\nYou may also use a footnote or endnote to comment on a source or provide information that\ndoes not fit easily in the text:\nText So far, no one has confirmed these results.2\nNote 2 Manter reports spending a year trying to replicate the ex-\nperiment, but he was never able to produce the high temperatures\n(616).\nIn a note, the raised numeral is indented five spaces or one-half inch and is followed by a\nspace. If the note appears as a footnote, place it at the bottom of the page on which the citation\nappears, set it off from the text with quadruple spacing, and single-space the note itself. If the note\nappears as an endnote, place it in numerical order with the other endnotes on a page between the\ntext and the list of works cited. Double-space all the endnotes. (See pp. 718–19 for examples of\nendnotes and the format to use in typing a page of endnotes.)\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 40\nPreparing the MLA list of works cited\nIn the documentation style of the MLA Handbook, your in-text parenthetical citations\n(discussed in 47a) refer the reader to complete information on your sources in a list you title Works\nCited\nand place at the end of your paper. The list should include all the sources you quoted, paraphrased,\nor summarized in your paper. (If your instructor asks you to include sources you examined but did\nnot cite, title the list Works Consulted.)\nFollow this format for the list of works cited:\nArrange your sources in alphabetical order by the last name of the author. If an author is\nnot given in the source, alphabetize the source by the first main word of the title (excluding A,\nAn, or The).\nType the entire list double-spaced, both within and between entries.\nIndent the second and subsequent lines of each entry one-half inch or five spaces from the\nleft. Your word processor can format this so-called hanging indent automatically. First\nhighlight the material to be indented. Then, on the Format menu, click Paragraph and then\neither Hanging Indent (in Word-\nPerfect) or Special: Hanging (in Microsoft Word).\nFor complete lists of works cited, see the papers by Edward Begay (p. 720) and Vanessa Haley (p. 730).\nThe box on the next two pages directs you to the MLA formats for works-cited entries. Use\nyour best judgment in adapting the models to your particular sources. If you can‘t find a model that\nexactly matches a source you used, locate and follow the closest pos-sible match. You will certainly\nneed to combine formats—for instance, drawing on model 2 (―A book with two or three authors‖)\nand model 26 (―An article in a daily newspaper‖) for a newspaper article with two authors.\nListing books\nThe next page shows the basic format for a book and the location of the required information\nin a book. When other information is required, it usually falls either between the author‘s name and\nthe title or between the title and the publication information, as in the following models.\n1. A book with one author\nGilligan, Carol. In a Different Voice: Psychological Theory and Women‘s Development. Cambridge:\nHarvard UP, 1982.\n2. A book with two or three authors\nLifton, Robert Jay, and Greg Mitchell. Who Owns Death: Capital Punishment, the American Conscience,\nand the End of Executions. New York: Morrow, 2000.\nWilcox, Dennis L., Phillip H. Ault, and Warren K. Agee. Public Relations: Strategies and Tactics. 6th ed.\nNew York: Irwin, 2005.\nGive the authors‘ names in the order provided on the title page. Reverse the first and last names of\nthe first author only, not of any other authors. Separate two authors‘ names with a comma and and;\nseparate three authors‘ names with commas and with and before the third name.\n3. A book with more than three authors\nLopez, Geraldo, Judith P. Salt, Anne Ming, and Henry Reisen. China and the West. Boston: Little, 2004.\nLopez, Geraldo, et al. China and the West. Boston: Little, 2004.\nYou may, but need not, give all authors‘ names if the work has more than three authors. If you\nchoose not to give all names, provide the name of the first author only, and follow the name with a\ncomma and the abbreviation et al. (for the Latin et alii, meaning ―and others‖).\n4. Two or more works by the same author(s)\nGardner, Howard. The Arts and Human Development. New York: Wiley, 1973.\n---. The Quest for Mind: Piaget, Lévi-Strauss, and the Structuralist Movement. New York: Knopf, 1973.\nGive the author‘s name only in the first entry. For the second and any subsequent works by the\nsame author, substitute three hyphens for the author‘s name, followed by a period. (If you are citing\ntwo\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 41\nor more works by the same editor, editors, or translator, follow the hyphens with a comma and ed.,\neds., or trans. as appropriate. See models 5, 6, and 7.) Note that the three hyphens stand for\nexactly the same name or names. If the second Gardner source were by Gardner and somebody else,\nboth names would have to be given in full.\nPlace an entry or entries using three hyphens immediately after the entry that names the\nauthor. Within the set of entries by the same author, arrange the sources alphabetically by the first\nmain word of the title, as in the Gardner examples (Arts, then Quest).\n5. A book with an editor\nHolland, Merlin, and Rupert Hart-Davis, eds. The Complete Letters of Oscar Wilde. New York: Holt,\n2000.\nHandle editors‘ names like authors‘ names (models 1–3), but add a comma and the abbreviation\ned. (one editor) or eds. (two or more editors) after the last editor‘s name.\n6. A book with an author and an editor\nMumford, Lewis. The City in History. Ed. Donald L. Miller. New York: Pantheon, 1986.\nWhen citing the work of the author, give his or her name first,\nand give the editor‘s name after the title, preceded by Ed. (singular only, meaning ―Edited by‖).\nWhen citing the work of the editor,\nuse model 5 for a book with an editor, adding By and the author‘s name after the title: Miller,\nDonald L., ed. The City in History. By Lewis\nMumford.\n7. A translation\nAlighieri, Dante. The Inferno. Trans. John Ciardi. New York: NAL, 1971.\nWhen citing the work of the author, give his or her name first, and give the translator‘s name after\nthe title, preceded by Trans. (―Translated by‖). When citing the work of the translator, give his or\nher name first, followed by a comma and trans.; then follow the title\nwith By and the author‘s name: Ciardi, John, trans. The Inferno. By Dante\nAlighieri.\nWhen a book you cite by author has a translator and an editor, give the translator‘s and\neditor‘s names in the order used on the book‘s title page.\n8. A book with a corporate author\nLorenz Research, Inc. Research in Social Studies Teaching. Baltimore:\nArrow, 2000.\nCorporate authors include associations, committees, institutions, gov-ernment bodies, companies,\nand other groups. List the name of the group as author when a source gives only that name and not\nan individual‘s.\n9. An anonymous book\nThe Dorling Kindersley World Reference Atlas. London: Dorling, 2005.\nList a book that names no author—neither an individual nor a group—by its full title. Alphabetize\nthe book by the title‘s first main word (here Dorling), excluding A, An, or The.\n10. The Bible\nThe Bible. King James Version.\nThe New English Bible. London: Oxford UP and Cambridge UP, 1970.\nWhen citing a standard version of the Bible (first example), do not underline the title or the name\nof the version, and you need not\nprovide publication information. For an edition of the Bible (second example), underline the title\nand give full publication information.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 42\n11. A later edition\nBolinger, Dwight L. Aspects of Language. 3rd ed. New York: Harcourt, 1981.\nFor any edition after the first, place the edition number after the title. (If an editor‘s name follows\nthe title, place the edition number after the name. See model 18.) Use the appropriate designation\nfor editions that are named or dated rather than numbered—for instance, Rev. ed. for ―Revised\nedition.‖\n12. A republished book\nJames, Henry. The Golden Bowl. 1904. London: Penguin, 1966.\nRepublished books include paperbound editions of books originally released in hard bindings and\nbooks reissued under new titles. Place the original date of publication (but not the place of\npublication or the publisher‘s name) after the title, and then provide the full publication\ninformation for the source you are using. If the book was originally published under a different\ntitle, add this title after Rpt. of (―Reprint of‖) at the end of the entry and move the origi-nal\npublication date to follow the title—for example, Rpt. of Thomas Hardy: A Life. 1941.\n13. A book with a title in its title\nEco, Umberto. Postscript to The Name of the Rose. Trans. William Weaver. New York: Harcourt, 1983.\nWhen a book‘s title contains another book title (as here: The Name of the Rose), do not underline\nthe second title. When a book‘s title contains a quotation or the title of a work normally placed in\nquo-tation marks, keep the quotation marks and underline both titles: Critical Response to\nHenry James’s ‘‘The Beast in the Jungle.’’ (Note that the underlining extends under\nthe closing quotation mark.)\n14. A work in more than one volume\nLincoln, Abraham. The Collected Works of Abraham Lincoln. Ed. Roy P. Basler. 8 vols. New Brunswick:\nRutgers UP, 1953.\nLincoln, Abraham. The Collected Works of Abraham Lincoln. Ed. Roy P. Basler. Vol. 5. New\nBrunswick: Rutgers UP, 1953. 8 vols.\nIf you use two or more volumes of a multivolume work, give the work‘s total number of volumes\nbefore the publication information (8 vols. in the first example). Your text citation will indicate\nwhich volume you are citing (see pp. 650–51). If you use only one volume, give that volume\nnumber before the publication information (Vol. 5 in the second example). You may add the total\nnumber of volumes to the end of the entry (8 vols. in the second example).\nIf you cite a multivolume work published over a period of years, give the inclusive years as\nthe publication date: for instance, Cambridge: Harvard UP, 1978-90.\n15. A work in a series\nBergman, Ingmar. The Seventh Seal. Mod. Film Scripts Ser. 12. New York:\nSimon, 1995.\nPlace the name of the series (not quoted or underlined) just before the publication information.\nAbbreviate common words such as modern and series. Add any series number after the series title.\n16. Published proceedings of a conference\nWatching Our Language: A Conference Sponsored by the Program in Architecture and Design Criticism.\n6-8 May 2005. New York: Parsons School of Design, 2005.\nWhether in or after the title of the conference, supply information about who sponsored the\nconference, when it was held, and who published the proceedings. Treat a particular presentation at\nthe conference like a selection from an anthology (model 18).\n17. An anthology\nKennedy, X. J., and Dana Gioia, eds. Literature: An Introduction to Fiction,\nPoetry, and Drama. 9th ed. New York: Longman, 2005.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 43\nCite an entire anthology only when citing the work of the editor or editors or when your instructor\npermits cross-referencing like that shown in model 19. Give the name of the editor or editors\n(followed by ed. or eds.) and then the title of the anthology.\n18. A selection from an anthology\nMason, Bobbie Ann. ―Shiloh.‖ Literature: An Introduction to Fiction, Poetry, and Drama. Ed. X. J.\nKennedy and Dana Gioia. 9th ed. New York: Longman, 2005. 643-54.\nThe essentials of this listing are these: author of selection; title of selection (in quotation marks);\ntitle of anthology (underlined); editors‘ names preceded by Ed. (meaning ―Edited by‖); publication\ninformation for the anthology; and inclusive page numbers for the selection (without the\nabbreviation ―pp.‖). In addition, this source requires an edition number for the anthology. If you\nwish, you may also supply the original date of publication for the work you are citing, after its title.\nSee model 12 on page 662.\nIf the work you cite comes from a collection of works by one author and with no editor, use\nthe following form:\nAuden, W. H. ―Family Ghosts.‖ The Collected Poetry of W. H. Auden. New York: Random, 1945. 132-\n33.\nIf the work you cite is a scholarly article that was previously printed elsewhere, provide the\ncomplete information for the earlier publication of the piece, followed by Rpt. in (―Reprinted\nin‖) and the information for the source in which you found the piece:\nMolloy, Francis C. ―The Suburban Vision in John O‘Hara‘s Short Stories.‖\nCritique: Studies in Modern Fiction 25.2 (1984): 101-13. Rpt. in Short Story Criticism: Excerpts\nfrom Criticism of the Works of Short Fiction Writers. Ed. David Segal. Vol. 15. Detroit: Gale,\n1989. 287-92.\nSan Juan, E. ―Theme Versus Imitation: D. H. Lawrence‘s ‗The Rocking-Horse Winner.‘‖ D. H. Lawrence\nReview 3 (1970): 136-40. Rpt. in From Fiction to Film: D. H. Lawrence‘s ―The Rocking-Horse\nWinner.‖ Ed. Gerald R.\nBarrett and Thomas L. Erskine. Dickenson Literature and Film Ser.\nEncino: Dickenson, 1974. 70-74.\n19. Two or more selections from the same anthology\nChopin, Kate. ―The Storm.‖ Kennedy and Gioia 127-31.\nKennedy, X. J., and Dana Gioia, eds. Literature: An Introduction to Fiction,\nPoetry, and Drama. 9th ed. New York: Longman, 2005.\nO‘Connor, Flannery. ―Revelation.‖ Kennedy and Gioia 443-58.\nWhen you are citing more than one selection from the same source, your instructor may allow you\nto avoid repetition by giving the source in full (as in the Kennedy and Gioia entry) and then simply\ncross-referencing it in entries for the works you used. Thus, instead of full information for the\nChopin and O‘Connor works, give Kennedy and Gioia and the appropriate pages in that book.\nNote that each entry appears in its proper alphabetical place among other works cited.\n20. An introduction, preface, foreword, or afterword\nDonaldson, Norman. Introduction. The Claverings. By Anthony Trollope. New York: Dover, 1977. vii-xv.\nAn introduction, foreword, or afterword is often written by someone other than the book‘s author.\nWhen citing such a piece, give its name without quotation marks or underlining. (If the piece has a\ntitle of its own, provide it, in quotation marks, between the name of the author and the name of the\npiece.) Follow the title of the book with its author‘s name preceded by By. Give the inclusive page\nnumbers of the part you cite. (In the preceding example, the small Roman numerals refer to the\nfront matter of the book, before page 1.)\nWhen the author of a preface or introduction is the same as the author of the book, give only\nthe last name after the title:\nGould, Stephen Jay. Prologue. The Flamingo‘s Smile: Reflections in Natural\nHistory. By Gould. New York: Norton, 1985. 13-20.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 44\n21. An article in a reference work\nMark, Herman F. ―Polymers.‖ The New Encyclopaedia Britannica: Macropaedia. 15th ed. 1991.\n―Reckon.‖ Merriam-Webster‘s Collegiate Dictionary. 11th ed. 2003.\nList an article in a reference work by its title (second example) unless the article is signed (first\nexample). For works with entries arranged alphabetically, you need not include volume or page\nnumbers. For well-known works like those listed on the previous page, you may also omit the\neditors‘ names and all publication information except any edition number and the year of\npublication. For works that are not well known, give full publication information:\n―Hungarians in America.‖ The Ethnic Almanac. Ed. Stephanie Bernardo. New York: Doubleday, 2001.\n109-11.\nSee also pages 677and 681,respectively, for encyclopedias appearing online or on a CD-ROM.\nListing periodicals: Journals, magazines, and newspapers\nThe facing page shows the basic format for an article in a periodical (a journal) and the\nlocation of the required information in a journal. See page 669for parallel information on a\nnewspaper\narticle.\nNote The treatment of volume and issue numbers and publication dates varies depending on\nthe kind of periodical being cited, as the models indicate. For the distinction between journals and\nmagazines, see page 582.\n22. An article in a journal with continuous pagination throughout the\nannual volume\nLever, Janet. ―Sex Differences in the Games Children Play.‖ Social\nProblems 23 (1996): 478-87.\nSome journals number the pages of issues consecutively throughout a year, so that each issue after\nthe first in a year begins numbering where the previous issue left off—say, at page 132 or 416. For\nthis kind of journal, give the volume number after the title (23 in the example above) and place the\nyear of publication in parentheses. The page numbers will be enough to guide readers to the issue\nyou\nused.\n23. An article in a journal that pages issues separately\nor that numbers only issues, not volumes\nSelwyn, Neil. ―The Social Processes of Learning to Use Computers.― Social\nScience Computer Review 23.1 (2005): 122-35.\nSome journals page each issue separately (starting each issue at page 1). For these journals, give\nthe volume number, a period, and the issue number (23.1 in the Selwyn entry above and opposite).\nWhen citing an article in a journal that numbers only issues, not annual volumes, treat the issue\nnumber as if it were a volume number, as in model 22.\n24. An article in a monthly or bimonthly magazine\nGarber, Marjorie. ―Our Genius Problem.‖ Atlantic Monthly Sept. 2002: 46-53.\nFollow the magazine title with the month and the year of publication. (Abbreviate all months\nexcept May, June, and July.) Don‘t place the date in parentheses, and don‘t provide a volume or\nissue number.\n25. An article in a weekly or biweekly magazine\nTalbot, Margaret. ―The Bad Mother.‖ New Yorker 5 Aug. 2004: 40-46.\nFollow the magazine title with the day, the month, and the year of publication. (Abbreviate all\nmonths except May, June, and July.) Don‘t place the date in parentheses, and don‘t provide a\nvolume or issue number.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 45\n26. An article in a daily newspaper\nZeller, Tom, Jr. ―To Go Global, Do You Ignore Censorship?‖ New York Times\n24 Oct. 2005, natl. ed.: C3+.\nSee the facing page for an analysis of this entry and the location of the required information in the\nnewspaper.\n27. An anonymous article\n―The Right to Die.‖ Time 11 Oct. 1996: 101.\nFor an article with no named author, begin the entry with the title of the article. In the list of works\ncited, alphabetize an anonymous source by the first main word of the title (Right in this model).\n28. An editorial or letter to the editor\n―Dualing Power Centers.‖ Editorial. New York Times 14 Jan. 2005, natl. ed.: A16.\nAdd the word Editorial or Letter after the title if there is one or after the author‘s name, as\nfollows:\nDowding, Michael. Letter. Economist 5-11 Jan. 2005: 4.\n(The numbers 5-11 in this entry are the publication days of the periodical: the issue spans January\n5 through 11.)\n29. A review\nNelson, Cary. ―Between Anonymity and Celebrity.‖ Rev. of Anxious Intellects: Academic Professionals,\nPublic Intellectuals, and Enlightenment Values, by John Michael. College English 64 (2002): 710-\n19.\nRev. is an abbreviation for ―Review.‖ The name of the author of the work being reviewed follows\nthe title of the work, a comma, and by. If the review has no title of its own, then Rev. of and the\ntitle of the reviewed work immediately follow the name of the reviewer.\n30. An abstract of a dissertation or article\nSteciw, Steven K. ―Alterations to the Pessac Project of Le Corbusier.‖ Diss. U of Cambridge, England,\n1986. DAI 46 (1986): 565C.\nFor an abstract appearing in Dissertation Abstracts (DA) or Disser-tation Abstracts International\n(DAI), give the author‘s name and the title, Diss. (for ―Dissertation‖), the institution granting the\nauthor‘s degree, the date of the dissertation, and the publication informa-tion. See page 683for\nlisting an entire dissertation rather than an abstract.\nFor an abstract of an article, first provide the publication information for the article itself,\nfollowed by the information for the abstract. If the abstract publisher lists abstracts by item rather\nthan page number, add item before the number.\nLever, Janet. ―Sex Differences in the Games Children Play.‖ Social Problems 23 (1996): 478-87.\nPsychological Abstracts 63 (1996): item 1431.\nNote Most instructors expect you to consult and cite full articles, not abstracts. See page 586.\nListing electronic sources\nElectronic sources include those you find online (either through the library Web site or\ndirectly over the Internet) and those you find on CD-ROM (see p. 681). The following list, adapted\nfrom the MLA Handbook, itemizes the possible elements of an online source, in order of their\nappearance in a works-cited entry. No source will include all the elements.\n1. Name of author, editor, compiler, or translator, arranged and punctuated as in models 1–3,\npage 659. Use ed., comp., or trans. after the name as appropriate, as shown in models 5\nand 7, page 661.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 46\n2. Title of a short work, in quotation marks. Short works include poems, articles, documents or\npages on a Web site, Web log entries, and postings to discussion groups. (Follow the last with\nOnline posting.)\n3. Title of a book, underlined.\n4. Name of editor, compiler, or translator of the source, if not cited before, preceded by Ed.,\nComp., or Trans. as appropriate. See models 6 and 7, page 661.\n5. Publication information for any print version of the source, following earlier models for\nbooks and periodical articles. For a periodical article, the publication information includes the\nperiodical title.\n6. Title of the online site, underlined. The title might be that of a periodical (if not already\ngiven), a scholarly project, a database, a Web log, and so on. For a site with no title, add Home\npage, Course home page, or another description.\n7. Name of site editor, if any, preceded by Ed.\n8. Version number, if any, or volume/issue numbers for an on-line journal. See models 22\nand 23, page 666, for journals.\n9. Date of electronic publication, latest revision, or posting.\n10.Title of a subscription database, name of the subscription service, and name and location of\nthe subscriber.\n11.Title of a discussion group.\n12.Inclusive page numbers, number of paragraphs, or other identifying numbers, if any.\n13.Name of site sponsor, such as an institution or organization, if not cited before.\n14.Date you consulted the source.\n15.URL of the source. To ensure the accuracy of URLs, use Copy and Paste to duplicate them in a\nfile or an e-mail to yourself. In the list of works cited, break URLs only after slashes—do not\nhyphenate. Unless you are submitting your paper online, use the Tools menu of your word\nprocessor to eliminate hyperlinks in works-cited entries (click on AutoCorrect in Microsoft\nWord, Settings in WordPerfect).\nNote A URL does not always provide a usable route to a source, especially with subscription\nservices. See models 31 and 32 below and on the next page.\n31. A work from an online service to which\nyour library subscribes\nGorski, Paul C. ―Privilege and Repression in the Digital Era: Rethinking the\nSociopolitics of the Digital Divide.‖ Race, Gender and Class 10.4 (2003): 145-76. Ethnic\nNewsWatch. ProQuest. U of Minnesota, Twin Cities,\nWilson Lib. 24 July 2005 <http://proquest.umi.com>.\nSee the next page for an analysis of the preceding entry and the\nlocation of the required information on the subscription-service screen.\nNote Many subscription services provide source URLs that are temporary, specific to the\nlibrary, or too long to copy with certain accuracy. If any of these applies to the URL of the source\nyou‘re consulting, you can use the service‘s home page URL instead or omit a URL. Some services\nprovide a ―Permanent link‖ or ―Document URL‖ on each source record: a URL for finding the\nsource from within the library‘s system (not from the open Web). You can see such a link on the\ndatabase screen on the next page. Like the one in the example, permanent links are often\nunmanageably long and complex. In that case, use the service‘s home-page URL, which runs\nthrough com in the permanent link.\n32. A work from an online service to which you subscribe\n―China—Dragon Kings.‖ The Encyclopedia Mythica. America Online. 6 Jan. 2005. Path: Research and\nLearn; Encyclopedia; More Encyclopedias;\nEncyclopedia Mythica.\nIf you find a source through America Online, MSN, or another personal online service, you may\nnot see a usable URL or any URL for the source. In that case, provide the path you used to get to\nthe source, as in the preceding example: 1 Title of source, in quotation marks, and title of larger\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 47\nwork, underlined. 2 Name of the service, neither underlined nor quoted. 3 Date of your access,\nfollowed by\na period. 4 Path: and the sequence of topics required to reach the source, with the topics\nseparated by semicolons.\nIf you used a keyword instead of a path to reach the source, give that information instead:\nKeyword: Chinese dragon kings.\n33. An entire online site (scholarly project, professional site, personal\nsite, etc.)\nA scholarly project or professional site:\nAmerican Verse Project. 16 May 2001. U of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative. 21 July 2005\n<http://www.hti.umich.edu/a/amverse>.\nWhen citing a scholarly project or professional site, include the following: 1 Title of the site,\nunderlined. 2 Date of publication or\nmost recent update. 3 Name of any organization or institution that sponsors the site. 4 Date of\nyour access. 5 URL. If the project or site has an editor, add the name, preceded by Ed., between\nthe site title and the publication date. See the Conrad entry on the next page.\nA personal site:\nLederman, Leon. Topics in Modern Physics—Lederman. 28 Aug. 2005.\n12 Dec. 2005 <http://www-ed.fnal.gov/samplers/hsphys/people/\nlederman.html>.\nCite a personal site with this information: 1 Author‘s name, if any. 2 Title, if any, underlined. If\nthe site has no title, describe it with\na label such as Home page, without quotation marks or underlining.\n3 Date of last revision. 4 Date of your access. 5 URL.\nA business site:\nPrius. 2006. Toyota Motor Corp. 2 Feb. 2006 <http://www.toyota.com/prius>.\nFor the site of a corporation or other business, give the following:\n1 Site title, underlined. 2 Date of site. 3 Name of sponsoring business. 4 Date of your\naccess. 5 URL.\n34. A poem, essay, or other short work from an online site\nWheatley, Phillis. ―On Virtue.‖ Poems on Various Subjects, Religious and Moral. London: A. Bell, 1773.\nAmerican Verse Project. 16 May 2001. U of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative. 21 July 2005\n<http://\nname.umdl.umich.edu/BAP5379>.\nSee the facing page for an analysis of the preceding entry and the location of the required\ninformation on the Web site.\n35. An online book\nAn entire book:\nAusten, Jane. Emma. 1816. Ed. R. W. Chapman. Oxford: Clarendon, 1926. Oxford Text Archive. 1994.\nOxford U. 15 Dec. 2005 <http://ota.ahds.ac.uk/ Austen/Emma.1519>.\nFor a book published online, give the following information: 1 Author and title. 2 Date of\noriginal publication of the book, if not given in item 4. 3 Name of any editor or translator. 4\nAny publication information for the original print version of the book, following one of models 1–\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 48\n17. 5 Title of the site, underlined. 6 Date of electronic publication. 7 Name of any sponsoring\norganization or institution. 8 Date of your access. 9 URL for the book. If the site has an editor,\nadd the name after the site‘s title, as below.\nA part of a book:\nConrad, Joseph. ―A Familiar Preface.‖ Modern Essays. Ed. Christopher Morley. New York: Harcourt,\n1921. Bartleby.com: Great Books Online. Ed. Steven van Leeuwan. Nov. 2000. 16 Feb. 2006\n<http://www.bartleby.com/237/ 8.html>.\nFor a part of a book published online, provide this information:\n1 Author of the part. 2 Title of the part, in quotation marks. (Do not use quotation marks for\nIntroduction, Foreword, or another standard part.) 3 Title of the book (underlined), editor of\nthe book (if any), and publication information for the print version of the book. 4 Title of the site\n(underlined) and editor of the site (if any). 5 Date of electronic publication. 6 Date of your\naccess. 7 URL for the part of the book. If the site as a whole has a sponsoring organization, give\nthe name between items 5 and 6, as in the Austen model above.\n36. An article in an online journal\nPalfrey, Andrew. ―Choice of Mates in Identical Twins.‖ Modern Psychology 4.1 (2003): 26-40. 25 Feb.\n2006 <http://www.liasu.edu/modpsy/\npalfrey4(1).htm>.\nGive the following information for an online scholarly article that you reach directly: 1 Author,\narticle title, journal title, volume and any issue numbers, and publication date, as in model 22 or 23\non page 666. 2 Page numbers in the journal or total number of pages, paragraphs, or sections, if\nany of these are given. Omit reference numbers if the source does not use them. 3 Date of your\naccess. 4 URL for the article.\nFor a journal article reached through a subscription service, see model 31 (pp. 671–72).\n37. An online abstract\nPalfrey, Andrew. ―Choice of Mates in Identical Twins.‖ Modern Psychology 4.1 (2003): 26-40. Abstract.\n25 Feb. 2006 <http://www.liasu.edu/modpsy/ abstractpalfrey4(1).htm>.\nTreat an online abstract like an online journal article (model 36), but add ―Abstract‖ (without\nquotation marks or underlining) between the publication information and the date of your access.\n38. An article in an online newspaper or on a newswire\nStill, Lucia. ―On the Battlefields of Business, Millions of Casualties.‖ New York Times on the Web 3\nMar. 2005. 17 Aug. 2005 <http://www.nytimes.com/ specials/downsize/05down1.html>.\nProvide the following information for an online newspaper article that you reach directly: 1\nAuthor, article title, newspaper title, and publication date as in model 26 (p. 668). Give section,\npage, or\nparagraph numbers if the newspaper does. 2 Date of your access. 3 URL for the article.\nTreat a newswire article similarly, substituting the title of the online wire service for the\nnewspaper title (this article has no named author):\n―Film, Fashion Asked to Stop Glamorizing Smoking.‖ Reuters 18 Feb.\n2003. 28 Feb. 2003 <http://www.reuters.com/\nnewsArticle.jhtml?type=industryNewsID2246811>.\nSee model 31 (pp. 671–72) when citing a newspaper or newswire article that you reached\nthrough a subscription service.\n39. An article in an online magazine\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 49\nLewis, Ricki. ―The Return of Thalidomide.‖ Scientist 22 Jan. 2001: 5. 24\nJan. 2006 <http://www.the-\nscientist.com/yr2001/jan/lewis_pl_010122.html>.\nProvide the following information for an online magazine article that you reach directly: 1\nAuthor‘s name, article title, magazine\ntitle, and publication date, as in model 24 or 25 on page 668. 2 Any page, paragraph, or other\nreference numbers. 3 Date of your access. 4 URL for the article.\nSee model 31 (pp. 671–72) when citing a magazine article that you reached through a\nsubscription service.\n40. An online review\nDetwiler, Donald S., and Chu Shao-Kang. Rev. of Important Documents of the Republic of China, ed.\nTan Quon Chin. Journal of Military History 56.4 (1992): 669-84. 16 Sept. 2005\n<http://www.jstor.org/fcgi-bin/jstor/ viewitem.fcg/08993718/96p0008x>.\nCite an online review as follows: 1 Author, any review title, Rev. of and the title of the\nreviewed book, author or editor of the reviewed book, and publication information—all as in model\n29 (p. 668). 2 Date of your access. 3 URL for the review.\nSee model 31 (pp. 671–72) when citing a review that you reached through a subscription\nservice.\n41. An online government publication\nUnited States. Dept. of Commerce. National Telecommunications and Informa-tion Admin. A Nation\nOnline: Entering the Broadband Age. Feb. 2005.\n1 Mar. 2005 <http://www.ntia.doc.gov/reports/anol/index.html>.\nSee page 682 for models of government publications in print. Provide the same information for\nonline publications, and add facts of electronic publication. The model above includes the\nfollowing: 1 Names of government, department, and agency. 2 Title of publication, underlined.\n3 Date of publication. 4 Date of your access. 5 URL for the publication.\n42. An article in an online encyclopedia\nor other information database\nDull, Jack L. ―Wu-ti.‖ Encyclopaedia Britannica Online. 2004. Encyclopaedia\nBritannica. 23 Dec. 2005 <http://www.britannica.com/eb/\narticle?tocid:9077599>.\nFor an article in an online encyclopedia or other information database, provide the following: 1\nAuthor‘s name, if any is given. 2 Title of the article, in quotation marks. 3 Title of the database,\nunderlined. 4 Date of electronic publication. 5 Name of sponsoring organization or publisher.\n6 Date of your access. 7 URL for the ar-ticle.\nSee models 31 and 32 (pp. 671–72) when citing an information database that you reached\nthrough a library or personal subscription service. For encyclopedias and other reference works that\nyou find in print or on CD-ROM, see pages 665and 681, respectively.\n43. An online television or radio program\nBase citations of online television and radio programs on model 60, page 684, adding your\naccess date and the URL.\nGross, Terry, host. Fresh Air. Course home page. Jan.-June 2003. Dept. of English, U of\nNorth Carolina. 16 Feb. 2003 <http:// sites.unc.edu/daniel/eng32/index.html>.\nFor the home page of a course, provide this information: 1 Instructor‘s name. 2 Course title,\nwithout quotation marks or underlining. 3 The description Course home page. 4 Inclusive\ndates of the course. 5 Names of the department and the school, separated by a comma. 6 Date of\nyour access. 7 URL for the home page.\nFor a department home page, give the department name first, followed by Dept. home page,\nthe name of the school, your access date, and the URL:\nComputer Engineering. Dept. home page. Santa Clara U School of Engineering. 12 Oct. 2005\n<http://www.cse.scu.edu>.\n48.
