(See p. 608 for tips on\nfinding the full names of authors who use only screen names.) 2 Title of the entry, in quotation\nmarks. 3 Ti-tle of the Web log, underlined. 4 Date of the entry. 5 Date of your access. 6\nURL for the entry.\n49. Electronic mail\nMillon, Michele. ―Re: Grief Therapy.‖ E-mail to the author. 4 May 2005.\nFor e-mail, give the following: 1 Writer‘s name. 2 Title, if any, from the e-mail‘s subject\nheading, in quotation marks. 3 Description of the transmission, including to whom it was sent. 4\nDate of posting.\n50. A posting to an e-mail discussion list\nTourville, Michael. ―European Currency Reform.‖ Online posting. 6 Jan. 2006. International Finance\nDiscussion List. 12 Jan. 2006 <http:// www.weg.isu.edu/finance-dl/archive/46732>.\nWhenever possible, cite an archived version of a posting to an\ne-mail discussion list so that readers can find it without difficulty. Give this information for the\nposting: 1 Author‘s name. (See p. 608 for tips on finding the full names of authors who use only\nscreen names.) 2 Title, if any, from the e-mail‘s subject heading, in quotation marks. 3 Online\nposting. 4 Date of posting. 5 Name of the discussion list, without quotation marks or\nunderlining. 6 Date of your access. 7 URL, if known, or e-mail address for the list‘s moderator\nor supervisor.\n51. A posting to a newsgroup or Web forum\nA newsgroup:\nCramer, Sherry. ―Recent Investment Practices in US Business.‖ Online posting. 26 Mar. 2005. Young\nEntrepreneurs. 3 Apr. 2005 <http://\nfinance.groups.yahoo.com/group/youngentrepreneurs3>.\nFor a posting to a newsgroup, give the following: 1 Author‘s name. (See p. 608 for tips on finding\nthe full names of authors who\nuse only screen names.) 2 Title from the subject heading, in quotation marks. 3 Online\nposting. 4 Date of posting. 5 Name of the newsgroup, without quotation marks or\nunderlining. 6 Date of your\naccess. 7 URL for the group. If you read the posting on a news server instead of on the Web, omit\nthe group name before your\naccess date and give the group name in the URL, preceded by news:\n<news:biz.startups.youngentrepreneurs.2700>.\nA Web forum:\nRazi, N. A synchronous communication\nBruckman, Amy. MediaMOO Symposium: Virtual Worlds for Business? 20 Jan. 2006. MediaMOO. 26\nFeb. 2006 <http://www.co.gatech.edu/fac/\nAmy.Bruckman/MediaMOO/cscw-symposium-06.html>.\nWhenever possible, cite an archived version of a synchronous communication so that readers can\nfind it without difficulty. A source on a periodical CD-ROM database\nHakim, Danny. ―Iacocca, Away from the Grind, Still Has a Lot to Say.‖ New York Times 19 July 2005,\nnatl. ed.: C1+. New York Times Ondisc. CD-ROM. UMI-ProQuest. Sept. 2005.\nDatabases on CD-ROM are issued periodically—for instance, every six months or every year. The\njournals, newspapers, and other publications included in such a database are generally available in\nprint as well, so your works-cited entry should give the information for both formats: 1\nInformation for the print version, following models on pages 666–70. 2 Title of the CD-ROM,\nunderlined. 3 Medium, CD-ROM. 4 Name of the vendor (or distributor) of the CD-ROM.\n5 Date of electronic publication.\n54. A source on a nonperiodical CD-ROM\nNunberg, Geoffrey. ―Usage in the Dictionary.‖ The American Heritage Diction-ary of the English\nLanguage. 4th ed. CD-ROM. Boston: Houghton, 2005.\nSingle-issue CD-ROMs may be encyclopedias, dictionaries, books, and other resources that are\npublished just once, like printed\nbooks. Use this format: 1 Author‘s name, if any. 2 Title of the source. Use quotation marks for\nshort works, such as an article.\nUnderline the title if it is a book. 3 Title of the entire CD-ROM,\nunderlined. This CD-ROM also includes an edition number. 4 Med-ium, CD-ROM. 5 CD-ROM‘s\nplace of publication, publisher, and date of publication.\nSee also pages 665 and 677, respectively, for models of print and online reference works.\n55. Computer software\nProject Scheduler 9000. Vers. 5.1. Orlando: Scitor, 2006.\nFor software, provide the following: 1 Title, underlined. 2 Version number. 3 Publication\ninformation, including place of publication, publisher, and date. If the software has a listed author,\ngive his or her name first in the entry. If you consulted or obtained the software online, replace the\npublication information with the date of your access and the URL, as in earlier examples.\nListing other print and nonprint sources\n56. A government publication\nBoard of Governors. US Federal Reserve System. Federal Reserve Bulletin Aug. 2005: 20-21.\nHawaii. Dept. of Education. Kauai District Schools, Profile 2004-05. Honolulu: Hawaii Dept. of\nEducation, 2005.\nStiller, Ann. Historic Preservation and Tax Incentives. US Dept. of Interior. Washington: GPO, 2002.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 53\nUnited States. Cong. House. Committee on Ways and Means. Medicare Payment for Outpatient\nOccupational Therapy Services. 108th Cong., 1st sess. Washington: GPO, 2003.\nIf an author is not listed for a government publication, give the appropriate agency as author, as in\nthe first, second, and last examples. Provide information in the order illustrated, separating\nelements with a period: the name of the government, the name of the agency (which may be\nabbreviated), and the title and publication information. For a congressional publication (last\nexample), give the house and committee involved before the title, and give the number and session\nof Congress after the title. In the last two examples, GPO stands for the US Government Printing\nOffice.\n57. A pamphlet\nMedical Answers About AIDS. New York: Gay Men‘s Health Crisis, 2006.\nMost pamphlets can be treated as books. In the example above, the pamphlet has no listed author,\nso the title comes first. If the\npamphlet has an author, list his or her name first, followed by the title and publication information\nas given here.\n58. An unpublished dissertation or thesis\nWilson, Stuart M. ―John Stuart Mill as a Literary Critic.‖ Diss. U of Michigan, 1990.\nThe title is quoted rather than underlined. Diss. stands for ―Dissertation.‖ U of Michigan is the\ninstitution that granted the author‘s degree.\n59. An image (artwork, advertisement, graph, etc.)\nA work of art:\nHockney, David. Place Furstenberg, Paris. 1985. College Art Gallery, New Paltz, New York. David\nHockney: A Retrospective. Ed. Maurice Tuchman and Stephanie Barron. Los Angeles: Los Angeles\nCounty Museum of Art, 1988. 247.\nFor a work of art, name the artist and give the title (underlined), the date of creation, and the name and\nlocation of the owner. For a work you see only in a reproduction, provide the complete publication\ninformation, too, as in the Hockney model. Omit such information only if you examined the actual\nwork.\nA photograph:\nHeinz, Thomas A. Fallingwater: Exterior Detail. Frank Lloyd Wright: Architect. Ed. Terence Riley. New\nYork: Museum of Modern Art, 2000. 236.\nTreat a photograph you find in a collection or book like a work of art, with photographer‘s name (if\nknown), photograph title (underlined), and date. Add the owner‘s name (as in the Hockney entry\nabove) if the photograph is an artwork and not an illustration. Give the publication information\nunless you examined an actual print of the photograph.\nFor a personal photograph by you or someone else, describe the subject (without quotation\nmarks or underlining), say who took the photograph, and add the date:\nChildren in Central Park. Personal photograph by the author. 16 Mar. 2006.\nAn advertisement:\nJetta by Volkswagen. Advertisement. New Yorker 25 July 2005: 31.\nCite an advertisement with the name of the product or company advertised, the description\nAdvertisement, and the publication information.\nA cartoon or comic strip:\nTrudeau, Garry. ―Doonesbury.‖ Comic strip. San Francisco Chronicle 28 Aug. 2005: E6.\nCite a cartoon or comic strip with the artist‘s name, the title (in quotation marks), the description\nCartoon or Comic strip, and the publication information.\nA map, chart, graph, or diagram:\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 54\nWomen in the Armed Forces. Map. Women in the World: An International Atlas. By Joni Seager and Ann\nOlson. New York: Touchstone, 2006. 44-45.\nList the image by its title (underlined) unless its creator is credited on the source. Provide a\ndescription (Map, Chart, and so on) and then the publication information.\n60. A television or radio program\n―I‘m Sorry, I‘m Lost.‖ By Alan Ball. Dir. Jill Soloway. Six Feet Under. HBO.\n2 July 2005.\nStart with the title unless you are citing the work of a person or persons. The example here includes\nan episode title (in quotation marks), the writer‘s and director‘s names, the program title\n(underlined), the name of the network, and the date. If the program aired on a local TV station,\nidentify the station between the network and the date—for example, WGBH, Boston.\n61. A sound recording\nBrahms, Johannes. Piano Concerto no. 2 in B-flat, op. 83. Perf. Artur Rubinstein. Cond. Eugene\nOrmandy. Philadelphia Orch. LP. RCA, 1972.\nSpringsteen, Bruce. ―Empty Sky.‖ The Rising. Columbia, 2002.\nBegin with the name of the individual whose work you are citing. If you‘re citing a song or song\nlyrics, give the title in quotation marks. Then provide the title of the recording, not underlined if it\nidentifies a composition by form, number, and key (first example). After the title, provide the\nnames of any other artists it seems appropriate to mention, the manufacturer of the recording, and\nthe date of release. If the medium is other than compact disk, provide it immediately before the\nmanufacturer‘s name—for instance, LP (as in the first example) or Audiocassette.\n62. A film, DVD, or video recording\nThe Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King. Dir. Peter Jackson. New Line, 2003.\nStart with the title of the work you are citing, unless you are citing the contribution of a particular\nindividual (see the next model). Give additional information (director, writer, lead performers, and\nso on) as you judge appropriate. For a film, end with the distributor and date.\nFor a DVD or videocassette, include the original release date (if any) and the medium (DVD,\nVideocassette) before the distributor‘s name:\nGeorge Balanchine, chor. Serenade. Perf. San Francisco Ballet. Dir. Hilary Bean. 1991. Videocassette.\nPBS Video, 1997.\n63. A musical composition\nFauré, Gabriel. Sonata for Violin and Piano no. 1 in A Major, op. 15.\nDon‘t underline musical compositions, such as the one above, that are identified only by form,\nnumber, and key. Do underline titled operas, ballets, and compositions (Carmen, Sleeping\nBeauty, The 1812 Overture). Use quotation marks for songs.\n64. A performance\nBarenboim, Daniel, cond. Chicago Symphony Orch. Symphony Center, Chicago. 22 Jan. 2006.\nThe English Only Restaurant. By Silvio Martinez Palau. Dir. Susana Tubert. Puerto Rican Traveling\nTheater, New York. 27 July 2005.\nAs with films and television programs, place the title first unless you are citing the work of an\nindividual (first example). Provide additional information about participants after the title, as well\nas the theater, city, and date. Note that the orchestra name in the first example is neither quoted nor\nunderlined.\n65. A letter\nButtolph, Mrs. Laura E. Letter to Rev. and Mrs. C. C. Jones. 20 June 1857. In The Children of Pride: A\nTrue Story of Georgia and the Civil War. Ed. Robert Manson Myers. New Haven: Yale UP, 1972.\n334-35.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 55\nList a published letter under the writer‘s name. Specify that the source is a letter and to whom it\nwas addressed, and give the date on which it was written. Treat the remaining information like that\nfor a selection from an anthology (model 18, p. 664). (See also\np. 668 for the format of a letter to the editor of a periodical.)\nFor a letter in the collection of a library or archive, specify the writer, recipient, and date, as\nabove, and give the name and location of the archive as well:\nJames, Jonathan E. Letter to his sister. 16 Apr. 1970. Jonathan E. James\nPapers. South Dakota State Archive, Pierre.\nFor a letter you receive, give the name of the writer, note the fact that the letter was sent to\nyou, and provide the date of the letter:\nPacker, Ann E. Letter to the author. 15 June 2005.\nUse the form above for personal e-mail as well, substituting E-mail for Letter: E-mail to the\nauthor (see p. 679).\n66. A lecture or address\nCarlone, Dennis. ―Architecture for the City of the Twenty-First Century.‖ Symposium on the City. Urban\nIssues Group. Cambridge City Hall, Cambridge. 22 May 2005.\nGive the speaker‘s name, the title (in quotation marks), the title of the meeting, the name of the\nsponsoring organization, the location of the lecture, and the date. If the lecture has no title, use\nLecture, Address, or another description instead.\nAlthough the MLA Handbook does not provide a specific style for classroom lectures in your\ncourses, you can adapt the preceding format for this purpose:\nChang, Julia. Class lecture on the realist novel. Homans College. 20 Jan. 2006.\n67. An interview\nGraaf, Vera. Personal interview. 19 Dec. 2005.\nRumsfeld, Donald. Interview. Frontline. PBS. WGBH, Boston. 10 Oct. 2005.\nBegin with the name of the person interviewed. For an interview you conducted, specify Personal\ninterview or the medium (such as Telephone interview or E-mail interview), and then\ngive the date. For an interview you read, heard, or saw, provide the title if any or Interview if\nthere is no title, along with other bibliographic information and the date.\nEXERCISE 47.1 Writing works-cited entries\nPrepare works-cited entries from the following information. Follow the models of the MLA Handbook\ngiven in this chapter unless your instructor specifies a different style. For titles, use underlining (as here)\nun-less your instructor requests italics. Arrange the finished entries in alphabetical order, not numbered.\n(You can do this exercise online at ablongman.com/littlebrown.)\n1. An article titled ―Use of Third Parties to Collect State and Local Taxes on Internet Sales,‖ appearing\nin The Pacific Business Journal, volume 5, issue 2, in 2004. The authors are Malai Zimmerman and\nKent Hoover. The article appears on pages 45 through 48 of the journal.\n2. A government publication you consulted on November 12, 2005, over the Internet. The author is the\nAdvisory Commission on\nElectronic Commerce. The commission is an agency of the United States government. The title of the\npublication is Report to Congress. It was published in April 2005 and can be reached at\nhttp://www.ecommercecommission.org/report.htm.\n3. A Web site with no listed author. The title is The Internet Tax Freedom Act Home Page, and the site\nis dated June 3, 2005. The address is http://cox.house.gov/nettax/frmain.htm. You consulted the site\non November 2, 2005.\n4. An article in the magazine Forbes, published December 17, 2003, on pages 56 through 58. The\nauthor is Anne Granfield. The title is ―Taxing the Internet.‖\n5. A book titled All‘s Fair in Internet Commerce, or Is It? by Sally G. Osborne. The book was published\nin 2004 by Random House in New York, New York.\n6. An e-mail interview you conducted with Nora James on Novem-ber 1, 2005.\n7. An article titled ―State and Local Sales/Use Tax Simplification,‖ ap-pearing on pages 67 through 80\nof an anthology, The Sales Tax in the Twenty-First Century. The anthology is edited by Matthew N.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 56\nMurray and William F. Fox. The article is by Wayne G. Eggert. The anthology was published in\n2004 by Praeger in Westport, Connecticut.\nUsing MLA document format\nThe document format recommended by the MLA Handbook is fairly\nsimple, with just a few elements. See also pages 112–26 for guidelines on\ntype fonts, headings, lists, illustrations, and other features that are not\nspecified in MLA style.\nThe samples below and on the next page show the formats for the first page and a later page of\na paper. For the format of the list of works cited, see page 657.\nMargins Use minimum one-inch margins on all sides of every page.\nSpacing and indentions Double-space throughout. Indent para-graphs one-half inch or five\nspaces. (See below for indention of poetry and long prose quotations.)\nPaging Begin numbering on the first page, and number consec-utively through the end\n(including the list of works cited). Type Arabic numerals (1, 2, 3) in the upper right about one-half\ninch from the top. Place your last name before the page number in case the pages later become\nseparated.\nIdentification and title The MLA Handbook does not require a title page for a paper. If your\ninstructor asks you to supply a title page, see pages 690–91. Otherwise, follow the sample on the\nprevious page, providing your name, the date, and other information requested by your instructor.\nPlace this identification an inch from the top of the page, aligned with the left margin and double-\nspaced.\nDouble-space again, and center the title. Do not highlight the title with underlining, boldface,\nlarger type, or quotation marks. Capitalize the words in the title according to guidelines on page\n491. Double-space the lines of the title and between the title and the text.\nPoetry and long prose quotations Treat a single line of poetry like any other quotation, running\nit into your text and enclosing it in quotation marks. You may run in two or three lines of poetry as\nwell, separating the lines with a slash surrounded by space.\nAn example of Robert Frost‘s incisiveness is in two lines from ―Death of the Hired Man‖: “Home is the\nplace where, when you have to go there / They\nhave to take you in” (119-20).\nAlways set off from your text a poetry quotation of more than three lines. Use double spacing\nabove and below the quotation and for the quotation itself. Indent the quotation one inch or ten\nspaces from the left margin. Do not add quotation marks.\nEmily Dickinson stripped ideas to their essence, as in this description of\n―A narrow Fellow in the Grass,‖ a snake:\nI more than once at Noon\nHave passed, I thought, a Whip lash\nUnbraiding in the Sun\nWhen stopping to secure it\nIt wrinkled, and was gone – (12-16)\nAlso set off a prose quotation of more than four typed lines. (See p. 620on when to use such long\nquotations.) Double-space and indent as with the poetry example above. Do not add quotation\nmarks.\nIn the influential Talley‘s Corner from 1967, Elliot Liebow observes that ―unskilled‖ construction work\nrequires more skill than is generally assumed:\nA healthy, sturdy, active man of good intelligence requires\nfrom two to four weeks to break in on a construction job. . . . It\nfrequently happens that his foreman or the craftsman he\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 57\nservices is not willing to wait that long for him to get into\ncondition or to learn at a glance the difference in size between\na rough 2 x 8 and a finished 2 x 10. (62)\nDo not use a paragraph indention for a quotation of a single complete paragraph or a part of a\nparagraph. Use paragraph indentions of one-quarter inch or three spaces only for a quotation of two\nor more complete paragraphs.\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 58\nVisit the companion Web site for more help and an additional exercise\non MLA documen-tation and format.\nMLA parenthetical text citations\n1. Shorten most publishers’ names (‘‘UP’’ for University Press,\n‘‘Little’’for\nLittle, Brown). (Begay‘s paper begins on the next page, Haley‘s on p. 725.)\nBoth students followed the style of the MLA Handbook for documenting sources and formatting\ntheir papers. Accompanying both students‘ papers are comments on format, source citations, and\nother matters.\nClosing the Digital Divide\nBy\nEdward Begay\nMs. Derryfield\nEnglish 105-03\n2 May 2005\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 63\nBegay i\nOutline\nThesis statement: Government and business must ensure that libraries and schools have the hardware,\nconnections, and training capabilities for computer technology to make Americans more rather than less equal.\nI. The digital divide is wide.\nA. Poor people have much less access to computer technology than middle-class and affluent people do.\nB. People who aren‘t online are at risk for missing important information.\nII. Public libraries can provide Internet access to those who do not own computers, but they face several\nchallenges.\nA. Those who have no access to computers at work or school take advantage of library computers for\nInternet access.\nB. Providing Internet access creates significant funding challenges for libraries.\nC. The FCC‘s E-Rate program is the most reliable funding source for library technology, but it is modest.\nIII. Schools offer many children their main exposure to computers, but computers raise educational as well as\nfunding issues.\nA. Some experts question the value of technology in the classroom, but evidence suggests that Internet\naccess can enhance learning.\n1. Some critics say technology undermines education.\n2. Some teachers say that technology fits in well with recent theories of education.\n3. Students in many schools are using the Internet effectively.\nB. Low-income students have far less access to tech-\nnology than high-income students do.\nBegay ii\n1. Low-income students use the Internet half as often.\n2. When low-income students have access to computers, they spend more of the time using\ninstructional software.\nIV. Governments and businesses must play a more active role in financing Internet access for libraries and\nschools.\nA. The federal government must reverse the recent cuts in funding of technology-assistance programs.\nB. Businesses must recognize their long-term interest in bringing potential employees online.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 64\nBegay 1\nClosing the Digital Divide\nFrom wireless connections in coffee shops to advertisements for all kinds of wireless devices, we see\nevidence nearly\neverywhere that our society is online. It‘s easy to assume that everyone who wants to be online is indeed online\nand that those who aren‘t simply don‘t want to be. But this assumption is misguided. The digital divide in the\nUnited States—the gap\nbetween those who have access to the Internet and those who don‘t—reflects a similar gap between rich and\npoor.\nIn 1999 a groundbreaking US Department of Commerce study gloomily predicted ―a widening gap\nbetween those with and without access to the Internet that threatens our demo-cratic society‖ (Falling xii;\nemphasis added). The prediction is still appropriate today. According to a recent report by the Benton\nFoundation, a watchdog group that observes and reports on the digital divide, the gaps in Internet access among\npeople of different educations, incomes, and racial and ethnic groups is not closing, even as the overall number\nof Americans online has increased. As more information is published only online, the unconnected have a lot\nmore catching up to do than they had just a few years ago when print information was still the norm. Most\nobservers look to public libraries and schools as the primary sites for connecting to the Internet\nbecause they are open to all. However, governments and businesses must ensure that these institutions have the\nhardware, connections, and training capabilities for computer technology to make Americans more rather than\nless equal.\nStatistics vary1 but all research agrees that people have much greater access to computer technology if\nthey are middle class or affluent than if they are not. (The divide also shows up between those below and above\nage 50 and between those without and with disabilities.) Very comprehensive data appear\nBegay 2\nin the most recent Department of Commerce study, which paints the unsettling picture shown in Table 1. The\nbottom three groups total 41.2 percent of all US households, yet many fewer than half of them use the Internet.\nTable 1\nInternet Use by Household Income, 2003\nAnnual Household Percentage of All Percentage Using Income USHouseholds the\nInternet\nLess than $15,000 16.1 31.2\n$15,000-$24,999 13.2 38.0\n$25,000-$34,999 11.9 48.9\n$35,000-$49,999 15.1 62.1\nMore than $75,000 26.3 82.9\nSource: Data from United States, Dept. of Commerce, Natl. Telecommunications and Information Admin., A\nNation\nOnline: Entering the Broadband Age, Feb. 2005, 1 Mar. 2005\n<http://www.ntia.gov/reports/anol/index.html> 9, 47.\nPeople who aren‘t online are at risk for missing important information and may not even know they are\nmissing it. For example, in early 2005 the US Department of Agriculture unveiled a new, interactive version of\nthe Food Pyramid, the familiar triangle that provides guidelines for healthy eating. Previous versions of the\npyramid had been published in print and distributed through schools, local health departments,\nlocal libraries, and so on. The new version, however, is available primarily on the Web, and its interactive\nfeatures can be\naccessed only online. According to the journalist Andy Carvin,\nBegay 3\nthe pyramid provides more specific information about nutrition, but being online it can‘t reach\nmany low-income people who, like everyone else, would benefit from its guidelines. The\nsociologists Susan Dykstra and William L. Brown observe that ―as US government agencies expand\ne-government . . . , a pressing question remains what will happen to underserved populations,\nparticularly as traditionally offline government services are replaced entirely by online services.‖\nThe answer, for now, is that those populations will be more underserved than before.\nFor people without home or work Internet access, an important link is public libraries. Nearly all public\nlibraries in the United States do have some level of Internet access to serve their patrons: 97 percent, according\nto recent numbers (Bertot, McClure, and Jaeger 4). The access specifically benefits those who need it most. The\nAmerican Library Association states the role of libraries and librarians this way:\nPeople from households making less than $15,000 annually are three times more likely to rely on\nlibrary computers than those earning more than $75,000. . . . [P]ublic access to the Internet through\npublic libraries is a major step toward closing the digital divide. But access is not enough:\nlibrarians and their interactions with patrons make the biggest difference. Librarians help patrons\ndevelop vital information-literacy skills by providing one-to-one tutoring in how to access relevant,\nwell-organized sources. (par. 2)\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 65\nHowever, providing not only up-to-date computers and Internet connections but also intensive training creates\nsignificant funding challenges for libraries. Almost 75 percent of public libraries have three or fewer computer\nterminals through which they can offer Internet access, and fewer than 30\nBegay 4\npercent of librarians believe they have the staff needed to train users (Bertot and McClure 35). Clearly, with the\nlength of time Internet searches can take, three terminals and an overstretched staff cannot serve many library\npatrons. Yet terminals and staff are costly.\nMany librarians worry that these costs will cause libraries themselves to fall into the digital divide.\nLibrary funding is\noften cut and rarely increased by state and local governments trying to trim their budgets. Among\nnongovernment groups,\naccording to the American Library Association, only the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation (established by the\nMicrosoft founder and his wife) has provided significant help for libraries. Since 1997 the foundation has given\ngrants of more than $250 million to provide libraries with public-access computers and software. The grants\nhave especially benefited poor, rural library systems, many of which otherwise could not have afforded the\nequipment (par. 7).\nThe most reliable source of government funding for\nlibrary technology is the Universal Service Program, established by the US Federal Communications\nCommission. Telecommunications providers and individuals who subscribe to their services pay a fee\ncommonly called the E-Rate. From the Universal Service Program, the FCC allocates up to $2.25 billion\nannually to help both libraries and primary and secondary schools purchase telecommunications services.\nHowever, the fund is modest and does not cover training of staff or purchase of computers. Given the\ntransformation of our economy and culture caused by the Internet, $2.25 billion barely amounts to a token\ngesture. More must be done to\nconnect libraries and help them train Internet users.\nWhereas mostly adults benefit from library funding, many children receive their exposure to computers\nin the\nBegay 5\npublic schools. In fact, nearly all schools in the United States now provide some computers for student use\n(Conte 924). But the digital divide in schools has as much to do with how students use computers as it does\nwith whether they have access to them.\nFor some time, schools have been using computers extensively for drill-and-practice exercises, in which\nstudents\nrepeat specific skills such as spelling words, using the multiplication facts, or, at a higher level, doing\nchemistry problems. But many education experts criticize such exercises for boring students and failing to\nengage their critical thinking and creativity. Jane M. Healy, a noted educational psychologist and teacher, takes\nissue with ―interactive‖ software for children as well as drill-and-practice software, arguing that ―some of the\nmost popular ‗educational‘ software . . . may be damaging to independent thinking, attention, and motivation‖\n(20). Another education expert, Harold Wenglinsky of the Educational Testing Service, found in a well-\nregarded 1998 study that fourth and eighth graders who used computers frequently, including for drill and\npractice, actually did worse on tests than their peers who used computers less often (Does It Compute? 21). In a\nlater article, Wenglinsky concludes that ―the quantity of use matters far less than the quality of use.‖ In schools,\nhe says, high-quality computer work, involving critical thinking, is still rare (―In Search‖ 17).2\nDrill-and-practice exercises reinforce the ―transmission‖ model of education, in which teachers transmit\nknowledge to\npassive students (Conte 925). Some experts argue that this type of teaching does not prepare students to work\nin the\ninformation age (Conte 923-24). Instead, these experts favor a model closer to cognitive psychology and\nconstructivism, emphasizing active learning and ―dealing with complex,\nBegay 6\nreal-world problems‖—a model well served by Internet-\nconnected computers (Conte 935-36).\nMany teachers see the Internet as a powerful resource for just this kind of teaching. Mary E. McArthur, a\nveteran teacher in Massachusetts, told me in an online interview that the Internet presents new possibilities for\nstudent learning:\nMy students have a much better sense of the relevance of their education how now that they‘re\nonline. When we were studying ecology, for example, some students e-mailed a representative of\nthe EPA [Environmental Protection Agency] in Washington, asking questions and offering\nsuggestions about a proposed local landfill, and received an immediate response. They took that\nresponse to the town‘s planning board when the landfill was discussed.\nThe Internet, according to McArthur, has made her students not only better learners but better citizens as well.\nAnd contrary to Healy‘s vision of uncreative, unmotivated students, McArthur told me that since her students\nbegan using technology, ―conversation is constant. Students are talking online, to each other, and to me—\nquestioning, criticizing, analyzing what they‘re learning.‖\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 66\nMcArthur‘s and her students‘ experiences are not unique:\nsuccess stories about online education are common in popular and scholarly sources.3 But many teachers and\nstudents are not discovering what they might accomplish with the Internet\nbecause their schools cannot afford enough terminals and\ntraining to give everyone ample hands-on experience.\nIn general, student access to online computers is improving considerably. A 2004 study conducted by\nMarket Data\nRetrieval and quoted in Students and Internet Access by the\nBegay 7\nUS Department of Education shows that the ratio of students to computers decreased from 12 to 1 in 1998 to 4\nto 1 in 2004. However, those are averages across all public schools. Another Department of Education source\nshows that in schools with students of low income, the ratio remains higher than the 1998 average, at 13 to 1,\nand only 34 percent of those students use the Internet at all in school, compared with 68 percent of their high-\nincome peers. At the same time, the difference in overall computer use between low- and high-income students\nis not nearly as pronounced: 80 percent for those of low income, 88 percent for those of high income (Internet\n29-30). (See fig. 1.)\nBegay 8\nThe increase in both kinds of schools is due to the use of instructional software programs, primarily drill and\npractice. Clearly, poorer schools rely much more on such use than wealthier schools do.\nReal stories back up the data shown in fig. 1. An example is La Entrada High School in a poor section of\nOakland, California (Richards). La Entrada‘s ratio of students to computers is 15 to 1, and the computers are\nused machines that are slow to load Web sites. With limited equipment, students at La Entrada cannot match\ntheir more affluent peers in using the Web or communicating online with experts. In fact, they are often so\nfrustrated by waiting in line for computers and then by the machines‘ slowness that they simply give up, using\nthe computers only when scheduled for drill in reading and math. In the words of Delia Neuman of the\nUniversity of Maryland, they ―learn to do what the computer tells them‖ (qtd. in Conte 931).\nStudents who do not learn to use the Internet may find themselves left out of a society in which computer\nskills will earn a high school graduate 39 percent more than another graduate without such skills (Twist 6). And\nthe nation‘s economy will suffer as well. The risk is described by Larry Irving, a former assistant secretary of\nthe Department of Commerce and the author of its first report on the digital divide, Falling through the Net:\nAlmost 60 percent of jobs created today . . . require an understanding of information technology.\nYet too many of our students are graduating from schools that don‘t give them the training\nrequired for the jobs they seek. Already, the nation‘s businesses are having trouble filling the\nskilled jobs they‘re creating, and in another five years the situation is likely to reach a crisis.\nBegay 9\nThe results, then, are unavailable jobs for the graduating students who need them and a shortage of just the\nkind of workers the country needs.\nThe problem for schools like La Entrada is, of course, money. Leaders in poor school districts are aware\nof the importance of technology, but they are also worried about leaking roofs, aging furniture, and\novercrowded classrooms. The money to buy the equipment, make the connections, and train teachers and staff\nto use and maintain the networks is not easily found even in middle-class school districts, much less in poorer\ndistricts.\nIf libraries and schools are to provide widespread access to the Internet, they must find ways not only to\nintegrate technology into their programs but also to pay the bills associated with going and staying online.\nAdapting the work of\nlibraries and schools to the technological age is the responsibility of the experts within those systems. But\nfinding the money to finance technological advances should involve more elements of society, specifically\ngovernments and businesses. These two groups must play a more active role in wiring\nlibraries and schools, providing hardware, and training librarians, teachers, and students to work with the\ntechnology.\nGovernments are already encouraging cooperation between businesses and schools. For instance, many\nstates organize annual NetDay campaigns designed to bring educators, community volunteers, and corporations\ntogether to keep schools online and up to date (Jordahl and Orwig 25; NetDay). However, to close the digital\ndivide, government support must be direct. The E-Rate program of the Federal Communications Commission\nis a start, but just a start, because it covers only connection fees, not hardware or training. Furthermore, divided\nas it is among nearly 17,000 public libraries and 114,000\nBegay 10\nprimary and secondary schools (World Almanac 251, 253), the program‘s $2.25 billion comes to less than\n$20,000 per institution per year. According to the Benton Foundation, the federal government once made\nclosing the divide a priority, funding programs to bring the disadvantaged online and train them in using\nInternet resources. But the foundation reports that since 2001 the federal government has actually slashed\nfunding of three significant programs: Preparing Tomorrow‘s Teachers to Use Technology, providing grants to\nhelp teachers gain more competence teaching with computers; Technology Opportunity Program, providing\nhardware grants to the public and nonprofit sectors; and Community Technology Centers, providing grants to\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 67\nexpand access of the rural and urban poor to technology. Fig. 2 shows the funding of these programs from 2001\nto 2005.\nBegay 11\nCuts like these propel government efforts backward rather than forward. If the digital divide is to\nclose, as it must, the federal government needs to reverse direction, taking the lead to ensure that its\ncitizens have equal access to information technology.\nBusinesses must join in as well. Commercial enterprises have long recognized their responsibility to the\nlarger community—for instance, supporting youth athletics and contributing to charities through the Chamber\nof Commerce. Some businesses also work with schools and libraries to increase Internet access. For many\nyears, computer manufacturers such as IBM and Apple have donated new and used computers to schools.\nRecently, the 3COM Corporation has provided grants and consultants to help train public school teachers and\nstudents in the use of technology (Jordahl and Orwig 25).\nFor computer companies, cooperation with schools and\nlibraries seems good business, paying off in free advertising,\nenhanced image, and potential sales. In some locations, other kinds of companies are also stepping in to\nimprove Internet access for the disadvantaged. For example, three Seattle banks assign employee mentors to\nlow-income public schools to help the students use the Internet effectively for schoolwork (Jordahl and Orwig\n24). And insurance companies and law firms in Boston have joined technology companies to provide computer\ntraining and equipment in the public libraries and schools, making Boston one of the most Internet-connected\ncities in the nation (Pace 36). But these efforts and a few others like them are unusual in the literature on the\ndigital divide. Most businesses, no doubt focusing on the short term and receiving little incentive from\ngovernment to do otherwise, may train their own employees but contribute nothing to bring the larger\ncommunity online. As Larry Irving notes in\nBegay 12\nthe quotation cited earlier, businesses are already suffering from such shortsightedness. They must recognize\ntheir interest in fostering widespread access to technology.\nThe Internet is now ―the central nervous system of our democracy,‖ says Jeff Chester of the Center for\nDigital Democracy (6). Providing Internet access through libraries and schools seems the only way to ensure\nequal access for poor and rich alike. The schools and libraries cannot close the digital divide on their own,\nhowever. They need strong financial support from government and business to make Chester‘s neural pathway\ntruly open to all.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 68\nBegay 13\nNotes\n1 The US Department of Commerce study cited here is the most recent and most comprehensive\navailable. Beyond this study, statistics on Internet use are difficult to compare and summarize because they\noften measure different variables. For example, one study may provide the number of households with Internet\naccess, while another may provide the number of persons or the number of adults. In addition, with a subject\nthis current the data are constantly changing. Nonetheless, all studies agree on the inequities between the\naffluent and the poor.\n2 For additional criticism of computers in education, see Goodson et al.\n3 See, for example, Conte, Jordahl and Orwig, and Pace.\nBegay 14\nWorks Cited\nAmerican Library Assn. ―Digital Divide Talking Points.‖ ALA. If so, or if you are required to submit an outline with your paper, prepare a title page\nas shown opposite.\nIf your instructor does not require a title page for your paper, follow MLA style: place\nyour name, the identifying information, and the date on the first page of the paper. See Vanessa\nHaley‘s paper, page 725, for this format.\nNext two pages\nOutline format. If your instructor asks you to include your final outline, place it between the\ntitle page and the text and number the pages with small Roman numerals (i, ii). Follow the\nformatting annotations on the next two pages.\nOutline content. Begay includes his final thesis statement as part of his outline so that his\ninstructor can see how the parts relate to the whole. Notice that each main division (numbered\nwith Roman numerals) relates to the thesis statement and that all the subdivisions relate to their\nmain division.\nBegay casts his final outline in full sentences. Some instructors\nrequest topic outlines, in which ideas appear in phrases instead of in sentences and do not\nend with periods. (See pp. 35–36 for this format.)\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 71\nTitle. Begay‘s title captures the image of a wide gap between two places. A more\ndescriptive title, such as ―Equality on the\nInternet,‖ would also have been appropriate. Paper format. Because he provides a title\npage as requested by his instructor, Begay does not repeat his full name on the first page of\ntext. For MLA style, which omits a title page, the following would appear in the upper left of\nthis first page:\nEdward Begay\nMs. Derryfield\nEnglish 105-03\n2 May 2005\n(See Vanessa Haley‘s paper, p. 725, for an example of a research paper without a title page.)\nFollow the formatting annotations on the facing page for margins and spacing.\nIntroduction. Begay defines digital divide, a term he uses to discuss the problem of Internet\naccess. He delays presenting his thesis in order to establish some background about the\npersistence of unequal access.\nCitation of two works with corporate authors. The sources Begay cites here do not name\nindividual authors, so he lists the sponsor organizations as authors. Citation when the author\nis named in your text. Because Begay names the US Department of Commerce in his text, he\ndoes not repeat the name in the parenthetical citation. Citation of a work by the author of\ntwo or more works. To distinguish this Department Commerce study from another one he also\ncites, Begay gives a shortened form of the title in the parenthetical citation. Adding emphasis\nto a quotation. Begay underlines important words in the quotation. He acknowledges this\nchange in the parenthetical citation with emphasis added, separated from the page number by\na semicolon.\nOmission of a parenthetical citation. The Benton Foundation report comes from the Internet\nand lacks page or other identifying numbers. Since Begay names the author in his text, he\ndoesn‘t include a parenthetical citation.\nThesis statement. Begay‘s introduction has led up to this statement, which asserts the claim\nthat he will support in the paper.\nRelation to outline. This paragraph begins part I of Begay‘s\noutline (see p. 692). Using an endnote for supplementary information. Here Begay inserts a\nreference to a note at the end of the paper in which he explains the difficulty of interpreting\nstatistics about Internet use. He signals the note with the raised numeral 1.\nUse of a table. Begay uses a table to present statistics from the Department of Commerce\nstudy so that the data are easy to compare and the differences are emphatic. Table format.\nFollowing MLA style, Begay double-spaces the entire table. Citation of a source for a table.\nAlso following MLA style, Begay provides a source note indicating where he obtained the data\nin the table. The note includes complete information on the source even though Begay also\ncites the work fully in his list of works cited. The numbers following the URL are the pages\nwhere Begay found the table‘s data.\nSelecting supporting evidence. Begay paraphrases and quotes two sources in this paragraph to\nsupport his point that low-\nincome people who aren‘t online may miss important information. The uses of the authors‘\nnames in the text clarify who said what.\nEditing a quotation with an ellipsis mark. Begay uses an ellipsis mark (three spaced periods)\nto show that he has omitted some words from the quotation.\nRelation to outline. This paragraph begins part II of Begay‘s outline. See page 692.\nCitation when the author is not named in your text. Because Begay does not give the three\nauthors‘ names in his text, he provides the names in the parenthetical citation along with a page\nnumber. Citation of a work with three authors. Begay gives all three authors‘ last names,\nseparating them with commas and and before final name.\nFormat of a long quotation. This quotation exceeds four typed lines, so Begay sets it off from\nhis text without quotation marks, with double spacing throughout, and with an extra indention\nof ten spaces or one inch. Editing a quotation with brackets. By using brackets around the\ncapital P, Begay indicates that he has omitted the beginning of the original sentence and\nchanged the capitalization.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 72\nCitation with displayed quotation. The parenthetical cita-\ntion after the quotation falls outside the sentence period. Citation of a source using a\nparagraph number. Begay uses par. (―paragraph‖) to indicate that the source numbers\nparagraphs rather than pages. He cites paragraph 2.\nRevision of a draft. In his first draft Begay sometimes strung his source information together\nwithout interpreting it. In revising he added comments of his own (in blue) to introduce the\ninformation in the context of his ideas:\nHowever, providing not only up-to-date computers and Internet connections but also intensive training\ncreates significant funding challenges for libraries. In his reading, Begay saw many references to government cuts in library\nfunding, so he treats this information as common knowledge and does not cite a source for it.\n(See pp. 632–33 for more on common knowledge.) Clarifying boundaries of source\nmaterial. The rest of this paragraph summarizes information from a report by the American\nLibrary Association. Begay makes the extent of the summary clear by giving the ALA‘s name\nat the beginning and a parenthetical citation at the end.\nOmission of a parenthetical citation. Begay does not provide a parenthetical citation for the\nFederal Communications Commission report because he names the author in his text and the\nonline source has no page or other reference numbers.\nRelation to outline. With this paragraph, Begay begins part\nIII of his outline (see p. 692). Transitional paragraph. Begay devotes a paragraph to the shift\nin focus from libraries to schools.\nIntegrating source material. Here and elsewhere, Begay establishes his source‘s credentials\nin a signal phrase and effectively integrates paraphrases and quotations into his own sentences.\nOmission of ellipsis mark. Begay does not use an ellipsis mark at the beginning of the Healy\nquotation beginning ―some‖ because the small s makes it clear that he omitted the opening of\nHealy‘s sentence.\nPunctuation with a parenthetical citation. The period that ends a sentence containing a\nquotation comes after the citation.\nCitation of two works by the same author. Begay gives brief versions of Wenglinsky‘s two\ntitles in the parenthetical citations here and below in order to distinguish the sources.\nClarifying boundaries of source material. By mentioning Wenglinsky‘s name at the\nbeginning of the paragraph‘s last two sentences and giving the rest of the citation at the end,\nBegay indicates that everything in between comes from Wenglinsky. Mixing quotation and\nparaphrase. Begay quotes and paraphrases from Wenglinsky‘s article to give readers a good\nsense of the issue Wenglinsky raises. Punctuation with a quotation. The period falls inside\nthe closing quotation mark because the quotation is not immediately followed by a\nparenthetical citation.\nIntroducing borrowed material. Begay here begins paraphrasing and quoting Conte as an\nexpert, so he should have named Conte in the text and identified him with his credentials.\nParaphrasing. Begay paraphrases Conte‘s words. His note for the second paraphrase\ntranscribed a quotation from Conte:\nCitation of paraphrases. Because he does not use Conte‘s name in the text, Begay correctly\ngives it in the citations.\nDefining terms. Begay uses two terms here, cognitive psychology and constructivism, that he\npicked up from Conte and other sources. He should have defined the terms to avoid confusing\nreaders.\nPrimary source: personal interview. Begay tested his ideas by conducting an e-mail interview\nwith a teacher in a public school. He uses both paraphrase and quotation from the interview,\nwith the subject‘s permission.\nAdding to a quotation with brackets. Begay spells out the full name of the EPA for readers\nwho may not recognize the abbreviation, and he encloses the addition with brackets.\nOmission of parenthetical citation. Begay does not use a parenthetical citation at the end of\nthe quotation because the source (an interview) has no page or other reference numbers and the\nnecessary information (McArthur‘s name) appears in the text before the quotation.\nSummary of sources. Rather than belabor the Internet suc-\ncess stories, Begay wraps up with a summary. Using an endnote for citation of several\nsources. Begay avoids a lengthy and obtrusive parenthetical citation by referring readers to\nendnote 3, which lists several sources (see p. 718). Transitional paragraph. This paragraph\nwithin the section on schools shifts the emphasis from the educational value of technology to\nits cost.\nSynthesis of sources. In this and the next several paragraphs, Begay integrates information\nfrom sources with his own conclusions about the significance of the data.\nCitation of a long source named in the text. A parenthetical citation here would have read\n(qtd. in United States, Dept. of Education, Students). Begay chose to avoid\nthe awkwardly long citation by naming both the indirect and the direct source in his text. The\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 74\nDepartment of Education site had no page or other reference numbers for Begay to cite.\nIndirect sources. Indirect sources are appropriate only when the quoted material is not\navailable to consult. Begay‘s use of the indirect source is appropriate here because he could not\nfind the original Market Data Retrieval report.\nUse of a figure. Begay created a bar chart to show the differences between low-income and\nhigh-income students. He refers to the figure in his text.\nFigure caption. Begay captions the figure so that readers know how to interpret it. Citation\nof a source for data. Following MLA style, Begay provides a source note indicating where he\nobtained the data in the chart. The note includes complete information for the source even\nthough Begay also cites the work fully in his list of works cited.\nCitation of an online article without page numbers. The Rich-ards article, which Begay\nfound online, does not have any page or other reference numbers, so the citation includes only\nthe author‘s name.\nEditing quotations. Begay had a long quotation by Neuman, but he selected from it only the\nwords that supported the point he was making. The entire quotation appears in his note:\nCitation of an indirect source. With the use of qtd. in, Begay indicates correctly that he\nfound the quotation by Neuman (an indirect source) in the article by Conte (a direct source).\nIndirect sources. Indirect sources are appropriate only when the quoted material is not\navailable to consult. Begay‘s source, Conte, gave full bibliographic information on Neuman‘s\narticle, and Begay should have gone directly to it.\nOmission of a parenthetical citation. Rather than leave it to his readers to figure out the significance of the\npreceding paragraphs, Begay here wraps up the discussion of schools with his own conclusions\nabout the costs and thus the limits of technological change in education.\nRelation to outline. With this paragraph, Begay begins part\nIV of his outline (see p. 693). Summary statement. Begay introduces this final section with a\nstatement that pulls together libraries and schools and clearly distinguishes their role from the\nfinancial responsibilities for broadening Internet access.\nParenthetical citation of more than one work. Begay discovered information about NetDay\ncampaigns in two sources, so he cites both in parentheses, separating them with a semicolon.\nThe second source lacks page or other reference numbers, so its citation does not include a\nnumber.\nUse of an almanac. Begay consulted an almanac for the number of public libraries and\nprimary and secondary schools so that he could calculate the average amount of aid under the\nE-Rate program. Placement of a parenthetical citation. Since Begay names the Benton Foundation in his text\nand the online source has no page or other reference number, he does not add a parenthetical\ncitation.\nUse of a figure. Begay uses a graph to show the dramatic funding decrease in the programs he\nmentions. He refers to the figure in his text.\nFigure caption. Begay captions the figure so that readers\nknow how to interpret it. Citation of a source for a figure. Following MLA style, Begay\nprovides a source note indicating where he obtained the figure. The note includes complete\ninformation for the source even though Begay also cites the work fully in his list of works cited.\nDrawing conclusions. Begay ends his discussion of government funding with his own\nconclusion about what has happened and what must be done.\nCommon knowledge. Begay already knew of manufacturers‘ programs to place computers in\nschools; in fact, he had used a donated computer in high school. Thus he treats this information\nas common knowledge.\nDrawing conclusions. Begay ends his discussion of business with his own conclusions about\nthe causes and results of low funding.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 75\nConclusion. In his final paragraph Begay summarizes the main points of his paper to remind\nreaders of both the need for universal Internet access and the ways it can be funded.\nFormat of notes. The heading Notes is centered one inch from the top of the page. (The\nheading would be singular—Note—if Begay had only one note.) Follow the annotations on the\nfacing page for formatting.\nEndnotes for additional relevant information. Begay uses endnotes for sources and\ninformation that are somewhat relevant to his thesis but not essential and that don‘t fit easily\ninto the text. Note 1 provides information on Begay‘s difficulties interpreting statistics. Note 2\nhighlights a notable critique of computers in education. And note 3 cites several sources that\nwould be obtrusive in a parenthetical citation. (See p. 656 for more on supplementary notes.)\nCitation of a source with more than three authors. The Goodson citation indicates with et\nal. (―and others‖) that Goodson was a coauthor with at least three others. See the works-cited\nentry for this source on the next page.\nFormat of a list of works cited. The heading Works Cited is centered at the top of the page.\nThe entries are alphabetized by the last name of the first author or (for sources without authors)\nby the first main word of the title. Each entry has a hanging indention (see p. 657 on creating\nthis indention). For additional formatting, see the annotations on the facing page.\nAn online source, including Begay‘s access date and the URL in angle brackets. A corporate\nauthor. Since the source does not list an individual as author, Begay names the organization as\nauthor. Paragraphs instead of pages. This source does not number pages but does number\nparagraphs, so Begay lists the total paragraphs.\nSource with three authors. The first name is reversed, and the other two are given in normal\norder, separated by and.\nScholarly project. The entry includes the title of the project, the name of the director, and the\nname of the sponsoring university.\nArticle from an online service to which the library subscribes (see pp. 671–72). Because the\nservice does not provide usable URLs for articles—that is, URLs that readers can use to reach\nthe articles directly—Begay instead gives the names of the database and service, the names of\nhis school and library, and the URL for the home page of the service.\nArticle in a journal with continuous pagination throughout an annual volume (see p. 666).\nSource with two authors. The first name is reversed. After and the second name is given in\nnormal order.\nSource with more than three authors. A source with more than three authors may be listed\nwith all authors‘ names or just with the first author‘s name followed by et al. (―and others‖).\n(See p. 659.) Begay had all the names in his working bibliography, but he opted not to use\nthem. His parenthetical citation is consistent with this decision (p. 718).\nBook with one author.\nArticle in a weekly magazine.\nArticle in a monthly magazine.\nPersonal interview by e-mail.\nA page on an organization’s Web site. MLA does not specifically cover an online site for this\ntype of organization, so Begay adapted the format for a short work from an online site (p. 673).\nHe provided all the information a reader would need to find the source—including the page\ntitle, site title, date of publication, and URL of the page—along with the date of his access.\nArticle from an online service to which the library subscribes. Much writing about nature lacks a\nrecognition that human beings and their civilization are as much a part of nature as trees and whales are,\nneither better nor worse. Yet without such a recognition, how can humans overcome the damaging sense of\nseparation between themselves and the earth? How can humans develop realistic solutions to environmental\nproblems that will work for humanity and the rest of nature?\nOne nature writer who seems to recognize the naturalness of humanity is Annie Dillard. In her best-\nknown work, the Pulitzer Prize-winning Pilgrim at Tinker Creek, she is a solitary person encountering the\nnatural world, and some critics fault her for turning her back on society. But in those encounters with nature,\nDillard probes a spiritual as well as a physical identity between human beings and nature that could help to heal\nthe rift between them.\nDillard is not renowned for her sense of involvement with human society. Like Henry David Thoreau,\nwith whom she is often compared, she retreats from rather than confronts human society. The critic Gary\nMcIlroy points out that although\nHaley 2\nThoreau discusses society a great deal in Walden, he makes no attempt ―to find a middle ground between it and\nhis experiment in the woods‖ (113). Dillard has been similarly criticized. For instance, the writer Eudora Welty\ncomments that\nAnnie Dillard is the only person in her book, substantially the only one in her world; I recall no\noutside human speech coming to break the long soliloquy of the author. Speaking of the universe\nvery often, she is yet self-surrounded and, beyond that, book-surrounded. Her own book might\nhave taken in more of human life without losing a bit of the wonder she was after. (37)\nIt is true, as Welty says, that in Pilgrim Dillard seems detached from human society. However, she\nactually was always close to it at Tinker Creek. In a later book, Teaching a Stone to Talk, she says of the\nneighborhood, ―This is, mind you, suburbia. It is a five-minute walk in three directions to rows of houses. . . .\nThere‘s a 55 mph highway at one end of the pond, and a nesting pair of wood ducks at the other‖ (qtd. in Suh).\nRather than hiding from humanity, Dillard seems to be trying to understand it through nature. In Pilgrim\nshe reports buying a goldfish, which she names Ellery Channing. She recalls once seeing through a microscope\n―red blood cells whip, one by one, through the capillaries‖ of yet another goldfish (124). Now watching Ellery\nChanning, she sees the blood in his body as a bond between fish and human being: ―Those red blood cells are\ncoursing in Ellery‘s tail now, too, in just that way, and through his mouth and eyes as well, and through mine‖\n(125). Gary McIlroy observes that this blood, ―a symbol of the sanctity of life, is a common bond between\nDillard and the fish, between animal and human life in general, and between Dillard and other people‖ (115).\nHaley 3\nFor Dillard, the terror and unpredictability of death unify all life. The most sinister image in Pilgrim—\none that haunts Dillard—is that of the frog and the water bug. Dillard reports walking along an embankment\nscaring frogs into the water when one frog refused to budge. As Dillard leaned over to investigate, the frog\n―slowly crumpled and began to sag. The spirit vanished from his eyes as if snuffed. His skin emptied and\ndropped; his very skull seemed to collapse and settle like a kicked tent‖ (6). The frog was the victim of a water\nbug that injects poisons to ―dissolve the victim‘s muscles and bones and organs‖ (6). Such events lead Dillard\nto wonder about a creator who would make all life ―power and beauty, grace tangled in a rapture with violence‖\n(8). Human beings no less than frogs and water bugs are implicated in this tangle.\nDillard is equally as disturbed by birth as by death. In a chapter of Pilgrim called ―Fecundity,‖ she\nfocuses on the\nundeniable reproductive urge of entire species. Her attitude is far from sentimental:\nI don‘t know what it is about fecundity that so appalls. I suppose it is the teeming evidence that\nbirth and growth, which we value, are ubiquitous and blind, that life itself is so astonishingly\ncheap, that nature is as careless as it is bountiful, and that with extravagance goes a crushing waste\nthat will one day include our own cheap lives. (160)\nThe cheapness and brutality of life are problems Dillard\nwrestles with, wondering which is ―amiss‖: the world, a ―monster,‖ or human beings, with their ―excessive\nemotions‖\n(177-78). No matter how hard she tries to leave human society, Dillard has no choice but to ―bring human\nvalues to the creek‖ (179). The violent, seemingly pointless birth and death of all life are, spiritually,\nHaley 4\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 78\ntwo branches of the same creek, the creek that waters the world. . . . We\ncould have planned things more mercifully, perhaps, but our plan would\nnever get off the drawing board until we agreed to the very compromising\nterms that are the only ones that being offers. (180)\nFor Dillard, accepting the monstrousness as well as the beauty of ―being‖ is the price all living things pay for\nfreedom.\nIn ―The Waters of Separation,‖ the final chapter of Pilgrim, Dillard writes about a winged maple key, or\nseed. At this point in the book, the critic Sandra Humble Johnson notes, Dillard ―has been humbled and\nemptied; she can no longer apply effort to her search for meaning in a parasitic world‖ (4). It is the winter\nsolstice—the shortest day of the year. And then Dillard spies the maple key descending to earth and\ngermination. ―It rose, just before it would have touched a thistle, and hovered pirouetting in one spot, then\ntwirled on and finally came to rest‖ (267). The key moved, says Dillard, ―like a creature muscled and vigorous,\nor a creature spread thin to that other wind, the wind of the spirit . . . , a generous, unending breath‖ (268).\nDillard vows to see the maple key in all of the earth and in herself. ―If I am a maple key falling, at least I can\ntwirl‖ (268).\nAccording to the critic John Becker, ―Annie Dillard does not walk out on ordinary life in order to bear\nwitness against it‖; instead, she uses the distance from other people ―to make meaning out of the grotesque\ndisjointedness of man and nature‖ (408). Gary McIlroy says, nonetheless, that Dillard ―does not succeed in\nencompassing within her vision any but the most fragmentary consequences for society at large‖ (116).\nPossibly both are correct. In Pilgrim at Tinker Creek, Annie Dillard suggests a vision of identity among all\nliving things\nHaley 5\nthat could inform modern humanity‘s efforts to thrive in harmony with its environment, but she does not make\nthe leap to practicalities. Life, she says, ―is a faint tracing on the surface of a mystery. . . . We must somehow\ntake a wider view, look at the whole landscape, really see it, and describe what‘s going on here‖ (9). The\ndescription, and acting on it, may take generations. As we proceed, however, we may be guided by Dillard‘s\nefforts to mend the disjointedness, to see that human beings and maple keys alike twirl equally.\n\nFOWLcp44-48.doc - 79\nHaley 6\nWorks Cited\nBecker, John E. ―Science and the Sacred: From Walden to\nTinker Creek.‖ Thought: A Review of Culture and Idea\n62 (1989): 400-13.\nDillard, Annie.
