3\n49\nCHAPTER\nWorking with the Goals\nand Requirements\nof the Disciplines\nChapter 6 outlines the general concerns of subject, purpose, and audience that figure in most\nacademic writing situations. The disciplines have more in common as well: methods of gathering\nevidence, kinds of assignments, scholarly tools, language conventions, and styles for source\ncitations and document formats. This chapter introduces these common goals and requirements.\nThe following chapters then distinguish the disciplines along the same lines, focusing on literature\n(Chapter 50), other humanities (51), the social sciences (52), and the natural and applied sciences\n(53).\n49a Using methods and evidence\nThe methodology of a discipline is the way its practitioners study their subjects—that is, how\nthey proceed when investigating the answers to questions. Methodology relates to the way\npractitioners analyze evidence and ideas. For instance, a literary critic and a social historian would\nprobably approach Shakespeare’s Hamlet quite differently: the literary critic might study the play\nfor a theme among its poetic images; the historian might examine the play’s relation to\nShakespeare’s context—England at the turn of the seventeenth century.\nWhatever their approach, academic writers do not compose entirely out of their personal\nexperience. Rather, they combine the evidence of their experience with that appropriate to the\ndiscipline, drawing well-supported conclusions about their subjects. The evidence of the discipline\ncomes from research like that described in Chapters 42–44—from primary or secondary sources.\nPrimary sources are firsthand or original accounts, such as historical documents, works of\nart, and reports on experiments that the writer has conducted. When you use primary sources,\nyou conduct original research, generating your own evidence. You might use your analysis of\na painting as evidence for an interpretation of the painting. Or you might use data from your\nown survey of students to support your conclusions about students’ attitudes.\nSecondary sources are books and articles written about primary sources. Much academic\nwriting requires that you use such sources to spark, extend, or support your own ideas, as\nwhen you review the published opinions on your subject before contributing conclusions from\nyour original research.\n49b Understanding writing assignments\nFor most academic writing, your primary purpose will be either to explain something to your\nreaders or to persuade them to accept your conclusions. To achieve your purpose, you will adapt\nyour writing process to the writing situation, particularly to your readers’ likely expectations for\nevidence and how you use it. Most assignments will contain key words that imply some of these\nexpectations—words such as compare, define, analyze, and illustrate that express customary ways\nof thinking about and organizing a vast range of subjects. Pages 91–100 and 145 explore these so-\ncalled patterns of development. You should be aware of them and alert to the wording in\nassignments that directs you to use them.\n\nFOWLcp49.doc - 4\n49c Using tools and language\nWhen you write in an academic discipline, you use the scholarly tools of that discipline,\nparticularly its periodical indexes. In addition, you may use the aids developed by practitioners of\nthe discipline for efficiently and effectively approaching research, conducting it, and recording the\nfindings. Many of these aids, such as a system for recording evidence from sources, are discussed\nin Chapters 42–44 and can be adapted to any discipline. Other aids are discussed in the following\nchapters.\nPay close attention to the texts assigned in a course and any materials given out in class, for\nthese items may introduce you to valuable references and other research aids, and they will use the\nspecialized language of the discipline. This specialized language allows practitioners to write to\neach other both efficiently and precisely. It also furthers certain concerns of the discipline, such as\naccuracy and objectivity. Scientists, for example, try to interpret their data objectively, so they\navoid undoubtedly, obviously, and other words that slant conclusions. Some of the language\nconventions like this one are discussed in the following chapters. As you gain experience in a\nparticular discipline, keep alert for such conventions and train yourself to follow them.\n49d Following styles for source citations\nand document format\nMost disciplines publish journals that require authors to use a certain style for source citations\nand a certain format for documents. In turn, most instructors in a discipline require the same of\nstudents writing papers for their courses.\nWhen you cite your sources, you tell readers which ideas and information you borrowed and\nwhere they can find your sources. Thus source citations indicate how much knowledge you have\nand how broad and deep your research was. They also help you avoid plagiarism, the serious\noffense of presenting the words, ideas, and data of others as if they were your own. (See Chapter 45\non avoiding plagiarism.)\nDocument format includes such features as margins and the placement of the title. But it also\nextends to special elements of the manuscript, such as tables or an abstract, that may be required by\nthe discipline.\nChapters 51–53 direct you to the style guides published by different disciplines and outline\nthe requirements of the ones used most often. If your instructor does not require a particular style,\nuse that of the Modern Language Association, which is described and illustrated at length in\nChapter 47.\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp49.doc - 5\nVisit the companion Web site for more help with writing in the academic\ndisciplines.\nGuidelines for academic writers\nBecome familiar with the methodology and the kinds of evidence for the discipline in which you\nare writing.\nAnalyze the special demands of each assignment. The questions you set out to answer, the\nassertions you wish to support, will govern how you choose your sources and evidence.\nBecome familiar with the discipline’s specialized tools and language.\nUse the discipline’s style for source citations and document format.\n\nFOWLcp50.doc - 1\n50\nCHAPTER\nReading and Writing\nAbout Literature\nBy Sylvan Barnet\nWhy read literature? Let’s approach this question indirectly by asking why people write\nliterature. A thousand years ago a Japanese writer, Lady Murasaki, offered an answer. Here is one\nof her characters talking about what motivates a writer:\nAgain and again something in one’s own life or in the life around one will seem so important that one\ncannot bear to let it pass into oblivion. There must never come a time, the writer feels, when people do not\nknow about this.\nWhen we read certain works—Murasaki’s The Tale of Genji is one of them—we share this feeling;\nwe are caught up in the writer’s world, whether it is the Denmark of Shakespeare’s Hamlet or the\nAmerica of Toni Morrison’s Beloved. We read literature because it gives us an experience that\nseems important, usually an experience that is both new and familiar. A common way of putting\nthis is to say that reading broadens us and helps us understand our own experience.\n50a Using the methods and evidence of literary analysis\nWhen we read nonliterary writings, it may be enough to get the gist of the argument; in fact,\nwe may have to peer through a good deal of wordiness to find the heart of the matter—say, three\nclaims on behalf of capital punishment. But when we read a story, a poem, or a play, we must pay\nextremely close attention to what might be called the feel of the words. For instance, the word\nwoods in Robert Frost’s ―Stopping by Woods on a Snowy Evening‖ has a rural, folksy quality that\nforest doesn’t have, and many such small distinctions contribute to the poem’s effect.\nLiterary authors are concerned with presenting human experience concretely, with showing\nrather than telling. Consider the following proverb and an unmemorable paraphrase of it:\nA rolling stone gathers no moss.\nIf a rock is always moving around, vegetation won’t have a chance to grow on it.\nThe familiar original offers a small but complete world: hard (stone) and soft (moss), inorganic and\norganic, at rest and in motion. The original is also shapely: each noun (stone, moss) has one\nsyllable, and each word of motion (rolling, gathers) has two syllables, with the accent on the first\nof the two. Such relationships unify the proverb into a pleasing whole that stays in our minds.\n1 Reading a work of literature\nReading literature critically involves interacting with a text. The techniques complement those\nfor critically reading any text, so if you haven’t read Chapter 8 on such reading, you should do so.\nResponding critically is a matter not of making negative judgments but of analyzing the parts,\ninterpreting their meanings, seeing how the parts relate, and evaluating significance or quality.\nPreviewing and responding\nYou can preview a literary text somewhat as you can preview any other text. You may gauge\nthe length of the text to determine whether you can read it in one sitting, and you may read a\n\nFOWLcp50.doc - 2\nbiographical note to learn about the author. In a literary text, however, you won’t find aids such as\nsection headings or summaries that can make previewing other texts especially informative. You\nhave to dive into the words themselves.\nDo write while reading. If you own the book you are reading, don’t hesitate to underline or\nhighlight passages that especially interest you for one reason or another. Don’t hesitate to annotate\nthe margins, indicating your pleasures, displeasures, and uncertainties with remarks such as Nice\ndetail or Do we need this long description? or Not believable. If you don’t own the book, make\nthese notes on separate sheets or on your computer.\nAn effective way to interact with a text is to keep a reading journal. A journal is not a diary\nin which you record your doings; instead, it is a place to develop and store your reflections on what\nyou read, such as an answer to a question you may have posed in the margin of the text. You could\nmake an entry in the form of a letter to the author or from one character to another. In many\nliterature courses, students collaborate to develop their understanding of a literary work. In such a\ncase, you may want to use your journal to reflect on what other students have said—for instance,\nwhy your opinion differs so much from someone else’s.\nYou can keep a reading journal in a notebook or on your computer. Some readers prefer a\ntwo-column format like that illustrated on page 154, with summaries, paraphrases, and quotations\nfrom the text on the left and with their own responses to these passages on the right. Or you may\nprefer a less structured format like that illustrated on page 740.\nReading a sample story\nHere is a very short story by Kate Chopin (1851–1904). (The last name is pronounced in the\nFrench way, something like ―show pan.‖) Following the story are a student’s annotations and\njournal entry on the story.\nKate Chopin\nThe Story of an Hour\nKnowing that Mrs. Mallard was afflicted with a heart trouble, great care was taken to break to her as\ngently as possible the news of her husband’s death.\nIt was her sister Josephine who told her, in broken sentences, veiled hints that revealed in half\nconcealing. Her husband’s friend Richards was there, too, near her. It was he who had been in the\nnewspaper office when intelligence of the railroad disaster was received, with Brently Mallard’s name\nleading the list of ―killed.‖ He had only taken the time to assure himself of its truth by a second telegram,\nand had hastened to forestall any less careful, less tender friend in bearing the sad message.\nShe did not hear the story as many women have heard the same, with a paralyzed inability to accept\nits significance. She wept at once with sudden, wild abandonment, in her sister’s arms. When the storm of\ngrief had spent itself she went away to her room alone. She would have no one follow her.\nThere stood, facing the open window, a comfortable, roomy armchair. Into this she sank, pressed\ndown by a physical exhaustion that haunted her body and seemed to reach into her soul.\nShe could see in the open square before her house the tops of trees that were all aquiver with the\nnew spring life. The delicious breath of rain was in the air. In the street below a peddler was crying his\nwares. The notes of a distant song which some one was singing reached her faintly, and countless\nsparrows were twittering in the eaves.\nThere were patches of blue sky showing here and there through the clouds that had met and piled\none above the other in the west facing her window.\nShe sat with her head thrown back upon the cushion of the chair quite motionless, except when a\nsob came up into her throat and shook her, as a child who has cried itself to sleep continues to sob in its\ndreams.\nShe was young, with a fair, calm face, whose lines bespoke repression and even a certain strength.\nBut now there was a dull stare in her eyes, whose gaze was fixed away off yonder on one of those patches\nof blue sky. It was not a glance of reflection, but rather indicated a suspension of intelligent thought.\nThere was something coming to her and she was waiting for it, fearfully. What was it? She did not\nknow; it was too subtle and elusive to name. But she felt it creeping out of the sky, reaching toward her\nthrough the sounds, the scents, the color that filled the air.\nNow her bosom rose and fell tumultuously. She was beginning to recognize this thing that was\napproaching to possess her, and she was striving to beat it back with her will—as powerless as her two\nwhite slender hands would have been.\nWhen she abandoned herself a little whispered word escaped her slightly parted lips. She said it over\nand over under her breath: ―Free, free, free!‖ The vacant stare and the look of terror that had followed it\nwent from her eyes. They stayed keen and bright. Her pulses beat fast, and the coursing blood warmed\nand relaxed every inch of her body.\n\nFOWLcp50.doc - 3\nShe did not stop to ask if it were not a monstrous joy that held her. A clear and exalted perception\nenabled her to dismiss the suggestion as trivial.\nShe knew that she would weep again when she saw the kind, tender hands folded in death; the face\nthat had never looked save with love upon her, fixed and gray and dead. But she saw beyond that bitter\nmoment a long procession of years to come that would belong to her absolutely. And she opened and\nspread her arms out to them in welcome.\nThere would be no one to live for her during those coming years; she would live for herself. There\nwould be no powerful will bending her in the blind persistence with which men and women believe they\nhave a right to impose a private will upon a fellow creature. A kind intention or a cruel intention made the\nact seem no less a crime as she looked upon it in that brief moment of illumination.\nAnd yet she had loved him—sometimes. Often she had not. What did it matter! What could love,\nthe unsolved mystery, count for in face of this possession of self-assertion which she suddenly recognized\nas the strongest impulse of her being.\n―Free! Body and soul free!‖ she kept whispering.\nJosephine was kneeling before the closed door with her lips to the keyhole, imploring for admission.\n―Louise, open the door! I beg; open the door—you will make yourself ill. What are you doing, Louise?\nFor heaven’s sake open the door.‖\n―Go away. I am not making myself ill.‖ No; she was drinking in the very elixir of life through that\nopen window.\nHer fancy was running riot along those days ahead of her. Spring days, and summer days, and all\nsorts of days that would be her own. She breathed a quick prayer that life might be long. It was only\nyesterday she had thought with a shudder that life might be long.\nShe arose at length and opened the door to her sister’s importunities. There was a feverish triumph\nin her eyes, and she carried herself unwittingly like a goddess of Victory. She clasped her sister’s waist\nand together they descended the stairs. Richards stood waiting for them at the bottom.\nSome one was opening the front door with a latchkey. It was Brently Mallard who entered, a little\ntravel-stained, composedly carrying his grip-sack and umbrella. He had been far from the scene of\naccident, and did not even know there had been one. He stood amazed at Josephine’s piercing cry; at\nRichards’ quick motion to screen him from the view of his wife.\nBut Richards was too late.\nWhen the doctors came they said she had died of heart disease—of joy that kills.\nFollowing a student’s work\nIn this chapter we’ll follow the analysis and writing of a student, Janet Vong, to see one\napproach to Chopin’s story. Vong first annotated the story while reading it. The opening five\nparagraphs, with her notes, appear below:\nKnowing that Mrs. Mallard was afflicted with a heart trouble, great care was\ntaken to break to her as gently as possible the news of her husband’s death.\nIt was her sister Josephine who told her, in broken\nsentences, veiled hints that revealed in half concealing.\nHer husband’s friend Richards was there, too, near her. It\nwas he who had been in the newspaper office when\nintelligence of the railroad disaster was received, with\nBrently Mallard’s name leading the list of ―killed.‖ He had\nonly taken the time to assure himself of its truth by a\nsecond telegram, and had hastened to forestall any less\ncareful, less tender friend in bearing the sad message.\nShe did not hear the story as many women have heard the\nsame, with a paralyzed inability to accept its significance.\nShe wept at once with sudden, wild aban-donment, in her\nsister’s arms. When the storm of grief had spent itself she\nwent away to her room alone. She would have no one\nfollow her.\nThere stood, facing the open window, a comfortable,\nroomy armchair. Into this she sank, pressed down by a\n\nFOWLcp50.doc - 4\nphysical exhaustion that haunted her body and seemed to\nreach into her soul.\nShe could see in the open square before her house the tops of trees that were all\naquiver with the new spring life. The delicious breath of rain was in the air. In the\nstreet below a peddler was crying his wares. The notes of a distant song which some\none was singing reached her faintly, and countless sparrows were twittering in the\neaves.\nWriting in her journal, Vong posed questions about the story—critical points, curiosities\nabout characters, possible implications:\nTitle nothing special. What might be a better title?\nCould a woman who loved her husband be so heartless? Is she heartless? Did she love him?\nWhat are (were) Louise’s feelings about her husband?\nDid she want too much? What did she want?\nCould this story happen today? Feminist interpretation?\nSister (Josephine)—a busybody?\nTricky ending—but maybe it could be true.\n―And yet she had loved him—sometimes. Often she had not.‖ Why does one love someone ―sometimes‖?\nIrony: plot has reversal. Are characters ironic too?\nVong’s journal entry illustrates brainstorming—the discovery technique of listing ideas (or\nquestions) however they occur, without editing (see pp. 21–22). Another productive journal\ntechnique is focused freewriting—concentrating on a single issue (such as one of Vong’s\nquestions) and writing nonstop for a set amount of time, again without editing (p. 20).\n2 Analyzing a work of literature\nLike any discipline, the study of literature involves particular frameworks of analysis—\nparticular ways of seeing literary works that help determine what parts the critical reader identifies\nand how he or she interprets them. For instance, some critics look at a literary work mainly as an\nartifact of the particular time and culture in which it was created, while other critics emphasize the\nwork’s effect on its readers.\nThis chapter emphasizes so-called formalist criticism, which focuses on a literary work\nprimarily as something to be understood in itself. This critical framework engages the reader\nimmediately in the work of literature, without requiring extensive historical or cultural background,\nand introduces the conventional elements of literature that all critical approaches discuss, even\nthough they view the elements differently. The box on the next two pages lists these elements—\nplot, characters, setting, and so on—and offers questions about each one that can help you think\nconstructively and imagi-\nnatively about what you read.\nOne significant attribute of a literary work is its meaning, or what we can interpret to be its\nmeaning. Readers may well disagree over the persuasiveness of someone’s argument, but they will\nrarely disagree over its meaning. With literature, however, disagreements over meaning occur all\nthe time because (as we have seen) literature shows rather than tells: it gives us concrete images of\nimagined\nhuman experiences, but it usually does not say how we ought to understand the images.\nFurther, readers bring to their reading not only different critical views, as noted earlier, but\nalso different personal experiences. A woman who has recently lost her husband may interpret ―The\nStory of an Hour‖ differently from most other readers. Or a story that bores a reader at age fifteen\nmay deeply move him at twenty-five. The words on the page remain the same, but their meaning\nchanges.\nIn writing about literature, then, we can offer only our interpreta-tion of meaning rather than\nthe meaning. Still, most people agree that there are limits to interpretation: it must be supported by\nevidence that a reasonable reader finds at least plausible if not totally convincing. For instance, the\nstudent who says that in ―The Story of an Hour‖ Mrs. Mallard does not die but merely falls into a\ndeathlike trance goes beyond the permissible limits because the story offers no evidence for such an\ninterpretation.\n\nFOWLcp50.doc - 5\n3 Using evidence in writing about literature\nThe evidence for a literary analysis always comes from at least one primary source (the work\nor works being discussed) and may come from secondary sources (critical and historical works).\n(See\np. 733 for more on primary and secondary sources.) For example, if you were writing about\nChopin’s ―The Story of an Hour,‖ the primary material would be the story itself, and the secondary\nmaterial (if you used it) might be critical studies of Chopin.\nThe bulk of your evidence in writing about literature will usually be quotations from the work,\nalthough you will occasionally summarize or paraphrase as well (see pp. 617–20). When using\nquotations, keep in mind the criteria in the box on page 747.\nYour instructor will probably tell you if you are expected to consult secondary sources for an\nassignment. They can help you understand a writer’s work, but your primary concern should\nalways be the work itself, not what critics A, B, and C say about it. In general, then, quote or\nsummarize secondary material sparingly. And always cite your sources.\n50b Understanding writing assignments in literature\nA literature instructor may ask you to write one or more of the following types of papers. The\nfirst two are the most common.\nA literary analysis paper: Give your ideas about a work of\nliterature—your interpretation of its meaning, context, or representations based on specific\nwords, characters, and events.\nA literary research paper: Combine analysis of a literary work with research about the work\nand perhaps its author. A literary research paper draws on both primary and secondary\nsources. For example, you might respond to what scholars have written about the symbolism\nin a play by Tennessee Williams, or you might research medieval England as a way to\nunderstand the context of Chaucer’s Canterbury Tales.\nA personal response or reaction paper: Give your thoughts and feelings about a work of\nliterature. For example, you might compare a novel’s description of a city with your\nexperience of the same city.\nA book review: Give a summary of a book and a judgment about the book’s value. In a\nreview of a novel, for example, you might discuss whether the plot is interesting, the\ncharacters are believable, and the writing style is enjoyable. You might also compare the work\nto other works by the author.\nA theater review: Give your reactions to and opinions about a theatrical performance. You\nmight summarize the plot of the play, describe the characters, identify the prominent themes,\nevaluate the other elements (writing, performances, direction, stage setting), and make a\nrecommendation to potential viewers.\n50c Using the tools and language of literary analysis\n1 Writing tools\nThe fundamental tool for writing about literature is reading\ncritically. Asking analytical questions such as those on pages 741–42 can help you focus your\nideas. In addition, keeping a reading\njournal can help you develop your thoughts. Keep careful, well-\norganized notes on any research materials. Finally, discuss the work with others who have read it.\nThey may offer reactions and insights that will help you shape your own ideas.\n2 Language considerations\nUse the present tense of verbs to describe both the action in\na literary work (Brently Mallard suddenly appears) and the writing\n\nFOWLcp50.doc - 6\nof an author (Chopin briefly describes the view or In his essay he comments that . . .). Use the past\ntense to describe events that actually occurred in the past (Chopin was born in 1851).\nSome instructors discourage students from using the first-\nperson I (as in I felt sorry for the character) in writing about literature. At least use I sparingly to\navoid sounding egotistical. Rephrase sentences to avoid using I unnecessarily—for instance, The\ncharacter evokes the reader’s sympathy.\n3 Research sources\nIn addition to these resources on literature, you may also want to consult some on other\nhumanities (pp. 761–64).\nSpecialized encyclopedias, dictionaries, and bibliographies\nBibliographical Guide to the Study of the Literature of the USA\nCambridge Bibliography of English Literature\nCambridge Encyclopedia of Language\nCambridge Guide to Literature in English\nDictionary of Literary Biography\nHandbook to Literature\nLiterary Criticism Index\nMcGraw-Hill Encyclopedia of World Drama\nMLA International Bibliography of Books and Articles on the Modern Languages and Literatures\nNew Princeton Encyclopedia of Poetry and Poetics\nOxford Companion to American Literature\nOxford Companion to the Theatre\nSchomburg Center Guide to Black Literature from the Eighteenth Century to the Present\nIndexes\nAbstracts of Folklore Studies\nDissertation Abstracts International (doctoral dissertations)\nHumanities Index\nLiterary Criticism Index\nMLA International Bibliography of Books and Articles on the Modern Languages and Literatures\nBook reviews\nBook Review Digest\nBook Review Index\nIndex to Book Reviews in the Humanities\nWeb sources\nFor updates of these sources and URLs, visit ablongman.com/ littlebrown.\nAlex Catalog of Electronic Texts (infomotions.com/alex)\nEServer (eserver.org)\nInternet Public Library: Online Literary Criticism (ipl.org/div/litcrit)\nKey Sites on American Literature (usinfo.state.gov/products/pubs/oal/ amlitweb.htm)\nLiterary Index (www.galenet.com/servlet/LitIndex)\nLiterary Resources on the Net (andromeda.rutgers.edu/~jlynch/Lit)\nOnline Books Page (online books.library.upenn.edu)\nVoice of the Shuttle: Drama, Theater, and Performance Art Studies\n(vos.ucsb.edu/browse.asp?id=782)\nVoice of the Shuttle: Literature (in English) (vos.ucsb.edu/\nbrowse.asp?id=3)\nVoice of the Shuttle: Literatures (Other than English) (vos.ucsb.edu/ browse.asp?id=2719)\n50d Citing sources and formatting documents\nin writing about literature\nUnless your instructor specifies otherwise, use the style of the Modern Language Association\n(MLA), detailed in Chapter 47. In this style, parenthetical citations in the text of the paper refer to a\n\nFOWLcp50.doc - 7\nlist of works cited at the end. Sample papers illustrating this style appear in Chapter 48 as well as in\nthis chapter.\nUse MLA format for headings, margins, and other elements, as detailed on pages 687–89.\n50e Drafting and revising a literary analysis\nThe process for writing a literary analysis is similar to that for any other kind of essay: once\nyou’ve done the reading and thought about it, you need to focus your ideas, gather evidence, draft,\nand revise.\n1 Conceiving a thesis\nAfter reading, rereading, and making notes, you probably will be able to\nformulate a tentative thesis statement—an assertion of your main point,\nyour argument. (For more on thesis statements, see\npp. 27–31.) Clear the air by glancing over your notes and by jotting down\na few especially promising ideas—brief statements of what you think your\nkey points may be and their main support. One approach\nis to seek patterns in the work, such as recurring words, phrases,\nimages, events, symbols, or other elements. (Go back to the work,\nif necessary, to expand the patterns your notes reveal.) Such pat-\nterns can help you see themes both in the work itself and in your ideas\nabout it.\nConsidering Kate Chopin’s ―The Story of an Hour,‖ Janet Vong at first explored the idea that\nMrs. Mallard, the main character, was unrealistic and thus unconvincing. (See Vong’s journal entry\non\np. 740.) But the more Vong examined the story and her notes, the more she was impressed by a\npattern of ironies, or reversals, that actually helped to make Mrs. Mallard believable. In her journal\nVong explored the idea that the many small reversals paved the way for Mrs. Mallard’s own\nreversal from grief to joy:\ntitle? ―Ironies in an Hour‖ (?) ―An Hour of Irony‖ (?) ―Kate Chopin’s Irony‖ (?)\nthesis: irony at end is prepared for\nchief irony: Mrs. M. dies just as she is beginning to enjoy life\nsmaller ironies:\n1. ‘‘sad message’’ brings her joy\n2. Richards is ‘‘too late’’ at end\n3. Richards is too early at start\n4. ‘‘joy that kills’’\n5. death brings joy and life\nFrom these notes Vong developed her thesis statement:\nThe irony of the ending is believable partly because it is consistent with earlier ironies in the story.\nThis thesis statement asserts a specific idea that can be developed and convincingly argued with\nevidence from Chopin’s story. A good thesis statement will neither assert a fact (Mrs. Mallard dies\nsoon after hearing that her husband has died) nor overgeneralize (The story is an insult to women).\n2 Gathering evidence\nIn writing about literature, you use mainly evidence gathered from the work itself: quotations\nand sometimes paraphrases that\nsupport your ideas about the work. You can see examples of such quoting and paraphrasing in\nJanet Vong’s final draft on pages 750–51. The box below offers guidelines for using quotations in\nliterary analysis.\n\nFOWLcp50.doc - 8\nYou may wonder how much you should summarize the plot of the work. A brief plot summary\ncan be helpful to readers who are unfamiliar with the work. Sometimes plot elements place your\nideas in the context of the work or remind readers where your quotations come from. Plot elements\nmay even be used as evidence, as Vong uses the ironic ending of the Chopin story. But plot\nsummary is not literary analysis, and summary alone is not sufficient evidence to support a thesis.\nKeep any plot summaries brief and to the point.\nFor a literary research paper, evidence will come from the work itself and from secondary\nsources such as scholarly works and critical appraisals. The thesis and principal ideas of the paper\nmust still be your own, but you may supplement your reading of the work with the views of\nrespected scholars or critics. Sometimes you may choose to build your own argument in part by\ndisputing others’ views. However you draw on secondary sources, remember that they must be\nclearly identified and documented, even when you use your own words.\nNote You can find student essays on the Web that may lead you to other sources or may\nsuggest ideas you hadn’t considered. If you want to use another student’s paper as a secondary\nsource, you must evaluate it with special care because it will not have passed through a reviewing\nprocess, as an article in a scholarly journal does. (See pp. 602–09 on evaluating online sources.)\nYou must also, of course, clearly identify and document the source: borrowing other students’ ideas\nor words without credit is plagiarism. (See\npp. 629–37.)\n3 Writing a draft\nDrafting your essay is your opportunity to develop your thesis or to discover it if you haven’t\nalready. The draft below was actually Janet Vong’s second: she deleted some digressions from her\nfirst draft and added more evidence for her points. The numbers in parentheses refer to the pages\nfrom which she drew the quotations. (See pp. 648–56 on this form of documentation.) Ask your\ninstructor whether you should always give such citations, especially for a short poem or story like\nChopin’s.\nIronies in an Hour\nAfter we know how the story turns out, if we reread it we find irony at the very start, as is true of many\nother stories. Mrs. Mallard’s friends assume, mistakenly, that Mrs. Mallard was deeply in love with her\nhusband, Brently Mallard. They take great care to tell her gently of his death. The friends mean well, and in fact\nthey\ndo well. They bring her an hour of life, an hour of freedom. They think their news\nis sad. Mrs. Mallard at first expresses grief when she hears the news, but soon she finds joy in it. So Richards’s\n―sad message‖ (23), though sad in Richards’s eyes, is in fact a happy message.\nAmong the ironic details is the statement that when Mallard entered the house, Richards tried to conceal\nhim from Mrs. Mallard, but ―Richards was too late‖ (24). This is ironic because earlier Richards ―hastened‖\n(23) to bring his sad message; if he had been too late at the start, Brently Mallard would have arrived at home\nfirst, and Mrs. Mallard’s life would not have ended an hour later but would simply have gone on as it had been.\nYet another irony at the end of the story is the diagnosis of the doctors. The doctors say she died of ―heart\ndisease—of joy that kills‖ (24). In one sense the doctors are right: Mrs. Mallard has experienced a great joy.\nBut of course the doctors totally misunderstand the joy that kills her.\nThe central irony resides not in the well-intentioned but ironic actions of Richards, or in the\nunconsciously ironic words of the doctors, but in her own life. She ―sometimes‖ (24) loved her husband, but in\na way she has been dead. Now, his apparent death brings her new life. This new life comes to her at the season\nof the year when ―the tops of trees . . . were all aquiver with the new spring life‖ (23). But, ironically, her new\nlife will last only an hour. She looks forward to ―summer days‖ (24), but she will not see even the end of this\nspring day. Her years of marriage were ironic. They brought her a sort of living death instead of joy. Her new\nlife is ironic, too. It grows out of her moment of grief for her supposedly dead husband, and her vision of a new\nlife is cut short.\n4 Revising and editing\nAs in other writing, use at least two drafts to revise and edit, so that you can attend separately\nto the big structural issues and the smaller surface problems. See pages 51 and 58–59 for general\nrevision and editing checklists. The additional checklist below can help you with a literary analysis.\nJanet Vong’s final draft appears below with annotations that highlight some of its features.\nAn essay on fiction (no secondary sources)\nJanet Vong\n\nFOWLcp50.doc - 9\nMr. Romano\nEnglish 102\n20 February 2006\nIronies of Life in Kate Chopin’s\n―The Story of an Hour‖\nKate Chopin’s ―The Story of an Hour‖—which takes only a few minutes to read—has an ironic ending:\nMrs. Mallard dies just when she is beginning to live. On first reading, the ending seems almost too ironic for\nbelief. On rereading the story, however, one sees that the ending is\nbelievable partly because it is consistent with other ironies in the story.\nIrony appears at the very start of the story. Because Mrs. Mallard’s friends and her sister assume,\nmistakenly, that she was deeply in love with her husband, Brently Mallard, they take great care to tell her gently\nof his death. They mean well, and in fact they do well, bringing her an hour of life, an hour of joyous freedom,\nbut it is ironic that they think their news is sad. True, Mrs. Mallard at first expresses grief when she hears the\nnews, but soon (unknown to the others) she finds joy. So Richards’s ―sad\nmessage‖ (23), though sad in Richards’s eyes, is in fact a happy message.\nAmong the small but significant ironic details is the statement near the end of the story that when\nMallard entered the house, Richards tried to conceal him from Mrs. Mallard, but ―Richards was too late‖ (24).\nAlmost at the start of the story, in the second paragraph, Richards ―hastened‖ (23) to bring his sad news. But if\nRichards had arrived too late at the start, Brently Mallard would have arrived at home first, and Mrs. Mallard’s\nlife would not have ended an hour later but would simply have gone on as it had been. Yet another irony at the\nend of the story is the diagnosis of the doctors. They say she died of ―heart disease—of joy that kills‖ (24). In\none sense they are right: Mrs. Mallard has for the last hour experienced a great joy. But of course the doctors\ntotally misunderstand the joy that kills her. It is not joy at seeing her husband alive, but her realization that the\ngreat joy she experienced during the last hour is over.\nAll of these ironic details add richness to the story, but the\ncentral irony resides not in the well-intentioned but ironic actions of Richards, or in the unconsciously ironic\nwords of the doctors, but in Mrs. Mallard’s own life. She ―sometimes‖ (24) loved her husband, but in a way she\nhas been dead, a body subjected to her husband’s will. Now, his apparent death brings her new life.\nAppropriately, this new life comes to her at the season of the year when ―the tops of trees . . . were all aquiver\nwith the new spring life‖ (23). But, ironically, her new life will last only an hour. She is ―Free, free, free‖\n(24)—but only until her husband walks through the doorway. She looks forward to ―summer days‖ (24), but\nshe will not see even the end of this spring day. If her years of marriage were ironic, bringing her a sort of\nliving death instead of joy, her new life is ironic, too, not only because it grows out of her moment of grief for\nher supposedly dead husband, but also because her vision of ―a long procession of years‖ (24) is cut short\nwithin an hour on a spring day.\n[New page.]\nWork Cited\nChopin, Kate. ―The Story of an Hour.‖ An Introduction to Literature: Fiction, Poetry, and Drama. Ed. Sylvan\nBarnet, William Burto, and William E. Cain. 13th ed. New York: Longman, 2004. 23-24.\n50f\nWriting about fiction, poetry, and drama\nA work of literature falls into a category, or genre—fiction, poetry, or drama—depending on\nhow it is structured. The different genres of literature require different approaches in writing.\n1 Writing about fiction\nThe ―Questions for a literary analysis‖ on pages 741–42 will help you think about any work of\nliterature, including a story or novel, and find a topic to write on. The following questions provide\nadditional prompts for thinking about fiction. For an example of writing about fiction, see Janet\nVong’s essay opposite and above.\n2 Writing about poetry\nTwo types of essays on poetry are especially common. One is an analysis of some aspect of the\npoem in relation to the whole—for instance, the changes in the speaker’s tone or the functions of\nmeter and rhyme. The second is an explication, a line-by-line (sometimes almost word-by-word)\nreading that seeks to make explicit everything that is implicit in the poem. Thus an explication of\nthe first line of Robert Frost’s ―Stopping by Woods on a Snowy Evening‖ (the line goes ―Whose\nwoods these are I think I know‖) might call attention to the tentativeness of the line (―I think I\nknow‖) and to the fact that the words are not in the normal order (―I think I know whose woods\nthese are‖). These features might support the explanation that the poet is introducing—very\nquietly—a note of the unusual, in preparation for the experience that follows. Although one might\n\nFOWLcp50.doc - 10\nconceivably explicate a long poem, the method is so detailed that in practice writers usually confine\nit to short poems or to short passages from long poems.\nThe ―Questions for a literary analysis‖ on pages 741–42 will help you think about any work of\nliterature, including a poem, and find a topic to write on. The questions on the facing page provide\nadditional ways to think about poetry.\nAn essay on poetry with secondary sources\nThe following sample paper on a short poem by Gwendolyn Brooks illustrates a literary\nanalysis that draws not only on the poem itself but also on secondary sources—that is, critical\nworks about the poem. In the opening paragraph, for instance, the writer uses brief quotations from\ntwo secondary sources to establish the problem, the topic that he will address. These quotations,\nlike the two later quotations from secondary material, are used to make points, not to pad the essay.\nNote In the paper, the parenthetical citations for Brooks’s poem give line numbers of the\npoem, whereas the citations for the secondary sources give page numbers of the sources. See pages\n648 and 652, respectively, for these two forms of citation.\nGwendolyn Brooks\nThe Bean Eaters\nThey eat beans mostly, this old yellow pair.\nDinner is a casual affair.\nPlain chipware on a plain and creaking wood,\nTin flatware.\nTwo who are Mostly Good.\nTwo who have lived their day,\nBut keep on putting on their clothes\nAnd putting things away.\nAnd remembering . . .\nRemembering, with twinklings and twinges,\nAs they lean over the beans in their rented back room that\nis full of beads and receipts and dolls and cloths,\ntobacco crumbs, vases and fringes.\nKenneth Scheff\nProfessor MacGregor\nEnglish 101A\n7 February 2006\nMarking Time Versus Enduring in\nGwendolyn Brooks’s ―The Bean Eaters‖\nGwendolyn Brooks’s poem ―The Bean Eaters‖ runs only eleven lines. It is written in plain language\nabout very plain people. Yet its meaning is ambiguous. One critic, George E. Kent, says the old couple who eat\nbeans ―have had their day and exist now as time-markers‖ (141). However, another reader, D. H. Melhem,\nperceives not so much time marking as ―endurance‖ in the old couple (123). Is this poem a despairing picture\nof old age or a more positive portrait?\n―The Bean Eaters‖ describes an ―old yellow pair‖ who ―eat beans mostly‖ (line 1) off ―Plain chipware‖\n(3) with ―Tin flatware‖ (4) in ―their rented back room‖ (11). Clearly, they are poor. Their existence is\naccompanied not by friends or relatives—children or grandchildren are not mentioned—but by memories and a\nfew possessions (9-11). They are ―Mostly Good‖ (5), words Brooks capitalizes at the end of a line, perhaps to\nstress the old people’s adherence to traditional values as well as their lack of saintliness. They are\nunexceptional, whatever message they have for readers.\nThe isolated routine of the couple’s life is something Brooks draws attention to with a separate stanza:\nTwo who are Mostly Good.\nTwo who have lived their day,\nBut keep on putting on their clothes\nAnd putting things away. (5-8)\nBrooks emphasizes how isolated the couple is by repeating ―Two who.‖ Then she emphasizes how routine their\nlife is by repeating ―putting.‖\n\nFOWLcp50.doc - 11\nA pessimistic reading of this poem seems justified. The critic Harry B. Shaw reads the lines just quoted\nas perhaps despairing: ―they are putting things away as if winding down an operation and readying for\nwithdrawal from activity‖ (80). However, Shaw observes, the word ―But‖ also indicates that the couple resist\nslipping away, that they intend to hold on (80). This dual meaning is at the heart of Brooks’s poem: the old\npeople live a meager existence, yes, but their will, their self-control, and their connection with another person—\ntheir essential humanity—are unharmed.\nThe truly positive nature of the poem is revealed in the last stanza. In Brooks’s words, the old couple\nremember with some ―twinges‖ perhaps, but also with ―twinklings‖ (10), a cheerful image. As Melhem says,\nthese people are ―strong in mutual affection and shared memories‖ (123). And the final line, which is much\nlonger than all the rest and which catalogs the evidence of the couple’s long life together, is almost musically\naffirmative: ―As they lean over the beans in their rented back room that is full of beads and receipts and dolls\nand cloths, tobacco crumbs, vases and fringes‖ (11).\nWhat these people have is not much, but it is something.\n[New page.]\nWorks Cited\nBrooks, Gwendolyn. ―The Bean Eaters.‖ An Introduction to Literature: Fiction, Poetry, and Drama. Ed.\nSylvan Barnet, William Burto, and William E. Cain. 13th ed. New York: Longman, 2004. 807.\nKent, George E. A Life of Gwendolyn Brooks. Lexington: UP of Kentucky, 1990.\nMelhem, D. H. Gwendolyn Brooks: Poetry and the Heroic Voice. Lexington: UP of Kentucky, 1987.\nShaw, Harry B. Gwendolyn Brooks. Twayne’s United States Authors Ser. 395. Boston: Twayne, 1980.\n3 Writing about drama\nBecause plays—even some one-act plays—are relatively long, analytic essays on drama\nusually focus on only one aspect of the play, such as the structure of the play, the function of a\nsingle scene, or a character’s responsibility for his or her fate. The essay’s introduction indicates\nwhat the topic is and why it is of some importance, and the introduction may also state the thesis.\nThe conclusion often extends the analysis, showing how a study of the apparently small topic helps\nto illuminate the play as a whole.\nThe ―Questions for a literary analysis‖ on pages 741–42 will help you think about any work of\nliterature, including a play, and find a topic to write on. The questions below provide additional\nprompts for thinking about drama.\nAn essay on drama (no secondary sources)\nThe following essay on William Shakespeare’s Macbeth focuses on the title character,\nexamining the extent to which he is and is not a tragic hero. Although the writer bases the essay on\nhis personal response to the play, he does not simply state a preference, as if saying he likes vanilla\nmore than chocolate; instead, he argues a case and offers evidence from the play to support his\nclaims.\nThe writer delays stating his thesis fully until the final paragraph: Macbeth is a hero even\nthough he is a villain. But this thesis is nonetheless evident throughout the essay, from the title\nthrough the opening three paragraphs (which establish a context and the case the writer will\noppose) through each of the five body paragraphs (which offer five kinds of evidence for the\nthesis).\nNote The parenthetical citations in this essay include act, scene, and line numbers—MLA style\nfor citations of verse plays (see p. 653).\nMichael Spinter\nProfessor Nelson\nEnglish 211, sec. 4\n6 May 2005\nMacbeth as Hero\nWhen we think of a tragic hero, we probably think of a fundamentally sympathetic person who is\nentangled in terrifying circumstances and who ultimately dies, leaving us with a sense that the world has\nsuffered a loss. For instance, Hamlet must avenge his father’s murder, and in doing so he performs certain\nactions that verge on the wrongful, such as behaving cruelly to his beloved Ophelia and his mother and killing\nRosencrantz and Guildenstern; but we believe that Hamlet is fundamentally a\ndecent man and that Denmark is the poorer for his death.\nMacbeth, however, is different. He kills King Duncan and Duncan’s grooms, kills Banquo, attempts to\nkill Banquo’s son, and finally kills Lady Macduff and her children and her servants. True, the only people\nwhom he kills with his own hands are Duncan and the grooms—the other victims are destroyed by hired\n\nFOWLcp50.doc - 12\nmurderers—but clearly Macbeth is responsible for all of the deaths. He could seem an utterly unscrupulous,\nsneaking crook rather than a tragic hero for whom a reader can feel sympathy.\nCertainly most of the other characters in the play feel no sympathy for Macbeth. Macduff calls him a\n―hell-kite,‖ or a hellish bird of prey (4.3.217), a ―tyrant‖ (5.7.14), a ―hell-hound‖ (5.8.3), and a ―coward‖\n(5.8.23). To Malcolm he is a ―tyrant‖ (4.3.12), ―devilish Macbeth‖ (4.3.117), and a ―butcher‖ (5.8.69). Readers\nand spectators can hardly deny the truth of these characterizations. And yet Macbeth does not seem merely\nvillainous. It would be going too far to say that we always sympathize with him, but we are deeply interested in\nhim and do not dismiss him in disgust as an out-and-out monster. How can we account for his hold on our\nfeelings? At least five factors play their parts.\nFirst, Macbeth is an impressive military figure. In the first extended description of Macbeth, the Captain\nspeaks of ―brave Macbeth—well he deserves that name‖ (1.2.16). The Captain tells how Macbeth valiantly\nfought on behalf of his king, and King Duncan exclaims, ―O valiant cousin! Worthy gentleman!‖ (1.2.2). True,\nMacbeth sometimes cringes, such as when he denies responsibility for Banquo’s death: ―Thou canst not say I\ndid it‖ (3.4.51). But throughout most of the play, we see him as a bold and courageous soldier.\nOf course, Macbeth’s ability as a soldier is not enough by itself to explain his hold on us. A second\nreason is that he is in some degree a victim—a victim of his wife’s ambition and a victim of the witches. Yes,\nhe ought to see through his wife’s schemes, and he ought to resist the witches, just as Banquo resists them, but\nsurely Macbeth is partly tricked into crime. He is responsible, but we can imagine ourselves falling as he does,\nand his status as a victim arouses our sympathy.\nA third source of his hold on us is that although Macbeth engages in terrible deeds, he almost always\nretains his conscience. For instance, after he murders Duncan he cannot sleep at night. When he tells Lady\nMacbeth that he has heard a voice saying, ―Macbeth does murder sleep‖ (2.2.35), she ridicules him, but the\nvoice is prophetic: he is doomed to sleepless nights. We in the audience are glad that Macbeth is tormented by\nhis deed, since it shows that he knows he has done wrong and that he still has some decent human feelings.\nA fourth reason why we retain some sympathy for Macbeth is that he eventually loses all of his allies,\neven his wife, and he stands before us a lonely, guilt-haunted figure. On this point, scene 2 of act 3 is especially\nsignificant. When Lady Macbeth asks Macbeth why he keeps to himself (line 8), he confides something of the\nmental stress that he is undergoing. But when she asks, ―What’s to be done?‖ (44), he cannot bring himself to\ntell her that he is plotting the deaths of Banquo and Fleance. Instead of further involving his wife, the only\nperson with whom he might still have a human connection, Macbeth says, ―Be innocent of the knowledge,\ndearest chuck . . .‖ (45). The word chuck, an affectionate form of chick, shows warmth and intimacy that are\ntouching, but his refusal or his inability to confide in his wife and former partner in crime shows how fully\nisolated he is from all human contact. We cannot help feeling some sympathy for him.\nFinally, Macbeth holds our interest, instead of disgusting us, because he speaks so wonderfully. The\ngreatness of his language compels us to listen to him with rapt attention. Some speeches are very familiar, such\nas ―My way of life / Is fall’n into the sear, the yellow leaf . . .‖ (5.3.23-24) and ―Tomorrow and tomorrow and\ntomorrow / Creeps in this petty pace from day to day . . .‖ (5.5.19-20). But\nalmost every speech Macbeth utters is equally memorable, from his first, ―So foul and fair a day I have not\nseen‖ (1.3.38), to his last:\nBefore my body\nI throw my warlike shield. Lay on, Macduff:\nAnd damned be him that first cries, ―Hold, enough!‖ (5.8.32-34)\nIf we stand back and judge Macbeth only by what he does, we of course say that he is a foul murderer.\nBut if we read the play attentively, or witness a performance, and give due weight to Macbeth’s bravery, his\nrole as a victim, his tormented conscience, his isolation, and especially his moving language, we do not simply\njudge him. Rather, we see that, villain though he is, he is not merely awful but also awesome.\n[New page.]\nWork Cited\nShakespeare, William. The Tragedy of Macbeth. Ed. Sylvan Barnet. Rev. ed. New York: NAL, 1987.\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp50.doc - 13\nVisit the companion Web site for more help with reading and writing about\nliterature.\n“heart disease” at end of story\nToo hasty, it turns out\nWould men\nhave heard differently?\nIs au. sexist?\n¨old-fashioned style\nNotices spring:odd in a story of death\nQuestions for a literary analysis\nSee later boxes for specific questions on fiction (pp. 751–52), poetry\n(p. 753), and drama (pp. 755–56).\nPlot\nThe relationships and patterns of events. Even a poem has a plot, such as a change in mood from\nbitterness to resignation.\nWhat actions happen?\nWhat conflicts occur?\nHow do the events connect to each other and to the whole?\nCharacters\nThe people the author creates, including the narrator of a story or the\nspeaker of a poem.\nWho are the principal people in the work?\nHow do they interact?\nWhat do their actions, words, and thoughts reveal about their personalities and the personalities of others?\nDo the characters stay the same, or do they change? Why?\nPoint of view\nThe perspective or attitude of the speaker in a poem or the voice who tells a story. The point of view\nmay be first person (a participant, using I) or third person (an outsider, using he, she, it, they). A first-\nperson narrator may be a major or a minor character in the narrative and may be reliable or unreliable\n(unable to report events wholly or accurately). A third-\nperson narrator may be omniscient (knows what goes on in all characters’ minds), limited (knows what\ngoes on in the mind of only one or two characters), or objective (knows only what is external to the\ncharacters).\nWho is the narrator (or the speaker of a poem)?\nHow does the narrator’s point of view affect the narrative?\nTone\nThe narrator’s or speaker’s attitude, perceived through the words (for instance, joyful, bitter, or\nconfident).\nWhat tone (or tones) do you hear? If there is a change, how do you ac-count for it?\nIs there an ironic contrast between the narrator’s tone (for instance, confidence) and what you take to be\nthe author’s attitude (for instance, pity for human overconfidence)?\nImagery\nWord pictures or details involving the senses: sight, sound, touch, smell, taste.\nWhat images does the writer use? What senses do they draw on?\nWhat patterns are evident in the images (for instance, religious or commercial images)?\nWhat is the significance of the imagery?\nSymbolism\nConcrete things standing for larger and more abstract ideas. For instance, the American flag may\nsymbolize freedom, a tweeting bird may symbolize happiness, or a dead flower may symbolize mortality.\nWhat symbols does the author use? What do they seem to signify?\nHow does the symbolism relate to the other elements of the work, such as character or theme?\nSetting\nThe place where the action happens.\nWhat does the locale contribute to the work?\nAre scene shifts significant?\n\nFOWLcp50.doc - 14\nForm\nThe shape or structure of the work.\nWhat is the form? (For example, a story might divide sharply in the\nmiddle, moving from happiness to sorrow.)\nWhat parts of the work does the form emphasize, and why?\nTheme\nThe central idea, a conception of human experience suggested by the work as a whole. Theme is neither\nplot (what happens) nor subject (such as mourning or marriage). Rather it is what the author says with\nthat plot about that subject.\nCan you state the theme in a sentence? For instance, you might state the following about Kate Chopin’s\n―The Story of an Hour‖: Happiness depends partly on freedom.\nDo certain words, passages of dialog or description, or situations seem to represent the theme most\nclearly?\nHow do the work’s elements combine to develop the theme?\nAppeal\nThe degree to which the work pleases you.\nWhat do you especially like or dislike about the work?\nDo you think your responses are unique, or would they be common to most readers? Why?\nYou can download these questions from ablongman.com/littlebrown. Copy the questions for each work\nyou read, and answer the questions in writing.\nGuidelines for using quotations in literary analysis\nUse quotations to support your assertions, not to pad the paper. Quote at length only when\nnecessary to your argument.\nSpecify how each quotation relates to your idea. Introduce the quotation—for example, At the\noutset Chopin conveys the sort of person Richards is: ―. . .‖ Sometimes, comment after the quotation.\n(See\npages 623–28 for more on integrating quotations into your writing.)\nReproduce spelling, punctuation, capitalization, and all other features exactly as they appear in\nthe source. See page 483 for the use of brackets when you need to add something to a quotation, and\nsee page 484 for the use of an ellipsis mark when you need to omit something from a quotation.\nDocument your sources. See page 637.\nChecklist for revising a literary analysis\nTitle: Does the title of your essay give the title and author of the work you discuss and also an idea of\nyour approach to the work?\nIntroduction: Does the introductory paragraph name the author and the title so that readers know\nexactly what work you are discussing? (Avoid opening sentences such as ―In this story. . . .‖) Does the\nintroduction state and develop your thesis a bit so that readers know where they will be going?\nOrganization: How effective is the organization? The essay should not dwindle or become\nanticlimactic; rather, it should build up.\nQuotations: What evidence does each quotation provide? Do quotations let readers hear the author’s\nvoice?\nAnalysis vs. summary: Is the essay chiefly devoted to analysis, not to summary? Summarize the plot\nonly briefly and only to further your own ideas. A summary is not an essay.\nVerb tenses: Have you used the present tense of verbs to describe both the author’s work and the\naction in the work (for example, Chopin shows or Mrs. Mallard dies)?\nEvaluation: How well will readers understand your evaluation of the work and what it is based on?\nYour evaluation may be implied (as in Janet Vong’s essay on ―The Story of an Hour‖), or it may be\nexplicit. In either case, give the reasons for judging the work to be effective or not, worth reading or\nnot. It is not enough to express your likes or dislikes; readers need the support of specific evidence\nfrom the work.\nAre all your sources documented in MLA style?\nYou can download the checklist from ablongman.com/littlebrown. Using a duplicate, write out answers\nfor each paper you revise.\nAuthor’s name and identification in MLA\nformat (p. 687)\nPaper title in-\ncorporating author and title of analyzed work\n\nFOWLcp50.doc - 15\nIntroduction naming author/ title and stating thesis\nDetailing of story’s ironies, using quotations and some summary to emphasize the reversals\nParenthetical\ncitations in\nMLA style referring to the work cited at the end of the paper (see p. 648)\nNew page for work cited in MLA style (p. 656)\nQuestions for analyzing fiction\nWhat happens in the story? For yourself, summarize the plot (the gist of the happenings). Think\nabout what your summary leaves out.\nIs the story told in chronological order, or are there flashbacks or flashforwards? On rereading,\nwhat foreshadowing (hints of what is to come) do you detect?\nWhat conflicts does the work include?\nHow does the writer develop characters? Is character revealed by explicit comment or through\naction? With which character(s) do you sympathize? Are the characters plausible? What motivates\nthem? What do minor characters contribute to the work?\nWho tells the story? Is the narrator a character, or does the narrator stand entirely outside the\ncharacters’ world? What does the narrator’s point of view contribute to the story’s theme? (On\nnarrative points of view, see p. 741.)\nWhat is the setting? What do the time and place of the action contribute to the work?\nAre certain characters, settings, or actions symbolic? Do they stand for something in addition to\nthemselves?\nWhat is the theme? That is, what does the work add up to? Does the theme reinforce your values, or\ndoes it challenge them?\nIs the title informative? Did its meaning change for you after you read the work?\nYou can download these questions from ablongman.com/littlebrown and answer them for each work you\nread.\nQuestions for analyzing poetry\nWhat parts of the poem interest or puzzle you? What words seem especially striking or unusual?\nHow can you describe the poem’s speaker (sometimes called the persona or the voice)? The\nspeaker may be very different from the au-thor.\nWhat tone or emotion do you detect—for instance, anger, affection, sarcasm? Does the tone change\nduring the poem?\nWhat is the structure of the poem? Are there stanzas (groups of lines separated by space)? If so,\nhow is the thought related to the stanzas?\nWhat is the theme of the poem? What is it about? Is the theme stated or implied?\nWhat images do you find? Look for evocations of sight, sound, taste, touch, or smell. Is there a\nsurprising pattern of images—say, images of business in a poem about love? What does the poem\nsuggest symbolically as well as literally? (Trust your responses. If you don’t sense a symbolic\novertone, move on. Don’t hunt for symbols.)\nYou can download these questions from ablongman.com/littlebrown and answer them for each poem you\nread.\nQuestions for analyzing drama\nHow does the plot (the sequence of happenings) unfold? Does it seem plausible? If not, is the\nimplausibility a fault? If there is more than one plot, are the plots parallel, or are they related by way\nof contrast?\nAre certain happenings recurrent? If so, how are they significant?\nWhat kinds of conflict are in the play—for instance, between two groups, two individuals, or two\naspects of a single individual? How are the conflicts resolved? Is the resolution satisfying to you?\nHow does the author develop the characters? How trustworthy are the characters when they\ndescribe themselves or others? Do some characters serve as foils, or contrasts, for other characters,\nthus helping to define the other characters? Do the characters change as the play proceeds? Are the\ncharacters’ motivations convincing?\nWhat do the author’s stage directions add to your understanding and appreciation of the play?\nIf there are few stage directions, what do the speeches imply about the characters’ manner, tone, and\ngestures?\nWhat do you make of the setting, or location? Does it help to reveal character or theme?\nDo certain costumes (dark suits, flowery shawls, stiff collars) or prop-\nerties (books, pictures, candlesticks) strike you as symbolic?\n\nFOWLcp50.doc - 16\nYou can download these questions from ablongman.com/littlebrown and answer them for each play you\nread.\n\nFOWLcp51.doc - 1\n51\nCHAPTER\nWriting in Other Humanities\nThe humanities include literature, the visual arts, music, film, dance, history, philosophy, and\nreligion. The preceding chapter discusses the particular requirements of reading and writing about\nliterature. This chapter concentrates on history. Although the arts, religion, and other humanities\nhave their own concerns, they share many important goals and methods with literature and history.\n51a Using the methods and evidence\nof the humanities\nWriters in the humanities record and speculate about the growth, ideas, and emotions of\nhuman beings. Based on the evidence of written words, artworks, and other human traces and\ncreations, humanities writers explain, interpret, analyze, and reconstruct the human experience.\nThe discipline of history focuses particularly on reconstructing the past. In Greek the word for\nhistory means ―to inquire‖: historians inquire into the past to understand the events of the past.\nThen they report, explain, analyze, and evaluate those events in their context, asking such questions\nas what happened before or after the events or how the events were related to then existing political\nand social structures.\nHistorians’ reconstructions of the past—their conclusions about what happened and why—are\nalways supported with reference\nto the written record. The evidence of history is mainly primary sources, such as eyewitness\naccounts and contemporary documents, letters, commercial records, and the like. For history\npapers, you might also be asked to support your conclusions with those in secondary sources.\nIn reading historical sources, you need to weigh and evaluate their evidence. If, for example,\nyou find conflicting accounts of the same event, you need to consider the possible biases of the\nauthors. In general, the more a historian’s conclusions are supported by public records such as\ndeeds, marriage licenses, and newspaper accounts, the more reliable the conclusions are likely to\nbe.\n51b Understanding writing assignments\nin the humanities\nPapers in the humanities generally perform one or more of the following operations:\nExplanation: for instance, showing how a painter developed a particular technique or\nclarifying a general’s role in a historical battle.\nAnalysis: examining the elements of a philosophical argument or breaking down the causes of\na historical event.\nInterpretation: inferring the meaning of a film from its images or the significance of a\nhistorical event from contemporary accounts of it.\nSynthesis: finding a pattern in a historical period or in a composer’s works.\nEvaluation: judging the quality of an architect’s design or a historian’s conclusions.\nMost likely, you will use these operations in combination—say, interpreting and explaining the\nmeaning of a painting and then eval-uating it. (These operations are discussed in more detail in\nChap-\nter 8.)\n\nFOWLcp51.doc - 2\n51c Using the tools and language of the humanities\nThe tools and language of the humanities vary according to the discipline. Major reference\nworks in each field, such as those listed on the next four pages, can clarify specific tools you need\nand language you should use.\n1 Writing tools\nA useful tool for the arts is to ask a series of questions to analyze and evaluate a work. (A list\nof such questions for reading literature appears on pp. 741–42.) In any humanities discipline, a\njournal—a log of questions, reactions, and insights—can help you discover and record your\nthoughts. (See pp. 152–53 and 559–60.)\nIn history the tools are those of any thorough and efficient researcher, as discussed in Chapters\n42–44: a system for finding and tracking sources; a methodical examination of sources, including\nevaluating and synthesizing them; a system for gathering source information; and a separate\nsystem, such as a research journal, for tracking one’s own evolving thoughts.\n2 Language considerations\nHistorians strive for precision and logic. They do not guess about what happened or speculate\nabout ―what if.‖ They avoid trying to influence readers’ opinions with words having strongly\nnegative or positive connotations, such as stupid or brilliant. Instead, historians show the evidence\nand draw conclusions from that. Generally, they avoid using I because it tends to draw attention\naway from the evidence and toward the writer.\nWriting about history demands some attention to the tenses of verbs to maintain consistency.\nGenerally, historians use the past tense to refer to events that occurred in the past. They reserve the\npresent tense only for statements about the present or statements of general truths. For example:\nFranklin Delano Roosevelt died in 1945. Many of Roosevelt’s economic reforms persist in programs such\nas Social Security, unemployment compensation, and farm subsidies.\n3 Research sources\nThe following lists give resources in the humanities. Turabian, A Manual for Writers of Term Papers, Theses, and Dissertations, 6th ed., rev. John\nGrossman and Alice Bennett, 1996\nThe recommendations of the MLA Handbook are discussed and illustrated in Chapter 47.\nUnless your instructor specifies otherwise, use these recommendations for papers in English and\nforeign languages. In history, art history, and many other disciplines, however, writers rely on The\nChicago Manual of Style or the student reference adapted from it, A Manual for Writers.\nBoth books detail two documentation styles. One, used mainly by scientists and social\nscientists, closely resembles the style of the American Psychological Association, covered in\n\nFOWLcp51.doc - 5\nChapter 52. The other style, used more in the humanities, calls for footnotes or endnotes and an\noptional bibliography. This style is described below.\n1 Using Chicago notes and a list of works cited\nIn the Chicago note style, a raised numeral in the text refers the reader to source information\nin endnotes or footnotes. In these notes, the first citation of each source contains all the information\nreaders need to find the source. Thus your instructor may consider a list of works cited optional\nbecause it provides much the same information. Ask your instructor whether you should use\nfootnotes or endnotes and whether you should include a list of works cited.\nWhether providing footnotes or endnotes, use single spacing for each note and double spacing\nbetween notes, as shown in the samples below. Separate footnotes from the text with a short line.\nPlace endnotes directly after the text, beginning on a new page. For the list of sources at the end of\nthe paper, use the format on the following page. Arrange the sources alphabetically by the authors’\nlast names.\nThe examples below illustrate the essentials of a note and a works-cited entry.\nNote\n6. Martin Gilbert, Pictorial Atlas of British History (New York: Dorset\nPress, 2001), 96.\nWorks-cited entry\nGilbert, Martin. Pictorial Atlas of British History. New York: Dorset Press,\n2001.\nNotes and works-cited entries share certain features:\nSingle-space each note or entry, and double-space between them.\nItalicize or underline the titles of books and periodicals. Ask your instructor for his or her\npreference.\nEnclose in quotation marks the titles of parts of books or articles in periodicals.\nDo not abbreviate publishers’ names, but omit ―Inc.,‖ ―Co.,‖ and similar abbreviations.\nDo not use ―p.‖ or ―pp.‖ before page numbers.\nNotes and works-cited entries also differ in important ways:\nNote Works-cited entry\nStart with a number that corre- Do not begin with a number.\nsponds to the note number in\nthe text.\nNote Works-cited entry\nIndent the first line five spaces. Indent the second and sub-\nsequent lines five spaces.\nGive the author’s name in Begin with the author’s last\nnormal order. name.\nUse commas between elements Use periods between elements.\nsuch as author’s name and title.\nEnclose publication information Precede the publication infor-\nin parentheses, with no preced- mation with a period, and don’t\ning punctuation use parentheses.\nInclude the specific page num- Omit page numbers except for\nber(s) you borrowed from, omit- parts of books or articles in\nting ―p.‖ or ―pp.‖ periodicals.\nYou can instruct your computer to position footnotes at the bottoms of appropriate pages. It\nwill also automatically number notes and renumber them if you add or delete one or more.\n\nFOWLcp51.doc - 6\n2 Following Chicago models\nThe Chicago models for common sources are indexed on the next page. The models show\nnotes and works-cited entries together for easy reference. Be sure to use the numbered note form\nfor notes and the unnumbered works-cited form for works-cited entries.\nBooks\n1. New York Review of Books, August 16, 1994, 17-27.\nElectronic sources\nThe Chicago Manual’s models for documenting electronic sources derive mainly from those\nfor print sources, with the addition of an electronic address (URL) or other indication of the\nmedium along with any other information that may help readers locate the source. Chicago requires\nthe date of your access to an online source only if the source could change significantly (for\ninstance, a report on medical research). However, your instructor may require access dates for a\nbroader range of online sources, so they are included in the following models (in parentheses at the\nend).\nNote Chicago style allows many ways to break URLs between the end of one line and the\nbeginning of the next: after slashes, before most punctuation marks (periods, commas, question\nmarks, and so on), and before or after equal signs and ampersands (&). Do not break after a hyphen\nor add any hyphens.\n16. A work on CD-ROM or DVD-ROM\n16. Artur Rubinstein. Philadelphia Orchestra. Eugene\nOrmandy. Compact disc. RCA BRC4-6731.\nShortened notes\nTo streamline documentation, Chicago style recommends short-ened notes for sources that are\nfully cited elsewhere, either in a complete list of works cited or in previous notes. Ask your\ninstructor whether your paper should include a list of works cited and, if so, whether you may use\nshortened notes for first references to sources as well as for subsequent references.\n\nFOWLcp51.doc - 11\nA shortened note contains the author’s last name, the work’s title (minus any initial A, An, or\nThe), and the page number. Reduce long titles to four or fewer key words.\nComplete note\n8. Janet Lever, ―Sex Differences in the Games Children Play,‖ Social Problems 23 (1996): 482.\nComplete works-cited entry\nLever, Janet. ―Sex Differences in the Games Children Play.‖ Social Problems 23 (1996): 478-87.\nShortened note\n12. Lever, ―Sex Differences,‖ 483.\nYou may use the Latin abbreviation ibid. (meaning ―in the same place‖) to refer to the same\nsource cited in the preceding note. Give a page number if it differs from that in the preceding note.\n12. Lever, ―Sex Differences,‖ 483.\n13. Gilligan, In a Different Voice, 92.\n14. Ibid., 93.\n15. Lever, ―Sex Differences,‖ 483.\nChicago style allows for in-text parenthetical citations when you cite one or more works\nrepeatedly. In the following example, the raised number 2 refers to the source information in a\nnote; the number in parentheses is a page number in the same source.\nBritish rule, observes Stuart Cary Welch, ―seemed as permanent as Mount Everest.‖2 Most Indians\nsubmitted, willingly or not, to British influence in every facet of life (42).\n51e Formatting documents in Chicago style\nThe following guidelines for document format come mainly from Turabian’s Manual for\nWriters, which offers more specific advice than The Chicago Manual on the format of students’\npapers. See pages 765–66 for the format of footnotes, endnotes, and a list of works cited. And see\npages 116–26 for advice on type fonts, lists, illustrations, and other elements of document design.\nMargins and spacing Use minimum one-inch margins on all pages of the body. (The first page\nof endnotes or works cited begins two inches from the top; see pp. 765–66.) Double-space your\nown text and between notes and works-cited entries; single-space displayed quotations (see below)\nand each note and works-cited entry.\nPaging Number pages consecutively from the first text page through the end (endnotes or\nworks cited). Use Arabic numerals\n(1, 2, 3) in the upper right corner of all pages except the first page of endnotes and first page of\nworks cited. On those pages, the number falls at the foot of the page (see pp. 765–66).\nTitle page On an unnumbered title page provide the title of the paper, your name, the course\ntitle, your instructor’s name, and the date. Use all capital letters, and center everything horizontally.\nDouble-space between adjacent lines, and add extra space as shown opposite.\nPoetry and long prose quotations Display certain quotations separately from your text: three or\nmore lines of poetry and two or more sentences of prose. Indent a displayed quotation four spaces\nfrom the left, single-space the quotation, and double-space both above it and below it. Do not add\nquotation marks.\nGandhi articulated the principles of his movement in 1922:\nI discovered that pursuit of truth did not permit violence being in-\nflicted on one’s opponent, but that he must be weaned from error\nby\n\nFOWLcp51.doc - 12\npatience and sympathy. For what appears to be truth to one may\nappear to be error to the other. And patience means self-suffering.9\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp51.doc - 13\nVisit the companion Web site for more help with writing in history, the\nvisual arts, and other humanities.\nChicago footnotes\nIn 1901, Madras, Bengal, and Punjab were a few of the huge Indian provinces governed\nby the British viceroy.6 British rule, observes Stuart Cary Welch, ‘‘seemed as\npermanent as Mount Everest.’’7\n6. Martin Gilbert, Pictorial Atlas of British History (New York: Dorset Press, 2001), 96.\n7. Stuart Cary Welch, India: Art and Culture (New York: Metropolitan Museum of Art, 1995), 421.\n1''\n1''\nLine\n5 spaces\nSingle-\nspace\nDouble-space\nSingle-\nspace\n1''\nChicago endnotes\n2''\nCenter\nNOTES\n1. Young India, 1919-1922. New York: Huebsch,\n1923.\nGilbert, Martin. Pictorial Atlas of British History. New York:Dorset Press, 2001.\nWelch, Stuart Cary. India:Art and Culture. New York: Metropolitan\nMuseum of Art, 1995.\n10\nSingle-\nspace entries\nTriple-space\n5 spaces\nDouble-\nspace\nbetween entries\nCenter\n3⁄4''\nChicago note and works-cited models\nBooks\n1.
