Though at first it was unified strongly behind\nresented were the Rowlatt Acts of 1919, by which the British Parliament withdrew\njuries from the trials of agitators. This action was seen\n1''\nChicago title page\nREYNA P. DIXON\nINDIAN NATIONALISM IN INDIAN ART\nAFTER WORLD WAR I\nDouble-space\nART HISTORY 236\nMS. PARIKH\nDECEMBER 16, 200 5\nDouble-space\n\nFOWLcp52.doc - 1\n52\nCHAPTER\nWriting in the\nSocial Sciences\nThe social sciences—including anthropology, economics, education, management, political\nscience, psychology, and sociology—focus on the study of human behavior. As the name implies,\nthe social sciences examine the way human beings relate to themselves, to their environment, and\nto one another.\n52a Using the methods and evidence\nof the social sciences\nResearchers in the social sciences systematically pose a question, formulate a hypothesis (a\ngeneralization that can be tested), collect data, analyze those data, and draw conclusions to support,\nrefine, or disprove their hypothesis. This is the scientific method developed in the natural sciences\n(see p. 807).\nSocial scientists gather data in several ways:\nThey make firsthand observations of human behavior, recording the observations in writing\nor on audio- or videotape.\nThey interview subjects about their attitudes and behavior, recording responses in writing\nor on tape. (See pp. 596–97 for guidelines on conducting an interview.)\nThey conduct broader surveys using questionnaires, asking people about their attitudes and\nbehavior. (See the box on the facing page.)\nThey conduct controlled experiments, structuring an environment in which to encourage and\nmeasure a specific behavior.\nIn their writing, social scientists explain their own research or analyze and evaluate others’\nresearch.\nSocial science research methods generate two kinds of data:\nQuantitative data are numerical, such as statistical evidence based on surveys, polls, tests,\nand experiments. When public-opinion pollsters announce that 47 percent of US citizens\npolled approve of the President’s leadership, they are offering quantitative data gained from a\nsurvey. Social science writers present quantitative data in graphs, charts, and other\nillustrations that accompany their text.\nQualitative data are not numerical but more subjective: they\nare based on interviews, firsthand observations, and inferences, taking into account the\nsubjective nature of human experience. Examples include an anthropologist’s description of\nthe initia-\ntion rites in a culture she is studying or a psychologist’s interpretation of interviews he\nconducted with a group of adolescents.\n52b Understanding writing assignments\n\nFOWLcp52.doc - 2\nin the social sciences\nDepending on what social science courses you take, you may be asked to complete a variety of\nassignments:\nA summary or review of research reports on the available research literature on a subject,\nsuch as infants’ perception of color.\nA case analysis explains the components of a phenomenon, such as a factory closing.\nA problem-solving analysis explains the components of a problem, such as unreported child\nabuse, and suggests ways to solve it.\nA research paper interprets and sometimes analyzes and evaluates the writings of other social\nscientists about a subject, such as the effect of national appeals in advertising. An example\nappears in Chapter 48, pages 690–724.\nA research report explains the author’s own original research or the author’s attempt to\nreplicate someone else’s research. A research report begins on page 803.\nMany social science disciplines have special requirements for the content and organization of\neach kind of paper. The requirements appear in the style guides of the disciplines, listed on page\n784. For instance, the American Psychological Association specifies the outline for research reports\nthat is illustrated on pages 803–06. Because of the differences among disciplines and even among\ndifferent kinds of papers in the same discipline, you should always ask your instructor what he or\nshe requires for an assignment.\n52c Using the tools and language\nof the social sciences\nThe following guidelines for tools and language apply to most social sciences. However, the\nparticular discipline you are writing in, or an instructor in a particular course, may have additional\nrequirements. Many of the research sources listed on the next several pages can tell you more about\nyour discipline’s conventions.\n1 Writing tools\nMany social scientists rely on a research journal or log, in which they record their ideas\nthroughout the research-writing proc-ess. Even if a research journal is not required in your courses,\nyou may want to use one. As you begin formulating a hypothesis, you can record preliminary\nquestions. Then when you are in the field conducting research, you can use the journal to react to\nthe evidence you are collecting, to record changes in your perceptions and ideas, and to assess your\nprogress. (See pp. 152–53 and 559–60 for more on journals.)\nTo avoid confusing your reflections on the evidence with the evidence itself, keep records of\nactual data—notes from interviews, observations, surveys, and experiments—separately from the\njournal.\n2 Language considerations\nEach social science discipline has specialized terminology for concepts basic to the discipline.\nIn sociology, for example, the words mechanism, identity, and deviance have specific meanings\ndifferent from those of everyday usage. And identity means something different in sociology, where\nit applies to groups of people, than in psychology, where it applies to the individual. Social\nscientists also use precise terms to describe or interpret research. For\ninstance, they say The subject expressed a feeling of rather than The subject felt because human\nfeelings are not knowable for certain; or they say These studies indicate rather than These studies\nprove because conclusions are only tentative.\nJust as social scientists strive for objectivity in their research, so they strive to demonstrate\ntheir objectivity through language in their writing. They avoid expressions such as I think in order\nto focus attention on what the evidence shows, not the researcher’s opinions. (However, many\nsocial scientists prefer I to the researcher when they refer to their own actions, as in I then\n\nFOWLcp52.doc - 3\ninterviewed the subjects. Ask your instructor for his or her preferences.) Social scientists also avoid\ndirect or indirect expression of their personal biases or emotions, either in discussions of other\nresearchers’ work or in descriptions of research subjects. Thus one social scientist does not call\nanother’s work sloppy or immaculate and does not refer to his or her own subjects as drunks or\ninnocent victims. Instead, the writer uses neutral language and ties conclusions strictly to the data.\n3 Research sources\nSpecialized encyclopedias, dictionaries, and bibliographies\nGeneral\nInternational (thomas.loc.gov)\nUltimate Political Science Links (www.rvc.cc.il.us/faclink/pruckman/\nPSLinks.htm)\nPsychology\nEncyclopedia of Psychology (www.psychology.org)\nPsychCrawler (sosig.ac.uk/roads/\nsubject-listing/World/sociol.html)\nSocioSite (www2.fmg.uva.nl/sociosite)\nSocioWeb (socioweb.com)\nWWW Virtual Library: Sociology (socserv2.socsci.mcmaster.ca/\nw3virtsoclib)\n52d Citing sources in APA style\nSome of the social sciences publish style guides that advise practitioners how to organize,\ndocument, and type papers. The following is a partial list:\nAmerican Anthropological Association, AAA Style Guide, 2002, http://\nwww.aaanet.org/pubs/style_guide.htm\nAmerican Political Science Association, Style Manual for Political Sci-ence, 2001\nAmerican Psychological Association, Publication Manual of the American Psychological Association,\n5th ed., 2001\nAmerican Sociological Association, ASA Style Guide, 2nd ed., 1997\nLinguistic Society of America, ―LSA Style Sheet,‖ published every De-cember in LSA Bulletin\nA Uniform System of Citation (law), 17th ed., 2001\nBy far the most widely used style is that of the American Psychological Association (APA), so we\ndetail it here. Always ask your instructor in any discipline what style you should use.\nNote If you use APA style frequently and write on a computer, you may want to obtain APA-\nStyle Helper, a student’s companion to the Publication Manual of the American Psychological\nAssociation that formats source citations in APA style. It can be downloaded (for a fee) from the\nAPA Web site at apa.org/software. Other bibliography programs can also help with APA style. See\npage 638.\n1 Using APA parenthetical text citations\nIn the APA documentation style, parenthetical citations within the text refer the reader to a list\nof sources at the end of the text. A parenthetical citation contains the author’s last name, the date of\npublication, and sometimes the page number from which material is borrowed.\n1. Author not named in your text\nOne critic of Milgram’s experiments insisted that the subjects ―should have been fully informed of the\npossible effects on them‖ (Baumrind, 1988, p. 34).\nWhen you do not name the author in your text, place in parentheses the author’s last name and the\ndate of the source. Separate the elements with commas. Position the reference so that it is clear\nwhat material is being documented and so that the reference fits as smoothly as possible into your\nsentence structure. (See pp. 654–56 for guidelines.) The following would also be correct:\nIn the view of one critic of Milgram’s experiments (Baumrind, 1988), the subjects ―should have been\nfully informed of the possible effects on them‖ (p. 34).\nUnless none is available, the APA requires a page or other identifying number for a direct\nquotation (as in the preceding examples) and recommends an identifying number for a\nparaphrase. Use an appropriate abbreviation or symbol before the number—for instance, p.\nfor page and ¶ for paragraph (or para. if you do not have the symbol). The identifying\n\nFOWLcp52.doc - 6\nnumber may fall with the author and date (first example) or by itself in a separate pair of\nparentheses (second example). See also model 11, page 788.\n2. Author named in your text\nBaumrind (1988) insisted that the subjects in Milgram’s study ―should have been fully informed of the\npossible effects on them‖ (p. 34).\nWhen you use the author’s name in the text, do not repeat it in the reference. Place the source date\nin parentheses after the author’s name. Place any page or paragraph reference either after the\nborrowed material (as in the example) or with the date: (1988, p. 34). If you cite the same\nsource again in the paragraph, you need not repeat the reference as long as it is clear that you are\nusing the same source and the page number (if any) is the same. Here is a later sentence from the\nparagraph containing the preceding example:\nBaumrind also criticized the experimenters’ rationale.\n3. A work with two authors\nPepinsky and DeStefano (1997) demonstrated that a teacher’s language often reveals hidden biases.\nOne study (Pepinsky & DeStefano, 1997) demonstrated the hidden biases\noften revealed in a teacher’s language.\nWhen given in the text, two authors’ names are connected by and. In a parenthetical citation, they\nare connected by an ampersand, &.\n4. A work with three to five authors\nPepinsky, Dunn, Rentl, and Corson (1999) further demonstrated the biases evident in gestures.\nIn the first citation of a work with three to five authors, name all the authors, as in the example\nabove.\nIn the second and subsequent references to a work with three to five authors, generally give\nonly the first author’s name, followed by et al. (Latin abbreviation for ―and others‖):\nIn the work of Pepinsky et al. (1999), the loaded gestures included head shakes and eye contact.\nHowever, two or more sources published in the same year could shorten to the same form—for\ninstance, two references shortening to Pepinsky et al., 1999. In that case, cite the last names\nof as many\nauthors as you need to distinguish the sources, and then give et al.: for instance, (Pepinsky,\nDunn, et al., 1999) and (Pepinsky, Bradley, et al.,\n1999).\n5. A work with six or more authors\nOne study (Rutter et al., 2003) attempted to explain these geographical differences in adolescent\nexperience.\nFor six or more authors, even in the first citation of the work, give only the first author’s name,\nfollowed by et al. If two or more sources published in the same year shorten to the same form,\ngive additional names as explained with model 4.\n6. A work with a group author\nAn earlier prediction was even more somber (Lorenz Research, 2003).\nFor a work that lists an institution, agency, corporation, or other group as author, treat the name of\nthe group as if it were an individual’s name. If the name is long and has a familiar abbreviation,\nyou may use the abbreviation in the second and subsequent citations. For example, you might\nabbreviate American Psychological Association as APA.\n7. A work with no author or an anonymous work\nOne article (―Right to Die,‖ 1996) noted that a death-row inmate may crave notoriety.\nFor a work with no named author, use the first two or three words of the title in place of an author’s\nname, excluding an initial The,\n\nFOWLcp52.doc - 7\nA, or An. Italicize book and journal titles, place quotation marks around article titles, and capitalize\nthe significant words in all titles cited in the text. (In the reference list, however, do not use\nquotation marks for article titles, and capitalize only the first word in all but periodical titles. See\npp. 788 and 789.)\nFor a work that lists ―Anonymous‖ as the author, use this word in the citation: (Anonymous,\n1999).\n8. One of two or more works by the same author(s)\nAt about age seven, most children begin to use appropriate gestures to re-\ninforce their stories (Gardner, 1973a).\nWhen you cite one of two or more works by the same author(s), the date will tell readers which\nsource you mean—as long as your reference list includes only one source published by the\nauthor(s) in that year. If your reference list includes two or more works published by the same\nauthor(s) in the same year, the works should be lettered in the reference list (see p. 792). Then\nyour parenthetical citation should include the appropriate letter: 1973a in the preceding\nexample.\n9. Two or more works by different authors\nTwo studies (Herskowitz, 1994; Marconi & Hamblen, 1999) found that periodic safety instruction can\ndramatically reduce employees’ accidents.\nList the sources in alphabetical order by their authors’ names. Insert a semicolon between sources.\n10. An indirect source\nSupporting data appeared in a study by Wong (cited in Marconi, 2004).\nThe phrase cited in indicates that the reference to Wong’s study was found in Marconi. Only\nMarconi then appears in the list of references.\n11. An electronic source\nFerguson and Hawkins (2002) did not anticipate the ―evident hostility‖ of participants (¶ 6).\nElectronic sources can be cited like printed sources, usually with\nthe author’s last name and the publication date. When quoting or paraphrasing electronic sources\nthat number paragraphs instead of pages, provide the paragraph number preceded by the symbol ¶\nif you have it, or by para. Even if the source does not number its paragraphs, you can still direct\nreaders to a specific location by listing the heading under which the quotation appears and then\n(counting paragraphs yourself) the number of the paragraph in which the quotation appears—for\nexample, (Morrison & Lee, 2004, Method section, ¶ 4). When the source does not\nnumber pages or paragraphs or provide frequent headings, omit any reference number.\n2 Using an APA reference list\nIn APA style, the in-text parenthetical citations refer to the list of sources at the end of the\ntext. This list, titled References, includes full publication information on every source cited in the\npaper. The list falls at the end of the paper, numbered in sequence with the preceding pages.\nThe following sample shows the format of the first page of the APA reference list:\nArrangement Arrange sources alphabetically by the author’s last name. If there is no author,\nalphabetize by the first main word of the title.\nSpacing Double-space everything in the references, as shown in the sample, unless your\ninstructor requests single spacing. (If you do single-space the entries themselves, always double-\nspace between them.)\nIndention As illustrated in the sample, begin each entry at the left margin, and indent the\nsecond and subsequent lines five to seven spaces or one-half inch. See page 657 for instructions on\ncreating this so-called hanging indent on your word processor.\n\nFOWLcp52.doc - 8\nPunctuation Separate the parts of the reference (author, date, title, and publication information)\nwith a period and one space. Do not use a final period in references to electronic sources, which\nconclude with an electronic address (see pp. 795–97).\nAuthors For works with up to six authors, list all authors with last name first, separating names\nand parts of names with commas. Use initials for first and middle names even when names are\nlisted fully on the source itself. Use an ampersand (&) before the last author’s name. See model 3,\npage 791, for the treatment of seven or more authors.\nPublication date Place the publication date in parentheses after the author’s or authors’ names,\nfollowed by a period. Generally, this date is the year only, though for some sources (such as\nmagazine and newspaper articles) it includes month and sometimes day as well.\nTitles In titles of books and articles, capitalize only the first word of the title, the first word of\nthe subtitle, and proper nouns; all other words begin with small letters. In titles of journals,\ncapitalize all significant words (see p. 491 for guidelines). Italicize the titles of books and journals.\nDo not italicize or use quotation marks around the titles of articles.\nCity of publication For sources that are not periodicals (such as books or government\npublications), give the city of publication. The following US cities do not require state names as\nwell: Baltimore, Boston, Chicago, Los Angeles, New York, Philadelphia, and San Francisco.\nFollow their names with a colon. For most other cities, add a comma after the city name, give the\ntwo-letter postal abbreviation of the state, and then add a colon. (You may omit the state if the\npublisher is a university whose name includes the state name, such as University of\nArizona.)\nPublisher’s name Also for nonperiodical sources, give the publisher’s name after the place of\npublication and a colon. Use shortened names for many publishers (such as Morrow for William\nMorrow), and omit Co., Inc., and Publishers. However, give full names for associations,\ncorporations, and university presses (such as Harvard University Press), and do not omit\nBooks or Press from a publisher’s name.\nPage numbers Use the abbreviation p. or pp. before page num-bers in books and in\nnewspapers. Do not use the abbreviation for journals and magazines. For inclusive page numbers,\ninclude all figures: 667--668.\nIf the following pages don’t provide a model for a kind of source you used, try to find one that\ncomes close, and provide ample information so that readers can trace the source. Often, you will\nhave to combine models to cite a source accurately—for instance, combining ―A book with two to\nsix authors‖ (model 2) and ―An article in a journal‖ (model 12) for a journal article with two or\nmore authors.\nBooks\n1. A book with one author\nRodriguez, R. (1982). A hunger of memory: The education of Richard Rodriguez. Boston: Godine.\nThe initial R appears instead of the author’s first name, even though the author’s full first name\nappears on the source. In the title, only the first words of title and subtitle and the proper name are\ncapitalized.\n2. A book with two to six authors\nNesselroade, J. R., & Baltes, P. B. (1999). Longitudinal research in behavioral studies. New York:\nAcademic Press.\nAn ampersand (&) precedes the last author’s name.\n3. A book with seven or more authors\nWimple, P. B., Van Eijk, M., Potts, C. A., Hayes, J., Obergau, W. R., Zimmer, S., et al. (2001). Case\nstudies in moral decision making among adolescents. San Francisco: Jossey-Bass.\nSubstitute et al. (Latin abbreviation for ―and others‖) for all authors’ names after the first six.\n\nFOWLcp52.doc - 9\n4. A book with an editor\nDohrenwend, B. S., & Dohrenwend, B. P. (Eds.). (1999). Stressful life events: Their nature and effects.\nNew York: Wiley.\nList the editors’ names as if they were authors, but follow the last name with (Eds.).—or (Ed.).\nwith only one editor. Note the periods inside and outside the final parenthesis.\n5. A book with a translator\nTrajan, P. D. (1927). Psychology of animals (H. Simone, Trans.). Washington, DC: Halperin.\nThe name of the translator appears in parentheses after the title, followed by a comma, Trans. and\na closing parenthesis, and a final period. Note also the absence of periods in DC.\n6. A book with a group author\nLorenz Research. (2003). Research in social studies teaching. Baltimore: Arrow Books.\nFor a work with a group author—such as a research group, government agency, or corporation—\nbegin the entry with the group name. In the references list, alphabetize the work as if the first main\nword (excluding The, A, and An) were an author’s last name.\n7. A book with no author or an anonymous book\nMerriam-Webster’s collegiate dictionary (11th ed.). (2003). Springfield, MA: Merriam-Webster.\nWhen no author is named, list the work under its title, and alphabetize it by the first main word\n(excluding The, A, An).\nFor a work whose author is actually given as ―Anonymous,‖ use this word in place of the\nauthor’s name and alphabetize it as if it were a name:\nAnonymous. (2006). Teaching research, researching teaching. New York: Alpine Press.\n8. Two or more works by the same author(s)\npublished in the same year\nGardner, H. (1973a). The arts and human development. New York: Wiley.\nGardner, H. (1973b). The quest for mind: Piaget, Lévi-Strauss, and the structuralist movement. New\nYork: Knopf.\nWhen citing two or more works by exactly the same author(s), published in the same year—as in\nthe examples above—arrange them alphabetically by the first main word of the title and distinguish\nthe sources by adding a letter to the date. Both the date and the letter are used in citing the source\nin your text (see p. 787).\nWhen citing two or more works by exactly the same author(s) but not published in the same\nyear, arrange the sources in order of their publication dates, earliest first.\n9. A later edition\nBolinger, D. L. (1981). Aspects of language (3rd ed.). New York: Harcourt Brace Jovanovich.\nThe edition number in parentheses follows the title and is followed by a period.\n10. A work in more than one volume\nLincoln, A. (1953). The collected works of Abraham Lincoln (R. P. Basler, Ed.). (Vol. 5). New\nBrunswick, NJ: Rutgers University Press.\nLincoln, A. (1953). The collected works of Abraham Lincoln (R. P. Basler, Ed.). (Vols. 1–8). New\nBrunswick, NJ: Rutgers University Press.\nThe first entry cites a single volume (5) in the eight-volume set. The second cites all eight volumes.\nUse the abbreviation Vol. or Vols. in parentheses, and follow the closing parenthesis with a\nperiod. In the absence of an editor’s name, the description of volumes would follow the title\ndirectly: The collected works of Abraham Lincoln (Vol. 5).\n\nFOWLcp52.doc - 10\n11. An article or chapter in an edited book\nPaykel, E. S. (1999). Life stress and psychiatric disorder: Applications of the clinical approach. In B. S.\nDohrenwend & B. P. Dohrenwend (Eds.), Stressful life events: Their nature and effects (pp. 239-\n264). New York: Wiley.\nGive the publication date of the collection (1999 above) as the publication date of the article or\nchapter. After the article or chapter\ntitle and a period, say In and then provide the editors’ names (in normal order), (Eds.) and a\ncomma, the title of the collection, and the page numbers of the article in parentheses.\nPeriodicals: Journals, magazines, newspapers\n12. An article in a journal with continuous pagination throughout the annual volume\nEmery, R. E. (2005). Marital turmoil: Interpersonal conflict and the children of discord and divorce.\nPsychological Bulletin, 92, 310-330.\nSee page 666 for an explanation of journal pagination. Note that you do not place the article title\nin quotation marks and that you capitalize only the first words of the title and subtitle. In\ncontrast, you italicize the journal title and capitalize all significant words. Separate the volume\nnumber from the title with a comma, and italicize the number. Do not add ―pp.‖ before the\npage numbers.\n13. An article in a journal that pages issues separately\nDacey, J. (1998). Management participation in corporate buy-outs. Management Perspectives, 7(4), 20-\n31.\nConsult page 666 for an explanation of journal pagination. In this case, place the issue number in\nparentheses after the volume number without intervening space. Do not italicize the issue number.\n14. An abstract of a journal article\nEmery, R. E. (2005). Marital turmoil: Interpersonal conflict and the children of discord and divorce.\nPsychological Bulletin, 92, 310-330. Abstract\nobtained from Psychological Abstracts, 2005, 69, Item 1320.\nWhen you cite the abstract of an article, rather than the article itself, give full publication\ninformation for the article, followed by\nAbstract obtained from and the information for the collection of abstracts, including title,\ndate, volume number, and either page number or other reference number (Item 1320 in the\nexample).\n15. An article in a magazine\nWilliams, N. (2005, October 24). Beethoven’s late quartets. The New York\nReview of Books, 16-18.\nIf a magazine has volume and issue numbers, give them as in models 12 and 13. Also give the full\ndate of the issue: year, followed by a comma, month, and day (if any). Give all page numbers even\nwhen the article appears on discontinuous pages, without ―pp.‖\n16. An article in a newspaper\nKolata, G. (2006, January 7). Kill all the bacteria! The New York Times, pp. B1, B6.\nGive month and day along with year of publication. Use The in the newspaper name if the paper\nitself does. Precede the page number(s) with p. or pp.\n17. An unsigned article\nThe right to die. (1996, October 11). Time, 121, 101.\nList and alphabetize the article under its title, as you would a book with no author (model 7, p.\n792).\n\nFOWLcp52.doc - 11\n18. A review\nDinnage, R. (1987, November 29). Against the master and his men [Review of the book A mind of her\nown: The life of Karen Horney]. The New York Times Book Review, 10-11.\nIf the review is not titled, use the bracketed information as the title, keeping the brackets.\nElectronic sources\nIn general, the APA’s electronic-source references begin as those for print references do:\nauthor(s), date, title. Then you add\ninformation on when and where you retrieved the source—for ex-\nample, an online source might end Retrieved January 8, 2006, from http://\nwww.isu.edu/finance-dl/46732 (in APA style, no period follows a URL at the end of the\nreference).\nUsing the following models for electronic sources, you may have to improvise to match your\nsource to a model. Try to locate all the information required by a model, referring to the sample\nsources on pages 567–70 for help. However, if you search for and still cannot find some\ninformation, then give what you can find. If a source has no publication date, use n.d. (for no\ndate) in place of a publication date (see model 28, p. 796).\nNote When you need to divide a URL from one line to the next, APA style calls for breaking\nonly after a slash or before a period. Do not hyphenate a URL.\n19. A journal article that is published online and in print\nPalfrey, A. (2003). Choice of mates in identical twins [Electronic version]. Modern Psychology, 4(1), 26-\n40.\nIf you consulted the online version of a journal article that appears the same way both online and in\nprint, follow model 12 or 13\n(p. 793) for a print journal article, and insert [Electronic version] between the article title and\nthe following period.\nIf you believe that the online version you consulted differs in some way from the print version,\nomit the bracketed insert and provide a retrieval statement with the date of your access and the\ncomplete URL for the article:\nGrady, G. F. (2003). The here and now of hepatitis B immunization. Today’s Medicine, 13, 145-151.\nRetrieved December 27, 2005, from http://www.fmrt.org/todaysmedicine/Grady050203.html\n20. A journal article retrieved from an electronic database\nWilkins, J. M. (1999). The myths of the only child. Psychology Update, 11(1), 16-23. Retrieved\nDecember 20, 2005, from ProQuest Direct database.\nMany reference works and periodicals are available full-text from electronic databases to which\nyour library subscribes, such as\nProQuest Direct or LexisNexis. Your reference need not specify how you reached the database—\nfor instance, through a Web site or on a CD-ROM. However, it should provide the appropriate\ninformation for the source itself—in the example here, for a journal article—and it should conclude\nwith a retrieval statement giving the date of your access and the name of the database.\n22. (Director). (1993). Schindler’s list [Motion picture]. United States: Viacom.\nA motion picture may be a film, DVD, or video. Depending on whose work you are citing, begin\nwith the name or names of the creator, director, producer, or primary contributor, followed by the\nfunction in parentheses. (The second model above would begin with the producer’s name if you\nwere citing the motion picture as a whole, not specifically the work of the director.) Add [Motion\npicture] after the title. A television series or episode\nCleveland, R., Andries, L., & Taylor, C. (Producers). (2005). Six feet under [Television series]. New\nYork: HBO.\nCleveland, R. (Writer), & Engler, M. (Director). (2005). Dillon Michael Cooper [Television series\nepisode]. In R. Cleveland, L. Andries, & C. Taylor (Producers), Six feet under. New York: HBO.\nFor a television series, begin with the producers’ names and identify their function in parentheses.\nAdd [Television series] after the series title, and give the city and name of the network. For\nan episode, begin with the writer and then the director, identifying the function of each in\nparentheses, and add [Television series episode] after the episode title. Then provide the\nseries information, beginning with In and the producers’ names and function, giving the series\ntitle, and ending with the city and name of the network.\n52e Formatting documents in APA style\nThe APA Publication Manual distinguishes between documents intended for publication\n(which will be set in type) and those submitted by students (which are the final copy). The\nguidelines below apply to most undergraduate papers. Check with your instructor for any\nmodifications to this format.\nNote See pages 788–90 for the APA format of a reference list. And see pages 116–26 for\nguidelines on type fonts, lists, tables and figures, and other elements of document design.\n\nFOWLcp52.doc - 15\nMargins Use one-inch margins on the top, bottom, and right side. Add another half-inch on the\nleft to accommodate a binder.\nSpacing and indentions Double-space your text and references. (See pp. 802–03 for spacing of\ndisplayed quotations.) Indent para-graphs and displayed quotations one-half inch or five to seven\nspaces.\nPaging Begin numbering on the title page, and number consec-utively through the end\n(including the reference list). Type Arabic numerals (1, 2, 3) in the upper right, about half an inch\nfrom the top.\nPlace a shortened version of your title five spaces to the left of the page number.\nTitle page Include the full title, your name, the course title, the instructor’s name, and the date.\nType the title on the top half of the page, followed by the identifying information, all centered\nhorizontally and double-spaced. Include a shortened form of the title along with the page number at\nthe top of this and all other pages.\nAbstract Summarize (in a maximum of 120 words) your sub-ject, research method, findings,\nand conclusions. Put the abstract on a page by itself.\nBody Begin with a restatement of the paper’s title and then an introduction (not labeled). The\nintroduction concisely presents the problem you researched, your research method, the relevant\nback-ground (such as related studies), and the purpose of your research.\nThe next section, labeled Method, provides a detailed discussion of how you conducted your\nresearch, including a description of the research subjects, any materials or tools you used (such as\nquestionnaires), and the procedure you followed. In the illustration below, the label Method is a\nfirst-level heading and the label Sample is a second-level heading.\nFormat headings (including a third level, if needed) as follows:\nFirst-Level Heading\nSecond-Level Heading\nThird-level heading. Run this heading into the text paragraph with a standard paragraph indention.\nThe Results section (labeled with a first-level heading) summarizes the data you collected,\nexplains how you analyzed them, and presents them in detail, often in tables, graphs, or charts.\nThe Discussion section (labeled with a first-level heading) interprets the data and presents\nyour conclusions. (When the discussion is brief, you may combine it with the previous section\nunder the heading Results and Discussion.)\nThe References section, beginning a new page, includes all your sources. See pages 788–\n90 for an explanation and sample.\nLong quotations Run into your text all quotations of forty words or less, and enclose them in\nquotation marks. For quotations of more than forty words, set them off from your text by indenting\nall lines one-half inch or five spaces, double-spacing above and below. For student papers, the\nAPA allows single-spacing of dis-played quotations:\nEchoing the opinions of other Europeans at the time, Freud (1961) had a\npoor view of Americans:\nThe Americans are really too bad. . . . Competition is much more pungent with them, not\nsucceeding means civil death to every one, and they have no private resources apart from their\nprofession, no hobby, games, love or other interests of a cultured person. And success means\nmoney. (p. 86)\nDo not use quotation marks around a quotation displayed in this way.\n\nFOWLcp52.doc - 16\nIllustrations Present data in tables, graphs, or charts, as appro-priate. (See the sample on p. 805\nfor a clear table format to follow.) Begin each illustration on a separate page. Number each kind of\nillustration consecutively and separately from the other (Table 1, Table 2, etc., and Figure 1,\nFigure 2, etc.). Refer to all illustrations in your text—for instance, (see Figure 3). Generally,\nplace illustrations immediately after the text references to them. (See pp. 121–25 for more on\nillustrations.)\n52f Examining a sample social science paper\nOn the following pages are excerpts from a sociology paper. The student followed the\norganization described on the preceding pages both in establishing the background for her study\nand in\nexplaining her own research. She also followed the APA style of source citation and document\nformat, although page borders and running heads are omitted here and only the required page\nbreaks are indicated.\nExcerpts from a research report\n[Title page. See also pp. 801–02]\nAn Assessment of\nDating Violence on Campus\nKaren M. Tarczyk\nSociology 213\nMr. Durkan\nMarch 6, 2006\n[New page.]\nAbstract\nLittle research has examined the patterns of abuse and violence occurring within couples during courtship.\nWith a questionnaire administered to a sample of college students, the extent and nature of such abuse and\nviolence were investigated. The results, some interpretations, and implications for further research are\ndiscussed.\n[New page.]\nAn Assessment of Dating\nViolence on Campus\nIn recent years, a great deal of attention has been devoted to family\nviolence. Numerous studies have been done on spouse and child abuse. However,\nviolent behavior occurs in dating relationships as well, yet the problem of dating violence has been relatively\nignored by sociological research. It should be examined further since the premarital relationship is one context\nin which individuals learn and adopt behaviors that surface later in marriage.\nThe sociologist James Makepeace (1989) contended that courtship violence is a ―potential mediating\nlink‖ between violence in one’s family of orientation and\nviolence in one’s later family of procreation (p. 103). Studying dating behaviors at Bemidji State University in\nMinnesota, Makepeace reported that one-fifth of the\nrespondents had had at least one encounter with dating violence. He then extended these percentages to\nstudents nationwide, suggesting the existence of a major hidden social problem.\nMore recent research supports Makepeace’s. Cates, Rutter, Karl, Linton, and Smith (2000) found that\n22.3% of respondents at Oregon State University had been either the victim or the perpetrator of premarital\nviolence. Another study (Cortes, 2005) found that so-called date rape, while much more publicized and\ndiscussed, was reported by many fewer woman respondents (2%) than was other violence\nduring courtship (21%).\n[The introduction continues.]\nAll these studies indicate a problem that is being neglected. My objective was to gather data on the extent\nand nature of premarital violence and to discuss\npossible interpretations.\nMethod\nSample\n\nFOWLcp52.doc - 17\nI conducted a survey of 200 students (134 females, 66 males) at a large state university in the\nnortheastern United States. The sample consisted of students\nenrolled in an introductory sociology course.\n[The explanation of method continues.]\nThe Questionnaire\nA questionnaire exploring the personal dynamics of relationships was distributed during regularly\nscheduled class. Questions were answered anonymously in a 30-minute time period. The survey consisted of\nthree sections.\n[The explanation of method continues.]\nSection 3 required participants to provide information about their current\ndating relationships. Levels of stress and frustration, communication between partners, and patterns of decision\nmaking were examined. These variables were expected to influence the amount of violence in a relationship.\nThe next part of the survey was adopted from Murray Strauss’s Conflict Tactics Scales (1992). These scales\ncontain 19 items designed to measure conflict and the means of conflict resolution, including reasoning, verbal\naggression, and actual violence.\nResults\nThe questionnaire revealed significant levels of verbal aggression and threatened and actual violence\namong dating couples. A high number of students, 50% (62 of 123 subjects), reported that they had been the\nvictim of verbal abuse. In\naddition, almost 14% (17 of 123) of respondents admitted being threatened with some type of violence, and\nmore than 14% (18 of 123) reported being pushed, grabbed, or shoved. (See Table 1.)\n[The explanation of results continues.]\n[Table on a page by itself.]\nTable 1\nIncidence of Courtship Violence\nNumber of Percentage of\nType of violencestudents reportingsample\nInsulted or swore 62 50.4\nThreatened to hit or throw something17 13.8\nThrew something 8 6.5\nPushed, grabbed, or shoved 18 14.6\nSlapped 8 6.5\nKicked, bit, or hit with fist 7 5.7\nHit or tried to hit with something 2 1.6\nThreatened with a knife or gun 1 0.8\nUsed a knife or gun 1 0.8\nDiscussion\nViolence within premarital relationships has been relatively ignored. The results of the present study\nindicate that abuse and force do occur in dating relationships. Although the percentages are small, so was the\nsample. Extending them to the entire campus population would mean significant numbers. For example, if the\nnearly 6% incidence of being kicked, bitten, or hit with a fist is typical, then 300 students of a 5,000-member\nstudent body might have experienced this type of violence.\n[The discussion continues.]\nIf the courtship period is characterized by abuse and violence, what accounts for it? The other sections of\nthe survey examined some variables that appear to influence the relationship. Level of stress and frustration,\nboth within the relationship and in the respondent’s life, was one such variable. The communication level\nbetween partners, both the frequency of discussion and the frequency of agreement, was another.\n[The discussion continues.]\n\nFOWLcp52.doc - 18\nThe method of analyzing the data in this study, utilizing frequency distributions, provided a clear\noverview. However, more tests of significance and correlation and a closer look at the social and individual\nvariables affecting the relationship are warranted. The courtship period may set the stage for patterns of married\nlife. It merits more attention.\n[New page.]\nReferences\nCates, R. L., Rutter, C. H., Karl, J., Linton, M., & Smith, K. (2000). Premarital abuse: A social psychological\nperspective. Journal of Family Issues, 13(1),\n79-90.\nCortes, L. (2005). Beyond date rape: Violence during courtship. Electronic Journal of Intimate Violence, 5(2).\nRetrieved February 16, 2006, from http://www.acast\n.nova.edu/health/psy/file-disc/file50.html\nGlaser, R., & Rutter, C. H. (Eds.). (1999). Familial violence [Special issue]. Family Relations, 43.\nMakepeace, J. M. (1989). Courtship violence among college students. Family Relations, 28, 97-103.\nStrauss, M. L. (1992). Conflict Tactics Scales. New York: Sociological Tests.\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp52.doc - 19\nVisit the companion Web site for more help with writing in the social\nsciences.\nConducting a survey\nDecide what you want to find out—what your hypothesis is. The questions you ask should be\ndictated by your purpose.\nDefine your population. Think about the kinds of people your hypothesis is about—for instance,\ncollege men, or five-year-old children. Plan to sample this population so that your findings will be\nrepresentative.\nWrite your questions. Surveys may contain closed questions that direct the respondent’s answers\n(checklists and multiple-choice, true/\nfalse, or yes/no questions) or open-ended questions allowing brief, descriptive answers. Avoid loaded\nquestions that reveal your own biases or make assumptions about subjects’ answers, such as ―Do you\nwant the United States to support democracy in China?‖ or ―How much more money does your father\nmake than your mother?‖\nTest your questions. Use a few respondents with whom you can discuss the answers. Eliminate or\nrecast questions that respondents find unclear, discomforting, or unanswerable.\nTally the results. Count the actual numbers of answers, including any nonanswers.\nSeek patterns in the raw data. Such patterns may confirm or contradict your hypothesis. Revise the\nhypothesis or conduct additional research if necessary.\nAPA parenthetical text citations\n1. Author not named in your text 785\n2. Author named in your text 785\n3. A work with two authors 786\n4. A work with three to five authors 786\n5. A work with six or more authors 786\n6. A work with a group author 787\n7. A work with no author or an anonymous work 787\n8. One of two or more works by the same author(s) 787\n9. Two or more works by different authors 787\n10. An indirect source 787\n11. A television series or episode 799\nAPA t itle page\n1⁄2''\n5 spaces\nDating Violence 1\nAn Assessment of\nDating Violence on Campus\nKaren M. Their purpose is to understand natural and\ntechnological phenomena. (A phenomenon is a fact or event that can be known by the senses.)\nScientists conduct experiments and write to explain the step-by-step processes in their methods of\ninquiry and discovery.\n53a Using the methods and evidence of the sciences\nScientists investigate phenomena by the scientific method, a process of continual testing and\nrefinement.\nScientific evidence is almost always quantitative—that is, it consists of numerical data\nobtained from the measurement of phenomena. These data are called empirical (from a Greek word\nfor ―experience‖); they result from observation and experience, generally in a controlled laboratory\nsetting but also (as sometimes in astronomy or biology) in the natural world. Often the empirical\nevidence for scientific writing comes from library research into other people’s reports of their\ninvestigations. Surveys of known data or existing literature are common in scientific writing.\n53b Understanding writing assignments in the sciences\nNo matter what your assignment, you will be expected to document and explain your evidence\ncarefully so that anyone reading can check your sources and replicate your research. It is important\nfor your reader to know the context of your research—both the previous experimentation and\nresearch on your particular subject (acknowledged in the survey of the literature) and the physical\nconditions and other variables surrounding your own work.\nAssignments in the natural and applied sciences include the following:\nA summary distills a research article to its essence in brief, concise form. (Summary is\ndiscussed in detail on pp. 140–42.)\nA critique summarizes and critically evaluates a scientific report.\nA laboratory report explains the procedure and results of an ex-periment conducted by the\nwriter. (See p. 820 for an example.)\nA research report explains the experimental research of other scientists and the writer’s own\nmethods, findings, and conclusions.\nA research proposal reviews the relevant literature and explains a plan for further research.\nA laboratory report has four or five major sections:\n1. “Abstract”: a summary of the report.\n2. “Introduction” or “Objective”: a review of why the study was undertaken, a summary of the\nbackground of the study, and a statement of the problem being studied.\n3. “Method” or “Procedure”: a detailed explanation of how the study was conducted, including\nany statistical analysis.\n\nFOWLcp53.doc - 2\n4. “Results”: an explanation of the major findings (including unexpected results) and a summary\nof the data presented in graphs and tables.\n5. “Discussion”: an interpretation of the results and an explanation of how they relate to the\ngoals of the experiment. This section also describes new hypotheses that might be tested as a\nresult of the experiment. If the section is brief, it may be combined with the previous section\nin a single section labeled ―Conclusions.”\nIn addition, laboratory or research reports may include a list of references (if other sources\nwere consulted). They almost always include tables and figures (graphs and charts) containing the\ndata from the research (see p. 822).\n53c Using the tools and language of the sciences\nTools and language concerns vary from discipline to discipline in the sciences. Consult your\ninstructor for specifics about the field in which you are writing. You can also discover much about\na discipline’s tools and language from the research sources listed on the next three pages.\n1 Writing tools\nIn the sciences a lab notebook or scientific journal is almost indispensable for accurately\nrecording the empirical data from observations and experiments. Use such a notebook or journal\nfor these purposes:\nRecord observations from reading, from class, or from the lab.\nAsk questions and refine hypotheses.\nRecord procedures.\nRecord results.\nKeep an ongoing record of ideas and findings and how they change as data accumulate.\nSequence and organize your material as you compile your findings and write your report.\nMake sure that your records of data are clearly separate from your reflections on the data so that\nyou don’t mistakenly confuse the two in drawing your conclusions.\n2 Language considerations\nScience writers prefer to use objective language that removes the writer as a character in the\nsituation and events being explained, except as the impersonal agent of change, the experimenter.\nAlthough usage is changing, scientists still rarely use I in their reports and evaluations, and they\noften resort to the passive voice of verbs, as in The mixture was then subjected to centrifugal force.\nThis conscious objectivity focuses attention (including the writer’s) on the empirical data and what\nthey show. It discourages the writer from, say, ascribing motives and will to animals and plants. For\ninstance, instead of asserting that the sea tortoise evolved its hard shell to protect its body, a\nscientist would write only what could be observed: that the hard shell covers and thus protects the\ntortoise’s body.\nScience writers typically change verb tenses to distinguish between established information\nand their own research. For established information, such as that found in journals and other\nreliable sources, use the present tense: Baroreceptors monitor blood pressure. For your own and\nothers’ research, use the past tense: The bacteria died within three hours. Marti reported some\nsuccess.\nEach discipline in the natural and applied sciences has a specialized vocabulary that permits\nprecise, accurate, and efficient communication. Some of these terms, such as pressure in physics,\nhave different meanings in the common language and must be handled carefully in science writing.\nOthers, such as enthalpy in chemistry, have no meanings in the common language and must simply\nbe learned and used correctly.\n\nFOWLcp53.doc - 3\n3 Research sources\nThe following lists give resources in the sciences.\nSpecialized encyclopedias, (physicsweb.org)\nScience@NASA (science.hq.nasa.gov/index.html)\nCiting sources in CSE style\n53d\nWithin the natural and applied sciences, practitioners use one of two styles of documentation,\nvarying slightly from discipline to discipline. Following are some of the style guides most often\nconsulted:\nAmerican Chemical Society, ACS Style Guide: A Manual for Authors and Editors, 2nd ed., 1997\nAmerican Institute of Physics, Style Manual for Guidance in the Preparation of Papers, 4th ed., 1997\nAmerican Medical Association Manual of Style, 9th ed., 1998\n\nFOWLcp53.doc - 5\nCouncil of Biology Editors, Scientific Style and Format: The CBE Manual for Authors, Editors, and\nPublishers, 6th ed., 1994\nThe most thorough and widely used of these guides is the last one, Scientific Style and Format. Its\nsponsoring organization, the Council of Science Editors, was until 2000 called the Council of\nBiology Editors, so the style is abbreviated either CSE (as in this book)\nor CBE.\nScientific Style and Format details both styles of scientific documentation: one using author\nand date and one using numbers. Both types of text citation refer to a list of references at the end of\nthe paper (see the next page). Ask your instructor which style you should use.\n1 Using CSE name-year text citations\nIn the CSE name-year style, parenthetical text citations provide the last name of the author\nbeing cited and the source’s year of publication. At the end of the paper, a list of references,\narranged alphabetically by authors’ last names, provides complete information on each source.\nThe CSE name-year style closely resembles the APA name-year style detailed on pages 785–\n88. You can follow the APA examples for in-text citations, making several notable changes for\nCSE:\nDo not use a comma to separate the author’s name and the date: (Baumrind 1968, p.\n34).\nSeparate two authors’ names with and (not “&”): (Pepinsky and DeStefano 1997).\nUse and others (not “et al.”) for three or more authors: (Rutter and others 1996).\nList unnamed or anonymous authors as Anonymous, both in the citation and in the list of\nreferences: (Anonymous 1976).\n2 Using CSE numbered text citations\nIn the CSE number style, raised numbers in the text refer to a numbered list of references at\nthe end of the paper.\nTwo standard references1,2 use this term.\nThese forms of immunity have been extensively researched.3\nHepburn and Tatin2 do not discuss this project.\nAssignment of numbers The number for each source is based on the order in which you cite the\nsource in the text: the first cited source is 1, the second is 2, and so on.\nReuse of numbers When you cite a source you have already cited and numbered, use the\noriginal number again (see the last ex-ample above, which reuses the number 2 from the first\nexample).\nThis reuse is the key difference between the CSE numbered citations and numbered references\nto footnotes or endnotes. In the CSE style, each source has only one number, determined by the\norder in which the source is cited. With notes, in contrast, the numbering proceeds in sequence, so\nthat each source has as many numbers as it has citations in the text.\nCitation of two or more sources When you cite two or more sources at once, arrange their\nnumbers in sequence and separate them with a comma and no space, as in the first example on the\nprevious page.\n3 Using a CSE reference list\nFor both the name-year and the number styles of in-text citation, provide a list, titled\nReferences, of all sources you have cited. Format the page as shown for APA references on page\n788, except that CSE entries are single-spaced.\nThe following examples show the differences and similarities between the name-year and\nnumber styles:\n\nFOWLcp53.doc - 6\nName-year style\nHepburn PX, Tatin JM. 2005. Human physiology. New York: Columbia Univ Pr. 1026 p.\nNumber style\n2. Hepburn PX, Tatin JM. Human physiology. New York: Columbia Univ Pr; 2005. 1026 p.\nSpacing In both styles, single-space each entry and double-space between entries.\nArrangement In the name-year style, arrange entries alphabetically by authors’ last names. In\nthe number style, arrange entries in numerical order—that is, in order of their citation in the text.\nFormat In both styles, begin the first line of each entry at the left margin and indent\nsubsequent lines.\nAuthors In both styles, list each author’s name with the last name first, followed by initials for\nfirst and middle names. Do not use a comma between an author’s last name and initials, and\ndo not use periods or spaces with the initials. Do use a comma to separate authors’ names.\nPlacement of dates In the name-year style, the date follows the author’s or authors’ names. In\nthe number style, the date follows the publication information (for a book) or the periodical title\n(for a journal, magazine, or newspaper).\nJournal titles In both styles, do not underline or italicize journal titles. For titles of two or more\nwords, abbreviate words of six or more letters (without periods) and omit most prepositions,\narticles, and conjunctions. Capitalize each word. For example, Journal of Chemical and\nBiochemical Studies becomes J Chem Biochem Stud.\nBook and article titles In both styles, do not underline, italicize, or use quotation marks around\na book or an article title. Capitalize only the first word and any proper nouns.\nPublication information for journal articles The name-year and number styles differ in the\nplacement of the publication date (see opposite). However, both styles end with the journal’s\nvolume number, any issue number in parentheses, a colon, and the inclusive page numbers of the\narticle, run together without space: 28:329-30 or 62(2):26-40.\nThe following box indexes the CSE models. White Plains (NY): Teaching Media; 2005. 40 min, sound, color.\n53e Formatting documents in CSE style\nScientific Style and Format is not specific about margins, spacing for headings, and other\nelements of document format. Unless your instructor specifies otherwise, you can use the format of\nthe APA (pp. 800–03). The CSE exception to this style is the list of references, which is described\non pages 814–15.\nThe most troublesome aspects of manuscript preparation in\nthe sciences are equations or formulas, tables, and figures. When typing equations or formulas, be\ncareful to reproduce alignments, indentions, underlining, and characters accurately. If your word\nprocessor lacks special characters, leave space for them and write them in by hand.\nBecause you will be expected to share your data with your readers, most of your writing for\nthe sciences is likely to require illustrations to present the data in concise, readable form. Tables\nusually summarize raw data (see p. 822 for an example), whereas figures (mainly charts and\ngraphs) recast the data to show noteworthy comparisons or changes. Follow the guidelines on\npages 121–25 for preparing tables and figures.\n\nFOWLcp53.doc - 10\n53f Examining a sample science paper\nThe following biology paper illustrates the CSE number style for documenting sources. On\npage 823 passages from the paper and a reformatted list of references show the name-year style.\nExcept for the citations and the references, the paper is formatted in APA style because CSE does\nnot specify a format.\nA laboratory report: CSE number style\n[Title page.]\nExercise and Blood Pressure\nLiz Garson\nBiology 161\nMs. Traversa\nDecember 13, 2005\n[New page.]\nAbstract\nThe transient elevation of blood pressure following exercise was demonstrated by pressure measurements of\ntwenty human subjects before and after exercise.\n[New page.]\nExercise and Blood Pressure\nIntroduction\nThe purpose of this experiment was to verify the changes in blood pressure that accompany exercise, as\ncommonly reported.1,2 A certain blood pressure is necessary for the blood to supply nutrients to the body\ntissues. Baroreceptors near the heart monitor pressure by determining the degree to which blood stretches the\nwall of the blood vessel.\n[The introduction continues.]\nDuring exercise, the metabolic needs of the muscles override the influence of the baroreceptors and result\nin an increase in blood pressure. This increase in blood pressure is observed uniformly (irrespective of sex or\nrace), although men demonstrate a higher absolute systolic pressure than do women.3 During strenuous\nexercise, blood pressure can rise to 40 percent above baseline.1\nMethod\nThe subjects for this experiment were twenty volunteers from laboratory classes, ten men and ten women.\nAll pressure measurements were performed using a standard sphygmomanometer, which was tested for\naccuracy. To ensure consistency, the same sphygmomanometer was used to take all readings. In addition, all\nmeasurements were taken by the same person to avoid discrepancies in method or\ninterpretation.\nThe first pressure reading was taken prior to exercise as the subject sat in a chair. This pressure was\nconsidered the baseline for each subject. All subsequent readings were interpreted relative to this baseline.\nIn the experiment, the subjects ran up and down stairs for fifteen minutes. Immediately after exercising,\nthe subjects returned to the laboratory to have their pressure measured. Thirty minutes later, the pressure was\nmeasured for the final time.\nResults\nTable 1 contains the blood pressure measurements for the male and female subjects. With the exception\nof subjects 3 and 14, all subjects demonstrated the expected post-exercise increase in blood pressure, with a\ndecline to baseline or near baseline thirty minutes after exercise. The data for subjects 3 and 14 were invalid\nbecause the subjects did not perform the experiment as directed.\nDiscussion\nAs expected, most of the subjects demonstrated an increase in blood pressure immediately after exercise\nand a decline to near baseline levels thirty minutes after exercise. The usual pressure increase was 20-40 mmHg\nfor the systolic pressure and 5-10 mmHg for the diastolic pressure.\n[Table on a page by itself.]\nTable 1. Accurately note all details of the phenomenon being researched.\nAsk questions about the observations.\nFormulate a hypothesis, or preliminary generalization, that explains the observed facts.\nTest the hypothesis with additional observation or controlled experiments.\nIf the hypothesis proves accurate, formulate a theory, or unified model, that explains why.\nIf the hypothesis is disproved, revise it or start anew.\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp53.doc - 12\nVisit the companion Web site for more help with writing in the natural and applied sciences.\nCSE reference-list models\nBooks\n1.
