Many forms of online writing\nexpand your options as a writer, but they also present distinctive challenges, both conceptual and\ntechnical. This chapter discusses some of the options and challenges of\ne-mail (below), online collaboration (p. 829), and Web composition (p. 832).\n54a Writing effective electronic mail\nYou may be using e-mail every day to converse quickly and casually with friends and family.\nIn college you’ll also use e-mail for a host of academic reasons, from collaborating with classmates\nto conducting research, and you’ll want to communicate both purposefully and efficiently. This\nsection covers composing and responding to messages and observing Internet etiquette. For more\non using\ne-mail to interact with the other students in a course, see pages 829–32. For more on using e-mail\nas a research tool, see page 592.\n1 Composing messages\nTo use e-mail productively, pause to weigh each element of the message. Consider especially\nyour audience and purpose and how your tone will come across to readers. In the message shown\nopposite, the writer knows the recipients well and yet has serious information to convey to them, so\nhe writes informally but states his points and concerns carefully. Writing to the corporation\nmentioned in the message, the writer would be more formal in both tone and approach. Although e-\nmail is typically more casual than printed correspondence, in academic settings a crafted message is\nmore likely to achieve the intended purpose. Proofread all but the most informal messages for\nerrors in grammar, punctuation, and spelling.\n\nFOWLcp54.doc - 2\n2 Responding to messages\nWhen you respond to a message, consider whom you’re addressing and what your readers will\nsee. The Reply function will automatically address the person who wrote you, whereas the Reply\nAll function will address others who may have been sent copies of the original message. The\nSubject line will automatically contain the original subject heading preceded by Re: (from Latin,\nmeaning ―In reference to‖), so change the heading if you change or expand the subject. Many e-\nmail programs can be set to reprint the entire original message, allowing you to insert your\nresponses where appropriate or to respond to part of the message and delete the rest. If you add\nmore recipients to your response, make sure not to pass on previous private messages by mistake.\n3 Observing netiquette\nTo communicate effectively online, you’ll need to abide by some rules of behavior and simple\ncourtesies. You won’t always see others observing this netiquette, or Internet etiquette, but you\nwill see that those who do observe it receive the more thoughtful and considerate replies.\nAddressing messages\nAvoid spamming. With a few keystrokes, you can broadcast a message to many recipients at\nonce—all the students in a course, say, or all the participants in a discussion group.\nOccasionally, you may indeed have a worthwhile idea or important information that everyone\non the list will want to know. But flooding whole lists with irrelevant messages—called spam-\nming—is rude and irritating.\nAvoid sending frivolous messages to all the members of a group. Instead of dashing off ―I\nagree‖ and distributing the two-word message widely, put some time into composing a\nthoughtful response and send it only to those who will be interested.\nComposing messages\nRemember that the messages you receive represent individuals. Don’t say or do anything\nthat you wouldn’t say or do face to face.\nUse names. In the body of your message, address your read-er(s) by name if possible and sign\noff with your own name and information on how to contact you. Your own name is especially\nimportant if your e-mail address does not spell it out.\nPay careful attention to tone. Refrain from flaming, or attacking, correspondents. Don’t use\nall capital letters, which SHOUT. And use irony or sarcasm only cautiously: in the absence of\nfacial expressions, they can lead to misunderstandings. To indicate irony and emotions, you\ncan use emoticons, such as the smiley :-). These sideways faces can easily be overused,\nthough, and should not substitute for thoughtfully worded opinions.\nAvoid saying anything in e-mail that you would not say in a printed document such as a\nletter or memo. E-mail can usually be retrieved from the server, and in business and academic\nsettings it may well be retrieved in disputes over contracts, grades, and other matters.\nReading and responding to messages\nBe a forgiving reader. Avoid nitpicking over spelling or other surface errors. And because\nattitudes are sometimes difficult to convey, give authors an initial benefit of the doubt: a writer\nwho at first seems hostile may simply have tried too hard to be concise; a writer who at first\nseems unserious may simply have failed at injecting humor into a worthwhile message.\nForward messages only with permission. You may want to send a message you’ve received\nto someone else, but do so only if you know that the author of the message won’t mind.\n\nFOWLcp54.doc - 3\nAvoid participating in flame “wars,” overheated dialogs that\ncontribute little or no information or understanding. If a war breaks out in a discussion,\nignore it: don’t rush to defend someone who is being attacked, and don’t respond even if\nyou are under attack yourself.\n54b Collaborating online\nWriting often involves collaborating with others as much as\nit does working in solitude. Indeed, many businesses and teachers\nexpect writers to collaborate on generating ideas and producing\nand revising drafts. Computers have vastly expanded the options for collaboration, ranging from\nsimple e-mail exchanges to video conferencing and virtual environments.\nIn this section we look at strategies for online collaboration in your courses. Your instructors\nor your school’s technology advisers will introduce you to the system and help set you up. Here we\nfocus on nontechnical matters of participating in discussions and working on drafts.\n1 Participating in discussions\nMany instructors use online conversations for discussing class readings and other topics and\nto help students generate ideas for writing. There are two basic types of online conversation:\ndelayed conversation, such as that occurring by e-mail or on a Web forum or blog; and real-time\nchat (also called synchronous communication), which occurs immediately, like a telephone\nconversation.\nDelayed conversation\nE-mail, Web discussion groups, and Web logs allow detailed, thoughtful messages and\nresponses, so they are good places to try out ideas, explore assignments, and respond to others’\nwork. When writing in such media, observe the netiquette guidelines on pages 828–29.\nThe screen shots on these pages show part of a discussion thread on Blackboard courseware\nand a query and responses on a course blog. The writing is casual but also thoughtful and specific.\nIn the Blackboard examples, notice that the second writer actually challenges the first writer’s\nassumption but frames the challenge productively in the context of the issue being discussed.\nDisagreements are bound to occur in online conversation, but they need not be unpleasant.\nOnline chat\nYou may be familiar with chat conversations from using instant messaging with friends and\nfamily. In academic settings, chat will likely occur with courseware such as WebCT or Blackboard.\nCollaborating via chat discussions will be more productive if you take a few tips:\nUse the chat space for brainstorming topics and exchanging impressions. The pace of\nonline chat rarely allows lengthy consideration and articulation of messages.\nFocus on a thread or common topic. Online chat can be the electronic equivalent of a party,\nwith different conversations occurring in the same space. If you have trouble tracking all the\nmessages, concentrate on the ones that relate to your interest.\nWrite as quickly and fluidly as possible. Don’t worry about pro-ducing perfect prose.\n2 Working on drafts\nIn writing and other courses, you and your fellow students may be invited to exchange and\nrespond to one another’s projects by\ne-mail or over the Web. To guide your reading of others’ work, use the revision checklist on page\n51 and the collaboration tips on pages 66–69. Focus on the deep issues in others’ drafts, especially\nearly drafts: thesis, purpose, audience, organization, and support for the thesis. Hold comments on\nstyle, grammar, punctuation, and other surface matters until you’re reviewing late drafts, if indeed\nyou are expected to comment on them at all.\nExchanging drafts online generally requires a file-naming system that identifies each project’s\nwriter, title, and version. Your instructor may establish such a system, or you and your classmates\ncan develop one. See pages 53–54 for tips.\n\nFOWLcp54.doc - 4\n54c Creating effective Web compositions\nCreating a Web page or site is sometimes as simple as saving a document in a different format,\nbut more often it means thinking in a new way.\nThe diagrams on the facing page show a key difference between traditional printed documents\nand Web sites. Most traditional documents are meant to be read in sequence from start to finish. In\ncontrast, most Web sites are so-called hypertexts: they are intended to be examined in whatever\norder readers choose as they follow links to pages within the site and to other sites.\nWhen you create a composition for the Web, it will likely fall into one of two categories\ndiscussed in this section: pages such as class papers that resemble printed documents in being\nlinear and text-heavy and that call for familiar ways of writing and reading; or ―native‖ hypertext\ndocuments that you build from scratch, which call for screen-oriented writing and reading.\nThese general guidelines will help you create effective Web sites:\nPlan the site carefully. A hypertext can disorient readers as they scroll up and down and\npursue various links. Page length, links, menus, and other cues should work to keep readers\noriented.\nAnticipate what readers may see on their screens. Each reader’s screen frames and\norganizes the experience of a Web composition. Screen space is limited, and it varies from one\ncomputer to another. Text and visual elements should be managed for maximum clarity and\neffectiveness on a variety of screens.\nIntegrate visual and sound elements into the text. Web compositions can include not only\ntables, charts, and photographs (which printed documents may also have) but also video (such\nas animation or film clips) and audio (such as music or excerpts from speeches). However, any\nvisual or sound elements should not merely embellish the text but contribute substantially to\nit. In addition, you should find out whether your readers’ equipment will likely be able to\nhandle multimedia elements and whether readers themselves may have disabilities that prevent\ntheir seeing or hearing such elements (see the note below).\nAcknowledge your sources. It’s easy to incorporate material from other sources into a Web\nsite, but you have the same obli-gation to cite your sources as you do in a printed document\n(see pp. 629–34). Further, your Web site is a form of publication, like a magazine or a book.\nUnless the material you are using explicitly allows copying without permission, you may need\nto seek the copyright holder’s permission, just as print publishers do. (See pp. 635–37 for\nmore on copyright.)\nNote If you anticipate that some of your readers may have visual, hearing, or reading\ndisabilities, you’ll need to consider their needs while designing Web sites. Some of these\nconsiderations are covered under document design on pages 125–26, and others are fundamental to\nany effective Web design, as discussed in this section. In addition, avoid any content that relies\nexclusively on images or sound, instead supplementing such elements with text descriptions. At the\nsame time, try to provide key concepts both as text and as images and sound. For more on Web\ndesign for readers with disabilities, visit the World Wide Web Consortium at w3.org/ tr/wai-\nwebcontent or the American Council for the Blind at acb.org/ accessible-formats.html.\n1 Using HTML\nMost Web pages are created using hypertext markup language, or HTML, and an HTML\neditor. The HTML editing program inserts command codes into your document that achieve the\neffects you want when the material appears on the Web.\nFrom the user’s point of view, most HTML editors work much as word processors do, with\nsimilar options for sizing, formatting, and highlighting copy and with a display that shows what\nyou will see in the final version. Indeed, you can compose a Web page without bothering at all\nabout the behind-the-scenes HTML coding. As you gain experience with Web building, however,\nyou may want to create more sophisticated pages by editing the codes themselves.\nThere are many HTML editors on the market. The Web site for this book\n(ablongman.com/littlebrown) provides links to free or low-cost editors.\n2 Creating online papers\nIf an instructor asks you to post a paper to a Web site, you can compose it on your word\nprocessor and then use the Save As HTML function available on most programs to translate it into\na Web page. After translating the paper, your word processor should allow you to modify some of\n\nFOWLcp54.doc - 5\nthe elements on the page, or you can open the translated document in an HTML editor. The\nillustration below shows the opening screen of a student’s project for a composition course.\n3 Creating original sites\nWhen you create an original Web site, you need to be aware that Web readers generally\nalternate between skimming pages for highlights and focusing intently on sections of text. To\nfacilitate this kind of reading, you’ll want to consider the guidelines on pages 833–34 for handling\ntext and also your site’s structure and content, flow, ease of navigation, and use of images, video,\nand sound.\nStructure and content\nOrganize your site so that it efficiently arranges your content and also orients readers:\nSketch possible site plans before getting started. (See p. 833 for an example.) Your aim is to\ndevelop a sense of the major components of your project and to create a logical space for each\ncomponent.\nConsider how menus on the site’s pages can provide over-views of the organization as\nwell as direct access to the pages. The Web page below includes a menu on the left side of\nthe page.\nTreat the first few sentences of any page as a get-acquainted space for you and your\nreaders. On the page below, the text hooks readers with questions and orients them with\ngeneral information.\nDistill your text so that it includes only essential information. Concise prose is essential in\nany writing situation, of course. But Web readers expect to scan text quickly and, in any event,\nhave difficulty following long text passages on a computer screen.\nFlow\nBeginning Web authors sometimes start at the top of the page and then add element upon\nelement until information proceeds down the screen much as it would in a printed document.\nHowever, by thinking about how information will flow on a page, you can take better advantage of\nthe Web’s visual nature:\nStandardize elements of your design to create expectations in readers and to fulfill those\nexpectations. For instance, develop a uniform style for the main headings of pages, for\nheadings within pages, and for menus.\nMake scanning easy for readers. Focus readers on crucial text by adding space around it.\nAdd headings to break up text and to highlight content. Use lists to reinforce the parallel\nimportance of items. (See pp. 116–20 for more on all these design elements.)\nEasy navigation\nA Web site of more than a few pages requires a menu on every page so that readers can\nnavigate the site. Like the table of contents in a book, a menu lists the features of the site, giving its\nplan at a glance. By clicking on any item in the list, readers can go directly to a page that interests\nthem.\nYou can embed a menu at the top, side, or bottom of a page. Menus at the top or side are best\non short pages because they will not scroll off the screen as readers move down the page. On longer\npages, menus at the bottom prevent readers from dead-ending—that is, reaching a point where they\ncan’t easily move forward or backward. You can also use a combination of menus.\nIn designing a menu, keep it simple: many different type fonts and colors will overwhelm\nreaders instead of orienting them. And make the menus look the same from one page to the next so\nthat readers recognize them easily.\nImages, video, and sound\nExploring the Web, you’ll see that site designers have taken\nadvantage of the Web’s ability to handle multimedia elements—\nimages, video, and sound. Most Web readers expect at least some\nenhancement of text.\nNote See pages 636–37 on observing copyright restrictions with images, video, and sound.\n\nFOWLcp54.doc - 6\nImages\nSeveral guidelines can help you use images effectively in your Web compositions:\nUse visual elements for a purpose. They should supplement or replace text, highlight\nimportant features, and direct the flow of information. Don’t use them for their own sake, as\nmere decoration.\nMake the size of your files a central concern so that readers don’t have to wait forever for\nyour site to download. If you are using lines or other icons, choose a limited number. If you\nare using photographs or other images, try to keep the file size below thirty kilobytes (30k).\nCompose descriptions of images that relate them to your text. Don’t ask the elements to\nconvey your meaning by themselves.\nProvide alternative descriptions of images to give a sense of them to readers with disabilities\nor readers whose Web brows-ers can’t display them.\nVideo and sound\nVideo and sound files can provide information that is simply unavailable in printed\ndocuments. For instance, as part of a film review you might place a short clip from the film on your\nWeb page and then provide a close reading of the clip. Or as part of a project on a controversial\nissue you might provide links to sound files containing political speeches.\nHowever, the advantages of video and sound in Web compositions are offset by at least two\ncomplications: the files are generally large and difficult to work with, and readers need a fast\nconnection and special software to download and open the files. Before you incorporate such\nelements into your Web compositions, make certain that they have a legitimate purpose. They\nshould add essential information that can’t be provided in any other medium, and they should be\nwell integrated with the rest of your composition.\nSources\nFor the multimedia elements in a Web composition, you can use your own or obtain them\nfrom other sources:\nCreate your own graphs, diagrams, and other illustrations\nusing a graphics program. See pages 120–25 for tips on creating effective images.\nIncorporate your own artwork, photographs, video clips, and sound recordings. You may\nbe able to find the needed equipment and software at your campus computer lab.\nObtain icons, photographs, video, and other multimedia elements from other electronic\nsources. Be sure that you have enough space on your hard drive or a disk to hold the file. Also\nbe sure to acknowledge your sources and to obtain reprint permission if neeeded (see pp. 635–\n37).\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp54.doc - 7\nVisit the companion Web site for more help with electronic mail, online\ncollaboration, and Web composition.\nE-mail message\nConsider who needs to read your message as you address it.\nUse the Subject line to describe accurately the content of the message.\nAttach files of shared work (see the box\nbelow).\nAdjust the content and tone to the intended audience.\nUse short paragraphs with space between them.\nIncompatible or encoded attachments\nE-mail attachments are not always readable because of incompatible file formats or because of\ncompression or encoding that requires special software at the recipient’s end. If you have trouble sending\nor receiving attachments, try these strategies:\nSend your documents in rich text format (RTF) or in text-only format, both of which can\nbe read by most word processors. RTF preserves most formatting, whereas text-only format\ndoes not. With either option, save a copy of your document (not the original) in the new\nformat. In your word processor’s File menu, first choose Save to preserve the original\ndocument. Then choose Save As, give the document a new name, go to the Save As Type\noption, and choose Rich Text Format or Text Only.\nCopy the document into the body of an e-mail message. You will lose most, if not all, of your\ndocument’s formatting.\nUse special software to read encoded or compressed files. Your school’s technology advisers can\nhelp you obtain and use such programs, which are usually free and available over the Internet.\nE-mail response\nMake sure the To field addresses the appropriate person or people.\nUse the subject of the original message unless you are changing or expanding it.\nWhen quoting the\noriginal message,\nselect only the parts you are responding to and delete the rest.\nDiscussion on Blackboard\nDiscussion on a course Web log\nTraditional print document\nWeb site\nPaper submitted on the Web\nWhite background\nproviding strong\ncontrast\nReadingWorks\nSpringfield Veterans Administration Hospital\nMaking a Difference:A Service-Learning Project\nAlex Ramirez\nIlliteracy or low literacy skills among military veterans is a pervasive problem in Springfield\nCounty and around the US. It usually involves the same basic writing process, discussed in Part 1:\nassessing the writing situation, developing what you want to say, freely working out your meaning\nin a draft, and editing and revising so that your writing will achieve your purpose with readers. It\noften involves research, as discussed in Part 9. And it involves the standards of conciseness,\nappropriate and exact language, and correct grammar and usage discussed in Parts 3–8.\nBut public writing has its own conventions, too. They vary widely depending on what you’re\nwriting and why, whether a proposal for your job or a flyer announcing a dinner for a community\ngroup. This chapter covers several types of public writing: business letters and memos (next page);\njob applications (p. 845); business reports and proposals (p. 848); and flyers, newsletters, and\nbrochures for community work (p. 851).\nNote Chapter 5 discusses type fonts, headings, illustrations, and other elements of document\ndesign. In addition, a word processor’s wizards or templates can help you format documents such\nas letters, résumés, and brochures. Before using such a tool, be sure the format is appropriate for\nyour writing situation. And remember that a formatting tool can do nothing to help you express\nyour ideas effectively.\nPublic writing in the United States, especially in business, favors efficiency. If you are\naccustomed to public writing in another culture, the US style may seem abrupt or impolite. A\nbusiness letter elsewhere may be expected to begin with polite questions about the addressee or\nwith compliments for the addressee’s company, whereas US business letters are expected to get\nright to the point. (See the sample letters in this chapter for examples.)\nWriting business letters and memos\nWhen you write in business, you are addressing busy people who want to see quickly why you\nare writing and how they should respond to you. A wordy letter or a memo with grammatical errors\nmay prevent you from getting what you want, either because the reader cannot understand your\nwish or because you present yourself poorly.\nIn all business writing, follow these general guidelines:\nState your purpose right at the start.\nBe straightforward, clear, concise, objective, and courteous.\nObserve conventions of grammar and usage, which make your writing clear and impress\nyour reader with your care.\nThe formats of business letters and memos are fairly standardized and are thus expected by\nyour correspondents.\nUsing a standard format for letters\nUse either unlined white paper measuring 81/2\" ¥ 11\" or what is called letterhead stationery\nwith your address printed at the top of the sheet. Type the letter single-spaced, with double space\nbetween elements, on only one side of a sheet.\nThe two most common forms for business letters—the full block and the modified block—are\nillustrated opposite and on page 846, respectively. Annotations on the samples indicate spacing,\nmargins, and other aspects of format.\n\nFOWLcp55.doc - 2\nThe letter\nReturn-address heading Unless you’re using letterhead stationery, the return-address heading\nof the letter gives your address (but not your name) and the date. If you are using letterhead, you\nneed add only the date.\nInside address The inside address shows the name, title, and complete address of the person\nyou are writing to. (See p. 842 for abbreviations of state names.)\nSalutation The salutation greets the addressee. Whenever pos-sible, address your letter to a\nspecific person. (Call the company or department to ask whom to address.) If you can’t find a\nperson’s name, then use a job title (Dear Human Resources Manager, Dear Customer Service\nManager) or use a general salutation (Dear Smythe Shoes). Use Ms. as the title for a woman when\nshe has no other title, when you don’t know how she prefers to be addressed, or when you know\nthat she prefers to be addressed as Ms. If you know a woman prefers to be addressed as Mrs. or\nMiss, use the appropriate title.\nBody The body of the letter, containing its substance, begins at the left margin in both letter\nstyles. Instead of indenting the first line of each paragraph, place an extra line of space between\nparagraphs so that they are readily visible.\nClose The letter’s close should reflect the level of formality\nin the salutation. For formal letters, Cordially, Yours truly, and\nSincerely are common closes. For less formal letters, you may choose to use Regards, Best wishes,\nor the like. Only the first word of the close is capitalized, and the close is followed by a comma.\nSignature The signature of a business letter falls below the close and has two parts. One is\nyour name typed on the fourth line below the close. The other is your handwritten signature, which\nfills the space between the close and your typed name. The signature should consist only of your\nname, as you sign checks and other documents.\nOther information Below the signature at the left margin, you may want to include additional\ninformation such as Enc. 3 (indicating that there are three enclosures with the letter) or cc:\nMargaret Newton (indicating that a copy is being sent to the person named).\nThe envelope\nThe envelope should accommodate the letter once it is folded horizontally in thirds. The\nfollowing are the common Postal Service abbreviations for addresses:\nStreet names\nAvenue AVE Expressway EXPY Road RD\nBoulevard BLVD Freeway FWY Square SQ\nCircle CIR Lane LN Street ST\nCourt CT Parkway PKY Turnpike TPKE\nCompass points\nNorth N West W Southwest SW\nEast E Northeast NE Northwest NW\nState names\nAlabama AL Kentucky KY North Dakota ND\nAlaska AK Louisiana LA Ohio OH\nArizona AZ Maine ME Oklahoma OK\nArkansas AR Maryland MD Oregon OR\nCalifornia CA Massachusetts MA Pennsylvania PA\nColorado CO Michigan MI Puerto Rico PR\nConnecticut CT Minnesota MN Rhode Island RI\nDelaware DE Mississippi MS South Carolina SC\nDistrict of Missouri MO South Dakota SD\nColumbia DC Montana MT Tennessee TN\nFlorida FL Nebraska NE Texas TX\nGeorgia GA Nevada NV Utah UT\nHawaii HI New Vermont VT\nIdaho ID Hampshire NH Virginia VA\n\nFOWLcp55.doc - 3\nIllinois IL New Jersey NJ Washington WA\nIndiana IN New Mexico NM West Virginia WV\nIowa IA New York NY Wisconsin WI\nKansas KS North Carolina NC Wyoming WY\nWriting requests and complaints\nLetters requesting something—for instance, a pamphlet, information about a product, a T-shirt\nadvertised in a magazine—must be specific and accurate about the item you are requesting. The\nletter should describe the item completely and, if applicable, include\na copy or description of the advertisement or other source that prompted your request.\nLetters complaining about a product or a service (such as a wrong billing from the telephone\ncompany) should be written in a reasonable but firm tone. (See the sample letter on p. 840.)\nAssume that the addressee is willing to resolve the problem when he or she has the relevant\ninformation. In the first sentence of the letter, say what you are writing about. Then provide as\nmuch background as needed, including any relevant details from past correspondence (as in the\nsample letter). Describe exactly what you see as the problem, sticking to facts and avoiding\ndiscourse on the company’s social responsibility or your low opinion of its management. In the\nclearest and fewest possible words and sentences, proceed directly from one point to the next\nwithout repeating yourself. Always include your opinion of how the problem can be solved. Many\ncompanies are required by law to establish a specific procedure for complaints about products and\nservices. If you know of such a procedure, be sure to follow it.\nWriting business memos\nUnlike business letters, which address people in other organizations, business memorandums\n(memos, for short) address people within the same organization. A memo can be quite long, but\nmore often it deals briefly with a specific topic, such as an answer to a question, a progress report,\nor an evaluation. Both the content and the format of a memo aim to get to the point and dispose of\nit quickly.\nContent\nState your reason for writing in the first sentence. You might\noutline a problem, make a request, refer to a request that prompted the memo, or briefly\nsummarize new findings. Do not, however, waste words with expressions like The purpose of\nthis memo is. . . .\nDevote the first paragraph to a succinct presentation of your solution, recommendation,\nanswer, or evaluation. The first paragraph should be short, and by its end your reader should\nknow precisely what to expect from the rest of the memo: the details and reasoning that\nsupport your conclusion.\nDeliver the support in the body of the memo. The paragraphs may be numbered or bulleted\nso that the main divisions of your message are easy to see. In a long memo, you may need\nheadings (see pp. 119–20).\nSuit your style and tone to your audience. For instance, you’ll want to address your boss or\na large group of readers more formally than you would a coworker who is also a friend.\nWrite concisely. Keep your sentences short and your language simple, using technical terms\nonly when your readers will understand them. Say only what readers need to know.\nCommunicating electronically\nElectronic communication—mainly e-mail and faxes—adds a few twists to business writing.\nE-mail plays such a prominent role in communication of all sorts that we discuss it extensively as\npart of writing online (see pp. 826–29). Generally, the standards for business e-mail are the same as\nfor other business correspondence.\nFaxes follow closely the formats of print documents, but there are some unique concerns:\nConsider legibility. Small type, photographs, horizontal lines, and other elements that look\nfine on your copy may not be legible to the addressee.\n\nFOWLcp55.doc - 4\nInclude a cover sheet. Most faxes require a cover sheet with the addressee’s name, company,\nand fax number; the date, time, and subject; your own name and fax and telephone numbers;\nand the total number of pages (including the cover sheet) in the fax.\nAdvise your addressee to expect a fax. The advice is essential if the fax is confidential\nbecause the machine is often shared.\nConsider urgency. Transmission by fax can imply that the correspondence is urgent. If yours\nisn’t, you may want to use the mail instead.\nWriting a job application\nIn applying for a job or requesting a job interview, send both a résumé and a cover letter. If\nyou need to submit your application electronically, see pages 847 and 849.\nWriting the cover letter\nThe cover letter should be formatted in block style (p. 840) or modified block style (below).\nUse the sample below and these guidelines in composing the letter:\nInterpret your résumé for the particular job. Don’t detail your entire résumé, reciting your\njob history. Instead, highlight and reshape only the relevant parts.\nAnnounce at the outset what job you seek and how you heard about it.\nInclude any special reason you have for applying, such as a specific career goal.\nSummarize your qualifications for this particular job, including relevant facts about\neducation and employment and emphasizing notable accomplishments. Mention that\nadditional information appears in an accompanying résumé.\nDescribe your availability. At the end of the letter, mention that you are free for an interview\nat the convenience of the addressee, or specify when you will be available (for instance, when\nyour current job or classes leave you free).\nWriting and formatting the résumé\nThe résumé that accompanies your letter of application should provide information in table\nformat that allows a potential employer to evaluate your qualifications. The résumé should include\nyour name and address, a career objective, your education and employment history, special skills or\nawards, and information about how to obtain your references. All the information should fit on one\nuncrowded page, unless your education and experience are extensive. See the sample on the next\npage for writing and formatting guidelines for a résumé that you submit in print.\nSome employers may ask for an electronic version of your résumé so that they can add it to a\ncomputerized database of applicants. The employers may scan your printed résumé to convert it to\nan electronic file, which they can then store in an appropriate database. Or they may ask you to\nprovide the résumé electronically, either attaching it to or embedding it in an e-mail message. If an\nemployer requests a scannable or electronic résumé, follow the guidelines below and consult the\nsample on page 849.\nKeep the design simple for accurate scanning or electronic transmittal. Avoid images,\nunusual type, more than one column, vertical or horizontal lines, italics, or underlining.\nUse concise, specific words to describe your skills and expe-rience. The employer’s\ncomputer may use keywords (often nouns) to identify the résumés of suitable job candidates,\nand you want to ensure that your résumé includes the appropriate keywords. Name your\nspecific skills—for example, the computer programs you can operate—and write concretely\nwith words like manager (not person with responsibility for) and reporter (not staff member\nwho reports). Look for likely keywords in the employer’s description of the job you seek.\nWriting business reports and proposals\nReports and proposals are text-heavy documents, sometimes lengthy, that convey information\nsuch as the results of research, a plan for action, or a recommendation for change. As with other\n\nFOWLcp55.doc - 5\nbusiness correspondence, you will prepare a report or proposal for a specific purpose, and you will\nbe addressing interested but busy readers.\nReports and proposals usually divide into sections. The sections vary depending on the\npurpose of the document, but usually they include an overview or summary, which tells the reader\nwhat the document is about; a statement of the problem or need, which justifies the report or\nproposal; a statement of the plan or solution, which responds to the need or problem; and a\nrecommendation or evaluation. Consider the following guidelines as you prepare a report or\nproposal:\nDo your research. The standard formats of reports and proposals require you to be well\ninformed, so be alert to where you have enough information or where you don’t.\nFocus on the purpose of each section. Stick to the point of each section, saying only what\nyou need to say, even if you have additional information. Each section should accomplish its\npurpose and contribute to the whole.\nFollow an appropriate format. In many businesses, reports and proposals have specific\nformatting requirements. If you are unsure about the requirements, ask your supervisor.\nWriting for community work\nAt some point in your life, you’re likely to volunteer for a community organization such as a\nsoup kitchen, a daycare center, a literacy program, or a tutoring center for immigrants learning\nEnglish. Many college courses involve service learning, in which you do such volunteer work,\nwrite about the experience for your course, and write for the organization you’re helping.\nThe writing you do for a community group may range from flyers to newsletters to grant\nproposals. Two guidelines in particular will help you prepare effective projects:\nCraft each document for its purpose and audience. You are trying to achieve a specific aim\nwith your readers, and the approach and tone you use will influence their responses. If, for\nexample, you are writing letters to local businesses to raise funds for a homeless shelter, bring\nto mind the person or people who will read your letter. How can you best persuade those\nreaders to donate money?\nExpect to work with others. Much public writing is the work of more than one person. Even\nif you draft the document on your own, others will review the content, tone, and design. Such\ncollaboration is rewarding, but it sometimes requires patience and goodwill. See pages 66–69\nand 829–32 for advice on collaborating.\nThe illustrations here and on the next page show a flyer, a news-letter, and a brochure\nprepared for ReadingWorks, a literacy program. See also pages 840–45 and 848–51 on business\nletters, memos, and proposals, as well as pages 861–62 on PowerPoint presentations.\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp55.doc - 6\nVisit the companion Web site for more help with job applications, business\nreports, and other kinds of public writing.\nBusiness lette r (block style)\nMinimum 1''\n17A Revere Street\nBoston, MA 02106\nJanuary 1, 2006\nMs. Ann Herzog\nCirculation Supervisor\nSporting Life\n25 W. 43rd Street\nNew York, NY 10036\nDear Ms. Herzog:\nThank you for your letter of December 20, which notifies me that Sporting Life will resume\nmy subscription after stopping it in error when I had\nreceived the July issue. Quick Wax has not received the promotion necessary for a new product.\nAdvertising----primarily on radio----has been sporadic and has not developed a clear,\nconsistent image for the product. In addition, the Quick Wax sales representative in\nTerritory 12 is new and inexperienced; he is not known to customers, and his sales\npitch (which I once overheard) is weak. As far as I can tell, his efforts are not\nsupported by phone calls or mailings from his home office.\n2. When Quick Wax does make it to the store shelves, buyers do not choose it over our\nproduct. Though priced competitively with our product, Quick Wax is poorly packaged.\nThe container seems smaller than ours, though in fact it holds the same eight ounces.\nThe lettering on the Quick Wax package (red on blue) is difficult to read, in\ncontrast to the white-on-green lettering on the Easy Shine package.\n3. Our special purchase offers and my increased efforts to serve existing customers\nhave had the intended effect of keeping customers satisfied with our product and\nreducing their inclination to stock something new.\nCopies: L. This report reviews the problem, considers\npossible solutions, and proposes that ABC\nInstitute and the US Fish and Wildlife Service cooperate to reduce the flock by\nhumane means.\nThe Problem\nCanada geese began living at Taylor Lake next to ABC Institute when they were re-\nlocated there in 1985 by the state game department. As a nonmigratory flock, the geese are present\nyear-round, with the highest population each year occurring in early spring. In recent years the\nflock has grown dramatically. The Audubon Society’s annual Christmas bird census shows a thirty-fold\nincrease from the 37 geese counted in 1986 to the 1125 counted in 2005.\nThe principal environmental problem caused by the geese is pollution of grass and water by\ndefecation. Geese droppings cover the ABC Institute’s grounds as well as the park’s picnicking\nareas. The runoff from these droppings into Taylor Lake has substantially affected the quality of\nthe lake’s water, so that local authorities have twice (2004 and 2005) issued warnings against\nswimming.\nPossible Solutions\nThe goose overpopulation and resulting environmental problems have several possible solutions:\no Harass the geese with dogs and audiovisual effects (light and noise) so that the geese choose to\nleave. This solution is inhumane to the geese and unpleasant for human neighbors.\no Feed the geese a chemical that will weaken the shells of their eggs and thus reduce growth of the\nflock. This solution is inhumane to the geese and also impractical, because geese are long-lived.\no Kill adult geese. This solution is, obviously, inhumane to the geese.\no Thin the goose population by trapping and removing many geese (perhaps 600) to areas less\npopulated by humans, such as wildlife preserves.\nThough costly (see figures below), the last solution is the most humane. It would be harmless\nto the geese, provided that sizable netted enclosures ar e used for traps.\n[Discussion of solution and ‘‘Recommendations’’ follow.]\nStandard for-mat: summary, statement of the problem, solutions, and (not shown)\nrecommendations\nMajor sections delineated by headings\nFormal tone,\nappropriate to a business-writing situation\nSingle spacing with double spacing between paragraphs and around the list\nBulleted list emphasizing alternative solutions\nInternal proposal\nMemo format for internal proposal (p. 844)\nSpringfield Veterans Administration Hospital\nTo: Jefferson Green, Director, Finance and Operations\nFrom: Kate Goodman, Director, ReadingWorks KG\nDate: October 17, 2005\nSubject: Budget proposal for ReadingWorks Awards Dinner\nOVERVIEW\nReadingWorks requests funding for an awards dinner.\nNEED\nReadingWorks, the literacy center operated by Springfield VA Hospital, has for 6 years served\nbetween 50 and 70 patients/students a year with a small paid staff and a corps of dedicated\nvolunteers. Oral presentation can be anxiety producing, even for those who are experienced at it. This\nchapter shows you how you can apply your experiences as a writer to public speaking, and it offers\nsome techniques that are uniquely appropriate for effective oral presentations.\n56a Writing and speaking\nWriting and speechmaking have much in common: both require careful consideration of your\nsubject, purpose, and audience. Thus the mental and physical activities that go into the writing\nprocess can also help you prepare and deliver a successful oral presentation.\nDespite many similarities, however, writing for readers is not the same as speaking to\nlisteners. Whereas a reader can go back and reread a written message, a listener cannot stop a\nspeech to rehear a section. Several studies have reported that immediately after hearing a short talk,\nmost listeners cannot recall half of what was said.\nEffective speakers adapt to their audience’s listening ability by reinforcing their ideas through\nrepetition and restatement. They use simple words, short sentences, personal pronouns,\ncontractions, and colloquial expressions. In formal writing, these strategies might seem redundant\nand too informal; but in speaking, they improve listeners’ comprehension.\n56b Considering purpose and audience\nThe most important step in developing an oral presentation is to identify your purpose: what\ndo you want your audience to know or do as a result of your speech? Topic and purpose are not the\nsame thing. Asking What am I talking about? is not the same as asking Why am I speaking?\nIn school and work settings, oral presentations may include anything from a five-minute report\nbefore a few peers to an hour-long address before a hundred people. Whatever the situation, you’re\nlikely to be speaking for the same reasons that you write in school or at work: to explain something\nto listeners or to persuade listeners to accept your opinion or take an action. See pages 14–15 for\nmore on these purposes.\nAdapting to your audience is a critical task in public speaking as well as in writing. You’ll\nwant to consider the questions about audience on page 11. But a listening audience requires\nadditional considerations as well:\nWhy is your audience assembled? Listeners who attend because they want to hear you and\nyour ideas may be easier to interest and motivate then listeners who are required to attend.\nHow large is your audience? With a small group you can be informal. If you are speaking to\na hundred or more people, you may need a public address system, a lectern, special lighting,\nand audiovisual equipment.\nWhere will you speak? Your approach should match the setting—more casual for a small\nclassroom, more formal for an auditorium.\nHow long are you scheduled to speak? Whatever the time limit, stick to it. Audiences lose\npatience with someone who speaks longer than expected.\nWhen speaking, unlike when writing, you can see and hear your audience’s responses during\nyour presentation. If you sense that an audience is bored, try to spice up your presentation. If an\naudience is restless, consult your watch to make sure you have not gone over your time. If you\nsense resistance, try to make midspeech adjustments to respond to that resistance.\n\nFOWLcp56.doc - 2\n56c Organizing the presentation\nAn effective oral presentation, like an effective essay, has a recognizable shape. The advice in\nChapter 2 for organizing and outlining an essay serves the speechmaker as well as the writer (see\npp. 32–43). Here are additional considerations for the introduction, conclusion, and supporting\nmaterial.\n1 The Introduction\nFirst impressions count. A strong beginning establishes an important relationship among three\nelements in an oral presentation: you, your topic, and your audience. More specifically, the\nbeginning of an oral presentation should try to accomplish three goals:\nGain the audience’s attention and interest. Begin with a question, an unusual example or\nstatistic, or a short, relevant story.\nPut yourself in the speech. If you demonstrate your expertise, experience, or concern, your\naudience will be more interested in what you say and more trusting of you.\nIntroduce and preview your topic and purpose. By the time your introduction is over,\nlisteners should know what your topic is and the direction in which you wish to take them as\nyou develop your ideas.\nIn addition to these guidelines for beginning a speech, there are some important pitfalls to\navoid:\nDon’t try to cram too much into your introduction. Focus on engaging the audience and\nquickly previewing your talk.\nDon’t begin with an apology. A statement such as I wish I’d been given more time to get\nready for this presentation will only undermine your listeners’ confidence in you.\nDon’t begin with My speech is about. . . . The statement is dull, and it does little to clarify\npurpose.\n2 Supporting material\nJust as you do when writing, you can and should use facts, statistics, examples, and expert\nopinions to support spoken arguments (see pp. 184–85). In addition, as a speaker you can draw on\nother kinds of supporting material:\nUse vivid description. Paint a mental image of a scene, a concept, an event, or a person.\nUse well-chosen quotations. They can add an emotional or humorous moment to your speech.\nUse true or fictional stories. A memorable narrative can rivet the audience’s attention and\nillustrate your point.\nUse analogies. Comparisons between essentially unlike things, such as a politician and a\ntightrope walker, link concepts memorably. (For more on analogy, see pp. 97–98.)\nUse a variety of supporting material in your speech. A presentation that is nothing but\nstatistics can bore an audience. Nonstop storytelling may interest listeners but fail to achieve your\npurpose.\n3 The conclusion\nLast impressions count as much as first impressions. You may hope that listeners will\nremember every detail of your speech, but they are more likely to leave with a general impression\nand a few ideas about you and your message. You want your conclusion to be clear, of course, but\nyou also want it to be memorable. Remind listeners of how your topic and main idea connect to\ntheir needs and interests.\n56d Delivering the presentation\nWriting and speaking differ most obviously in the form of delivery: the writer is represented in\nprint; the speaker is represented in person. This section describes the methods and techniques of\noral presentation as well as some ways of coping with stage fright.\n1 Methods of delivery\nAn oral presentation may be delivered impromptu, extemporaneously, from a text, or from\nmemory. No one technique is best for all speeches; indeed, a single speech may include two or\n\nFOWLcp56.doc - 3\nmore forms or even all four—perhaps a memorized introduction, an extemporaneous body in which\nquotations are read from a text, and impromptu responses to audience questions during or after the\nspeech.\nSpeaking impromptu\nImpromptu means ―without preparation‖: an impromptu pres-\nentation is one you deliver off-the-cuff, with no planning or practice. You may be called on in a\nclass to express your opinion or to summarize something you’ve written. You may speak up at a\nneighborhood meeting. An audience member may ask you a question at the end of an oral\npresentation. The only way to prepare for such incidents is to be well prepared in general—to be\ncaught up on course reading, for instance, or to know the facts in a debate.\nSpeaking extemporaneously\nExtemporaneous speaking—that done with some preparation, but without reading from a\ntext—is the most common form of pres-entation, typical of class lectures and business briefings.\nWith extemporaneous speaking, you have time to prepare and practice in advance. Then, instead of\nfollowing a script of every word, you speak from notes that guide you through the presentation.\nSpeaking from a text\nDelivering a presentation from a text involves writing the text out in advance and then reading\naloud from it. With a text in front of you, you’re unlikely to lose your way. However, a reading\nspeaker can be dull for an audience. Try to avoid this form of delivery for an entire presentation.\nIf you do use a text, write it so that it sounds spoken (less formal) rather than written (more\nformal): for instance, the sentence Although costs rose, profits remained steady would sound fine\nin writing but stiff and awkward in speech because in conversation we rarely use such a structure.\nIn addition, rehearse thoroughly so that you can read with expression and can look up frequently to\nmake eye contact with listeners (see the next page).\nSpeaking from memory\nA memorized presentation has a distinct advantage: complete freedom from notes or a text.\nThat means you can look at your audience every minute and can move away from a lectern and\neven into the audience. However, you may be like most speakers in seeming less relaxed, not more\nrelaxed, when presenting from memory: your mind is too busy retrieving the next words to attend\nto the responses of the audience. Further, you risk forgetting your place or a whole passage.\nFor these reasons, many experts discourage memorization. At least reserve the method for the\nintroduction, perhaps, or some other part with which you want to make a strong impression.\nRehearse not only to memorize the words but, beyond that, to deliver the words fresh, as if for the\nfirst time.\n2 Vocal delivery\nThe sound of your voice will influence how your listeners receive you. When rehearsing,\nconsider volume, speed, and articulation.\nSpeak loudly. In a meeting with five other people, you can speak in a normal volume. As your\naudience grows in size, so should your volume. Most speakers can project to as many as a\nhundred people, but a larger audience may require a microphone.\nSpeak slowly enough to be understandable.\nSpeak clearly and correctly. To avoid mumbling or slurring words, practice articulating.\nSometimes it helps to open your mouth a little wider than usual.\n3 Physical delivery\nYou are more than your spoken words when you make an oral presentation. Your face and\nbody also play a role in how your speech is received.\nMake eye contact with listeners. Move your gaze around the entire room, settle on someone,\nand establish direct eye contact; then move on to someone else.\nStand up. Always stand for a presentation, unless it takes place in a small room where\nstanding would be inappropriate. You can see more audience members when you stand, and\nthey in turn can hear your voice and see your gestures more clearly.\n\nFOWLcp56.doc - 4\nStand straight, and move around. Turn your body toward one side of the room and then the\nother, step out from behind any lectern or desk, and gesture appropriately, as you would in\nconversation.\n4 Visual aids\nYou can supplement an oral presentation with visual aids such as posters, models, slides,\nvideos, or presentation software such as PowerPoint. Visual aids can emphasize key points,\norganize related concepts, and illustrate complex procedures. They can gain the attention of\nlisteners and improve their understanding and memory.\nThe following guidelines can help you create effective and appropriate visual aids:\nUse visual aids to underscore your points. Short lists of key ideas, illustrations such as\ngraphs or photographs, or objects such as models can make your presentation more interesting\nand memorable. But use visual aids judiciously: a constant flow of illustrations or objects will\nbury your message.\nMatch visual aids and setting. An audience of five people may be able to see a photograph\nand share a chart; an audience of a hundred will need projected images.\nCoordinate visual aids with your message. Time each visual aid to reinforce a point you’re\nmaking. Tell listeners what they’re looking at—what they should be getting from the aid. Give\nthem enough viewing time so they don’t mind turning their attention back to you.\nShow visual aids only while they’re needed. To regain your audience’s attention, remove or\nturn off any aid as soon as you have finished with it.\nMany speakers use PowerPoint or other software to project visual aids. (See the sample slides\non the next page.) Screens of brief points supported by data, images, or video can help listeners\nfollow your main points. To use PowerPoint or other software effectively, follow the guidelines\nabove and also the following:\nDon’t put your whole presentation on screen. Select key points, and distill them to as few\nwords as possible. Think of the slides as quick, easy-to-remember summaries.\nUse a simple design. Avoid turning your presentation into a show about the software’s many\ncapabilities.\nUse a consistent design. For optimal flow through the presentation, each slide should be\nformatted similarly.\nAdd only relevant illustrations. Avoid loading the presentation with mere decoration.\n5 Practice\nPracticing an oral presentation is the speechmaker’s equivalent of editing and proofreading a\nwritten text. You won’t gain much by practicing silently in your head; instead, you need to rehearse\nout loud, with the notes you will be using. For your initial rehearsals, you can gauge your\nperformance by making an audio- or videotape of yourself or by practicing in front of a mirror. A\nrecording will let you hear mumbling, too-rapid delivery, grammatical errors, mispronounced\nwords, and unclear concepts. A mirror or video will reveal your stance, your gestures, and your eye\ncontact. Any of these practice techniques will tell you if your presentation is running too long or\ntoo short.\nIf you plan to use visual aids, you’ll need to practice with them, too, preferably in the room\nwhere you’ll make the presentation and certainly with the help of anyone who will be assisting you.\nYour goal is to eliminate hitches (upside-down slides, missing charts) and to weave the visuals\nseamlessly into your presentation.\n6 Stage fright\nMany people report that speaking in front of an audience is their number-one fear. Even many\nexperienced and polished speakers have some anxiety about delivering an oral presentation, but\nthey use this nervous energy to their advantage, letting it propel them into working hard on each\npresentation, and rehearsing until they’re satisfied with their delivery. They know that the\nsymptoms of\nanxiety are usually imperceptible to listeners, who cannot see or hear a racing heart, upset stomach,\ncold hands, and worried thoughts.\nSeveral techniques can help you reduce your level of anxiety:\nUse simple relaxation exercises. Deep breathing or tensing and relaxing your stomach\nmuscles can ease some of the physical symptoms of speech anxiety.\n\nFOWLcp56.doc - 5\nThink positively. Instead of worrying about the mistakes you might make, concentrate on how\nwell you’ve prepared and practiced your presentation and how significant your ideas are.\nDon’t avoid opportunities to speak in public. Practice and experience build speaking skills\nand offer the best insurance for success.\nhttp://www.ablongman.com/littlebrown\n\nFOWLcp56.doc - 6\nVisit the companion Web site for more help with oral presentations and\nPowerPoint.\nChecklist for an oral presentation\nPurpose: What do you want your audience to know or do as a result of your presentation? How can\nyou achieve your purpose in the time and setting you’ve been given? (See the previous page and\nbelow.)\nAudience: What do you know about the characteristics and opinions of your audience? How can this\ninformation help you adapt your\npresentation to your audience’s interests, needs, and opinions? (See below.)\nOrganization and content: How are your ideas arranged? Where might listeners have difficulty\nfollowing you? What functions do your introduction and conclusion perform? How relevant and\ninteresting\nis your supporting material for your topic and your audience? (See opposite.)\nMethod of delivery: What method of delivery do you plan: extemporaneous? reading from a text?\nmemorized? a mixture? How does your method suit the purpose, setting, and occasion of your\npresentation? (See pp. 855–57.)\nVocal and physical delivery: In rehearsing your presentation, what do you perceive as your strengths\nand weaknesses? Is your voice suitably loud for the setting? Are you speaking clearly? Are you able to\nmove your eyes around the room so that you’ll be making eye contact during the presentation? Is your\nposture straight but not stiff? Do your gestures reinforce your ideas? Do you use visual aids\nappropriately? (See pp. 861–62.)\nConfidence and credibility: What techniques will you use to overcome the inevitable anxiety about\nspeaking? How will you project your confidence and competence? (See pp. 862–63.)\nPowerPoint slides\nMaking a Difference?\nFirst slide, introducing the project and\npresentation\nA Service-Learning Project\nat ReadingWorks\nSpringfield Veterans\nAdministration Hospital\nJessica Cho\nNathan Hall\nAlex Ramirez\nFALL 2005\nSimple, consistent\nslide design focusing\nviewers’ attention\non information, not PowerPoint features\nSemester goals\nLater slide, using brief, bulleted points to be explained by the speaker\n• Tutor military veterans\n• Research adult literacy\n• Keep a journal\n• Collaborate on documents\nfor ReadingWorks\n• Report experiences and findings\nPhotographs\nreinforcing the\nproject’s activities",
